0.64 meq/g
to
2.29 meq/g (i.e. 11.5 mg/g to 41.2 mg/g (Wang and Peng, 2010)).
The capacity range of 12.13 mg/g to 18.19 mg/g (±10–20%) were
investigated in the sensitivity analysis – the results indicated that
the inﬂuence of exchange capacity on LCIA proﬁles vary with sce-
narios and impact categories (as shown in Figs. 6 and S4 in SI).
With an assumption of increased exchange capacity, the overall
environmental proﬁles of ion exchange shifted dramatically (with
10–200% impact reduction, except for eutrophication), which is
above our chosen sensitivity threshold; however, the eutrophica-
tion scores are not sensitive to the capacity change. The LCIA ranks
in abiotic depletion shifted while exchange capacity increased by
20% – ion exchange moved to an advantageous position with
regards to the other two routes. Our research outcome indicated
that the greatest potential for ion exchange could be achieved via
the addition of phosphorus eliminating unit, and selection of a
high-performance zeolite adsorbent.
4. Conclusion
The study was carried out to evaluate three alternatives to
remove and recover nitrogen from municipal wastewater i.e. con-
ventional nitriﬁcation–denitriﬁcation, Anammox and anaerobic
route with ion exchange. Our modelling results demonstrate that
ion exchange as a promising technology has great potential to
achieve high N removal efﬁciencies and deliver economically and
environmentally optimal performance via process design and opti-
misation. Our research highlights the insights such integrated
modelling tools as proposed in this study could provide for
decision-making on waste resource recovery system designs which
will
underpin
the
transition
towards
a
sustainable
circular
economy.

We gratefully acknowledge Dr Benoit Chachuat for kindly pro-
viding access to GPS-X and CapdetWorks. We wish to acknowledge
Dr Channarong Puchongkawarin for kindly sharing his expertise in
GPS-X simulation.

records.full_text:
Enhanced biomethane production with a low carbon footprint via anaerobic
co-digestion of swine wastewater with rice husk
Hung-Cheng Lin a, Shu-Yuan Pan a,b,⁎, Yu-I Lin a, Dan-Thanh Ngoc Cao a
a Department of Bioenvironmental Systems Engineering, National Taiwan University, Taipei City 106, Taiwan, ROC
b Agricultural Net-Zero Carbon Technology and Management Innovation Research Center, National Taiwan University, Taipei City 106, Taiwan, ROC
H I G H L I G H T S
G R A P H I C A L
A B S T R A C T
• EAAD exhibits positive improvement
(2.6–14.5 %) in biogas production com-
pared to AD.
• A substantial amount of carbon intensity
(17.6–21.7 %) could be reduced by EAAD.
• Biogas production rate in EAAD ranged
1.87–5.23 mL/g-VS/d, higher than that
in AD.
• Acetoclastic methanogens accounts for
56.6 % ± 0.6 % of the biomethane pro-
duction.
• The preliminary economic analysis indi-
cated
a
beneﬁt-cost
ratio
of
1.33
for EAAD.
A B S T R A C T
A R T I C L E
I N F O
Editor: Qilin Wang
Keywords:
Co-digestion
Modiﬁed Gompertz model
Microbial analysis
Reaction pathways
Life-cycle assessment
Economic analysis
An electricity-assisted anaerobic co-digestion (EAAD) process was developed and compared with conventional anaer-
obic co-digestion (AD) using piggery wastewater and rice husk as feedstocks. Various methodologies, including kinetic
models, microbial community analyses, life-cycle carbon footprints, and preliminary economic analysis, were inte-
grated to comprehensively evaluate the performance of the two processes. The results demonstrated that EAAD exhib-
ited a positive improvement of 2.6 % to 14.5 % in biogas production compared to AD. The suitable wastewater-to-husk
ratio for EAAD was found to be 3:1, which corresponded to a carbon-to‑nitrogen ratio of approximately 14. This ratio
demonstrated positive co-digestion effects and electrical enhancements in the process. According to the modiﬁed
Gompertz kinetics, the biogas production rate in EAAD ranged from 1.87 to 5.23 mL/g-VS/d, signiﬁcantly higher
than the range of 1.19 to 3.74 mL/g-VS/d observed in AD. The study also investigated the contributions of acetoclastic
and hydrogenotrophic methanogens to biomethane formation, revealing that acetoclastic methanogens accounted for
56.6 % ± 0.6 % of the methane production, while hydrogenotrophic methanogens contributed to 43.4 % ± 0.6 %. No
signiﬁcant difference in the methanogenic reaction pathways was observed between AD and EAAD, indicating that the
introduction of an external electric ﬁeld did not alter the predominant pathways (p > 0.05, two-sample t-test). Further-
more, retroﬁtting existing AD plants with EAAD units can reduce the carbon intensity of piggery wastewater treatment
by 17.6 % to 21.7 %. The preliminary economic analysis indicated a beneﬁt-cost ratio of 1.33 for EAAD, conﬁrming the
feasibility of implementing EAAD for wastewater treatment while simultaneously producing bioenergy. Overall, this
study provides valuable insights into upgrading the performance of existing AD plants by introducing an external elec-
tric ﬁeld. It demonstrates that EAAD can achieve higher and cost-effective biogas production with a lower life-cycle
carbon footprint, thus enhancing the sustainability and efﬁciency of the biogas production process.
Science of the Total Environment 891 (2023) 164612
⁎ Corresponding author.
E-mail address: sypan@ntu.edu.tw (S.-Y. Pan).
http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.scitotenv.2023.164612

1. Introduction
Taiwan is one of the countries with the highest density of pig production
(Yeh et al., 2010), i.e., ∼5.5 million head of pigs with a daily discharge of
swine wastewater at over 110,000 t. Piggery wastewater (PW) contains a
high portion of organic matter and nutrients, which might pose a signiﬁcant
threat to the environment without proper treatment (Chen et al., 2021). An-
aerobic digestion (AD) has been considered as a sustainable approach to the
valorization of organic matter in waste streams as bioenergy and
bioresource (Holohan et al., 2022). Therefore, PW treatment using AD
has received tremendous attention to realize circular bioeconomy and sus-
tainable waste management. Despite the extensive research and develop-
ment on AD, several challenges including technology improvement,
evolution of regulatory policies, and ﬁnancial incentives (Satchwell et al.,
2018) still exist. Particularly, Zheng et al. (2022b) indicated that low con-
version efﬁciency of swine manure should be a challenge for commercial
AD operations to achieve an economically feasible model.
A few studies attempted to improve the biogas production from AD of
swine wastes using various approaches, such as solid-state AD (Zheng
et al., 2022a), an anaerobic membrane bioreactor (Jiang et al., 2020), a
coupled
microbial
electrolysis
cell
(Zou
et
al.,
2021),
biochar-
incorporated AD (Wang et al., 2022), and semi-dry AD with zero valent
iron and magnetite (Zheng et al., 2022b). For instance, Zou et al. (2021)
conducted a study on the combined use of AD and microbial electrolysis
cell (MEC) for simultaneous energy recovery and swine manure valoriza-
tion. They achieved a maximum biomethane (CH4) production of
∼348 mL/g-TS through the coupled process. An et al. (2023) further inves-
tigated the application of black phosphorus as accelerants in the AD-MEC
system, resulting in a signiﬁcant enhancement of ∼72 % in CH4 produc-
tion. It is worth noting that the MEC operates on a similar principle to AD
in terms of organic substance breakdown; however, the end products differ
as the ultimate electron acceptor is varied (Koul et al., 2022). In an MEC,
microorganisms on the anode facilitate the decomposition of organic sub-
stances into proton and CO2. The generated protons can then be electro-
chemically converted into hydrogen on the cathode. Moreover, in the
MEC, CH4 can be produced either through electrochemical or biochemical
processes, leading to improved biogas production. Similarly, Zheng et al.
(2022b) introduced zero valent iron and magnetite in AD processes, and
achieved an increased CH4 yield by 17.6–22.7 % due to the promotion of
interspecies hydrogen transfer. However, no synergetic effect was observed
in their experiments. This reveals that a fundamental understanding is still
needed to unravel the synergetic effect of integrated system on digestion ef-
ﬁciency of swine wastes.
From the perspective of process improvement, an external electric ﬁeld
can be introduced in bioreaction systems to stimulate the electrons transfer
between intracellular and extracellular of cells. For instance, the
electrofermentation process has been developed to improve the redox im-
balance in fermentation by regulating the oxidation reduction potential
and NAD+/NADH (Bhagchandanii et al., 2020). When the routes for elec-
tron transfer increased, the rate of bioreactions would increase, potentially
with the formation of new products (Bhagchandanii et al., 2020; Jiang
et al., 2019). A number of studies have been conducted to use
electrofermentation for the synthesis of value-added products, such as eth-
anol (Mohan et al., 2016), volatile fatty acids (Zhou et al., 2019), organic
acids (Chandrasekhar et al., 2015), lysine (Vassilev et al., 2018), and
biohydrogen (Modestra et al., 2015). Gong et al. (2020) used microbial
electrofermentation to synthesize chemicals and biofuels, and noticed a
high yield of products due to the bi-directional extracellular electron trans-
fer. Similar to electrofermentation, electricity-assisted anaerobic digestion
(EAAD) represents a promising approach for enhancing the performance
of AD systems and optimizing biogas production. It is important to high-
light that the primary end products of electrofermentation, MEC and
EAAD differ. Electrofermentation is primarily focused on the production
of biochemicals such as organic acids. On the other hand, MEC is speciﬁ-
cally designed to produce biohydrogen, while EAAD aims to produce
biomethane.
To the best of our knowledge, little-to-no research has been conducted
on comprehensively evaluating the performance of EAAD for biogas pro-
duction using PW. Therefore, the objective of this study was to conduct a
comprehensive performance evaluation of EAAD processes. This evaluation
was accomplished through the integration of various methodologies, in-
cluding kinetic models, microbial community analyses, life-cycle carbon
footprints, and a preliminary economic analysis. In this study, the EAAD
process was applied for PW to evaluate the effect of co-digestion and elec-
trical enhancement, and rice husks (RHs) were selected as the co-
digestate with PW. RHs in Taiwan are readily available due to the proximity
to the production of PW, making them a convenient and cost-effective sub-
strate. RH possesses a ﬁbrous structure that offers favorable porosity and
surface area, facilitating the colonization of microorganisms during AD.
Furthermore, RH helps maintain an appropriate C/N ratio for microbial ac-
tivity and efﬁcient biogas production. In this study, the effect of different
co-digestion ratios and applied voltages on the biogas production was eval-
uated. A kinetic model was established to determine key digestion parame-
ters, such as the maximum production rate and the lag-phase time of
digestion. The microbial communities of representative digestate samples
were also analyzed to elucidate the mechanisms for enhanced biogas pro-
duction. Lastly, a preliminary economic analysis and life-cycle environmen-
tal beneﬁts (including carbon footprints) of the EAAD process were
calculated to understand the feasibility of large-scale operations. By com-
bining these multidisciplinary approaches, the study aimed to provide a
comprehensive understanding of the engineering performance, environ-
mental impact, and economic viability of EAAD, encompassing the three as-
pect of engineering, environment and economy (3E). This study is crucial
for guiding future deployment of EAAD as an efﬁcient biogas production
technology, paving the way for its integration into existing energy systems.
2. Materials and methods
2.1. Materials
In this study, PW and seed sludge (inoculum) discharged from a pig
farm in Chiayi city, Taiwan were used as substrates. All collected PW was
crude as-received without passing through any treatment facility. RH
from a local farmer in Yunlin, Taiwan was used as the co-digestate.
Table 1 presents the chemical properties of the collected PW, RH and inoc-
ulum used in this study. The pH value and conductivity of PW were 6.9 and
14.0 mS/cm, respectively, by using a multipurpose meter (WA2017SD,
Lutrin, Taiwan). It is noted that the pH of PW was within the optimal pH
value (i.e., 6.8–7.2) (Ward et al., 2008) for AD. The PW also exhibited
high concentrations of TKN, TP, and COD. The C/N ratios of PW, RH and
inoculum were 7.9, 34.3 and 9.7, respectively.
2.2. Experimental set-up
In this study, anaerobic co-digestion (AcD) was operated in a batch
mode using serum bottles (2 L) at a mesophilic temperature (37 ± 1 °C).
Each digestor bottle containing 1.0 kg of PW and 0.25 kg of sludge inocu-
lum was mounted within a water bath for 80 days. As the suggested C/N
ratio for AD was 16–30 (Adekunle and Okolie, 2015; Ning et al., 2019),
RH was added in PW to regulate C/N ratio of digestate in this study. Five
levels of co-digestion ratios (PW:RH based on the weight of the substrate
as received) were selected, i.e., 3:0 (C/N = 8.3), 3:1 (C/N = 13.7), 3;2
(C/N = 17.3), 3:3 (C/N = 19.8), and 0:3 (C/N = 29.4). The contents of
total solids for blended digestate were <20 %, which was classiﬁed to be
the wet digestion (Rocamora et al., 2020). Prior to AcD experiments, the
manure sludge was initially treated at 40 °C under anaerobic conditions
to reduce the water content. Acclimatization on the inoculum was also con-
ducted by adding an activation medium (10.0 g/L of NaHCO3, 10.0 g/L of
CH3COONa, 0.5 g/L of NH4Cl, 0.1 g/L of MgSO4·7H2O, and 0.1 g/L of
KH2PO4) (Ren et al., 2018) and glucose (5.0 g/L) to provide energy for mi-
croorganism and carbon source for biological reaction. Each digestor bottle
was ﬂushed with inert nitrogen gas to create an anaerobic environment
H.-C. Lin et al.
Science of the Total Environment 891 (2023) 164612
2

before experiments. In the EAAD system, two graphite electrodes (L
150 mm × D 20 mm, purchased from Nippon Carbon Co. Ltd., Japan)
were inserted into the batch digestors at a low but constant voltage of
0.6 V. The axis distance of two electrodes in the bioreactor was 40 mm.
The current range was tested from 0.5 to 3.0 mA, which was relatively
smaller than the microbial electro synthesis system (Ren et al., 2018).
The biogas produced from each bottle was daily collected by water dis-
placement method (see Fig. S1 in the Supplementary Information). Every
biogas sample was examined at least three times by a gas chromatography
(7890GC, Agilent Technologies, USA) equipped with a thermal conductiv-
ity detector. The HP-PLOT-Q column (30.0 m long, 0.53 mm diameter)
was used for biogas (CH4, H2 and CO2) measurements. The oven in GC
was set at 35 ± 1 °C with a retention time of 2.2 min. The temperatures
of injection and detector were controlled at 200 °C and 220 °C, respectively.
The calibration curves of gas were constructed by a standard gas (Airgas,
Inc., USA) (Pan et al., 2020).
2.3. Performance evaluation and kinetic models
In this study, the performance of AcD processes was evaluated with sev-
eral indicators, including co-digestion enhancement factor, electrical en-
hancement factor, cumulative biogas (e.g., CH4 and CO2) production, and
kinetic rate constant. To determine the inﬂuence of co-digestion on biogas
production, the co-digestion enhancement factor (Ec, as described by
Eqs. (1) and (2)) was used.
Ec ¼ Pexp  Pthe
Pthe
 100%
(1)
Pthe ¼ XA  PA þ XB  PB
(2)
where Pexp is experimental biogas production (mL/g-VS); Pthe is theoretical
biogas production (mL/g-VS); PA and PB are the experimental biogas pro-
duction (mL/g-VS) using pure A and B as the substrate, respectively; XA
and XB are the ratio of VS from A and B in the co-digestion experiment, re-
spectively. If Ec is >0, it represents that the real biogas production is higher
than the expected co-digestion production (i.e., synergistic effect). If Ec is
<0, the activity of microorganisms with the mix-substrates might be
inhibited (i.e., antagonistic effect).
Similarly, to address the effect of external applied voltage (i.e., 0 V vs.
0.6 V) on biogas production, the electrical enhancement factor (Ee, as de-
scribed by Eq. (3)) was calculated.
Ee ¼ PEAAD  PAD
PAD
 100%
(3)
where PEAAD and PAD are the biomethane (CH4) production from the EAAD
and AD systems (mL/g-VS), respectively. If Ee is >0, it represents that the
biomethane production from EAAD is higher than that from AD. If Ee is
<0, it indicates that the biomethane production might be inhibited by intro-
ducing an external electric ﬁeld in AD. To further understand the indepen-
dent effect of voltage on the biogas production, an additional experiment
was conducted with an external voltage of 1.2 V to the pure PW case
(PW:RH = 3:0).
The cumulative biomethane production (B) and the kinetics of AcD in a
batch culture were evaluated using the modiﬁed Gompertz model (see
Eq. (4)) (Lay et al., 1997):
B ¼ B0 exp −exp μme
B0
λ−t
ð
Þ þ 1




ð4Þ
where t is the digestion duration (day), B0 is the cumulative (maximum)
biomethane production (mL/g-VS), μm is the maximal biomethane
production rate (mL/g-VS/d), e is the Euler's number (i.e., 2.718), and λ
is the lag-phase duration (day). The obtained kinetic parameters can be
used to estimate the performance of the full-scale operations, and to im-
prove the digestor design. In this study, the analyses and model regression
were performed using SigmaPlot (version 12, Gambit COiS Sp. Z. O. O.,
Poland).
2.4. Microbial community analyses
To elucidate the reaction pathways of co-digestion, DNA extraction of
microorganisms was performed on the samples collected from representa-
tive experiments, including the inoculum, original PW and samples after
the AD and EAAD reaction. In this study, the DNA of 13 samples were puri-
ﬁed and extracted by a local biotechnology company (Genomics Co., Ltc,
Taiwan). The 16S genes were ampliﬁed by the speciﬁc primers with the
overhang linker sequence. The forward primer 341F-805R and the reverse
primer were used to amplify the V3-V4 hypervariable regions of the bacte-
ria and archaeal 16S rRNA gene. All samples for polymerase chain reactions
were conducted using KAPA® HiFi HotStart Ready Mix (KAPA
BIOSYSTEMS), primers, and DNA template. The ampliﬁcation was carried
out at 95 °C for 3 min, with a subsequent 25-cycle denaturation at 95 °C, an-
nealing at 57 °C, elongation at 72 °C, and extension at 72 °C. The products
from polymerase chain reactions, using an Agarose Gel electrophoresis,
were examined on Qsep400 with a Standard Cartridge (Bioptic Inc.,
Taiwan) and a Qubit 2.0 Fluorometer (Thermo Scientiﬁc, Waltham, MA,
USA). For the reaction pathway, the standard free energy change (ΔGo,f,
kJ per mol of CH4) was calculated from the Gibbs free energy of formation
of the most abundant ionic species at pH 7.
2.5. Environmental and economic analyses
For the environmental impact and carbon footprint, life cycle assess-
ment (LCA) is a standardized approach to systematic evaluation of the
whole process in different aspects. In this study, the environmental beneﬁts
and carbon footprint were evaluated by LCA using Umberto software
(LCA+, ifu Hamburg, Germany). Fig. S2 (see the Supplementary Informa-
tion) show the system boundary and material ﬂows for AD and EAAD. Con-
sidering the data of lab-scale experiments in this study, “one gram of
methane production” was used as the functional unit for LCA. The inputs in-
clude feedstock, utility, and energy usage for an oven and an external power

study.

To evaluate the feasibility of large-scale operations, three scenarios,
i.e., (A) conventional AD system with power generation using combined
heat and power (CHP), designated as the business-as-usual (BAU),
(B) EAAD system with CHP, and (C) integrated AD and EAAD system with
CHP, were developed. Table S1 (see the Supplementary Information) pre-
sents the inventory data for large-scale operations. In scenario C, it was as-
sumed that 50 % of the current PW would be delivered to the EAAD
process, while the remaining 50 % would continue to be treated using con-
ventional AD. Consequently, the bioelectricity generated from AD would be
utilized to fulﬁll the energy requirements of EAAD. For all scenarios, the func-
tion unit was “one tonne of PW delivered to the system”. The emission factor
of carbon dioxide for power generation in Taiwan in 2021 (i.e., 0.502 kg CO2-
eq per kWh) was used. The CHP power generation by the biogas was assumed
to be 2.941 kWh per m3 of biogas. The distance of transportation between the
source of wastewater to AD plants was assumed to be 20 km.
In the preliminary economic analysis, the beneﬁt-cost ratio of AD and
EAAD was determined based on the production of one cubic meter of biogas
from these processes. The potential beneﬁts considered in the analysis in-
clude bio-electricity selling price (feed-in-tariff in Taiwan, i.e., US$ 0.234
per kWh) and avoidance of PW treatment (estimated at US$ 0.167 per
tonne of PW) and discharge (i.e., US$ 0.228 per tonne of PW) fees. The
costs taken into account in the analysis include operation expenses of AD
(Al-Wahaibi et al., 2020) and additional energy use in EAAD processes
(gathered from experimental data). It is noted that several potential bene-
ﬁts and costs, such as digestate valorization revenue, capital expenses,
maintenance expenses, labor costs, and transportation expenses, were not
considered in this analysis.
3. Results and discussion
3.1. Performance of biogas production and its compositions
Fig. 1 shows the effect of different co-digestion ratios and digestion du-
ration on cumulative biogas (including CH4, CO2, and H2) productions from
AD and EAAD. In general, a rapid biogas production can be observed be-
tween 10 and 20 days, and the biogas production after 40 days exhibited
a stationary phase. The results also indicated that the sum of cumulative
CH4, CO2 and H2 production was lower than the cumulative biogas yield.
The speciﬁc portions of CH4 and H2 within the biogas yield vary, ranging
from 25.3 % to 51.3 % (see Fig. S3), depending on the microbial activity
involved. In other words, the biogas contained other components such as
moisture and trace gases. These trace gases include hydrogen sulﬁde
(H2S), nitrogen (N2), ammonia (NH3), carbon monoxide (CO), and volatile
compounds such as alcohols, aldehydes, and aromatics (Lecharlier et al.,
2022). The presence of these components in biogas underscores the impor-
tance of subsequent puriﬁcation processes for bioenergy generation. Proper
biogas treatment is necessary to remove these components for meeting the
desired biogas quality standards and ensuring the safe utilization of biogas
for various applications (Werkneh, 2022).
For all experiments, it was observed that the cumulative CH4 production
of EAAD was generally higher than AD at the same level of PW:RH ratio, as
shown in Figs. 1(b-c). In the treatment of one tonne of PW co-digested with
RH, the approximate CH4 production in AD and EAAD was 2.60–3.30 and
2.80–3.39 m3, respectively. For digestion of pure PW, it was demonstrated
that EAAD (∼37.9 mL/g-VS) had better performance on cumulative CH4
production than AD (∼32.9 mL/g-VS). For digestion of pure RH (corre-
sponding to a C/N ratio of 29), the cumulative CH4 yields in AD and
EAAD were approximately 64.1 and 74.9 mL/g-VS, respectively. It is
noted that C/N ratio of digestate is the key parameters of feedstock for
AD. The suitable C/N ratio for AD was regarded between 20 and 30, and
a ratio of 25 was the most commonly used (Mao et al., 2015). In the cases
of co-digestion, the cumulative CH4 yield with a PW:RH ratio of 3:1 was
found to be the highest (∼68.6 mL/g-VS).
For cumulative CO2 production, the highest yields for AD and EAAD
were found to be 13.4 (PW:RH = 3:1) and 12.8 (PW:RH = 0:3) mL-CO2/
g-VS, respectively (see Fig. 1(e)). In the rest groups, no signiﬁcant differ-
ence (p = 0.977 > 0.05; two sample t-test) in CO2 yield between AD and
EAAD was observed. CO2 can be generated not only from the degradation
of organic acids (through the decomposition of organic matter), but also
from the metabolism (respiration) of microbial cells (Cheng et al., 2009;

Fasbender et al., 2018). The generated CO2 in digestors also could be fur-
ther converted to CH4 through hydrogenotrophic methanogens (Rotaru
et al., 2014). Notably, in the case of PW:RH ratio of 3:1 (corresponding to
a C/N ratio of ∼14), EAAD can achieve a lower CO2 production while an
enhanced CH4 production, compared to AD. This provides an insight to
the potential of a better conditions for biogas due to a lower puriﬁcation
loading for the subsequent CO2 removal in biogas.
Fig. 1(f) shows the cumulative H2 production, indicating a rapid H2 pro-
duction at the beginning 5 days, and another production peak within
10–20 days (in some cases, 20–30 days). In this study, H2 was not consid-
ered as the primary end-product of the complex digestion reactions. This
was attributed to the fact that the portion of H2 in the total biogas generated
ranged only between 0.2 % and 0.4 %, as illustrated in Fig. S3. The highest
H2 production was observed in the case of AD (e.g., ∼0.57 mL/g-VS). The
application of an external electric ﬁeld at 0.6 V did not have a signiﬁcant
effect on H2 production (p > 0.05). This implies that EAAD did not lead to
a substantial increase in H2 production compared to AD, even in cases
where EAAD exhibited relatively higher production rates, such as a PW:
RH ratio of 3:2 or 0:3. It is noted that H2 should be mainly generated
from the hydrolysis stage and the NADH+/NADH balance reaction
(Buswell and Mueller, 1952); however, it may further oxidize to form H2
and/or acetic acid during AD (Zhou et al., 2019) through fermentation re-
actions.
3.2. Co-digestion and electrical enhancement factors
The co-digestion enhancement factor (Ec) was calculated to determine
the synergistic or antagonistic effect of co-digestion reactions, compared
to AD using a pure substrate. Fig. 2(a) shows the effect of different PW:
RH ratios on the Ec value. The results indicated that the PW/RH ratio
would greatly determine the synergistic effect of co-digestion. For both
AD and EAAD, a synergistic co-digestion effect was occurred only at the
PW:RH ratio of 3:1, and the Ec value (enhancement of biogas production)
for AD and EAAD were 64.2 % and 45.5 %, respectively. For other tested
PW:RH ratio, the antagonistic effect was observed probably due to the
ﬁber content and/or the inappropriate VS content with the C/N ratio. Bah
et al. (2014) suggested that some types of plant ﬁber as the substrate in
co-digestion would mitigate the biogas production rate, and exhibit a par-
tial antagonistic effect.
Similarly, Figs. 2(b-c) show the effect of different PW:RH ratios and ap-
plied external voltages on the electrical enhancement factor. The electrical
enhancement factor (Ee) is deﬁned as the level of biogas production im-
provement due to the external electric ﬁeld. The results indicated that the
external electric ﬁeld exhibited a positive improvement on biogas produc-
tion, except for the PW/RH ratio of 3:3. This might be attributed to the re-
duced electrical conductivity of digestate in the case of a PW/RH ratio of
3:3. In general, the Ee value at an applied voltage of 0.6 V was found to
be in the range between 2.6 % and 14.5 %, indicating a positive improve-
ment in biogas production. However, when the external voltage further in-
creased to 1.2 V, the Ee value for CH4, H2 and CO2 production became
negative (see Fig. 2(c)). This reveals an inhibition effect on biogas produc-
tion at a high external voltage. Similar observations were reported that an
applied voltage higher than 1.0 V would inhibit biogas production
(Modestra et al., 2015; Zhao et al., 2015). Furthermore, compared to AD,
the EAAD can provide a signiﬁcantly higher cumulative CH4 production
(n = 11; p = 0.06), as shown in Fig. 2(d).
In summary, the combined effects of co-digestion and the applica-
tion of an external electric ﬁeld on biogas production are complex inter-
actions. Experimental ﬁndings have indicated that a suitable PW:RH
ratio for EAAD is 3:1, which corresponds to a C/N ratio of ∼14. This
particular ratio demonstrates positive both co-digestion and electrical
enhancements. It can achieve a lower CO2 production while an
enhanced CH4 production, ultimately facilitating easier CO2 removal
during biogas puriﬁcation.

3.3. Maximum biogas production (rate) by modiﬁed Gompertz kinetics
In this study, the modiﬁed Gompertz model was used to evaluate the cu-
mulative biogas production for AD and EAAD processes. Table 2 presents
the kinetic parameters based on the modiﬁed Gompertz model. The R2
values of the modiﬁed Gompertz model varied from 0.965 to 0.997, indi-
cating that the performance of biogas production can be well described.
Similar results were found in the literatures that the AD performance can
be well described by the modiﬁed Gompertz model (Bah et al., 2014).
Fig. 2(e) shows the experimental and predictive data of CH4 production
with the PW/RH ratio of 3:1. It was demonstrated that the modiﬁed
Gompertz model was suitable for the prediction of AD and EAAD in this
study. The ﬁtting results for other experiments using the modiﬁed
Gompertz model were included in Figs. S4 and S5 (see the Supplementary
Information).
The results also indicated that the biogas production rate in EAAD was
greater than that in AD. The μm values for EAAD (1.87–5.23 mL/g-VS/d)
were signiﬁcantly higher than that for AD (1.19–3.74 mL/g-VS/d). EAAD
possesses different electron transfer mechanisms from AD, and EAAD can
accelerate the biogas production through electroactive microorganism re-
actions (Moscoviz et al., 2016b). For the same PW/RH ratio, the EAAD sys-
tem generally exhibited a shorter lag-phase time (λ) especially in the case of
the PW/RH ratios of 3:1 and 3:2. The lag-phase time for EAAD was found to
be in the range of 7.6–13.0 days. Moreover, the relative percent differences
between experimental and predictive maximum biogas production (B0) by
the modiﬁed Gompertz model were 0.3–12.0 %. Particularly, the modiﬁed
Gompertz model can well describe the cumulative CH4 production within
an error of ±20 %, as shown in Fig. 2(f). The observations align with ﬁnd-
ings in the literature, indicating that the modiﬁed Gompertz model is suit-
able for predicting AD behavior using various feedstocks, such as dairy
manure (Ke et al., 2022).
3.4. Microbial community analyses
The next generation sequencing (known as massive parallel sequencing)
was used to reveal the microbial communities and the potential reaction
mechanisms. Fig. S6(a) shows the community richness of bacteria and ar-
chaeal in AD and EAAD systems. The results indicated that the bacteria
were the dominant kingdom for both AD and EAAD systems. The ratio
of archaea to bacteria was <3 %, and the maximum number of genera
was about 93,000. The top taxonomic groups at the family level (Fig. 3
(a))
include
Clostridiaceae
1,
Erysipelotrichaceae,
Rikenellaceae,
Dysgonomonadaceae,
Peptostreptococcaceae,
and
Bacteroidetes
vadinHA17. These bacteria are commonly associated with the degradation
of carbohydrate into smaller molecules such as acids and hydrogen. For in-
stance, Clostridiaceae comprises anaerobic bacteria that can produce the
hydrogenase enzyme, assisting in hydrogen production. Similarly,
Erysipelotrichaceae can oxidize carbohydrates such as glucose to generate
organic acids and/or hydrogen.
For the bacteria domain, Fig. S6(b) illustrates the distribution of de-
tected sequences at the phylum level. The top three abundant population
phyla were found to be Firmicutes (33–59 %), Bacteroidetes (21–37 %),
and Proteobacteria (5–15 %). The Firmicutes group, which was commonly
detected in AD or activated sludge system (Ros et al., 2017), can degrade
volatile fatty acids. For the EAAD system, the composition of Firmicutes in-
creased with the increase of external applied voltage. Similar, the
Bacteroidetes group, which can produce lytic enzymes to hydrolyze poly-
saccharides and carbonates (Li et al., 2020), was identiﬁed in this study.
Fig. S6(c) (see the Supplementary Information) also shows the top 15 gen-
era of the bacterial in different samples. The dominant bacteria genus in-
cluded Clostridium, Turicibacter, and Terrisporobacter. They were reported
as the common active members in AD system (Guo et al., 2020), and
were beneﬁcial for interspecies hydrogen transfer. In this study, it was
also observed that both Clostridium and Turicibacter signiﬁcantly decreased
as the C/N ratio of digestate increased.
For the archaeal domain, only two phyla, i.e., Euryarchaeota and
Crenarchaeota, were detected in this study. In general, the proportion of
Euryarchaeota

The Euryarchaeota group, involved the most methanogens, could adapt to
the extreme environment (Castro-Fernandez et al., 2017). In particular,
the two most abundant genera of archaea were Methanosaeta (30–70 %)
and Methanoculleus (10–23 %), as depicted in Fig. S6(d). Methanosaeta is
the acetoclastic methanogen, and becomes dominant in systems with low
acetate concentrations and a relatively high retention time (Pavlostathis,
2011). Methanoculleus is a hydrogenotrophic methanogen, which could
produce methane from hydrogen with syntrophic acetate-oxidizing bacte-
ria (Xu et al., 2021). It was also observed that Methanosaeta signiﬁcantly de-
creased

Furthermore,
Methanosarcina was relatively abundant in both PW (∼23.6 %) and inocu-
lum (∼15.1 %) samples. However, following AD/EAAD processes, the ratio
of Methanosarcina to the total archaea signiﬁcantly reduced to 1.2–8.5 %.
Compared to Methanosaeta, Methanosarcina has faster growth rates but a
lower afﬁnity for acetate and is more sensitive to turbulence (Liu and
Whitman, 2008). Methanosarcina is capable of consuming either acetate
or methyl compounds to produce methane.
3.5. Elucidation of reaction pathways and mechanisms

on the lab-scale experiments, as depicted in Fig. 3(b). The results indicated
that the contributions of acetoclastic and hydrogenotrophic methanogens
to biomethane formation were 56.6 ± 0.6 % (n = 11, p < 0.05) and
43.4 ± 0.6 % (n = 11, p < 0.05), respectively. No signiﬁcant difference

(p > 0.05; two sample t-test). Fig. 3(c) also illustrates the alpha diversity of
AD and EAAD. Based on the Chao1 richness and Simpson dominance re-
sults, EAAD exhibited higher species richness but lower diversity compared
to AD. This ﬁnding is consistent with the results obtained from the top 20
differentially expressed amplicon sequence variants using Welch's t-test
(Fig. S7) and the heat trees (Fig. S8) between AD and EAAD. In other
words, the bacterial community is highly dependent not only on the compo-
sition of the digestate both also on the presence of an applied electric ﬁeld.
In electro-microbiology, three types of electron transfer are commonly ob-
served: (1) direct transportation, (2) extracellular electron transport
through the oxidation-reduction potential difference, and (3) electroactive
microorganism reactions, which can increase the intermediates and facili-
tate indirect reactions (Moscoviz et al., 2016a; Nevin et al., 2011).
According to the microbial community analysis in this study, both
Methanosaeta (as indicated by Eq. (5)) and Methanoculleus (as indicated by
Eq. (6)) were found to be the dominant methanogens responsible for
biomethane formation. The portions of Methanosaeta (p = 0.44 > 0.05;
two sample t-test) and Methanoculleus (p = 0.33 > 0.05; two sample t-
test) in the digestate samples of AD and EAAD were similar. This implies
that the archaeal community was primarily inﬂuenced by the digestate
composition rather than the presence of an applied electric ﬁeld.

To further understand the methanogenesis pathways, a Spearman corre-
lation analysis was performed between the top 30 genera (see Fig. 3(d)). It
was observed that Methanosaeta exhibited a negatively correlation with
Sphaerochaeta, indicating a possible competitive relationship between
them. It is worth noting that Sphaerochaeta (belonging to the family
Sphaerochaetaceae) is involved in lignocellulose and hemicellulose hydro-
lysis, as well as acidogenesis and hydrogen-producing acetogenesis stages
(Abt et al., 2012). Additionally, Methanosaeta showed positive correlations
with Sulfurospirillum (family Campylobacteraceae), Alkaliphilus (family
Clostridiaceae), W5053 (Family XI, within the phylum Firmicutes), and
an uncultured prokaryote. This suggests a cooperative interaction between
Methanosaeta and these four genera.

3.6. Environmental beneﬁts, carbon footprints and preliminary economic
analysis
In this study, LCA was applied to determine the life-cycle environmental
beneﬁts and carbon footprints for AD and EAAD. For the data of lab-scale
experiments, only the uses of electricity, heat, and chemicals for process op-
erations were considered, and the functional unit was designated to be one
gram of biogas production. As shown in Fig. 4(a), the developed EAAD ex-
hibited a relatively lower environmental impacts on ecosystem quality,
human health, and resource depletion. Compared to AD, the endpoint envi-
ronmental impacts of biogas production by EAAD can be generally reduced
by 9.5 %. The effect of applied external voltage on environmental impacts
was also evaluated, as shown in Fig. 4(b). The results indicated that the
lab-scale experiments with an external applied voltage of 0.6 V exhibited
the lowest environmental impacts for all endpoint categories.
To understand the feasibility of commercial operations, the system
boundary of assessment was further extended by considering feedstock
transportation, biogas storage, and power generation using CHP. In this
study, three scenarios were established, and Fig. 4(c) shows the system
boundary of scenarios A (AD), B (EAAD), and C (integrated AD-EAAD).
As shown in Fig. 4(d), the results indicated that, compared to AD, the
EAAD operations could effectively avoid life-cycle impacts on ecosystem
quality (by 17.1 %), human health (by 16.9 %), and resource depletion
(by 6.2 %). The integrated AD-EAAD operations could further reduce the
environmental impacts on human health (by 21.3 %) and resource deple-
tion (by 19.5 %), compared to AD. Moreover, the life-cycle carbon foot-
prints of three scenarios were estimated, as shown in Fig. 4(e). The results
indicated that the carbon footprints for AD, EAAD and AD-EAAD were
33.1, 27.3, and 25.9 t-CO2/t-PW, respectively. In other words, the carbon
intensity for AD of PW can be reduced by 17.6–21.7 % through retroﬁtting
EAAD units in existing processes. It is important to highlight that the inte-
grated AD-EAAD system in scenario C offers the advantage of eliminating
the need for fossil-based electricity in the EAAD process. As a result, the in-
tegrated system contributes to lower life-cycle CO2 emissions.
Table S3 presents the preliminary economic analysis of AD and EAAD
using PW to produce one m3 of biogas. The analysis revealed that the
beneﬁt-cost ratio for AD was 1.12, while for EAAD it was 1.33. These ﬁnd-
ings conﬁrm the feasibility of implementing AD for treating PW while si-
multaneously producing bioenergy. One advantage of EAAD over AD is
its shorter lag-phase duration, which refers to the hydraulic retention
time needed to produce the same amount of biogas. This shorter duration
allows for a greater treatment capacity of PW and higher biogas production.
Consequently, EAAD offers increased beneﬁts in terms of average PW treat-
ment capacity and bioenergy production. In summary, the results indicate
that both AD and EAAD processes are economically viable for treating
PW and generating bioenergy. However, EAAD demonstrates advantages
over AD in terms of shorter lag-phase duration, which translates to higher
treatment capacity for PW and increased biogas production, resulting in
greater overall beneﬁts from PW treatment and bioenergy production.
4. Conclusions
In this study, a comprehensive evaluation of various aspects related to
biogas production enhancement, including kinetic models, microbial com-
munity analyses, life-cycle carbon footprints, and preliminary economic
analysis, was conducted. The study assessed the effect of different co-
digestion ratios and applied voltages on biogas production. The results indi-
cated that the introduction of an external electric ﬁeld, i.e., EAAD, led to a
positive improvement in biogas production. Both AD and EAAD demon-
strated a synergistic co-digestion effect only at a PW:RH ratio of 3:1, with
co-digestion enhancement values of 64.2 % for AD and 45.5 % for EAAD.
Furthermore, a kinetic model was developed to determine key digestion pa-
rameters, revealing that the biogas production rate in EAAD exceeded that
of AD. The μm values, representing the speciﬁc biogas production rate, were
signiﬁcantly higher for EAAD (ranging from 1.87 to 5.23 mL/g-VS/d) com-
pared to AD (ranging from 1.19 to 3.74 mL/g-VS/d). Microbial community
analyses were also conducted to shed light on the pathways and mecha-
nisms contributing to enhanced biogas production. The bacterial
Fig. 4. (a) System boundary of scenarios A (conventional AD, business-as-usual), B (EAAD), and C (Integrated AD and EAAD) for life-cycle assessment. Life-cycle endpoint
impacts for (b) lab-scale AD or EAAD processes, and (c) EAAD processes at different voltages. The functional unit is “one gram of biogas production” using the lab-scale
digestors in this study. (d) Life-cycle endpoint impacts and (e) carbon footprints for three scenarios at a large-scale operation, and the functional unit is “one tonne of
piggery wastewater treatment”.
H.-C. Lin et al.
Science of the Total Environment 891 (2023) 164612
8

community composition was found to depend on both the presence of the
applied electric ﬁeld and the digestate composition, while the archaeal
community composition was solely related to the digestate composition.
The contributions of acetoclastic and hydrogenotrophic methanogens to
biomethane formation were determined as 56.6 % ± 0.6 % and 43.4 % ±
0.6 %, respectively (p < 0.05). Lastly, the environmental beneﬁts and carbon
footprints of the EAAD process were assessed using life-cycle assessment. The
integrated AD-EAAD scenario showed reductions in environmental impacts
on human health (by 21.3 %) and resource depletion (by 19.5 %) compared
to AD alone. The retroﬁtting of EAAD units in existing AD processes resulted
in a carbon intensity reduction of 17.6 % to 21.7 % for PW treatment. Eco-
nomic analysis results indicated the feasibility of employing AD for treating
primary waste while simultaneously producing bioenergy, with beneﬁt-cost
ratios of 1.12 for AD and 1.33 for EAAD. This study should be recognized
as a pioneering work in developing an electricity-assisted anaerobic co-
digestion (EAAD) process for swine wastewater. It provides valuable insights
into upgrading the performance of existing AD plants by introducing an exter-
nal electric ﬁeld, thereby achieving higher and cost-effective biogas produc-
tion with a lower life-cycle carbon footprint.

Available online 13 August 2021

From wastewater treatment to water resource recovery: Environmental and 
economic impacts of full-scale implementation 
Maria Farag`o a,*, Anders Damgaard b, Jeanette Agertved Madsen c, Jacob Kragh Andersen c, 
Dines Thornberg d, Mikkel Holmen Andersen e, Martin Rygaard a 
a Department of Environmental Engineering, Water Technology and Processes, Technical University of Denmark, Bygningstorvet, Building 115, Lyngby 2800, Denmark 
b Department of Environmental Engineering, Circularity and Environmental Impact, Technical University of Denmark, Bygningstorvet, Building 115, Lyngby 2800, 
Denmark 
c EnviDan A/S, Fuglebækvej 1A, Kastrup 2770, Denmark 
d BIOFOS, Refshalevej 250, København K 1432, Denmark 
e Unisense, Tueager 1, Aarhus N 8200, Denmark   
A R T I C L E  I N F O   
Keywords: 
Pollution control 
Nutrients recovery 
Biomethane 
EASETECH 
Added value 
Abatement costs 
A B S T R A C T   
To reduce greenhouse gas emissions and promote resource recovery, many wastewater treatment operators are 
retrofitting existing plants to implement new technologies for energy, nutrient and carbon recovery. 
In literature, there is a lack of studies that can unfold the potential environmental and economic impacts of the 
transition that wastewater utilities are undertaking to transform their treatment plants to water resource re­
covery facilities (WRRFs). When existing, literature studies are mostly based on simulations rather than real plant 
data and pilot-scale results. This study combines life cycle assessment and economic evaluations to quantify the 
environmental and economic impacts of retrofitting an existing wastewater treatment plant (WWTP), which 
already implements energy recovery, into a full-scale WRRF with a series of novel technologies, the majority of 
which are already implemented full-scale or tested through pilot-scales. We evaluate five technology alternatives 
against the current performance of the WWTP: real-time N2O control, biological biogas upgrading coupled with 
power-to-hydrogen, phosphorus recovery, pre-filtration carbon harvest and enhanced nitrogen removal. Our 
results show that real-time N2O control, biological biogas upgrading and pre-filtration lead to a decrease in 
climate change and fossil resource depletion impacts. The implementation of the real-time measurement and 
control of N2O achieved the highest reduction in direct CO2-eq emissions (-35%), with no significant impacts in 
other environmental categories. Biological biogas upgrading contributed to counterbalancing direct and indirect 
climate change impacts by substituting natural gas consumption and production. Pre-filtration increased climate 
change reduction by 13%, while it increased impacts in other categories. Enhanced sidestream nitrogen removal 
increased climate change impacts by 12%, but decreased marine eutrophication impacts by 14%. The reserve 
base resource depletion impacts, however, were the highest in the plant configurations implementing biological 
biogas upgrading coupled with power-to-hydrogen. Environmental improvements generated economic costs for 
all alternatives except for real-time N2O control. The results expose possible environmental and economic trade- 
offs and hotspots of the journey that large wastewater treatment plants will undertake in transitioning into 
resource recovery facilities in the coming years.   
1. Introduction 
Only a decade ago, wastewater was considered a serious environ­
mental and health problem, while it is now increasingly valued as a 
resource in many parts of the world (Larsen, 2015; Mo and Zhang, 2013; 
Van Der Hoek et al., 2016). Wastewater is rich in nutrients, and when 
discharged untreated into receiving water bodies, severe eutrophication 
problems can occur. Additionally, efficient biological treatment pro­
cesses demand energy, and incomplete nitrification/denitrification can 
lead to unwanted emissions of the potent greenhouse gas nitrous oxide 
(N2O), 265 times more powerful than CO2 (Myhre et al., 2013). 
The role of wastewater treatment plants (WWTPs) currently 
* Corresponding author. 
E-mail addresses: mfar@env.dtu.dk (M. Farag`o), mryg@env.dtu.dk (M. Rygaard).  
Contents lists available at ScienceDirect 
Water Research 
journal homepage: www.elsevier.com/locate/watres 
https://doi.org/10.1016/j.watres.2021.117554 
Received 5 May 2021; Received in revised form 23 July 2021; Accepted 9 August 2021

Water Research 204 (2021) 117554
2
transforms from removal of nitrogen (N), phosphorus (P) and organic 
materials, into integrated resource recovery and pollution control. The 
majority of the removed phosphorus and part of the removed nitrogen 
ends up in residual wastewater sludge, which is therefore important to 
valorise when moving towards wastewater treatment integrated with 
resource recovery. There are many ways of valorising the sludge, e.g. 
through anaerobic digestion to produce biogas, and subsequent use of 
the degassed sludge as fertiliser on soil (Morero et al., 2017; Pfluger 
et al., 2019). However, high levels of heavy metals in sludge may limit 
its applicability as a fertiliser (Jensen and Jepsen, 2005). Consequently, 
four major Danish WWTPs incinerate degassed sludge and dispose of its 
ashes in landfills. Worldwide, technologies are now being developed for 
energy recovery (biogas, electricity, heat) and for recovering phos­
phorus (e.g. Hukari et al., 2016), heavy metals and sand from sludge 
ashes, to recycle P in its mineral form and reduce the consumption of 
conventional feed phosphate, chemicals, and silica sand, respectively 
(EasyMining, 2020). 
Due to these benefits, the circular economy is high on the agenda of 
wastewater utilities, and it is believed to be the right strategy for 
reaching resource-, energy- and CO2-neutrality (e.g. Danish EPA, 2020; 
SMART-Plant, 2021). Hence, major Danish wastewater utilities aim to 
retrofit their WWTPs into water resource recovery facilities (WRRFs), 
such as the VARGA project in Copenhagen (BIOFOS, 2021). 
Increasing circularity and reducing environmental impacts require 
investments and may decrease economic value for affected stakeholders. 
Resource recovery technologies may also produce environmental 
burden shifts, i.e. decreasing GHG emissions, while increasing the effects 
of other environmental impact categories. Hence, holistic assessments 
are needed. 
Researchers currently use life cycle assessment (LCA) and life cycle 
costing (LCC) to quantify the environmental and economic impacts of 
conventional wastewater treatment (e.g. Mcnamara et al., 2016; Niero 
et al., 2014), and a best practice guide has been published (Corominas 
et al., 2020). Lorenzo-Toja et al. (2016) used LCA and LCC to provide an 
eco-efficiency assessment (ISO 14045, 2012) of 22 conventional Spanish 
WWTPs. 
Our review of recent existing studies on the subject (Table 1) illus­
trates that authors with few exceptions (Tian et al., 2020) have 
approached the problem either from an environmental (Delre et al., 
2019; Fang et al., 2016; Guven et al., 2018; Hao et al., 2019; Kehrein 
et al., 2020; Pradel and Aissani, 2019) or an economic viewpoint 
(Boiocchi et al., 2017; Riley et al., 2020). To our knowledge, the ma­
jority of economic and environmental assessments of resource recovery 
facilities have been based on literature studies (e.g. Hao et al., 2019) or 
pilot- and lab-scale experiments (Guven et al., 2018; Pradel and Aissani, 
2019). 
Tian et al. (2020) carried out a combined economic and environ­
mental assessment of a full-scale WRRF including thermal energy re­
covery from effluent, the pyrolysis of sludge and combined heat and 
power generation. The study focused on a state-of-the-art North Amer­
ican WWTP and covered the full life cycle, albeit without considering 
methane losses and laughing gas emissions from the plant. The study 
built 
upon 
proposed 
design 
scenarios 
and 
not 
the 
actual 
implementation. 
Due to the numerous options for retrofitting WWTPs, we hypothesise 
that the true impacts of resource recovery facilities need to be uncovered 
from several real-life and full-scale retrofitting cases. Therefore, as a 
novelty, we provide a combined environmental and economic assess­
ment of technological advancements on an existing state-of-the-art, 
energy-efficient WWTP. By 2025, the plant will be a full-scale energy 
and resource recovery facility that valorises waste by-products and re­
duces GHG emissions. Retrofitting covers five alternatives to recover 
nutrients and energy, and reduce emissions, compared to a business-as- 
usual baseline WWTP in 2025. The novelty of our study relies therefore 
not only on the combined environmental and economic assessment of 
integrated resource recovery and pollution control measures but also on 
the reduced uncertainty in the data input that are mostly based on real 
plant data and pilot-scale results. Our results provide insights into the 
potential hotspots and trade-offs that come as a consequence of a new 
benchmark for efficient wastewater management. 
The study aims to answer four important research questions: 
• Is the transition from currently operating WWTPs to WRRFs envi­
ronmentally and economically sustainable?  
• Who are the major affected stakeholders, and how do retrofitted 
WWTPs change economic value across stakeholders? 
• Where are the main hotspots and uncertainties for an environmen­
tally and economically sustainable WRRF?  
• Are there trade-offs between environmental and economic impacts 
or between different environmental categories? 
2. Materials and methods 
We combined an environmental impact assessment with an eco­
nomic value evaluation, using a standardised eco-efficiency framework 
(ISO 14045:2012). The environmental impact assessment was per­
formed through LCA, while the system value was expressed in total 
value-added (TVA) supplemented by estimated abatement costs of CO2- 
eq emissions and phosphorus recovery costs. 
2.1. Goal, scope and functional unit 
The main goal of the study was to assess the environmental and 
economic performances of technological advancements in WRRF, 
compared to a WWTP baseline in 2025. The “treatment of 1 m3 of 
wastewater up to legally defined discharge limits of TN, TP, COD, BOD 
for a period of 50 years” constitutes the functional unit (FU), which is 
relevant for comparing treatment alternatives with the same wastewater 
inlet composition. Wastewater composition of Avedøre WWTP is pre­
dominantly municipal (industrial wastewater < 10%). Further details 
about wastewater characteristics and discharge limits are reported in SI 
A. 
2.2. Case study, alternative definitions and system boundaries 
Our starting point is the ongoing conversion of Avedøre WWTP to a 
WRRF by 2025 (VARGA, 2017) (Fig. 1). The treatment plant services 
Copenhagen, has a capacity of 400,000 person equivalent (PE) and 
treats approx. 26 million m3 wastewater annually, or around 271,000 
PE, assuming a BOD PE of 0.06 kg/PE per day (Table A1. SI). The plant 
discharges into the Bay of Køge. 
2.2.1. Alternatives 
We define five levels of technological advances to retrofit the base­
line WWTP (Table 2). Modelling of the baseline WWTP (RF Baseline) in 
2025 starts with the plant’s 2016 performance, assuming that biogas 
production by 2025 will be upgraded for city gas distribution. 
RF Baseline includes mechanical treatment and activated sludge for 
nitrogen removal, followed by phosphorus chemical removal. The 
sludge is anaerobically digested to produce biogas, cleaned with amine 
scrubbing, while the dewatered sludge is incinerated and then 
landfilled. 
Our retrofitting alternatives (Table 2) make incremental improve­
ments in resource and emission removal efficiency for the reference 
WWTP (RF Baseline). RF N2O augments RF Baseline with real-time 
monitoring and control of N2O production and emissions from biolog­
ical processes. RF N2OþCH4 includes biological upgrading (bio- 
methanation) of the biogas for increased methane production, in com­
bination 
with 
a 
power-to-hydrogen 
(P2H) 
technology. 
RF 
N2OþCH4þP adds sludge ash treatment to recover phosphorus, 
including co-production of ferric chloride, aluminium hydroxide and 
sand. RF N2OþCH4þPþC substitutes the existing primary clarifier

2.2.2. System boundaries 
Foreground processes included wastewater influent, treatment, 
sludge management, receiving waterbody discharge and advancement 
options (Fig. 1). Background processes included directly linked up­
stream external processes, namely electricity and heat, raw material 
requirement and chemicals, and included the substitution of down­
stream background process impacts such as natural gas, heat, chemicals, 
silica sand, and feed phosphate and artificial fertiliser, that obtain inputs 
from the foreground system (Hauschild et al., 2018) (Fig. 1). 
2.3. Data inventory: TVA + LCA 
Life cycle inventory and cost data were based on results from the 
pilot-scale, full-scale implementation, water utility reports, augmented 
with literature values and expert estimates from project partners, where 
data were not available. Wastewater amounts and loads were assumed 
to increase by 8.4% from 2016 to 2025. Value-added taxes (VAT) were 
excluded from the analysis, while landfill and pollution taxes were 
included. All costs are reported as 2019 prices. One-time costs were 
amortised using a 4% interest rate. Table 3 lists key inventory data for 
the LCA. 
2.3.1. Background energy production in 2025 
Average electricity and heat mixes in 2025 were modelled based on 
the Danish energy outlook report – frozen policy (Danish Energy Agency, 
2018) that forecasts sources of energy production and consumption in 
Denmark up to 2030. Given the uncertainty behind the energy sector’s 
actual development, we used a scenario analysis for the energy mix to 
explore how a changed pathway for future power generation could 
affect the results. Further details regarding the modelled electricity and 
heat mixes can be found in SI B. 
2.4. Environmental impact assessment: LCA 
LCA modelling followed an attributional framework. Since all the 
scenarios are multifunctional systems, we performed system expansion 
by crediting recovered resources, such as biogas and heat production 
and export, and the substitution of conventional feed phosphate, sand 
and chemicals production. Avoided impacts in conventional background 
processes were credited as a mix of average market data. This choice is 
justified for “micro-level decision support” with no relevant conse­
quences on the installed capacity of the background system (e.g. energy 
market on a national scale) (EC-JRC, 2010; Laurent et al., 2014). Most 
background processes were downloaded from ecoinvent database 
version 3.5 along with the “allocation at the point of substitution” sys­
tem model approach (Wernet et al., 2016) that fits our choice of 
modelling the alternatives with an attributional framework. A complete 
database with the selected ecoinvent processes and inventory data for 
this system model is reported in the SI B1-B6. 
Modelling of the impact assessment was performed with EASETECH 
v.3.3.7. (Clavreul et al., 2014) that is an LCA software that allows for 
tracking water/mass balances and substance flows across the different 
treatment steps. The ILCD method provides 15 impact categories 
(EC-JRC, 2010). We chose to limit the reporting to six particularly 
relevant categories, namely climate change (CC), terrestrial eutrophi­
cation (TE), freshwater eutrophication (FE), marine eutrophication 
(ME), depletion of abiotic resources-fossil (DAR-F) and -reserve base 
(DAR-RB). A primary screening for selecting categories was carried out

categories.

method, but it does propose defining value creation through economic 
indicators. Similar to Angelis-Dimakis et al. (2016), we evaluated eco­
nomic impacts as the total value added (TVA):

RF BASELINE: A BIO DENIPHO wastewater process, combining 
conventional activated sludge for nitrogen and chemical phosphate 
removal. Sludge is anaerobically digested (AD) to produce biogas (60% 
CH4 biomethane, 40% CO2). Before being emitted to the gas grid, the 
biogas is upgraded through ammine scrubbing, to remove CO2 and 
provide purified biomethane. The degassed sludge is then dewatered 
and subsequently incinerated in an onsite incineration plant. Sludge 
ashes are finally deposited in the onsite internal landfill.

2.5.2. Abatement costs for CO2-eq emissions and P-recovery costs 
Abatement costs for carbon dioxide emissions and recovered phos­
phorus were calculated by considering the total costs, revenues and 
savings induced by the alternative, relative to avoided CO2-eq emissions 
and P-recovery. More details are reported in SI D2. 
2.6. Data quality framework 
Data quality revision (DQR) was performed with a pedigree matrix, 
using five data quality categories (Weidema and Wesnæs, 1996). An 
average data quality rating score (ADQ) was calculated and then re­
ported against sensitivity ratios for selected parameters to identify the 
most uncertain and sensitive parameters for a Monte Carlo analysis (SI 
F). 
2.7. Perturbation, uncertainty and scenario analysis 
Perturbation and uncertainty analyses were carried out for both 
environmental and economic evaluation. A perturbation analysis was 
performed by increasing by 10% the parameters contributing more than 
5% to environmental and economic impacts. Normalised sensitivity 
ratios were calculated as:

An uncertainty analysis was performed on the most sensitive and un­
certain parameters (ADQ>4) by assigning an uncertainty range and 
probability distribution to the parameters (SI F & G). The parameters’ 
contribution to overall uncertainty was estimated according to Bisinella 
et al. (2016) and built into EASETECH. 
To place our results in the context of varying external factors, we 
provided a scenario analysis for alternate situations: (1) wastewater 
effluent recipient changed from marine to freshwater; (2) P2H elec­
tricity mix assumed to be 20% fossil power generation instead of excess 
wind power alone; (3) wind power-based electricity for all requirements; 
(4) sand substitutes cement; (5) the consumption of HCl for sludge ash 
dissolution for P-recovery is considered LCA burden-free, since the P- 
recovery company can currently benefit from HCl produced as a by- 
product at the Kemira plant in Sweden and (6) a scenario analysis of 
the TVA results was performed by internalising CO2-eq emissions, using 
the current CO2 European emission allowance. 
3. Results and discussion 
The results present the environmental and economic impacts of the 
six alternative plant configurations. The alternatives build sequentially 
upon each other, adding another technology for each step from RF 
Baseline until RF N2O+CH4+P+C+AX.

3.1. Mass flow analysis 
Throughout the alternatives, the resource recovery facility treated 
approx. 27 Million m3 of wastewater with a load of 1300 ton TN/year, 
193 ton TP/year and a 16,484 ton COD/year. In each case, around 77% 
of TN was converted into N2-N (Table 4). In the RF Baseline, 2.6% of the 
TN was converted into N2O-N, the majority of which was produced in 
biological processes during nitrification/denitrification. This factor

(2.6% N2O-N/TNinlet) is approx. two times higher than the recom­
mended IPCC factor of 1.6% (IPCC, 2019). Real-time N2O control 
reduced markedly the conversion to 1.6% in the WRRF alternatives and 
this factor is more in line with IPCC (2019) recommended factor. Side­
stream anammox – implemented to reduce the nitrogen load from reject 
water – might also have a severe effect on N2O emissions (RF 
N2O+CH4+P+C+AX), i.e. 3% of the TN removed from reject water by 
anammox ended up as N2O-N gas, with experimental studies showing 
variations in the N2O-N emission factor between 2 and 8% of TN 
removed by anammox (Andersen et al., 2016; Uri Carre˜no, 2016). We 
assumed that 3% of the TN removed from anammox was converted into 
N2O-N in the alternative RF N2O+CH4+P+C+AX, so total N2O-N 
emissions increased to 1.7% of TN; 13% of TN sedimented in sludge, and 
8% ended up in the effluent. 
In total, 93% of TP ended in the sludge ashes and was either land­
filled in the alternatives RF Baseline - RF N2O+CH4 or recovered to 
produce high-value products such as feed phosphate (monocalcium 
phosphate (MCP)) in the alternatives with P recovery. P-recovery 
recycled 95% of TP from the sludge ashes, corresponding to approx. 171 
tons per year. 
In the four alternatives, namely RF Baseline until RF N2O+CH4+P, 
33% of the COD was converted in the biological activated sludge system, 
while 34% was anaerobically digested and converted into energy, i.e. 
biogas (60% CH4, 40% CO2). Implementing primary filtration reduced 
COD availability in the activated sludge system but increased the COD 
available for anaerobic digestion by approx. 8% (RF N2O+CH4+P+C - 
RF N2O+CH4+P+C+AX). The increase of COD in AD increased 
methane production compared to RF Baseline. 
3.2. Environmental impacts 
Overall, the five alternatives reduced climate change impacts 
compared to the baseline (Fig. 3). The four alternatives RF N2O+CH4 - 
RF N2O+CH4+P+C+AX also reduced their impacts compared to both 
RF Baseline and RF N2O in the categories terrestrial eutrophication (TE) 
and fossil depletion (DAR-F) impacts, while freshwater eutrophication 
(FE) impacts were equally the highest for RF Baseline, RF N2O and RF 
N2O+CH4. Marine eutrophication (ME) did not significantly vary across 
alternatives, except for the last alternative implementing sidestream 
anammox. Only in the category reserve base resource depletion (DAR- 
RB), RF baseline showed lower impacts compared to RF N2O+CH4. In 
2025, the strategy to upgrade all biogas, rather than having CHP, 
seemed to pay off to obtain negative fossil depletion impacts already in 
the baseline plant performance (-0.23 MJ m−3), although a higher 
reduction is produced by the other alternatives (Fig. 3). 
3.2.1. Climate change 
Despite only 1.6–2.6% of TN (Table 4) emitted as N2O, the climate 
change impacts were very large. They decreased by between -35% and 
-59% compared to the baseline following the implementation of new 
technologies. Implementing real-time N2O measurements and control as 
a first step decreased impacts by almost 40% compared to the baseline 
treatment plant performance, without a significant increase of impacts 
in the other environmental categories (<0.4%) (Fig. 3). For the uncer­
tainty assessment, we used a 30–60% reduction range based on previous 
full-scale results (Chen et al., 2019). We consider it a conservative es­
timate of long-term reduction, because the future optimisation of 
real-time controls (e.g. increasing suspended solids in the aeration 
tanks) may decrease N2O emissions even further. 
Implementing bio-methanation for biogas upgrading (RF N2O+CH4 
– RF N2O+CH4+P+C+AX) had a beneficial effect on both reducing 
climate change (up to -0.13 kg CO2-eq m−3) and avoiding fossil resource 
depletion up to -2.4 MJ m−3 (avoidance of natural gas extraction and 
combustion). However, it increased reserve base resource depletion 
impacts (+93% increase, Fig. 3). 
Implementing pre-filtration induced an additional 13% reduction in 
CO2 emissions compared to the previous alternative. However, installing 
pre-filters may increase the production and emission of N2O, as the fil­
ters will harvest more COD that is needed for denitrification. Adding 
artificial carbon sources could counterbalance this negative effect. The 
potential increase in N2O, and the use of artificial carbon sources such as 
methanol, has not yet been established, so they are not included herein. 
The alternatives implementing P-recovery and anammox (RF 
N2O+CH4+P and RF N2O+CH4+P+C+AX) increased climate change 
impacts by 4% and 12% compared to the previous alternative. More 
efficient nitrification/denitrification with anammox increased N2O 
emissions and used additional electricity, as also reported by Fenu et al. 
(2019). As studied by Andersen et al. (2016) and Uri Carre˜no (2016) in 
another Danish WWTP, up to 8% of the TN removed by anammox can be 
emitted as N2O. Herein, we assumed that 3% of TN removed from 
anammox would end up as N2O, hence the increase in climate change 
impacts in this last alternative. 
3.2.2. Terrestrial eutrophication 
TE impacts primarily stemmed from the onsite incineration of sludge 
ashes, while a negative contribution was made by avoided nitrogen 
oxides (NOx) during natural gas combustion, avoided heat production 
and the substitution of conventional monocalcium phosphate (MCP) 
production. TE impacts decreased along with the increased imple­
mentation of pollution control and resource recovery technology (-11% 
to - 6%), except for RF N2O, with the same impacts as RF Baseline. P- 
recovery (RF N2O+CH4+P - RF N2O+CH4+P+C+AX) induced a net 
increase of 2% of TE impacts, as impacts from using chemicals and 
electricity exceeded impacts avoided from substituting conventional 
MCP production; however, overall TE impacts were still lower than RF 
Baseline. 
3.2.3. Freshwater eutrophication 
FE impacts were primarily induced by chemicals and electricity 
consumption in the baseline treatment plant and increased following the 
implementation of novel technologies by up to 22%. P-recovery imple­
mentation increased FE impacts by 7% compared to RF N2O+CH4, as 
impacts avoided from the beneficiation of phosphate rocks could not 
counterbalance the impacts from consumption (chemicals and elec­
tricity) needed for the sludge ash treatment to recover phosphorus. On 
the positive side, in alternatives with P-recovery, only 5% of the total P 
sludge ashes ended up being landfilled against 95% in alternatives 
without P-recovery, i.e. RF Baseline, RF N2O, RF N2O+CH4 (Table 4). 
The last two alternatives, specifically RF N2O+CH4+P+C, increased FE 
impacts by an additional 11%, due to the additional consumption of iron 
chloride and polymer for pre-filtration. 
3.2.4. Marine eutrophication 
Across alternatives, more than 95% of the ME impacts were caused 
by discharging nitrogen in the effluent. No marked changes of ME im­
pacts were seen when implementing novel technologies (up to -2%), 
except for the alternative implementing anammox (-16% compared to 
baseline). The discharged concentration of nitrogen across alternatives 
was 51% lower than the permitted discharge limit of 8 mg/l TN. How­
ever, the anammox plant decreased total nitrogen in the effluent by an 
additional 15%. 
3.2.5. Fossil resource depletion 
Fossil resource depletion impacts decreased following the imple­
mentation of new technologies. Increasing the production and export of 
bio- and neo-methane avoided extracting approx. 3.2 Million Nm3 nat­
ural gas yearly in the alternatives RF N2O+CH4-RF N2O+CH4+P. Pre- 
filtration (RF N2O+CH4+P+C- RF N2O+CH4+P+C+AX) additionally 
increased biogas production, and hence the amount of upgraded biogas 
to natural gas, by 18% with total annual biogas production of approx. 
3.7 Million of Nm3. The alternative RF N2O+CH4+P increased DAR-F 
impacts by 6%, due to chemicals required for the dissolution of sludge

ashes and the separation of P, Fe and Al. 
3.2.6. Resource depletion – reserve base 
As opposed to fossil impacts, DAR-RB impacts generated a burden to 
the environment in all alternatives except for RF N2O+CH4+P. The 
alternative RF N2O+CH4 increased impacts by 93% compared to RF 
Baseline and RF N2O. Electrolysers for hydrogen production (RF 
N2O+CH4 - RF N2O+CH4+P+C+AX) consumed two times more elec­
tricity than the entire baseline plant operation (Table 3). P2H technol­
ogy used excess wind power for electrolysis that affected the DAR-RB 
category, due to the production of wind turbines requiring minerals 
from the ground, such as Indium. The electricity mix is therefore of 
primary importance when designing a strategy for reducing CO2-eq 
emissions. 
In the alternative RF N2O+CH4+P, phosphorus is recovered in its 
mineral form, and its DAR-RB impacts reduced by -118% compared to 
the baseline – thanks to substituting conventional MCP, aluminium 
hydroxide, iron chloride and sand production that counterbalance 
additional impacts from additional chemical and heat consumption. Pre- 
filtration (RF N2O+CH4+P+C) induced an additional 273% increase in 
impacts compared to the previous alternative; however, net impacts 
were still 68% lower compared to the baseline. The increase was due to 
additional equipment and the use of iron chloride and polymers (poly­
mer for the dewatering unit and iron chloride used as a flocculant). DAR- 
RB impacts did not significantly increase, due to the implementation of 
anammox (1% increase). 
3.3. Scenario analysis: LCA 
The environmental performance for implementing novel technolo­
gies changed in different scenarios (SI H). For example, FE impacts 
increased if effluent was released into a freshwater body (scenario 1), in 
which case the discharged phosphorus had the greatest impact. This 
means that wastewater operators should focus on reducing the effluent 
discharge concentration of P rather than optimising resource recovery. 
With a change in water recipient, ME impacts did not decrease, since the 
characterisation factor (CF) of nitrogen discharging into surface water is 
“1” in both the ME and FE impact categories. The LCIA method ReciPe 
2016 updated CF of TN in surface water from 1 to 0.2968 in the ME 
category, which reduced ME impacts by up to 67% across alternatives 
(Fig. H2, SI H). 
Electricity mix was fundamental in determining whether novel 
technologies performed better than the baseline. P2H for biogas 
upgrading running with a share of 21% fossil-based electricity (scenario 
2) increased CC, TE and fossil resource depletion impacts, equalising or 
worsening impacts compared to RF Baseline (Fig. H1, SI H). A 100% 
wind power-based electricity provision for all requirements (scenario 3) 
decreased climate change impacts across alternatives. 
Assuming that sludge ash treatment for P-recovery utilised hydro­
chloric acid as a waste by-product (scenario 5) reduced DAR-RB impacts 
of the last three alternatives compared to the baseline. 
3.4. Total economic value added 
With the implementation of new technologies for resource recovery 
and pollution control, the TVA decreased from 0.23 to a minimum 0.18 € 
m−3 for the pre-filtration and anammox alternatives (Fig. 4.e). RF 
Baseline and RF N2O showed the highest TVA, which reflects that by- 
product revenues did not counterbalance the increased costs of imple­
menting resource recovery. Implementing real-time N2O control (RF 
N2O) reduced the available surplus refunded to the water user by less 
than 0.2%. 
Across alternatives, water user VA contributed between 68 and 85% 
of the TVA (Fig. 4.e). The state/government was the second-highest 
contributor to the TVA (between 12 and 15%), while the P-recovery 
company contributed only 0.8% in the last three alternatives 
implementing sludge ash treatment and P-recovery. 
The concept of full cost recovery defines the VA of the wastewater 
operator to zero (Fig. 4.b). Any change in operation and investment cost 
is reflected in the actual wastewater fee paid by the water user. Reve­
nues from the sale of by-products (bio-methane, neo-methane and heat) 
and from expected wastewater fees generated a surplus (Available Sur­
plus in Fig. 4.b). Costs of implementing resource recovery technologies 
reduced the surplus by between 13 and 19%, except for RF N2O, which 
solely implemented N2O measurements and control. 
The water user domain always showed a positive VA, due to the 
expected wastewater fee (EWWF) being 0.44 € m−3 and 36 to 45% 
higher than actual wastewater fees (AWWFs) in all alternatives, 
including the baseline (Fig. a.). 
The VA of the state and P-recovery company was only affected by a 
change in the last three alternatives RF N2O+CH4+P - RF 
N2O+CH4+P+AX, due to sludge ash treatment for phosphate recovery 
and anammox affecting pollution taxes. 
3.4.1. Economic performance of novel technologies 
3.4.1.1. P2H and biological biogas upgrading. The bio-methanation plant 
coupled with P2H (RF N2O+CH4) increased the TOTEX (CAPEX +
OPEX) by 24% (+0.08 € m−3), mainly caused by the significant cost of 
increased electricity consumption. Revenues from selling bio- and neo- 
methane and heat in the alternative RF N2O+CH4 increased by 91% 
(+0.05 € m−3) from the baseline, but they did not counterbalance the 
costs of additional electricity consumption (0.06 € m−3), drinking water 
(0.0006 € m−3) and investments (0.01 € m−3). In conclusion, biogas 
upgrading increased wastewater fees and consequently reduced VA for 
the water user. 
3.4.1.2. P-recovery from sludge ashes. By implementing P-recovery from 
sludge ashes (RF N2O+CH4+P), the wastewater operator avoided 
paying landfill taxes but did pay the P-recovery company to perform the 
sludge ash treatment. The wastewater operator’s VA, and consequently 
the water user’s VA, increased by +0.5% (0.0009 € m−3), while for the 
state it decreased by 13% (-0.004 € m−3) in RF N2O+CH4+P, RF 
N2O+CH4+P+C and RF N2O+CH4+P+AX. The P-recovery company 
increased its profit by +0.002 € m−3. Overall, the TVA was 11% lower 
compared to RF Baseline in this alternative, primarily because the state 
decreased its VA by 14%, and this was not counterbalanced by the P- 
recovery company’s income. 
3.4.1.3. Pre-filtration for increased carbon harvesting. Additional pre- 
filtration decreased the TVA by 18% compared to the baseline and by 
an additional 6% compared to the bio-methanation alternative. This 
alternative (RF N2O+CH4+P+C), together with the last anammox 
alternative, generated the lowest TVA. The additional loss of TVA (-0.01 
€ m−3) was due to an increase in the TOTEX, which was not balanced by 
an increase in revenue from selling additional bio/neo-methane and 
heat (+0.018 € m−3). 
3.4.1.4. Sidestream anaerobic ammonium oxidation plant. The last and 
most complex alternative, RF N2O+CH4+P+C+AX, required additional 
investments of 0.003 € m−3, while operation costs reduced by 0.002 € 
m−3 compared to previous alternatives, due to anammox decreasing TN 
in the effluent – and hence reducing pollution taxes. 
3.4.1.5. Internalising CO2 allowance prices. Our results suggest that 
increased wastewater management environmental performance, beyond 
biogas production, is not economically feasible in the current market. 
Internalising CO2-eq emission costs, using the current European allow­
ance price of 25 €/ton CO2-eq (Markets Insider, 2019), will decrease the 
TVA by another 3-6% (Fig 4.e, red markers), but the alternative RF N2O 
will increase the TVA by only 2% above RF Baseline. The limited
