3.5. Abatement cost for CO2-eq emissions and P-recovery costs 
3.5.1. Abatement cost for CO2-eq emissions 
According to our evaluation, it cost 2 €/ton CO2-eq to reduce emis­
sions with real-time N2O control. In the alternatives with enhanced 
biomethane production and carbon harvesting, costs were 675 and 641 
€/ton avoided CO2-eq avoided (Table 5). Including savings and revenues 
from resource recovery, costs were reduced by 67% to 222 and 57% to 
273 €/tons avoided CO2-eq for the bio-methanation plant and the pre- 
filtration alternatives. Except for N2O control, abatement costs with 
income and savings are nine to 16 times higher than the current Euro­
pean allowance price for CO2-eq. 
3.5.2. P-recovery costs 
The costs of P-recovery in RF N2O+CH4+P, without and with sav­
ings and income, were estimated at 0.56 and -0.14 € kg P−1, respectively, 
i.e. between two and 12 times less than the cost of P-recovery in Egle 
et al. (2016). Our P-recovered price, reported as “€/ton MCP P2O5”, was 
estimated at 238 €/ton MCP as P2O5 (monocalcium phosphate) and 
close to the 2019 market price of diammonium phosphate P-fertiliser 
reported as 110–169 €/ton DAP as P2O5 (AMIS, 2019), and the 2005 
triple superphosphate as P2O5 price of 241 €/ton P2O5 (Wernet et al., 
2016). Since the examined P-recovery technology is under continuous 
development, costs are expected to change over time. 
3.6. Eco-efficiency and decision support matrix 
Our results showed that for the current case, environmental im­
provements come with a financial cost, and no alternatives are eco- 
innovative. However, real-time N2O control showed a similar eco­
nomic performance to RF Baseline and produced a visible reduction in 
CC impacts (Table 6). Several technologies were placed in a trade-off 
position in some impact categories, e.g. RF N2O+CH4. 
4. Conclusions and final recommendations 
Our study uncovered the environmental and economic potentials 
and drawbacks of retrofitting an existing energy-efficient wastewater 
treatment plant into a full-scale resource recovery facility. The results 
revealed that:  
• Not all novel technologies were able to reduce impacts in all LCA 
categories, and none of the retrofitting alternatives showed 
improved financial performance compared to the baseline.  
• N2O measurement and control seemed to be both environmentally 
sound and financially feasible by achieving a 40% reduction in N2O 
emissions and a 35% reduction in climate change (CC) impacts while 
decreasing economic value by only 0.2% compared to the baseline.  
• Power-to-hydrogen 
and 
bio-methanation 
could 
additionally 
decrease climate change and fossil impacts, but only if excess wind 
power is used as background electricity.  
• The main strength of sludge ash treatment was in the high recovery 
of phosphorus (95%) and improved LCA performance by substituting 
the conventional production of monocalcium phosphate.  
• Pre-filtration replacing a physical primary clarification process was 
beneficial for decreasing climate change and fossil depletion im­
pacts, while anammox reduced marine eutrophication impacts. Re­
ductions came at the cost of increased impacts in reserve base 
resource depletion (DAR-RB) and potentially increased N2O 
emissions.  
• All alternatives showed a positive added economic value (0.18–0.23 
€ m−3), although novel technologies increased CAPEX and OPEX, 
and TVA decreased compared to the baseline except for RF N2O.  
• In our case, abatement costs for CO2-eq reduction and P-recovery 
were in the range 2-957 €/ton CO2-eq avoided and -140 and 561 € ton 
P recovered. 
Our findings provide a clear understanding of the possible hotspots 
and trade-offs of novel technologies for wastewater treatment and 
resource recovery. The study will help decision-makers understand 
system performance according to environmental and economic criteria. 
To uncover the full socio-economic benefits of implementing GHG 
control and resource recovery, other criteria, including the effect of non- 
marketed values, will need attention in future studies.

Unisense is a supplier of N2O sensor technology. EnviDan is a con­
sultancy company within wastewater treatment technologies. BIOFOS is 
a publicly owned wastewater treatment service provider. The partners 
UNISENSE, EnviDan and BIOFOS supported the project with data pro­
vision and discussion of the results. The final choice of parameter values, 
ranges for uncertainty analysis and calculations were carried out by the 
university authors independently of commercial partners. All values 
were assessed and justified by the university authors and reported by the 
inventory in our supporting information. Specifically, N2O emissions 
from UNISENSE were based on peer-reviewed studies (Chen et al., 
2019).

records.full_text:
Microbial community-based polyhydroxyalkanoates (PHAs) production
from wastewater: Techno-economic analysis and ex-ante environmental
assessment
Cora Fernández-Dacosta a,⇑, John A. Posada a, Robbert Kleerebezem b, Maria C. Cuellar b, Andrea Ramirez a
a Copernicus Institute, Utrecht University, Heidelberglaan 2, 3584 CS Utrecht, The Netherlands
b Department of Biotechnology, Delft University of Technology, Julianalaan 67, 2628 BC Delft, The Netherlands
h i g h l i g h t s
 Fermentation of wastewater towards polyhydroxyalkanoates is scaled-up.
 Intracellular polyhydroxyalkanoates release by three downstream processes.
 Process design, techno-economic and life cycle assessment are developed.
 Key features of the industrial process are identiﬁed prior to commercialisation.
 Sensitivity analysis of the costs and environmental impacts is performed.
a r t i c l e
i n f o
Article history:
Received 16 January 2015
Received in revised form 2 March 2015
Accepted 4 March 2015
Available online 10 March 2015
Keywords:
Polyhydroxyalkanoates
Waste valorisation
Life cycle assessment
Economic evaluation
Circular economy
a b s t r a c t
This work investigates the potential for polyhydroxybutyrate (PHB) production from wastewater, from a
techno-economic and an environmental perspective, examining scale-up opportunities and bottlenecks
prior to commercialisation. Conceptual process design, economic, environmental impacts and sensitivity
analysis are developed for one fermentation process and three downstream processing routes, based on
alkali, surfactant-hypochlorite and solvent treatments. Environmentally and cost-wise, the alkali
treatment is the most favourable with production costs of 1.40 €/kg PHB, global warming potential of
2.4 kg CO2-eq/kg PHB and non-renewable energy use of 106 MJ/kg PHB. The solvent-based process yields
the highest costs and environmental burdens: 1.95 €/kg PHB, 4.30 kg CO2-eq/kg PHB and 156 MJ/kg PHB.
The production of PHB from wastewater is identiﬁed as an interesting alternative to pure culture-
polyhydroxyalkanoates production from sugars. However, these results are not yet competitive with
those for the petrochemical counterparts. Additional performance improvements may be possible,
through process integration and optimisation.
 2015 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.
1. Introduction
Global bio-based plastic production is approximately 1400 kt/a,
accounting for nearly 0.5% of the current worldwide plastic pro-
duction capacity (European Bioplastics, 2013; Plastics Europe,
2013). Hence, the development of competitive industrial processes
which utilise renewable resources as feedstock for polymer
production, can be a relevant aspect in the shift from a petrochemi-
cal-based chemical industry, towards a bio-based one.
In this context, the family of polyhydroxyalkanoates (PHAs) and
its most common type, polyhydroxybutyrate (PHB), can be an
interesting alternative to investigate. PHAs are biodegradable
polyesters, naturally synthesized by bacterial fermentation, which
can serve as polymer material (Endres and Siebert-Raths, 2011) or
as raw material for the production of chemical building blocks
(Koller et al., 2012). PHAs have attracted widespread interest as
an alternative to conventional plastics due to their natural origin,
biodegradability, and functionality. High production costs, which
are estimated at 20–80% higher than for their petrochemical coun-
terparts (Marketsandmarkets, 2013), remain a key bottleneck for
their commercialisation. Three main factors contribute to this rela-
tive increase of the production costs of PHAs: (i) the energy used
for the sterilisation of the fermentation equipment (Van Wegen
http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.biortech.2015.03.025
0960-8524/ 2015 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.
⇑Corresponding author. Tel.: +31 302537687.
E-mail
addresses:
C.FernandezDacosta@uu.nl
(C.
Fernández-Dacosta),
J.A.PosadaDuque@uu.nl (J.A. Posada), R.Kleerebezem@tudelft.nl (R. Kleerebezem),
M.C.Cuellar@tudelft.nl (M.C. Cuellar), C.A.Ramirez@uu.nl (A. Ramirez).
Bioresource Technology 185 (2015) 368–377
Contents lists available at ScienceDirect
Bioresource Technology
journal homepage: www.elsevier.com/locate/biortech

et al., 1998), (ii) the PHA yield on the substrate, and (iii) the efﬁ-
ciency of the downstream processing (DSP) (Reddy et al., 2003).
In terms of potential environmental impacts, it is not clear whether
the production of PHAs using pure culture bacterial fermentation
shows any clear advantage compared to fossil-based polymers.
The substrates used in the pure culture bacterial fermentation,
i.e. glucose, methanol or acetic acid, contribute signiﬁcantly to
the overall environmental impacts of the process, (Patel et al.,
2005).
In order to address these issues on higher production costs and
environmental impacts, a microbial community engineering pro-
cess for PHA production has been proposed, as an alternative strat-
egy (Salehizadeh and van Loosdrecht, 2004). Instead of the
traditional pure culture bacterial fermentation, which consumes
expensive feedstocks, the novel process is based on the selection
of a microorganisms’ populations from a variety present in the
wastewater, which resulted in the provision of an enhanced PHA
producing capacity (77% dry weight). Lower production costs and
decreased related environmental impacts are expected as the
process uses substrates such as industrial waste and non-aseptic
process conditions (Salehizadeh and van Loosdrecht, 2004). A
proof-of-concept has been achieved at laboratory and pilot scale
using separately wastewater from a paper mill, and from the food
industry (Jiang et al., 2012; Tamis et al., 2014). The ﬁrst full scale
demonstration plant of PHAs production has been initiated by
Veolia (Press release, 06-14-2013).
In addition to an efﬁcient fermentation process, the develop-
ment of a competitive DSP facility to release the intracellular
PHA is needed to further reduce manufacturing costs and environ-
mental impacts. Based on studies with synthetic wastewater,
Gurieff and Lant (2007) concluded that the main drawback of the
mixed culture production of PHA from wastewater is the relatively
large energy consumption within the DSP. However, their work is
based on literature studies and only considers one DSP conﬁg-
uration. Several approaches have been described in the literature
for recovery and puriﬁcation of intracellular PHB after fer-
mentation (Akiyama et al., 2003; Choi and Lee, 1997; Jacquel
et al., 2008; Naranjo et al., 2013; Posada et al., 2011). The most
common procedures involve the use of organic solvent, or treat-
ment by chemical digestion. The use of solvent leads to improved
purity and recovery yield. However, environmental impacts can
increase if the solvent is not 100% recovered due to the surplus
chemicals being released into the environment (de Koning and
Witholt, 1997). From an environmental and an economic perspec-
tive, digestion with chemicals appears to be more attractive (Choi
and
Lee,
1997;
Jacquel
et
al.,
2008;
Posada
et
al.,
2011).
Nevertheless, the thermal stability of the ﬁnal product is lower,
whilst in a solvent-based DSP the quality of the PHB is comparable
to that of a commercial polymer (Jiang et al., 2015).
The aim of this research is twofold: (i) to design a system for the
industrial production of PHB, with improved economic and
environmental performance, and (ii) to identify speciﬁc processing
steps that may be further improved prior to commercialisation. To
aid in these objectives, data from laboratory and pilot plant scale,
based on real efﬂuents from industrial wastewater (Jiang et al.,
2012; Tamis et al., 2014) is used. Whereby, one fermentation pro-
cess and three DSP options are evaluated: two DSP routes are based
on chemical treatment with surfactant combined with alkali or
hypochlorite and the third one is based on solvent extraction with
dichloromethane (DCM).
2. Methods
An ex-ante economic and environmental analysis of the full-
scale production of PHB from wastewater is performed in this
study. The analysis is comprised of a conceptual process design,
techno-economic
evaluation
and
environmental
Life
Cycle
Assessment (LCA).
2.1. Process design
The conceptual process design follows a hierarchical design
strategy based on heuristics and experience, to develop the pro-
cessing ﬂowsheets. The processing units and operating conditions
are selected from either data available at laboratory or pilot scale
(Jiang et al., 2012, 2015; Tamis et al., 2014), or from literature
review. Once the processing ﬂowsheets are well deﬁned, the mass
and energy balances are obtained from conducting process mod-
elling in ASPEN Plus software. The production capacity of PHB from
wastewater deriving from a paper mill or the food industry is ﬁxed
at 1.5 kilotons per annum (kt/a) based on 6.8 kt COD (chemical
oxygen demand)/a availability from wastewater and the yields
reported in Section 2.1.1. The ﬁnal product purity is 99.9 wt%.
2.1.1. Fermentation
PHB is produced in an aerobic conversion reaction by a micro-
bial enrichment culture according to the schemes validated at lab-
oratory and pilot plant scale (Jiang et al., 2012; Tamis et al., 2014).
This microbial process consists of three sequential fermentation
steps, as presented in Fig. 1a.
First, the organic material present in the wastewater is fer-
mented into volatile fatty acids (VFA) in the acidiﬁcation reactor
(R-101) with a yield of 0.91 g COD/g COD (based on Jiang et al.,
2012: CODinitial = 26.3 g/L, CODend = 24.0 g/L, Table 1). Second, the
resulting VFA rich solution is split into two streams. One stream
is directed towards the selector (R-102), a sequencing batch reac-
tor operating with a solids retention time of 1 day and a cycle dura-
tion of 0.5 days (Tamis et al., 2014). In the selector, the enrichment
of PHA producing bacteria (X) takes place with a biomass yield on a
substrate of 0.34 g X/g COD (Tamis et al., 2014). The maximal bio-
mass concentration in the selector ([X]max) is 0.5 kg/m3, based on a
maximal biomass oxygen uptake rate (qO2) of 1 kg O2/kg X h and a
maximal
oxygen
transfer
rate
(OTRmax)
of
0.5 kg
O2/m3 h
([X]max = OTRmax/qO2). A dilution ﬂow recycled from downstream
(R-103) avoids surpassing the maximal biomass concentration.
The third and last step is PHB accumulation, carried out in the
fed-batch accumulation reactor (R-103), where the content of
intracellular PHB is maximised to 70 wt% (Tamis et al., 2014). To
avoid substrate inhibition (maximal allowed substrate concentra-
tion assumed is 0.25 kg COD/m3), the other sub-stream of the acid-
iﬁcation reactor product stream is continuously dosed during the
entire batch length. The total suspended solids (TSS) concentration
in the outlet stream of the accumulation reactor is 2.7 kg TSS/m3.
To concentrate the product stream, the solids are separated via set-
tling during 30 min at the end of the cycle (based on laboratory and
pilot plant experience). The clariﬁed fraction is removed from the
top of the settler and 75% of this stream is recycled for the purpose
of dilution water to the selector. The settled product is sent to the
buffer tank (T-101) and subsequently fed continuously to the DSP
conﬁguration for PHB extraction and puriﬁcation.
2.1.2. Downstream processes (DSP)
Three DSP routes were evaluated for the recovery of intra-
cellular PHB. The parameters of the cell disruption steps are based
on both literature (Dong and Sun, 2000; Jacquel et al., 2008; Lee
and
Choi,
1998)
and
laboratory
work
carried
out
at
Delft
University and Eindhoven University in the Netherlands.
2.1.2.1. Case I: alkali-surfactant. The ﬂow diagram of the DSP for
Case I is shown Fig. 1b. The concentrated fermentation broth,
containing 20 kg TSS/m3, with 70 wt% PHB, is preheated to 30 C
C. Fernández-Dacosta et al. / Bioresource Technology 185 (2015) 368–377
369

(in H-201). The chemical digestion of the cell material is carried
out in the alkali reactor (R-201), with sodium hydroxide (NaOH,
0.2 M) and sodium dodecyl sulphate (SDS, 0.2 wt/v%), and a resi-
dence time of 1 h (Jiang et al., 2015). 95% of the intracellular PHB
is released (Jiang et al., 2015) and subsequently recovered in two
consecutive
solid–liquid
separation
units.
70%
of
the
total
wastewater content is assumed to be removed in a hydrocyclone
(C-201) and 90% of the remaining wastewater is removed by cen-
trifugation (C-202). Impurities, which correspond to about 9% of
the solid phase, are removed by washing with counter current
fresh water in mixing tanks and centrifuges (M-301, C-301,
M-302, C-302). PHB is further concentrated by centrifugation
(C-401) and evaporation of the residual humidity in a ﬁnal air drying
step (D-401). Air drying is favoured because it does not affect the
thermal stability of PHB neither molecular weight during the melt
processing, as temperatures below 65 C are implemented (Chen
et al., 2013). A 99.9 wt% purity of the ﬁnal product is obtained.
2.1.2.2. Case II: surfactant-hypochlorite. Cell disruption by chemical
digestion can also be achieved by using surfactant and hypochlo-
rite (Lee and Choi, 1998). The non-PHB cell material (NPCM) in
the fermented wastewater is solubilised by sodium dodecyl sul-
phate (SDS) in the surfactant reactor (R-201), as depicted in
Fig. 1c. Treatment conditions are 3:1 SDS:NPCM mass ratio,
55 C, and 15 min of residence time based on Jacquel et al.
(2008). 88% of the intracellular PHB (Jacquel et al., 2008) is recov-
ered in the SDS treatment and subsequently concentrated in a
hydrocyclone (C-201), followed by a centrifuge (C-202). The liquid
phase is subjected to a crystallisation process (R-301) at 9 C to
recover and recycle 80% of the SDS (Smith et al., 2001). Further
treatment with sodium hypochlorite (NaOCl) in a second reactor
for chemical digestion (R-401), during 10 min at 30 C, recovers
extra intracellular PHB in the solid phase. A mass ratio of 8:1
NaOCl:NPCM is used to recover 95% of the remaining PHB. The rest
of the DSP is analogous to the alkali-surfactant route: PHB concen-
tration by centrifugation (C-401), counter current water washing
(M-501, C-501, M-502, C-502), centrifugation (C-601) and ﬁnal
air drying (D-601) allows the process to obtain a 99.9 wt% PHB.
2.1.2.3. Case III: dichloromethane solvent. PHAs are highly soluble in
some halogenated solvents such as dichloromethane (22.8 kg PHB/
m3 DCM, Jacquel et al., 2007). The boiling point of DCM is 40 C,
which makes its recovery and recycle comparatively easy and
results in potential energy and solvent costs savings. This also per-
mits performing DSP at milder temperatures, thus, avoiding ﬁnal
product degradation.
Prior to an extraction step using DCM, and due to the limited
solubility of PHB in water (7.6 106 kg PHB/m3 H2O, Jacquel et al.,
2007), 100% water is removed from the fermentation product (as
shown in Fig. 1d) by centrifugation (C-201), followed by ﬁltration
(F-201), and air drying (D-201). The intracellular PHB extraction is
carried out in the solvent reactor (R-201) at 20 C with an efﬁciency
of 86%, as observed at laboratory conditions. Subsequently, the solid
and liquid phases are separated via a ﬁlter (F-202). Only 50% of the
DCM in the liquid phase is evaporated (H-201), and then condensed

(H-202) for recycling. This prevents high local supersaturation at
the wall of the equipment and PHB ﬁlm formation, which affects
product quality. Following this, ethanol is fed to the precipitation
reactor (R-301), with a mass ratio of 2 kg EtOH/kg DCM to precipi-
tate 98% of the PHB (the solubility of PHB in the solvent mixture is
0.22 kg PHB/m3, Jacquel et al., 2007). The solid and liquid phases
are then separated via the ﬁlter (F-301), the remaining solvent mix-
ture in the solid phase is vaporised (D-301) to obtain a 99.9 wt% pur-
ity of PHB. In order to reduce operating costs and environmental
concerns, DCM and ethanol are separated and recycled via dis-
tillation (V-401). High separation efﬁciency is required to reuse
DCM and ethanol. Large amounts of energy are needed because
DCM and ethanol form a homogeneous azeotrope (Gmehling
et al., 2014). 99% purity of DCM and ethanol is reached over the
top and bottoms, respectively by using 15 distillation stages at vac-
uum conditions, i.e. 0.5 bar.
2.2. Economic evaluation
The economic potential of the three process options was anal-
ysed by considering total capital investment, and material and
energy consumption costs, which together account for the majority
of the total production costs of PHB. Total capital expenditure was
calculated using typical factors for delivered equipment costs
(Smith, 2005). Delivered equipment costs were estimated from
equipment manufacturers’ indications and using correlations
based on individual equipment’s characteristic size (Sinnott,
2005). The calculated costs were updated to 2013 prices using
the
Chemical
Engineering
Plant
Cost
Index
(CEPCI).
Annual
depreciation (AD) was calculated based on the total capital (fI)
and the start-up material costs (mSU) with an interest rate (i) of
5% and a payback time (PB) of 20 years. See Eq. (1):
AD ¼ ðf I þ mSUÞ 
ið1 þ iÞ
PB
ð1 þ iÞ
PB  1
ð1Þ
The total annual costs (TAC) were estimated using Eq. (2):
TAC ¼ AD þ U þ m þ L þ M
ð2Þ
Utilities (U) and materials (m) costs were based on consumption
levels from the energy and mass balances and Ulrich and
Vasudevan (2006). Maintenance costs (M) were assumed to
account for 3% of the total ﬁxed capital costs and the labour costs
(L) were assumed to be 10% of the total annual costs (TAC) (Peters
et al., 2003).
Furthermore, the wastewater generated in any factory has to
undergo a wastewater treatment (WWT) process (which causes
additional costs for the attached industries). It is here assumed that
the PHB production process developed, produces water efﬂuent at
the same quality standards of the existing wastewater treatment
process. Therefore, avoided wastewater treatment costs can be
accounted as economic credits which are directly deducted from
the total annual costs. A similar approach is followed for the
environmental credits in the LCA. These economic credits were
estimated based on the treatment cost per m3 of wastewater and
the volumetric ﬂow entering to the fermentation tank (Ulrich
and Vasudevan, 2006). No additional WWT costs were considered
in the PHB production process since the wastewater efﬂuent was
already treated during fermentation and thus suitable for being
returned to the water cycle.
2.3. Life cycle assessment (LCA)
An LCA was performed according to ISO 14040 and ISO 14044
standards (ISO, 2006a,b). A cradle-to-gate approach was selected
for the system boundaries, including wastewater fermentation
and PHB recovery, excluding the product use and end-of-life treat-
ment. The functional unit selected was 1 kg of PHB production.
An assessment of mechanical properties and applicability of the
ﬁnal product is out of the scope of this research and thus are not
discussed. Despite this omission, it is important to mention that
the quality for thermoplastic application might not be achieved
in Case I but reached in Case III (Jiang et al., 2015). Nevertheless,
if PHB is considered as an intermediate for the production of
chemical building blocks (e.g. methyl crotonate and methyl acry-
late), its ﬁnal quality is not considered a limiting factor. This value
chain from wastewater to chemical building blocks via PHB will be
addressed in a forthcoming paper.
The Life Cycle Inventory (LCI), including input and output ﬂows
of materials and energy, was derived from the mass and energy
balances obtained from the process simulation. The focus of this
study is on two impact categories: the global warming potential
(GWP) and the non-renewable energy use (NREU). Individual
impacts for materials and energy carriers were taken from the
database EcoInvent v2.2. Combined with the inventory, the
individual impacts when summed provided the overall impacts
of each process option. Similarly as applied within the economic
analysis, the environmental impacts that would have arisen from
the avoided wastewater treatment (WWT) are considered as
environmental credits. These environmental credits as well as
the biogenic carbon embedded in the PHB are directly deducted
from the total GWP and NREU following the guidelines of
Pawelzik et al., 2013; U.S. EPA, 2011.
2.4. Sensitivity analysis
The four most critical variables and assumptions of the process
designs were analysed to determine their inﬂuence on the techno-
economic and environmental performance, and to identify oppor-
tunities for further improvement: (i) the cell disruption efﬁciency
obtained in laboratory was varied ±10%, (ii) the SDS crystallisation
step in Case II was removed, (iii) the distillation of methanol in
Case III was carried out in two columns (instead of one), and (iv)
100% of DCM was vaporised after solvent extraction in Case III
(instead of 50% DCM vaporisation).
3. Results and discussion
3.1. Process design
The considered DSP led to a ﬁnal product purity of 99.9 wt% in
the three cases investigated; however, the global PHB recovery
yield was slightly different for each case, i.e. 73.5% for Case I,
75.8% for Case II and 82.2% for Case III.
The PHB released by chemical treatment (Cases I and II) was in
solid phase and further puriﬁed in a 2 steps counter-current wash-
ing process, with the addition of water to remove the non-PHB cell
material. This washing led to a PHB loss of about 10% of the
released PHB. In Case III, PHB was extracted with DCM as solvent.
The PHB in the liquid phase was recovered via precipitation with
ethanol, therefore, no water-washing was required. Additionally,
the overall recovery was enhanced in Case III, as the non-precipi-
tated PHB was separated out at the bottoms stream of the dis-
tillation tower and recycled for further precipitation. No recycle
loops, which could increase the recovery yield, were incorporated
into Cases I and II.
3.2. Economic evaluation
The outcome of the economic evaluation of the fermentation
and each DSP route is discussed in this section. The obtained
C. Fernández-Dacosta et al. / Bioresource Technology 185 (2015) 368–377
371

results are subsequently compared to the costs associated to the
traditional sugar-based production process of PHAs and those of
the production processes utilising petrochemical counterparts.
Based on the process conditions described in Section 2.1, and
the models and assumptions presented in Section 2.2, the eco-
nomic evaluation shows that Case I is the most cost-competitive
alternative amongst the various options designed (see Table 2
and Fig. 2a). The total production costs, including economic credits,
were: 1.40 €/kg PHB in Case I, 1.56 €/kg PHB in Case II and 1.95 €/kg
PHB in Case III.
In all three options, the costs attached to DSP are larger than
those for the fermentation stage, with contributions of 70% (Case
I), 73% (Case II) and 79% (Case III) (Table 2). Utility costs represent
a major share of the total production costs: 65% (Case I), 51% (Case
II) and 74% (Case III). The largest share in Case III is due to the dis-
tillation column used to recover DCM and ethanol, leading to high
duties in the reboiler and condenser.
Within the utilities (Table 2 and Fig. 2b), steam costs were 14%
and 12% of the total production costs in Cases I and II, and 10% in
Case III. The higher steam costs in Cases I and II were due to the
lower PHB recovery. Cooling water costs were similar to those of
steam: 13% (Case I), 12% (Case II) and 10% (Case III). Electricity con-
sumption in Case I is observed as the most signiﬁcant utility cost
factor (21% of total costs) but its share is signiﬁcantly lower in
cases II and III (11% and 8%, respectively). The variance can be
attributed to the larger volumetric ﬂow of the centrifuges in Case
I due to the more diluted process streams.
Case III was the least economically attractive option, mainly due
to the high utilities consumption for hot water and hot-dry air. The
air was applied to remove water from the fermentation product
(95 wt%) before the extraction process with DCM since the water
presence decreases the PHB solubility in DCM. Supply of hot-dry
air accounted for 11% of the total costs. In Cases I and II, PHB
was released in a solution, therefore, drying was not required.
Hot water dominated the operating costs of Case III with a share
of 25% of the total costs.
Chemical material costs represented 16%, 31% and 2% of the
total costs for the Cases I, II and III, respectively. The material costs
in Case I were the half of the ones in Case II because of the rela-
tively low price of NaOH (the cheapest of the utilised chemicals).
In Case II, NaOCl and SDS were used. NaOCl has a comparable price
to NaOH, but the price of SDS is three times higher. A similar
amount of chemicals were used in Case I and II, but in Case III
the make-up of solvents was minimal (1%) due to high recovery
level of DCM and ethanol by distillation.
The total production costs endured from the processes assessed
in this study were lower than the expected price of PHA production
for a scaled-up industrial biotechnology based process (2–4 €/kg
PHB, Jacquel et al., 2008). Fig. 2c. compares the PHA production
costs estimated to those values reported by previous studies and
by commercial processes, considering different C-sources, microor-
ganisms and DSP options.
Current industrial PHA production typically uses sugars, which
can account for 23% of the total production costs (Lee and Choi,
1998). Wastewater as a feedstock for the mixed-culture bacterial
fermentation is a key parameter that can explain the lower costs
found
in
this
study
since
sterilisation
costs
are
negligible.
Moreover, the costs of the avoided wastewater treatment were
assigned as credits and therefore subtracted from the overall pro-
duction costs. In Case I, the credits accounted for 30% of the total
production costs, 27% in Case II and 21% in Case III. Economic cred-
its are higher for Case I due to the higher volumetric ﬂow of
wastewater that is required to compensate its lower PHB recovery.
Although PHB production costs from wastewater are lower
compared to sugar-based production routes, the production costs
need to be reduced further to provide a competitive alternative
to the petrochemical counterparts. Polyethylene terephthalate
(PET), for example, has a current market price of approximately
1.3 €/kg (Packham, 2014). It should be noted that production pro-
cess for PET has been commercially available for many years and
has matured towards optimum conditions. There is therefore
potential for increasing the efﬁciency of PHB production via
wastewater fermentation, because the current design of the indus-
trial process is still at a moderately early stage of development
compared to fossil resources based production of PET. PET is
selected as indicative benchmark because it is a polyester as are
PHAs. Mechanical properties of the ﬁnal product are not discussed
in this study. When the application of PHAs is for thermoplastic
material, isotactic polypropylene should be used for comparison.
3.3. Life cycle assessment
The overall GWP including; fermentation and DSP stages, the
credits from avoided wastewater treatment, and biogenic carbon,
was: 2.38 kg CO2-eq/kg PHB for Case I, 2.06 kg CO2-eq/kg PHB for
Case II and 4.30 kg CO2-eq/kg PHB for Case III. The NREU was
106, 109 and 158 MJ/kg PHB for Cases I, II and III, respectively
(Table 3). Results of LCA are shown in Fig. 3. The environmental
impacts of the fermentation step were relatively low compared
to those induced by the DSP. The latter(s) accounted for 61% of
total GWP in Case I, 60% in Case II and 76% in Case III. The share
of total NREU arising from DSP was 70% in Case I, 72% in Case II
and 82% in Case III.
The key factors driving the environmental impacts caused by
the fermentation process were steam use for heating the fer-
menters, and growth nutrients. Steam use for fermentation
accounted for 15% of the GWP in Cases I and II, and 9% in Case
III. Steam accounted for 12% of the total NREU in cases I and II,
and 6% in Case III. Urea and phosphate, the growth nutrients
required for fermentation, represent 19% of the GWP in Cases I

In Case III, the chemicals did not represent a signiﬁcant environ-
mental impact because they were recovered and recycled with
very minor losses.

less preferred route for intracellular PHB release and puriﬁcation. It

3.4. Sensitivity analysis
3.4.1. Cell disruption efﬁciency
As shown in Table 4, sensitivity analysis was performed on the
base design for each of the three cases. The observed efﬁciency of
the cell disruption when taken from laboratory data was varied
±10% with respect to the base design. The costs (€ per kg PHB)
and the environmental impacts (GWP and NREU per kg PHB)
increased with reduced recovery due to a lower overall PHB yield.
At a higher efﬁciency, more PHB was recovered, thus, the costs and
environmental impacts decreased. The solvent route (Case III) was
the least affected by the changes. This can be explained by the
most inﬂuential factors (i.e. energy use in the distillation for sol-
vents recovery) not varying under conditions of lower cell disrup-
tion efﬁciency.
3.4.2. SDS crystallization (Case II)
In Case II, the surfactant was recovered and recycled via crys-
tallisation. A ﬁlter was also included for the separation of the crys-
tallised surfactant from the liquid phase. The process was carried
out at 9 C, requiring the use of chilled water in a pre-cooler. As
an alternative, the crystallisation step can be removed, and all of
the SDS would enter the process as fresh material. The capital
and utilities costs would then be reduced by 13% and 4%, respec-
tively; but material costs would increase by 22%. Thus, the draw-
backs of using extra material outweigh the beneﬁts in terms of
investment and utilities savings. The total production costs would
then be 3.07 €/kg PHB, doubling the base design costs.
The GWP and NREU, both with and without SDS recovery are
compared in Fig. 4a and b. Although the electricity consumption
dropped because no chilled water was required when the crys-
tallisation step was bypassed, the overall environmental impacts
increased. Without crystallisation, the larger amount of make-up
material would result in a 90% larger GWP and 60% higher NREU
compared to the base design.
3.4.3. Distillation (Case III)
The utilities costs in Case III were 74% of the total production
costs, of which 40% is directly linked to the energy demand for
the distillation process. Instead of one column operating at
0.5 bar of absolute pressure, two sequential columns were consid-
ered within the sensitivity analysis. The ﬁrst separation is per-
formed at atmospheric pressure and the second at vacuum
conditions (0.5 bar) to obtain the desired purity in the product
streams. The process option with two columns did not show any
beneﬁts compared to a single vacuum column. The separation efﬁ-
ciency was lower, therefore, additional DCM was needed. The
material costs rose from 0.06 €/kg PHB to 0.51 €/kg PHB and the
annual depreciation increased by 4% due to the extra equipment.
The total production costs increased 27%, from 1.95 €/kg PHB to
2.48 €/kg PHB.
The LCA results for both process options using either one or two
distillation columns are shown in Fig. 4c and d. Electricity impacts
decreased in the case with two columns due to less chilled water
being required in the condenser of the vacuum column, but the
impacts caused by DCM increased because more fresh material
was used. The NREU in the two columns setup dropped by 15%
with respect to the base design, but the GWP was 12% higher.
3.4.4. DCM evaporation (Case III)
In the base design, only 50% of the DCM was vaporised after
extraction to avoid reaching supersaturation in the wall of the
equipment and lower product quality, due to a less controlled crys-
tallisation. On the assumption that optimised large-scale equip-
ment is used and all DCM is evaporated, the DSP was simpliﬁed
because no precipitation with ethanol or distillation was neces-
sary. On the contrary, extra energy and larger heat exchangers
were considered due to the absolute amount of DCM passing
through vaporisation, condensation and recycling stages, which is
doubled. The product purity and recovery yield decreased to
97.6 wt% and 81.9%, respectively. Capital and utilities costs
dropped by 7% and 26%, resulting in 21% lower total production
costs (1.54 €/kg PHB).
Fig. 4c and d compare LCA results of three alternative designs
for Case III: (i) 50% DCM evaporation and one distillation column
for ethanol-DCM recovery (base case), (ii) 50% DCM evaporation
and two distillation columns, and (iii) all DCM vaporised. Since dis-
tillation is not used in this third case, chilled water and large
amounts of natural gas are not necessary. Furthermore, the GWP
decreased to negative values (0.10 kg CO2-eq/kg PHB) due to
the credits from the avoided wastewater treatment and the bio-
genic carbon embedded in the PHB product. Additionally, the
NREU values were halved (83 MJ/kg PHB).
Evaporation of all DCM simpliﬁes the DSP signiﬁcantly, leading
to the most promising alternative from both an economic and
environmental perspective. However, this high-evaporation level
concept should experimentally be proven and its inﬂuence on
the ﬁnal product quality should be determined.

4. Conclusions
Results of this study indicate that PHB production from
wastewater could become an interesting alternative to the expen-
sive sugar-based PHAs production. The process beneﬁts from the
use of wastewater as feedstock. An integrated approach including
process design, economic and environmental assessments identi-
ﬁes alkali treatment for cell disruption as the most promising
route. When compared to the alkali route, treatment with surfac-
tant-hypochlorite needs one additional chemical digestion step. A
downstream process using solvent includes a distillation step for
the solvent recovery, increasing the ﬁnal product costs and
environmental impacts.

records.extracted_title: Life cycle assessment of a nanomaterial-based adsorbent developed on lab scale for cadmium removal: Comparison of the impacts of production, use and recycling
records.full_text:
Life cycle assessment of a nanomaterial-based adsorbent developed on lab
scale for cadmium removal: Comparison of the impacts of production, use
and recycling
Maria Nelly Garcia Gonzalez a,*, Roxana Quiroga-Flores b, Pål B€orjesson a
a Environmental and Energy Systems Studies, Department of Technology and Society, Lund University, P.O. Box 118, SE-221 00 Lund, Sweden
b Division of Biotechnology, Department of Chemistry, P.O. Box 124, Lund University, SE-223 62 Lund, Sweden
A R T I C L E I N F O
Keywords:
Nanoadsorbents
Life cycle assessment
Cadmium
Wastewater
Recycling
A B S T R A C T
Many nanoadsorbents are reported in the literature, exhibiting remarkable properties in the removal of low
concentrations of toxic metals. However, their environmental performance has not yet been well studied. A life
cycle assessment (LCA) was carried out for a previously reported nanomaterial-based adsorbent, silicate-titanate
nanotubes chitosan beads (STNTs-Ch beads), used to remove cadmium from wastewater. The environmental
impacts associated with the synthesis of the nanomaterial and the ﬁnal adsorbent, including use and recycling,
were evaluated. The hotspot of the process was the synthesis of the nanomaterial, mainly due to high electricity
consumption, suggesting that energy use must be reduced in the scaling up. The chemicals used may become an
environmental problem once electricity consumption in the process is optimised on a large-scale process. The
environmental impacts associated with the synthesis and the use of the process were compared with granular
activated carbon (produced on industrial scale), a resin based on titanium dioxide (pilot scale), and an adsorbent
based on a residue after alginate extraction (laboratory scale). Granular activated carbon had the lowest impacts,
suggesting again that optimisation of energy and chemicals should be prioritised in the production of emerging
materials.
1. Introduction
Nanomaterials possess remarkable physicochemical properties that
have been exploited in many applications, such as electronics (Lee and
Lu, 2018), skin care products (Botta et al., 2011), catalysts (Bhanja and
Bhaumik, 2016), agriculture (Rastogi et al., 2019), and water treatment
(Adeleye et al., 2016). Nanomaterials are small in size and have a large
surface area, producing high adsorption capacities and fast adsorption
rates (Lu and Astruc, 2018), and can therefore be used to remove organic
and inorganic pollutants such as dyes (Cheung et al., 2009) and metals
(Kyzas and Matis, 2015) from contaminated water. Some nanomaterials
are designed as composites, where the other component is usually
included to improve the adsorption capacity, increase the mechanical
strength and/or act as a support material to retain the small nano-
particles, thereby facilitating application and recovery. The nano-
adsorbents used to remove toxic metals from industrial wastewater or
drinking water (Burakov et al., 2018) are able to selectively target low
concentrations of metals such as As (Habuda-Stanic and Nujic, 2015), Cd
(Wang et al., 2013), Pb (Qu et al., 2012), and Hg (Kazemi et al., 2018).
The ability to target low concentrations of metals is a great advantage,
and as more stringent wastewater discharge limits are being proposed
(Mattsson et al., 2017), many nanomaterials with high adsorption ca-
pacities are being developed.
Many new nanomaterials with high adsorption capacities for Cd have
been suggested to meet these guidelines. However, this technology is still
at an early stage of development, and most of these novel nanoadsorbents
are still being studied on laboratory scale. Knowledge is required on the
feasibility of nanoadsorbent production, their environmental perfor-
mance, emissions, and impacts on public health, to guide the develop-
ment of these new materials.
Life cycle assessment (LCA) can be used to examine the potential
impacts of a product or service on the environment over its entire life-
span. This tool is also used to guide the development of emerging tech-
nologies through preliminary assessments (by providing anticipated
environmental impacts), allowing the design and improvement of a
process
and/or
product,
while
considering
its
environmental
* Corresponding author.
E-mail address: nelly.garcia_gonzalez@miljo.lth.se (M.N. Garcia Gonzalez).
Contents lists available at ScienceDirect
Cleaner Environmental Systems
journal homepage: www.journals.elsevier.com/cleaner-environmental-systems
https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cesys.2022.100071
Received 16 September 2021; Received in revised form 21 January 2022; Accepted 21 January 2022
2666-7894/© 2022 The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd. This is an open access article under the CC BY-NC-ND license (http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-
nc-nd/4.0/).
Cleaner Environmental Systems 4 (2022) 100071

performance (Gifford et al., 2016). In LCA, an impact category (or
environmental problem ﬁeld) is deﬁned as “a class representing envi-
ronmental issues of concern to which life cycle inventory analysis results
may be assigned” (ISO-14044). The reason for the choice of impact
category is based on both its applicability to a particular product, system
and/or service, and the overall goal of the LCA study.
A recent study described a new nanomaterial called silicate-titanate
nanotubes (STNTs), embedded in chitosan named STNTs-Ch beads.
This material was used for the adsorption of Cd from wastewater
(Quiroga-Flores et al., 2019). The STNTs were produced using the alka-
line hydrothermal method as synthesis procedure, and the adsorbent was
characterised and subjected to recycling. An LCA study of this new
adsorbent would not only identify the critical inputs or steps of the
synthesis procedure, but would also enable data on its environmental
performance to be related to those from other materials already in use or
intended for the same purpose.
Comparisons are somewhat difﬁcult, as previous LCA studies have
assessed similar materials intended for other purposes, e.g. silica nano-
spheres for insulating materials (Gao et al., 2013). Other studies have
produced adsorbents to treat Cd, but the nature of the material differed
from the STNTs structure, e.g. a carbon-based adsorbent (Nishikawa
et al., 2018). Furthermore, comparison with commercial adsorbents is
not always meaningful due to the difference in the scales of use.
Nevertheless, based on information currently available in the litera-
ture, we identiﬁed three materials suitable for comparison. (i) A resin
that is also based on nanosized nano-TiO2 (TiO2 is a precursor of both the
STNTs and Ti-AX), where the data were scaled to pilot scale (Gifford
et al., 2016). (ii) An adsorbent used for Cd adsorption (same metal
treated by the STNTs-Ch beads) on lab scale that was produced from
waste after alginate extraction from seaweed (AEW) (Nishikawa et al.,
2018), and (iii) the well-known granular activated carbon (GAC), which
is produced on commercial scale. Despite the inherent differences in the
materials and processes, we believe that these can serve as adequate
candidates for rough comparisons.
In this study, an LCA of STNTs-Ch beads developed on laboratory
scale (Quiroga-Flores et al., 2019) was performed. The study followed a
cradle-to-use approach including production, use, and recycling (i.e.,
excluding disposal) to provide relevant information for sustainable
scaling up of the process. Thus, the maintenance and end-of-life phases
are not included in this study. The environmental impacts associated
with each stage of the process were evaluated when the adsorbent was
produced, used for Cd adsorption, recycled, and reused. Swedish elec-
tricity supply mix was assumed in the base case, and a sensitivity analysis
was performed by comparison with a Nordic electricity supply mix. We
compared the impacts of the production and use of STNTs-Ch beads with
the three materials identiﬁed above (i-iii) based on adsorption capacity.
2. Methods
The LCA was performed according to the protocol of the International
Organization for Standardization (ISO 14040:2006, ISO 14044:2006),
and consists of four main phases: (i) deﬁnition of the goal and scope of
the LCA, (ii) the life cycle inventory analysis (LCI) phase, (iii) the life
cycle impact assessment (LCIA) phase, and (iv) interpretation, including
sensitivity analysis, the conclusions and recommendations.
2.1. Goal and scope deﬁnition
The goal of the present study was to conduct an LCA for a novel
adsorbent, STNTs-Ch beads, which was studied on laboratory scale for
the adsorption of Cd in wastewater, as described by Quiroga-Flores et al.
(2019), to determine the environmental impacts associated with its
production, use, and recycling. It is hoped that the information obtained
can be used for sustainable scaling up of the process.
Fig. 1 shows a simpliﬁed scheme of the complete process, from the
production
of
the
STNTs-Ch
beads
to
their
use
and
recycling.
Quiroga-Flores et al. (2019) studied the effects of 7 cycles of recycling
(desorption and regeneration), and reported that the adsorption capacity
of the STNTs-Ch beads was maintained up until the 4th cycle. This was
determined on the basis of the desorption of Cd, the adsorption capacity
at equilibrium (Qe), and the percentage of Cd removal. Qe was measured
in each cycle, and denoted Qe’. The value for each cycle was then used to
calculate the recovered Qe, denoted Qe recov (%). After 4 cycles, the
nanoadsorbent showed a Cd desorption efﬁciency of 70%, Qe recov was
86%, and the Cd removal efﬁciency was 85%, when treating water
containing 10 mg Cd⋅L-1. These values decreased after further recycling,
reaching a Cd removal efﬁciency of 54% after the 7th cycle. They also
reported that the mass loss was non-detectable up until the 7th cycle
(below the detection limit of the analytical balance). Based on their
ﬁndings, 4 recycling cycles were considered the base case in the present
study. One recycling time refers to all stages from Stage I to Stage VI, i.e.,
the adsorbent is produced and subjected to 1 cycle of recycling, and
denoted 1REC. Similarly, 4REC denotes 4 recycling times, i.e., the case in
which the adsorbent is produced and recycled 4 times. The case when the
STNTs-Ch beads were not recycled, i.e. stages I-III, is denoted NREC. The
information regarding the life cycle inventory (LCI) is described in Sec-
tion 2.5.
The system boundary is shown in Fig. 2, deﬁning the cradle-to-use life
cycle of the STNTs-Ch beads, i.e., the production of the adsorbent (Stages
I-II, blue boxes), use (Stage III, grey box) and 4 recycling cycles (Stages
IV-VI, green boxes). The extraction process of the raw materials (cradle)
corresponds to all the chemicals needed during the process, such as
NaOH, TiO2 and HCl (details concerning the life cycle inventories are
given in Table 1). The use stage corresponds to the application of the
STNTs-Ch beads to remove Cd from the solution, but the ﬁnal disposal of
the exhausted STNTs-Ch beads containing Cd was not included in the
system. Neither the treatment of the waste produced at each stage, nor
the treatment of the desorbed Cd at Stage V were included. The disposal
of this nanoadsorbent at the end of its lifetime is not included in this
study since no data related to this are currently available in the literature.
Fig. 1. Simpliﬁed scheme of the complete process divided into six stages. Stages
I to III comprise production of the nanoadsorbent STNTs-Ch beads (blue boxes)
and use (grey box), Stages IV-VI involve 1 cycle of recycling the STNTs-Ch beads
(green boxes).
M.N. Garcia Gonzalez et al.
Cleaner Environmental Systems 4 (2022) 100071
2

Moreover, this LCA study is only the ﬁrst step, and additional studies will
be performed when the necessary data become available. This study
could be integrated to some application facilities and end-of-life treat-
ment in future cradle-to-grave studies. The waste produced at each stage
is given in Table 1 and explained in Section 2.5. The waste output in
Fig. 2 includes all the waste produced in each stage. The functional unit
(FU) was deﬁned as 1 g of STNTs-Ch beads that adsorbs 10.4 mg Cd from
1.053 L of Cd-contaminated water (10 mg Cd⋅L-1).
2.1.1. Assumptions and limitations
The following assumptions and limitations were employed.
(i) Transportation of the materials from the company to the labora-
tory was not considered.
(ii) Transportation of the raw materials to the company was included.
(iii) The geographical coverage for the electricity was based on Swe-
den (base case), and on the European Union for almost all the
materials and water (see Tables S1–S4 in the Supporting Infor-
mation (SI) for more details).
(iv) “Market process for electricity, low voltage” (name of the process
in the Ecoincent database) was chosen in our modelling, as this is
recommended when using supply mixes on lab scale in LCA
studies (Itten et al., 2012). (More details can be found in
Tables S1–S4.)
(v) “Transformation processes” were chosen for all the chemicals
(process in the Ecoinvent database). All the inputs required to
manufacture a product, and inputs from its associated emissions
and resource extractions, are included. Transportation and related
losses were not considered.
(vi) The production of the different chemicals comes from the Ecoin-
vent database (see Tables S1–S4).
a. Sodium hydroxide (NaOH): The most common chlor-alkali
process involving the electrolysis of aqueous sodium chloride
in a membrane cell was chosen for the production of NaOH.
b. Hydrochloric acid (HCl): It was assumed that hydrochloric acid
was produced by the combustion of chlorine with hydrogen,
since this was the process used to make the NaOH used in the
laboratory.
c. Titanium dioxide (TiO2): It was assumed that TiO2 was pro-
duced by the sulphate process (same process of TiO2 used in the
laboratory).
d. Silicon dioxide (SiO2): Activated silica was chosen, since it
corresponds to thermally dried SiO2.
e. Chitosan: Indian chitosan production described by Mu~noz et al.
(2018) was chosen, as this was the only study providing pub-
licly available primary data.
2.2. Life cycle inventory analysis
The inputs and outputs of each stage used in the LCI are shown in
Table 1. Most data (primary data) were obtained from Quiroga-Flores
et al. (2019), and scaled to 1 g of TiO2. In the present study, the data were
recalculated to 1 g STNTs-Ch beads, the assigned FU. Additional exper-
iments were conducted to calculate the chemicals consumed when
required (further details are given in Section 2.5). The energy
Fig. 2. System boundary deﬁning the cradle-to-use life cycle of 1 g of the
nanoadsorbent (STNTs-Ch beads). The production of the nanoadsorbent corre-
sponds to the blue boxes, use corresponds to the grey box, and 4 recycling cycles
corresponds to the green boxes.

production of STNTs-Ch beads (Stages I-II), use (Stage III), and 1 recycling cycle

consumption (electricity) is expressed in kWh, and was calculated
theoretically according to the information provided by the manufacturer
for each piece of equipment used in the laboratory.
2.3. Description of the production and recycling stages of STNTs-Ch beads
The steps involved in the production and recycling of STNTs-Ch beads
are described brieﬂy below (in-depth details are given in Quiroga-Flores
et al. (2019)). The emphasis in the description is on the theoretical and
experimental data used in this study.
2.3.1. Stage I
The process starts with the synthesis of STNTs via an alkaline hy-
drothermal reaction carried out for 48 h. The electricity consumed by the
oven, stirrer, and digester was calculated to be 41.7 kWh. During the
reaction, water was lost as vapour during both the synthesis and drying of
the STNTs to produce a powder. The heat loss was considered part of the
waste and was not included in the system. The amount of NaOH waste in
this stage (alkaline water) was calculated using the stoichiometric bal-
ance reported for the synthesis of titanate nanotubes (Liu et al., 2014;
Wang et al., 2013) (see Table S5).
2.3.2. Stage II
STNTs-Ch beads (0.28 g of STNTs per g of chitosan) were made by
gelation in a NaOH bath with constant agitation (0.07 kWh). To calculate
the NaOH waste in this stage, an experiment based on pH measurements
was carried out, from which the theoretical NaOH concentration was
calculated (see Table S5).
2.3.3. Stage III
The STNTs-Ch beads (dosage of STNTs was 0.24 g L-1) were placed on
a rocking table for 24 h (0.83 kWh) to treat 10 mL of a solution of 10 mg
Cd⋅L-1. Electricity consumed in Stages II and III was 596 and 50 times
lower than in Stage I. After treatment, the wastewater contained 1% Cd,
as reported by Quiroga-Flores et al. (2019).
2.3.4. Stages IV-VI
Stages IV to VI comprise one recycling cycle. This was carried out
under static conditions, apart from the washing step in Stage VI, which
included the use of an agitator (0.003 kWh). The total amount of NaOH
waste in Stages IV and VI was calculated from an additional experiment
based on pH measurements and by calculating the theoretical concen-
tration of NaOH used in Stage IV, and using a theoretical relationship
reported for desorption and regeneration (Du et al., 2011) for Stage VI.
The desorbed Cd (desorption efﬁciency 70%) is obtained as a concen-
trated solution in Stage V. (Further details are presented in Table S5).
2.4. Life cycle impact assessment
The SimaPro LCA software (version 9.0.0.48) was used to conduct the
LCA study, and the Ecoinvent database version 3.5 was used as a back-
ground source for the processes used (further details can be found in
Tables S1–S4). The methodological approach of attributional modelling
was adopted in which burdens are attributed proportionally to the spe-
ciﬁc processes (also known as the allocation at the point of substitution,
APOS, system model in the Ecoinvent database). This system model was
chosen to gain detailed insight into the different environmental impacts
in this LCA at an early stage. The data collected were then modelled using
the ReCiPe Midpoint (H) (v1.02) method (Huijbregts et al., 2016). The
European Commission recommends the use of this method, and it was
chosen because it addresses uncertainties in the model and provides
detailed method documentation (Chen et al., 2016) and a variety of
impact categories. An LCA study based on lab scale data is associated
with inherent uncertainties, and the quality of input data (amounts of
materials used and the calculated energy consumption) is therefore
crucial. To determine the absolute uncertainty in the results for each
main category and for each case study (NREC, 1REC and 4REC) a basic
Monte Carlo method was applied and the results can be found in Table S6
in the Supporting Information. It should be noted that the uncertainties in
an LCA study based on a laboratory scale may show signiﬁcant differ-
ences regarding commercial scale as theoretical calculations and/or as-
sumptions are much less needed when the processes are veriﬁed in
large-scale commercial production.
2.5. Impact categories considered in the study
The ReCiPe Midpoint method covers 18 impact categories (Huijbregts
et al., 2016). Attention was focused on eight in this study: global
warming, GW (kg CO2-eq), freshwater eutrophication, FE (kg P-eq), ma-
rine eutrophication, ME (kg N-eq), terrestrial ecotoxicity, TEc (kg 1,
4-DCB-eq), freshwater ecotoxicity, FEc (kg 1,4-DCB-eq), marine ecotox-
icity, MEc (kg 1,4-DCB-eq), human carcinogen toxicity, HcT, and
non-carcinogenic toxicity, Hnon-cT (kg 1,4-DCB-eq). The characterisation
factors used in each impact category are also taken from the ReCiPe
Midpoint method, which is well-explained in the ReCiPe 2016 v1.1
report (Huijbregts et al., 2016). The justiﬁcation for the choice of impact
categories is presented in Table S7, and was based on the probability of
the inputs (Table 1) inﬂuencing certain impact categories.
2.6. Comparison with other LCA studies on adsorbents used for treating
wastewater to remove the metal
The results of the present LCA study were compared to those obtained
for a resin (Ti-AX) scaled from laboratory data to pilot (Gifford et al.,
2016), an adsorbent based on alginate extraction waste (AEW) produced
on lab scale (Nishikawa et al., 2018), and commercial granular activated
carbon (GAC). The data for GAC were obtained from the Ecoinvent
database v3.5. Due to the variety of GAC available on the market, and the
generic description of GAC in Ecoinvent, we obtained additional global
warming emission data from Chemviron Carbon AB (Chemviron Carbon
AB, 2019) for a GAC that is used to treat industrial wastewater (denoted
GAC-CCAB). These materials were chosen for comparison as they had
similar features to our nanoadsorbent, either they share the same starting
material or are used for the same purpose. Recycling and fate were not
included in the comparison as the mentioned studies did not conduct
recycling and we do not have data available for determining the envi-
ronmental impact of the produced nanoadsorbent.
Ti-AX is an anion-exchange resin coated with nano-TiO2, and was
used to remove As from drinking water. The FU in this case was deﬁned
as the mass of the resin needed to treat 20 gallons of drinking water
(annual average domestic water use of 500 people), to reduce the con-
centration from 20 μg As⋅L-1 to 8 μg As⋅L-1 (Gifford et al., 2016). This
system was divided into three stages: sorbent synthesis, use, and disposal.
The impact assessment was taken from the TRACI midpoint method
(Gifford et al., 2016). The adsorbent AEW was produced on lab scale and
consisted of the residue left after alginate extraction from seaweed, and
was used to remove Cd. The FU in the original study was deﬁned as the
mass of adsorbent needed to treat 1 L of wastewater to reduce the con-
centration from 0.05 mg L-1 to 0.001 mg L-1. The system was divided into
transportation, production and use, and waste management. The method
used for impact assessment was CML (Nishikawa et al., 2018).
The comparison of the above adsorbents focused on the production
and use stages (Stages I-II and III, Fig. 2). Recycling was not considered,
as in the studies it was assumed that both Ti-AX and AEW that they would
be disposed of directly in landﬁlls after use. The results obtained must be
interpreted with great caution due to the use of different LCIA methods,
involving different ways of calculating the emissions, and should be seen
as a ﬁrst, rough attempt to assess the performance of the adsorbent
studied here. The categories global warming, marine eutrophication and
human toxicity were compared for the different adsorbents. For the
comparison, the FU was deﬁned as the mass of adsorbent needed to
remove 1 mmol of the metal that they were designed to adsorb (i.e. 1
M.N. Garcia Gonzalez et al.
Cleaner Environmental Systems 4 (2022) 100071
4

mmol Cd in the case of AEW, GAC and STNTs-Ch beads, and 1 mmol As in
the case of Ti-AX). Equimolar terms were chosen in this step in an attempt
to make a fair comparison since the Ti-AX was used to remove As. The
calculations were based on the value of Qmax reported in each study,
being 656 mg Cd⋅g-1 for STNTs-Ch beads, 8 μmol As⋅g-1 for Ti-AX, 0.43
mmol Cd⋅g-1 for AEW and 0.24 mmol Cd⋅g-1 for GAC (the value for GAC
was obtained from Asuquo et al. (2017). The amount of each adsorbent
required to remove 1 mmol of the metal was 0.17 g STNTs-Ch beads, 125
g Ti-AX, 2.32 g AEX, and 4.17 g GAC.
It should be noted that removing equimolar amounts of Cd and As
from wastewater will not necessarily have the same impact on the
environment, as the toxicity may vary depending on where and how
these metals are exposed.
2.7. Sensitivity analysis
In the LCA model, the Swedish electricity production mix was used as
the base case. A sensitivity analysis was performed using the Nordic
electricity supply mix instead, which has a somewhat higher proportion
of fossil-fuel-based electricity. The results of Nordic electricity were
normalised to 4REC calculated in the Swedish context (base case).
3. Results and discussion
3.1. Environmental impact assessment
The results of the LCA analysis for the eight impact categories studied
are given in Table 2 for no recycling of the adsorbent (NREC), 1 cycle
(1REC), and 4 cycles (4REC). The results for each stage are given in
Table S8. Results of the total impacts for other impact categories that are
not the main objective of this eight study are also given Table S8. It can
be seen from Table 2 that the differences between NREC, 1REC, and
4REC are almost insigniﬁcant in absolute terms, which means that there
was no signiﬁcant increase in the total impact in any of the categories
when recycling was carried out 4 times. This indicates that the stages
contributing most to the total impact are Stages I-III.
Fig. 3 shows the relative impact of each stage normalised to the total
impact of 4REC, in order to facilitate comparisons across the various
impact categories. It can be seen that the stage contributing most to the
total impact in each impact category is Stage I, i.e., the synthesis of the
STNTs, while Stages IV-VI had the lowest impact. To determine which of
the inputs in Stage I was the main contributor, the results for electricity,
water and chemicals were expressed as percentages (see Table S9),
showing clearly that electricity accounted for 99% of involvement in the
results obtained for Stage I. This is partly due to the assumption that the
process heat in Stage I was not recovered by heat exchangers (see
Table S1). If some of this heat were to be recovered, the results would be
lower for all the impact categories (see results in Table S12), but Stage I
would still be the hotspot in the production of the STNTs-Ch beads. The
next largest input (<1%) corresponds to the chemicals required, with
TiO2 and NaOH contributing most (Tables S10–S11).
The contributions of the other stages (II-VI) to the total impacts in
each category ranged from 2 to 7% (Table S8). The contributions of each
input in stages II, III and IV-VI were calculated, and are given in
Tables S13–S17. In Stage II, electricity was the main input contributing to
the impact categories, apart from global warming, where the chemicals
contributed 70% (Table S13). Of this 70%, 62% was derived from chi-
tosan production (Table S14). NaOH and HCl had values of 5.3% and 3%,
respectively. In Stages IV-VI, all the contributions derived mainly from
the chemicals (see Table S16). The largest contribution was from NaOH
for all the impact categories, in particular marine and freshwater eutro-
phication, global warming, and human carcinogenic toxicity (see
Table S17). This indicates that the amount of NaOH used in the process
should be carefully monitored. Based on the overall results for each stage,
it can be seen that electricity was the greatest contributor to the impact in
all the categories studies. This may be the result of the preceding pro-
cesses leading up to the production of electricity, which depends on the
production method and the grid mix (see sensitivity analysis below).
Fig. 4 compares the impacts (normalised to 4REC) when the nano-
adsorbent is recycled and compared to a non-recycling scenario (NREC).
The difference between Table 2 and Fig. 4 is that the results in Table 2 are
absolute values, while Fig. 4 gives relative values. If the adsorbent is
recycled once (1REC) and 4 times (4REC), this implies it is used 2 and 5
times, respectively, whereas NREC indicates single use. To give a total of
5 uses, for the 1REC and NREC cases, the adsorbent would be produced
2.5 and 5 times, respectively. As the synthesis stage (Stage I) contributed
the most to the total of impacts, the impacts of NREC and 1REC depicted
in Fig. 4 appear to increase proportionally. Although a virgin adsorbent
provides 100% adsorption, compared to 86% with an adsorbent recycled
4 times, the environmental impacts are clearly increased when the
adsorbent is not recycled. These results demonstrate the importance of
recycling in order to reduce the impact on the environment. It should also
be noted that 100% adsorption capacity would only be important when
high levels of Cd (e.g. >10 mg L-1) are present in the wastewater.

3.2. Implications and challenges in optimizing energy consumption
This study has shown that electricity is the hotspot in the process,
which is consistent with other LCA studies on nanoadsorbents (Gifford
et al., 2016). reported a high energy demand (>50% of the total) in the
production of a Ti-AX resin, 3.6 kWh⋅kg-1 Ti-AX, with precipitated
nano-TiO2. Kazemi et al. (2018) described the synthesis of two nano-
adsorbents on lab scale – thiol-functionalized magnetic graphene oxide
(MGO–NH–SH)
and
superparamagnetic
Fe3O4@SiO2
(Fe3O4@-
SiO-NH-SH) – and found that at least 4–5 MWh⋅kg-1 was required. The
synthesis of nano-TiO2 produced on industrial scale from titanium oxy-
sulphate has been reported to require 28 kWh⋅kg-1 TiO2 (Car-
amazana-Gonzalez et al., 2017), while the production of nanospheres
coated with silica on lab scale (HSNSs), required 900 kWh⋅kg-1 HSNSs
(Gao et al., 2013).
High electricity consumption has not only been reported in the pro-
duction of nanomaterial-based adsorbents. Nishikawa et al. (2018) re-
ported that the electricity consumed in production contributed 99% to
the total impacts for an adsorbent based on AEW. We calculated energy
consumption to be 22 MWh⋅kg-1 AEW. By extrapolating our results, we
found that the electricity required to produce 1 kg of STNTs was 167
MWh (41.7 kWh per 0.25 g STNTs, Table 1), which is even higher than in
the other studies cited. Although electricity consumption is certainly
overestimated when extrapolating data, these comparisons indicate the
signiﬁcant need to optimise electricity use.
Electricity can be optimised in Stage I by modifying the synthesis of
the STNTs. The electricity demand could be reduced by reducing the
temperature of synthesis or reaction time; however, the change must not
compromise the morphology of the nanotubes in order to ensure a fair
quality of the STNTs. STNTs are hollow nanotubes and the curling of the
nanotubes is only achieved at both high temperature and high alkalinity
(Zhang et al., 2005). The concentration of NaOH used in the reaction
could also be changed. Increasing or decreasing the concentration of
NaOH could speed up or slow down the reaction, thereby affecting the
reaction time and the energy demand. A third approach is to optimise the
recycling of the STNTs-Ch beads. Although Stages IV-VI do not contribute
signiﬁcantly to the total impact, increasing the number of times the
STNTs-Ch beads are successfully recycled would extend the lifetime of
the nanoadsorbent and indirectly result in a lower production cost.
The impact of the process could also be reduced by recycling NaOH,
as it is used in almost all the stages in this process. The molar concen-
trations of NaOH in Stages II, IV and VI are 50–100 times lower than in
Stage I (10 M NaOH), so the waste alkaline water from Stage I could be
used in the subsequent stages to reduce the total amount of NaOH used.
The feasibility of this would have to be carefully investigated, as the
concentrations of both Naþ and OH are important in maintaining the
structure of the nanotubes (Wang et al., 2013).
3.3. Comparison of the environmental impacts of the nanoadsorbent with
other adsorbents
Table 3 presents the results for all the impact categories for the syn-
thesis (Stages I-II) and use (Stage III) of the nanoadsorbent compared
with the synthesis and use of the other adsorbents: Ti-AX, AEW, and GAC.
GAC had the lowest impacts during synthesis in all impact categories. If
we assume that the mass calculated for GAC is the same as that needed for
GAC-CCAB from Chemviron Carbon AB, the impact on global warming
would be 0.029 kg CO2eq for virgin carbon production (7 kg CO2⋅kg-1
GAC), which is similar to the value obtained for GAC. The impact of the
synthesis of the resin Ti-Ax on global warming is almost twice as high as
that of its use, and both (synthesis and use) are higher than those of the
nanoadsorbent. For example, the values for the use of the materials are
0.4 kg CO2eq and 8⋅10-3 CO2eq for the Ti-Ax resin and the STNTs-Ch
beads, respectively. The Ti-AX resin was also associated with higher
marine eutrophication emissions than the nanoadsorbent.
The impact of the use of the AEW adsorbent is higher than that of its
synthesis. The impact on global warming was signiﬁcantly higher than
for the nanoadsorbent for both synthesis and use, but the impact on
human toxicity was lower for AEW. While the results for the Ti-Ax resin,
AEW, and the nanoadsorbent indicate that electricity requirement is the
main contribution to the impacts, it should be noted that it does not arise
from the same stage. The hotspot for the nanoadsorbent and for Ti-AX is
the synthesis stage, whereas the hotspot for AEW is the use stage, ac-
counting for 77% of the total energy consumption. This arises from
drying and calcinating the AEW after use, which was the treatment
before disposal in a landﬁll. It should be noted that a pre-treatment
before disposal was not considered for Ti-AX and the nanoadsorbent,
thereby explaining a lower impact of the use stage. Another factor that
should be considered is that the comparisons were based on the
adsorption capacity of each adsorbent, which implies that different
masses were needed in the FU. In some cases, e.g. GAC and AEW (both C-
based adsorbents), higher masses are required to remove equimolar
amounts of Cd than in the case of the nanoadsorbent, i.e. 13- and 24-
times higher mass, as these adsorbents have a lower adsorption capac-
ity. This is simply due to differences in the adsorption mechanism, which
are based on the metal-adsorbent interactions on the surface of the
material.
Recycling procedures are already reported in the literature, e.g. resins
can be regenerated using salts and heat (Chandrasekara and Pashley,
2015) and/or by using strong acids or bases (Cavaco et al., 2007), and
carbon-based adsorbents are thermally reactivated (Çeçen, 2000).
However, LCA-related data to these processes is not always reported. We
encourage future studies of new materials to include recycling in their
reports, in order to provide sets of comparable data.
Overall, it can be noted that energy demand is the common denom-
inator in optimizing the processes. This is relevant for the synthesis phase
in some cases (STNTs-Ch beads and Ti-AX) and in the use phase for the
AEW adsorbent. When scaling up, there could be additional processes
that might add even more the energy consumption (Piccinno et al.,
2016). Based on the synthesis procedures, we expect the following steps
will require additional equipment, emphasizing more in the STNTs-Ch
beads: 1) ﬁltration and/or centrifugation of the STNTs and STNTs-Ch
beads, 2) pumping of the solutions (e.g. NaOH, H2O, HCl, dissolved
chitosan) in all cases most of the stages, 3) use of a sonicator to
homogenise the STNTs-Chitosan solution before gelling in Stage II, and
4) heat exchangers to recover the heat in Stage I. Another very important
factor in nanomaterial manufacturing is the need for in situ monitoring
equipment, enabling control of product quality (Charitidis et al., 2014).
Some steps that would imply energy use in large-scale applications of

Ti-AX and AEW (not accounted for in the data presented) include the

3.4. Sensitivity analysis comparing Swedish to nordic electricity mix
The sensitivity analysis showed signiﬁcant differences between the
Swedish and Nordic electricity mixes. Fig. 5 shows the results using the
Swedish and Nordic electricity mixes for the 4REC case. More informa-
tion on the data used in the sensitivity analysis and the results for all
impact categories for the Nordic countries are given in Tables S18–19.
Fig. 5 shows that the impact on global warming (GW) and freshwater
eutrophication (FE) increased when the Nordic electricity mix was
assumed, rather than Swedish electricity. There were no signiﬁcant dif-
ferences in the other categories. About 80% of Sweden's electricity is
produced from nuclear and hydroelectric power, with low percentages
from wind power and biomass-fuelled combined heat and power plants
(Birgitta Resvik, 2017). This explains the lower carbon dioxide emissions
and therefore lower impact on global warming, as electricity production
in Sweden is almost fossil-free (Byman, 2016). Emission of CO2 equiva-
lents per kWh electricity in Sweden was calculated and found to be 16 g
CO2-eq/kWh, whereas the ﬁgure for Nordic electricity was 25 g
CO2-eq/kWh, approximately 1.5 times higher than that of Sweden (in
agreement with the results shown in Fig. 5). The carbon dioxide emis-
sions from Eastern Europe are 693 g CO2-eq/kWh, 43 times higher than in
Sweden, indicating a high proportion of fossil fuels, especially coal, in
electricity generation (Moreno Ruiz et al., 2018). Apart from illustrating
the differences in emissions from electricity production in various Eu-
ropean countries, these results also indicate that there are areas where
the production of nanomaterial-based adsorbents is not feasible on a full
scale.
4. Conclusions
A life cycle assessment of a nanomaterial-based adsorbent, STNTs-Ch
beads, developed to adsorb Cd in wastewater, was carried out, based on
lab scale data. The hotspot of the system, including production, use and
recycling, was synthesis of the nanomaterial. The main input in this stage
was electricity. This means that measures should be taken to improve
energy efﬁciency and to choose energy sources with low environmental
impact when scaling-up this process.
A comparison of our ﬁndings with those concerning other adsorbents
showed that GAC produced on commercial scale had the lowest envi-
ronmental impacts. When electricity demands for the production of the
materials were compared, lab scale studies were prone to overestimate
the energy use and should be constantly determined and adjusted. In the
ideal scenario, we expect that energy use can be optimised in large-scale
production by adding energy recovery in the overall production system.
This study showed that electricity was the main input contributing to the
impacts in a lab scale production, so the contribution of the chemicals
could have been overlooked (Stage I) and could potentially become
important later. Based on the disaggregated data, we recommend that
especially NaOH and TiO2 should also be monitored and action taken to
reduce their impacts if needed.
The ﬁndings of this study also conﬁrmed the importance of recycling,
as the environmental impacts decreased signiﬁcantly when the adsorbent

was recycled and reused several times. Finally, the sensitivity analysis
showed that CO2 emissions from Swedish electricity were considerably
lower than those from the Nordic electricity mix and electricity generated

Future LCA studies should also include the end-of-life phase together
with the maintenance when the system is developed further towards a
potential commercial application leading to generation of new and
complementary data. These phases were not included in this study due to
the lack of data in the current scientiﬁc literature. Finally, lab scale data
are more uncertain than data from large-scale commercial production, so
the results should be interpreted with caution.

interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to inﬂuence
the work reported in this paper.
