records.full_text:
Environmental Research 231 (2023) 116143
Available online 13 May 2023
0013-9351/© 2023 Elsevier Inc. All rights reserved.
Graphene and biochar-based cathode catalysts for microbial fuel cell: 
Performance evaluation, economic comparison, environmental and 
future perspectives 
Anil Dhanda a, Rishabh Raj b, S.M. Sathe a, B.K. Dubey a,b, M.M. Ghangrekar a,b,* 
a Department of Civil Engineering, Indian Institute of Technology Kharagpur, Kharagpur, 721302, West Bengal, India 
b School of Environmental Science and Engineering, Indian Institute of Technology Kharagpur, Kharagpur, 721302, West Bengal, India   
A R T I C L E  I N F O   
Keywords: 
Biochar 
Catalysis 
Economic analysis 
Graphene 
Life cycle analysis 
Microbial fuel cell 
Waste to wealth 
Wastewater treatment 
A B S T R A C T   
Microbial fuel cells (MFCs) have been the prime focus of research in recent years because of their distinctive 
feature of concomitantly treating and producing electricity from wastewater. Nevertheless, the electrical per­
formance of MFCs is hindered by a protracted oxygen reduction reaction (ORR), and often a catalyst is required 
to boost the cathodic reactions. Conventional transition metals-based catalysts are expensive and infeasible for 
field-scale usage. In this regard, carbon-based electrocatalysts like waste-derived biochar and graphene are used 
to enhance the commercialisation prospects of MFC technology. These carbon-catalysts possess unique properties 
like superior electrocatalytic activity, higher surface area, and high porosity conducive to ORR. Theoretically, 
graphene-based cathode catalysts yield superior results than a biochar-derived catalyst, though at a higher cost. 
In contrast, the synthesis of waste-extracted biochar is economical; however, its ability to catalyse ORR is 
debatable. Therefore, this review aims to make a side-by-side techno-economic assessment of biochar and 
graphene-based cathode catalyst used in MFC to predict the relative performance and typical cost of power 
recovery. Additionally, the life cycle analysis of the graphene and biochar-based materials has been briefly 
discussed to comprehend the associated environmental impacts and overall sustainability of these carbo- 
catalysts.   
1. Introduction 
Microbial fuel cell (MFC), the first derivative of the bio­
electrochemical system, is well known owing to its capability of pro­
ducing one-step bio–electricity from organic sources, such as municipal 
wastewater, industrial wastewater, and sewage sludge (Logan et al., 
2006). Treating wastewater using conventional aerobic processes is 
energy–intensive, with 60% of the total energy demand of conventional 
wastewater treatment plants arising from the aeration requirements. 
Additionally, unwarranted sludge production from aerobic treatment 
units incurs surplus handling charges. In contrast, energy-free anaerobic 
degradation of wastewater with minimal sludge production is carried 
out in the anodic chamber of the MFC using electrogenic bacteria as 
bio–catalysts. In fact, the sludge generated in MFC is 1/5th of that pro­
duced by the aerobic biological process; thus, reducing the post–treat­
ment sludge handling costs. Moreover, it is estimated that domestic 
wastewater contains sufficient stored chemical energy (1.9 kWh m−3), 
which is proximate to the energy needed (0.3–2.1 kWh m−3) to treat it 
(Blatter et al., 2021). Hence, MFC can replace the prevailing ener­
gy–intensive treatment of wastewater, since energy intensive waste­
water treatment technologies are not only unsustainable rather seem 
incongruous (Raj et al., 2023). 
The MFC has two distinct chambers, i.e., the anodic and the cathodic 
chambers, which are separated by a proton exchange membrane (PEM). 
The electrodes are linked via an external circuit to facilitate the move­
ment of electrons. Wastewater is fed into the anodic chamber, where the 
exoelectrogenic bacteria (e.g., Geobacter and Shewanella) degrade the 
organic matter and produce e⁻, H+, and CO2. The e⁻ thus generated are 
transferred to the anode either directly or with the help of an electron 
shuttle and ultimately reach the cathode via the external circuit. At the 
same time, the H+ migrate towards the cathodic chamber through the 
PEM. At the cathode, an electron acceptor is required to accept the 
incoming e⁻ and complete the electrical circuit, thus generating elec­
tricity (Logan et al., 2006). Typically, O2 is used as an ultimate electron 
* Corresponding author. Department of Civil Engineering, Indian Institute of Technology Kharagpur, Kharagpur, 721302, West Bengal, India. 
E-mail address: ghangrekar@civil.iitkgp.ac.in (M.M. Ghangrekar).  
Contents lists available at ScienceDirect 
Environmental Research 
journal homepage: www.elsevier.com/locate/envres 
https://doi.org/10.1016/j.envres.2023.116143 
Received 18 March 2023; Received in revised form 11 April 2023; Accepted 13 May 2023

Environmental Research 231 (2023) 116143
2
acceptor due to its easy and abundant availability, and the cathodic 
half–reaction is commonly referred to as the oxygen reduction reaction 
(ORR). Thus, the MFCs offer environmentally friendly energy generation 
directly from wastewater. Moreover, MFC offers an energy–neutral 
route for remediating wastewater and can offset the environmental 
burden associated with conventional wastewater treatment (Pham et al., 
2006). 
For the efficient performance of microbial fuel cells (MFCs), various 
factors should be considered. One crucial factor is the selection of the 
appropriate microbial species, such as Shewanella onediensis, Geobacter 
sulfurreducens, or Bacillus subtilis, among others (Alonso-Lemus et al., 
2022; Kim et al., 2002). Biocatalysts, such as enzymes or nanoparticles, 
may also improve the performance of MFC by increasing the electron 
transfer rate. Additionally, optimising operating conditions such as 
temperature (usually around 30–35 ◦C), pH (around 6.5 to 8.0), and 
salinity can have a significant impact on the efficiency of MFC (Tremouli 
et al., 2017). The choice of cathode catalysts and electrode materials, 
including carbon–based materials like graphite, activated carbon, gra­
phene, and biochar, can also reduce the internal resistance of the MFC 
and enhance its overall performance (Boas et al., 2022). Furthermore, 
the design and configuration of the MFC should be optimised, such as 
maximising the electrode surface area and ensuring that the system has a 
suitable feed flow rate. 
Even though MFCs can harvest energy from wastewater facilely, the 
recovered electricity is insignificant for practical applications (Khan­
delwal et al., 2021). The nominal electricity production can be attrib­
uted to distinctive losses: activation, ohmic, and mass transfer. The 
sluggish electro–kinetics causes activation losses at both electrodes; 
however, it is more perceptible at the cathode owing to the increased 
activation energy required to break the O=O bond during the ORR. 
Therefore, often a catalyst is employed to accelerate the ORR at the 
cathode, lower the overpotential losses, and stimulate the 4 e– pathway 
to maximise the power output. 
Ideally, a cathode catalyst should possess robust catalytic activity 
towards ORR, copious active sites, and bridge mode interaction. Besides, 
strong electrical conductivity, adequate e⁻–accepting capacity to curtail 
ohmic losses, high surface area, porous structure, and a high concen­
tration of positive atomic charge to tackle mass transfer loss are also 
desirable. At the same time, the catalyst must be affordable, readily 
accessible, highly durable and easily synthesisable for large–scale ap­
plications. Conventionally, Pt/C–based catalysts are used in fuel cells 
because of their robust performance, lower activation, and overpotential 
losses. However, the application of these catalysts over the field–scale 
biological fuel cells cannot be implemented because of higher costs and 
low availability (Verma et al., 2020). 
Carbon–based materials like graphite, graphene, carbon nanotubes, 
carbon nanofibers, carbon black, activated carbon, graphitic carbon 
nitride (GCN), and carbon derived from waste materials in the form of 
biochar have been utilised in MFC for varied applications. Extensive 
surface area, high porosity, high electrical conductivity, and inexpen­
siveness make carbonaceous materials lucrative for application as 
cathode catalysts (Chen et al., 2022; Peera et al., 2021; Rai et al., 2022). 
For instance, Chen and co–researchers synthesised GCN doped copper as 
cathode catalyst for MFC. The synthesised catalyst showed an excellent 
performance with 456.98 mW m⁻2 power density, which was attributed 
to higher specific surface area, exceptional electrical conductivity, and 
notable ORR activity (Chen et al., 2023). Apart from catalytic applica­
tions, carbon–based materials are also used to fabricate electrodes and 
cation exchange membranes (CEM) in MFCs (Sonu et al., 2022; ul Haque 
et al., 2023). For example, Yuvan and co–researchers utilised blended 
sewage sludge and coconut shell–derived biochar as a bifunctional 
electrode in an air cathode MFC, which yielded 2.4 times higher power 
density (969 mW m−2) than the MFC having conventional graphite 
anode and Pt cathode (Yuan et al., 2015). Thus, proving the efficiency of 
biochar as a low–cost electrode material in MFC, compared to the costly 
graphitic anode and Pt cathode. 
Furthermore, Chakraborty and co–researchers synthesised a low­
–cost PEM using food waste–derived biochar amended with sulphona­
tion and supported on polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) matrix. The proton 
conductivity of the sulfonated biochar amended membrane was 0.077 S 
cm–1, which is 85.50% of the Nafion membrane (0.09 S cm–1) (Chak­
raborty et al., 2020b). The cost analysis revealed that the biochar 
membrane has 32 times higher proton conductivity per unit cost (0.42 S 
cm−1 $−1). Likewise, Ayyaru and co–researchers synthesised sulfonated 
graphene oxide (SGO) modified SPEEK membrane (Ayyaru and Ahn, 
2021). The addition of graphene oxide (GO) incorporated the –OH and 
–COOH groups, increasing proton conductivity through the vehicular 
mechanism by up–taking the water molecules. The MFC utilizing the 
synthesised membrane (SPEEK + 2% by weight SGO) produced a 
maximum power density of 1028 mW m−2, which was two-folds higher 
than MFC with Nafion 115 membrane, thus proving its efficacy to be 
cast–off as a separator in MFC (Ayyaru and Ahn, 2021). Though the 
proton transfer efficiency of the membrane is vital to the power pro­
ductivity of dual chamber MFC, the power output is predominantly 
pivoted on the ability of the cathode to reduce O2. The high activation 
losses at the cathode slow down the ORR rate, which plummets the 
power output. Many efforts have been made to accelerate the ORR by 
applying metallic cathode catalysts and their composites (Peera et al., 
2021). 
Nevertheless, these metallic catalysts are expensive and prone to 
poisoning by ionic species such as CO and S2⁻. These ionic species get 
adsorbed on the surface of the catalyst and block the active catalytic 
sites, which forbids their practical application in MFCs (Yang et al., 
2018). Carbon–derived catalysts have been investigated as a cost­
–effective and eco–friendly substitute for conventional cathode cata­
lysts. Especially graphene is an attractive carbon–based catalyst due to 
its excellent physicochemical properties like high surface area, porosity, 
and electrical conductivity that are known to boost the ORR activity 
yielding enhanced power output in MFC (Kannan and Gnana kumar, 
2016). Despite the remarkable electrocatalytic activity, graphene has a 
chemical–intensive and cumbersome synthesis procedure, severely 
affecting its long–term economic feasibility. 
Recently, waste biomass–derived biochar has been investigated as a 
cathode catalyst, which can arguably reduce the operating cost of MFCs 
(Chakraborty et al., 2020c). Biochar catalysts are much more inexpen­
sive owing to raw materials abundance and free availability; however, 
their catalytic performance is impeded due to the non–uniformity of 
precursors and the corresponding inherent impurities. These drawbacks 
of biochar can be overcome by thermochemical activation, which ac­
centuates the active sites by enlarging the surface area of the char par­
ticles and removing residual impurities. However, the activation of 
biochar incurs an additional cost that may not be economically 
competitive. The distinct properties of biochar and graphene–based 
catalysts pertaining to MFC can be visualised from Table 1. 
Graphene and biochar–based cathode catalysts are widely exploited 
for application in MFCs. A bibliographic analysis of the keyword “gra­
phene as a catalyst in microbial fuel cells” results in 189 investigations, 
whereas “biochar as a catalyst in microbial fuel cell” results in 26 doc­
uments in the Scopus database. As presented in Fig. 1, the variation of 
published articles can be seen year–wise. The primary research on gra­
phene and biochar catalysts in MFC bloomed after 2016 for both cata­
lysts. Many researchers have tried to summarise the importance of these 
cathode catalysts (Kannan and Gnana kumar, 2016; Peera et al., 2021). 
However, to the best of the authors knowledge, a review critically 
focused on performance evaluation, physicochemical characteristics 
and economic assessments are still missing in the literature. 
Therefore, the present review attempts to assess the performance of 
graphene and biochar–based cathode catalysts for application in MFC. 
Also, the current review goes beyond the typical performance–based 
assessment of these carbonaceous cathode catalysts and explores their 
financial aspects to check and compare their suitability for actual ap­
plications. The power production is normalised over the synthesis cost of 
A. Dhanda et al.

Environmental Research 231 (2023) 116143
3
the respective cathode catalyst to compare performance in terms of 
monetary benefits. Moreover, the life cycle aspects of synthesising and 
applying these carbonaceous materials have also been discussed to 
check their environmental suitability. Overall, this review will aid in the 
sustainable development of cathode catalysts for boosting the com­
mercialisation prospects of MFC–based technologies. 
2. Physicochemical characteristics of graphene and 
biochar–based cathode catalysts 
2.1. Graphene 
The crystal structure of GO plays a crucial role in enhancing the 
performance of MFCs. The X–ray diffraction (XRD) analysis is a powerful 
tool for evaluating minerals, unknown materials, and their crystal 
structure. The XRD spectra exhibit sharp peaks at 10.50◦and 42.50◦, 
corresponding to (001) and (100) planes of GO, indicating the destruc­
tion of the graphite (precursor for GO) crystal structure (Gnana kumar 
et al., 2014; Hidayah et al., 2017). In XRD spectra of rGO, two peaks are 
observed around 24◦and 43.50◦, corresponding to the (002) and (100) 
planes, signifying the restoration of the graphitic structure. The resto­
ration of the graphitic structure in rGO is attributed to removal of 
oxygen–containing groups from GO, resulting in a more crystalline 
structure with higher electrical conductivity, porosity, and surface area 
(Sengupta et al., 2018). Restoration of crystallinity can be further vali­
dated by the Raman spectra in which the decreased ID/IG ratio indicates 
the presence of the ordered sp2 carbons (Sengupta et al., 2018). The 
enhanced electrical conductivity of the restored graphitic structure of 
GO contributes to the higher e⁻ mobility, thus facilitating the exchange 
of e⁻ at the electrode with a minimal charge transfer resistance. At the 
same time, higher porosity allows easy oxygen diffusion, whereas higher 
surface area provides a greater number of active catalytic sites, which 
are an essential requirement of a cathode catalyst in MFCs (Verma et al., 
2020). 
The Fourier–transform infrared spectroscopy (FT–IR) analysis is used 
to examine the structure and composition of materials. For GO, the 
broad peak between 3000 and 3800 cm−1 corresponds to the –OH group, 
and peaks at 1733, 1404, and 1227 cm−1 correspond to carbonyl, car­
boxylic, and epoxy groups, respectively (He et al., 2015; Sengupta et al., 
2018). However, reducing GO to rGO at low temperatures (up to 450 ◦C) 
results in significantly fewer peaks for the –OH group and dampened 
peaks ascribing to the epoxy group. Only higher temperatures above 
1000 ◦C can remove these groups from the GO matrix (Sengupta et al., 
2018). The long–range conjugated network of the graphitic lattice is 
predominantly responsible for the conductivity of graphene. Function­
alisation with the epoxy and –OH groups during graphite conversion to 
GO disrupts the conjugated structure and localises e⁻, reducing carrier 
mobility and concentration (Pei and Cheng, 2012). Thus, removing 
these oxygen–containing groups from GO leads to enhanced electrical 
conductivity due to higher e⁻ mobility caused by the material 
approaching the sp2 hybridisation that is favourable for enhancing the 
ORR activity (Lazarte et al., 2018; Pattanayak et al., 2019). 
The X–ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS) analysis of graphene 
provides insights into the changes in its chemical structure before and 
after reduction. The C1s spectra of GO show a peak around 284 eV, 
corresponding to C–C and C=C groups, which signifies sp2 hybridisation 
(Drewniak et al., 2016). Reduction of GO significantly sharpens these 
peaks, indicating a more stable structure in rGO. On the other hand, 
peaks representing oxygen–containing functional groups (around 532 
eV), such as the –OH group and epoxy group C–O bond, get dampened in 
rGO (Drewniak et al., 2016; Stankovich et al., 2007). The elimination of 
oxygen–containing groups imparts hydrophobicity to the material and 
improves its conductivity, which is a critical requirement of an ORR 
catalyst. The thermal reduction under the Nitrogen atmosphere or 
external Nitrogen source may also incorporate the peaks corresponding 
to N1s (near 400 eV). The N1s spectra can be deconvoluted into 
graphitic N, pyrrolic N, and pyridinic N. All these species act as active 
ORR site and helps to accelerate cathodic reactions in MFC (Verma et al., 
2020). The advantages and limitations of graphene–based catalysts can 
be visualised in Fig. 2.

A. Dhanda et al.

Environmental Research 231 (2023) 116143
4
2.2. Biochar 
The carbon–rich substance generated via pyrolysis with little or no 
oxygen supply is known as biochar. During the carbonisation of biomass 
at higher temperatures, dehydrogenation occurs, which makes biochar a 
highly porous and carbon–rich material (Sharma et al., 2015). The 
physicochemical properties of the biochar also depend primarily on the 
precursor material and synthesis temperature. However, the catalytic 
properties of biochar are contingent upon accessibility to active catalytic 
sites disturbed across the internal pores. They often require thermo­
chemical activation to accentuate the catalytic activity (Anto et al., 
2021). 
Before activation, biochar exhibits poor sorption of pollutants owing 
to a relatively less surface area due to the presence of condensates, in­
organics, and clogged pores (Hagemann et al., 2018). Broadly, biochar is 
activated by physical treatment using steam and CO2 or by applying 
chemicals like H2O2, KOH, ZnCl2, and K2CO3 (Anto et al., 2021). 
Physical activation via steam or CO2 effectively removes trapped im­
purities and volatile compounds from the precursor feedstock. However, 
physically activated biochar exhibits a limited increase in specific sur­
face area and pore volume compared to chemically activated biochar 
(Panwar and Pawar, 2022). In contrast, chemical activation techniques 
yield higher–quality biochar, which outperforms physically activated 
biochar in different environmental applications, such as the adsorption 
of contaminants and catalytic remediation of wastewater (Wang and 
Wang, 2019). 
Alkali modification of biochar using NaOH and KOH is simple and 
highly effective in enriching the physicochemical properties of biochar; 
thus, it is a widely adopted activation process. The alkali–processed 
biochar yields a much higher surface area enlargement owing to the 
effect of chemical etching compared to physically activated biochar. In 
an investigation, pyrolysis of KOH–activated corn straw biochar at 
800 ◦C under an N2 atmosphere soared the specific surface area from 
114.36 m2 g−1 to 2183.80 m2 g−1 (Qu et al., 2021). This drastic jump in 
the surface area could be because of the crystalline deposition of KOH in 
the pores of biochar entailed by the etching of C atoms at higher tem­
peratures by alkaline compounds like K2CO3, K2O, and K formed due to 
the breakdown of KOH. Also, diffusion of CO, CO2 and other gases may 
assist in the formation of micropores on the surface of KOH blended 
biochar (Qu et al., 2021). Moreover, alkali–treated biochar displays 
increased oxygen–containing functional groups, carbonyl groups, 
carboxyl groups, and aromatic structures (Sun et al., 2015; Zhao et al., 
2020a). These functional groups impart higher adsorption capacity to 
biochar that can facilitate the surface binding of pollutants in aqueous 
media. 
In the XRD diagram of unadulterated biochar (e.g., banana peels), a 
broad peak from 22◦to 28◦(002) is observed, corresponding to the 
stacking structure of aromatic carbon layers. The peak broadening is 
observed due to smaller–size crystallites perpendicular to aromatic 
layers (Liu et al., 2012). Some sharp peaks can also be observed due to 
the presence of SiO2, CaO, and MgO in the precursor material (e.g., 26◦
for silica in sludge–derived biochar). Furthermore, pyrolysis tempera­
ture plays a determining role in the graphitisation of biochar. Higher 
synthesis temperature yields char with a higher degree of graphitisation, 
as evident from the sharp XRD peak at around 25.5◦(Chatterjee et al., 
2020). In contrast, a lower temperature yields a comparatively broader 
peak. The high graphitisation pattern at higher temperatures can also be 
affirmed by a low ID/IG value obtained from the synthesised Raman 
spectra of the biochar at temperatures above 900 ◦C (Chakraborty et al., 
2020c). 
Graphitised biochar exemplifies superior electrical conductivity, a 
desirable property of cathode catalysts functionalised with biochar. 
Biochar with a highly graphitised structure displays a sharp C–C bond 
peak in the XPS spectrum. In the biochar containing N, the XPS spectra 
exhibited N1s peak, C1s and O1s peak. The presence of N1s peak adds to 
the enhanced ORR activity of the catalyst because of the presence of 
pyridinic (398 eV) and graphitic N (400 eV), which offers more active 
sites and lower mass transfer losses (Chakraborty et al., 2020a). The 
physicochemical properties of biochar are conducive for facilitating 
ORR and can be easily tailored for application as a cathode catalyst in 
MFCs. The advantages and limitations of biochar–based catalysts can be 
visualised in Fig. 3. 
3. Application of graphene and biochar–based cathode catalyst 
in MFC 
3.1. Graphene 
The GO and rGO–based materials are becoming promising as cathode 
catalysts because their higher specific surface area provides copious 
active sites, high electrical conductivity, and inherent properties to 
promote a 4 e−ORR pathway in MFC. Using a facile and cost–effective 
method, Meng Li and co–researchers synthesised a novel GO catalyst 
coupled with magnesium oxide nanocomposite (GO/MgO). The MFC 
using standalone MgO–catalysed cathode produced the maximum 
power density of 350.44 mW m−2, which was 1.92 times more power 
output as compared to MFC using GO–catalyst (Li et al., 2017). To 
further enhance the electrical performance, GO/MgO composite was 
utilised as a cathode catalyst that resulted in 755.63 mW m−2 power 
density, which was 1.12 times the power density of pristine GO and only 
0.86-times than that of commercial Pt/C catalysed MFC. It was 
concluded from the EIS spectra that the charge transfer resistance of the 
GO/MgO catalyst was 3.2 Ω, which was nearly 3–folds lower than that of 
MgO (9.5 Ω) and 1.94–folds lower than GO (6.2 Ω). The decreased 
charge transfer resistance showed that the composite might have 
improved the electrical conductivity, robust oxygen transport from the

environment to the reaction surface, and a low activation energy barrier 
for ORR. The excellent electrical performance of the composite catalyst 
was attributed to its lower charge transfer resistance, higher surface 
area, and superior porous structure, which facilitated greater oxygen 
penetration, resulting in efficient charge migration (Li et al., 2017). 
It has been observed that GO has a reduced electrical conductivity, 
and consequently, poorer electrocatalytic activity is encountered. 
Further enhancement in conductivity can be achieved by reducing the 
GO and further doping and making composites of rGO with transition 
metals. The added heteroatoms can transform the 2D graphene sheets 
into 3D macromolecular assemblies such as foams, sponges, or aerogels. 
These 3D graphene–based macromolecular assemblies (GMAs) can 
rapidly transport ions and electrons, enabling the development of 
renewable energy devices and systems with remarkable energy den­
sities. In one investigation, rGO coupled with N and S co–doping pro­
duced a two–times more volumetric power density (14 W m–3) than MFC 
utilizing Pt/C catalyst (Verma et al., 2020). The cyclic voltammetry 
analysis showed superior activity with a higher current response and 
positive onset potential as the concentration of N and S increased in the 
rGO matrix. Additionally, the charge transfer resistance calculated from 
electrical impedance spectroscopy analysis was lower (5.5 Ω) for rGO 
doped with a higher concentration of N and S compared to catalyst with 
a lower concentration of heteroatoms doped rGO (14.8 Ω) and even Pt/C 
(13.0 Ω). The higher electrochemical response and electrical perfor­
mance of catalysed MFC were attributed to the interconnected meso­
porous structure of rGO and incorporation of heteroatoms, which 
promoted rapid oxygen diffusion, provided highly accessible surface 
area, and offered multidimensional electron transport pathways (Verma 
et al., 2020). 
In a similar investigation by Xin and co–researchers, the rGO was 
mixed with Cu2O for its application as a cathode catalyst in MFC (Xin 
et al., 2020). The MFC catalysed with composite catalyst produced 1362 
mW m−2 power density, nearly 16% higher than the Pt/C catalysed 
cathode used in MFC. The improved performance was attributable to the 
increased surface area of the Cu2O/rGO catalyst, which provided an 
abundance of catalytically active sites and encouraged the 4e−pathway. 
In addition, the reduced War–burg impedance (21.82 Ω) of Cu2O/rGO 
compared to Pt/C (102.10 Ω) suggested the quick diffusion of O2 to the 
cathode surface, hence enhancing the ORR rate. Other GO/rGO–based 
cathode catalysts have also been used in MFC, as summarised in Table 2. 
3.2. Biochar 
The favourable catalytic properties and inexpensive production cost 
of biochar make it a lucrative prospect for large–scale application in 
MFCs, especially as a cathode catalyst and support material. In this re­
gard, Yuan and co–researchers used biochar derived from sewage sludge 
pyrolysed at 900 ◦C, 700 ◦C, and 500 ◦C as a cathode catalyst in a sin­
gle–chambered MFC. The power attained by biochar synthesised at 
900 ◦C (500 mW m−2) was three-times and five-times higher than that 
obtained using biochar pyrolysed at 700 ◦C and 500 ◦C, respectively 
(Yuan et al., 2013). Nevertheless, pristine biochar has a lower surface 
area, which reduces the number of surface–active sites where ORR can 
be facilitated. To increase these active sites, the surface area of biochar is 
expanded via different physical and chemical activation processes. The 
increased surface area helps retain more O2 functional groups like 
carbonyl and carboxylic groups that can enhance the ORR activity and 
ultimately improve the power outcome of MFC. In another investigation, 
biochar synthesised from banana peels was further activated by KOH 
and pyrolysed at 900 ◦C, yielding a maximum power density of 528.2 
mW m–2 when used in MFC (Yuan et al., 2014). Further, the specific 
surface area of banana biochar (172.3 m2 g−1) rose by 65% post­
–thermochemical treatment, attaining 1.3-times higher power density 
than the pristine biochar (Yuan et al., 2014). 
Besides, biochar particles pyrolysed at higher temperatures exhibited 
swifter e−mobility due to graphitisation, imparting excellent electrical 
conductivity. Consequently, the ohmic and charge transfer resistance of 
biochar–catalysed MFC reduced significantly as the synthesis tempera­
ture was raised. Zhong and co–researchers observed that increasing the 
pyrolysis temperature of watermelon rind–derived biochar from 400 ◦C 
to 700 ◦C reduced both ohmic and charge transfer resistances, with 
levels dropping from 34.35 Ω to 8.85 Ω and 47.33 Ω–20.63 Ω, respec­
tively (Zhong et al., 2019). The higher ORR activity of the watermelon 
rind–derived biochar synthesised at 700 ◦C was further reflected by the 
superior power output of MFC generating an operating voltage of 0.27 V, 
which was 1.33-times higher compared to the voltage output of MFC

having biochar catalyst synthesised at 400 ◦C (Zhong et al., 2019). Other 
biochar–modified MFCs have also shown similar observations, empha­
sising the crucial role of pyrolysis temperature in improving the ORR 
activity of biochar (Chakraborty et al., 2020a; Li et al., 2018). 
The usual two–step thermochemical activation of biochar is a 
cumbersome process that incurs additional synthesis costs. Recently, a 
one–step activation and synthesis method for biochar using K3[Fe 
(C2O4)3].3H2O at 800 ◦C, with eggplant serving as the precursor, was 
proposed (Zha et al., 2020). The activated eggplant biochar catalyst 
possessed a high specific surface area of 1137 m2 g−1, a greater degree of 
graphitisation and an ‘n’ value (indicating the number of electrons 
transferred in redox reaction) of 3.9, indicating the 4e−ORR pathway 
(Zha et al., 2020). Remarkably, with the same catalyst loading of 0.5 mg 
cm−2, the eggplant biochar–coated cathode in MFC achieved a 
maximum power density of 667 mW m−2, surpassing the Pt/C (20% by 
weight) cathode catalyst used in MFC (621 mW m−2) (Zha et al., 2020). 
In addition to being a self–standing cathode catalyst, biochar can also 
function as a robust framework to support and ameliorate the catalytic 
activity of metallic catalysts. Distributing and fixing transition metals on 
the porous carbon structures can induce a synergistic effect on ORR 
activity by intensifying surface–active sites, promoting the mass transfer 
and flow of e−(Shibuya et al., 2018). Concurring results were obtained 
by doping magnetised manganese into watermelon rind–derived bio­
char, when used in MFC, attained a maximum power density of 399.3 
mW m−2 and internal resistance of just 15.24 Ω (Jiang et al., 2022). The 
excellent electrochemical performance could be attributed to the 
inherent presence of nitrogen elements in the watermelon rind, resulting 
in a relatively high content of pyridine nitrogen in the synthesised bio­
char. Additionally, doping of Fe and Mn followed by pyrolysis at 900◦
pronounced the active surface area of the doped catalyst from 214.32 
m2 g−1 to 657.6 m2 g−1, thereby ensuring a more significant number of 
active sites for facilitating ORR (Jiang et al., 2022). 
In one investigation, tea residue–derived biochar co–doped with Co 
and N exhibited excellent performance as a cathode catalyst in an air 
cathode MFC when fed with high–strength real swine wastewater, 
notably in terms of electrical performance (Zhu et al., 2022). The bio­
char–modified MFC was endowed with excellent O2 reduction activity 
due to the presence of Co–Nx and pyridinic N, manifesting a maximum 
power density of 748.9 mW m−2 and a maximum output voltage of 
0.529 V (Zhu et al., 2022). The microbial community analysis of biochar 
catalysed MFC revealed dominance for electricity producers, denitrifies 
and sulphate reducers that abated chemical oxygen demand, Total Ni­
trogen, and ammonia nitrogen (NH4
+–N) content reduction by 81.8%, 
33.5%, and 35%, respectively (Zhu et al., 2022). Though bio­
mass–derived chars impregnated with metals vastly improve the ORR 
activity, the fabrication cost of the composite catalyst also shoots up. 
This may undermine the significance of biochar as a cost–effective 
alternative to conventional cathode catalysts. Hence the biochar–metal 
composite catalysts should be meticulously optimised for an economical 
operation of the MFC. The performance of other biochar–based catalysts 
used in MFCs is summarised in Table 3. 
4. Performance and economic comparison 
The economic analysis of biochar and graphene as cathode catalysts 
in terms of cost per unit of electricity generated ($ W–1) was carried out 
to compare the performance of both materials. The methodology 
adopted for economic analysis was analogous to the protocol reported 
by Peera et al. (2021) and Xu et al. (2018), except that instead of elec­
tricity generated per unit cost (W $–1), cost per unit of electricity 
generated ($ W–1) was estimated. The power generation (W) was 
calculated based on the power density, area of the cathode electrode, 
and volume reported by different researchers, as presented in Tables 2 
and 3. Further, the cost of the cathode catalyst was estimated based on 
the quantity of catalysts and materials used for catalyst synthesis in each 
investigation separately. As infered from Tables 2 and 3, most studies 
have used either a membrane–less reactor or a Nafion membrane as a 
separator. Hence the performance variations caused by the membrane 
were disregarded. Finally, the cost per unit of electricity generation was 
assessed by dividing the cathode catalyst cost by power produced for 
each study separately. As biochar and graphene costs vary widely from 
place to place and different values have been reported in the literature, 
hence certain assumptions were made as presented below.  
1. Depending on the purity and place of purchase, the cost of GO, rGO, 
and biochar can vary significantly. Hence, to maintain uniformity, 
their cost was estimated manually. In the case of GO, precursor cost 
(i.e., graphite powder etc.) was taken from Sigma Aldrich. The cost of 
power required (for reduction of graphene oxide in tube furnace and 
activation of biochar) was considered according to the industrial cost 
of electricity in India. At the same time, considering the easy and free 
availability of precursors used for biochar, their cost was dis­
regarded. However, the cost of biochar activation via one–step or 
two–step activation procedure was considered, and the cost of ma­
terials used was taken from Sigma Aldrich. It was also discovered

that the estimated costs were in excellent accord with those stated by 
Sharma et al. (2021) in the case of graphene.  
2. The rGO and biochar yield using thermal reduction/activation was 
assumed to be 30%, which is commonly observed in past in­
vestigations at temperatures higher than 500 ◦C (Tomczyk et al., 
2020). Further, biochar and graphene are assumed to be activate­
d/reduced using the thermal method in all studies to maintain uni­
formity. While other methods, such as chemical or hydrothermal, 
may produce different results, hence the thermal method was used to 
directly compare the performance of rGO and activated biochar.  
3. To maintain price parity, material costs other than GO, rGO and 
biochar (e.g., precursors for transition metals used) were taken from 
the Sigma Aldrich website. The overall cost of a cathode catalyst for 
each investigation was estimated by adding the individual costs of 
precursors used, power used, etc., for synthesising the catalyst.  
4. The electrical performance of the MFC can vary significantly as the 
reactor size increases due to increased losses incurred on both the 
cathodic and anodic sides. To overcome this anomaly and differen­
tiate the performance of graphene and biochar–based catalysts, two 
different reactor sizes were considered, i.e., less than 50 mL anolyte 
volume and more than 50 mL anolyte volume. The sizes of the 
reactor assumed are based on the anolyte volume used in graphene 
and biochar–based investigations reported in the literature (Tables 2 
and 3). 
4.1. Outcomes of the economic analysis 
The average cost per unit of electricity produced ($ W−1) for the 
biochar–based catalyst was determined to be 1.01–41.06 $ W−1 
(average 17.15 $ W−1) for MFC with anolyte volumes under 50 mL and 
11.66–34.85 $ W−1 (average 21.77) for those with anolyte volumes over 
50 mL. In contrast, it was discovered that the cost for graphene–based 
catalysts was 3.11–107.18 $ W−1 (average 32.05 $ W−1) and 3.22–26.96 
$ W−1 (average 16.97 $ W−1) for anolyte volumes of less than 50 mL and 
above 50 mL, respectively. Biochar catalysts cost less per unit of power 
produced when the reactor capacity is less than 50 mL; however, gra­
phene–based catalysts cost less on average when the reactor volume 
exceeds 50 mL. Additionally, it was noted that investigations using 
biochar had somewhat greater loading of cathode catalyst, with most 
using more than 4 mg cm−2 loadings (Table 3). In the investigation re­
ported by Jiang et al. (2022), a sharp decline in performance was 
observed when the cathode catalyst loading was dropped to 2 mg cm−2 
(399 mW m−2) as compared to 1502 mW m−2 with 5 mg cm−2 of catalyst 
loading with the same reactor setup (Ma et al., 2014). Whereas, most of 
the published graphene–based investigations used cathode catalyst 
coatings with less than 3 mg cm−2 of catalyst loading, yielding an equal 
or superior power density to biochar (Table 2). 
The Fig. 4 depicts the cost variation (centre of the circle corre­
sponding to the Y axis) for different catalysts with the power density 
(dia. of the circle) for an anolyte volume less than 50 mL. The colour of 
the circles varies from red to green. It depicts electrical performance 
ranging from poor to excellent, with larger, greener circles on the bot­
tom side of the picture signifying more effective catalysts at lower costs 
and vice versa. Although graphene–based catalysts achieved greater 
power densities, biochar–based catalysts still outperform them due to 
their lower prices and comparable power densities. 
On the other hand, when the anolyte volume was more than 50 mL, 
the graphene–based catalyst performed better than the biochar–based 
catalysts. As highlighted in Fig. 5, graphene–based catalysts offer sub­
stantially higher power densities than biochar–based catalysts and are 
less expensive per unit of power generated. Use of smaller electrodes and 
a significantly lower catalyst loading is responsible for the exceptional 
performance of graphene–based catalysts. On the contrary, for bio­
char–based explorations, a substantially larger electrode area with a 
greater catalyst loading was utilised, which led to a higher amount of 
catalyst being used, which increased the cost per unit of power gener­
ated. As the reactor volumes increase, the graphene–based catalysts 
exhibit encouraging results, which is also desirable for the MFCs to be 
used in field–scale applications. As the electrode area can significantly 
interfere with the power produced, one more criterion, volume/cathode 
electrode area (V/A), was also considered. However, no conclusions 
could be derived, possibly because of the arbitrary use of electrode area 
in the investigations reported. This also necessitates optimising the 
electrode area being used in the MFC. 
5. Environmental impacts of using graphene and biochar–based 
materials 
Graphene and biochar are among the most widely used nano­
materials, especially in environmental applications. Nevertheless, many 
other nanomaterials like carbon nanotubes, nano clays, and nanofibers 
are predicted to exert adverse ecological and health impacts (Kabir et al., 
2018). Therefore, it is necessary to evaluate the environmental burdens

associated with the usage of graphene and biochar. The life cycle 
assessment (LCA) is a reliable tool for identifying the main environ­
mental hotspots during the entire operating cycle of a product or pro­
cess. Thus, LCA interpretations can be utilised for making a more 
informed decision while optimising a system. 
In general, the negative effect of the synthesis and application of 
graphene arises from direct contact with the material itself and the 
process throughout the entire working life. A prospective LCA carried on 
the two main production routes of manufacturing graphene suggested 
that the chemical reduction technique had higher energy use and water 
footprint than the ultrasonication reduction (Arvidsson et al., 2014). The 
potential to recover the spent solvents considerably reduced the envi­
ronmental impacts of the ultrasonication production route (Arvidsson 
et al., 2014). Moreover, an estimated energy requirement in chemical 
exfoliation is about 1000 MJ kg⁻1 of graphene, which is at least twice 
that required in ultrasonication exfoliation. The LCA of graphene and 
activated carbon–based supercapacitor by Cossutta et al. (2020) also 
blamed the energy–intensive production process and poor quality of 
synthesised graphene as the leading cause for increasing environmental 
penalties. Hence, a more sustainable approach is required to minimise 
the ill effects of graphene manufacturing. The chemical vapour deposi­
tion and epitaxial growth methods for graphene synthesis have lower 
energy requirements of 0.2 MJ cm⁻2 and 80 MJ cm⁻2 of substrate area; 
however, these methods require quartz and silicon support that may 
have derived impacts on the ecosystem (Arvidsson, 2017). To further 
alleviate the adverse impacts on human and ecological health, graphene 
can be synthesised using waste biomass. Even though graphene extrac­
ted from non–standard precursors have a lesser environmental footprint, 
any significant application is limited by the presence of defects, 
contamination, and imperfections in morphology (Munuera et al., 
2021). 
On the other hand, biochar is unequivocally acknowledged as a 
green material since it is typically produced from naturally abundant 
waste biomass. In fact, conversion into biochar is a prominent technique 
for reducing carbon footprint and generating bioenergy (Lehmann, 
2007). To highlight, the energy demand of biochar as sorbent was 
16–folds lower than that required for the synthesis of commercial acti­
vated carbon (97 MJ kg⁻1), while the global warming potential of the 
biochar sorbent was −0.9 kg CO2 eq. kg⁻1 (Alhashimi and Aktas, 2017). 
Further, according to an LCA estimate, biochar and bio–oil production 
via pyrolysis of sludge may result in net negative greenhouse gas 
emissions of −0.05 kg CO2 eq. kg⁻1 and −0.31 kg CO2 eq. kg⁻1, respec­
tively (Huang et al., 2022). This clearly indicates that waste–to–biochar 
conversion is an effective strategy to mitigate environmental burdens. 
Past research has already affirmed the pivotal role of biochar catalysts in 
offsetting the deteriorating ecological impacts of biodiesel and mono­
phenol production (anak Erison et al., 2022; Cao et al., 2022). Hence, 
applying biochar–based catalysts can undoubtedly expand the envi­
ronmental benefits of MFC technology. 
6. Future perspective 
There is no gainsaying the fact that MFCs are arguably superior 
anaerobic attached growth wastewater treatment technology owing to 
the one–step electricity production and minimal sludge generation. The 
minimal energy requirement for the operation of MFC makes it a net 
energy–producing system, even though the broader application of this 
innovative technology is restricted due to sluggish ORR. Carbon–based 
cathode catalysts play a critical role in addressing this issue. Graphene 
holds particular importance among numerous carbon–based catalysts 
due to its unique physicochemical characteristics that dramatically 
boost the ORR in MFCs. Graphene–based catalysts have demonstrated 
remarkable efficiency in transferring e−and catalysing oxygen reduc­
tion, inching MFC technology towards commercial application (ElMe­
kawy et al., 2017). Unfortunately, there is only a superficial 
understanding of the electrocatalytic behaviour of graphene and its 
interaction with sessile biofilm. A detailed analysis should assess the 
electrochemistry involved in graphene–catalysed anodes and cathodes. 
In addition, metals and alloys that can enhance the electrocatalytic 
properties of graphene should further be explored to fabricate state­
–of–the–art graphene composites for catalysing ORR. Also, the electrode 
area and graphene conjugation with inexpensive materials require 
optimisation to reduce the catalyst synthesis and electrode cost, which is 
crucial for industrial applicability. 
Alternatively, waste–derived biochar has also been utilised to boost 
the sluggish ORR in MFC. However, further modifications are required 
to bring the biochar–based catalysts efficiency in parity with other 
contemporary and proven metallic cathode catalysts. Furthermore, char 
can be synthesised using waste biomass, often publicised as cheaper and 
lower–footprint material. The lab–scale biochar synthesis blatantly ig­
nores the capital requirement for entailing biomass collection, trans­
portation, 
storage, 
drying, 
and 
grinding/chipping/palletisation, 
resulting in an underestimation of the synthesis cost. An extensive 
investigation is desired to determine the actual costs of biochar catalysts 
to verify the purported sustainability claims of biochar . Future research 
should be oriented towards developing prudent activation techniques to 
synthesise superior quality biochar catalysts for reliable performance. 
Additionally, most of the investigations on graphene and biochar

catalysts have been implemented on benchtop reactors (working volume 
<50 ml). Therefore, more analysis on the pilot– and field–scale pro­
totypes should be conducted. As a result, the transition of MFC to 
standard wastewater management infrastructure can be made more 
accessible, and the capital burden can be lessened. 
Further, the scope of the current investigation is limited to evalu­
ating catalyst costs incurred for unit energy production in MFC. There­
fore, as an extension to this work, a thorough techno–economic 
appraisal of MFCs, including derived benefits such as electricity pro­
duction, wastewater treatment, and minimal sludge handling, can be 
performed in future. Moreover, the techno–economic assessment should 
also be supplemented with the associated environmental cost of 
different stages of MFC operation. Specific LCA of graphene and bio­
char–based cathode catalysts in MFCs has not yet been undertaken. A 
comparative environmental assessment of these carbon–catalysts will 
help to identify critical hotspots entailing the manufacturing and 
application stages. The feedback from LCA investigations can be utilised 
for selecting the best production alternatives of graphene and biochar to 
ensure large–scale sustainable supplies. Additionally, the application of 
graphene and biochar composite as a catalyst in MFC has not been un­
dertaken until now. Considering the outstanding properties of both 
materials, they can prove complementary. Graphene can provide a 
higher surface area and high electrical conductivity, while biochar 
provides necessary nitrogen active sites and reduces the catalyst cost. 
Hence, the graphene–biochar composite can be an excellent ORR cata­
lyst. The budding researchers can take up this research gap in future 
research. 
7. Conclusion 
Emerging carbon–based materials like GO and waste–derived bio­
char have extensive applications in MFCs because of their strong ORR 
affinity, low cost, easy synthesis procedure, and higher specific surface 
area. As a standard convention, waste–derived biochar materials are 
considered as low-cost catalysts and past investigations claimed their 
suitability for field–scale MFCs. However, the economic assessment 
suggested that biochar and graphene costs as cathode catalysts are fairly 
similar when the reactor volume was increased beyond 50 mL. However, 
this might be due to the arbitrary use of electrode area and catalyst 
loading in the investigation reported, which has successively increased 
the quantity of catalyst required. The GO–based catalysts have a sig­
nificant upper hand in this regard and are deemed suitable to be applied 
over the MFCs in field–scale applications. Hence, further extensive 
research with biochar derived from different precursors as sole or doped 
with metals and nitrogen is required in this direction to make an 
authoritative comment on the economic feasibility of both catalysts. 
Aside from that, performance–cum–economic analysis of graphene and 
biochar–based catalysts in field–scale MFC may give a more realistic 
cost.

Journal of Cleaner Production 328 (2021) 129588
Available online 5 November 2021
0959-6526/© 2021 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.
Phosphorus recovery and iron, copper precipitation from swine wastewater 
via struvite crystallization using various magnesium compounds 
Qian Guan a, Guisheng Zeng a,*, Baichuan Gong a, Yingpeng Li a, Haiyan Ji a, Jiefei Zhang a, 
Jiatian Song b, Chunli Liu a, Zhongbing Wang a, Chunjian Deng a 
a National-Local Joint Engineering Research Center of Heavy Metals Pollutants Control and Resource Utilization, Nanchang Hangkong University, Nanchang, 330063, 
China 
b School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Frontiers Science Center for Transformative Molecules, Shanghai Jiao Tong University, Shanghai, 200240, China   
A R T I C L E  I N F O   
Handling Editor: Bin Chen  
Keywords: 
Phosphorus 
Struvite 
Heavy metals 
Magnesium compounds 
Life cycle assessment 
A B S T R A C T   
Swine wastewater contains phosphorus, ammonia nitrogen, and heavy metals that affect the struvite purity 
produced from the wastewater and increase the ecological hazards. In addition, struvite crystallization process is 
highly dependent on the usage of magnesium sources. Thus, it is necessary to explore the effects of three 
magnesium sources, including MgCl2, Mg(OH)2 and MgO, on the phosphorus recovery and minimizing Cu and Fe 
contents via struvite crystallization process. The observations revealed that the predominant parts of Cu and Fe 
precipitate in the form of hydroxide during pH adjustment. Meanwhile, when using MgCl2 as the magnesium 
source, the optimal removal efficiency of phosphorus reached 94.55%, followed by MgO and Mg(OH)2. Simul­
taneously, 90.60% Cu and 98.80% Fe precipitate under MgCl2 as magnesium source. The redissolution behavior 
of Cu occurs when MgO and Mg(OH)2 are used as magnesium sources, increasing the Cu concentration. The 
smallest operation cost is from MgCl2 as magnesium source. The potential environmental impact analyzed by life 
cycle assessment method shows that MgCl2 in struvite crystallization process saves more fossil energy and 
protects resource while lessens the nature’s eutrophication risk. The technical, economic, and environmental 
assessments confirm MgCl2 as a promisable source of magnesium in struvite crystallization process for achieving 
cleaner production goal.   
1. Introduction 
As modern industries and swine farms are expanding in China, huge 
quantities of various kinds of wastewater are produced from commer­
cial, agricultural, food production, and other human activities. It is well 
known that nutrients (i.e., PO4
3−, NH4
+) and organic pollutants are 
abundant in wastewater streams (Hao et al., 2021). The large amounts of 
nutrients and organic pollutants accumulation in the lakes destroy the 
ecosystem (Liu et al., 2021). Therefore, from the environment protection 
and sustainable development perspective, it is urgent to remove organic 
pollutants and recover the nutrients from wastewater (Yao et al., 2021), 
which could make reuse of these resources possible. 
Swine wastewater is a typical product from the rapid expansion of 
the farm in recent years. Some researchers reported that the main 
pollutant of swine wastewater was phosphorus (PO4–P, P), ammonia 
nitrogen (NH4–N, N), heavy metals (HMs), and significant amounts of 
microorganisms (Lv et al., 2018; Qu and Fan, 2010). At present, various 
techniques are used to recover P and N from wastewater to prevent its 
harmful effects on the environment (Guan et al., 2021a; Peng et al., 
2018a). 
Compared with other techniques, chemical precipitation technology 
has the obvious advantages to simultaneously recovering P and N owing 
to the formation of the products like struvite (MgNH4PO4⋅6H2O, mag­
nesium ammonium phosphate, MAP), which can be used as slow release 
fertilizer in agricultural sector (Larsen et al., 2013). The equimolecular 
molar ratio of Mg2+, NH4
+, and PO4
3−could be formed in MAP under 
alkaline conditions based on the following reaction (Eq. (1)). Moreover, 
because of the lower Mg2+ concentration in the swine wastewater, it is 
difficult to form the struvite in natural conditions. It is necessary to 
supply the external magnesium source to maintain a favorable struvite 
formation ratio. Various magnesium resources have been reported that 
form struvite, such as MgCl2 (Zhang et al., 2020), MgSO4 (Lee et al., 
2013), Mg(OH)2 (Rabinovich et al., 2021), MgO (Krishnamoorthy et al., 
2021), and seawater (Shaddel et al., 2020). However, there are no re­
ports about the use of a variety of magnesium sources in the swine

2
wastewater treatment process.  
Mg2+ + NH4
+ + HnPO4
(n−3)– + 6H2O → MgNH4PO4⋅6H2O + nH+ (n =
0,1,2)                                                                                             
(1) 
Heavy metals (Cu2+, Zn2+, Fe3+ and other ones) may also be present 
in the swine wastewater. Some researchers reported the structure of 
struvite as tetrahedral of PO4
3−, tetrahedral of NH4
+, and octahedral of 
Mg(H2O)6
2+, which linking by hydrogen bonds (Tansel et al., 2018). 
This structure provides the convenience of HMs transferring into stru­
vite, which affects the purity of the precipitates. Previous study reported 
that the hexahydrate crystals on the surface of struvite could adsorb HMs 
in the wastewater (Peng et al., 2018b), precipitating struvite together 
with HMs. The effects of Cu2+, Zn2+ (Lu et al., 2020a), and Pb2+ (Lu 
et al., 2020b) on the struvite precipitation were studied. The results 
showed HMs combined with PO4
3−in the wastewater to form HMs–PO4 
compounds, which were enriched on the surface of struvite, affecting the 
purity of struvite. Rouff and Juarez (2014) found that different con­
centrations of Zn ions would provide different sediments on the surface 
of struvite. At low concentrations, the main formation of Zn was 
observed as Zn–PO4, while at high concentrations, the formation of Zn 
was found as Zn–OH compounds. Meanwhile to the best of our knowl­
edge, Cu and Fe co-existence in the wastewater makes the struvite 
crystallizations process more complicated, affecting the composition 
and purity of the final products. The removal operation in the struvite 
crystallization process is highly dependent on the usage of magnesium 
source; thus, it is essential to explore the feasibility and performance of 
recovering phosphorus and minimizing Cu and Fe contents through the 
use of various magnesium resources. The potential environmental im­
pacts of various magnesium sources on the phosphorus recovery and 
minimizing the Cu and Fe contents, in addition to technical feasibility 
study, are also needed to be considered. 
In this study, in order to explore the optimal removal parameters of 
Fe/Cu and P from wastewater, a series of experiments were conducted. 
First, the effects of pH and initial concentrations of Cu/Fe on the struvite 
crystallization process were examined. Next, different magnesium 
sources (i.e., MgCl2, Mg(OH)2, and MgO) were used to remove the Cu, 
Fe, and P through struvite crystallization process. Then, the influence of 
Cu and Fe co-existence state on the structure, morphology, and chemical 
speciation of the precipitates were analyzed with XRD, SEM, and XPS 
analytical techniques. Finally, the life cycle assessment (LCA) method 
was applied to evaluate the environmental benefits of various magne­
sium sources during struvite crystallization process. The results offer 
new insights to recover P and minimize HMs from the swine wastewater, 
which is vital for the future applications in the production of high purity 
struvite. 
2. Materials and methods 
2.1. Materials 
Series experiments were carried out in a jacketed glass crystallizer 
(1000 mL) stirred at 300 rpm at a constant temperature of 298.15 K 
(Guan et al., 2020, 2021b). The swine wastewater samples for the ex­
periments were taken from a dairy products company in the south of 
Jiangxi province, China. The chemical composition of the wastewater is 
presented in Table 1. For facilitating the research, the concentrations of 
Fe3+ and Cu2+ ions in the wastewater were controlled to 5 mg/L through 
addition of commercial CuSO4 and FeCl3 chemicals (Huang et al., 2019). 
The chemical compounds used in the experiments, including magnesium 
chloride hexahydrate (MgCl2⋅6H2O, AR), magnesium hydroxide (Mg 
(OH)2, AR), magnesium oxide (MgO, AR), ammonium chloride (NH4Cl, 
AR), copper sulfate (CuSO4, AR), and ferric chloride (FeCl3, AR) were 
purchased from Aladdin Company (Shanghai, China). Sodium hydroxide 
(NaOH) and hydrochloric acid (HCl) solutions at the concentrations of 
0.5 mM were used to adjust the reactions pH. 
2.2. Struvite crystallization process 
Crystallization process was conducted to investigate the effects of 
reaction pH and initial concentrations of Cu/Fe on the struvite crystal­
lization. Simultaneously, the effects of struvite amount on the removal 
efficiency of heavy metals were also explored. First, a real sample of the 
wastewater was pretreated through suction to remove suspended solids 
and sediments (Huang et al., 2016). Then, 1000 mL of the wastewater 
was transferred into the jacketed glass crystallizer, which was placed on 
the magnetic stirrer. At last, the Mg2+:NH4
+:PO4
3−molar ratio was set 
and controlled at 1:1:1 and the pH at the desired value in the range of 
8.5–11.0 for 60 min. To further determine the effects of the initial 
concentration of Cu/Fe on the struvite crystallization process, it was set 
from 2 to 10 mg/L. Various amount of presynthesized struvite (0.5–3.0 
g) were used in the experiments for removing HMs from the swine 
wastewater. Meanwhile, the distribution species of Cu and Fe under 
different pH values were calculated using PHREEQC computer program 
(Agunbiade et al., 2009), which could help to understand the existing 
forms of Cu and Fe compounds in the swine wastewater. 
2.3. Struvite crystallization using different magnesium sources 
The filtered wastewater samples were measured and poured into the 
jacketed glass crystallizer. Analytical grades of magnesium sources of 
MgCl2, Mg(OH)2, and MgO were added to the wastewater based on the 
desired molar ratio of Mg:N:P in the range of 1–4:1:1. The reaction time 
was set at 150 min and the solution pH was adjusted to 9.0 with 0.5 mM 
NaOH and HCl solutions(Wang et al., 2018). Moreover, because the 
initial PO4
3−concentration is not so high in accordance with the cal­
culations, the MgO and Mg(OH)2 that we added to the wastewater did 
not need to be initially dissolved (Li et al., 2020). After the reaction, an 
amount of 1 mL supernatant was filtered through a 0.22 μm membrane 
in a 10-mL colorimetric tube to determine the concentrations of PO4
3−,

Cu2+, and Fe3+. The precipitates were collected after the filtration 
process with a 0.22 μm membrane, washed with pure water three times, 
and then dried in an oven at 50 ◦C for 18 h. 
2.4. Life cycle assessment 
2.4.1. Overview 
Life cycle assessment is an approach based on ISO 14040 (ISO, 2006) 
standard method to evaluate the potential impacts of a products or 
processes that directly or indirectly cause on the environment. The 
system boundary is associated with a series of background processes 
factors, including chemical production and transportation, electricity 
consumption, pollutant discharge, and gaseous emissions. The func­
tional unit of evaluation of the treatment process is 1 m3 raw waste­
water. The loss of apparatus and construction process was not included 
and some literatures reported that compared with operations, the system 
infrastructure provide smaller environmental impacts (Ashraf et al., 
2021). 
2.4.2. Scenario settings 
The scenarios were established through SimaPro 9.0 software, and 
the environmental impacts were accessed through the CML 2 method. 
Four scenarios were considered, including one routine scenario (SC1) 
and three struvite scenarios (SC2–SC4). Struvite scenarios of SC2, SC3, 
and SC4 were operated with MgCl2, Mg(OH)2, and MgO, respectively 
(showed in Fig. S1 and Table S1). Four environmental factors were 
established, including GW, FF, HT, and ME. The more detailed infor­
mation of the scenario settings is showed in Supplementary Materials 
(SM) document. 
2.5. Analytical methods 
The wastewater sample was filter extracted with a vacuum pump 
(SHZ-DIII, YuHua, China) and diluted into the 50 mL colorimetric tube 
for the chemical analysis. The concentrations of PO4
3––P in the colori­
metric tube were determined using auto discrete analyzers (Clever­
chemAnna, DeChem-Tech, Germany). The concentrations of Cu2+ and 
Fe3+ ions were examined using ICP-MS method (iCAP RQ, Thermo 
Fisher Scientific, USA). The structures and morphologies of the dried 
precipitates were characterized with XRD (Shimadzu, Japan) and SEM 
(JEOL7800, Japan) techniques. The chemical compositions and surface 
properties of HMs were determined using XPS (Thermo ESCALAB250, 
Thermo Scientific, USA). An amount of 0.1 g dried product was dis­
solved in 3 mL 0.5% nitric acid (HNO3) solution and diluted into 50 mL 
colorimetric tube for HMs contents and purity analysis of the surface of 
precipitates particles. In this study, all the tests were conducted in 
triplicate, and the average values were reported. The analysis of vari­
ance (ANOVA) were analyzed and correlated using SPSS 22.0 (IBM, 
USA). In addition, P＜0.05 represent statistically significan. 
3. Results 
3.1. Effects of initial pH 
Solution pH is a crucial parameter during the struvite crystallization 
process (Huang et al., 2014). In this work, we investigated the Cu, Fe, 
and P removal processes from the swine wastewater under different 
solution pH. The experimental results are presented in Fig. 1. The 
removal efficiency of P increased with the increase of pH value (Fig. 1a). 
However, at higher pH values, the purity of struvite decreased owing to 
the formation of other magnesium compounds, such as Mg3PO4 (Bat­
tistoni et al., 2000; Tünay et al., 1997). Meanwhile, as graph in Fig. 1b 
shows for the effects of various solution pH on the remaining concen­
trations of Cu/Fe during the struvite crystallization process, the residual 
Fe was determined as approximately 0.3 mg/L. This observation implies 
that 94% of Fe was precipitated during the pH adjustment process, while 
Fig. 1. Effects of pH variations on: (a) P removal efficiency, (b) Cu/Fe concentrations, (c) Cu species distribution, and (d) Fe species distribution. Groups with 
different letters are significantly different(p＜0.05).

the residual Cu was approximately 0.7 mg/L, indicating that 86% of Cu 
precipitated. These results suggest that parts of Cu and Fe would be 
precipitated as Cu/Fe-hydroxide in the alkaline reaction conditions. 
Given that the significant difference is observed for various pH value for 
P and heavy metals removal efficiency (p＜0.05), indicating that the pH 
value will have effect on P and heavt metal removal. 
The chemical speciation of HMs is closely related to the solution 
toxicity and reaction behavior. Thus, it is vital to comprehend the 
possible existing forms of HMs in the solution (Exp´osito et al., 2017). 
Some researchers reported that the speciation of Cu in the P-containing 
wastewater may exist in the forms of Cu2+, CuHPO4, Cu3(PO4)2, Cu 
(OH)+, Cu(OH)2 (aq), Cu(OH)2+, and Cu(OH)4

under different pH (8.5–11.0) were simulated using the PHREEQC 
interactive software (Fig. 1c and d). As showed in graph of Fig. 1c, the 
predominate of Cu species was formed as Cu(OH)2, which was the main 
reason for Cu precipitation in the alkaline environment. The plot also 
reveals that some free states of Cu may form compounds with NH4
+, 
PO4

ones, making further contribution for the Cu removal. However, as 
graph in Fig. 1d reveals, the species of Fe exist in the more complex 
forms in the wastewater. The compounds of Fe(OH)3 and Fe(OH)4

compounds based on Eqs. (5)–(7). In this work, a series of experiments 
were conducted under the reaction pH of 9.0.

3.2. Effects of initial heavy metals concentration 
The initial HMs concentration have a impact on struvite 
crystallization process, the results were shown in Fig. 2. The statistical 
analysis showed that significant difference between initial HMs con­
centration and P/HMs removal efficiency was observed (Fig. 2a, p＜ 
0.05). With an increase in the initial concentrations of Cu and Fe from 2 
to 10 mg/L, the residual concentration of P progressively decreased from 
11.4 to 8.1 mg/L. This observation implies that the increase of HMs 
contents could facilitate the P removal process. With an increase in the 
initial concentrations of HMs, the Cu and Fe remaining concentrations 
increased from 0.14 to 0.36 mg/L and from 0.27 to 1.04 mg/L, respec­
tively. It suggests that about 90% of Fe and 85% of Cu precipitate during 
the struvite crystallization process. The struvite purity decreased with an 
increase of the initial concentrations of HMs (Fig. 2b, p＜0.05). As we 
known, the HMs (Cu, Fe) in solution could be coordinated with bonding 
with hydrate, phosphate, and ammonium groups of struvite as com­
plexes compounds, a reason for decreasing the struvite purity (Lu et al., 
2020a). Meanwhile, the HM-hydroxide with struvite co-precipitate 
under alkaline conditions is also a possibility to decrease the struvite 
purity. After filtration, the products precipitated with 4 mg/L of Fe/Cu 
were white (photo in Fig. S2a), whereas they were observed as light 
yellow under the concentration of 8 mg/L of Fe/Cu (Fig. S2b). 
In order to examine a potential effect of struvite on the removal of 
heavy metals (p＜0.05), the remaining concentrations of HMs at 
different struvite amounts and pH 9.0 were determined. As shown in 
Fig. 3, the contents of Cu and Fe in the solution declined with the 
increasing of struvite amount from 0.5 to 3.0 g/L. The removal trends of 
Cu and Fe were found similar; however, the order of precipitation could 
likely be different owing to the differences of heavy metals’ thermody­
namic equilibrium. The solubility constant of Ksp for ferric hydroxide 
and copper hydroxide are 4.0 × 10−38 and 2.2 × 10−20, respectively (Liu 
et al., 2019). These values mean that initially Fe precipitates as Fe(OH)3 
followed by Cu(OH)2 on the struvite surface. Meanwhile, when the 
struvite content was increased to 3.0 g/L, lower concentrations of heavy 
metals (0.8 mg/L for Fe and 0.64 mg/L for Cu) were observed. In brief, 
the precipitation of HMs in the solution is greatly strengthened by 
interaction with struvite. 
3.3. Struvite crystallization using magnesium chloride 
The experiments were conducted using MgCl2 as the magnesium 
source for struvite crystallization from the swine wastewater. The 
changes in the remaining concentrations of P, Cu, Fe, and Fe/Cu in the 
struvite under various Mg:P molar ratios at different reaction times were 
recorded (Fig. 4). With the increases of reaction time and magnesium 
dosage, the P removal efficiency also increased (Fig. 4a). When the Mg:P 
molar ratio escalated from 1:1 to 4:1, the P removal efficiency also 
Fig. 2. Effects of initial heavy metals concentrations on: (a) P, Cu, and Fe removal, and (b) struvite purity. Experiments performed at pH 9.0 and Mg:N:P ratio of 1:1:1 
for 60 min. The different letters above each column represent significant differences at p < 0.05.). 
Q. Guan et al.

5
increased from 83.42% to 94.55% at the reaction time of 150 min. The 
remaining concentrations of Fe (Fig. 4b) and Cu (Fig. 4c) in the solution 
declined with the increase of Mg:P molar ratio. When the Mg:P molar 
ratio was set at 4:1 and the reaction time at 150 min, the Fe and Cu 
removal efficiency values could reach to 98.8% (Fig. 4b) and 90.6%, 
respectively (Fig. 4c). It was found that the addition of soluble Mg source 
could significantly promote the struvite crystallization and HMs pre­
cipitation. It was observed that the contents of Fe and Cu in the pre­
cipitates (Fig. 4d) gradually decreased with the increase of Mg:P molar 
ratio. This result implies that Mg2+ ions compete with HMs during the 
precipitation process, causing some HMs not combined with OH−/PO4
3−
but bonding with the other ions in the wastewater to precipitate, which 
does not affect the struvite purity. 
Furthermore, the obtained products at the MgCl2:P molar ratio of 4:1 
for 150 min were characterized with XRD, SEM, and XPS techniques. 
The results are shown in Fig. 5 and Fig. S3. These examinations could 
provide fundamental understanding on the relationship between heavy 
metals and struvite. Struvite was found as the main component in the 
obtained precipitates (Fig. 5a). The SEM micrographs (Fig. 5b) revealed 
the morphology of the precipitate as short and orthorhombic with 
visible impurities. The microstructure was indicative of surface in­
teractions of Cu and Fe with struvite, inhibiting the struvite growth 
(Muhmood et al., 2018). To further understand the composition of the 
impurities on the surface of struvite, the XPS spectrums of Cu and Fe 
were obtained (Fig. S3). As for Fe, the binding energy was divided into 
709.098 and 710.292 eV, which could be assigned to Fe(III) and Fe–O, 
respectively (Yamashita and Hayes, 2008). However, the presence of 
Fe3+ can form a more complex Fe–P compound with PO4
3−, making the 
precipitates more complexible and significantly decreasing the produc­
tion of struvite (Yan and Shih, 2016a). The two peaks of Cu were divided 
and located at 932.432 and 934.665 eV, which can prove the presence of 
Cu–NH3 and Cu–OH, respectively (Arslano˘glu, 2018). The complex 
Cu–NH3 can gradually transform into Cu–OH while the struvite reaction 
progressing, which finally co-precipitates with struvite.

3.4. Struvite crystallization using magnesium hydroxide 
The chemical compound Mg(OH)2 is the most common Mg source to 
recover P owing to providing OH−ion during the dissolution process, 
which can decrease the cost of NaOH use for pH adjustment (Wang et al., 
2018). In this work, the struvite crystallization experiments were con­
ducted by addition of Mg(OH)2, as shown in Fig. 6. The P removal ef­
ficiency progressively increased from 78.42% to 85.42% with the 
increase of the Mg:P molar ratio from 1:1 to 4:1 (Fig. 6a). The results of 
Fe and Cu remaining concentrations in the solution are exhibited in 
Fig. 6b and c, respectively. In the initial 30-min period of reaction, the Fe 
concentration decreased from 0.65 to 0.54 mg/L when the molar ratio of 
Mg:P increased from 1:1 to 4:1 and then slightly changed in the 
remaining time (Fig. 6b). When the molar ratio of Mg:P was set at 4:1, 
89.2% of Fe precipitated. In the initial 5-min period of the reaction, 
when using Mg(OH)2, the Cu concentration in the solution sharply 
decreased to approximately 0.6 mg/L. Then, it increased to 1.38 (at Mg: 
P of 1:1), 1.98 (at Mg:P of 2:1), 2.22 (at Mg:P of 3:1), and 2.76 mg/L (at 
Mg:P of 4:1) (see plot in Fig. 6c). The Fe/Cu contents in the precipitates, 
as exhibited in Fig. 6d, indicate that the Fe content declines but the Cu

The precipitates obtained at molar ratio Mg(OH)2:P of 4:1 for 150 
min were characterized using XRD, SEM, and XPS analytical methods. 
The XRD depicted that the struvite was the main components of the 
product (Fig. 7a). The SEM micrograph (Fig. 7b) revealed that some 
regular and plate crystals are still detected while most of them are 
covered with impurities. Moreover, to clarify the metallic state of HMs in 
obtained struvite with Mg(OH)2 as magnesium source, XPS analysis was 
performed on the precipitates collected at a molar ratio Mg:P of 4:1 
versus the reaction time of 150 min, as exhibited in Fig. S3a. The binding 
energies of Fe (709.084 and 710.277 eV) were found using CaseXPS 
software, corresponding to Fe(III) and Fe–O, respectively. Compared 
with MgCl2, the similar peaks of these elements were found in the 
struvite by adding Mg(OH)2, whereas, the three peaks of Cu were 
located at 934.602, 935.812, and 932.406 eV (Fig. S3b), corresponding 
to Cu–OH, Cu–HPO4, and Cu–NH3 compounds, respectively (Lu et al., 
2020a). The results when using alkaline magnesium source in the ex­
periments indicate that Cu(OH)2 and Cu[(NH3)n]2+ are converted into 
CuHPO4. 
3.5. Struvite crystallization using magnesium oxide 
Several researchers have reported the use of MgO in the struvite 
crystallization process (Huang et al., 2017), particularly in the pilot-test 
stage. These studies were conducted mainly owing to the smaller dosage 
added, less costs, and the capability of improving the wastewater pH 
compared with Mg(OH)2. The PO4–P, Fe, and Cu removal process and 
the contents of Fe/Cu in the products were investigated (Fig. 8). The 
removal trend was observed the same as those of MgCl2 and Mg(OH)2 in 
which with the increase of Mg:P and reaction time, the P removal effi­
ciency increased (Fig. 8a). When the molar ratio of Mg:P increased from 
1:1 to 4:1, the removal efficiency of P increased from 80.62% to 87.59%. 
Compared with the other magnesium sources for removal of P from 
wastewater, MgO was found superior to Mg(OH)2, whereas inferior to 
MgCl2. As the results presented in the graph of Fig. 8b, addition of MgO 
also is useful to promote Fe precipitation. When the Mg:P molar ratio 
increased, the residual concentration of Fe dropped to 0.5 (at Mg:P ratio 
of 1:1), 0.42 (at Mg:P ratio of 2:1), 0.32 (at Mg:P ratio of 3:1), and 0.18 
mg/L (at Mg:P ratio of 4:1), respectively. These experimental observa­
tions indicate that most of Fe2+ ion compounds could be precipitated 
during the struvite crystallization process. In contrast, with the increase 
of reaction time, the Cu precipitation ratio increased in the first 10 min 
and then decreased to the end (Fig. 8c). During the reaction time be­
tween 5 and 10 min, the MgO utilizes the H+ ions generated in Eq. (2) to 
form MgOH+, then converting it to Mg2+ based on the reactions of Eqs. 
(8) and (9) (Park et al., 2021). Simultaneously, the remaining OH−ions 
in the solution continue to react with Cu and form more precipitates, 
resulting in further removal of Cu. As graph in Fig. 8d displays for the 
Fe/Cu contents in the struvite at various molar ratios of Mg:P, when the 
molar ratio of Mg:P increased, the contents of Fe dropped from 0.08 to 
0.02 mg/g and Cu concentration increased from 0.30 to 0.40 mg/g.

Furthermore, the characteristics of struvite produced at Mg:P molar 
ratio of 4:1 for 150 min were examined with XRD and SEM techniques 
(Fig. 9). Struvite was found the main component of the obtained prod­
uct, as XRD patterns in Fig. 9a show. The collected precipitate particles 
were observed with a lumpy morphology in the SEM micrograph 
(Fig. 9b) with the impurities adhering on the surface. The specific 
composition of the sediments on the struvite particles surfaces when 
MgO was used is presented in Fig. S3 of XPS spectra for Fe and Cu. For Fe 
2p, the results of composition in the precipitates were similar to that of 
the precipitates collected when using Mg(OH)2 or MgCl2 as Mg source. 
In the meantime, as for the XPS spectra of Cu, three peaks (Cu–OH, 
Cu–NH3, and Cu–HPO4) were also detected in this experiment. 
3.6. Effects of various magnesium sources on environmental factors 
The Life cycle assessment method was used for evaluation of the 
environmental impacts for the various magnesium sources on P recovery 
via struvite crystallization process. The results of environmental impacts 
of treatment process, water, electricity, and chemical materials factors 
are exhibited in Fig. 10 and Fig. S4. For global warming (GW) factor 
(Fig. S4), the CO2 emission of each scenario was determined as 43.71 
(SC1), 29.74 (SC2), 37.91 (SC3), and 31.84 (SC4) kg CO2, with main 
contributions from the chemical materials and the treatment process 
(Fig. 10a). The decreasing CO2 emission mainly depends on the chem­
icals production process. Compared with the lime production process, 
the less energy input needed for the chemicals used in the struvite 
production process, which decreases the CO2 emission (Amann et al., 
2018). For fossil fuels (FF) factor, the use of MgCl2 could significantly 
reduce the influence of fossil depletion (Fig. 10b), indicating that using 
MgCl2 saves more fossil energy and protects resource. For human 
toxicity (HT) factor, a larger amount of chemical materials used in SC2 
was observed (Fig. 10c), however, the toxicity index of treatment pro­
cess was the lowest, owing to the use of MgCl2 that results in remarkable 
decrease in the HMs pollution (Wang et al., 2018). For marine eutro­
phication (ME) factor, four scenarios could diminish the eutrophication 
risk in nature due to the decrease of P contents. Meanwhile, the chem­
icals burden observed in the three struvite scenarios was attributed to
