4. Discussion 
4.1. Technical aspects 
Struvite crystallization process is highly dependent on the usage of 
magnesium sources. Compared with MgO and Mg(OH)2, using MgCl2 as 
magnesium source could reach satisfactory P removal efficiency under 
the same molar ratio condition. In addition, the MgO and Mg(OH)2 
materials would release OH−
ions into the neutral aqueous solution, 
increasing the localized pH. This process produces impurity nucleation 
and growth and makes the system thermodynamically unstable (Ye 
et al., 2021). As for the HMs removal, Fe could minimize it in a lower 
value via struvite crystallization process using three magnesium sources. 
However, the Cu redissolve phenomenon observed in the presence of 
alkaline magnesium source increases the Cu concentration in the 
wastewater. The reason for this phenomenon is speculated that the 
redissolved Cu in the system in the presence of alkaline magnesium 
Fig. 8. Effects of MgO:P molar ratios in range of 1:1 to 4:1 on: (a) P removal efficiency, (b) remaining Fe concentration, (c) remaining Cu concentration, and (d) Fe/ 
Cu contents in precipitates. Experiments were conducted at pH 9.0 for 150 min.Groups with different letters are significantly different(p＜0.05). 
Fig. 9. (a) XRD patterns and (b) SEM micrograph of struvite precipitates (MgO:P molar ratio of 4:1 and reaction time 150 min).  
Q. Guan et al.

Journal of Cleaner Production 328 (2021) 129588
9
source causes the thermodynamical system unstable, increasing the 
concentration of the re-dissolved Cu. When the solution pH increased, 
the concentration of Cu[(NH4)n]2+ gradually increased. However, the 
struvite crystallization process needs to continuously consume free state 
of PO4
3−and Cu2+ ions, causing the formed Cu(OH)2 and Cu3(PO4)2 to 
reach their unsaturated states again (Huang et al., 2010), beginning to 
release Cu and P into the solution. Because the saturation index (SI) of 
Cu(OH)2 is smaller than that of Cu3(PO4)2 (Perwitasari et al., 2017), the 
Cu3(PO4)2 compound will preferentially be redissolved in the solution. 
The dissolved PO4
3−will continue to combine with NH4
+ and Mg2+, 
forming struvite crystals. Similar observations have been reported about 
the redissolution behavior of cushion during struvite crystallization 
process (Lee et al., 2013; Li et al., 2020). Thus, it is essential to avoid 
using alkaline magnesium source in Cu-containing wastewater owing to 
the occurrence of redissolution phenomenon. 
4.2. Economic analysis 
Recovering P and removing HMs through struvite crystallization 
technology not only have significant environmental benefits, but from 
an economic perspective, the product is also a highly valuable material 
as a slow-release fertilizer or secondary use of phosphate rock (Shu et al., 
2006). The P recovery from swine wastewater can decrease the P con­
tent of wastewater in the treatment plants and decrease the costs of the 
treatment process. Similar literature (Sakthivel et al., 2012) reported 
that the choice of Mg source is closely related to the obtained product 
purity, owing it directly to the contents of HMs in the struvite. 
As mentioned earlier, the choice of magnesium source not only 
affected the P and HMs removal efficiency in the entire removal process, 
but also directly influenced the cost of crystallization process. Therefore, 
it was necessary to evaluate the operating costs using different magne­
sium sources, including chemical costs, energy consumption, 
manpower, and maintenance costs (Table S5). In this experiment, the 
energy consumption was estimated as 0.1 $/kw⋅h and the manpower 
and maintenance costs together were considered as 0.2 $/m3. As shown 
in Table S5, the smallest operation cost incurred was for MgCl2 (4.88 
$/m3 wastewater) as magnesium source, followed by MgO (4.99 $/m3 
wastewater) and Mg(OH)2 (5.14 $/m3 wastewater). Because of the low 
solubility values of Mg(OH)2 and MgO, once the dosage required for 
these two magnesium sources is greatly increased, the first step needs to 
add HCl or HNO3 to dissolve and release Mg2+, increasing the operation 
costs (Carballa et al., 2010). Although the use of alkaline magnesium 
sources can improve the wastewater pH, but when the wastewater 
contains Cu2+ ion, it is easy to make Cu to redissolve, which is not 
beneficial to obtain highly pure struvite products. Wang et al. (2018) 
reported that the selection of the most economical and optimal mag­
nesium sources are thus density dependent on the reagents prices. When 
NaOH is more expensive, alkaline magnesium sources are preferable. 
However, when the prices of HCl and HNO3 are more expensive, MgCl2 
as magnesium sources is more economical option. In contrast, the 
high-quality (higher than 95% purity) struvite was collected and the 
greatest removal efficiency of 94.55% was achieved when MgCl2 was 
used as magnesium source. From the economic comparison mentioned 
above, the performance of the whole process was the best when MgCl2 
was used as Mg source for treatment of the swine wastewater, followed 
by MgO and Mg(OH)2. There is no doubt that cost-efficient usage of 
magnesium sources for removal of P and minimizing heavy metals 
contents is mainly determined by the price of the reagents. It should be 
noted that in its present form, calculations can only roughly be applied 
to the swine wastewater matrix to predict the most cost-efficient mag­
nesium sources. Meanwhile, as for the pilot test, a combination of 
magnesium source can be considered to minimize the operation costs.

4.3. Environmental impact 
Comparison of three magnesium sources, MgCl2 causes less envi­
ronmental burden than other ones. In addition, from the results of LCA 
analysis, using MgCl2 as magnesium source in struvite crystallization 
process would reduce the fossil energy consumption and the human 
health risk, and diminish the eutrophication problem in the nature. Ye 
et al. (2021) used LCA analysis to reveal that the use of MgCl2 in struvite 
crystallization process for treatment of WAC wastewater reduces the 
CO2 emission and fossil fuels demand, creating less environmental 
burden. The P resources is unsustainable, but the application of struvite 
not only reduces the risk of P-carrying capacity in water bodies but also 
saves phosphate rock resources. Meanwhile, Besson et al. (2021) re­
ported that from ecological and economic value perspective and 
compared with the commercial phosphate fertilizer, using struvite as 
fertilizer causes less greenhouse gas emissions with higher economic 
impacts. In short, it is a feasible method to conduct struvite crystalli­
zation process using MgCl2 as the optimal magnesium source. 
5. Conclusion 
Struvite crystallization and P recovery process from wastewater 
could be affected under the presence of HM ions. In this work, the 
optimal magnesium source used to recover P and minimize HMs con­
tents were systematically studied. We firstly found pH as the main factor 
affecting the removal of P and HMs. During the pH adjustment process, 
Fe and Cu ions first co-precipitate with struvite in the form of Cu/ 
Fe–hydroxide. Meanwhile, at different pH conditions, the chemical 
speciation composition of HMs in the wastewater was also observed 
different. Moreover, the higher contents of heavy metals in the waste­
water influences the collected struvite purity. Comparing the three 
magnesium sources of MgCl2, Mg(OH)2, and MgO, we observed that at 
MgCl2:P molar ratio of 4:1 for 150 min processing time, significant 
amounts of P, Fe, and Cu could be removed. Smaller heavy metals 
contents on the surface of struvite particles were also observed. How­
ever, when the other two magnesium sources of Mg(OH)2 and MgO were 
used, the removal effects of P and Fe were significantly smaller than that 
when using MgCl2. Simultaneously, the Cu compounds were prone to 
redissolution when using alkaline magnesium source, resulting in the 
accumulation of Cu on the struvite surface. In the economic analysis, we 
found MgCl2 more cost-effective than Mg(OH)2 and MgO, which could 
save 0.26 and 0.11 $/m3 wastewater, respectively. Moreover, using a 
soluble Mg source causes less environmental risks assessed through the 
LCA analysis, which is beneficial for improving the environmental 
footprint. It is more advantageous to maintain phosphorus recovery and 
product quality. In addition, the phosphorus resources were unsustain­
able, but the application of struvite not only lessens the risk of phos­
phorus carrying capacity in water bodies, but also saves phosphate rock 
resources. It was reported that from ecological and economic value 
perspective, compared with the phosphate fertilizer, using struvite as 
fertilizer creates less greenhouse gas emission larger economic benefits. 
Therefore, for the treatment process of the swine wastewater via struvite 
crystallization process, MgCl2 is suggested as the optimal magnesium 
source.

Life Cycle Assessment of Urine Diversion and Conversion to
Fertilizer Products at the City Scale
Stephen P. Hilton, Gregory A. Keoleian,* Glen T. Daigger, Bowen Zhou,∥and Nancy G. Love
Cite This: Environ. Sci. Technol. 2021, 55, 593−603
Read Online
ACCESS
Metrics & More
Article Recommendations
*
sı
Supporting Information
ABSTRACT: Urine diversion has been proposed as an approach for
producing renewable fertilizers and reducing nutrient loads to wastewater
treatment plants. Life cycle assessment was used to compare environ-
mental impacts of the operations phase of urine diversion and fertilizer
processing systems [via (1) a urine concentration alternative and (2) a
struvite precipitation and ion exchange alternative] at a city scale to
conventional systems. Scenarios in Vermont, Michigan, and Virginia were
modeled, along with additional sensitivity analyses to understand the
importance of key parameters, such as the electricity grid and wastewater
treatment method. Both urine diversion technologies had better
environmental performance than the conventional system and led to
reductions of 29−47% in greenhouse gas emissions, 26−41% in energy
consumption, approximately half the freshwater use, and 25−64% in
eutrophication potential, while acidiﬁcation potential ranged between a
24% decrease to a 90% increase. In some situations, wastewater treatment chemical requirements were eliminated. The
environmental performance improvement was usually dependent on oﬀsetting the production of synthetic fertilizers. This study
suggests that urine diversion could be applied broadly as a strategy for both improving wastewater management and decarbonization.
■INTRODUCTION
About half of the world food supply depends on synthetic
fertilizers produced from nonrenewable resources.1 Phosphate
rock is used to produce phosphorus fertilizers. While the extent
of the resource base is contested, supply is ﬁnite, demand has
increased partly because of increased meat consumption and
biofuel production, and supplies are dominated by a few
countries.2−5 Production of nitrogen fertilizer depends on
natural gas and is responsible for about 1.2% of world energy
use and associated greenhouse gas emissions.6,7 Prices for
phosphate rock and other fertilizer commodities have
ﬂuctuated as much as 800% in recent years, which has led to
food riots in many countries.3,4,8 Given the impacts and
resource constraints of conventional fertilizers, renewable and
reliable alternatives are needed.
Food consumption by humans is the principal source of
these vital nutrients in domestic wastewater, and signiﬁcant
resources are invested to remove them to protect the aquatic
environment. A more recent study (Pabi et al., 2013) states
that water and wastewater treatment systems consume 2% of
the total electricity in the U.S.9,10 Some propose separately
collecting urine and using it to produce fertilizer.11,12 Although
it comprises less than 1% of wastewater volume, urine contains
approximately 50% of the phosphorus and 80% of the nitrogen
contained in domestic wastewater.13−15 As utilities increasingly
focus on sustainability, large-scale urine diversion has the
potential to improve regional wastewater management, recover
essential resources, and reduce energy consumed in processes
such as aeration.11,16−19
Compared to synthetic fertilizers, urine-derived fertilizers
recover important nutrients, can be as eﬀective in stimulating
plant growth and contain lower levels of heavy metals.19−26
However, processing fertilizers from urine will have environ-
mental impacts.15 Collecting and transporting urine will
require new infrastructure systems, such as pressurized pipe
networks or truck collection.
Use of acetic acid or other chemicals may be needed to
prevent the spontaneous release of ammonia gas and formation
of precipitates that clog piping infrastructure.15,27−29 Urine
concentration, through processes such as reverse osmosis
(RO), freeze-thaw, or distillation, may be required to make
nutrient concentrations in urine, which are much lower than
synthetic fertilizers, high enough for eﬃcient agricultural
application.15,30−34 Alternatively, nutrients may be concen-
trated through removal processes such as struvite precipitation,
ammonia capture via ion exchange, or urea adsorp-

tion.15,20,35−41 Additional treatment to deactivate pathogens
and remove pharmaceuticals found in urine may also be
needed.25,42,43
Life cycle assessment (LCA) is well suited to compare the
environmental performance of urine-diverting systems to
conventional systems, determine environmental hotspots, and
highlight trade-oﬀs and opportunities for system improve-
ment.44,45 LCA has been used to compare a range of
wastewater treatment alternatives46−50 and in most cases has
indicated that urine diversion has lower environmental impacts
than conventional systems.13,14,51−58 However, these studies
have focused on small-scale systems, have evaluated only a few
locations and urine-derived fertilizers and simpliﬁed how
diverting urine will aﬀect wastewater treatment plants. These
studies measure changes to wastewater through volume
reduction or a static oﬀset for denitriﬁcation, which may not
capture signiﬁcant changes to wastewater treatment as nutrient
ratios change or how urine diversion could change treatment
conﬁgurations.51,52,59−61
This study expands upon previous research by evaluating the
environmental impacts of urine diversion and conversion to
fertilizers relative to conventional alternatives in large and
diverse settings57,62 and by a more detailed assessment of how
this will aﬀect wastewater treatment. This conventional
alternative manages urine through the wastewater system and
produces and transports equivalent amounts of nutrients in the
form of synthetic fertilizers. The relative diﬀerences between
these two diﬀerent approaches are quantiﬁed. Wastewater
treatment is modeled in detail to better account for the
ramiﬁcations of urine diversion. Three distinct locations,
namely the States of Vermont, Michigan, and Virginia
(referred to subsequently as scenarios) are considered to
explore how important parameters such as population, extent
of nutrient removal at wastewater treatment plants, electricity
grid fuel mix, and the amount of the urine-derived fertilizer
produced inﬂuence the environmental performance. Sensitivity
analysis is conducted using Monte Carlo in order to further
evaluate these parameters and the uncertainty of many others.
■METHODS AND MATERIALS
Urine Processing Alternatives. Two distinct urine-
derived fertilizer alternatives were evaluated to represent the
range of products that can be produced. They consist of (1)
concentrated urine, where organics such as pharmaceuticals are
removed from diverted urine through activated carbon and
Figure 1. (a−c) System diagram for each alternative. (a) Urine concentration alternative, (b) struvite and ammonium sulfate alternative, and (c)
conventional system. Yellow boxes indicate that a process is either unique to that alternative or that urine diversion signiﬁcantly aﬀects its
environmental impact.

pubs.acs.org/est
Article
https://dx.doi.org/10.1021/acs.est.0c04195
Environ. Sci. Technol. 2021, 55, 593−603
594

urine is subsequently concentrated by RO and then heat-
pasteurized and (2) struvite and ammonium sulfate, where
urine is processed to produce struvite through precipitation
and ammonium sulfate through ion exchange. Use of urine-
derived fertilizer products are compared to commercial
fertilizers. For the urine-derived fertilizer alternatives it was
assumed that 70% of urine in each of the three scenarios
(Vermont, Michigan, and Virginia) considered was diverted for
fertilizer production.63 This was done to simulate large-scale
collection within these locations but to allow for some
ineﬃciency in collection. As shown in Figure 1, production
and distribution of ﬂush water, collection of wastewater
(including separated urine), production and transportation of
fertilizers, and wastewater treatment were included in the
scope of the study to capture system-wide diﬀerences. More
information on these alternatives can be found below and in
Section 3 in Supporting Information.
The inputs to treat and distribute ﬂush water were
determined using the ratio of surface and groundwater treated
in each location,64 and literature data for both types of
treatment.51,65−76 When urine was diverted, urine diversion
toilet ﬂush volumes were used. In the conventional alternative,
for people not using urine-diverting toilets, and during
defecation, low-ﬂow toilet ﬂush volumes were used, as shown
in Tables S6 and S7. When urine is diverted, acetic acid is
added to stabilize it, followed by transportation to a fertilizer
production center via a pressurized pipe system.
Magnesium oxide is added to precipitate phosphorus as
struvite, and the remaining ammonium from the eﬄuent is
captured through ion exchange using a resin such as Dowex
Mac 3.39 The exhausted resin is regenerated with 3 M sulfuric
acid, producing a liquid ammonium sulfate fertilizer. Addi-
tional acetic acid is needed for the concentrated urine fertilizer
to consistently maintain nitrogen in the urea form. Following
pharmaceutical removal using activated carbon sized for
pharmaceutical removal, urine is concentrated to a ﬁfth of its
original volume using RO with an energy recovery device and
then heat-pasteurized. Chemical and energy inputs for
regeneration of activated carbon77−81 and RO membrane
cleanings82 are included. Eﬄuents from the urine-derived
fertilizer production facilities are sent to the wastewater
treatment plant, and the urine-derived fertilizers are trucked
to a regional fertilizer distributor.
The methodology described in Hilton et al.83 is used to
model wastewater treatment for all alternatives to determine
electricity consumption, chemical consumption, secondary
sludge production, and water and air emissions. All alternatives
assumed equal amounts of feces and graywater, steady-state
conditions, and compliance with all regulatory requirements.
Processes that were unaﬀected by urine diversion, such as
primary sludge treatment and hauling screenings to landﬁlls,
were excluded. Further details can be found in Supporting
Information, Figure S1, and Hilton et al.83
The production of urea and monoammonium phosphate
fertilizers and transportation to the regional fertilizer
distribution center was used to ensure all alternatives provided
the same mass of nitrogen and phosphorus as fertilizers. These
synthetic fertilizers were added in the conventional and both
diversion alternatives to provide equal amounts of nitrogen and
phosphorus, despite diﬀering nutrient recovery ratios. Trans-
portation from the regional fertilizer distribution center and
application at the farm were not analyzed, as previous research
did not ﬁnd plant uptake and runoﬀfrom urine-derived
fertilizers to diﬀer from synthetic fertilizers.25,84−86
Life Cycle Assessment. The treatment of one person
equivalent’s (p.e.) wastewater for 1 year is the functional unit
of analysis used. Treatment of all wastewater produced
(including urine as appropriate) is considered because urine
diversion can lead to signiﬁcant reductions in the nitrogen and
phosphorus of wastewater arriving at the treatment plant and
can signiﬁcantly aﬀect treatment. All alternatives provided
equal masses of nitrogen and phosphorus in the fertilizer.
Environmental burdens of capital equipment and the end of
life of wastewater and fertilizer infrastructure were excluded
because the operational-phase impacts are expected to
dominate.87−91
Parameters used for the life cycle inventory and mass
balance were obtained from literature sources and pilot scale
systems and can be found in Tables 1, S6 and S8. The United
States Life Cycle Inventory (USLCI) was used for most unit
processes, although Ecoinvent was used when unit processes
were not available.92,93 A life cycle impact assessment was
conducted using global warming potential (GWP), cumulative
energy demand (CED), freshwater use,94 eutrophication
potential (EP), and acidiﬁcation potential (AP). GWP was
calculated using GWP 100a from the Fifth Assessment
Report,95 and the TRACI 2.0 methodology was used for
eutrophication and AP.96 These categories represent key
impacts for changes in energy use, chemical manufacturing,
water quality, and water use that are caused by urine diversion.
Description of Scenarios Evaluated. Three scenarios
were modeled to provide an initial assessment of how location-
speciﬁc factors aﬀect the environmental merits and drawbacks
of urine diversion. The Vermont scenario represents a smaller
urban community without strict nitrogen eﬄuent limits located
in a largely rural state. The Michigan scenario was developed as
a statewide average and was constructed by categorizing the
range of communities in the State, the types of wastewater

The Virginia scenario represents a more densely populated
urban location with strict eﬄuent limits. Hypothetical
scenarios were modeled based on these treatment plants
where the wastewater was assumed to predominately comprise
domestic and commercial wastewater. Further description of
these scenarios can be found in Supporting Information,
Tables 2 and S9−S15 and Hilton et al.83 All alternatives were
evaluated for each scenario.
Sensitivity Analysis. Sensitivity analysis was conducted to
evaluate the robustness of the results, test urine diversion in a
broader range of contexts, and to elucidate how model

parameters and key assumptions inﬂuenced the environmental
performance of urine diversion. A total of 12 separate
simulation scenarios were created. As shown in Figure S2, six
of these simulation scenarios modeled the ﬁve-stage
Bardenpho treatment plant because it had the highest level
of nutrient removal, while six modeled the single aeration basin
with phosphorus removal because it had the lowest level of
nutrient removal. Three electric grids, coal, natural gas, and
renewable comprising 50% wind and 50% hydropower were
considered for each wastewater treatment type. Both the urine
concentration and struvite and ammonium sulfate urine-
derived fertilizer alternatives were compared, given the six
simulation scenarios for each wastewater treatment type. Table
S16 lists the distributions of each parameter used. The Excel
plugin Simvoi was used to conduct a Monte Carlo analysis
with 10,000 repetitions for each sensitivity scenario.111
■RESULTS
Life Cycle Impacts Across Scenarios. Urine diversion
consistently provides improved environmental performance
relative to the conventional system for each scenario for all
impact categories, except AP, as shown in Figure 2 (see Table
S17 for data plotted in this ﬁgure). Both diversion alternatives
reduced the GWP, CED, freshwater use, and EP categories
from anywhere between 24 and 63%. The urine concentration
alternative typically led to larger improvements than the
struvite and ammonium sulfate alternative. Urine concen-
tration alternatives decreased the AP modestly compared to
the conventional alternative for all scenarios (9−22%), while
struvite and ammonium sulfate alternatives increased the AP
by 34 to 91% relative to the conventional alternative. Figures 2
and S17 provide the relative diﬀerences in environmental
performance for each alternative.
The magnitude of environmental impacts diﬀered substan-
tially between the three scenarios. Michigan had the highest
GWP, CED, and AP impacts, while Vermont had the lowest.
Much of this is because Michigan’s electricity grid is composed
primarily of fossil fuels and uses natural gas to thermally dry
sludge, while Vermont’s electricity grid is mostly comprised of
renewable energy sources. The Vermont scenario had an EP
approximately four times larger than in Virginia as a result of
the large diﬀerences in eﬄuent standards. The urine diversion
alternatives in states with less stringent eﬄuent standards
(Vermont and Michigan) saw the largest decreases in EP. The
diﬀerences between the urine concentration and struvite and
ammonium sulfate alternatives were smaller for scenarios
where the environmental impacts of producing electricity were
larger, such as in Michigan.
Life Cycle Impacts by the Process. Figure 2 shows the
contribution of system components to each environmental
impact category. Wastewater treatment dominated the EP
(81−99%), was usually responsible for the largest proportion
of impacts in GWP (24−45%) and CED (21−49%) categories,
and was a major contributor to AP (10−48%). Fertilizer
production had the next largest impacts on the GWP (14−
35%), CED (14−42%), and EP (0−17%) categories and was a
major contributor to AP (8−60%). In Michigan and Vermont,
the EP from fertilizer production was negligible relative to its
contribution from wastewater eﬄuent. Potable water produc-
tion and urine collection, respectively, had the next largest
impacts in the GWP and CED categories. The largest
contributor to AP was sulfuric acid (33−49% when producing
ammonium sulfate) followed by acetic acid (10−17% when
producing ammonium sulfate, 35−58% when concentrating
urine).
In the conventional alternative, 10.4 m3 of water was needed
per person per year for ﬂushing excluding leaks between the
drinking water plant and the consumer. This decreases to 5.3
m3 in the urine diversion alternatives and can be as low as 3.1
cm3 if all urine is diverted. Reduced ﬂush volumes from urine-
diverting toilets were responsible for the majority of decreased
freshwater used, although 6 to 21% came from upstream
sources such as production of synthetic fertilizer, ferric
chloride, and other chemicals.
For urine collection, producing acetic acid led to higher
environmental impacts than the electricity consumed to collect
urine. More acetic acid was used to ensure that urine remained
stable in the urine concentration alternative. While urine
diversion reduced the volume of wastewater that was needed to
be collected, the impacts of collecting and stabilizing urine
were substantially larger than any beneﬁts of collecting less
wastewater in sewers.
Urine-derived fertilizer production resulted in about 35−
73% as much GWP as synthetic fertilizers and decreased most
other environmental impacts. The exception was AP, which
ranged anywhere from an 81% decrease to a 231% increase
from synthetic fertilizers. Oﬀsetting synthetic fertilizers was
almost always required to reduce GWP and CED.
The impacts of concentrating urine were dominated by
electricity consumed for RO. Unless urine diversion led to
major reductions in electricity consumed at wastewater
treatment plants, such as in Virginia, concentration increased
total electricity within a municipality. The environmental
impacts of producing concentrated urine were low in Vermont
because of the high proportion of renewable energy. The
impacts of producing struvite and ammonium sulfate were
relatively independent of the electricity grid, with sulfuric acid
being responsible for much of the GWP and leading to this
alternative always having the largest AP. Processes such as
regenerating activated carbon, cleaning RO membranes,
producing magnesium oxide and ion exchange resin, and
electricity for pumping in the fertilizer production facility had
small overall impacts.
The GWP and CED of shipping urine-derived fertilizers to
the fertilizer depot comprised a relatively small portion of the
net impact but were up to 3.5 times higher than shipping
synthetic fertilizers. Synthetic fertilizers were shipped much
longer distances but only required about 4−8% as much mass
and were more likely to use larger and more eﬃcient
transports.
Urine diversion signiﬁcantly decreased the impacts (GWP,
CED, and AP) of nutrient removal from treatment plants with
stringent eﬄuent limits, whereas more lenient plants reduced
the EP of releasing eﬄuent to aquatic ecosystems. As shown in
Figure 3, all treatment plants beneﬁtted by reducing the
amount of ferric chloride required to remove phosphorus.
Treatment plants with stricter eﬄuent limits had larger
reductions of electricity, methanol, and nitrous oxide emissions
in biological treatment. These beneﬁts were so large in Virginia
that even if no synthetic fertilizer were oﬀset, urine diversion
would still reduce net greenhouse gas emissions. In certain
cases, urine diversion could eliminate the need for ferric
chloride and methanol during average conditions. Reducing
total wastewater volume, capturing biological oxygen demand
(BOD) in concentrated urine, and minor changes to secondary

sludge production led to small changes in environmental
impacts.
Figure S3 shows that the methodology used in this study and
the simpler methodologies used in other studies to estimate
how much urine diversion reduces greenhouse gas emissions
from wastewater treatment are often within a reasonable
range.51,52 However, the beneﬁts from increasing urine are not
linear because of elimination of chemical requirements or
changes in wastewater treatment plant conﬁguration, so the use
of a linear oﬀset results in some level of inaccuracy. For
example, one oﬀset methodology (Kavvada et al. 2017,
including substrate emissions) estimated reductions in green-
house gas emissions from wastewater treatment within 1% of
the ﬁve-stage Bardenpho treatment plant modeled in this study
at 60% of urine diverted. At other levels of diversion, it
underestimated and overestimated these reductions by 20 and
53%, respectively.
Sensitivity Analysis. Figures S4−S7 demonstrate that the
results of this study were largely robust. Urine diversion always
decreased freshwater use and EP. The number of repetitions
where urine concentration increased GWP and CED were
negligible but occurred occasionally for struvite and
ammonium sulfate when renewable electricity was used.
Urine concentration alternatives did increase AP in a few
repetitions with the ﬁve-stage Bardenpho when renewable
electricity was used, and approximately 30% of repetitions in
the single aeration basin. The AP for struvite and ammonium
sulfate was always higher than the conventional alternative
even as the eﬃciency of ammonium sulfate use approached
100%. Figures 4 and S8 show that urine concentration typically
had a better environmental performance than struvite and
ammonium sulfate. These diﬀerences were more pronounced
when producing electricity that had lower environmental
impacts because the added burden of electricity consumption
to concentrate urine was lessened. Environmental improve-
ments in GWP, CED, and AP categories are highest in
locations with electricity produced from fossil fuels and large
levels of nutrient removal, as shown in Figures S4−S7.
Environmental improvements are also greater in locations with
less wastewater volume per person and lower performing
aeration systems.
Tables S18 and S19 show that excluding fertilizer oﬀsets
from the scope can change the conclusion of the analysis. As
the environmental impact of producing electricity decreased,
reducing greenhouse gas emissions without considering
fertilizer oﬀsets are less likely. The exception is for urine
concentration alternatives with limited nutrient removal
because net electricity consumption in a municipality increases.
When excluding fertilizer oﬀsets, urine concentration alter-
natives often still led to reductions in greenhouse gas
emissions.

■DISCUSSION
Similar to other LCAs,51,52,54,55,57,58 this study found urine
diversion reduced most environmental impacts. It expanded
upon previous research by conducting a more comprehensive
characterization of wastewater treatment and by evaluating a
range of large-scale systems. Simpler methods to estimate the
changes in environmental impacts of treating wastewater are
valid as an approximation, but the more complete methods
used in this study may be more appropriate when increased
accuracy is needed or when diﬀerent extents of urine diversion
are being evaluated. Scenario and sensitivity analyses showed
that freshwater use and EP impacts were always reduced, GWP
and CED were consistently reduced, and urine concentration
usually reduced the AP.
Urine collection is the uncertain aspect of this analysis
because of a lack of large-scale examples. This study modeled a
centralized system conveying urine from an urban area to a
central processing facility in order to create a reasonable
estimate of the environmental burdens from urine collection. It
suggested the importance of the acetic acid dosage used for
stabilization. Other options include a more distributed system
consisting of multiple processing facilities strategically located
throughout an urban area to reduce both the distance collected
urine would need to be transported, as well as the transport
time which could reduce urine stabilization require-
ments.52,56,58 The optimal scale of decentralization of urine
collection still needs to be assessed and depends on many
factors including topography, size, and population density.50
Large-scale urine collection is at an early stage of development
and may diﬀer signiﬁcantly from the urine collection system in
this study, which was selected as a reasonable case.
The advantages urine diversion provides wastewater treat-
ment are clearly demonstrated in this study and corroborated
by previous research.18,55,59 Where nutrient removal is
practiced, these primarily include elimination of chemical
inputs (metal salts for phosphorus removal, supplemental
carbon such as methanol for nitrogen removal) and reduced
energy use. In many cases, urine diversion can eliminate the
need to expand existing wastewater treatment plants for
nutrient removal capabilities. While not considered in this
study, eliminating the need for nutrient removal could allow
further changes to treatment process such as increased capture
and utilization of organic matter contained in the inﬂuent
wastewater. In locations where nutrient removal is not a goal
for wastewater treatment, eutrophication can be reduced as less
nutrients are discharged to local waterways. Urine diversion
leads to decreases in environmental impacts through a wide
range of conditions but can be a particularly eﬀective
decarbonization strategy in areas with high levels of nutrient
removal, electricity produced primarily from fossil fuels, and
relatively little wastewater per capita.
Producing fertilizer from urine instead of mineral sources
leads to signiﬁcant environmental beneﬁts. These urine-derived
fertilizer production methods were characterized using
laboratory and demonstration-scale studies,25,26,37−39,61,112
but demonstration of other available approaches15,33,40,41,105
and larger scale systems will provide an improved basis for
assessing environmental impacts.15,33,40,41,105 They were
selected to represent a range of fertilizer products and
production methods. Urine concentration is more heavily
dependent on energy, produces a fertilizer with nitrogen in the
form of urea, retains much of the potassium in urine, and has a
relatively consistent nitrogen-to-phosphorus ratio (depending
on the composition of urine and whether additional nutrients
are added). Struvite precipitation and ammonium sulfate
largely use chemical inputs and could easily be applied with
diﬀerent nitrogen-to-phosphorus ratios. Throughout all elec-
tricity grids, the environmental burdens of producing
concentrated urine were usually lower even as the eﬃciency
of sulfuric acid use approached 100%. The environmental
burdens of producing these urine-derived fertilizers were lower
than synthetic fertilizers and will be signiﬁcantly improved as
use of sulfuric acid for ion exchange and energy for RO are
optimized, or renewable energy is used for urine concentration.
The urine-derived fertilizers evaluated could be applied
similarly to fertilizers commonly used in the US.113 Beyond the
impacts of fertilizer production, other important factors such as
the higher popularity of single-nutrient fertilizers will aﬀect
which fertilizers are produced.113 Implementation eﬀorts need
to consider the fertilizer demands of adjacent communities and
the transportation costs and environmental impacts associated
with shipping urine-derived fertilizers from population
centers.12,114
Urine can replace a signiﬁcant fraction of synthetic fertilizers.
Researchers estimate 16−30 kg of nitrogen and 4 kg of
phosphorus in fertilizer are currently used per person per year
in aﬄuent countries.115−118 If all nutrients were recovered
from domestic wastewater, it would likely produce less than 5
kg of nitrogen and 1 kg of phosphorus per person. Regardless,
urine diversion can provide signiﬁcant environmental beneﬁts
and can be used with other strategies such as dietary changes,
manure application, and reduction of nutrient runoﬀduring
mineral extraction and fertilizer application to signiﬁcantly
improve nutrient use eﬃciency.115,116
The development of large-scale urine collection and
processing systems is still at a conceptual stage. Research is
ongoing to understand and address the many challenges of
urine diversion, including economic, market and regulatory
acceptance,12,26,43,51,52,58,119−121 potential user error,26,122 risk
aversion and lack of conﬁdence in performance,8,43,120 and
lock-in to conventional systems.120,123 Irrespective of the urine
processing method considered, net beneﬁts were observed for
each scenario evaluated. In some cases, the environmental
beneﬁts associated with water and wastewater management
alone were suﬃcient to oﬀset the environmental burden
associated with urine collection, processing, and transport. The
analyses presented here clearly indicate that the more well-
deﬁned beneﬁts (reduced wastewater management require-
ments and avoided synthetic fertilizer production) exceed the
environmental impacts of urine collection, processing, and
transport, suggesting that further eﬀorts to develop such
systems are warranted.

records.full_text:
Journal of Cleaner Production 408 (2023) 137227
Available online 19 April 2023
0959-6526/© 2023 Published by Elsevier Ltd.
Incorporation of main line impact into life cycle assessment of nutrient 
recovery from reject water using novel membrane contactor technology 
Sofia H¨ogstrand a, Juho Uzkurt Kaljunen b,*, Raed A. Al-Juboori b,c, Karin J¨onsson a, 
Hamse Kjerstadius d, Anna Mikola a, Greg Peters e, Magdalena Svanstr¨om e 
a Water and Environmental Engineering at the Department of Chemical Engineering, Lund University, PO Box 124, SE-221 00, Lund, Sweden 
b Water and Environmental Engineering Research Group, Department of Built Environment, Aalto University, P.O. Box 15200, Aalto, FI-00076, Espoo, Finland 
c NYUAD Water Research Center, New York University-Abu Dhabi Campus, P.O. Box 129188, Abu Dhabi, United Arab Emirates 
d NSVA, Box 2022, 250 02, Helsingborg, Sweden 
e Division of Environmental Systems Analysis, Department of Technology Management and Economics, Chalmers University of Technology, 41296, Gothenburg, Sweden   
A R T I C L E  I N F O   
Handling Editor: Maria Teresa Moreira  
Keywords: 
Nitrogen 
Phosphorus 
Municipal wastewater 
Recovery 
Main line impact model 
A B S T R A C T   
Wastewater treatment plant (WWTP) nutrient recovery has recently gained traction in the search for new 
pathways for fertilizer production. In particular, concentrated waste streams such as reject water from sludge 
digestion are suitable. The environmental impact of a novel nutrient recovery technology using a membrane 
contactor (NPHarvest) was examined with an environmental life cycle assessment (LCA). Impact hotspots were 
benchmarked against a comparable technology (struvite precipitation and ammonia stripping), and the impacts 
of the two technologies were found to be similar for most studied environmental impact categories. To allow for 
the inclusion of effects on other parts of the WWTP while limiting the general system boundaries to the reject 
water treatment, a novel approach to capture the main line impact was developed. The effects on the main line 
contributed substantially to the overall results. The overall results indicated clear nutrient recovery benefits 
related to substituted materials in mineral fertilizer production. Additionally, reject water nutrient recovery 
provided even greater benefits due to reduced N2O emissions and the reduced use of precipitation chemicals in 
the WWTP main line. Nonetheless, both nutrient removal and recovery were necessary for the two technologies 
to reach a net zero climate impact in their current pilot scales. Further development of the NPHarvest tech­
nology—such as mitigating NH3 emissions, exploring alternative input chemicals and optimizing energy con­
sumption (especially for crystallizing the ammonium salt solution that is produced)—is recommended before 
full-scale implementation.   
1. Introduction 
The current production of nitrogen (N) fertilizer uses considerable 
amounts of primarily fossil-based energy when N is captured from the 
atmosphere through the Haber-Bosch process (Kyriakou et al., 2020). 
This leads to greenhouse gas emissions contributing to climate change 
(Smith et al., 2020). The production of phosphorus (P) fertilizer, on the 
other hand, requires the finite element phosphorus (P), which is regar­
ded by the EU as a critical resource that should be recycled (European 
Commission, 2014). Lately, the COVID-19 pandemic, the increasing fuel 
and energy prices and the war in Ukraine have accelerated the discus­
sion regarding the danger of global fertilizer dependency on a limited set 
of countries (Hammond and Gadanakis) calling for alternative pathways 
for fertilizer production from local sources. 
One possible pathway is nutrient recovery from municipal and in­
dustrial wastewater streams. Concentrated streams such as reject water 
from dewatered, anaerobically digested sludge in wastewater treatment 
plants (WWTPs) are particularly suitable (Shaddel et al., 2019), see 
Fig. 1. Besides recovering nutrients, implementing nutrient removal 
technologies on reject water can improve the WWTP effluent quality and 
reduce the need for precipitation chemicals and aeration energy in the 
main line (Amann et al., 2018). Furthermore, decreasing the N load in 
the main line also has the potential of reducing direct emissions of 
nitrous oxide (N2O) (Kampschreur et al., 2009), a potent greenhouse gas 
that typically contributes substantially to the overall WWTP carbon 
footprint (Vasilaki et al., 2019). Technologies to recover single nutrients 
exist, but they can be expensive, energy-intensive or require substantial 
* Corresponding author. 
E-mail address: juho.kaljunen@aalto.fi (J. Uzkurt Kaljunen).  
Contents lists available at ScienceDirect 
Journal of Cleaner Production 
journal homepage: www.elsevier.com/locate/jclepro 
https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jclepro.2023.137227 
Received 14 October 2022; Received in revised form 28 February 2023; Accepted 18 April 2023

Journal of Cleaner Production 408 (2023) 137227
2
chemical input (Eekert et al., 2012). 
At Aalto University in Finland, the water and environmental engi­
neering research group has developed a novel technology named 
NPHarvest, which recovers N and P in separate products: the former as 
pure ammonium salt and the latter as a P- and calcium-rich sludge-like 
product (Uzkurt Kaljunen et al., 2021). The technology is based on a 
pre-treatment with precipitation of P and pH increase through lime 
addition with subsequent N recovery by ammonia transfer through 
gas-permeable, hydrophobic membranes. The strengths of this tech­
nology include its potentially low energy need and tolerance for high 
suspended solids levels (Uzkurt Kaljunen et al., 2021). 
Previously, the economic feasibility and applicability of various 
wastewaters have been investigated for the NPHarvest technology 
(Uzkurt Kaljunen et al., 2021). It was established that streams with high 
nutrient content and low concentrations of suspended solids (200 mg 
SS/l), such as reject water from well-functioning digested sludge dew­
atering, were preferable for the process to function most efficiently and 
economically. Prior to full-scale implementation, it is important to also 
identify the hotspots of environmental impact to guide the development 
of the technology towards environmental sustainability. Before 
commercialization, a comparison to alternative and potentially 
competing technologies must also be done. 
One way of performing an environmental assessment is through a life 
cycle assessment (LCA). LCA has been widely applied since the 1990s 
(Tillman et al., 1998) to wastewater treatment in general (Corominas 
et al., 2013, 2020) as well as for nutrient removal and recovery

S. H¨ogstrand et al.

Journal of Cleaner Production 408 (2023) 137227
3
technologies (Lam et al., 2020). The goal of this study was to perform an 
LCA on the novel NPHarvest technology and to benchmark its hotspots 
against the established technology. An alternative technology on similar 
scale as NPHarvest employing struvite precipitation for P recovery and 
ammonia stripping for N recovery was selected as these are commer­
cially available recovery technologies. When comparing different pro­
cesses of varying technology readiness levels (TRL) (European 
Commission, 2017), however, there are issues with how to make that 
comparison as reliable and relevant as possible (Remy, 2015). An LCA 
that is exploring a novel technology is generally challenging because 
access to primary data for full-scale operation is usually non-existent, 
and many uncertainties exist about both the technology itself and the 
system it will be implemented within. Studies of this kind are sometimes 
referred to as prospective LCA and may involve various attempts to scale 
up and make various future scenarios (see, e.g., Arvidsson et al., 2018). 
This article was based on a study described in a Svenskt Vatten 
Utveckling report (H¨ogstrand et al., 2022) that contains further details 
on the technologies and the trials. Data used in the assessment was 
therefore for pilot scale; no attempts were made to scale up the tech­
nologies, but critical parameters were tested in sensitivity analyses. 
In this study, we limited the system to the reject water treatment as 
delimited by the red line in Fig. 1 and excluded other parts of the WWTP. 
But we also developed and used an innovative approach to include the 
influence of the reject water treatment on the WWTP main line, here­
after called the main line impact (MLI). The significance of MLI has 
remained elusive in the literature, even Corominas et al. (2020) did not 
account for it in their guideline for wastewater treatment LCAs. MLI has 
been acknowledged as potentially important in some previous LCA 
studies (e.g. Amann et al., 2018; Remy, 2015; Schaubroeck et al., 2015). 
In some studies, aspects of MLI are omitted entirely.(Rodriguez-Garcia 
et al., 2014), while in other studies, single aspects of the MLI have been 
considered, such as decreased aeration energy and precipitation chem­
ical need (Amann et al., 2018; Remy, 2015). A few studies also included 
N2O emissions from the biological main line processes (Longo et al., 
2017; Schaubroeck et al., 2015). However, emission factors for N2O are 
highly uncertain (Delre et al., 2017). We could not find a study where 
the MLI was included more completely, and therefore we set our goal to 
encompass many factors in a single study to reveal the significant impact 
that side stream processes have on WWTPs, and suggesting a model, 
allowing for better inclusion in future studies. 
2. Methods 
The LCA in this study has been performed using ISO 14040:2006 as a 
starting point. 
2.1. Goal and scope definition 
The goal of this study was to identify environmental hotspots for the 
operation of the NPHarvest technology to inform further process 
development. While primarily focusing on describing reject water 
technology in detail, the influence on the main line of the WWTP was 
also considered. The identified hotspots were benchmarked against 
those of a functionally comparable technology already on the market. 
NPHarvest technology at TRL = 5–6 was benchmarked against an 
alternative technology at TRL = 8 using data for a similar scale and 
operating on the same wastewater stream. Different scenarios were 
modelled to understand the significance of different parameters and the 
importance of including MLI in the model. 
2.1.1. Functional unit 
The choice of functional unit (FU) will have a considerable impact on 
what the LCA will show, so it is important to choose one that can 
appropriately answer the questions at hand. For multifunctional pro­
cesses, such as wastewater treatment with nutrient recovery, several 
choices are possible. In this study, the assessed technologies have two 
main functions: wastewater treatment (process-related) and nutrient 
recovery (product-related). A process perspective is relevant for WWTP 
utilities and operators when deciding whether a certain recovery tech­
nology is to be implemented or how to optimize it. Conversely, an LCA 
with a product perspective is more relevant when optimizing or 
choosing between different recycled nutrient products based on envi­
ronmental impact (Lam et al., 2020). The main target groups for this 
study are WWTP utilities and NPHarvest technology developers. Hence, 
the process-related FU ‘treatment of 1 cubic metre (m3) of incoming 
reject water from dewatered, digested sludge’ was applied. The reject 
water’s characteristics were ~1200 mg N/l, ~90 mg P/l and ~100 mg 
SS/l. The treatment is not defined to any specific degree. Instead, the 
differences in treatment are accounted for in the MLI and the amount of 
generated fertilizers. See Sections 2.1.3 and 2.2.6. for more details. 
2.1.2. Type of LCA 
In this study, the focus was to find hotspots of environmental impact 
for the technologies, hence accounting LCA (ALCA) methodology was 
applied. Accounting LCA, as opposed to consequential LCA (CLCA), al­
lows for inclusion also of activities that are the same for all compared 
systems which is important for finding hotspots and improvement op­
portunities and will avoid confusing this specific audience with marginal 
and market effects. Therefore, the average data was selected rather than 
marginal data. Although it can be argued that multifunctionality should 
then be dealt with through allocation (see Heimersson et al., 2017), this 
has proven to be a challenge in an LCA of nutrient recovery technologies 
(see also Lam et al., 2020). Therefore, multifunctionality was handled 
according to common practice (Heimersson et al., 2019; Lam et al., 
2020) through system expansion by substitution. However, as is always 
the case when using datasets from databases and other sources for 
background processes, multifunctionality may have been handled in 
other ways in these parts. 
2.1.3. Study setting 
During summer 2021, the NPHarvest pilot (2.4 m3/d) and the 
alternative technology (struvite precipitation and ammonia stripping, 
12 m3/d) were both installed at the RecoLab test site in ¨Oresund WWTP 
in Helsingborg, Sweden, and tests were performed with black water 
digestate. NPHarvest pilot was operated for two months, and samples 
were collected on average three times per week. Data for the alternative 
technology relied more on literature sources (see Section 2.2). Black 
water digestate has a high nutrient content and low concentration of 
suspended solids and thus have properties very similar to that of reject 
water from well-functioning digested sludge dewatering, allowing for a 
generalization of results across wastewaters. This generalization is less 
relevant if the reject water does not originate from an efficient dew­
atering process because then there would be higher SS content. Further, 
WWTPs using chemical P removal typically have lower P content in their 
reject waters. However, if the WWTP employs biological P removal, like 
¨Oresund WWTP, and has an efficient dewatering process, this general­
ization is valid. 
NPHarvest reached 90% P and 50% N recovery efficiencies while the 
alternative technology reached 90% recovery efficiency for both nutri­
ents. Different recovery performances lead to different amount of nu­
trients recycling back to the main line which consequently affects energy 
and chemical consumption as well as emissions of N2O. This was 
captured in our study by the development and use of an MLI model, 
which is a novelty of this paper (see Section 2.2.6.). Furthermore, 
different quantities of recovered nutrient products are obtained, which 
was captured in the considered substitution of fertilizers. 
The pilot trial data from both technologies were used as such with no 
upscaling but rescaled to the functional unit of one cubic of incoming 
reject water. The MLI impacts were calculated per cubic meter of 
incoming reject water. The NPHarvest pilot (Fig. 2a, here shown for a 
digester reject input instead of the black water that was used in the 
actual trials) consists of two main parts: P recovery (functionally also 
S. H¨ogstrand et al.

Journal of Cleaner Production 408 (2023) 137227
4
pre-treatment) and N recovery (Konola, 2019; Righetto et al., 2021; 
Uzkurt Kaljunen et al., 2021). In the pre-treatment, Ca(OH)2 is added for 
the precipitation of P and organic material and a simultaneous increase 
in pH. In the subsequent N recovery, ammonia is stripped from the 
clarified wastewater through hydrophobic, gas-permeable membranes 
at atmospheric pressure. Sulphuric acid (H2SO4) circulating inside the 
membrane fibres reacts with ammonia to form ammonium sulphate 
((NH4)2SO4). Two products are thus extracted from the process: a P-rich 
sludge-like product and liquid ammonium sulphate. 
The alternative technology (Fig. 2b, here also shown for a digester 
reject input instead of black water), developed by Ekobalans Fenix AB, 
consists of the so-called eco:P (struvite recovery) and eco:N (ammonia 
stripping with subsequent absorption and crystallization) processes 
(Kjerstadius et al., 2016, 2017; Styles et al., 2018). Hence, the nutrient 
products struvite (NH4MgPO4⋅6H2O) and ammonium sulphate – both 
solids – are recovered. 
2.1.4. System boundaries 
The studied system is depicted in Fig. 3. Included within the scope 
were the production and transport of used chemicals, energy and rele­
vant consumables, direct emissions from the treatment technologies, 
management and transport of nutrient products, substituted mineral 
fertilizer and the influence of the treated water on the WWTP main line 
(the MLI). Raw material extraction was included in background datasets. 
Infrastructure was excluded from the scope of this study due to its 
typically lower environmental impact than operation for these types of 
technologies, owing to a long facility life span (Lundie et al., 2004). It is 
noteworthy that the guide by Corominas et al., (2020) recommends 
inclusion of infrastructure into the scope. However, the discussion in the 
guide and the source they refer to, Morera et al. (2017), are for large 
WWTPs with buildings and large concrete tanks. The nutrient recovery 
technologies included in this paper do not contain this kind of infra­
structure but instead plastic containers and pipes and thus, the exclusion 
of process infrastructure is justified (Corominas et al., 2020; Morera 
et al., 2017). 
The system takes in reject water from dewatered, digested sludge. 
Therefore, management upstream (i.e., sewage system, main line 
treatment and sludge management) was not considered. The system 
ends when treated water is recirculated back to the main line WWTP, 
which means that there are no direct emissions to a recipient from the 
system. It was assumed that observing legal limits on water quality 
would result in similar effluent characteristics regardless of the reject 
water treatment option. Due to the differences in removal efficiency 
between the two reject water treatment options, the cost and other ef­
forts of adhering to the specified limits may, however, vary. These dif­
ferences were instead accounted for by an attempt to model the most 
important changes in the main line that would result from implementing 
each of the reject water treatment options but keeping to the legal limits. 
Further details are given in Section 2.2.6. In WWTPs which struggle with 
their effluent quality limits, reject water treatment can bring additional 
relief also to the recipient. However, this aspect falls outside the scope of 
this study. 
The recovered nutrient products will likely become raw materials for 
the fertilizer industry and are not finished fertilizer products.

5
Nevertheless, substitution of mineral fertilizer was considered to happen 
when the recovered nutrient products reached the fertilizer factory gate, 
and based on mass of N and P. This is a simplification as the recovered 
nutrient products would in fact need some further processing that could 
involve the use of energy and chemicals as well as losses of nutrients, 
and to different degrees for the two technologies. This has to be kept in 
mind when interpreting the results. 
Geographically, data regarding transport distances were based on 
the location of the ¨Oresund WWTP, Helsingborg, Sweden. Swedish 
conditions were assumed for electricity and heat mixes in the base case 
scenarios. Average European conditions were considered for chemical 
production due to data availability. Data for the reject water treatment 
technologies were, as far as possible, measured (primary) data from the 
trials at ¨Oresund WWTP or literature data for the same process setup. 
Other data were from the literature or the databases GaBi Professional 
Database v 10.6 (Sphera, 2022) and ecoinvent version 3.7.1 (ecoinvent, 
2022). 
Regarding the time horizon, it was assumed that the NPHarvest 
technology would require further development before full-scale imple­
mentation but that this could be achieved within a few years and that the 
current conditions regarding transport and energy systems and other 
aspects would be sufficiently relevant to use in the present study. The 
most recent data have been sought. In general, the data from GaBi was 
more up-to-date (v. 10.6) than the data from ecoinvent (v. 3.7.1, valid 
until 2020). 
2.2. Life cycle inventory 
Data was gathered for a base case and also for different scenarios that 
tested important assumptions or uncertainties or explored variations, 
see Table 2 and Tables SI1.1 and SI1.2. The base case and scenarios are 
further described in Section 2.4. 
For NPHarvest, measured data for the investigated scale was used 
(H¨ogstrand et al., 2022), except for ammonia emissions, which were 
calculated through a mass balance. For the alternative technology, the 
technical data from the recovery process was incomplete and needed to 
be complemented with data from the literature; only electricity and 
consumables consumption and ammonia emissions were measured 
on-site. Additional data were mainly taken from Kjerstadius et al. (2017) 
and the tender by Ekobalans Fenix AB on the installed facility at Rec­
oLab, ¨Oresund WWTP. The former reported on the same technology and 
location, but as a prospective study for a larger scale (72 m3/d), thus 
corresponding to a best case scenario. The latter, however, provided less 
optimized data for the same technology and scale as the present study 
tests (12 m3/d). Estimation of MgCl2 consumption is based on Kjer­
stadius et al. (2017) and data on struvite precipitation from liquid phase 
was estimated based on two different sources ((Amann et al., 2018; 
Remy, 2015)). Estimations of consumption of NaOH and H2SO4 are 
based on Kjerstadius et al. (2017), the tender by Ekobalans Fenix AB and 
Eekert et al. (2012). Full details on values and references are fund in 
Table SI1.3. The LCA software GaBi v. 10.6. was used to handle data and

on the main line of the wastewater treatment and of the recovered nutrient-rich flows. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader

facilitate further calculations. The datasets used are found in 
Table SI1.4. Table 1 provides a summary of the input data. 
2.2.1. Chemicals 
For NPHarvest, Ca(OH)2 was used for precipitation of P and organics 
as well as for pH adjustment, and H2SO4 was used for ammonia stripping 
and cleaning. Regarding the alternative technology, MgCl2 was used for 
struvite precipitation, NaOH for pH adjustment, H2SO4 as a stripping 
solution and citric acid for cleaning. NPHarvest utilized H2SO4 because 
it cleans and maintains the hydrophobicity of the membranes better 
than the safer citric acid. In explorative scenarios, H2SO4 was exchanged 
with HNO3 and H3PO4. In the MLI model (see details in Section 2.2.6), 
FeCl3 and AlCl3 were the precipitating agents that were considered 
reducible. Regarding the production of MgCl2, no process currently ex­
ists in the databases that were consulted. Instead, an estimation based on 
the production of NaCl was used as in Raymond et al. (2021). 
2.2.2. Energy 
For the base cases of both technologies, Swedish conditions 
regarding electricity (40% nuclear, 40% hydro, 11% wind, 6% biomass 
and 3% miscellaneous (Sphera, 2022)) were assumed. We did not, 
however, make sure that this was also the case in aggregated back­
ground datasets. The choice of electricity mix was evaluated in scenarios 
with Finnish (33% nuclear, 22% water, 16% biomass, 12% coal and gas 
(Sphera, 2022)) and European average electricity (25% nuclear, 10% 
water, 10% wind, 40% coal and gas (Sphera, 2022)). For the alternative 
technology, heating was considered to be supplied by district heating. 
However, as no dataset for Swedish conditions was available, a process 
was constructed according to Table SI1.5. 
To make the comparison between technologies as relevant as 
possible, a theoretically calculated energy demand for thermal drying of 
the liquid N product of NPHarvest was added (details in Section SI1.4). 
No further management of the other nutrient products was considered. 
2.2.3. Direct air emissions 
Emissions of CO2 and CH4 were theoretically estimated based on 
Henry’s law and were based on the same influent for both technologies 
and all scenarios, but as CO2 emissions in this context are deemed 
biogenic by IPCC (IPCC, 2019, 2006), direct CO2 emissions were 
excluded from further evaluation. Recent studies, however, report 
ranges of fossil carbon in wastewater up to 28% of the incoming carbon 
content (Tseng et al., 2016). Nevertheless, when testing the inclusion of 
this in the model, no greater impact was noted on the overall result. 
Emissions of NH3 for NPHarvest were based on mass balance cal­
culations from flow and liquid concentration measurements (see Section 
SI1.5.1) which introduced large uncertainties. NH3 emissions for the 
alternative technology were assumed to be zero, based on experimental 
data. Neither process generates N2O emissions. 
2.2.4. Miscellaneous 
The PTFE membranes used in NPHarvest were produced by Zeus 
Industrial Products Ltd. and modelled as by Holmquist (2020), assuming 
a five-year life expectancy. This can be considered to be conservative 
compared to figures reported for membranes in WWTPs: 5.5–10 years 
for hollow fibres in membrane bioreactors (MBRs) (Cote et al., 2012; 
Mailler et al., 2021), albeit fouling events and cleaning frequency are 
expected to be higher in MBRs compared to membrane contactors. For 
the alternative technology, the weekly exchanged PP filters were 
included in the model. Details are provided in Table SI1.7. 
Transport distances for the chemicals used are found in Table SI1.8. 
For the transportation of nutrient products, a distance of 76 km to fer­
tiliser factory was assumed as a worst case in order to reveal the po­
tential impact of not drying the product onsite. 
2.2.5. Substituted fertilizers 
The obtained nutrient products were substituted with mineral fer­
tilizers based on nutrient content. For each FU, 0.47 kg N and 0.09 kg P 
were recovered (and thus substituted) for the NPHarvest base case. In 
the phosphoric acid scenario, the values were 0.47 kg N and 0.44 kg P 
(0.35 kg in ammonium phosphate and 0.09 kg in P- and calcium-rich 
sludge-like product), respectively. The alternative technology recov­
ered 0.9 kg N/FU in ammonium sulphate and 0.07 kg P/FU and 0.03 kg 
N/FU in struvite. N content was considered to substitute ammonium 
nitrate (AN 33.5%), and P-content was considered to substitute triple 
superphosphate (TSP, 46% P2O5). The degree of substitution was set to 
1:1 for both N and P. 
2.2.6. Main line impact 
A model that would allow for understanding the influence of reject 
water treatment without modelling the entire main line was developed. 
It was assumed that the operation of the plant will be set to fulfil effluent 
quality demands and that the effort to fulfil these demands after addition 
of reject water treatment was modelled. Based on the previous literature, 
it was decided to correlate P removal with lower use of precipitation 
chemicals, and N removal with decreased need for aeration energy as 
well as lower N2O emissions. The aeration process is energy-intensive 
due to compressors that transfer atmospheric air to the treatment 
containers.

mol active substance (Fe3+ or Al3+) per mol Premoved was considered. In 
practice, however, chemical precipitation technologies need to add 
chemicals in excess (usually 1.5 mol/mol (Jansen et al., 2019)), whereas 
biological P removal processes need fewer chemicals (at ¨Oresund 
WWTP, 0.4 mol/mol is added (NSVA, 2021)). To account for these po­
tential variations, a range of 0.4–1.5 mol/mol was tested in a sensitivity 
analysis. Similar to Amann et al. (2018), it was decided to use FeCl3 
(13.8% Fe3+) as the model chemical, as this was also the precipitation 
chemical used at ¨Oresund WWTP (NSVA, 2021). However, the choice of 
precipitation chemical was further evaluated in the sensitivity analysis 
by also considering AlCl3. 
When estimating electricity savings resulting from a reduced need 
for aeration, a theoretical estimation similar to Remy (2015) and 
Amann et al. (2018) was used. The oxygen demand was deemed to be 
4.57 (nitrification) – 2.86 (denitrification) = 1.71 g O2/g Nremoved, 
which is also reported by Jansen et al. (2019). Energy demand was set to 
0.5 kWh/kg O2 (Amann et al., 2018; Bengtsson et al., 2019; Remy, 
2015). This would mean a total of 3.1 MJ/kg Nremoved. 
However, in practice, the specific energy for aeration could reach 
~12–25 MJ/kg Nremoved (UNIVPM, 2018), and this assumption was 
therefore tested in a sensitivity analysis. In Sweden, the average energy 
need for aeration has been reported to be 15 kWh/(PE, year) (Bengtsson 
et al., 2019), which would mean a specific energy need of 17.6 MJ/kg 
Nremoved (assuming an N load of 14 g N/(PE, day) and 60% removal 
efficiency), which is within the aforementioned range. ¨Oresund WWTP 
has reported that their aeration energy consumption is 41.4 MJ/kg 
Nremoved which, however, is very unoptimized. Thus, 25 MJ/kg Nremoved 
was selected as a scenario in the MLI model. 
N2O emissions are today considered one of the main environmental 
impacts of a WWTP, while also being difficult to estimate (Vasilaki et al., 
2019). No N2O measurements exist for the ¨Oresund WWTP, and it was 
therefore decided to use an emission factor (EF) of 1.6% of the removed 
N load being emitted as N2O (IPCC, 2019). This is similar to the EF of 
1.9% reported by Kosonen et al. (2016) for a Finnish WWTP. The general 
EF of 1.6% was also used by Awaitey (2021), who considered an EF 
range of 0.8–2.1%. In this study, that same range has been used in the 
sensitivity analysis. Valkova et al. (2021) have made a recommendation 
that instead of a flat emission factor, assessing the WWTP operational 
efficiency and basing N2O emission on that would be a better approach, 
possibly with the help of modelling (Rodriguez-Garcia et al., 2012; 
Valkova et al., 2021). 
2.3. Impact assessment 
The results were analysed to the mid-point level in the categories of 
global warming, eutrophication, acidification, ecotoxicity, human 
toxicity and abiotic depletion of elements. These categories, together 
with stratospheric ozone depletion, are among the most commonly used 
within LCAs on wastewater and sludge management and nutrient re­
covery (Corominas et al., 2020; Lam et al., 2020; Yoshida et al., 2013). 
In this study, stratospheric ozone depletion was not considered because 
it was perceived to have lower relevance (see arguments in e.g. 
Svanstr¨om et al., 2016). Furthermore, when testing this assumption by 
checking the contribution of stratospheric ozone depletion on the 
end-point level, the impact on human health was negligible. Primary 
energy demand from renewable as well as non-renewable resources was 
also considered (Arvidsson and Svanstr¨om, 2016). 
Several different methods for impact assessment are in contemporary 
use. The latest versions of the methods recommended in the ILCD 
handbook (EC-JRC, 2011) were chosen, except for eutrophication and 
acidification, for which the latest (i.e. 2016) version of CML 2001 
(Guin´ee, 2001) was used. This reflects recent assessments of e.g. 
Mikosch et al. (2022) that the CML approach for these categories is 
better in several respects than the PEF recommendation (FazioBi­
ganzioliDe Laurentiis et al., 2018) to use Sepp¨al¨a et al. (2006) and 
Porsche et al. (2008). The environmental impact was then calculated as 
implemented in the latest GaBi LCA software (v. 10.6). See Table SI2.1 
for a detailed description of chosen categories, units, and methods. 
2.4. Interpretation 
To understand the potential effect of various technical choices, a set 
of scenarios was created (see Table 2). The NPHarvest base case (B.C.) is 
the process configuration used in the pilot trial with a theoretical 
calculation of energy for drying the N product and the Swedish context 
regarding the electricity mix. The two scenarios for chemical variation 
explore the use of either HNO3 or H3PO4 instead of H2SO4. The no- 
drying (No D.) scenario excludes the theoretically calculated energy 
needed for drying the N product and instead takes into consideration the 
extra weight in the liquid product that needs to be transported to the 
fertilizer industry. The energy-related scenarios (FI el. and EU el.) 
change the choice of the electricity mix. Instead of a Swedish average, 
they employ Finnish and European average (EU-28) electricity mixes, 
respectively, for everything modelled in the top layer. Different sce­
narios were also made for the alternative technology (Alt.), but only its 
base case (H2SO4, Swedish electricity and heat mixes) is shown here. 
(Full results are in SI3). 
To also explore the sensitivity to different uncertainties and as­
sumptions in the modelling of the MLI, a sensitivity analysis was per­
formed for important parameters (Table 3). These included the N2O EF 
(0.8–2.1%), the molar ratio (0.4–1.5) and type (Fe3+ or Al3+) of pre­
cipitation chemical as well as the energy required for aeration (3.1–25 
MJ/kg N). 
3. Results and discussion 
3.1. Environmental hotspots for both technologies 
Fig. 4 displays the environmental impact of the different activities of 
the NPHarvest system for all the investigated impact categories in 
different scenarios as well as for the alternative technology. 
3.1.1. Climate change (GWP) 
The climate impact (GWP) of the NPHarvest base case (excluding the 
benefits related to fertilizers and MLI) amounted to 7.5 kg CO2-eq./m3, 
which would correspond to 16.4 kg CO2-eq./(person, year) if the black 
water from all the Swedish population was collected and treated in this 
way, it would amount to 0.33% of the total Swedish GHG emissions per 
capita and year (calculated using Table SI4.1). For the alternative 
technology, the corresponding values are 19.8 kg CO2-eq./(person, year) 
and 0.40%. These are low numbers, but it should be noted that this is 
only a portion of the total impact of the wastewater treatment as reject 
water treatment is only a part of it. The net results (also considering the 
benefits) were below zero (0.3 and −4.3 kg CO2-eq./(person, year) if 
calculated in the same way as before) for the NPHarvest base case and 
the alternative, respectively, indicating a potential decrease in impacts if

the benefits can be realized. The numbers can be related to numbers 
from the study by Gustavsson and Tumlin (2013) on the carbon foot­
prints of 16 Scandinavian WWTPs, which varied between 7 and 108 kg 
CO2-eq./(PE,1 year) with an average of 46 kg CO2-eq./(PE, year). In 
addition, Delre et al. (2019) reported carbon footprint values of 
0.15–0.66 kg CO2-eq/(Mg plant material input), where plant input 
material was mostly wastewater but also other waste flows (Delre et al., 
2019). This value translates to 11–48 kg CO2-eq/(PE, year), if 1 Mg plant 
material input is assumed to be 5 PE (assumption based on sewage 
biological oxygen demand (BOD) concentration of 300 mg/l (Bodkhe, 
2009)), which is lower than Gustavsson and Tumlin (2013) but in the 
same range. This shows that without benefits included, impacts are in 
the lower range of those of an actual full-scale WWTP, which indicates 
that the results are reasonable but also stresses the importance of real­
izing the benefits (decreased MLI and substituted mineral fertilizers). If 
the benefits are achieved, the implementation of reject water treatment 
on the side stream may decrease the carbon footprint of the WWTP. 
However, if they are not fulfilled, this may lead to an increase of the 
footprint of the WWTP. 
The largest environmental hotspot for NPHarvest was the Ca(OH)2 
production, and its size was the same for all the six scenarios. For the 
alternative, the main cause of impact was NaOH production. The 
importance of the choice of energy mix became apparent when 
comparing the B.C. to the FI el. and EU el. scenarios. The Swedish 
electricity mix consists mainly of fossil-free energy sources such as hydro 
and nuclear power, whereas Finnish and European energy mixes consist 
of more fossil-based ditto (see Section 2.2.2). For the EU el. scenario, the 
energy activity became the main hotspot of environmental impact and 
resulted in a net total impact on the climate for the studied system. Due 
to the small impact of the Swedish energy mix on the climate, the dif­
ference between the B.C. and the No D. scenarios is not very apparent. 
However, the calculated energy demand for N-product drying made up 
~72% of the total energy need for the process in the base case, meaning 
that in the Finnish or European contexts, the difference would be sub­
stantial when drying is disregarded. For the alternative technology, the 
influence of heating was low as district heating was assumed. This is a 
relevant choice for Sweden and Finland but might not be as relevant in
