other countries. Other relevant options could be to either use a natural 
gas boiler, which would give a notable contribution to the climate 
impact, or to use some biogas produced onsite. However, this would 
possibly lead to that natural gas use increases elsewhere and might 
therefore have the same overall effect, depending on how this is 
modelled. 
The main avoided impact by both technologies was mainly related to 
N2O emissions in the WWTP main line and the substituted production of 
mineral N fertilizer. Due to its great impact on the overall result, the 
estimates of N2O emissions are further discussed in Section 3.2.1. 
Nevertheless, if the benefits are realized and if the Swedish context is 
assumed, the NPHarvest technology appears to be climate-neutral 
already in this TRL 5–6 setup. 
3.1.2. Eutrophication (EP) and acidification (AP) 
For both the eutrophication (EP) and acidification (AP) impact cat­
egories, the NH3 emissions from NPHarvest dominated all its scenarios. 
These emissions amounted to 0.2 kg NH3/m3 or if this technology was 
applied to black water from the whole Swedish population, 0.44 kg/ 
(person, year). This compares to 8.6% of the total Swedish NH3 emis­
sions per capita for the year 2019 (calculated using Table SI4.1). In other 
words, these emissions were substantial and need to be mitigated for the 
process to be relevant for full-scale implementation. The most likely 
reason for these emissions was the lack of airtight sealing, leaving escape 
routes for ammonia. This would not be an issue in a full-scale facility. 
However, NH3 emissions were not measured but were based on mass 
balance calculations (elaborated on in Section SI1.5), and these values 
may therefore involve higher levels of uncertainty than if they had been 
measured for the pilot. Hence, it is likely that these emissions would be 
substantially lower in a future, full-scale implementation. It is important 
to remember that maintaining the N in the system and having it in the N- 
product flow can also be very important for the economic performance. 
Next to NH3 emissions, electricity production and hence the choice of 
electricity mix was the most important aspect for NPHarvest for both 
impact categories (Fig. SI3.1). However, for acidification, the choice of 
reagent acid was also important. H3PO4 and H2SO4 exhibit a greater 
impact than HNO3. Regarding the alternative technology, the main 
cause of impact in the eutrophication category was the production of 
NaOH and citric acid, whereas for the acidification category, the pro­
duction of H2SO4 and NaOH had the greatest impact. 
The avoided eutrophication impacts were mainly due to avoided N2O 
emissions and the avoided use of precipitation chemicals. However, for 
the H3PO4 scenario, the substituted amount of P fertilizer (TSP) repre­
sented the greatest benefit, which can be explained by the fact that its 
production is known for contributing to the release of P to recipients 
(Svanstr¨om et al., 2016). Avoided acidification impacts were also mainly 
related to the substitution of mineral P fertilizers. 
3.1.3. Toxicity (ETP, HTP c. and HTP n.c.) 
Typically, toxicity impacts are dominated by impacts related to 
metals due to their persistence in the environment. The disposal of fly 
ash from coal combustion and associated heavy metals often have a 
strong impact on results (Roos, 2017), and this kind of activity may 
appear in many parts of a product’s life cycle. 
In our study, regarding the impact categories of ecotoxicity (ETP) 
and human toxicity – cancer (HTP c.), the results were rather similar 
with respect to the dominating activity for all investigated scenarios: the 
avoided production of precipitation chemicals. This resulted in net 
savings in all scenarios. For human toxicity – non cancer (HTP n.c.), 
however, Ca(OH)2 production as well as the choice of electricity mix had 
a considerable impact on the overall result, which did not indicate 
overall savings. The greatest avoided impact was still related to pre­
cipitation chemicals. 
For the alternative technology, the pattern for all toxicity categories 
was similar to the NPHarvest base case, except for a greater impact from 
the production of heat and citric acid. However, for ETP and HTP c., the 
avoided precipitation chemicals in the main line made up for these 
impacts. 
3.1.4. Resources (ADP e and PED) 
For abiotic depletion of elements (ADP e), the net results for all 
scenarios for NPHarvest were below zero, which indicates a net saving of 
mineral resources. All scenarios presented themselves rather similarly 
except for the H3PO4 scenario, where the impact from chemicals (H3PO4 
itself) was considerably greater than for the other scenarios due to the 
demand for the finite element P. However, the benefit from substituted 
fertilizers (TSP) was also substantially greater in this scenario, resulting 
in a net impact similar to other NPHarvest scenarios. In effect, with the 
addition of P in H3PO4 already in reject water treatment, fertilizer 
production can be considered to start already in this step. The alterna­
tive technology, on the other hand, exhibited a net result above zero due 
to the use of NaCl and colemanite (Elginoz et al., 2020) in the production 
of NaOH. 
Regarding primary energy demand (PED), the difference between 
scenarios was more pronounced, especially for the ones concerning 
variations in energy demand (No D.) or energy source (FI and EU el.). 
This highlights the importance of energy-efficient drying (e.g., through 
an initial membrane concentration step). For the B.C. scenario, the PED 
net value (including MLI but excluding substituted fertilizers to allow for 
comparison to other recycled fertilizers2) ranged from 180 to 480 MJ/kg 
N, depending on whether or not product drying was included. The 
alternative technology reached 410 MJ/kg N (N.B. that heat demand 
was estimated). When compared to values in the literature, it is evident 
that current PEDs for both studied technologies are at the higher end of 
the scale. Eekert et al. (2012) investigated various full-scale technologies 
for N recovery from wastewater and reported values in the range of 
95–156 MJ/kg N. However, this is not directly comparable to our system 
as Eekert et al. (2012) evaluated full-scale facilities that do not include P 
recovery or drying of the N product. For mineral N fertilizers and 
fossil-free production of ammonia through electrolysis (Green 
Ammonia), PEDs have been reported to be ~40 MJ/kg N (SOU, 2021) 
and ~60 MJ/kg N (Ahlgren et al., 2015), respectively. However, to fairly 
compare the wastewater-related technologies, the energy demand for N 
removal in the WWTPs needs to be included. Reference values for 
aeration in nitrification range between 12 and 25 MJ/kg N (UNIVPM, 
2018). 
It is important to consider the stage of technology development as 
well as scale. The current small scale of both the NPHarvest and the 
alternative technology makes us expect a higher specific energy demand 
than for mature and large-scale technologies. As the technologies in­
crease their TRL level in the coming years, energy-efficient drying for 
NPHarvest and a heat exchanger might be installed to minimize heat 
demand for the alternative technology, among other things. 
3.1.5. Comparison of technologies 
As shown in Fig. 4 (and Fig. SI3.2) and sections 3.1.1–3.1.4, there 
were both similarities and differences between environmental hotspots 
for NPHarvest and the alternative technology. For climate, toxicity and 
PED impact categories the hotspots were rather similar, whereas for 
eutrophication and acidification, NH3 emissions from NPHarvest 
completely dominated. Apart from these emissions, the main impacts for 
both technologies emanated from the production of energy and chem­
icals – mainly Ca(OH)2 for NPHarvest and NaOH for the alternative. 
The avoided impacts were also similar for both technologies. Of 
greatest importance were the avoided N2O emissions and the avoided 
use of precipitation chemicals in the main line. Consequently, the im­
pacts on the main line have a significant effect on the overall results, and 
proper estimation of these is necessary to ascertain relevance. The MLI 
2 In LCA modelling terms, this is equal to modelling the reject water treat­
ment as a fertilizer production activity. 
S. H¨ogstrand et al.

10
model is further discussed in Section 3.2.1. 
The benefit of recovering and recycling nutrients was also obvious 
from the result (the substituted fertilizers). However, this impact was 
not as substantial as the aforementioned MLI. Hence, in this respect, 
nutrient removal can be said to be more important than nutrient re­
covery. Nevertheless, both nutrient removal and recovery were neces­
sary for the two technologies to reach net zero in the climate change 
category. 
3.2. Sensitivity analysis for NPHarvest and evaluation of the MLI model 
3.2.1. MLI model 
This section discusses other LCA studies that have considered some 
impacts on the main line. We aimed to support the discussion with both 
technical papers and LCA papers. However, we did not find any tech­
nical papers that considered the main process implications. Most papers, 
like Gonz´alez-Martínez et al. (2021), report only the N recovery effi­
ciency and the side stream process performance. Sivalingam et al. 
(2020) included the main line in their process schematic to demonstrate 
where the side stream treatment solution is, but they did not elaborate 
on how decreased N concentration affects the main line treatment. In 
technical papers, the MLI is therefore clearly an underlying assumed 
benefit derived from the decreased N load. Since the MLI model had a 
large impact on the overall results (Fig. 4), it is important that it 
correctly reflects the potential influence on the main line of the WWTP. 
The sensitivity analysis (Fig. 5) showed that the model was highly 
responsive to choices such as the N2O EF, the molar ratio and the type of 
precipitation chemical. Energy for aeration did not, however, seem to 
affect the result much due to the low-carbon Swedish energy mix. With 
an energy mix that relies more on fossil resources, the energy savings 
might have a larger influence. 
Regarding direct N2O emissions, there is great variation between 
locations, depending on WWTP processes and local climate, among 
other things. For instance, Delre et al. (2017) reported a range in EF from 
<0.1% to 5.2% for five Scandinavian WWTPs. Kampschreur et al. (2008, 
2009) focused on N2O emissions from both main line (2009) and bio­
logical side stream (2008) treatment processes. They do not draw defi­
nite conclusions on reduced N2O emissions, but it is clear from their 
work 
that 
the 
biological 
side 
stream 
treatment, 
like 
the 
nitritation-anammox process (anaerobic ammonium oxidation), emits 
N2O. Schaubroeck et al. (2015) conducted an LCA study on a WWTP 
with a biological side stream process (DEMON) and found similar re­
sults: EF = 1–2%. One possible reason that earlier LCA studies did not 
account for decreased main line N2O emissions (even if N2O emissions 
were included in the study) could be that the side stream treatment 
processes they evaluated emit N2O with roughly equal emission factor as

11
the main line treatment process, but this has not been stated. This 
highlights the potential of chemical side stream treatment options (such 
as NPHarvest and the alternative technology) to mitigate N2O emissions 
from WWTPs, as well as the need to include these effects in LCA studies. 
The decreased precipitation chemical consumption as an MLI was 
considered in only two previous studies. Amann et al. (2018) evaluated a 
wide range of technologies and found that the effect of decreased 
chemical consumption is noticeable for cumulative energy demand, 
GWP and AP for technologies that treat liquid streams, like reject water. 
In their LCA study, Linderholm et al. (2012) assumed a 20% reduction in 
precipitation chemical consumption since 20% of P is bound in struvite. 
However, they did not discuss the relevance of this assumption to their 
results. 
The aeration energy consumption change is perhaps the most com­
mon MLI aspect to include, possibly because biological side stream 
treatment options have gained more attention over the last decades from 
researchers and industry (e.g. Schaubroeck et al., 2015). Longo et al. 
(2017), for example, stated that N removal in biological side stream 
treatment leads to decreased aeration needs in the main line. Amann 
et al. (2018) made an LCA of 18 P recovery technologies and took into 
account electricity savings due to N removal. They showed that reduced 
aeration demand has a noticeably decreasing effect on impact cate­
gories. Therefore, the decreased energy need is well-documented in LCA 
studies and should be included in LCA studies exploring side stream 
treatment options. 
Several studies took creative or unclear approaches to the MLI. 
Rodriguez-Garcia et al. (2014), who also assessed side-stream technol­
ogies using LCA, instead selected to model the impact on the main line as 
a change in nutrients release to the recipient. The way their LCA system 
model was set up, the side stream pilot effluent was in effect released 
directly to the recipient, and the differences between side-stream tech­
nologies could therefore be captured in this way. We instead decided to 
model the MLI based on adherence to discharge limits. Bradford-Hartke 
et al. (2015) assessed P recovery in decentralized and centralized sys­
tems. For their struvite scenario, they used software (BioWin) to model 
struvite removal. However, they did not specify what MLI effects were 
considered and how. Remy (2015) compared ten P recovery technolo­
gies in an LCA and present a factor in their results called ‘Treatment of 
return load’ that is decreasing the impact for several impact categories. 
However, it is not clear what constitutes this factor. 
If performed appropriately, the MLI model may provide a way to 
discern differences between reject water treatment technologies, as the 
side stream process has a considerable impact on the cost and the 
environmental performance of the main line treatment. Unfortunately, 
not many studies take all relevant factors into account, even if they 
consider parts of the MLI in their LCA model. There are further con­
siderations that should be included. Methanol and calcium hydroxide 
consumption were omitted from this study because they were not rele­
vant in the case location (but they are used in WWTPs elsewhere). 
Hopefully, this study encourages inclusion and the further development 
of the MLI in LCA models that focus on WWTPs. 
3.2.2. Choice of chemicals 
From Figs. 4 and 5, it is apparent that the choice of chemicals affects 
the results. This choice is a foreground decision, i.e., performed by the 
technology implementer, and therefore deserves careful consideration. 
Furthermore, when comparing the NPHarvest B.C. to the Alt. scenario 
(see Fig. 4), it is evident how chemicals may contribute differently to the 
PED. The PED for NaOH (Alt.) is substantially greater than for Ca(OH)2 
(NPHarvest) even though the climate impact is almost the same. This 
may be at least partly explained by the energy used for the calcination of 
CaCO3 in Ca(OH)2 production (Vallejo Casta˜no et al., 2021) whereas 
NaOH production is relying on. Furthermore, the location of the pro­
duction site may greatly affect the environmental impact, e.g., in terms 
of local energy mixes and technology choices. This may be especially 
pertinent for chemicals with an energy-intensive production (such as 
NaOH; 8.2 MJ/kg NaOH (Kumar et al., 2021)) since the electricity mix 
may vary greatly between locations. In the present study, the datasets 
for chemical production were based on the European average due to 
data availability. A more carbon-lean energy mix would result in lower 
impacts related to chemicals for some impacts. 
In addition to altering environmental burdens, exchanging chemicals 
for others may also affect the process performance due to differences in 
chemical properties. A thorough analysis of possible implications is 
therefore important before altering the input chemicals. However, in 
early-stage technology development, testing the importance of different 
chemicals to environmental outcomes can hint at preferable develop­
ment routes, especially when scaling up from levels where environ­
mental impacts and costs have been of low importance. 
3.2.3. Modelling of nutrient products 
As earlier explained, nutrient products were modelled in a way that 
only considers their input to fertilizer production and only in terms of 
how N and P could replace mineral fertilizer. This means that there can 
be potential impacts, benefits and differences that are not sufficiently 
captured. For example, for NPHarvest, the impact of Ca(OH)2 was seen 
to be one of the main hotspots. However, the calcium ends up in the P 
product (7–8 wt%) so when applying the P product to soils in need of 
additional calcium, the function of soil amendment should potentially 
also be included in the LCA. Substitution of production of calcic products 
may in such a case be motivated. To allow for modelling in an LCA, a 
more thorough analysis of the recovered nutrient products, their 
nutrient and possible pollutant contents as well as their suitability and 
applicability as fertilizer or soil amendment products is needed. 
4. Conclusion and recommendations 
In the present study, the environmental impacts of the recently 
developed NPHarvest technology were determined by LCA, and the 
identified hotspots were compared to a functionally equivalent alter­
native technology (struvite precipitation and ammonia stripping). The 
LCA model was based on pilot scale (2.4 m3/d) operation and mea­
surements which were complemented with literature sources for the 
alternative technology (12 m3/d). To allow for the inclusion of impacts 
of the reject water treatment on the main line of the WWTP without 
having to model the entire plant, a model was developed that described 
this impact in a novel way. 
The results clearly show the benefits of recovering and recycling 
nutrients in terms of the avoided production of mineral fertilizers that 
can be achieved. Even greater potential benefits were observed 
regarding nutrient removal on account of the impacts on the WWTP 
main line due to avoided N2O emissions and avoided production of 
precipitation chemicals. Several impact categories demonstrate overall 
gains, while others demonstrate overall impacts. Both nutrient removal 
and recovery were necessary for the two technologies to reach net zero 
climate impact, which was nevertheless possible even on this TRL 5–6 
scale. However, this was only the case when a Swedish energy mix was 
considered. Nevertheless, it is clear that applying nutrient recovery 
technology to a WWTP offers benefits on top of the recovered nutrients 
products. 
The types of hotspots displayed by the two technologies were similar, 
and the impacts are in the same order of magnitude for most impact 
categories except for eutrophication and acidification where NH3 
emissions of NPHarvest dominated. In addition to these NH3 emissions, 
the main hotspots are related to chemical production (Ca(OH)2 for 
NPHarvest and NaOH for the alternative technology) and energy use. 
Hence, mitigation of NH3 emissions, exploration of alternative input 
chemicals and energy optimization are recommended before full-scale 
implementation of NPHarvest. If full-scale data had been available, 
the significance of energy consumption, especially for drying the N- 
product, would probably have been lower. 
The MLI model revealed a large contribution to the overall result in 
S. H¨ogstrand et al.

Journal of Cleaner Production 408 (2023) 137227
12
most categories. It is therefore important that the model correctly re­
flects the influence on the main line. It was shown that the model is 
particularly sensitive to modelling choices such as the N2O EF and type 
and ratio of the precipitation chemical. These factors depend on local 
conditions, and the model may therefore be adjusted to the specifics of 
each study context. However, there may be large uncertainties regarding 
the N2O EF, and further research and data acquisition in this area are 
recommended. Still, if utilized properly, the MLI model may provide 
possibilities to see the impact on the main line as well as to distinguish 
differences between reject water treatment technologies.

records.full_text:
Environmental Pollution 307 (2022) 119556
Available online 5 June 2022
0269-7491/© 2022 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.
Simultaneous recovery of nutrients and improving the biodegradability of 
waste algae hydrothermal liquid☆ 
Zi-Ying Hu a, Shun-Feng Jiang a, Xian-Yang Shi b, Hong Jiang a,* 
a Department of Applied Chemistry, University of Science and Technology of China, Hefei, 230026, China 
b Anhui Province Key Laboratory of Wetland Ecosystem Protection and Restoration, School of Resource and Environmental Engineering, Anhui University, Hefei, 230601, 
China   
A R T I C L E  I N F O   
Keywords: 
Hydrothermal carbonization 
Algae 
Struvite 
Three-dimensional fluorescence spectra 
Bioavailability 
Life cycle assessment 
A B S T R A C T   
The ever-increasing algae biomass due to eutrophication brings an enormous destruction and potential threat to 
the ecosystem. Hydrothermal carbonization (HTC) is a potential means converting algae to value added products 
such as sustainable bioenergy and biomaterials. However, the waste aqueous phase (AP) produced during the 
HTC of algae biomass needs to be treated carefully in case of the second pollution to environment. In this study, a 
model microbe (E. coli) was adopted for the microbial pretreatment of AP, by which the bioavailability of AP 
could be improved, and the nutrients could be reclaimed though struvite precipitation. Three-dimensional 
fluorescence spectra and GC-MS results illustrated that E. coli pretreatment could convert a large number of 
organic nitrogenous compounds to ammonia nitrogen by degrading aromatic protein substances and deami­
nating nitrogenous heterocyclic compounds. Afterwards, a serious of characterizations confirmed that 81.13% of 
ammonia nitrogen could be recovered as struvite though precipitation. Life cycle assessment indicates the cost of 
the two-step treatment process was much lower than that of conventional wastewater treatment processes, and is 
beneficial to environment. This work provides an environment-friendly strategy for the comprehensive utiliza­
tion of algae, which may contribute to alleviating the algal disasters and bring certain economic benefits though 
algal treatment.   
1. Introduction 
In recent years, as a result of urbanization and agriculturalization, 
the widespread discharge of nutrients from agricultural irrigation wastes 
and sewage have led to the increasing eutrophication of water bodies, 
which is the main source of harmful algal blooms (Hodgskiss et al., 
2016; Zou et al., 2018). Continual occurrence of algal blooms such as red 
and green tides can reduce oxygen levels and produce potential persis­
tent biotoxins in water bodies, which has brought an irreversible dam­
age to the ecosystem (Duan et al., 2019; Wang et al., 2022a). Most of 
algae organism is composed of proteins, polysaccharides, and lipids, 
which can be transformed into additional value of products such as 
sustainable bioenergy and biomaterials through hydrothermal carbon­
ization (HTC) (de Siqueira Castro et al., 2021; Kumar et al., 2020; Vo 
Hoang Nhat et al., 2018). HTC is a thermochemical technology oper­
ating at moderate temperatures and low pressures (Deng et al., 2020; 
Peng et al., 2017; Li et al., 2018), mainly for the production of solid fuels 
(hydrochar). However, a large number of by-products will be produced 
during HTC and concentrated in aqueous phase (AP). The disposal and 
utilization of AP propose a novel challenge for the development of HTC 
technology.. 
After HTC, approximately 80% of the original nutrients and 40% of 
organic matter can be concentrated in AP. (Yu et al., 2011). The high 
levels of soluble organic carbon, nitrogen heterocyclic compounds, and 
a variety of toxic substances in AP bring challenges to the utilization and 
treatment of AP through biochemical methods (Leng et al., 2020; Swe­
tha et al., 2021). The current biological treatment methods for AP uti­
lization mainly include anaerobic digestion and methane production 
(Bareha et al., 2018; Fernandez et al., 2018), microalgae culture (Pham 
et al., 2013; Godwin et al., 2017) and bacteria culture (Orfield et al., 
2014; Cordova et al., 2020) as nutrient solution. For instance, Fernandez 
et al. (2018) co-digested HTL wastewater with manure anaerobically 
and found that methane could be produced through the degradation of 
organic matter. Godwin et al. (2017) successfully recycled nutrients and 
☆This paper has been recommended for acceptance by Dr SU SHIUNG LAM. 
* Corresponding author. 
E-mail address: jhong@ustc.edu.cn (H. Jiang).  
Contents lists available at ScienceDirect 
Environmental Pollution 
journal homepage: www.elsevier.com/locate/envpol 
https://doi.org/10.1016/j.envpol.2022.119556 
Received 21 March 2022; Received in revised form 21 May 2022; Accepted 28 May 2022

Environmental Pollution 307 (2022) 119556
2
reduced resource waste by utilizing the diluted AP for algae culture. 
However, the algae can only survive in low concentration of AP (<10%), 
which is ineffective for degrading the toxic substances in AP (Pham 
et al., 2013). Compared with algae, Bacteria show a better tolerance for 
AP so as to effectively degrade and convert toxic substances (Nelson 
et al., 2013; He et al., 2017; Cordova et al., 2020). For example, He et al. 
(2017) highlighted that the under-utilized carbon in AP could be con­
verted and degraded by Rhodococci. Cordova et al. (2020) confirmed 
that Yarrowia lipolytica could convert recalcitrant components in AP to 
such easy biodegradable chemicals as echinacea, triacetin, lipids, and 
triacetic acid. However, the transform of organic nitrogen in AP was 
neglected during the microbial pretreatment. 
Commonly, the high concentrations of organic nitrogen in AP is first 
converted to ammonia nitrogen and further reduced to nitrogen by a 
denitrification process with the need of large treatment facilities, which 
is time-consuming and costly. The struvite precipitation method is 
viable for the recovery of ammonia nitrogen from a variety of highly 
concentrated wastewater with fast reaction rates and a low residual 
ammonia concentration (Cai et al., 2020; Zeng et al., 2018). 
The two-step treatment method including microbial pretreatment 
and struvite precipitation may be environmentally friendly for the 
disposal of recalcitrant AP, which has not been reported previously to 
our best knowledge. 
In addition, without denitrification, the bioavailability or environ­
mental toxicity of the treated wastewater is unclear, which requires a 
systematic evaluation. The main purpose of this study is to provide a 
novel strategy for recovering the nutrients in AP and improving the 
bioavailability of AP by microbe pretreatment. To this end, AP was 
pretreated with microorganism to improve the bioavailability of 
effluent, in which the Org-N in AP was converted to NH4
+-N and recov­
ered though struvite precipitation. E. coli is a model microorganism with 
the advantages of facile cultivation and rapid growth widely used in 
industrial processes (Pontrelli et al., 2018; Gravina et al., 2018; Wu 
et al., 2021). The conversion pathway of AP by E. coli was investigated 
by three-dimension excitation emission matrix fluorescence spectros­
copy (3D-EEM) and other characterizations. Moreover, the nitrogen 
conversion condition during microbial pretreatment was optimized by 
changing the incubation rotational speeds and inoculum sizes. 
2. Materials and methods 
2.1. Materials 
The algae was salvaged from the Chaohu Lake and dried at 105 ◦C 
overnight. The driedalgae was crushed and collected the particles under 
100 mesh, and the obtained powder was stored in sealed glass con­
tainers. The E. coli was supplied by Anhui Province Key Laboratory of 
Wetland Ecosystem Protection and Restoration. All the reagents used in 
this experiment were analytical grade and obtained from Sinopharm 
Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. 
2.2. Hydrothermal carbonization 
2 g of algae was added into the autoclave containing 70 mL deionized 
water. The sealed autoclave was transferred to an oven and heated at 
200 ◦C for 60 min. After cooling, the obtained products in autoclave was 
separated to hydrochar and AP by centrifugal filtration. The hydrochar 
solid phase was further dried and collected.. The obtained AP was stored 
at −20 ◦C in the refrigerator for further use. 
2.3. Activation and incubation of E. Coli culture 
The E. coli strains were stored in glycerol tubes and frozen in the 
refrigerator at −80 ◦C. 20 μL of preserved liquid E. coli was inoculated 
into a centrifuge tube with 2 mL of Luria-Bertani culture (LB culture) 
medium and incubated on a rotating shaker for about 10 h under 35 ◦C. 
The pure E. coli strains were then preserved on solid medium by plate 
scribing. Finally, single colonies of E. coli were picked on the plates and 
inoculated into conical flasks with 100 mL of LB culture medium, and 
the E. coli strains in conical flasks were further incubated on a 35 ◦C 
rotating shaker at 150 rpm overnight. 
E. coli was cultured in MOPS medium (a zwitterionic buffer which 
chemical structure contains morpholine rings propyl sulfonic) according 
to previous research (Nelson et al., 2013). Before used, the MOPS culture 
solution was sterilized at high pressure and elevating temperature for 25 
min. 
The raw AP needs to be diluted to reduce the concentration of toxic 
compound, because concentrations of AP above 40% (by volume) of the 
culture medium are too toxic to E. coli (Orfield et al., 2014; Stablein 
et al., 2020). Therefore, the MOPS medium containing 30% AP was 
prepared by mixing 70 mL MOPS solution with 30 mL of AP. The acti­
vated E. coli in LB culture solution was separated by centrifugation at 
8000 rpm for 5 min. The bacteria were resuspended with an appropriate 
amount of sterile PBS solution, transferred into the MOPS medium 
containing 30% AP with an OD of approximately 0.2. Finally, the bac­
teria were incubated on a 150 rpm rotating shaker at 35 ◦C for 72 h. The 
effect of incubation rotational speeds (100 rpm, 150 rpm, 200 rpm) and 
inoculum sizes (The OD values were about 0.05, 0.2 and 0.5 respec­
tively) on the nitrogen conversion rate was investigated by a 
single-factor optimization test. 
2.4. Chemical analysis of 30% AP MOPS culture medium 
After incubation of E. coli for 3 days, the residual culture mediums 
were collected and analyzed after filtering through 0.45 μm filter 
membrane. The water quality parameters including chemical oxygen 
demand (COD), NH4 
+-N, NO3
−N, NO2
−N, PO4
3−
were determined ac­
cording to the standard method (Zhou et al., 2015). The biodegrad­
ability of nutrient solution before and after E. coli treatment was 
determined by biochemical oxygen demand (BOD) analyzer (BODTrak 
II, America). 
2.5. Three-dimensional excitation-emission matrix (3D-EEM) and 
fluorescence regional integration analysis (FRI) analysis 
Prior to 3D-EEM analysis, the samples were diluted with deionized 
water (sample: water = 1:20). The emission wavelength was 280–560 
nm (Em) with a scanning interval of 5 nm, and the excitation wavelength 
was 200–450 nm (Ex) with a scanning interval of 5 nm to obtain the 3D- 
EEM spectra. 
Fluorescence region integration (FRI) can be used to analyzed the 
dissolved organics under various environment conditions, which can 
quantitative determine the dissolved organics by integrating the volume 
beneath an operably defined EEM area (Chen et al., 2003; He et al., 
2013). In this work, the area-specific EEM volume representing the 
relative intensity of Dissolved organic matter (DOM) components can be 
calculated and standardized by FRI. The following formulae (1-3) were 
used for this calculation (He et al., 2013):  
Φi,n = MFiΦi = MFi∬I (λexλem) dλex dλem                                     
(1)  
ΦT,n = ∑Φi,n                                                                               
(2)  
Pi,n = Φi,n/ΦT,n × 100%                                                                 
(3) 
Where Фi,n (AU-nm2) represents the integral standard volume of region 
i; MFi is the multiplication factor (the ratio of the integral volume of the 
total region to region i); Фi and ФT,n (AU-nm2) represent the integral 
volume of region i and the total integral standard volume, respectively; 
Pi,n is the percentage of integral standard volume of region i related to 
the total integral standard volume. λex and λem stand for Ex and Em 
wavelength (nm), respectively; I(λexλem) represents the fluorescence 
intensity between Ex and Em wavelength. 
Z.-Y. Hu et al.

Environmental Pollution 307 (2022) 119556
3
2.6. Gas chromatography-mass spectrometer (GC-MS) 
GC-MS was used for the detection and analysis of organic matter in 
the medium. The sample was extracted by 30 mL of dichloromethane for 
three times, and the organic phase obtained from the extraction was 
evaporated to about 1 mL by rotary evaporation at 40 ◦C to remove the 
excess solvent from the recovered organic phase (Yang et al., 2017). 
GC-MS analysis was performed on a Gas chromatograph mass spec­
trometer (TSQ8000EVO, China) under helium gas flow. The injection 
port temperature and detector temperature were 280 ◦C and 150 ◦C, 
respectively. 1 μL of sample was injected, and the temperature of furnace 
was increased from 50 ◦C to 300 ◦C in 50 min. The residence time at the 
beginning and end of the sample injection was both set as 5 min. 
2.7. Harvest and characterization of struvite 
The struvite precipitation was realized by adding Na2HPO4⋅12H2O, 
MgCl⋅6H2O and NaOH into incubation solution at room temperature 
under stirring until the solution pH no longer decreased (Shaddel et al., 
2020). The sediments in culture medium after incubation were dried at 
45 ◦C (Barbosa et al., 2016). The crystals of the sediments were deter­
mined by X-ray diffraction (XRD, Empyrean S3, China), and the peaks in 
the diffraction pattern of the samples were compared with the standard 
diffraction pattern. The morphology of precipitates were analyzed by 
scanning electron microscopy (SEM, REGULUS 8230, China), and the 
composition was determined by energy dispersive spectroscopy (EDS). 
3. Results and discussion 
3.1. Transformation of nitrogen 
As reported in the literature, approximately 50–70% of N in biomass 
was concentrated into the AP after HTC treatment (Leng & Zhou, 2018). 
As shown in Fig. 1, the main nitrogen species in the original AP were 
NH4
+-N and Org-N. Similar studies have been reported in previous re­
searches (Zhuang et al., 2017). However, the contents of organic and 
inorganic nitrogen in the solution after biological treatment were 
changed. After the biological treatment, the form of nitrogen was mainly 
inorganic nitrogen, especially NH4
+-N (Hui et al., 2019). Fig. 1 shows the 
content of inorganic nitrogen in the 30% AP MOPS culture medium 
before and after E. coli treatment. In the culture medium after incubation 
with E. coli, the content of NH4
+-N increased from 30.98 mg/L to 147.92 
mg/L, and NO2 
– N from 0.66 mg/L to 2.45 mg/L, while NO3 – N 
decreased from 34.50 mg/L to 0.45 mg/L, and Org-N from 105.11 mg/L 
to 17.88 mg/L. Total nitrogen (TN) refers to the sum of dissolved and 
suspended nitrogen, including nitrite nitrogen (NO2 – N), nitrate nitro­
gen (NO3 – N), inorganic ammonium salts (NH4+–N), dissolved 
ammonia and nitrogen from most organic nitrogenous compounds. 
There was no evident change in TN content before and after biological 
treatment, and the content of TN in the culture solution was about 170 
mg/L. Those results indicate that the E. coli treatment can effectively 
convert the Org-N in AP to NH4
+-N, which facilitates the further recla­
mation of N (Zheng et al., 2021). 
3.2. Effect of rotational speed and inoculum size on nitrogen conversion 
rate 
A single factor experiment was carried out to investigate the influ­
ence of incubation rotational speeds and E. coli inoculum sizes on the 
nitrogen conversion rate. As shown in Fig. 2a and Fig.S1a, the NH4
+ –N 
concentration barely changed within the 3 days when the incubation 
rotational speeds increased from 100 to 300 rpm, which indicates an 
almost complete conversion of nitrogen in the culture medium at 100 
rpm for 3 days. 
The concentration of NH4
+ –N increased with an increasing inoculum 
size (Fig. 2b and Fig. S1b), and a maximum of NH4
+-N concentration was 
reached at OD = 0.2. What’s more, an OD of 0.5 led to a decrease in the 
amount of ammonia nitrogen, which can be explained by the fact that 
larger inoculums lead to microbial overproduction and more NH4+–N is 
taken up as a nitrogen source (Hui et al., 2019). Therefore, the E.coli 
treatment parameters for AP are the incubation rotational speed of 100 
rpm for 3 d and the inoculum size of OD = 0.2, which are used in sub­
sequent experiments. As shown in Fig. S1, the phosphorus source in AP is 
mainly in the form of phosphate and has not been affected by the speed 
of incubation and the inoculum of bacteria. The reduction in PO4
3−
content after biological treatment indicates that part of the phosphorus 
in AP was assimilated by E. coli. 
3.3. Struvite precipitation 
When wastewater contains oversaturated magnesium ions (Mg2+), 
ammonium (NH4
+), and orthophosphates (PO4
3−, HPO4
2−, and H2PO4
−), 
struvite may be formed as shown in Eq. (4) (Kim et al., 2018). 
Mg2+ + NH+
4 + HPO2−
4 + 6H2O→MgNH4PO4⋅6H2O↓+ H+
(4) 
Org-N in AP was transformed to NH4
+ –N through E. coli treatment 
and recovered in the form of struvite. Owing to the relatively low 
phosphorus concentration in AP, both phosphorus and Mg resource were 
added to obtain struvite precipitates. The powder obtained after treat­
ment was white crystals. 
The concentration of NH4
+ –N in solution decreased from 129.34 mg/ 
L to 24.41 mg/L after precipitation, indicating that 81.13% of NH4
+-N 
could be recovered in the form of struvite. The concentration of PO4
3−in 
the solution was 6.67 mg/L, and the concentration after E. coli treatment 
was 1.67 mg/L, indicating a PO4
3−recovery of 74.98% was realized. The 
obtained white crystals were collected and dried at 45 ◦C and it was 
found that 16.76 g of precipitate could be formed in 1 L of solution 
(Table S1). 
The collected precipitates were subjected to crystal characterization. 
XRD, SEM, and SEM-EDS techniques were used to determine the struc­
ture and content of struvite in the precipitates. As shown in Fig. 3a, the 
XRD pattern of obtained precipitate was in line with the XRD spectrum 
of the standard struvite (JCPDS No. 15–0762) (Barbosa et al., 2016). The 
prominent peaks of the precipitates were found to match the standard 
model of struvite, but the position of individual peaks differed. As re­
ported in previous research, struvite crystals are typically elongated 
longitudinally in needle-like or flaky shapes, ranging in diameter from 
10 to 30 μm (Shu et al., 2019). To confirm the crystal morphology of 
struvite in the precipitates, SEM image of precipitates was observed. In 
Fig. 1. Changes of NH4
+ –N, NO3
−N, NO2 – N and Org-N in the 30% AP MOPS 
culture medium. 
Z.-Y. Hu et al.

Environmental Pollution 307 (2022) 119556
4
Fig. 3b, the obtained crystal has a square and plate structure and a 
relatively smooth surface, which was consistent with the typical struvite 
crystals, indicating the formation of struvite during the treatment. At the 
same time, the crystal of struvite was also confirmed by EDS analysis 
(Fig. 3c), and irregular-sized broken crystals with cracks and fractures 
were also observed on their surface. This is owing to the presence of 
other metal ions such as K2+, Ca2+ and humic acid can affect the 
nucleation and growth of struvite crystals, as well as the purity of 
struvite. The struvite precipitation reactionled to significant changes in 
the electrical potential and ionic concentration in the AP solution, 
resulting in destabilization and aggregation of the other components in 
AP solution. As a result, a certain amount of organic matter was 
precipitated in the final solid, leading to the impurity of the prepared 
struvite (Lou et al., 2018; Shu et al., 2019). 
3.4. Composition changes after biological treatment 
3.4.1. Change in dissolved organic matter (DOM) 
DOM is usually composed of tryptophan-like and tyrosine-like 
organic compounds, fulvic and humin acid-like materials, and soluble 
microbial byproduct-like materials, according to their fluorescence 
features (Chen et al., 2003; Chen et al., 2020a). Table 1 shows the range 
of Ex and Em in the various DOM components in AP. Five pronounced 
peaks were observed in three-dimensional fluorescence spectra to 
represent tryptophan-like substances tyrosine-like organic substances, 
fulvic acid-like compounds, soluble microbial byproduct-like substances 
and humic substances (Wang et al., 2022b). 
As shown in Fig. 4a and b, the fluorescence peaks of the 3D-EEM 
spectra of culture medium were altered after biological treatment. 
Fig. 2. Effects of rotational speed on Inorg-N a) and effect of inoculum size on Inorg-N b).  
Fig. 3. XRD diffraction spectra corresponds to standard struvite (JCPDS No. 15–0762) a), SEM picture b) and respective EDS analysis c) of the precipitates.  
Z.-Y. Hu et al.

Environmental Pollution 307 (2022) 119556
5
After E. coli pretreatment, the intensity of fluorescence peaks repre­
senting tyrosine-like organic compounds and tryptophan-like organic 
compounds in the culture medium were remarkably decreased, indi­
cating that microorganisms in biological treatment could use aromatic 
proteins as a carbon source and convert them into simpler small mole­
cules of organic matter. These small molecules usually have lower 
toxicity than their father compounds (Pham et al., 2013; Zheng et al., 
2017; Usman et al., 2019; Chen et al., 2021). 
As shown in Fig. 4c, the percentages of tyrosine-like substances, 
tryptophan-like substances, fulvic acid-like compounds, soluble micro­
bial byproduct-like compounds, and humic acid-like compounds in the 
original AP solution were 45.03%, 10.23%, 11.02%, 28.91%, and 
4.80%, respectively. After E. coli pretreatment, the percentages of the 
five components were 1.40%, 56.83%, 23.71%, 10.41%, and 7.65%, 
respectively. The intensity distribution change of those fluorescent 
substance in the AP culture solution was mainly caused by E. coli 
metabolism (Pontrelli et al., 2018). As Fig. 4d shown, the total fluores­
cence intensity in the AP culture broth was calculated by FRI calcula­
tions and a decrease of 42.83% in intensity was observed after E. coli 
pretreatment. Specifically, compared with raw culture broth, a decrease 
of 98.23%, 79.41% and 8.90% in the intensity of tyrosine-like organic 
compounds, soluble microbial byproduct-like compounds and humic 
acid-like materials were observed after treatment, respectively. The 
reduction of tyrosine-like organic compounds was the result of degra­
dation and transformation of aromatic protein-like substances (Jacquin 
et al., 2017; Ouarda et al., 2020). The intensity of tryptophan-like 
organic compounds and the fulvic acid-like materials were increased 
by 217.47% and 22.99% after treatment, respectively. Tryptophan-like 
organic compounds are more easily degraded than tyrosine-like organic 
compounds, suggesting that AP cultures with increased concentrations 
of tryptophan-like organic compounds have greater bioavailability (Li 
et al., 2014). Meanwhile, the decrease of aromatic humic acid-like 
substance suggests the highly stable and potentially toxic substance 
was converted to more degradable small DOM molecules (e.g., 
xanthophyll-like substances and protein-like substances) in the biolog­
ical treatment (Ouarda et al., 2020; Kang et al., 2015). The increase of 
lower molecular weight and easily decomposed fulvic acid-like mate­
rials may be related to the conversion of humic acid-like materials or 
tryptophan-like organic compounds (Jouraiphy et al., 2005; Rajapaksha 
et al., 2019; Yu et al., 2019). In addition, bacterial activity can increase 
the concentration of carboxyl groups during organic conversion, which 
may be beneficial in reducing the aromatization of humic acid-like 
materials (Wu et al., 2017). In a word, E. coli pretreatment can reduce 
the toxicity of the AP solution by degrading aromatic protein-like sub­
stances and part of the refractory humic acid-like materials. 
3.4.2. Detecting organic matter in the medium 
Fig. 5a and b shows the GC-MS analysis of the 30% AP MOPS culture 
medium before and after E. coli treatment, and the identified compounds

Z.-Y. Hu et al.

Environmental Pollution 307 (2022) 119556
6
are listed in detail in Tables S2 and S3. The area (%) of each compound is 
defined as the chromatographic area of each compound as a percentage 
of the total peak area. Components with a relative content of less than 
0.5% are neglected and not listed here (Pedersen et al., 2017). By 
categorization, it was found that a total of 27 classes of compounds were 
detected in culture medium. 
According to the aromatic structure, the compounds were classified 
into aromatic, heterocyclic, and aliphatic compounds. Most of the aro­
matic and heterocyclic compounds in the original 30% AP MOPS culture 
medium were nitrogenous (Table S2). Basically, the sequential cycliza­
tion and cyclocondensation of nitrogen-containing intermediates via the 
Diels-Alder reaction give rise to the formation of pyrrole-N and pyridine- 
N (Zhuang et al., 2017). Most of the nitrogenous compounds dis­
appeared after 3 days of incubation with E. coli (Table S3). As shown in 
Fig. 5c, the biological treatment resulted in a substantial decrease in 
nitrogen-containing heterocyclic and aromatic compounds, but an in­
crease in aliphatic compounds. The peak area of heterocyclic and aro­
matic compounds decreased significantly from 42.90% to 20.30%– 
2.66% and 11.39%, respectively, while the peak area of aliphatic com­
pounds increased from 9.86% to 33.85%. The nitrogen-containing het­
erocyclic and aromatic compounds have larger cytotoxicity than their 
daughter compounds(Chen et al., 2020b; Pham et al., 2013; Zhuang 
et al., 2017; Zhou et al., 2013). The decrease in nitrogen-containing 
heterocyclic and aromatic compounds indicates a decrease in the 
toxicity of AP solutions. 
At the same time, the organic compounds were classified into short- 
chain, medium-chain and long-chain compounds based on the number 
of carbon atoms. It can be seen from Fig. 5d that most of the organic 
compounds are medium-chain ones, especially the organic compounds 
with carbon atomic number of 8–16. This is due to the low degree of 
hydrocarbon polymerization under the mild hydrothermal conditions 
(Lu et al., 2018). After E. coli treatment, the contents of all three types of 
compounds decreased. 
As shown in Tables S2 and S3, and Fig. S3, the major organic com­
pounds detected by GC-MS were benzene,1,3-dimethyl, styrene, Pyrrolo 
[1,2-a]pyrazine-1,4-dione, hexahydro-3-(phenylmethyl)-, other aro­
matic or heterocyclic compounds such as pyrrolo, pyrazine, 113-pecose­
namide, and aliphatic compounds such as 2-methylhexacosane and 
heptane,2,3-dimethyl. After E. coli treatment, the aromatic compounds 
are mainly simple aromatic ones, such as ethybenzene, xylene, and the 
fewer aromatic heterocyclic compounds, most of which have lost their 
nitrogen atoms. In general, recalcitrant compounds in AP with cyclic 
structures or nitrogen heteroatoms can be decomposed by the biological 
treatment, which can facilitate the assimilation of AP by microbes (Zhou 
et al., 2013). 
Nitrogen-containing compounds with complex structures usually 
have high cytotoxicity (Pham et al., 2013; Lu et al., 2018). After treat­
ment with E.coli, all nitrogen-containing organic compounds were 
remarkably reduced. As shown in Fig. S3, the structures of aromatic 
compounds were decomposed after biological treatment and the rings in 
heterocyclic compounds were broken through deamination, which in­
dicates that the AP has a high bioavailability after E. coli treatment. 
3.5. Life cycle assessment 
Life cycle assessment (LCA) is an international standard method for 
evaluating the environmental impact of a product or process, which 
systematically quantify the various resources, energy consumption and 
environmental emissions during the life cycle (i.e. ISO 14040 series). In 
this study, the environmental impact of the biological treatment of AP 
Fig. 5. GC-MS spectra of the culture medium before a) and after b) E. coli treatment, c) relative peak area of heterocyclic compounds, aromatic compounds and 
aliphatic compounds, d) relative peak area of short chain, mediun chain and long chain. 
Z.-Y. Hu et al.

Environmental Pollution 307 (2022) 119556
7
wastewater and the production of struvite were assessed using the 
software SimaPro version 7.1. In accordance with LCA principle of 
‘cradle to grave’, the scope of the inventory included the construction of 
the separation system AP, the E.coli treatment AP and the production of 
struvite precipitation, as well as the background production of any 
chemicals and energy required for the treatment process. Because the 
study focuses on the de-toxification and resource isation study of AP, the 
energy consumption and environmental impacts resulting from the 
collection, transport, and HTC of algae are not considered within the 
system boundaries of this study. In addition, in order to simulate the 
environmental impact of the discharged wastewater, the concentrations 
of total nitrogen and total phosphorus were assumed to discharge 
directly to surface water. The process flow diagram is shown in the 
following Fig. 6a. 
A summary of the characteristics of the AP wastewater before and 
after treatment is shown in Table S4. The functional units are per m3 of 
treated AP wastewater. In the production of struvite, magnesium is 
added in the form of magnesium chloride (MgCl2). However, as the 
SimaPro 7.1 software does not include a process for the production of 
MgCl2, a new process was created based on the inventory data from 
literature (Igoset al., 2017) (Table S5). 
Depending on the type of environment affected, impact assessment is 
currently divided into three main categories, namely resource depletion, 
ecological impact and environmental health. Through the evaluation 
method CML 2 baseline 2000 v2.04/World, 1990, impacts are further 
divided into Abiotic depletion, Acidification, Eutrophication, Global 
warming (GWP 100), Ozone layer depletion, Human toxicity, Fresh 
water aquatic ecotoxicity, Marine aquatic ecotoxicity, Terrestrial eco­
toxicity, and Photochemical oxidation (Zhang et al., 2020). Table S6 
shows the impact of this study assessment phase results. It can be seen 
from Fig. S4 that the struvite AP segment had a greater impact on 
Acidification and Eutrophication, accounting for 66.40% and 82.75% of 
the total score, respectively, which was mainly due to the discharge of 
nitrogen and phosphorus compounds in the wastewater. The environ­
mental impacts of Global warming (81.41%), Terrestrial ecotoxicity 
(76.79%), and Photochemical oxidation (47.27%) were mainly caused 
by the addition of chemical reagents to the AP wastewater during E. coli 
treatment and the loss of electricity in the experimental unit. The 
environmental impacts in terms of photochemical oxidation (47.27%) 
were mainly caused by the addition of chemical reagents to the AP 
wastewater at the E.coli treatment stage and the power loss of the 
experimental unit. Figure S5 shows the relative contribution sizes of the 
ten different environmental impact types determined by weighted 
averaging. There results are consistent with the findings of previous LCA 
analyses of wastewater treatment plants (Li et al., 2013; Chen et al., 
2020c). 
Since the project assumed that AP wastewater was directly dis­
charged into surface water after struvite was produced, the composition 
of AP wastewater was more complex and the content of nitrogen and 
phosphorus was higher than that of wastewater discharge standard, so 
the relative order of contribution of environmental eutrophic substances 
in the AP wastewater treatment process was explored. According to 
Fig. 6b, COD made the largest contribution to environmental eutrophi­
cation, accounting for 49.54%, followed by nitrogen oxide in air 
Fig. 6. a) Process flow chart of wastewater treatment，b) Relative contribution of different discharges to the environmental impact of eutrophication.  
Z.-Y. Hu et al.

Environmental Pollution 307 (2022) 119556
8
(39.55%), and then by the emission of Phosphoric acid (7.26%). 
In order to assess the environmental impact of struvite, Sena et al. 
(2021) offset the environmental impact by converting struvite into equal 
amounts of nitrogen and phosphorus (5% of struvite’s nitrogen content 
as ammonium nitrate and 28% of struvite’s phosphorus content as triple 
superphosphate) as fertilizer. 55.81 kg of struvite were formed in 1 m3 of 
AP wastewater by this process, and could be converted into 2.79 kg of 
ammonium nitrate and 15.62 kg of triple superphosphate, offsetting the 
eutrophication impact of 1.8623 PO4 eq. This result indicate that this 
process has a beneficial effect on the environment. 
4. Conclusion 
In this study, E coli was adopted as a model microbe to pretreat the 
AP, and results showed that the organic nitrogen in AP can be mostly 
converted into ammonia nitrogen, with a conversion rate as high as 
82.99%. Subsequently, 81.13% of ammonia nitrogen and 74.98% of 
phosphorus resource can be recovered in the form of struvite though a 
precipitation method. Simultaneously, three-dimensional fluorescence 
spectra and GC-MS results demonstrate that E. coli treatment can 
improve the environmental friendliness of AP by degrading aromatic 
protein substances, and opening and deamination of nitrogenous het­
erocyclic compounds. LCA results show that this combined process is 
beneficial to environment. 
This study provides data support and theoretical basis for future 
application in the degradation and transformation of wastewater con­
taining high nitrogen and phosphorus in sewage treatment plants. 
However, the microorganism used in biological treatment in this study is 
limited in E.coli, therefore other microbial methods or WWTP and 
related microbial community analysis need to be further study. In the 
experimental process of struvite formation, effects of phosphorus source, 
magnesium source and other relevant conditions should be further 
investigated to determine the formation mechanism of struvite and 
optimize the yield of struvite. Meanwhile, in the current study, the used 
algae and hydrothermal conditions are single, and more relevant data 
are needed to establish the relationship between composition of waste 
hydrothermal fluids and bioavailability as well as struvite yield.

Assessment of environmental impacts and operational costs of the
implementation of an innovative source-separated urine treatment
Elorri Igos a, *, Mathilde Besson b, c, d, Tomas Navarrete Gutierrez a,
Ana Barbara Bisinella de Faria b, c, d, Enrico Benetto a, Ligia Barna b, c, d, Aras Ahmadi b, c, d,
Mathieu Sperandio b, c, d
a Luxembourg Institute of Science and Technology (LIST), 5, Avenue des Hauts-Fourneaux, L-4362, Esch-sur-Alzette, Luxembourg
b Universite de Toulouse, INSA,UPS,INP, LISBP, 135 Avenue de Rangueil, F-31077, Toulouse, France
c INRA, UMR792 Ingenierie des Systemes Biologiques et des Procedes, F-31400, Toulouse, France
d CNRS, UMR5504, F-31400, Toulouse, France
a r t i c l e
i n f o
Article history:
Received 13 February 2017
Received in revised form
18 August 2017
Accepted 5 September 2017
Available online 8 September 2017
Keywords:
Source-separated urine treatment
Process simulation
Sustainability assessment
Innovative technology
Integrated modelling
a b s t r a c t
Innovative treatment technologies and management methods are necessary to valorise the constituents
of wastewater, in particular nutrients from urine (highly concentrated and can have signiﬁcant impacts
related to artiﬁcial fertilizer production). The FP7 project, ValuefromUrine, proposed a new two-step
process (called VFU) based on struvite precipitation and microbial electrolysis cell (MEC) to recover
ammonia, which is further transformed into ammonium sulphate. The environmental and economic
impacts of its prospective implementation in the Netherlands were evaluated based on life cycle
assessment (LCA) methodology and operational costs. In order to tackle the lack of stable data from the
pilot plant and the complex effects on wastewater treatment plant (WWTP), process simulation was
coupled with LCA and costs assessment using the Python programming language. Additionally, particular
attention was given to the propagation and analysis of inputs uncertainties. Five scenarios of VFU
implementation were compared to the conventional treatment of 1 m3 of wastewater. Inventory data
were obtained from SUMO software for the WWTP operation. LCA was based on Brightway2 software
(using ecoinvent database and ReCiPe method). The results, based on 500 iterations sampled from inputs
distributions (foreground parameters, ecoinvent background data and market prices), showed a signif-
icant advantage of VFU technology, both at a small and decentralized scale and at a large and centralized
scale (95% conﬁdence intervals not including zero values). The beneﬁts mainly concern the production of
fertilizers, the decreased efforts at the WWTP, the water savings from toilets ﬂushing, as well as the
lower infrastructure volumes if the WWTP is redesigned (in case of signiﬁcant reduction of nutrients
load in wastewater). The modelling approach, which could be applied to other case studies, improves the
representativeness and the interpretation of results (e.g. complex relationships, global sensitivity anal-
ysis) but requires additional efforts (computing and engineering knowledge, longer calculation time).
Finally, the sustainability assessment should be reﬁned in the future with the development of the
technology at larger scale to update these preliminary conclusions before its commercialization.

1. Introduction
In the context of circular economy and energy constraints, the
treatment of source-separated urine seems very promising to efﬁ-
ciently recover nutrients in the form of fertilizers due to the very
high nutrient content of urine (Ishii and Boyer, 2015). Numerous
technologies were developed since the 1990s to face this challenge
(e.g. Kirchmann and Pettersson,1995; Koster and Koomen,1988). In
the frame of the project ValuefromUrine (2012e2016), funded by
the European 7th Framework Programme and coordinated by
Wetsus (www.valuefromurine.eu), an innovative two-step process
(Fig. 1) was developed to recover nutrients from urine into valuable
products (Zamora et al., 2017). First, struvite crystals are generated
from precipitation with magnesium e Mg salt (eliminating phos-
phorous e P). Then, a microbial electrolysis cell (MEC) aims at
recovering nitrogen e N by transforming ammonium contained in

0043-1354/© 2017 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.
Water Research 126 (2017) 50e59

urine into volatile ammonia, as a result of electron transport be-
tween the two electrodes of the cell through bacteria catalysis. The
volatilized ammonia is recovered as ammonium sulphate via
transmembrane chemisorption with sulfuric acid. This new recov-
ery technique aims at mitigating chemical and energy demand
compared to common ammonia stripping. The technology, here-
after called VFU (Value From Urine), was installed and tested at the
pilot scale to pre-treat male urine from an ofﬁce building (collected
from water-free urinals) in Leeuwarden (the Netherlands).
New processes should comply with sustainability principles
where generated beneﬁts compensate implementation costs. The
life cycle assessment (LCA) methodology (ruled by ISO 14040/44,
2006) was used by several authors to compare the environmental
performances of urine separation systems with conventional
wastewater systems. Tillman et al. (1998), Maurer et al. (2003),
Benetto et al. (2009) and Spa_ngberg et al. (2014) focused on the
direct urine application after storage. Other studies included urine
treatment technologies: oxidation (Remy, 2010), struvite precipi-
tation (Ishii and Boyer, 2015; Bisinella de Faria et al., 2015), as well
as ion-exchange treatment (Landry and Boyer, 2016). All these
studies showed potential beneﬁts of urine separation systems,
thanks to ﬂushing water savings, lower efforts at the waste water
treatment plant (WWTP) and mineral fertilizer substitution, but
with some trade-offs between impact categories. The economic
impacts are evidently important. To that respect, the conclusions
from the few available studies (Landry and Boyer, 2016; Ishii and
Boyer, 2015; Berndtsson, 2006) diverge.
For both environmental and economic assessments, scholars
underlined issues related to data quality and uncertainty, limiting
the validity of the conclusions. In particular, the consequences of
urine separation in the WWTP were modelled with quite basic
assumptions, either using the ecoinvent database tool (Doka, 2009)
or empirical ratios for energy/chemicals consumption. In a few LCA
studies of wastewater treatment (Foley et al., 2010; Flores-Alsina
et al., 2010; Corominas et al., 2012, 2013), simulation tools (Bio-
Win, IWA Benchmark Simulation Model or WEST®) were used to
compute the life cycle inventory (LCI) of the processes, in steady
state or dynamic conditions. This approach was followed by
Bisinella de Faria et al. (2015) to better represent the effects of urine
source-separation by coupling dynamic modelling (BioWin model)
and LCA (Python interface embedded in Umberto® software).
This study aims at assessing the environmental consequences
following the prospective market penetration of the VFU technol-
ogy within a sewage network in the Netherlands, taken as test bed
case for the project. To this end, the coupling approach of Bisinella
de Faria et al. (2015) was adapted to investigate the consequences
on wastewater treatment and extended by integrating operational
cost calculations and characterising and analysing result un-
certainties. The speciﬁc objectives of the study are: i) to couple
process-based simulation with LCA and operational cost calcula-
tions, ii) compare the sustainability performances of different sce-
narios for VFU implementation and iii) discuss the interests and
limitations of the modelling framework and results.
2. Material and methods
2.1. Goal and scope of the study
The study focuses on the consequences of the implementation
of the VFU technology within a sewage network of 50,000 popu-
lation equivalents (PE) in the Netherlands, which represents a
common network size (EEA, 2016). A consequential LCA approach
was therefore adopted to identify the possible direct and indirect
changes induced by urine source separation, following the market-
based perspective of Weidema (2003). VFU-based scenarios were
founded on common strategies for urban wastewater management,
specifying
objectives
of
urine
separation,
and
were
further
compared with conventional centralized wastewater treatment
(Reference scenario, i.e. a business as usual situation). SVFU scenario
corresponds to the decentralized pre-treatment of urine (as done
for the pilot plant). Several VFU units would be installed in sufﬁ-
ciently large buildings (e.g. ofﬁce buildings, concert halls) to pre-
treat 10% of urine volume from the WWTP inﬂow. The second
implementation option, called LVFU, would be to install a larger VFU
unit as pre-treatment in the centralized WWTP. In that case, the
urine collected at buildings would be transported by trucks to the
plant, which would pre-treat 50% of urine inﬂow. While 10% urine
separation (SVFU scenario) was predicted to have no inﬂuence on
the WWTP design, the LVFU scenario could modify it due to the
signiﬁcant decrease of nutrients load. Three scenarios were there-
fore added in the comparison analysis (see section 2.3.3. for
detailed description of the processes): i) simple redesign without
post-denitriﬁcation and adapted tank volumes (LVFU-SR scenario);
ii) adjunction of primary sedimentation tank (LVFU-PST scenario);
and iii) adjunction of enhanced primary clariﬁcation with anaerobic
digestion of the sludge (LVFU-EPC-AD scenario).
The functional unit chosen was the treatment of 1 m3 of
wastewater, of which 10% and 50% of urine volume was treated
separately by VFU technology for SVU scenario and LVFU-based
scenarios, respectively. While all the scenarios are compared based
on the same inﬂows (volume and quality of wastewater and urine

differences of efﬂuent quality are considered in the LCA via the
characterization of impacts from the treated wastewater released.
The inventory results from the consequential modelling at the
actual scale of technology implementation were further normalized
to 1 m3 for the sake of simplicity. The study encompassed all the
life-cycle stages of the wastewater system (Fig. 2): collection (i.e.
toilets use, sewer grid, as well as urine transport for LVFU-based
scenarios), treatment (including urine pre-treatment with VFU
technology for VFU-based scenarios) and co-products/waste fate.
For the LCA, following a consequential approach, system expansion
for the produced struvite and ammonium sulphate involved the
avoided production from marginal suppliers of fertilizers. The same
applied to energy produced and used outside the system under
study. The evaluation of operational costs included the co-products
as revenues based on their market prices.
2.2. Coupling methodology
The aim of the coupling between process-based simulation tools
and environmental and economic assessments was to enable an
advanced interpretation of results, i.e. the understanding of phys-
icochemical mechanisms, uncertainty and sensitivity analysis, and
the automation of data transfer between the complex modelling of
process operation and impact calculation. Two process simulation
models were used to generate the foreground inventory data: one
made with Python for the VFU technology and one built within
SUMO software (version 15.69.1 developed by Dynamita SARL) for
the WWTP (based on Bisinella de Faria et al., 2015). The obtained
inventory data fed the LCA model based on Brightway2 software
(version 2.0.2; Mutel, 2016) and the cost calculation script. The
implementation of these elements in the integrated modelling
platform is illustrated in Fig. 3. One Python script provided the
difference of LCA scores and operational costs between one VFU-
based scenario and the Reference scenario, therefore analysing if
there was a signiﬁcant advantage or disadvantage of VFU technol-
ogy implementation.
First, input variables were deﬁned, either with constant values
(e.g. physicochemical properties) or with uncertainty distributions
(e.g. infrastructures lifetime, wastewater characteristics, and mar-
ket prices, see Supporting Information e SI 1). For each parameter
described with an uncertainty distribution, the “rvs” function of
scipy.stats Python module was used to generate 500 random
samples. In order to get the same inputs for all scenarios, the
function relied on a speciﬁc seed number which initialized the
sampling and generated identical samples (the seed value must be
