different for each parameter to avoid biased correlation between
them). A mass balance check was carried out for the wastewater
and urine inputs (to avoid negative concentrations). For each
sample, the foreground inventory data (e.g. energy consumption,
efﬂuent concentration) were calculated from the process-based
simulation. For the Reference scenario, only wastewater treatment
was simulated based on the SUMO model. For VFU-based scenarios,
VFU pre-treatment was described by engineering equations in Py-
thon and estimations from pilot experiments (see SI 1). The
wastewater characteristics after urine pre-treatment were used as
inputs to execute the SUMO model and simulate WWTP operation.
The Sumo2 full-plant model described chemical and biological re-
actions considering phosphorus precipitation, colloidal matter
ﬂocculation, bacteria growth and two-step nitriﬁcation. The ob-
tained simulation outputs, normalized to the functional unit, fed
the impact evaluation. For the LCA, the LCI was completed with
ecoinvent 2.2 database (Frischknecht et al., 2005) and impacts were
assessed with ReCiPe method (Goedkoop et al., 2009). To consider
ecoinvent uncertainties, the Monte Carlo function in Brightway2
sampled the background processes for each of the 500 iterations. As
for foreground inputs, a ﬁxed list of random seed values allowed
the same sampling for each scenario. Operational costs were
calculated from the product of foreground data with their associ-
ated market prices. The impact difference between VFU-based and
Reference scenarios could then be calculated for each of the 500
samples. Finally, the results were analysed through the determi-
nation of the mean and 95% conﬁdence interval for all obtained
scores and through the Spearman rank correlation coefﬁcients in
order to calculate the contribution to variance (CTV) of input un-
certainties. This global sensitivity analysis method was chosen as
the Monte Carlo samples could be directly used, without any post-
treatment required (contrary to Sobol or Fourier variance decom-
position). Only foreground parameters (see SI 1) were analysed
because sampled background data from ecoinvent could not be
retrieved.
2.3. Life cycle inventory (LCI)
This section presents the data and models used to simulate the
foreground inventory data and to further perform the environ-
mental and economic assessments.
2.3.1. Urine and wastewater characteristics
Inventory data ﬁrst depended on the characteristics of urine and
wastewater. Composition and ﬂow per inhabitant for urine and
wastewater were determined from a compilation of literature data
speciﬁc to the Dutch wastewater systems (see SI 1). Truncated
normal distributions (to avoid negative values) were deﬁned for
each parameter (volume ﬂow per inhabitant, chemical oxygen
demand e COD, and N and P concentration). The number of in-
habitants for the network was based on 1.04 PE/inh, calculated
from EEA (2016) and Eurostat statistics (EC, 2016), i.e. 48,077 in-
habitants generate the pollution load (including industrial contri-
bution) for a network of 50,000 PE.
2.3.2. Urine collection and treatment
The infrastructure for conventional and separated toilets (used
for VFU-based scenarios) were assumed to have similar environ-
mental impacts (e.g. similar mass of materials and lifetime between
the different systems) and were therefore neglected for the sce-
narios comparison. The additional piping system for separated
toilets could have an inﬂuence on LCA results but was not inves-
tigated in this study. The consumption of potable water for ﬂushing
was based on ﬂushing volumes and number per day, deﬁned as
probability distributions (see SI 1). Regarding LVFU-based scenarios,
the transport of urine to the WWTP was also considered and based
on an estimated distance of 10 km (Bisinella de Faria et al., 2015).
For SVFU, the pre-treatment of 10% of total urine ﬂow corre-
sponded to 7.6 m3/day, assumed to be treated in 10 VFU units
installed in buildings. LVFU technology was estimated to treat
38 m3/day at the WWTP. Based on these ﬂows, two technology
designs (e.g. reactor volumes, pipes dimensions) were estimated by
the project partners. Indeed, the VFU pilot is still at a smaller scale
(treating about 40 L/day) and extrapolations should therefore be
performed. The related amounts of materials (polyvinylchloride for
the precipitation reactor; concrete and polypropylene for the MEC
reactor with naﬁon for the membrane and nickel for the electrodes;
polypropylene for the absorption reactor; and polyethylene for the
tubing system) were normalized to the functional unit for the LCA,
based on lifetime, and deﬁned by probability distribution. For
struvite precipitation, the consumption of magnesium chloride was
based on the molar ratio Mg:P, determined from project experi-
ments and literature (see SI 1). The equilibrium of precipitation
reactions were modelled to calculate supernatant concentrations
based on Davies approximation from Ronteltap et al. (2007) and
solubility coefﬁcients from Rahaman et al. (2006, 2014). Superna-
tant is treated in the MEC to remove nitrogen. Electrochemical
equations (Kuntke, 2013) are used to simulate the MEC operation,
assuming an N-transport efﬁciency of 90%, a current density (un-
certain parameter), and a coulombic efﬁciency of 85% (data
collected from VFU pilot). The consumption of sulphuric acid to
absorb the recovered ammonia and related ammonium sulphate
production were determined from the stoichiometric balance.
Electricity consumption of the VFU technology was derived from
pumping engineering (Darcy equations) and from 1 V applied to the
MEC. Due to the high difference between calculated and measured
consumption (for pumping, lighting and cell voltage) and to reﬂect
scaling effects, a threshold value was added based on VFU experi-
ments (see SI 1).
2.3.3. Wastewater collection and treatment
The WWTP inﬂuent characteristics were recalculated for VFU-
based scenarios (removal of ammonia, phosphate and COD in the
form of volatile fatty acids and readily biodegradable compounds).
For wastewater collection, the length of the sewer was calculated
from the average length per inhabitant and from its lifetime (see SI
1).
The WWTP was modelled and calibrated at a temperature of
12 C and discharge limits of 10 mg N/L and 1 mg P/L. The pre-
liminary simulation of 200 days of operation for each scenario
aimed at providing initial steady state values and therefore reduced
the calculation time for the Monte Carlo sampling (additional runs
based on 60 days of simulation). The design of the plant was
determined according to Henze (2008) and Tchobanoglous et al.
(2003). The conventional treatment was based on biological and
chemical phosphorus removal, representing 41% of WWTPs (based
on PE load) in the Netherlands in 2009 (de Zeeuw and Baas, 2010).
The Johannesburg conﬁguration has been chosen in this study,
without digestion since digestion is quite rare for 50,000 PE plants
in the Netherlands (STOWA, 2011). The WWTP comprised an
anaerobic tank, a pre-denitriﬁcation reactor (anoxic), and an aer-
obic reactor, followed by a post-denitriﬁcation tank (Fig. 4). The
returned activated sludge passed through an anoxic reactor to
eliminate nitrate before going into the anaerobic reactor. In LVFU-
PST and LVFU-EPC-AD scenarios, a sedimentation tank was added as
primary treatment, enhanced by coagulation/ﬂocculation in LVFU-
EPC-AD. With plant redesign, the post-denitriﬁcation step can be
removed (scenarios LVFU-SR and LVFU-PST). In the LVFU scenario,
the anoxic reactor was bypassed in order to avoid methane pro-
duction (high reactor volume without aeration). Finally, sludge was
E. Igos et al. / Water Research 126 (2017) 50e59
53

thickened and dewatered up to a solids content of 28% (efﬁciency
from Wilsenach and van Loosdrecht, 2006) before being sent to
incineration facilities (99% of Dutch sludge; EEA, 2016). The
anaerobic digester of LVFU-EPC-AD treated both primary and bio-
logical sludge. The related cogeneration unit produced electricity
and heat (used to keep 35 C inside the digester).
The consumption of iron chloride after the anaerobic reactor
and methanol for post-denitriﬁcation were determined in an iter-
ative approach to respect the discharge limits. The SUMO software
modelled the fate of wastewater into the treatment chain, i.e.
pollutant emissions to water and sludge production. For air emis-
sions, methane and carbon dioxide ﬂows were calculated consid-
ering biogenic carbon, except for methanol produced from fossil
resources. The emissions of nitrous oxide were considered as an
uncertain share of ammonia nitriﬁed (see SI 1). Electricity demand
calculation (aeration, pumping, mixing, thickening, dewatering and
lighting) was adapted from Bisinella de Faria et al. (2015). For the
cogeneration process, air emissions and biogas production were
modelled (including reactor leaks) (see SI 1). Electricity was
valorised as an export to the network, contrary to heat over-
production, due to the long distance between WWTP and heating
network systems. Concerning infrastructure modelling, the esti-
mation of the construction processes would have been too uncer-
tain. To tackle the lack of consistent data, the ecoinvent dataset was
used and normalized based on the assumed plant lifetime and
treated wastewater ﬂow. In order to consider the modiﬁed design
for LVFU-SR, LVFU-PST and LVFU-EPC-AD scenarios, the ratio of the
total volume of the reactors between these scenarios and the
Reference scenario was applied. Finally, the distances with suppliers
and the plant lifetime were taken from Doka (2009) (see SI 1).
2.4. Life cycle assessment
Using the Brightway2 software, inventory data were ﬁrst linked
to background processes from the ecoinvent 2.2 database for
transformed inputs (version 3 was not available in Brightway2 at
that time) or biosphere ﬂows for direct emissions to air and water.
Background processes were chosen to be representative for the
Netherlands or, if not available, for Europe or Switzerland (see SI 2).
Due to lack of available data, proxies were used for tetraﬂuoro-
ethylene (naﬁon) and for the magnesium chloride solution (water
content and reaction of magnesium oxide with black carbon and
chlorine from Kanari and Gaballah, 1999).
The production of struvite and ammonium sulphate and their
use on agricultural ﬁelds could affect the supply of chemical fer-
tilizers. From a purely functional (chemical) perspective, there
should be ammonium sulphate and superphosphate or ammonium
phosphate as a struvite replacement (as considered in Bisinella de
Faria et al., 2015; Ishii and Boyer, 2015; Landry and Boyer, 2016).
However, following a marginal consequential approach, a market
analysis is required to assess the future trends and economic
relevance of these assumptions. Consumption data of fertilizers for
the Netherlands (IFA, 2016) showed similar consumed nutrient
quantities of ammonium sulphate (2 Ktons N/year) and ammonium
phosphate (2 Ktons phosphorous pentoxide-P2O5/year and 1 Kton
N/year) between 2010 and 2014. The fertilizer production of the
VFU technology within a network of 50,000 PE (59 tons N/year of
ammonium sulphate, 13 tons P2O5/year and 2 tons N/year of stru-
vite) represents less than 3% of this total demand. It is therefore fair
to suppose that market suppliers will adjust their production at the
margin without any further indirect consequences on the market.
The marginal supply of ammonium sulphate and monoammonium
phosphate (chemically closer than struvite) was considered based
on P2O5 (see SI 1) and N content. Different transport distances to
the agricultural ﬁeld were considered for the replaced fertilizers
and for the VFU co-products (see SI 1). The spreading process was
assumed similar and thus not modelled. The additional production
of electricity of the LVFU-EPC-AD scenario is negligible (about 0.1%
of the total Dutch demand), therefore the marginal supplier is
assumed to be the main energy production source of the country,
i.e. natural gas (IEA, 2014) (which should be substituted by
renewable energy in the future).
LCI ﬂows were then classiﬁed and characterised by ReCiPe
method, based on hierarchical and average perspectives (H,A) for
normalization and weighting. This commonly used method in-
cludes 18 impact categories called midpoints (e.g. climate change,
eutrophication, mineral depletion), which can be expressed as
damage on the three areas of protection (human health, ecosystems
and resources) and further aggregated into one single score (after
normalization and weighting). All these different stages were
assessed in this study.
2.5. Operational costs
For the economic evaluation, inventory data are multiplied by
their respective market prices. Chemicals, energy and sludge
incineration costs were collected from suppliers' website, literature
and chemicals databases (see SI 1). These are not speciﬁc to the
Netherlands, except electricity and water for which national data
could be retrieved. Regarding electricity, the price for households
was applied to a decentralized VFU technology while the price for

3. Results
3.1. Life cycle inventory analysis
Main inventory data are summarized in Table 1 while all ﬂows
are detailed in SI 3. Regarding VFU technology, the use of chemicals
was quite signiﬁcant but necessary to produce the added-value
chemicals. The electricity consumption represented about 3% of
the overall demand of both SVFU and LVFU-based scenarios. Even if
SVFU technology pre-treats only 10% of the urine volume, its lower
energy-efﬁciency (small scale) led to similar total consumption for
the FU (per m3 of wastewater, see SI 3) with LVFU technology.
The Reference results were cross-checked with literature. For a
similar WWTP (but based on Hardenberg WWTP with BCFS pro-
cess), Wilsenach and van Loosdrecht (2006) determined equivalent
energy consumption (6% difference) and a methanol demand of
0.14 kg/m3, which was 50% lower than the one from our study. This
could be explained by the better denitriﬁcation obtained from
Hardenberg WWTP (Henze, 2008). The methanol demand is,
however, in the same order of magnitude as the ﬁve-stage Bar-
denpho process from Jimenez et al. (2015). Finally, the same range
of iron chloride than the one from STOWA (2001) was modelled.
No chemical was required for VFU-based scenarios (decrease of
nutrient load of 9% for SVFU scenario and of 45% for LVFU-based
scenarios) and iron chloride demand (kg/m3) for SVFU scenario is
7% lower than Reference scenario. The only exception was for the
LVFU-EPC-AD scenario which consumed more iron chloride than
the Reference scenario in order to extract COD for biogas produc-
tion. Additionally, the methanol consumption is higher to replace
the COD extracted by EPC and to denitrify the ammonia mass ﬂow
from digester supernatant. Electricity consumption decreased with
the level of sophistication of the scenario, from 2% with SVFU
to 30% with LVFU-PST scenario, until achieving autarky with LVFU-
EPC-AD scenario thanks to the cogeneration process (85 Wh/m3 of
exported electricity). The produced sludge mass increased by 29%
in the LVFU-PST scenario but decreased by 13% in the LVFU-EPC-AD
scenario due to the additional sludge treatment. Emissions to water
and air were in the same order of magnitude for all scenarios. We
can nevertheless notice the signiﬁcant decrease of nitrate and
phosphate concentration in the efﬂuent for VFU-based scenarios,
especially in LVFU scenario due to the oversized plant. Lower
dinitrogen monoxide and carbon dioxide emissions were observed
for the VFU-based scenarios, since less nitrogen was nitriﬁed. The
emissions of hydrogen and biogenic methane were slightly
increased in LVFU in comparison to Reference scenario, because the
plant was oversized and fermentation also occurred with some
acetogen and methanogen bacteria growing in the anaerobic re-
actors. The higher methane emissions in the LVFU-EPC-AD scenario
were due to the stripping of dissolved methane produced in the
digester and recycled into the water line. Concerning the WWTP
infrastructure, the redesign of the plant could decrease the volume
of reactors of 18%, 27% and 31% for LVFU-SR, LVFU-PST and LVFU-EPC-
AD scenarios, respectively.
3.2. Impact assessment analysis
Since the aim of the study was to analyse the impacts of VFU
implementation, the relative differences between VFU-based sce-
narios and Reference scenario are discussed here and not the ab-
solute impact scores of each scenario. Fig. 4 presents the different
LCA impact categories (global warming potential e GWP, fresh-
water eutrophication potential e FEP, marine eutrophication po-
tential e MEP, terrestrial acidiﬁcation potential e TAP and single
score) and operational costs for each VFU-based scenario (see SI 4
for other LCA scores and relative differences). A positive value
means that the VFU implementation scenario is performing worse
than the Reference scenario, and vice-versa. The total line and
associated error bar (representing 95% conﬁdence interval) were
obtained from the 500 simulations while the contribution of each
process was calculated with the mean input values. The average
result from the 500 input samples and the total result from the
mean input simulations differed slightly, as expected, but stayed in
the same order of magnitude.
Regarding the LCA results, VFU-based scenarios generated less
environmental impacts than the Reference scenario, except for
three midpoint categories (ozone depletion, particulate matter
formation and terrestrial acidiﬁcation). The obtained advantages
are due to the lower consumption of methanol (impacting mostly
fossil resources depletion because of its production from natural
gas) and electricity of the WWTP, the lower water volume for toilet
ﬂushing, the lower WWTP emissions to air (mainly dinitrogen
monoxide affecting GWP) and to water (nitrate and phosphate in
the efﬂuent dominating MEP and FEP, respectively), and the avoi-
ded fertilizer production. With the redesign of the WWTP, addi-
tional beneﬁts were obtained from the decreased amount of
infrastructure. The LVFU-EPC-AD scenario also had avoided impacts
thanks to the production of electricity. However, the increased iron
chloride consumption (supernatant treatment) and the cogenera-
tion process (mainly greenhouse gas emissions) led to negative
effects. In Fig. 5, the sludge disposal process, including cogeneration
and incineration, has higher environmental impacts than the
Reference scenario, even if the amount of sludge mass sent to
incineration is lower (i.e. the contribution of impacts from

and nitrogen concentration, the sensitivity could be explained by

simulated by SUMO). The contribution of WWTP lifetime un-
certainties to the three last scenarios clearly came from the high
impacts of infrastructure on the environment. The current density
obtained from the MEC was highly uncertain (45% of variation) and
its value affected the size of the VFU technology and, therefore, the
associated materials and electricity inputs. Finally, we observed
that dinitrogen oxide emissions had signiﬁcant effects on the re-
sults and the input variability of 50% made the results very sensitive
to the associated factor.
Regarding operational costs, the advantage was clearly driven by
the water savings for toilet ﬂushing (urine collection process). This
result could be interpreted as if the main economic advantage of
the VFU-based scenarios would come from the use of separated
toilets and not from the recovery of nutrients from source-
separated urine. However, this outcome should not be taken as
such since it is based on a relatively simple calculation focusing on
the operational costs without considering infrastructure, exter-
nalities and personnel costs (discussed in section 4.1). The price of
drinking water usually includes the costs of wastewater treatment
indirectly, which could therefore be affected by the modiﬁcation of
the sewage system. In addition, the economic gain of VFU-based
scenarios was still valid if we excluded the urine collection process
in the calculation. This was because of the fertilizer revenues and
the electricity savings of the WWTP as well as the electricity pro-
duction and the lower sludge amount generated for LVFU-EPC-AD
scenario. The costs of VFU technology were dominated by elec-
tricity consumption. The amplitude of the errors bars was small and
mostly explained by the uncertainties of the ﬂushing parameters
(volume and number) and the water price.
4. Discussion
4.1. Results representativeness
This study highlighted signiﬁcant environmental and economic
advantages of the implementation of the new source-separation
technology, both at the small and decentralized scale and at the
large and centralized scale. These environmental beneﬁts of urine
separation systems were also obtained by previous authors (Remy,
2010; Ishii and Boyer, 2015; Landry and Boyer, 2016; Bisinella de
Faria et al., 2015), who highlighted the interest of saving water,
chemicals, energy and of applying recovered nutrients on agricul-
tural ﬁelds. Different outcomes for economic impacts were drawn
by Ishii and Boyer (2015) who did not observe signiﬁcant differ-
ences between scenarios and by Landry and Boyer (2016) who
concluded that urine separation would generate additional costs.
These two studies included capital expenditures, contrary to this
one. To avoid quick extrapolation and biased generalization of the
results of this study, it is important to mention their main
limitations.
First, by deﬁnition, this is a prospective evaluation, implying
unknown factors for the future operation and management of
sewage systems (e.g. policy decisions, new legislation constraints).
In particular, for the VFU technology, uncertainties remain on its
performances and costs at a larger scale. To model new technolo-
gies, numerous hypotheses have to be made because of the lack of
knowledge. However, modelling is probably the best way to tackle
the lack of information and the variability/uncertainty of pilot
measurements, which are retrieved from a smaller technology scale
than the one assumed for the studied scenarios. Beside the uncer-
tainty of the foreground data, forecasts of background data, such as
future electricity mix or future price of chemicals, also induce un-
certainties that are hardly quantiﬁed and therefore difﬁcult to
reduce.
Regarding the WWTP modelling, the steady state simulation
could bias the results, as shown in Bisinella de Faria et al. (2015)
with an underestimation of 10% of endpoint impacts compared to
a dynamic simulation. Due to the high calculation time of a dy-
namic simulation, in this study, we reduced the time variability of
inﬂuent characteristics (composition and ﬂow) to a speciﬁcation of
uncertainty distributions for the Monte Carlo analysis. Then, the
results were obtained from representative Dutch conditions with a
speciﬁc WWTP benchmark.
The authors assumed that urine separation at the large scale
could foster the optimization of sewage treatment (in particular,
the LVFU-EPC-AD scenario). In order to check if the obtained ad-
vantages were not only due to WWTP reconﬁguration, the LVFU-
EPC-AD scenario was compared with a new reference scenario
including enhanced primary sedimentation and digestion. The
advantages were lower than with the initial Reference scenario, but
still signiﬁcant (about a 10% decrease of environmental impacts and
operational costs). Compared to other European countries, the
main difference in terms of wastewater management likely comes
from the sludge disposal route. In the Netherlands, almost all of the
sludge produced is incinerated while other countries can spread a
signiﬁcant part on agricultural ﬁelds (e.g. 43% of sludge is spread in
France based on MEDDE-DEB, 2014). The sustainability advantage
could be lower for urine separation technology implementation
because of the existing nutrients recovery scheme and of the lower
nutrient content of digested sludge. The impacts of VFU imple-
mentation with other wastewater characteristics and process
chains (highly variable within and between countries) could look
very different. The use of calibrated simulation tools can therefore
allow for reﬁning the results, but this makes it more difﬁcult to
generalize the conclusions.
Concerning the LCA, further assumptions were made, e.g. dis-
tances with suppliers and WWTP infrastructures (calculated based
on reactors volume ratio). The main criticism concerns the
modelling of the consequences of struvite and ammonium sulphate
production, which were based on a simpliﬁed market analysis.
Struvite could be used as raw material for fertilizer production and
therefore replace phosphorous ore. Market trends should be
considered (e.g. the potential overproduction of fertilizers in the
Netherlands could lead to exports to neighbouring countries) as
well as further investigation into the impacts on the agricultural
sector (e.g. nutrient uptake, fate of micropollutants) and the
equivalence between fertilizers (e.g. struvite also brings magne-
sium to the plants). These questions would deserve speciﬁc analysis
which goes beyond the scope of the present work. Another limi-
tation concerns the exclusion of the toilets infrastructure. From the
results of Landry and Boyer (2016), the related processes can
represent between 10% and 20% of the environmental score of the
scenarios with urine source separation (in particular due to addi-
tional piping system). Finally, uncertainties also come from the
ecoinvent background processes: geographical representativeness
(Swiss, European average data), temporal representativeness (some
datasets from 2003) and the use of proxies for some processes.
Finally, the evaluation of the economic impacts were restricted
to the operational costs, therefore excluding the costs related to
infrastructure (capital expenditures of a new technology, treatment
device replacements), pollution (externalities of emissions) and
maintenance (personnel costs). These additional data could have a
signiﬁcant inﬂuence on the results, as highlighted by Landry and
Boyer (2016). In particular, the installation costs of diverting urine
systems and of VFU technology could dominate the operational
costs and therefore impact the conclusions regarding the potential
advantage of source-separated urine treatment. However, the
extrapolation of capital expenditures for a large scale technology
E. Igos et al. / Water Research 126 (2017) 50e59
57

based on pilot data is very challenging. This evaluation should
therefore be carried out at a later stage of the technology devel-
opment. Price volatilities could also have large effects on the eco-
nomic proﬁle of VFU technology. In particular, the market evolution
of recovered fertilizers, such as struvite, is difﬁcult to predict. On
the one side, the evolution of legislation could boost the interest for
alternative fertilizers and therefore increase their market price. On
the other side, the development of this sector could lead to a
competition between recovery technologies and therefore decrease
the price of the recovered products.
4.2. Coupling approach
The coupling framework applied in this paper aimed ﬁrst at
evaluating the consequences of urine pre-treatment on WWTP
operation based on rigorous modelling of each unit of operation. In
previous literature, except from Bisinella de Faria et al. (2015), the
authors used either empirical ratios based on inﬂuent ﬂow or
nutrient removal to calculate a few inventory data (Landry and
Boyer, 2016; Ishii and Boyer, 2015; Spa_ngberg et al., 2014) or the
ecoinvent tool (Doka, 2009) to calculate all the LCI ﬂows based on
inﬂuent characteristics (Benetto et al., 2009). The comparison of
inventory data calculated with these previous approaches and the
ones obtained in this study are detailed in SI 5. The use of empirical
ratios limits the coverage of impacts induced by urine separation
(focus on few inventory data). The representativeness of the results
can also be questioned. For example, the application of an elec-
tricity factor based on inﬂuent ﬂow (Ishii and Boyer, 2015) does not
consider the wastewater and treatment quality and led to very
different results than the ones obtained in this study. The use of
ratios based on nutrient removal can partly reﬂect treatment per-
formances and lead to similar consumption like the one from this
study. However, the reductions induced by VFU-based scenarios are
linear and equal for all inventory data (chemicals consumption and
nitrous oxide emissions modelled by Spa_ngberg et al., 2014). The
absence of chemical use for VFU-based scenarios cannot be
modelled with the ratios approach. The use of the ecoinvent tool
could be an alternative because it calculates all inventory ﬂows
based on speciﬁed inﬂuent characteristics. However, the treatment
line modelled for Swiss conditions can be very different from the
studied case. It results in a list of inputs, not necessarily used by the
analysed WWTP (e.g. consumption of iron sulphate and aluminium
sulphate). Also, some calculations are quite simplistic in the
ecoinvent tool, e.g. chloride or sulphate emissions to river do not
include the addition of chemicals. From this comparison and with
less sophisticated approaches from literature, it is clear that
coupling LCA with advanced process simulation tools can better
represent the consequences of urine pre-treatment on the sewage
network, due to the quantiﬁcation of all inventory ﬂows, the in-
clusion of complex relationships, process modiﬁcations, and the
calibration of the case study.
The automated information ﬂow between the process simulator
and the impacts calculator, implemented using the Python pro-
gramming language, is also an added value. It allows for performing
a high number of simulations for operation parameter variability
and uncertainty evaluation, which would be otherwise unfeasible.
Additionally, the use of Brightway2 instead of common LCA
software (such as SimaPro or GaBi) allows for using speciﬁc tech-
niques, in particular, ﬁxing the sampling seed. This is important for
comparing scenarios based on the same set of inputs and avoid
artiﬁcial variability between them. Indeed, when comparing sce-
narios, many parameters or processes are usually in common and
therefore the uncertainties associated to the difference could be
lower than the absolute uncertainties of one scenario. LCA practi-
tioners should therefore be careful with the correlation between
scenarios and the interpretation of the outputs uncertainties.
Commercial LCA tools also have limited functions for uncertainty
and sensitivity analyses. In particular, they do not provide functions
for global sensitivity analysis, but only for scenarios analysis (one-
at-a-time approach). This simpliﬁed approach can be useful to
analyse the inﬂuence of modelling choices, but it does not consider
the inputs' distributions nor quantify the contribution of variance
for parameters' uncertainties and their strengths (e.g. p-value of
correlation coefﬁcients).
Despite the reﬁnement of the calculation and of the analysis, the
applied approach also has its drawbacks. First, the modelling of
processes requires an existing knowledge, which can be lacking for
completely new technologies, such as the MEC step of VFU tech-
nology. The use or development of process simulation tools needs
engineering competences, while its automated interaction with
LCA calculation require computing competences. Not all compe-
tences mobilized for this work were fully transferred among all
team members, but the programming competences were at the
centre of the coupling. The compatibility between software tools
also represent a bottleneck, which could be solved here thanks to
Python formatting of the SUMO outputs and of the Brightway2 li-
brary. Finally, the high calculation time (between 1 day and one
week for 500 iterations of one scenario) can represent a barrier. LCA
practitioners are used to calculation times of a few hours for this
sampling number. In this study, this long duration comes from
WWTP simulation, which needs to solve a non-linear model con-
taining more than 60 reactions. For example, reaching steady state
for each sample needs simulating 60 days of operation, requiring
several minutes of real time per iteration.
5. Conclusions
This study aimed at assessing the environmental and economic
impacts of a new source-separated urine technology, VFU, for
prospective implementation scenarios in the Netherlands. It was
based on the coupling of process simulation tools with LCA and
operational cost evaluation. The results showed that signiﬁcant
beneﬁts (even considering inputs' uncertainties) could be achieved
by pre-treating 10% or 50% of urine volume from the sewage
network based on a WWTP with a capacity of 50,000 PE equipped
with biological phosphorus removal. Indeed, the impacts of tech-
nology implementation were compensated by the avoided pro-
duction of fertilizers, by the decrease of chemicals and electricity
consumption, as well as by the lower direct emissions of the WWTP
(in particular nitrous oxides to air as well as nitrate and phosphate
to water), and by the water savings from reduced toilet ﬂushing.
Additionally, to optimize the WWTP operation, a redesign of the
treatment line (without post-denitriﬁcation and adding primary
sedimentation and anaerobic digestion) would lead to higher
beneﬁts by reducing the infrastructure volume and even producing
energy due to a sludge digester. These advantages, and in particular
the potential energy autonomy of the WWTP, represent very
promising arguments for source-separated treatment, even if they
depend on the reference WWTP design. The future development of
the studied technology would allow for reﬁning the sustainability
assessment and therefore better substantiating the arguments for
its further commercialization (main limitations of the study related
to the modelling of fertilizer substitution and cost evaluation). The
use of a simulation tool to derive the LCI allowed for a better rep-
resentation of the consequences of urine pre-treatment and the
extrapolation of pilot data. Meanwhile, uncertainty treatment
techniques improved the sampling method and results interpre-
tation (as compared to common LCA software). From our side, there
is no doubt that the integration of the best available simulation and
analysis tools with LCA evaluation should foster the inclusion of

programming competences to ensure the reliability and trans-
parency of the results communicated to stakeholders.

records.full_text:
Is SCENA a good approach for side-stream integrated treatment from
an environmental and economic point of view?
Stefano Longo a, *, Nicola Frison b, Daniele Renzi c, Francesco Fatone d,
Almudena Hospido a
a Department of Chemical Engineering, Institute of Technology, Universidade de Santiago de Compostela, 15782 Santiago de Compostela, Galicia, Spain
b Department of Biotechnology, University of Verona, Strada Le Grazie 15, 37134 Verona, Italy
c Alto Trevigiano Servizi Srl, Ufﬁcio Ottimizzazione Processi depurativi, Via Schiavonesca Priula 86, 31044 Montebelluna, Italy
d Department SIMAU, Faculty of Engineering, Polytechnic University of Marche, Via Brecce Bianche 12, Ancona, Italy
a r t i c l e
i n f o
Article history:
Received 14 March 2017
Received in revised form
1 September 2017
Accepted 2 September 2017
Available online 6 September 2017
Keywords:
Short cut enhanced nutrient abatement
(SCENA)
Side-stream treatment
Life cycle analysis (LCA)
Life cycle cost (LCC)
Monte carlo analysis
Sludge phosphorus availability
a b s t r a c t
The environmental and economic beneﬁts and burdens of including the ﬁrst Short Cut Enhanced
Nutrient Abatement (SCENA) into a real municipal wastewater treatment plant were evaluated using life
cycle assessment (LCA) and life cycle cost (LCC). The implications of accomplishing nitrogen (N) removal
and phosphorus (P) recovery via nitrite in the side stream were assessed taking into account the actual
efﬂuent quality improvement, the changes in the electricity and chemical consumption, N2O, CO2 and
CH4 emissions and the effects of land application of biosolids, among others. In addition, a case-speciﬁc
estimation of the P availability when sludge is applied to land, therefore replacing conventional fertilizer,
was performed. Furthermore, to account for the variability in input parameters, and to address the
related uncertainties, Monte Carlo simulation was applied.
The analysis revealed that SCENA in the side stream is an economic and environmentally friendly
solution compared to the traditional plant layout with no side-stream treatment, thanks to the reduction
of energy and chemical use for the removal of N and P, respectively. The uncertainty analysis proved the
validity of the LCA results for global warming potential and impact categories related to the consumption
of fossil-based electricity and chemicals, while robust conclusions could not be drawn on freshwater
eutrophication and toxicity-related impact categories. Furthermore, three optimization scenarios were
also evaluated proving that the performance of the WWTP can be further improved by, for instance,
substituting gravitational for mechanical thickening of the sludge or changing the operational strategy to
the chemically enhanced primary treatment, although this second alternative will increase the opera-
tional cost by 5%. Finally, the outcomes show that shifting P removal from chemical precipitation in the
main line to biologically enhanced uptake in the side stream is key to reducing chemicals use, thus the
operational cost, and increasing the environmental beneﬁt of synthetic fertilizers replacement.
© 2017 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.
1. Introduction
Within the last decade it has been required that wastewater
treatment plants (WWTPs) meet increasing efﬂuent quality stan-
dards at affordable cost. Separate treatment of the reject water
resulting from dewatering of anaerobic digested sludge at WWTP
can be a solution for meeting higher load requirements or stricter
efﬂuent standards regarding nitrogen (N) (Malamis et al., 2014).
Regarding this issue, several technologies have been developed and
successfully implemented at full scale (Lackner et al., 2014). Among
them, the implementation of one-stage partial nitritation/anam-
mox, also known as DEMON (Wett, 2006), CANON (Third et al.,
2001) and OLAND (Kuai and Verstraete, 1998), could positively
contribute to the optimization of the energy balance of WWTPs by
minimizing energy for N removal. As an example, at the Strass
WWTP, DEMON in the side stream has reduced electricity
consumed per kg of N removed by 44% (Schaubroeck et al., 2015).
Furthermore, as a consequence of the growing awareness of the
need to control phosphorus (P) emissions, which is reﬂected in the
increasingly stringent regulations (Gaterell et al., 2000), a boost
* Corresponding author.
E-mail address: stefano.longo@usc.es (S. Longo).
Contents lists available at ScienceDirect
Water Research
journal homepage: www.elsevier.com/locate/watres
http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.watres.2017.09.006
0043-1354/© 2017 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.
Water Research 125 (2017) 478e489

was given to the existing wastewater industry in order to increase
its removal (Hukari et al., 2016). Until now, P has been regarded
more as a contaminant than a resource. This perspective has started
to change in recent years, since unlike N, P is a limited resource,
whose recovery should be promoted as much as possible (Desmidt
et al., 2015). However, the most common approach for removing
phosphate from wastewater is metal salt precipitation, which
makes the precipitate unrecoverable for possible industrial pro-
cessing into fertilizer (De-Bashan and Bashan, 2004; Melia et al.,
2017; Torri et al., 2017).
In this regard, the recent introduction of Short-Cut Enhanced
Nutrient Abatement (SCENA), for treatment of nutrient-rich reject
water, brings an important improvement because P can also be
removed from reject water by its enhanced bioaccumulation in the
same system (Renzi et al., 2015). In fact, SCENA is a side-stream
technology, which combines via-nitrite ammonia oxidation (also
known as short-cut nitriﬁcation) with denitrifying enhanced bio-
logical phosphorus removal (EBPR) processes by the addition of
external volatile fatty acids (VFAs) produced on-site (Longo et al.,
2015). In this process NH4 is oxidized to NO2, which is then
reduced
to
gaseous
N2
by
denitrifying
polyphosphate-
accumulating organisms (DPAO), hence resulting in the potential
production of a high added value product (Frison et al., 2016).
As a holistic environmental assessment approach, life cycle
assessment (LCA) (ISO, 2006) has proved to act as a valuable tool in
evaluating the environmental aspects and improvement opportu-
nities of wastewater systems (Corominas et al., 2013; Yoshida et al.,
2013; Zang et al., 2015), including most impacts upon the envi-
ronment, which helps avoid the risk of ‘problem transfer’ (Guinee,
2002). In a LCA study Rodriguez-Garcia et al. (2014) compared
different side-stream technologies. The authors emphasized that
eutrophication impacts can be signiﬁcantly reduced by using side-
stream technologies, while global warming potential was only
marginally reduced. Moreover, there is a certain consensus on the
trade-offs between higher N removal achieved by implementation
of side-stream technologies and increased direct emissions, with
the results being very sensitive to the N2O emission balance
(Schaubroeck et al., 2015; Hauck et al., 2016).
Furthermore, the evaluation of appropriate schemes for waste-
water treatment has to consider not only environmental concerns
but also economic aspects (Corominas et al., 2013) (e.g., investment,
operation and maintenance costs). In this respect life cycle cost
(LCC) (Fabrycky and Blanchard, 1991) is a useful framework for
assessing the economic sustainability of different wastewater
treatment schemes over the whole life span of the plant.
Thus, to investigate the holistic environmental and economic
life cycle implications of N and P removal through SCENA, this study
aims to answer the following questions: i) Is the SCENA system
environmentally and economically sustainable? ii) How does
SCENA improve the environmental and economic performance of a
real WWTP? To provide answers to our questions preliminary re-
sults of the ﬁrst full-scale SCENA system were analysed.
2. Material and methods
2.1. Goal and scope
The goal of the study was to assess the environmental and
economic beneﬁts of implementing SCENA in the side-stream
treatment of reject water at a real municipal WWTP; the Carbon-
era facilities (Treviso, Northern Italy).
The system boundary included the WWTP operation with all its
associated background processes, such as provision of energy and
production and transportation of chemicals, efﬂuent discharge and
direct gaseous emissions deriving from the treatment process as
well as composting and posterior land application. Regarding the
construction phase, only data from SCENA were accounted for in
the assessment, as the rest of the facilities remain the same. Up-
stream wastewater collection and transportation were not included
in the analysis although it has been shown that the sewer system
makes an important contribution in comparison to the WWTP
(Risch et al., 2015); however, these impacts would be the same for
each scenario, and are therefore not relevant for comparative
purposes.
The functional unit (FU) has been based on the reduction of
eutrophication as deﬁned by the CML methodology (Guinee, 2002):
1 kg PO43 eq. removed. This approach helps with the comparison of
different inﬂuent wastewater loads by weighting the main pollut-
ants, and reﬂects the service that the WWTP delivers: the removal
of
contaminants
(e.g.,
chemical
oxygen
demand,
N
and
P)
(Rodriguez-Garcia et al., 2014).
2.2. Treatment options
The ﬂow diagram of the SCENA system implemented at the
Carbonera WWTP is shown in Fig. 1, and an elaborate description of
the plant is given in Section A of the supplementary ﬁle.
In Table 1 are reported the different treatment conﬁgurations of

S. Longo et al. / Water Research 125 (2017) 478e489

the WWTP under investigation. The environmental and economic
performance of the plant with no additional nutrient removal from
reject water (2015, scenario 0) was compared with the new plant
layout (2016, scenario 1) in which the full-scale SCENA is operating
in the side stream. Additionally, three different alternative conﬁg-
urations (scenarios 2A-C) for the optimization of the SCENA system
and the whole plant performance were simulated. Since the success
of EBPR depends on the constant availability of VFAs, the objective
of the optimization was to increase the organic loading rate of the
SBFR in order to improve and stabilize the VFAs production, which
could enable higher rates of biological nutrient removal in the
short-cut sequencing batch reactor (scSBR). To do so, in scenario 2A
sludge dynamic thickening (SDT) was considered as an effective
technology to increase the total solids (TS) concentration of the
mixed sludge by up to 5%; in scenario 2B, Chemically assisted pri-
mary sedimentation (CAPS), consisting of adding chemicals in or-
der to increase the coagulation, ﬂocculation and sedimentation of
raw wastewater, was simulated. The application of CAPS was cho-
sen since it does not require any further signiﬁcant structural
intervention, thus saving investment costs and footprint (De Feo
et al., 2012); ﬁnally, in scenario 2C, the primary sedimentation
tank was replaced by a rotating belt screen (RBS) system in order to
improve the performance of primary treatment (removal of TS up
to 70%) without use of chemicals (Paulsrud et al., 2014). The CAPS
system, apart from TS and chemical oxygen demand (COD), can
enhance also the removal of P from wastewater. As a consequence
scenario 2B will be characterized by a lower efﬂuent discharge for P,
despite the fact that all scenarios have the same efﬂuent discharge
limits. However, considering the increasingly lower discharge limit
policies with regard to P or the spreading of the efﬂuent load tax
incentives in Europe (consider, for example, the case of Germany)
this scenario is interesting to study for its potential future
application. The inventory assumptions for scenarios 2A-C are
summarized in Section C of the supplementary ﬁle.
2.3. Inventory data
2.3.1. Operational data and background processes
Operational data (e.g., water and sludge ﬂow/composition, en-
ergy consumption, chemical usage etc.) were directly gathered in-
situ and provided by the company that operates the plant from
the daily log results of 2015 and 2016, unless mentioned otherwise.
Data for the processes of the background system (production of
electricity, chemicals and Ne and P-based fertilisers) come from the
ecoinvent v3.1 database (cut-off system model) (Weidema et al.,
2013). A complete discussion of the inventory data collection is
given in Section B of the supplementary ﬁle.
2.3.2. Fertilizer replacement
The beneﬁts of sludge application on arable land can be
accounted by crediting for the avoided production of mineral N and
P fertilizer. The selection of the replacement ratio at which the N
and P in sludge replace mineral fertilizer is challenging and it was
shown to be important for the overall LCA results (Heimersson
et al., 2017). Contrary to N, the availability of P in chemical and
biological solids is an area of on-going research (Menezes-
Blackburn et al., 2016), although it is largely accepted that the P
in solids containing high levels of aluminium (Al) or iron (Fe) is less
available for uptake by plants (Qin et al., 2015). The reason is that
metal phosphates (e.g., iron and aluminium phosphates) are un-
available to plants (Melia et al., 2017; Torri et al., 2017), since they
are among the most difﬁcult of soil compounds from which to
solubilize P, which makes the P removed by metal salt precipitation
unrecoverable for possible industrial processing into fertilizer (De-
Fig. 1. Flow diagram of the SCENA system implemented at the Carbonera WWTP (sc. 1). Solid lines show ﬂow of water, dashed lines show sludge or solid ﬂow and dotted lines show
biogas ﬂow. In sc. 0, the anaerobic supernatant is recycled to the head of the plant without further treatment. Note: WAS ¼ waste activated sludge; GST ¼ gravitational sludge
thickener; SBFR ¼ sequencing batch fermentation reactor; AD ¼ anaerobic digester; VFAs ¼ volatile fatty acids; scSBR ¼ short-cut sequencing batch reactor.
S. Longo et al. / Water Research 125 (2017) 478e489
480

Bashan and Bashan, 2004).1 The substitution should be based on
the amount of mineral fertilizer that is actually replaced rather than
theoretically, as outlined in several recent LCA studies (Niero et al.,
2014; Heimersson et al., 2016, 2017). Thus, in this study case spe-
ciﬁc P replacement ratios were estimated for each scenario to ﬁll
this gap (see Section E of the supplementary ﬁle). On the contrary,
due to the much more stable N content in sludge, a replacement
ratio of 0.5 was used for N (Foley et al., 2010), which is a ratio widely
used in LCA studies (Heimersson et al., 2016). Sludge stabilization
treatments may also inﬂuence the plant availability of chemical
sludge. To the best of our knowledge, only one study (Alvarenga
et al., 2017) has tackled the effect of anaerobic digestion (AD) on
the P availability of biosolids deriving from chemical precipitation
and there, the effect of AD was reported as not signiﬁcant. More-
over, if biosolids had undergone thermal stabilization during
composting, metal-bound P minerals in biosolids is considerably
reduced and, consequently, the release of available P is restricted
(Hogan et al., 2001). Therefore, based on the reasoning above, we
have assumed that the stabilization processes of biosolids produced
at the Carbonera WWTP before the application to agriculture (i.e.,
AD followed by sludge composting) will not alter the P availability.
The corresponding synthetic fertilisers considered for substitution
were ammonium sulphate ((NH4)2SO4) and diammonium phos-
phate ((NH4)2HPO4) as the generic N and P2O5 sources, respectively
(Rodriguez-Garcia et al., 2011).2
2.4. Life cycle impact assessment
SimaPro v8.1 software was used for the impact assessment.
Excluding global warming potential (GWP), where the last version
of the IPCC method (for a 100-year time horizon) (IPCC, 2013) was
used, impacts were assessed using the Hierarchist ReCiPe(H)
(v1.08) which is based on common policy principles including the
frame (Goedkoop et al., 2008a). Although endpoint results may be
perceived more relevant for decision-making, midpoint assessment
directly relates the inventory results into environmental impacts.
Moreover, the use of endpoint indicators relies on additional as-
sumptions and introduces greater uncertainty in the modelling
process compared to midpoint indicators (Corominas et al., 2013).
Therefore, the ReCiPe midpoint method was selected as it complies
with all essential aspects for human toxicity (EC-JRC, 2011) and
includes terrestrial ecotoxicity (Goedkoop et al., 2008a). However,
human toxicity assessment is also advised to be estimated using
USEtox (Hauschild et al., 2013), hence we also applied the USEtox
method (Rosenbaum et al., 2008) solely for the human toxicity, and
the results are presented in Section H of the supplementary ﬁle.
Other impacts covered in this study are ozone depletion, terrestrial
acidiﬁcation, photochemical oxidant formation and particular
matter formation. Although these impact categories have gained
less attention than the others discussed above, they are becoming a
standard in the LCA studies (Zang et al., 2015). Land-use related
environmental indicators were excluded for their limited relevance
in the wastewater treatment sector (Zang et al., 2015). Furthermore,
resource depletion categories were excluded since these impacts
are
not
well
deﬁned
and
modelled
in
LCA
methodologies
(Hauschild et al., 2013), and there is still no globally agreed method
that is sufﬁciently robust (Drielsma et al., 2016).
Finally, to shows the environmental trade-offs between avoided
impact due to wastewater treatment and generated impact by the

WWTP's life cycle, the net environmental beneﬁt (NEB) approach
(Godin et al., 2012) was also applied and the results are presented in
Section G of the supplementary ﬁle.
2.5. Uncertainty analysis
The importance to test and statically evaluate the environmental
impacts in the LCA context has been reported (Nhu et al., 2016).
This is particular crucial in the wastewater treatment sector where
the large data variability and input data coverage can heavily in-
ﬂuence the LCA results (Yoshida et al., 2014). Different types of
uncertainty include those relating to parameters (e.g., inaccuracies
in measurement, mismatch between the representativeness and
use of data, and variability resulting from horizontal averaging),
and those concerning the LCA model (e.g., the uncertainties of the
characterization factors) and the scenario choices (e.g., choice of
functional unit or characterization/weighing methods) (Huijbregts,
2002). In this study, we will mainly focus on data uncertainty but
will also discuss brieﬂy the inﬂuence of some model and scenario
choices (Section H and G of the supplementary ﬁle, respectively).
When an uncertainty calculation is performed, it is also important
to keep track of the correlations in the data (Nhu et al., 2016). Two
kinds of correlation can be identiﬁed: (1) correlations between
process chains of production systems and (2) correlations within a
process record. In this study, only the type of correlation between
process chains of production systems is considered, as a compari-
son Monte Carlo is carried out with SimaPro (in which such issues
are excluded).
Based on the uncertainty of the life cycle inventory (LCI) data
expressed
as probability
distributions
(see
Table
S7
of
the
supplementary ﬁle), the Monte Carlo simulations were run with
1000 iterations at a signiﬁcance level of 95%. Clearly, the output
distributions generated by any Monte Carlo simulation, and any
conclusion derived therefrom, are sensitive to the choice of input
distribution. In the current study, when the class of distribution was
known a priori we proceeded with direct estimation of the pa-
rameters of the distribution (e.g., mean, standard deviation). If the
class of the distribution was unknown, default distributions were
consulted for possible use based on the range of possible values
(Lipton et al., 1995).
2.6. Life cycle cost
In general, costs can be divided into capital and operational
costs. The capital cost was estimated by summing the construc-
tion, mechanical instrument and consulting costs, as obtained
from
the
WWTP
operation
ofﬁce
(see
Table
S6
of
the
supplementary ﬁle). As major operating costs, the electricity and
chemical consumptions, sludge disposal and staff costs were
considered. In order to share the same time boundary as the LCA,
the time value of money is considered. Net present value (NPV)
was adopted as an indicator to evaluate the economic perfor-
mance of the different scenarios. The NPV for wastewater treat-
ment was calculated based on Eq. (1):
NPV ¼ CAPEX þ
X
n
OPEX
ð1 þ iÞn;
(1)
Where CAPEX and OPEX denote capital cost and operational cost,
respectively, i refers to discount rate adjusted for inﬂation, equal to
5% (this is a compromise ﬁgure based on market interest rate, cost
of capital and time preference considerations (Hermelink and de
Jager, 2015)), and n represents years.
Furthermore, in order to share the same functional unit as the
LCA, the total cost is divided into LCC per FU (1 kg PO4 eq. removed).
3. Results
3.1. Inventory analysis
An overview of the LCI of the treatment options under analysis is
reported in Table S3 of the supplementary ﬁle. Once SCENA was
operating, the electricity consumed per kg of N removed was
reduced from 7.51 kWh in the main line to 3.21 kWh in the side
stream. This is consequence of the joint effects of a more efﬁcient N
removal pathway (i.e., via nitrite compared to complete nitriﬁca-
tion in the main line) and aeration system (i.e., superﬁne bubble
diffusers as opposed to course bubble diffusers in the main line),
which make removing N in the side stream more convenient. Be-
sides, energy consumed for aeration in the main line can also be
further reduced by about 20% due to the implementation of CAPS
and RBS systems (sc. 2B and 2C, respectively), since they can in-
crease the organic matter recovered from wastewater, thus result-
ing in a reduced COD load entering the biological bioreactor of the
main line.
On a process level, the via-nitrite N removal in the scSBR
improved the efﬂuent quality of the WWTP in terms of total ni-
trogen (Table 2), while the beneﬁts regarding total phosphorus
were less evident since the additional P removal in the side stream
is compensated for by a lower chemical P removal in the main line
(i.e., in order to maintain the required efﬂuent quality, thus opti-
mizing costs). However, sc. 2B is characterized by a much better P
efﬂuent quality. This is a consequence of the fact that the CAPS
system can attain higher P removal (up to 70%) in the primary
sedimentation tank due to a signiﬁcantly higher dosage of poly
aluminium chloride (PAC) (Table S3).
For sc. 0, N2O emissions were found to be approximately
4.55 kgNN-N2O/d that represents 1.16% of the N inﬂuent load.
Compared with previous studies (see for example Kampschreur
et al. (2009)), N2O emissions were remarkably high at the Car-
bonera WWTP. Some operational features employed during the
process, i.e., intermittent aeration of the activated sludge process,
have been identiﬁed as possible features promoting N2O emissions.
However, our emission factor is in accordance with the outcomes
for long-term online measurement campaign carried out at the
Viikinmaki WWTP,3 which presented an emission factor of 1.9% for
inﬂuent N (Kosonen et al., 2016). In the scSBR of the SCENA system,
the N2O emissions were 1.42% of the N inﬂuent load of the side-
stream line, which is in accordance with the range of 0.24e1.49%
previously measured by Frison et al. (2015). Moreover, the N2O
emissions were comparable with values in the literature for bio-
logical systems accomplishing N removal via partial nitritation/
anammox (Schaubroeck et al., 2015). Thus, shifting N removal from
the main stream to the side stream of the WWTP did not increase
N2O emissions as one might expect due to the higher risk of nitrite
accumulation.
3.2. Life cycle impact assessment of the treatment options
By integrating the recommendation of Zang et al. (2015), the
results are grouped into the following: freshwater and marine
eutrophication (Fig. 2); global warming potential (Fig. 3); toxicity
and
terrestrial
ecotoxicity
(Fig.
4);
and
ozone
depletion,
3 The process scheme of the Viikinmaki WWTP is different in comparison to the
Carbonera WWTP. At Viikinmaki the biological reactions are carried out in six
consecutive reactors, while at the Carbonera WWTP, aerated and anoxic phases are
performed in the same reactor. However, the two WWTPs share similar operational
features, e.g., ﬂuctuating inﬂuent ﬂow and intermittent aeration at the activated
sludge process.
S. Longo et al. / Water Research 125 (2017) 478e489
482

photochemical oxidant formation, particular matter formation, and
terrestrial acidiﬁcation (Fig. 5). All the impacts are expressed per kg
of PO4 eq. removed.
3.2.1. Eutrophication potential
Eutrophication has been emphasized by earlier studies and is
still considered the most relevant impact category when perform-
ing environmental evaluation of WWTPs (Corominas et al., 2013;
Zang et al., 2015). Thus, is especially important here as eutrophi-
cation impacts can be reduced immediately by implementing side-
stream treatment technologies to enhance the nutrient removal
efﬁciency. As expected, the impact of wastewater treatment on
eutrophication is dominated by the efﬂuent discharge and the
nutrient-related emissions during compost land application (Fig. 2).
After the implementation of SCENA, a reduction of 27% of the
marine eutrophication has been found (sc. 1) as a consequence of
the better efﬂuent quality with respect to N compounds (e.g., NH4
and NO3), which could also be further reduced (by an additional
18%) with the optimization of SCENA (sc. 2A-C). The beneﬁts in
terms of freshwater eutrophication are less evident since following
the installation of the SCENA system the operation of the WWTP
was adjusted to guarantee the same P efﬂuent quality. Neverthe-
less, the substitution of the primary clariﬁcation with the CAPS
system (sc. 2B) would imply a much lower P in the efﬂuent thanks
to the additional dosage of PAC, which translates in the reduction
by 43% of the freshwater eutrophication.
Finally, a considerable portion of the marine eutrophication
impact (19e21% depending of the scenario) is represented by the N
emissions due to land application.
3.2.2. Global warming potential
Impacts on this category are associated mainly with electricity
use and direct gaseous emissions during wastewater treatment
(Fig. 3).

For sc. 0, electricity accounts for 35% of the total impact and the
total plant greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions for 56%. According to
our measurements, the N2O emissions comprised 80% of the total
emissions, which is in line with the results reported by Kosonen
et al. (2016). Remaining impacts are characterized as follow: com-
posting (4.5%), efﬂuent release (2.3%), transport (1.5%) and pro-
duction of chemicals used (1.3%). On the beneﬁcial side, i.e.,
mitigating effects, two elements from compost application to land
are contributing here: C sequestration and avoided fertilizers, ac-
counting for 4.1% and 1.9% of the total impact, respectively.
The reduction of electricity consumption and N2O emissions
translates to a reduction of about 14% of GWP, respectively with the
following shares: 27% and 65%. The additional energy saving in sc.
2A could further decrease the GWP up to an additional 4%. In terms
of avoiding the impact from offset fertilizers, sc. 1 did not show any
improvement as the SCENA system was not yet optimized for P
removal (Table 2). When the potential optimization is evaluated,
the additional P removal in the side stream achieved in sc. 2A re-
sults in a lower PAC consumption (12%) in the main line and in a
higher P recovered in biosolids (8%) that leads to a beneﬁt from
avoided fertilizer. On the contrary, the increased P removal by the
CAPS conﬁguration (sc. 2B) did not increase the amount of fertilizer
avoided since the P recovered by the chemical process is not
available for plant uptake. Besides, sc. 2B and 2C do not result in a
net beneﬁt over the other conﬁgurations, since their advantages
(i.e., better performance of the SBFR and lower energy consumption
for aeration) were balanced by the higher chemical consumption in
sc. 2B and a slightly higher energy consumption of the RBS system
in sc. 2C.
3.2.3. Human toxicity and terrestrial ecotoxicity
Direct emissions of heavy metals dominate toxicity-related
impact categories, accounting for half of the total impact of hu-
man toxicity and almost the entire impact of terrestrial ecotoxicity
(Fig. 4). Once SCENA was up and running, no large variations took
place in heavy metals behaviour, which are mostly attributable to
anthropogenic activities anyway, such as trafﬁc emissions and
weather characteristics (Pe~na-Fernandez et al., 2015). In fact, the
lower impact of terrestrial ecotoxicity reported for sc. 1 and fol-
lowings is principally due to the better efﬂuent quality achieved
(i.e., higher FU). Emissions from transport of biosolids increase the
human toxicity impact; however, these are overcome by the avoi-
ded contaminants due to fertilisers’ replacement and their corre-
spondent
production.
Moreover,
emissions
associated
with
chemical manufacture increase the human toxicity and hence
shifting P removal from chemical precipitation to biological
removal by SCENA entails an additional advantage.

3.2.4. Other impact categories
The impact linked to ozone depletion mainly occurs due to the
electricity production and to a lesser extent to transport and
chemical manufacturing (Fig. 5a). Consequently, implementing
SCENA reduced the ozone depletion potential by 9% (sc. 1), which
can be further reduced (up to 14%) by sc. 2A and 2B. However, the
beneﬁcial effects generated by avoiding fertilizers and chemicals
use are offset by emissions from transport of biosolids.
The photochemical oxidant and particular matter formation
potentials (Fig. 5b and c, respectively) reﬂect the difference be-
tween the impacts due to the electricity and chemicals use for the
wastewater treatment and the ones avoided thanks to the land
application of biosolids. As seen for ozone depletion, implementing
energy efﬁciency with SCENA is beneﬁcial also for these impact
categories. Sc. 1 reduces the photochemical oxidant formation po-
tential by 9%. Furthermore, the increasing of P recovery in sc. 2A has
the potential to reduce the impact for the same category up to 13%.
The particular matter formation and terrestrial acidiﬁcation
(Fig. 5d) follow the trend observed for the photochemical oxidant
formation potential, even if in sc. 1 the ammonia emissions from
the anaerobic supernatant storage tank increased the particular
matter formation and terrestrial acidiﬁcation impacts. Connecting
this plant section to the air treatment system may mitigate these
impacts; however, until this is done, these emissions will likely
continue to be an issue. For the other scenarios (2A-C), the
ammonia emissions may be offset by eliminating the gravity
thickener of the digested sludge. Another source of contamination
derives from ﬂaring the biogas; this impact contributes about 5% to
the particular matter formation potential, and is similar for the
different scenarios.

Carlo frequency highest for sc. 2B (98.5%), followed by sc. 2A
(97.6%), sc. 2C (95.8%) and sc. 1 (92.8%). Regarding eutrophication,
the results are less clear, especially in terms of freshwater eutro-
phication. This is due to the already mentioned compensation ef-
fect in total P removal in order to maintain the same quality efﬂuent
and, consequently, the role of SCENA was favourable at lower
Monte Carlo frequencies of 55e83% and 75e88% with respect to
freshwater eutrophication and marine eutrophication, respectively.
The beneﬁts of SCENA (e.g., lower energy consumption for N
removal and chemical consumption for P removal) are more
evident in the fossil-based electricity and chemicals related impact
categories, such as ozone depletion (favourable at Monte Carlo
frequencies of 86e97%). Among the toxicity-related impact cate-
gories, human toxicity shows the highest uncertainty. Even if the
negative impacts of these categories are reduced per FU, after the
uncertainty analysis robust conclusions could not be drawn on the
effect of the SCENA system. This can be explained by the fact that
the concentration of heavy metals in the ﬁnal sludge and the
treated efﬂuent is not inﬂuenced by SCENA. Although the LCA re-
sults show that SCENA is also beneﬁcial in terms of photochemical
oxidant formation, particular matter formation and terrestrial
acidiﬁcation, the inclusion of the uncertainty revealed that these
differences are favourable with lower Monte Carlo frequencies with
respect to GWP. In short, the uncertainty analysis revealed sc. 2A as
the most environmentally friendly scenario, having lower impacts
for most of the categories with conﬁdent level of 90%.
3.3. Economic analysis
Results from the LCC are categorized as investment cost and
operational cost, such as cost for electricity and chemical provision,
cost for sludge compost and disposal and labour costs (Table 3), and
identify sc. 2B as the option with the highest cost per FU (5.75 V).
From an economic perspective, the inclusion of SCENA in the
side stream (sc. 1) reduces costs by about 3% per FU, which can be
further reduced (up to 5%) by sc. 2A thanks to a further reduction in
electricity consumption for aeration and chemical consumption for
chemical P removal. Conversely, sc. 2B and 2C increase the total cost
due to higher PAC dosing. The investment cost for each scenario
does not make a big difference, apart for sc. 2C that is slighter
higher, and it is almost negligible in comparison with the opera-
tional cost (between 1% and 3% of the total cost). Regarding the cost
distribution, this is uniform among the different scenarios under
study. On average, 48% of the total cost is associated with energy
consumption, 27% with sludge management, 16% with labour and
the remaining 7% with chemicals provision. The further reduction
of energy use for aeration in sc. 2B and 2C is compensated for by the
additional cost of sludge management, which makes these sce-
narios not recommendable unless energy recovery from the
increased biogas production is guaranteed (which is not done at the
moment). Moreover, for sc. 2B the additional chemical use of the
CAPS conﬁguration has the double drawback of increasing cost for
chemical consumption as well as contributing to an increase in the
amount of chemical sludge to be disposed of, and as consequence
also the cost for its management.
4. Discussion
4.1. Energy efﬁciency improvements
One of the aims of upgrading the sludge line with side-stream
treatment is to improve the energy efﬁciency of the WWTP. The
measurements show lower electricity consumption at a process
level, due to a lower need for aeration in the scSBR compared to the
main biological reactor. The reduction of electricity consumption
per kg N removed for sc. 1 was 57%, which led on a system level to a
reduction of the total consumption of about 12%. The fact that about
half of the total costs are associated with energy use makes once
more evident the importance of energy efﬁciency both from an
economic and an environmental point of view.
Although the integration of SCENA in the side stream has proved
to be a good example of energy efﬁciency improvement, energy
self-sufﬁciency is still a challenge at the Carbonera WWTP. Fully
energy self-sufﬁcient WWTPs are possible through improving the
energy efﬁciency as well as harvesting energy from the biogas (Gu
et al., 2017). Electrical energy autarchy is already reported to have
been achieved for the Strass WWTP (Schaubroeck et al., 2015),
where the largest electricity saving was provided through the
addition of a co-substrate to the anaerobic digester (e.g., kitchen
waste and fat). At the Carbonera WWTP the biogas produced is
used only for heating the digester, while the excess is burnt in a
torch. However, in light of the fact that after the implementation of
SCENA about 10% of the mixed sludge is sent to the fermenter of the
SCENA system for the VFAs production, the anaerobic digester in
now under exploited. Therefore, the addition of a co-substrate in
the digester would imply a better usage of the plant's infrastruc-
ture, which could help the Carbonera WWTP moving forward to-
wards energy self-sufﬁciency, as well as mitigating the negative
environmental impacts due to energy use. Finally, it should be
noted that when implementing SCENA within a WWTP that uses
biogas for electricity production, a reduction of the energy pro-
duced should be considered as it might partially offset the energy
savings due to SCENA. In this case, when evaluating the WWTP
upgrading, the methane yield of the post-fermented sludge should
be measured to evaluate the possibility to recycle the residual
sludge from the SBFR to the digester, thus limiting the reduction in
biogas production.
4.2. Environmental performance improvements
The eutrophication potential impact can be decreased imme-
diately by implementing more sophisticated technologies to
enhance the nutrient removal (however, generally associated with
increase in other environmental impacts) (Zang et al., 2015). For
example, Rodriguez-Garcia et al. (2014) found a substantial
reduction of eutrophication associated with side-stream treat-
ments. However, they stated that to reduce signiﬁcantly the
nutrient load of the efﬂuent, side-stream N-removal technologies
must be followed by struvite crystallization for P removal, at the
cost of increasing electricity and chemical use, which implies
higher costs and GWP impacts. In this sense SCENA represents a

convenient alternative as it combines N and P biological removal in
a single system, which can optimize use of electricity and chem-
icals. Also Hauck et al. (2016) reported a 16% reduction of eutro-
phication after the implementation of a two-step anammox in the
side stream. This is in line with our ﬁndings for marine eutrophi-
cation (22e39% depending of the scenario).
In terms of GWP, other elements contribute besides electricity
consumption and there is a certain consensus in the literature on
the importance of N2O emissions, which can be more important
than electricity use (Schaubroeck et al., 2015; Hauck et al., 2016). At
the Carbonera WWTP, shifting N removal from the main line to the
side stream has the advantage of reducing the N2O emissions in the
main line as result of more favourable process conditions in the
biological reactor of the mainline, i.e., a higher degree of N removal
and more stable conditions (Parravicini et al., 2016).
The total heavy metals load of untreated wastewater ends up in
the sludge or remains in the treated efﬂuent, as consequence no
metal biodegradation is achieved during the wastewater treatment.
The Carbonera WWTP is a signiﬁcant source of heavy metals to
both aquatic and terrestrial recipients. The impact of terrestrial
ecotoxicity was found to exceed the beneﬁt of avoided fertilizers.
The latter was already acknowledged in the literature (Schaubroeck
et al., 2015).
Finally, it can be seen that the impact categories of ozone
depletion, photochemical oxidant formation, particular matter
formation, and terrestrial acidiﬁcation are mostly attributed to the
consumption of electricity and chemicals, and thus the results
reveal an improvement in the environmental performance of the
new layout compared to the old conﬁguration.
4.3. Phosphorus considerations on land application of biosolids
Selecting a P replacement ratio is challenging and especially
important in this study where the additional biological P removal
by SCENA is expected to reduce the intensity of the chemical P
removal. The prediction of the P fertilizer value from sludge char-
acteristics is possible through modelling. For example, Falk Øgaard
and Brod (2016) developed a multiple linear regression model to
predict the P fertilizer value using Fe and Al content in activated
sludge as predictors and reported a signiﬁcant negative effect of
both Al and Fe on the fertilization effect. The results of this study
contribute considerably to the understanding of P availability in
biosolids. However, we were not able to check for the model pre-
diction accuracy due to data unavailability.
The results of the P replacement ratio estimation are reported in
Table 2. Apart from sc. 2B, these values are in line with the values
commonly used in the literature (50e70%). Sludge containing high
levels of P does not necessarily imply a greater potential for P
supply. In sc. 2B, the dosage of PAC used for the increasing of the
organic matter recovery has the additional effect of increasing the
total P removal from 54% to 73% (compared to sc.1). Much more P is
recovered in this scenario, even if the majority is in the form of
aluminium phosphate, and hence not available for plant uptake.
Furthermore, as a result of the higher organic matter removal
(through the CAPS system) the growth activities of activated sludge
are also reduced (and as a consequence the P removal) in com-
parison to the other scenarios. For these reasons, the P availability
of biosolids in sc. 2B is only 9.5%. These ﬁndings are in line with Falk
Øgaard and Brod (2016) who demonstrated that the concentration
of the precipitation salts in sludge are inversely correlated with the
P fertilization effect of sludge. Thus, considering that an economi-
cally feasible process for recovering P from metal salt compounds
does not yet exist (Wilfert et al., 2015), these results suggest that in
the perspective of P recovery from wastewater, biological P removal
should be preferred to a chemical process if the destiny of the
sludge
produced
during
treatment
is
land
application
to
agriculture.
5. Conclusions
Updates to process improvements of existing WWTP, i.e., inte-
gration of SCENA as side-stream treatment, imply changes in the
plant operating conditions that affect efﬂuent and sludge quality,
electricity and chemical use, GHG emissions and total cost. Hence,
this study emphasizes the need to evaluate all of these elements by
means of a holistic approach based on environmental and eco-
nomic indicators. The main ﬁndings of the study are:
 SCENA was identiﬁed as a more sustainable option for providing
a greater degree of environmental protection at lower cost. In
particular, SCENA induced signiﬁcant improvement in terms of
(i) reduction of GHG emissions, mainly due to increased energy
efﬁciency, (ii) reduction of eutrophication, due to a higher level
of N abatement and (iii) reduction of total cost, due to the lower
amounts of electricity and chemicals consumed.
 Moreover, the performance of the Carbonera WWTP can be
further improved if the operation of the whole plant is opti-
mized, i.e., substituting gravitational with mechanical thick-
ening of the sludge (sc. 2B). Although a signiﬁcant reduction of
freshwater eutrophication as well as further energy savings
could be obtained changing the operational strategy to the
chemically enhanced primary treatment, due to the higher
consumption of chemicals the operational cost will increase by
5%.
 The uncertainty analysis proved the validity of the LCA results
for GWP and impact categories related to the consumption of
fossil-based electricity and chemicals, while robust conclusions
could not be drawn on freshwater eutrophication and toxicity-
related impact categories.
 From a methodological point of view, the suggested method for
the estimation of the P availability of biosolids, in contrast to the
classical approach consisting in the application of a ﬁxed P
replacement ratio, provided a better picture of the real situation
and revealed that improving P recovery by chemical precipita-
tion does not give rise to an environmental beneﬁt by replace-
ment of synthetic fertilizer.
 Although results from different LCA studies depend on the as-
sumptions, this case study conﬁrms the dominant role of N2O
emissions on the GWP and emphasizes once again that more
consideration should be paid to N2O emissions at WWTPs.
The conclusions presented here have partially motivated the
Horizon 2020 ‘SMART-Plant’ action,4 which will allow for the
optimization of the best scenario for SCENA integration in the
Carbonera WWTP.
