records.full_text:
Life cycle comparison of centralized wastewater
treatment and urine source separation with
struvite precipitation: Focus on urine nutrient
management
Stephanie K.L. Ishii*, Treavor H. Boyer
Department of Environmental Engineering Sciences, Engineering School of Sustainable Infrastructure and
Environment, University of Florida, PO Box 116450, Gainesville, FL 32611-6450, USA
a r t i c l e i n f o
Article history:
Received 21 November 2014
Received in revised form
9 April 2015
Accepted 10 April 2015
Available online 22 April 2015
Keywords:
LCA
Wastewater treatment
Nutrient recovery
Phosphorus
Nitrogen
Water conservation
a b s t r a c t
Alternative approaches to wastewater management including urine source separation
have the potential to simultaneously improve multiple aspects of wastewater treatment,
including reduced use of potable water for waste conveyance and improved contaminant
removal, especially nutrients. In order to pursue such radical changes, system-level eval-
uations of urine source separation in community contexts are required. The focus of this
life cycle assessment (LCA) is managing nutrients from urine produced in a residential
setting with urine source separation and struvite precipitation, as compared with a
centralized wastewater treatment approach. The life cycle impacts evaluated in this study
pertain to construction of the urine source separation system and operation of drinking
water treatment, decentralized urine treatment, and centralized wastewater treatment.
System boundaries include fertilizer offsets resulting from the production of urine based
struvite fertilizer. As calculated by the Tool for the Reduction and Assessment of Chemical
and Other Environmental Impacts (TRACI), urine source separation with MgO addition for
subsequent struvite precipitation with high P recovery (Scenario B) has the smallest
environmental cost relative to existing centralized wastewater treatment (Scenario A) and
urine source separation with MgO and Na3PO4 addition for subsequent struvite precipita-
tion with concurrent high P and N recovery (Scenario C). Preliminary economic evaluations
show that the three urine management scenarios are relatively equal on a monetary basis
(<13% difference). The impacts of each urine management scenario are most sensitive to
the assumed urine composition, the selected urine storage time, and the assumed elec-
tricity required to treat inﬂuent urine and toilet water used to convey urine at the
centralized wastewater treatment plant. The importance of full nutrient recovery from
urine in combination with the substantial chemical inputs required for N recovery via
struvite precipitation indicate the need for alternative methods of N recovery.
© 2015 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.
* Corresponding author. Tel.: þ1 407 256 4515.
E-mail addresses: ishii@uﬂ.edu (S.K.L. Ishii), thboyer@uﬂ.edu (T.H. Boyer).
Available online at www.sciencedirect.com
ScienceDirect
journal homepage: www.elsevier.com/locate/watres
w a t e r r e s e a r c h 7 9 ( 2 0 1 5 ) 8 8 e1 0 3
http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.watres.2015.04.010
0043-1354/© 2015 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.

1.
Introduction
Wastewater systems employing urine source separation de-
mand attention as an alternative to conventional wastewater
treatment (Larsen et al., 2009; Etter et al., 2011; O'Neal and
Boyer, 2013). The potential beneﬁts of urine source separa-
tion, in which urine is collected and treated as a separate
waste stream, stem from the largely disproportionate impacts
of urine on the treatment requirements of combined waste-
water. Typically, less than 1% of the inﬂuent volumetric ﬂow
at a wastewater treatment plant is attributable to urine, yet
urine contributes greater than 50% of the phosphorus (P) and
80% of the nitrogen (N) mass load to municipal wastewater
(Wilsenach and van Loosdrecht, 2006).
Conventional wastewater treatment plants are designed
and perform well with regard to the removal of chemical ox-
ygen demand (COD), but nutrient removal efﬁciencies are
extremely variable with wastewater treatment plants often
serving as signiﬁcant point sources of nutrient contamination
(Carpenter et al., 1998; Larsen et al., 2007). The dispropor-
tionate contributions of urine to combined wastewater indi-
cate that efforts to remove nutrients are being applied to
larger than necessary volumes of water, thus resulting in
increased energy and resource consumption and reduced
effectiveness. Urine partitioning contaminants of emerging
concern that are minimally affected by conventional waste-
water treatment (Oulton et al., 2010; Salgado et al., 2012) could
also be targeted at higher concentrations in urine, e.g., phar-
maceuticals that are primarily excreted via urine (Combalbert
and Hernandez-Raquet, 2010) could be targeted for removal
prior to mixing with other waste streams, such as through
electrodialysis with ozonation (Lamichhane and Babcock,
2012) or ion exchange treatment (Landry and Boyer, 2013).
Additionally, wastewater treatment must cease to be viewed
as the handling of unwanted residuals alone and instead be
treated as the opportunity to recover valuable, depletable re-
sources, such as water, nutrients, and energy (Guest et al.,
2009).
Current
wastewater
collection
makes
efﬁcient
resource recovery a challenge, as resources, e.g., nutrients, are
diluted and contaminated by the mixing of waste streams.
Urine source separation presents the opportunity to recover N
and P from a low ﬂow, nutrient rich solution, as well as to
simplify centralized treatment of the remaining nutrient
depleted wastewater (Wilsenach and van Loosdrecht, 2006).
Although the
drawbacks of
conventional
wastewater
treatment and the potential beneﬁts of urine source separa-
tion are easily enumerated, implementation of urine source
separation requires radical change to existing infrastructure.
Such action demands a quantitative understanding of the
expected environmental and economic impacts incurred
throughout the life cycle of urine source separation as
compared with centralized wastewater treatment. Accord-
ingly, several researchers have conducted system level eval-
uations of urine source separation in a community context,
such as those summarized in Table 1.
The studies summarized in Table 1 provide insight into
how urine source separation compares with conventional
wastewater treatment. However, the work is limited to hy-
pothetical communities, i.e., “NA” in Table 1, or communities
in Sweden, Germany, and Luxembourg. Additionally, the
studies are lacking in several of the factors required for real-
izing the maximum life cycle environmental and economic
beneﬁts of urine separating systems. Most notable is the lack
of “urine volume reduction” in existing assessments, indi-
cating that no processes were included to further concentrate
nutrients in urine prior to their use as fertilizer. Examples of
these processes include evaporation or distillation to reduce
the water content of urine (Udert and W€achter, 2012), pre-
cipitation of nutrient-rich products from urine (Wilsenach
et al., 2007), and air stripping of ammonia from urine into
sulfuric acid (Basakcilardan-Kabakci et al., 2007a). Life cycle
assessments (LCAs) that include “urine volume reduction” are
needed, as the social barriers, environmental costs, and eco-
nomic costs associated with the transport of liquid urine are
typically prohibitive (Berndtsson, 2006; Lienert et al., 2003).
Furthermore, struvite precipitation is the most thoroughly
researched method of nutrient recovery from source sepa-
rated urine with studies spanning the overall effectiveness of
the method (Etter et al., 2011; Wilsenach et al., 2007; Liu et al.,
2008a, 2008b; Antonini et al., 2011) and the quality of the end-
product as a fertilizer (Ronteltap et al., 2007; Decrey et al., 2011;
Winker et al., 2009); however, struvite precipitation is largely
absent from LCAs of urine source separation.
The purpose of this study is to quantify the life cycle
environmental and economic impacts of managing N and P in
urine through urine source separation and struvite precipita-
tion, as compared with centralized wastewater treatment.
This study is unique due to the selected community focus and
the inclusion of all factors shown in Table 1. The results of this
study are expected to highlight the potential beneﬁts and
costs of urine source separation in a university community
with respect to potable water consumption, infrastructure,
nutrient recovery, fertilizer offsets, and centralized waste-
water treatment, thus also identifying major areas of the
system requiring a deeper investigation.
2.
Life cycle model
2.1.
Scope of the study
The University of Florida, located in Gainesville, FL, USA, was
selected as an appropriate community for urine source sepa-
ration because of its small geographical footprint, high pop-
ulation density, and wastewater treatment facility solely
dedicated to the campus community. University communities
offer a unique opportunity to evaluate alternative wastewater
regimens because wastewater production, treatment, and
potential nutrient reuse are all within close proximity. The
university is also a notable stakeholder, as it serves as the
ratepayer for water and wastewater, the manager of the
wastewater treatment system, and the purchaser for fertilizer
use on campus. It should be noted, however, that although
university communities are unique with regard to the allo-
cation of impacts to stakeholders, the total quantiﬁcation of
impacts related to urine source separation could well be
transferrable to other communities.
This comparative life cycle assessment focuses on the
environmental and economic impacts of processes required
w a t e r r e s e a r c h 7 9 ( 2 0 1 5 ) 8 8 e1 0 3
89

for the handling of urine produced in residence halls on
campus (Fig. 1). Life cycle environmental impacts were
quantiﬁed using the framework and standards outlined in ISO
14040 (2006), with the exception of the “Reporting and critical
review of the LCA” phase involving a self-designated panel of
experts. This change-oriented life cycle comparison includes
three urine management scenarios for the University of Flor-
ida, in which the existing conventional system and potential
retroﬁts are evaluated. Decommission of the conventional
and urine diverting systems was not taken into consideration.

comparison of three urine management scenarios for urine produced in residence halls at the University of Florida; solid
black arrows pertain to Scenario A, dashed black arrows pertain to Scenario B, and dashed gray arrows pertain to Scenario
C; the “Chemical N and P fertilizer production” box represents the synthetic fertilizer offsets enabled by the production of
urine-based struvite fertilizer in Scenarios B and C.
w a t e r r e s e a r c h 7 9 ( 2 0 1 5 ) 8 8 e1 0 3
90

The functional unit for this study is the conveyance, storage,
and nutrient management of 1920 m3 of urine, which is
equivalent to the expected production of urine in residence
halls at the University of Florida during one year. The func-
tional unit was calculated based on residence hall populations
throughout the year, e.g., 8823 maximum residents, 100% ca-
pacity during fall and spring semesters, 33% capacity during
the summer semester, and typical urination behavior from
the literature, e.g., 9.44  105 m3 of urine produced per female
resident per waking hour and 9.62  105 m3 of urine produced
per male resident per waking hour. Detailed calculations are
provided in the Supplementary data document (Table S1 and
preceding text). The functional unit in this study was based
on the annual production of urine to better communicate the
role of equipment lifetimes (multiple years) and storage times
(a fraction of a year) relative to the functional unit.
Scenario A serves as the baseline scenario, in which all
toilets in residence halls are replaced with new conventional
ﬁxtures for subsequent delivery of combined wastewater to a
centralized wastewater treatment facility. Considering the
treatment processes and biosolids disposal methods used at
the centralized wastewater treatment plant on which this
work is based, nutrients are assumed to be partially removed
by biological means and no nutrients are recovered for reuse.
It should be noted that land application of biosolids and other
technologies could be added to the treatment scheme to
enable subsequent nutrient recovery. In Scenarios B and C, all
residence hall toilets are replaced with urine diverting toilets,
e.g., the WC-Dubbletten model. Urine and toilet water used to
convey urine (hereafter referred to as urine ﬂush water) are
collected as one waste stream for storage and treatment
(struvite precipitation) at each residence hall, ultimately
resulting in the production of urine based struvite fertilizer.
Scenarios B and C differ with regard to struvite precipitation
procedures and the extent to which nutrients are recovered
from urine. In Scenario B, magnesium oxide (MgO) is added for
maximum P recovery as struvite, while Scenario C involves
the addition of MgO and sodium phosphate (Na3PO4) for
maximum P and N recovery as struvite. In Scenarios B and C,
treated urine and urine ﬂush water are sent to the centralized
wastewater treatment plant from Scenario A. The system
boundary includes processes related to potable water con-
sumption, urine treatment, fertilizer production, and com-
bined wastewater composition. The treatment requirements
of mixed wastewater that do not directly pertain to nutrients
in urine, e.g., COD removal, are not quantiﬁed in this study.
Furthermore, the system boundaries do not include processes
at the centralized wastewater treatment plant that follow
nutrient removal from wastewater, e.g., biosolids disposal
and/or reuse, nor processes that follow the central storage of
struvite, e.g., redistribution of struvite to agriculture.
The LCA model for all scenarios was built in spreadsheet
format and then put into SimaPro 8.0.3.14 software (PRe
Consultants, 2014). In SimaPro, processes were constructed
using “input parameters” and “calculated parameters” to
beneﬁt subsequent sensitivity analyses. The life cycle inventory
data for chemicals, urine storage tanks, urine pipes, synthetic
fertilizers, electricity, and emissions were obtained from two
databases, including Ecoinvent unit processes (version 2.2) and
the U.S. Life Cycle Inventory Database (USLCI) (Ecoinvent
Centre, 2007; NREL, 2012). The TRACI (Tool for the Reduction
and Assessment of Chemical and other environmental Im-
pacts) impact assessment method was used to evaluate the
contributions of urine management to multiple impact cate-
gories. This impact assessment was selected because this study
pertains to water and wastewater treatment in a U.S. com-
munity and TRACI was developed by the U.S. Environmental
Protection Agency, the federal agency governing water and
wastewater treatment in the U.S. (U.S. EPA, 2014).
2.2.
Model inputs
A brief explanation of how SimaPro process inputs were
determined is provided below (Table 2) and details are pro-
vided in the Supplementary data. The Supplementary data
Excel spreadsheet lists all SimaPro “input parameters” and
“calculated parameters” used in this study. The model inputs
described below are divided into four categories, all of which
pertain to the management of urine produced in residence
halls at the University of Florida over the course of a year:
ﬂush water, urine source separation infrastructure, struvite
precipitation, and centralized wastewater treatment. The
economic analysis presented here is a preliminary investiga-
tion including costs/revenue associated with toilets, urine
pipes, urine storage tanks, ﬂush water, chemical inputs for
urine precipitation, struvite production, and electricity use at
wastewater treatment plant. Costs are either given on an
annual basis, e.g., ﬂush water costs per year, and thus already
appropriate for the functional unit or they are normalized to
the functional unit by dividing the total initial cost by the
expected lifetime of the investment, e.g., initial toilet cost
divided by 25 years.
2.2.1.
Flush water
Potable water use as urine ﬂush water was estimated for each
scenario using the ﬂush water speciﬁcations for conventional
and urine diverting toilets, residence hall populations, and
typical urination behavior (Table S1, Supplementary data
document). The substantial difference in the ﬂush water re-
quirements of urine diverting toilets (0.05 L per small ﬂush)
and conventional toilets (6 L per ﬂush) should be noted.
Included in the use of potable water as urine ﬂush water is not
only the water resource itself, but also the upstream impacts
required for its production. Inputs at the drinking water
treatment plant that serves the University of Florida (opera-
tion phase) were determined using normally distributed daily
operational data (chemical inputs, inﬂuent and efﬂuent ﬂows)
and
monthly
electricity
use
data
from
November
2011eOctober 2012, as well as typical chemical transport and
residual disposal data for the facility. The drinking water
treatment train includes groundwater withdrawal, primary
chlorination,
lime
softening,
recarbonation,
ﬂuoridation,
ﬁltration, and secondary chlorination. The unit cost of potable
water is assumed to be $0.917 per cubic meter (UF PPD, 2015).

separate piping in each residence hall. The material and

93

formation processes required for urine diverting toilets, pipes,
and storage tanks are normalized to the functional unit based
on expected equipment lifetimes. The environmental impacts
of manufacturing and installing conventional and urine
diverting toilets are assumed to be equal, thus negating the
contributions of either ﬁxture type to the environmental as-
pects of this life cycle comparison. For the economic com-
parison, conventional toilets are assumed to cost $200 per
toilet (personal communication with University of Florida
Physical Plant Department) and urine diverting toilets are
assumed to be $700 per toilet (personal communication with
U.S. distributor of Dubbletten toilets). Pipe requirements per
urine diverting toilet are based on the requirements for a
model apartment in Remy (2010) and pipe speciﬁcations were
provided by HD Supply Water works in Ocala, FL, USA,
including material type, weight per pipe length, and cost per
pipe length (Supplementary data, Table S2).
Each residence hall requires two storage tanks. One tank
collects new urine and urine ﬂush water while the other stores
previously collected urine and urine ﬂush water for the
selected storage time. The purpose of storage is two-fold: 1) to
establish favorable conditions for struvite (MgNH4PO4$6H2O)
precipitation and 2) to inactivate potential pathogens origi-
nating from urine and/or fecal contamination. Conditions fa-
voring struvite precipitation and disinfection are established
by urease induced hydrolysis of urea to ammonia/ammonium
and bicarbonate, thus resulting in a pH increase, NH3 for
pathogen inactivation, and NH4
þ for struvite formation. The
duration of the storage period is determined by the kinetics of
pathogen inactivation, as the time required for favorable
precipitation conditions is only on the order of days (Udert
et al., 2003a, 2003b, 2003c). This study assumes a 60 day stor-
age time considering storage conditions in Gainesville, FL,
USA and previous pathogen inactivation studies (Nordin et al.,
2009; Vinneras et al., 2008); however, sensitivity to the selected
storage time is discussed. The required capacity of each urine
storage tank is the maximum expected production of urine
and urine ﬂush water per storage period. The cost and mate-
rial inputs for urine storage tanks per functional unit were
estimated using an average of the linear regressions show in
Fig. S1 and an expected storage tank lifetime of 40 years
(Supplementary data document).
2.2.3.
Struvite precipitation
Following storage, MgO (Scenario B) or MgO and Na3PO4
(Scenario C) are added to stored urine and urine ﬂush water to
maximize
struvite
(MgNH4PO4$6H2O)
precipitation.
The
composition of fresh, undiluted urine, as well as stored urine
is needed to determine the appropriate chemical dosing pro-
cedures
and
expected
nutrient
recovery
(Table
S3,
Supplementary data document). The composition of undi-
luted, fresh urine is based on the median of 5e13 concentra-
tions of each constituent from previous studies (Etter et al.,
2011; Udert et al., 2003a; Fittschen and Hahn, 1998; Kabdasli
et al., 2006; Basakcilardan-Kabakci et al., 2007b; Tilley et al.,
2008; Baykal et al., 2009; Beler-Baykal et al., 2011; Kocaturk and
Baykal, 2012). The median values shown in Table S3 for fresh,
undiluted urine were closely aligned with the concentrations
reported by Kabdasli et al. (2006) for fresh urine composite
samples collected from 20 individuals (2050 years old,
equally distributed between genders). The wide range of fresh
urine nutrient concentrations reported by researchers is
largely due to nutrient concentrations being a function of diet
and the time between excretion and analysis. The composi-
tion of stored urine solution is based on the original undiluted,
fresh urine composition, urine separation efﬁciency, dilution
with urine ﬂush water, and spontaneous precipitation of
struvite and hydroxyapatite in the collection system. It is
assumed that all magnesium and calcium are lost in the
collection system due to spontaneous precipitation of struvite
and hydroxyapatite, respectively, thus also reducing N and P
in the urine storage tank (Udert et al., 2003b). Spontaneous
precipitation is assumed to take place in collection pipes
instead of storage tanks due the rate at which urine undergoes
hydrolysis. This precipitation represents both a nutrient loss
and a potential cause of system malfunction. A logical
expansion of this LCA model is the inclusion of maintenance
required to prevent or remove precipitates in pipes.
The molar ratio of Mg:PO4:NH4 in pure struvite is 1:1:1. In
Scenario B, stored urine solution is dosed with enough MgO to
achieve a molar Mg:PO4 ratio of 1.2:1 in an effort to achieve
maximum P recovery as struvite (Liu et al., 2008b). In Scenario
C, MgO additions are increased and Na3PO4 is also added to
achieve a ﬁnal molar Mg:PO4:NH4 ratio of 1.2:1:1 in urine
storage tanks, thus enabling maximum P and N recovery as
struvite (Liu et al., 2008b).
Table S4 (Supplementary data document) provides the
overall struvite precipitation procedures and costs for Sce-
narios B and C. Drying processes to achieve minimal struvite
water content are not included in this study. Instead, the ex-
pected water content (30%, Decrey et al., 2011) and resulting
weight of wet struvite are accounted for in the collection and
transport of struvite from each residence hall to a central
location on campus at the end of every storage period. Once
centrally collected, the value of struvite as a fertilizer is
assumed to be $0.57/kg dry weight, which is slightly less than
the $0.62/kg value determined by Liu et al. (2008b). The stru-
vite price in this study was determined using a regression
model to disaggregate the total cost of common fertilizers into
the costs of contributing nutrients (Supplementary data,
Fig. S2; Tilley et al., 2009). The regression model was based
on the 2013 average U.S. farm prices of selected fertilizers
provided by the U.S. Department of Agriculture and assumes a
market for urine based fertilizers (USDA, 2013). The struvite
produced from urine contributes to the environmental bene-
ﬁts of Scenarios B and C because it is considered a replace-
ment for conventional fertilizers, thus making conventional
fertilizer an “avoided product” in SimaPro (ISO 14040, 2006).
“Monoammonium phosphate, as P2O5, at regional storehouse/
RER U” and “monoammonium phosphate, as N, at regional
storehouse/RER U” (NH4H2PO4) were used to represent the
avoided conventional fertilizer because it is a commonly used
fertilizer product containing both N and P, similar to struvite.
2.2.4.
Centralized wastewater treatment
In order to compare Scenario A with Scenarios B and C, one
must estimate the impacts of urine from residence halls on
centralized wastewater treatment of combined wastewater,
e.g., electricity use and nutrient discharges. For all scenarios,
the inﬂuent ﬂow at the centralized wastewater treatment plant
w a t e r r e s e a r c h 7 9 ( 2 0 1 5 ) 8 8 e1 0 3
94

attributed to urine from residence halls per functional unit is
the total volume of urine (1920 m3) and the associated urine
ﬂush water. For Scenario A, the mass loads of inﬂuent urine
nutrients are based on the composition of fresh urine. For
Scenarios B and C, inﬂuent nutrient mass loads from urine are
affected by prior struvite precipitation. For all scenarios, it is
assumed that 84.3% and 49.8% of inﬂuent N and P mass loads
are removed during centralized treatment through biological
means with no nutrient recovery. This assumption is based on
total N and P removal rates at the centralized wastewater
treatment plant at the University of Florida (FebruaryeSep-
tember 2013, 20 inﬂuent 24-h composite samples, 7 efﬂuent 24-
h composite samples). N and P from residence hall urine that
remain in treated wastewater efﬂuent are assumed to be
released to water (“emissions to water” in SimaPro), the extent
of which varies by scenario due to the differing inﬂuent
nutrient loads determined for each scenario. The electricity
requirements for urine treatment at the centralized waste-
water treatment plant are based on the inﬂuent volumetric
ﬂow of urine and urine ﬂush water combined with the ﬂow
normalized electricity use at the plant, e.g., kWh/m3. Based on
monthly electricity use data from February 2011 to December
2012, the wastewater treatment plant at the University of
Florida uses 1.336 kWh of electricity per m3 of inﬂuent waste-
water. It should be noted that this facility has a treatment ca-
pacity of 3 million gallons per day and that larger facilities are
expected to require less electricity per unit ﬂow due to econo-
mies of scale. The unit cost of electricity is assumed to be
$0.10 per kWh (UF PPD, 2015). An alternative approach to esti-
mating electricity requirements based on inﬂuent nutrient
loads in used in the sensitivity analyses. Direct air emissions
from centralized wastewater treatment processes, as well as
from urine storage and struvite precipitation, e.g., CO2, N2O,
NH3, are not included in this study, but would serve as a logical
addition to this life cycle inventory in a follow-up study.
2.3.
Uncertainty and sensitivity analyses
The uncertainty of overall impact assessment results for each
urine management scenario was evaluated using the inte-
grated Monte Carlo module in SimaPro. For each Monte Carlo
analysis, 3000 iterations were conducted. These uncertainty
analyses estimate the effects of variability in Ecoinvent data-
base inputs on impact assessment results, as Ecoinvent
database inputs assume a lognormal distribution with a
deﬁned standard deviation. Although USLCI database items
are also used in this LCA (see Table 2), uncertainty analyses do
not include variability resulting from these inputs, as USLCI
database inputs have an undeﬁned distribution.
Sensitivity analyses were conducted to evaluate the inﬂu-
ence of assumed model inputs on resulting environmental
impacts for each urine management scenario. Eight assumed
input parameters were selected for sensitivity analyses: Ni-
trogen content of urine, phosphorus content of urine, mag-
nesium content of urine, calcium content of urine, urine
storage time, electricity to produce urine ﬂush water, elec-
tricity use at the wastewater treatment plant, and user
compliance with urine diverting toilets. These parameters
were chosen based on contribution analyses and known
variation in the assumed value (Table S5, Supplementary data
document). For example, the literature presents a wide range
of N, P, calcium, and magnesium concentrations in fresh
urine. Sensitivity analyses evaluate the inﬂuence of selecting
the minimum, median, or maximum concentrations found in
literature, as these assumptions affect the estimated loss of
nutrients to spontaneous precipitation in the urine collection
system, struvite precipitation procedures, and wastewater
treatment plant nutrient processes. Table S5 lists all input
parameters considered in the sensitivity analyses, the asso-
ciated range of variation for each selected parameter, and
justiﬁcation for this range.
3.
Results and discussion
3.1.
Impact assessment and cost results
3.1.1.
Overall comparison
Table 3 provides a summary of the estimated economic im-
pacts associated with the infrastructure materials, ﬂush
water, struvite precipitation chemicals, centralized waste-
water treatment electricity, and urine-based fertilizer sales for
each urine management scenario. It is important to note that
capital costs are normalized based on expected lifetime
(years), as the functional unit pertains to urine production
over the course of a year. The total economic cost of each
scenario was estimated to decrease in the order of Scenario
B > Scenario A > Scenario C, with ~13% difference between
Scenarios B and C. Overall, the costs of infrastructure mate-
rials (toilets, pipes, storage tanks) and chemicals in Scenarios
B and C are compensated for with potable water savings,
reduced wastewater treatment costs, and struvite revenue.
The reduced wastewater treatment costs presented here are
based solely on reductions in electricity use, thus implying
that more comprehensive estimates of cost reductions at the
centralized wastewater treatment plant would be greater in
magnitude.

Fig. 2 shows the cumulative environmental impact of the
three urine management scenarios across impact categories,
as calculated by TRACI. Results are expressed as a percentage
of the urine management scenario with the maximum envi-
ronmental impact. For all impact categories, Scenario C has
the greatest environmental cost, followed by Scenario A, and
then Scenario B. Uncertainty results for each independent
urine management scenario are represented by the brackets
above and below each bar.
In Fig. 3, the overall environmental impact of each urine
management scenario is broken down into its contributing
parts. Each bar represents 100% of one urine management
scenario's impact within a category with colored segments
pertaining to each process input from Table 2. Positive percent
values indicate environmental costs and negative percent
values indicate environmental beneﬁts. The majority of Sce-
nario A's environmental impact comes from electricity use at
the centralized wastewater treatment plant to treat inﬂuent
ﬂows of urine and urine ﬂush water (light blue) and electricity
use at the drinking water treatment plant to produce ﬂush
water (purple) (Fig. 3a). One caveat, however, is the eutrophi-
cation impact category, which is dominated by the discharge
of treated wastewater efﬂuent containing N and P into the
environment.
For Scenario B, the discharge of treated wastewater
efﬂuent containing urine N and P continues to dominate the
eutrophication impact category, whereas all other impact
categories are now more evenly distributed amongst process
inputs (Fig. 3b). Inputs related to the production of ﬂush water
at the drinking water treatment plant are combined and
represented as “Flush H2O”, contributing much less to the
total environmental impact than in Scenario A. In terms of
environmental costs (positive impact percentage values),
urine storage tank materials (orange) and formation processes
(light green), pipe materials (dark blue) for urine conveyance,
and MgO for struvite precipitation (red) are the major con-
tributors, as well as electricity use at the wastewater treat-
ment plant (light blue). The beneﬁt of recovering urine N and P
for use as a fertilizer, thus offsetting the use of conventional
fertilizers, is shown by the negative impact percentage values.
The beneﬁt of reusing urine nutrients as fertilizer is most
attributable to P.
The large environmental costs associated with Scenario C
are a result of the Na3PO4 inputs, and to a lesser extent MgO
inputs, required for struvite precipitation (Fig. 3c, green and
red bars). Whereas in Scenario B only MgO was required for
maximum P recovery via struvite precipitation, Scenario C
requires MgO and Na3PO4 to capture both P and N as struvite.
This is due to the 1:1:1 Mg:PO4:NH4 molar ratio of struvite, as
compared with the 0:1:30 Mg:PO4:NH4 molar ratio of stored
urine. Although large MgO and Na3PO4 additions to stored
urine enable high recoveries of N and P in Scenario C, the
resulting fertilizer offsets and reduced nutrient discharges in
treated wastewater efﬂuent do not justify the environmental
burden of MgO and Na3PO4 production in this model. This
point is further illustrated by the environmental impacts of
producing 1 mol P as Na3PO4 as compared with the environ-
mental impacts of producing 1 mol P and 1 mol N as part of
NH4H2PO4
using
the
Ecoinvent
unit
processes
database
(Supplementary information, Fig. S3). This comparison was
made because for every 1 mol of P that is added to urine as
Na3PO4, 1 mol of P and 1 mol of N is recovered as struvite,
which can offset NH4H2PO4 fertilizer production. Although the
majority of the P that is added to stored urine is subsequently
recovered, the environmental costs of the input (Na3PO4) are
greater than those that are being offset by the output (struvite
fertilizer that offsets NH4H2PO4 use). These results suggest the
need for further exploration of non-traditional reactants for
precipitation to serve as replacements for high quality Mg and

P sources, e.g., groundwater rich in hardness, lime residuals,
and seawater for Mg (Dai et al., 2014). For example, Sakthivel
et al. (2012) evaluated wood ash as an Mg source for struvite
precipitation in place of expensive Mg salts. Wood ash
enabled 99% ﬁxation of urine phosphate via precipitation;
however, due to the loss of potassium from wood ash to the
urine solution and poor quality struvite, wood ash was
deemed an unsuitable Mg source. Etter et al. (2011) demon-
strated the potential for bittern to serve as an alternative
magnesium source, so long as this waste stream from salt
production was locally available. Furthermore, the need to
focus on non-precipitation methods of N recovery is sup-
ported by Visual MINTEQ simulations that were conducted to
provide further insight into Scenario B and C's precipitation
processes. In Scenario B, MgO additions result in the greatest
degree of oversaturation for struvite, thus implying that
struvite would be the primary component of the end-product.
The large MgO and Na3PO4 additions in Scenario C, however,

3.1.2.
Sensitivity results
The purpose of the sensitivity analysis is to evaluate the in-
ﬂuence of selected assumptions on the resulting environ-
mental impacts for each urine management scenario. Using
parameter sets in SimaPro, each urine management scenario
was analyzed using baseline, minimum, and maximum
values for the eight selected input parameters in Table S5
(Supplementary data document). Fig. 4 shows the results of
the sensitivity analysis for two impact categories, eutrophi-
cation (Fig. 4a) and fossil fuel depletion (Fig. 4b). Similar
graphs for the other eight impact categories are provided in
the Supplementary data document (Fig. S4). The results are
presented such that the x-axis represents the eight selected
input parameters and the bars show the magnitude of impact
deviation from the baseline resulting from minimum values
(gray bars) and maximum input values (black bars). The
baseline impact values for each urine management scenario
are provided below the ﬁgure legends. An alternative inter-
pretation of the sensitivity results is also provided in Table S6
(Supplementary data document). Table S6 shows the percent
change in each urine management scenario's impact within a
category relative to the baseline assumption. Any impact
changes >50% resulting from a minimum or maximum
parameter value are highlighted in red. For the majority of all
minimum and maximum parameter values tested, the order
of total environmental cost remained Scenario C > Scenario
A > Scenario B. The one exception pertained to assuming the
minimum total nitrogen content in urine, which resulted in
~equal total environmental costs for Scenarios A and C in the
ozone depletion, smog, and eutrophication impact categories.
This is due to the fact that assuming less nitrogen in urine
requires less Na3PO4 inputs to source separated urine, thus
reducing the magnitude of the major contributor to Scenario
C's total impacts.
Overall, Figs. 4 and S4 show that Scenario C is especially
sensitive to the assumed N content of urine. This is expected
because the assumed urine N concentration not only affects
nutrient loads and discharges at the centralized wastewater
treatment plant, but also the amount of N available for re-
covery as struvite and the corresponding Na3PO4 dose needed
for precipitation. Considering the signiﬁcance of Na3PO4 use in
Scenario C (Fig. 3c), the additional Na3PO4 required for higher
N concentrations has a substantial effect on Scenario C's
environmental cost across impact categories. The dominance
of Na3PO4 on Scenario C's impact and the fact that the extent
of Na3PO4 use is dictated by the N content of stored urine also
explains why all other inputs considered in the sensitivity
analysis result in minimal percent change for Scenario C's
impact across categories (Table S6, Supplementary data
document).
With regard to Scenario B, the environmental impact
within each category is most affected by the assumed urine
storage time (Table S6, Supplementary data document).
Longer urine storage times help ensure adequate disinfection
of urine with ammonia, but also require larger urine storage
tanks and urine storage tank materials (HDPE) are a major
contributor to the environmental impact of Scenario B
(Fig. 3b). Similarly, electricity use at the centralized waste-
water treatment plant greatly contributes to Scenario A's
environmental impact (Figs. 3a) and Figs. 4 and S4 show that
Scenario A is predominantly inﬂuenced by the assumption for
electricity use at the centralized wastewater treatment plant
to treat inﬂuent urine and urine ﬂush water.
In addition to the eight input parameters in Table S5, the
assumed paradigm for estimating electricity use at the
centralized wastewater treatment plant was evaluated. In all
previously discussed analyses in this study, electricity use at
the centralized wastewater treatment plant is based on the
volumetric ﬂow (quantity) of inﬂuent urine and urine ﬂush
water (baseline value ¼ 1.366 kWh/m3), as determined by
normalizing monthly electricity use to inﬂuent ﬂows. This
means that Scenarios B and C would require the same amount
of electricity use at the wastewater treatment plant because
they both have equal reductions in ﬂush water use relative to
Scenario A. However, the wastewater treatment plant at the
University of Florida practices biological nutrient removal,

98

thus indicating that electricity use is a function of both
inﬂuent ﬂow (quantity) and inﬂuent nutrient loads (quality).
Electricity use at the centralized wastewater treatment
plant could have instead been based on inﬂuent urine N and P
in each urine management scenario. For example, Maurer
et al. (2003) estimated that nitriﬁcation/pre-denitriﬁcation at
a wastewater treatment plant requires 14 MJ/kg N removed
(3.89 kWh/kg N removed) and enhanced biological P elimina-
tion at a wastewater treatment plant requires 5 MJ/kg P
removed (1.39 kWh/kg P removed). Electricity use attributed to
N and P removal can also be estimated with data provided by
Gallego et al. (2008) in a study of two wastewater treatment
plants using the biodenipho process, which is the system
conﬁguration used at the University of Florida wastewater
treatment plant. Considering the electricity, N removal, P
removal, and COD removal reported by Gallego et al. (2008), as
well as the electricity estimates from Maurer et al. (2003) as
initial solutions, the “Solver” tool in Microsoft Excel arrives at
the following electricity allocation averages: 3.94 kWh/kg N
removed and 1.39 kWh/kg P removed. Using these values and
average N and P load reductions at the University of Florida
wastewater treatment plant, an alternative estimate for
urine-related electricity use at the plant can be estimated.
Fig. 5 compares the overall impact assessment results of
each urine management scenario using the original electricity
assumption based on inﬂuent volumetric ﬂow of urine and
urine ﬂush water (bars) and the alternative assumption based
on inﬂuent N and P loads from urine (symbols). The general
order of environmental impact remains Scenario C > Scenario
A > Scenario B for both electricity assumptions. However, the
difference between Scenario B and Scenario A is less marked
when electricity use at the wastewater treatment plan is
based on inﬂuent nutrient loads. This is due to the fact that
Scenario B has a major reduction in ﬂush water relative to
Scenario A, but less dramatic of a reduction in N and P loads.
Struvite precipitation in Scenario B mostly targets urine P with
only minimal recovery of urine N.
This model is limited by the simpliﬁcation of the relation-
ship between urine source separation and centralized waste-
water treatment. In reality, electricity use at the centralized
wastewater treatment plant will be affected by both reduced
inﬂuent volumetric ﬂows and nutrient loads. Furthermore,
assumptions should include the effects of urine source sepa-
ration on biological treatment efﬁciencies, biosolids produc-
tion, maintenance, and direct air emissions from treatment
processes. Shu et al. (2006) found that struvite precipitation of
digester supernatant resulting from the treatment of com-
bined wastewater was proﬁtable mainly due to a reduction in
chemical
requirements,
sludge
handling,
and
P
scaling
throughout the plant, not electricity requirements. This study
serves as a foundation on which to build additional work that
speciﬁcally addresses the relationship between different urine
source separation scenarios, inﬂuent wastewater quality, and
the
resulting
wastewater
treatment
requirements.
For
example, Jimenez et al. (2015) recently explored the hypo-
thetical impacts of varying degrees of urine source separation
on inﬂuent nutrient loads and associated treatment re-
quirements for an existing biological nutrient removal facility.
3.2.
Implications for urine source separation
The results of this LCA reveal that urine source separation
with struvite precipitation for maximum P recovery (Scenario
B) has a lower overall environmental cost than treatment of
urine as part of a combined wastewater (Scenario A) and urine
source separation with struvite precipitation for maximum P
and N recovery (Scenario C). The signiﬁcant differences be-
tween the environmental costs of each scenario and the lack
of signiﬁcant differences between the economic costs of each
scenario indicate that Scenario B is the preferred management
strategy for urine produced in residence halls at the University
of Florida. LCA results also highlight the signiﬁcant roles of
ﬂush water production, urine storage, and urine treatment
inputs in the overall impacts of urine source separation.
Although this work focuses on urine source separation in a
university setting, the signiﬁcance of these factors is pertinent
to urine source separation across communities. The implica-
tions of each of these factors with regard to urine source
separation are discussed below.
3.2.1.
Flush water production
Due to the dual ﬂush mechanism and small urine ﬂush water
volume of urine diverting toilets, Scenarios B and C lend
themselves to decreased potable water consumption. This

treatment plant estimated as a function of inﬂuent volumetric urine and urine ﬂush water ﬂow (vertical bars, 1.366 kWh/m3)
and as a function of urine N and P removal (symbols, 0.996 kWh/kg N, 1.004 kWh/kg P).
w a t e r r e s e a r c h 7 9 ( 2 0 1 5 ) 8 8 e1 0 3
99

plays a major role when comparing Scenarios B and C to
Scenario A, as electricity use at the drinking water treatment
plant for ﬂush water production is a substantial portion of
Scenario A's overall environmental impact. Potable water
savings were also found to be a dominant factor in an eco-
nomic and environmental analysis of alternative wastewater
systems in two buildings at the University of Toledo, OH, USA
(Anand and Apul, 2011). It is important to note that the envi-
ronmental and economic beneﬁts of potable water savings are
expected to be highly dependent on the selected electricity
generation proﬁle, e.g., hydroelectricity vs. coal, due to the
fact that electricity use often dominates the environmental
impact
of
drinking
water
treatment
(Igos
et
al.,
2014;
Sombekke et al., 1997; Racoviceanu et al., 2007). For example,
Bonton et al. (2012) found that a conventional granular acti-
vated carbon water treatment plant had higher environ-
mental impacts than a nanoﬁltration water treatment plant,
whereas Sombekke et al. (1997) concluded that the two
treatment conﬁgurations had similar environmental impacts.
This discrepancy was attributed to the fact that each study
included different electricity proﬁles, with Bonton et al. (2012)
including mostly hydroelectricity thus giving less weight to
the increased electricity required for nanoﬁltration. The
electricity input used in this study, “electricity, medium
voltage, at grid/US U” version 2.2, is based on the U.S. elec-
tricity mix in Ecoinvent, with the majority coming from hard
coal (~50%), nuclear power (~20%), and natural gas (~20%).
Additionally, the beneﬁcial impacts of potable water sav-
ings associated with urine source separation are expected to
be even more signiﬁcant in other communities, as the source
water used by the University of Florida is a high quality
groundwater requiring minimal treatment. The substantial
reduction in potable water consumption enabled by urine
source separation would certainly result in more notable
environmental beneﬁts in communities requiring advanced
water treatment and intensive water demand management.
The beneﬁts of reduced potable water use resulting from urine
source separation could be more thoroughly evaluated by
quantifying the total environmental impacts of producing
ﬂush water at a range of drinking water treatment plants, as
well as through the use of impact assessment methods with
water related impact categories, e.g., IMPACT Worldþ which
considers local water availability and quality.
3.2.2.
Urine storage
Storage is the predominant mode of disinfection considered
for source separated urine. This method beneﬁts from the
naturally occurring urea hydrolysis process that takes place in
stored urine and the biocide properties of the resulting NH3.
However, the storage time required for adequate disinfection
is difﬁcult to determine, as it depends on the pathogen con-
tent (type and concentration), NH3 content, pH, and temper-
ature of the stored urine mixture, which are often highly
uncertain and variable over time (Nordin et al., 2009; Vinneras
et al., 2008; Maurer et al., 2006; H€oglund et al., 2002). As a result
of varying degrees of pathogen sensitivity to NH3 and a dis-
tribution of expected storage conditions, a conservative stor-
age time must be selected. For example, urine solution
composition and storage conditions expected in this study
would require a storage time of only 1e2 days for Salmonella
enterica inactivation, but 50e250 days for Ascaris egg inacti-
vation, based on results seen by Vinneras et al. (2008) and
Nordin et al. (2009). Conservative storage times require large
urine storage tanks, which occupy more land area and also
present a higher potential for human and environmental
concerns in the case of malfunction, e.g., liquid and odor
leaks.
Furthermore, disinfection by storage requires that storage
capacity and protocol consider all non-urine contributions to
storage tanks. If storage capacity is designed based on users
employing one small ﬂush per urination event, then excessive
use of urine ﬂush water would cause accelerated ﬁlling of
urine storage tanks and loss of urine nutrients to the
centralized
wastewater
treatment
plant
upon
overﬂow.
Additionally, one-time inputs have the potential to adversely
affect large volumes of urine that have been collected for long
periods of time. For example, if hazardous chemicals are
periodically added to urine diverting toilets for maintenance
purposes and not properly diverted, they have the potential to
contaminate urine in storage tanks collected long before and
after the chemical input. Accordingly, additional attention
should be dedicated to exploring alternative methods for
ensuring a pathogen-free nutrient end-product from source
separated urine, such as disinfecting the end-product itself
instead of the entire volume of urine (Decrey et al., 2011).
Bischel (2015) found that biological nitriﬁcation of source
separated urine achieved some pathogen inactivation, but
was insufﬁcient as a standalone disinfection process. Direct
air emissions that result in environmental concern, as well as
nutrient loss, e.g., NH3, should be quantiﬁed when comparing
alternative disinfection processes to storage.
3.2.3.
Urine treatment inputs and centralized wastewater
treatment
In Scenario A, 13,200 kg N and 1070 kg P from residence hall
urine are expected to enter the centralized wastewater treat-
ment plant each year (Fig. 6, dashed lines), which relate to
aqueous emissions of 2080 kg N and 539 kg P with treated
efﬂuent. The inﬂuent P load and efﬂuent P emissions in Sce-
nario B are greatly reduced to 272 and 137 kg P, respectively,
while the N load and emissions are only slightly reduced. The
greater reduction in P loads and emissions relative to N is due
to the magnitude of MgO additions during struvite precipita-
tion being based on high P recovery.
In Scenario C, large additions of MgO and Na3PO4 promote
additional struvite precipitation and thus increased recovery
of N and P from urine. Scenario C's inﬂuent N load and efﬂuent
N emissions at the centralized wastewater treatment plant
are expected to be 6190 and 973 kg N, respectively. Regarding
P, however, Scenario C's inﬂuent load and efﬂuent emissions
are expected to be higher than those in Scenarios A and B due
to the use of Na3PO4 for N recovery and imperfect struvite
precipitation. Precipitation efﬁciency in urine storage tanks is
assumed to be 90%, thus indicating that 10% of Na3PO4 addi-
tions end up at the centralized wastewater treatment plant.
Increasing Na3PO4 additions result in a decreasing inﬂuent N
load due to Na3PO4 enabling N recovery as struvite; however,
the same Na3PO4 additions eventually raise the inﬂuent P load
to levels that exceed Scenario A (Fig. 6). There is an inverse
relationship between N recovery via struvite precipitation and
w a t e r r e s e a r c h 7 9 ( 2 0 1 5 ) 8 8 e1 0 3
100

P loads to the centralized wastewater treatment plant, as
increased N recovery requires increased Na3PO4 inputs. These
results highlight the need to consider alternative methods of
N recovery from urine, which is important due to the major
contributions of urine to N in combined wastewater, the re-
sources
required
to
comply
with
increasingly
stringent
efﬂuent N regulations, and the increasing U.S. demand for N
fertilizer as compared with the constant demand for P fertil-
izer (Fig. S5, Supplementary data document).
4.
Conclusions
In this comparative LCA of urine nutrient management, Sce-
nario B (urine source separation with MgO addition to urine
for subsequent struvite precipitation) proved to result in the
smallest environmental burden, as compared with Scenario A
(centralized wastewater treatment with no nutrient recovery)
and Scenario C (urine source separation with MgO and Na3PO4
addition to urine for subsequent struvite precipitation).
 Preliminary economic evaluations show that the three
urine management scenarios are relatively equal on a
monetary basis (<13% difference).
 The environmental impact of Scenario A mostly suffered
from high electricity usage at the centralized wastewater
treatment plant to treat large inﬂuent volumes of urine and
urine ﬂush water, as well as electricity use at the drinking
water treatment plant to produce urine ﬂush water. These
results are a function of the large ﬂush water volume of
conventional toilets relative to urine diverting equivalents.
 Although struvite precipitation methods in Scenario C
enable high recoveries of both P and N as urine based
struvite fertilizer, the upstream impacts of manufacturing
chemicals (Na3PO4, MgO) required for these precipitation
methods are substantial.
 The process of constructing this LCA and the correspond-
ing results highlight the potential for greater water saving
beneﬁts in other communities requiring advanced treat-
ment of water used as urine ﬂush water, as well as the need
to further evaluate methods of N recovery and disinfection
in source separated urine that extend beyond struvite
precipitation and storage.

records.full_text:
Life cycle assessment and techno-economic analysis of nitrogen recovery by
ammonia air-stripping from wastewater treatment
Saurajyoti Kar a,⁎,1, Rajveer Singh a, Patrick L. Gurian a, Adam Hendricks b, Paul Kohl b,
Sean McKelvey b, Sabrina Spatari a,c
a Civil, Architectural & Environmental Engineering, Drexel University, Philadelphia 19104, United States
b Philadelphia Water Department, Philadelphia 19107, United States
c Civil and Environmental Engineering, Technion Israel Institute of Technology, Haifa 32000, Israel
H I G H L I G H T S
G R A P H I C A L
A B S T R A C T
• Environmental impact assessed for NH3
recovery from WWTP for (NH4)2SO4 pro-
duction
• Air-stripping evaluated for varying side-
stream ﬂow rate and NH3 concentrations
• WWTP speciﬁc GHG emissions estimated
to be 0.43 kg CO2e per kg (NH4)2SO4
• Estimated Haber-Bosch GHG emissions
are 6 times higher than if recovered from
WWTP.
• Break-even price estimated at $0.046 is
92 % lower than prevalent farm prices.
A B S T R A C T
A R T I C L E
I N F O
Editor: Jacopo Bacenetti
Keywords:
Ammonia stripping
Nutrient recovery
Wastewater treatment
Life cycle assessment
Techno-economic analysis
Haber Bosch
Wastewater treatment plants (WWTPs) with anaerobic digestion of biosolids produce an ammonia-rich sidestream out
of which nitrogen can be recovered through air stripping. Recovered ammonia can be used to produce ammonium sul-
fate (AS) for agricultural use, enabling the circular return of nitrogen as fertilizer to the food system. We investigate the
cost and life cycle environmental impact of recovering ammonia from the sidestream of WWTPs for conversion to AS
and compare it to AS production from the Haber Bosch process. We perform life cycle assessment (LCA) to investigate
the environmental impact of AS fertilizer production by air-stripping ammonia from WWTP sidestreams at varying
sidestream nitrogen concentrations. Techno-economic analysis (TEA) is performed to assess the break-even selling
price of sidestream AS production at a WWTP in the City of Philadelphia. Greenhouse gas emissions for air-stripping
technology range between 0.2 and 0.5 kg CO2e/kg AS, about six times lower than the hydrocarbon-based Haber-
Bosch process, estimated at 2.5 kg CO2e/kg AS. Further reduction of greenhouse gas emissions is feasible by replacing
fossil-based energy use in air-stripping process (82–98 % of net energy demand) with renewable sources. Also, a sig-
niﬁcant reduction in mineral depletion and improvement in human and ecosystem health are observed for the air-
stripping approach. Furthermore, the break-even selling price for installing sidestream-based AS production at the
Philadelphia's WWTP, considering capital and operating costs, is estimated at $0.046/kg AS (100 %), which is 92 %
lower than the 2014 estimate of AS's average selling price at farms in the United States. We conclude that even with
varying ammonia concentrations and high sidestream volume, air-stripping technology offers an environmentally
and economically favorable option for implementing nitrogen recovery and simultaneous production of AS at WWTPs.
Science of the Total Environment 857 (2023) 159499
⁎ Corresponding author.
E-mail address: saurajyoti.kar@gmail.com (S. Kar).
1 Currently appointed as Postdoctoral Researcher at Energy Systems and Infrastructure Analysis, Argonne National Laboratory, IL, United States.
http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.scitotenv.2022.159499
Received 10 March 2022; Received in revised form 12 October 2022; Accepted 12 October 2022
Available online 17 October 2022
0048-9697/© 2022 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
Contents lists available at ScienceDirect
Science of the Total Environment
journal homepage: www.elsevier.com/locate/scitotenv

1. Introduction
Ammonia is one of the essential forms of inorganic fertilizer that pro-
vides the largest source of nitrogen to the world's expanding agricultural
needs (Frink et al., 1999; Reis et al., 2016). The nitrogen in ammonia can
either be ﬁxed from atmospheric nitrogen or recovered from nitrogen-
rich wastewater that concentrates nitrogen from food waste. Atmospheric
nitrogen can be ﬁxed through nitrogen ﬁxing bacteria those residing at
the roots of crops (e.g., in legumes) or through chemical processes produc-
ing inorganic fertilizer. Municipal and agricultural wastes contain a high
concentration of ammonia, which can be recovered through chemical and
biological processes. The chemical process deﬁned by Haber-Bosch ﬁxes at-
mospheric nitrogen to produce ammonia and is the largest source of syn-
thetic ammonia fertilizer used in agriculture. With continued growth in
agriculture, simultaneous growth of circular nitrogen recovery and use,
and nitrogen ﬁxation through Haber Bosch process is expected.
Ammonia is applied to ﬁelds in more chemically stable forms like am-
monium sulfate (AS) and ammonium nitrate (AN) compound solutions
rather than as ammonia. Reducing the manufacturing cost and environ-
mental impact of producing ammonia-based fertilizers can improve the ag-
ricultural sector's life cycle environmental performance and reduce
production costs at the same time. Ammonia produced through the
Haber-Bosch process consumes hydrogen sourced from natural gas and ni-
trogen from the air (Erisman et al., 2008; Haber, 1918). The process is
energy-intensive and produces greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions, both of
which call for innovative alternative technologies to mitigate those effects
(Jennings, 1991). A large fraction of AS and AN production occurs as co-
products in nylon, nickel mining, and nickel smelting and reﬁning pro-
cesses. The current market share of AS production from Haber Bosch
sourced ammonia is 7.2 % (Wernet et al., 2016). Although the Haber
Bosch based production of AS is a marginal fraction in the market, due to
its high energy demand and signiﬁcant environmental impacts, there is in-
centive to investigate alternatives to Haber-Bosch based ammonia produc-
tion. For example, expanding ammonia production worldwide is tending
towards use of marginal fossil fuels like coal as feedstock for hydrogen pro-
duction, which raises ammonia's energy and greenhouse gas (GHG) emis-
sions intensity (Zhang et al., 2022). Reducing or limiting the use of the
Haber Bosch process to ﬁx atmospheric nitrogen by increasing circular
sources of nitrogen would have the highest marginal reduction on energy
intensity and environmental impacts of AS production.
Municipal wastewater usually contains high ammonia concentrations.
Discharge of ammonia from wastewater treatment plants is a challenging
issue throughout the United States and worldwide as it leads to multiple en-
vironmental impacts, including acidiﬁcation, eutrophication and secondary
air pollution, all of which affect one or both of human and ecosystem health
(Wu et al., 2016; Zhang et al., 2017). At many North American facilities, the
sidestreams from the dewatering of anaerobically digested biosolids, usu-
ally high in ammonia concentration, are recirculated to the headworks of
the WWTPs and contribute to nearly 15–20 % additional ammonia load
(Constantine, 2006). The recirculation of sidestreams increases energy con-
sumption at facilities' intermittent processing steps and increases the BOD/
N ratio, which further requires calibration of treatment parameters at the
intermittent steps to reach desired treatment targets at the facility
(Neethiling et al., 2021). An increase in the BOD/N ratio can reduce the
performance of simultaneous nitrogen and phosphorous removal processes,
increasing the risk of struvite formation, which can lead to increased oper-
ation and maintenance expenditures at the treatment facilities. Biological
and chemical ammonia removal technologies are implemented to help re-
duce ammonia load at wastewater treatment plants (WWTPs). However,
implementing conventional biological denitriﬁcation to remove ammonia
poses a signiﬁcant burden on wastewater treatment. It requires a signiﬁ-
cantly high residence time at anaerobic and aerobic treatment tanks, high
oxygen demand at 4.2 g of O2 per gram NH3-N, and controlled environmen-
tal conditions (e.g., temperature, pH, and alkalinity) (Gujer and Jenkins,
1974). At times, additional electron donors such as methanol may be re-
quired for complete denitriﬁcation, which further increases operational
costs. The high chemical and processing costs of denitriﬁcation present an
incentive and opportunity for alternative ammonia treatment, that of recov-
ering and recycling ammonia from the dewatering sidestream. An ammonia
recovery approach can help reduce the treatment cost to WWTPs and at the
same time wastewater can be a source of nitrogen supply to agriculture if
converted to a saleable product. Returning nitrogen stripped from wastewa-
ter recovers the nitrogen originating from the food system, creating a circu-
lar nitrogen economy that can potentially generate revenue from WWTPs.
Nutrient recovery from wastewater for agricultural purposes has been
recommended by researchers, especially for recovering phosphorous and ni-
trogen (Diaz-Elsayed et al., 2019). Recovering phosphorus from WWTPs is
environmentally and energetically efﬁcient (Amann et al., 2018), and its sub-
sequent use as fertilizer promotes a circular economy. Similar example stud-
ies of ammonia‑nitrogen recovery from wastewater treatment plants are
found in the literature. In addition to the conventional biological ammonia
removal process, three mechanisms are identiﬁed for primary ammonia re-
covery for useful purposes (Ye et al., 2018). A chemical process of simulta-
neous recovery of ammonia and phosphate to form struvite precipitate can
be used when equal molar concentrations of magnesium, ammonia, and
phosphate ions are present at alkaline pH (Abbona et al., 1982). A second al-
ternative, ammonia enrichment through membrane concentration, requires
little energy investment and is often coupled with chemical and biological
processes (Nancharaiah et al., 2016; Neoh et al., 2016). As a third option,
which is the focus of our study, ammonia can be recovered from wastewater
using air-stripping technology (Katehis et al., 1998).
Few studies have investigated the life cycle environmental impact or
performed techno-economic evaluations of air-stripping technology imple-
mentation for resource recovery from municipal WWTPs. Zelm et al. (2020)
examined air stripping to recover ammonia and produce AS in a European
WWTP with low sidestream ﬂow (270 m3/day) and high sidestream NH4-N
concentration (2.5 kg/m3). Their results showed overall environmental
beneﬁts of the integrated removal, recovery, and AS fertilizer production
over ammonia removal-only systems; however, the authors did not exam-
ine varying NH4-N concentration or process economics. The ammonia con-
centration in the sidestream of Zelm et al. study is signiﬁcantly higher than
the average ammonia concentrations (0.9 kg/m3) in the anaerobic digested
sidestream in the wastewater treatment plants in North America
(Constantine, 2006). Similarly, the average sidestream ﬂow rate from 14
treatment facilities in New York City (∼15,000 m3/day) (Constantine,
2006) is 50-fold higher than the ﬂow rate studied by Zelm et al. (270 m3/
day) in the Netherlands. Both the environmental impact and economic fea-
sibility of ammonia air stripping to produce AS must be evaluated for a
range of concentrations and operating conditions, as reported in North
American WWTPs, which can have high sidestream ﬂow rates and low am-
monia concentration. Our objective is to evaluate the environmental and
economic tradeoff of implementing air-stripping for ammonia recovery
and simultaneous AS production in municipal WWTPs through life cycle as-
sessment (LCA) and techno-economic analysis (TEA), respectively. To un-
derstand the sensitivity of variability in sidestream ﬂow rate and NH4-N
concentration on life cycle impact assessment metrics, we investigate oper-
ating conditions from multiple North American WWTPs. Furthermore, we
examine the sensitivity of processing conditions on the break-even selling
price of AS to test its feasibility and investment risk by varying the price
of foreground materials required for AS production. To the best of our
knowledge, this is the ﬁrst detailed TEA that assesses the economic feasibil-
ity of implementing air-stripping at wastewater treatment facilities in the
United States and evaluates the cost-effectiveness and life cycle environ-
mental performance of ammonia recovery from varying sidestream ammo-
nia concentration from biosolids dewatering facilities at WWTPs.
2. Methods
We compare production of ammonium sulfate by air stripping and the
Haber-Bosch process. We collected data from one WWTP in the City of
Philadelphia, PA, USA as the primary data source on anaerobic digester oper-
ation and sidestream NH4-N concentration from several other WWTPs
S. Kar et al.
Science of the Total Environment 857 (2023) 159499
2

located in North America and Europe that apply anaerobic digestion as a ter-
tiary wastewater treatment process (Eskicioglu et al., 2018; Vineyard et al.,
2021). The WWTP facility, operated by the Philadelphia Water Department
(PWD), processes anaerobic digestate from primary and secondary sludge
into Class A biosolids at facility's Biosolids Recycling Center (BRC), produc-
ing sidestream. Periodic data on ﬂow rate and essential characteristics (pH,
Alkalinity, Ammonia, Nitrate, Phosphate, COD, TSS, etc.) of the WWTP side-
stream from the BRC were obtained from PWD for the years 2018 and 2019.
The median sidestream ﬂow rate across 2018 and 2019 is 7008 m3/day, and
the median NH4-N concentration is 0.625 kg/m3. As a current practice, this
sidestream is mixed with the WWTP's incoming wastewater stream. The side-
stream ammonia stripping is expected to reduce the WWTP's ammonia load
as well as produce marketable AS. While some studies refer to the liquid ef-
ﬂuent from the anaerobic digester as centrate and some studies refer it as
sidestream, here, we refer the efﬂuent in our study as sidestream only.
To study the sensitivity of varying sidestream NH4-N concentration, data
were collected from studies by Vineyard et al. (2021) and Eskicioglu et al.
(2018). Varying sidestream NH4-N concentration allows investigating its sen-
sitivity towards life cycle environmental impact of air-stripping based AS pro-
duction. Data from Vineyard et al., 2021 represent a WWTP in Madison
Wisconsin, United States with sidestream ﬂow rate of 946 m3/day and aver-
age sidestream NH4-N concentration of 1.44 kg NH4-N/m3. Several WWTPs
considered in the review study by Eskicioglu et al. (2018) represent various
sidestream NH4-N concentrations at different North American and
European locations: Regina, Saskatchewan, Canada; Salmon Arm, British Co-
lumbia, Canada; Wards Island, New York; Castres, France; and, King County,
Washington, United States. As ﬂow rate data were not available for these
WWTPs, we assume an average sidestream ﬂow rate of 1000 m3/day.
We consider a target of 80 % NH4-N removal rate from the sidestream.
Higher removal rates are possible but may require unrealistically large
energy inputs. For WWTP's other than PWD's WWTP where detailed
physical-chemical properties of sidestream are unknown, we scale the
unknown parameter values based on PWD's WWTP, while considering the
varying sidestream NH4-N concentration. We apply this assumption be-
cause of having limited data on the WWTPs to assess the sensitivity of vary-
ing sidestream NH4-N concentration on life cycle environmental impacts.
The LCA modeling is performed using SimaPro software (“SimaPro”,
2019) using data primarily from the ecoinvent database (Wernet et al.,
2016). Environmental impact data in ecoinvent represent region-speciﬁc
industrial data in three primary categories: 1) European subcontinent, de-
ﬁned as RER, 2) Rest of the World, deﬁned as RoW, and 3) Global, referring
to industries across the world, deﬁned as GLO. Life cycle inventory (LCI)
data for electricity production is modeled with higher speciﬁcity per coun-
try and for sub-regions in the North America. In this analysis, we apply GLO
LCI data sets whenever available, and where not available, we apply the
RoW data set. A functional unit of 1 kg of AS (100 %) is considered in the
study. The next sub-sections are arranged as follows. Sections 2.1 and 2.2
explain the life cycle designs for air-stripping technology and Haber
Bosch technology, respectively. Section 2.3 explains the LCA metrics imple-
mented in the study; and Section 2.4 explains the TEA for air-stripping.
2.1. Life cycle inventory of WWTP sidestream air-stripping technology
The block diagram in Fig. 1 shows the primary unit operations for recov-
ering AS from the WWTP sidestream. The industrial process is divided into
two phases, Phase A - the production or extraction of ammonia and Phase B
- ammonia reaction with sulfuric acid to form AS. The phase A of the air
stripping process for recovering ammonia differs from the Haber-Bosch,
while the phase B process is similar for both. In Phase A, the ammonia air
stripping process starts with pre-heating the wastewater sidestream to
55 °C after which the sidestream passes through a CO2 stripping column re-
moving dissolved CO2 from the sidestream. This step minimizes the forma-
tion of carbonic sediments during pH adjustment in the next step. The
sidestream is then heated to raise the temperature from 65 to 70 °C and
its pH is adjusted with lime before being sprayed in a top-down direction
in an air-stripping column to release ammonia from the liquid phase to
the gas phase. The airﬂow rate is adjusted to achieve about 80 % of ammo-
nia partitioning from the liquid phase to the gaseous phase. The high pH
(basic) and high temperature enhances the transfer of ammonia from the
liquid to the gas phase. Next, in phase B, the ammonia-rich air is passed
through a neutralization column to maintain pH = 6 and then passed
through a scrubbing column with H2SO4 crossﬂowing to form liquid ammo-
nium sulfate, as per the reaction (1). The outﬂowing liquid ammonium sul-
fate is transported to storage tanks through pipes. The cross ﬂowing H2SO4
stream is appropriately diluted to maintain outﬂowing ammonium sulfate
concentration at 35 % (by weight). It is necessary to maintain the solution
concentration below 40 % to avoid crystallization.
2NH3 þ H2SO4↔NH4
ð
Þ2SO4
(1)
Table 1 summarizes the “gate-to-gate” inputs for the unit operations used
to recover and convert ammonia to AS described as Phases A and B (Fig. 1).
Individual life cycle inventories for varying NH4-N concentrations are devel-
oped for analysis using SimaPro. The foreground chemical and energy require-
ments are calculated based on Zelm et al. (2020) data by linearly scaling with
respect to PWD's WWTP sidestream ﬂow rate and ammonia concentration. For
our proposed design, the heat required for temperature adjustment in Phase A
is assumed to be supplied from a locally installed natural gas boiler. Electricity
input is assumed supplied by the Reliability First Commission (RFC) grid, a
medium voltage regional electricity grid that supplies electricity to the City
of Philadelphia. Citric acid required for the stripping column's periodic
cleaning is also added to the foreground inventory. Foreground inventory
data for the additional WWTPs are calibrated to PWD's WWTP parameter
values, as per varying sidestream NH4-N concentration among the WWTPs.
2.2. Life cycle inventory of AS production by Haber Bosch process
Production of ammonia in the Haber Bosch process is achieved using
natural gas and steam at high temperature and pressure, steps correspond-
ing to Phase A of the air-stripping process shown in Fig. 1. The Haber-Bosch
process starts with the steam reforming of fossil fuel-sourced hydrocarbons
to produce hydrogen gas (Reaction 2).
CH4 þ H2O↔CO þ 3H2
(2)
N2 þ 3H2↔2NH3
(3)
Natural gas is the primary fossil fuel source for obtaining hydrogen gas.
Nitrogen gas is extracted from air and reacted with hydrogen gas
endothermically when passed over an iron catalyst bed at reaction condi-
tions of 15–25 MPa pressure and 400–500 °C temperature, requiring
91.8 kJ/mol energy to produce ammonia gas (Appl, 2011) (Reaction 3).
Once ammonia is produced in the gaseous state, similar process steps are
followed, as in Phase B of Fig. 1 for AS production. As previously explained
in Section 2.1, the ammonia-rich gaseous phase is reacted with sulfuric acid
to produce AS solution (35 % wt.), as per Reaction (1).
Cradle-to-gate LCA modeling of the Haber Bosch process is performed in
SimaPro, using the ecoinvent database. The ecoinvent's default AS produc-
tion process in SimaPro does not include the unit processes for materials but
provides details on process energy use and emissions. Hence, Ammonia
produced from the steam reforming unit process is added to AS production
inventory. For the ‘Ammonia liquid {RoW}’ ecoinvent unit process, process
heat is not present in the default inventory, this is overcome by process heat
accounted in the AS unit process. The ‘Ammonium sulfate, as N {RoW}’
ecoinvent unit process is modeled assuming its electricity is sourced from
the US RFC electricity grid and heat from global market natural gas. As
shown in Phase B (Fig. 1), the unit processes of liquid ammonia and sulfuric
acid from the ‘rest-of-the-world’ market (sulfuric acid {RoW}) are added to
the foreground inventory based on the stoichiometric mass balance. For a
fair comparison, identical foreground electricity and heat sources unit pro-
cesses are considered for both AS production from air-stripping and the
Haber-Bosch unit processes.
S. Kar et al.
Science of the Total Environment 857 (2023) 159499
3

2.3. Life cycle impact assessment
We use select life cycle impact assessment (LCIA) metrics, focusing on en-
ergy resource consumption, GHG emissions given the Haber-Bosch process's
high energy intensity, and metrics affecting human and ecosystem health to
evaluate the relative merit of AS recovery from WWTP. We apply the Inter-
governmental Panel for Climate Change (IPCC) 100-year global warming po-
tential (IPCC GWP 100a) to account for greenhouse gas (GHG emissions in kg
CO2 equivalents, kg CO2e) following IPCC's AR5 methodology (Stocker et al.,
2013). Additionally, to investigate the energy source of the LCI, we imple-
ment the cumulative energy demand (CED) metric. The lower heating
value (LHV) of fuels, which considers energy release from fuel combustion
without an accounting of latent heat of vaporization of water in the reaction
product, is selected for performing the CED analysis. Furthermore, we apply
ReCiPe midpoint hierarchical LCIA metrics in the analysis based on their
relevance to water impacts, resource scarcity, human health, and land use.
Initially developed in 2008, the ReCiPe 2016 midpoint indicators provide a
comprehensive set of environmental impact assessment metrics (Goedkoop
et al., 2009; Huijbregts et al., 2017). ReCiPe has been implemented widely
across several LCA studies for wastewater treatment (Niero et al., 2014;
Tian et al., 2020), green stormwater infrastructure (Wang et al., 2013), con-
struction (Tian and Spatari, 2022), pharmaceutical products (Galant et al.,
2022; Riazi et al., 2019), and bioenergy (Boschiero et al., 2016). In our
study, we consider select few of them to identify the key differences across
the compared scenarios, which are listed in Table 2.
2.4. Techno-economic analysis
An economic assessment is performed to estimate the break-even selling
price of 35 % AS produced through the ammonia air-stripping process from
