4. Results and discussion 
4.1. Relative impact contributions of the process to the different routes 
The relative impact contributions of the different processes for each 
route were assessed in order to potentially improve the environmental 
performance of the treatments (Fig. 2). Cement manufacturing turned to 
be the main impact contributor of route 1, with values between 46 and 
80% for the different categories except in terrestrial ecotoxicity (20%) 
and water depletion (25%). Sand obtaining is the process with higher 
impact in water depletion (value of 47%). The obtaining of this material, 
together with the treatment of the metalliferous hydroxide sludge also 
contributes to the impacts associated to other categories. Despite 
transport plays a minor role in impacts contribution for most categories, 
its contribution impacting terrestrial ecotoxicity may be considered as 
significant (around 28%). 
The highest impacts associated to route 2 (metal recovery) are 
related to the use of toluene and TBP (Fig. 2), chemical reagents 
commonly used for the recovery of metals, with values higher than 79% 
in each impact category. Minor impacts were associated to the use of 
sulphuric and oxalic acids during the metal recovery scheme, especially 
in the category of metal depletion (values of 10% and 14%, respec­
tively). This fact points out to the need of applying green chemistry 
procedures to extract metals from these materials. On the contrary, the 
route 3 (P removal) exhibited negative values in all categories except for 
water depletion due to the elevated use of wastewater. The use of 
electricity to prepare the pelletized material also caused positive impact 
values in this route (Fig. 2). 
On the other hand, the use of clay and the treatment of metalliferous 
hydroxide sludge accounts for >94% of the impacts for route 4 (brick 
fabrication), except for water depletion, with values dropping to 27%. 
The electricity necessary for the sludge drying and the brick 
manufacturing phases is the main contributor to water depletion (value 
of 42% and 31%, respectively). Thus, this impact distribution highlights 
the need of using more sustainable materials and renewable energies 
during the manufacturing of bricks using waste materials. 
4.2. Life cycle impact assessment 
The environmental impacts generated by the different management/ 
valorisation routes obtained with OpenLCA are shown in Table 2. The 
main environmental impacts associated to the encapsulation of wastes 
and disposal in landfills are mainly related to climate change (331 kg 
CO2 eq), human toxicity (51 kg 1,4-dB eq) and fossil depletion (47 kg oil 
eq), metal and water depletion (6 kg Fe eq and 3 m3, respectively). The 
route 2, based on metal recovery from the sludge, causes the greatest 
impact in all categories, with a significant impact on climate change 
(24,103 kg CO2 eq), fossil depletion (20,943 kg oil eq), human toxicity 
(2441 kg 1,4-dB eq) or metal depletion (172 kg Fe eq). In the case of the 
route 3, based on the use of sludge to remove P, it stands out the impact 
associated to water depletion (value of 7663 m3) due to the volume of 
water consumed in the life cycle. In turn, route 3 shows minimum im­
pacts in other categories, having negative values in all of them due to the 
use of wastewater within its life cycle. The negative values usually ex­
press that the credits/benefits are larger than the burdens to the envi­
ronment. In this case, recycling wastewater implies credits/benefits 
from the environment, because freshwater is not taken as a resource, and 
the phosphorus contained originally in water is removed. Comparing the 
impacts among different routes, the routes 1 (waste encapsulation and 
landfilling) and route 4 (brick fabrication) have much lower impact in 
all categories than route 3 (phosphorus removal) and to a lesser extent to 
route 2 (metal recovery). 
Each impact indicator was normalized using regional Europe release 
per capita reference values (ReCiPe H Europe 2008 V1.13) to facilitate 
the comparison across impact categories. The normalized results (REQs) 
are expressed on a common scale providing information on the relative 
significance of the valorisation routes for each category indicator (Fig. 3 
and Table SM1). In this sense, the highest impact is caused by route 2 in 
the fossil depletion impact category (value of 13 REQs), followed by 
marine ecotoxicity and freshwater ecotoxicity (value of 7 and 5 REQs, 
respectively). In addition, route 2 also generated low impacts in

terrestrial ecotoxicity and metal depletion (value of 1.6 REQs and 0.2 
REQs, respectively). In contrast, the route 3 exhibited the lowest impacts 
in the categories of marine and freshwater ecotoxicity (values of −14

However, an overall benefit to the environment is observed for this 
route. Route 1 causes its higher impacts in the marine and freshwater 
ecotoxicity categories (value of 0.14 and 0.10 REQs, respectively), while 
the impact in terrestrial ecotoxicity category was low (value of 0.003 
REQs). Finally, route 4 exhibited generally lower impact values than 
route 1, except for metal depletion which exhibited similar values. The 
greatest impact generated by this route was in the marine ecotoxicity 
category (value of 0.1 REQs), while the lowest was observed in terres­
trial ecotoxicity (value of 0.002 REQs). 
4.3. Potential improvements to the environmental performance of the 
sludge treatment routes 
The availability of abiotic resources, such as water, minerals, or fossil 
fuels, is an issue related to their depletion for future generations 
(Harmsen et al., 2013; Tao et al., 2022). In this sense, more sustainable 
raw materials have to be used to avoid these effects and cause a lower 
environmental impact. The previous sections highlighted the impacts 
associated to several processes and raw materials within the different 
explored valorisation routes. As previously commented, cement 
manufacturing and sand obtaining caused the highest impacts during 
the encapsulation of sludge (Route 1). Cement production causes a high 
environmental impact and intensive energy consumption. Therefore, the 
modification of the process or the substitution of cement by a more 
environmentally friendly material would significantly reduce the im­
pacts caused by this route. In this sense, Mikulˇci´c et al. (2016) propose 
an improvement of the production system using alternative fuels and a 
more energy efficient kiln process. These actions made possible to 
reduce the consumption of fossil fuels and consequently reduce CO2 
emissions. On the other hand, coal ash and dicalcium silicate γ phase 
(γ-2CaO⋅SiO2) could also be used as a substitute for cement, as proposed 
by Higuchi et al. (2014) for the manufacture of concrete. Thus, CO2 
generated by a power plant can be captured and reacts with 
γ-2CaO⋅SiO2, hardening concrete. A more sustainable solution could be 
achieved by replacing the cement by other wastes with pozzolanic 
properties. In this sense, Dandautiya and Singh (2019) tested the joint 
use of fly ash and copper tailing as replacement of cement. These authors 
reported a significant environmental improvement, with a notable 
reduction in the selected midpoint categories (from 25 to 38%). More 
recently, Liu et al. (2021) used gold mine tailings rich in Si and Al oxides 
as precursors to produce geopolymers, achieving a marketable product 
containing up to 60% of tailings. Considering the abundance of this type 
of wastes within the mines, this latter option would improve notably the 
environmental (and economic) performance of this route. 
The main impacts of route 2 were associated to the use of commercial 
chemical reagents, such as toluene and TBP. Therefore, the substitution 
of these products within a greener chemistry framework would help to 
reduce the impacts associated to the recovery of metals from the sludge. 
In this respect, the production of toluene from biomass is more envi­
ronmentally sustainable that its obtaining by traditional chemical pro­
cesses (Clark et al., 2015; Niziolek et al., 2016). A similar case is 
observed for TBP which conventionally is synthetized after the reaction 
of n-butanol with phosphorus oxychloride in batch/semi-batch process. 
However, more efficient processes would reduce the environmental 
impacts associated to the synthesis of this reagent. For example, Sen 
et al. (2020) proposed an improvement of the process using a continuous 
synthesis method in a micro-reactor. These authors reported a 100% 
yield for a residence time of 44 min at reaction temperature of 60 ◦C. 
The utilization of the sludge to prepare pelletized materials leads to 
impacts associated to water removal and electricity. This process, which 
turned to be the most environmentally friendly of those studied, can be 
therefore improved by the reutilization of treated wastewater and the 
use of renewable energies. This latter impact is common to all explored 
valorisation routes, therefore, the use of renewable energies such as 
biomass or solar thermal and geothermal technologies in low and me­
dium temperature may reduce the environmental impacts produced 
(IRENA, 2015). 
The valorisation of sludge for brick manufacturing leads to impacts 
mainly associated to clay obtaining and energy required to treat the 
sludge. The environmental performance of the process can be notably 
improved if clay is partially or totally replaced. For that matter, Taha 
et al. (2016) propose substituting this raw material by treated calamine 
processing wastes. These authors obtained decreasing costs and waste 
generation while producing a brick which fulfils the requirements for 
construction. These facts highlight the need to evaluate individual val­
orisation schemes of generated sludge during waste water treatment in 
the mining industry as well as the potential to improve these schemes. 
5. Conclusions 
Metal-rich sludge generated by AMD treatment may be of environ­
mental concern and requires proper management. In this study, 4 
different management/valorisation routes have been proposed for this 
type of sludge and evaluated their environmental impacts. The evalua­
tion of the routes studied by LCA allows to quantify the environmental 
impacts associated with each process and valorisation route. In this case, 
the route with the lowest impacts was route 3, focused on the 
manufacturing of pelletized material to remove phosphorus from agri­
cultural/urban wastewaters while producing a fertilizer product. This 
route has mainly net negative impact values due to the environmental 
benefits of wastewater treatment. However, the highest impacts were 
associated to water depletion (value of 7663 m3) and energy con­
sumption. The recovery of valuable metals from sludge has an un­
doubtedly 
economic 
interest, 
however 
exhibited 
the 
highest 
environmental impacts in the categories of fossil depletion, marine and 
freshwater ecotoxicity (values of 6–14 REQs) due to the use of toluene 
and TBP (impact>79%). An improvement of different processes within 
this route, such as the production of toluene from biomass or the use of 
continuous TBP synthesis method in a microreactor would cause a sig­
nificant reduction of environmental impacts. The other two routes 
exhibited much lower impacts than the route focused on metal recovery 
(values <1 REQs). The manufacture of bricks could also be an alterna­
tive route by giving economic value to wastes while exhibiting low 
environmental impacts, mainly on marine and freshwater ecotoxicity 
(value of 0.7 kg 1,4-dB eq). These impacts can be notably reduced if 
waste materials are used replacing clays with wastes such as calamine 
processing wastes or using renewable energies. The encapsulation of 
these wastes is also a suitable option with a low environmental impact, 
but slightly greater than the brick manufacturing and with no economic 
return. The substitution of cement by wastes or more environmentally 
friendly materials can be a good alternative to reduce these impacts. 
The results obtained in this work can be used to support the decision- 
making process for the sustainability of waste management after the 
AMD treatment. In this sense, the environmental performance of any 
proposed action must be assessed using a LCA approach, considering all 
environmental impacts of materials and processes. An adequate valor­
isation of the large amount of waste produced would contribute to more 
environmentally friendly mining. In addition, an important economic 
profit can be obtained from sludge valorisation, although these pro­
cesses should be improved to reduce the impacts. This study proposes an 
interesting approach to improve the environmental performance of 
sulphide mining worldwide, however some issues remained unsolved, 
such as a carefully life cost analysis of proposed measures or the inclu­
sion of endpoint indicators. 
R. Moreno-Gonz´alez et al.

Phosphorus
 is  an  important

 cultivation.
 Global
 reserves
 of phosphate
 rock  worth

ing
 are  limited.
 Reuse  of  phosphorus
 in  wastewater
 is  therefore
 important.
 This
 LCA  study
 assessed
the
 environmental
 impact
 of four  ways  to  supply
 Swedish  agriculture
 with
 phosphorus
 fertiliser
 of
acceptable
 quality
 as  regards
 cadmium
 content:
 mineral
 fertiliser;  certiﬁed  sewage  sludge;
 struvite
(MgNH4PO4·6H2O)  precipitated
 from  wastewater;
 and  phosphorus
 recovered
 from  sludge  incineration.
These
 were  examined
 with  respect
 to  impacts
 on  global
 warming,
 eutrophication,
 energy  demand
 and
cadmium
 ﬂows  to farmland.
 The
 functional
 unit
 chosen
 was  11 kg  P (25.2  kg P2O5)
 to
 agricultural
 land,
which
 was
 the  average
 phosphorus
 output
 (removal
 with  harvest)
 per  hectare
 from
 Swedish
 farmland
in
 2007.  Using  sewage  sludge
 directly  on  farmland
 was  the  most
 efﬁcient
 option
 in  terms  of energy
and
 emissions
 of  greenhouse
 gases,
 but  also  added  most  cadmium
 to the soil.
 Phosphorus
 recovery
 from
incinerated
 sludge  was  the most  energy
 demanding
 option
 and  gave
 most  emissions
 of  greenhouse
 gases.
Despite
 great  concerns
 about  cadmium
 in  Swedish

 and  sludge,
 it  was  found  that
 almost

ular

1.
 Introduction
For
 food
 production
 to
 be
 sustainable,
 there
 is
 a
 need
 for
 sus-
tainable
 access
 to
 the
 nutrient
 phosphorus
 (P).
 Phosphorus
 is
indispensable
 and
 not
 replaceable
 in
 plant
 cultivation
 and
 the
 sur-
vival
 of
 animals
 and
 humans
 is
 also
 strictly
 dependent
 on
 receiving
adequate
 amounts
 of
 phosphorus
 in
 the
 diet.
 Most
 of
 the
 phos-
phorus
 in
 animals
 and
 humans
 is
 found
 in
 bones
 and
 teeth,
 but
phosphorus
 is
 also
 a
 part
 of
 many
 enzymes,
 the
 energy
 carrier
in
 cells
 (ATP)
 and
 is
 an
 important
 part
 of
 nucleic
 acid,
 the
 main
structure
 of
 genes.
Phosphorus
 is
 an
 element,
 and
 cannot
 disappear,
 but
 can
become
 dispersed
 and
 thereby
 difﬁcult
 to
 reuse.
 The
 reserves
 of
phosphate
 rock
 worth
 mining
 are
 limited,
 although
 it
 is
 not
 known
exactly
 how
 limited
 (Driver
 et
 al.,
 1999).
 Most
 frequently
 a
 time
horizon
 of
 50–100
 years
 has
 been
 suggested
 (Cordell
 et
 al.,
 2009).
However,
 a
 recent
 report
 stated
 that
 world
 resources
 of
 phospho-
rus
 have
 been
 underestimated
 and
 concluded
 that
 with
 current
rates
 of
 use,
 the
 existing
 global
 reserves
 and
 resources
 should
 be
available
 for
 the
 foreseeable
 future
 (IFDC,
 2011).
 However,
 even
if
 the
 phosphorus
 resources
 that
 can
 be
 used
 as
 fertiliser
 will
 last
for
 more
 than
 this
 generation,
 they
 are
 ultimately
 limited
 (Global
Phosphorus
 Network,
 2011).

 author.
 Tel.:
 +46
 243
 25
 30
 39.
E-mail
 address:
 kersti.linderholm@silvberg.se
 (K.
 Linderholm).
Igneous
 phosphate
 rock
 (apatite)
 from
 the
 Kola
 Peninsula
 in
Russia
 or
 Silinjärvi
 in
 Finland
 is
 largely
 used
 in
 fertilisers
 sold
 in
Sweden
 and
 this
 form
 has
 a
 low
 content
 of
 the
 heavy
 metal
 cad-
mium
 (Cd).
 However,
 only
 13%
 of
 global
 phosphate
 reserves
 are
bound
 in
 igneous
 phosphate
 rock
 and
 most
 of
 the
 world
 resources
of
 phosphate
 ore
 are
 in
 sedimentary
 form
 and
 normally
 have
 higher
cadmium
 content
 (Swedish
 EPA,
 2010a). Sedimentary
 phosphate
rock,
 which
 occurs
 in
 much
 larger
 amounts
 than
 igneous
 phosphate
rock,
 is
 used
 in
 agriculture
 all
 over
 the
 world.
Most
 of
 the
 phosphorus
 in
 the
 food
 we
 eat
 passes
 through
the
 body
 and
 ends
 up
 in
 wastewater.
 Phosphorus
 is
 also
 essen-
tial
 for
 aquatic
 life
 and
 is
 often
 the
 limiting
 nutrient.
 Therefore,
efﬂuent
 of
 phosphorus
 via
 wastewater
 normally
 leads
 to
 higher
biomass
 production
 in
 water,
 a
 process
 known
 as
 eutrophication.
Swedish
 wastewater
 treatment
 plants
 (WWTP)
 are
 designed
 to
capture
 phosphorus.
 About
 95%
 of
 the
 phosphorus
 entering
 WWTP
is
 found
 in
 the
 sewage
 sludge
 and
 the
 rest
 goes
 into
 the
 receiving
water
 (Statistics
 Sweden,
 2010a).
Recycling
 of
 the
 nutrients
 in
 wastewater
 to
 agriculture
 has
 been
discussed
 since
 the
 water
 closet
 was
 ﬁrst
 introduced
 in
 the
 early
1900s.
 In
 1972
 the
 Swedish
 University
 of
 Agricultural
 Sciences
 pub-
lished
 a
 report
 entitled
 Sewage
 Sludge
 –
 Dangerous
 or
 Beneﬁcial
 for
Farmland?
 (Ericsson,
 1972)
 The
 answer
 appears
 to
 be
 the
 same
 then
as
 it
 is
 today
 –
 it
 depends
 on
 the
 quality.
The
 most
 discussed
 pollutant
 in
 sludge
 is
 probably
 the
 cadmium
content.
 The
 reason
 for
 this
 is
 that
 cadmium
 is
 easily
 taken
 up
by
 plants,
 in
 combination
 with
 high
 toxicity
 for
 humans
 (Lindberg

 Recycling
 66 (2012) 27–
 39
et
 al.,
 2007).
 Cadmium
 occurs
 together
 with
 phosphorus
 in
 organic
fertilisers
 such
 as
 manure
 as
 well
 as
 in
 phosphate
 rock,
 which
 is
the
 raw
 material
 for
 mineral
 fertilisers.
 Sludge
 also
 contains
 other
substances
 that
 are
 unwanted
 in
 a
 fertiliser,
 since
 it
 reﬂects
 the
substances
 used
 in
 households
 and
 society.
 At
 the
 end
 of
 the
 1980s,
Greenpeace
 launched
 an
 action
 about
 sewage
 sludge
 in
 Sweden
 and
the
 Federation
 of
 Swedish
 Farmers
 decided
 to
 ban
 the
 use
 of
 sludge
on
 farmland
 (Augustinsson,
 2003).
In
 May
 1990
 the
 Swedish
 Environmental
 Protection
 Agency
(EPA)
 was
 given
 a
 Government
 commission
 named
 ‘Cleaner
 Sludge’.
In
 the
 following
 year,
 the
 Swedish
 EPA
 adopted
 a
 policy
 for
connections
 to
 municipal
 wastewater
 treatment
 plants
 (WWTP)
with
 the
 aim
 of
 protecting
 waters
 and
 obtaining
 good
 qual-
ity
 sludge.
 The
 principle
 was
 to
 treat
 all
 industrial
 wastewater
locally,
 while
 ensuring
 that
 only
 material
 that
 could
 be
 treated
in
 municipal
 WWTP
 without
 polluting
 the
 sludge
 entered
 the
sewage
 system.
 Because
 of
 this
 policy,
 many
 industrial
 plants,
 land-
ﬁlls
 and
 other
 activities
 eventually
 built
 their
 own
 WWTP.
 Many
sources
 of
 heavy
 metals
 and
 other
 unwanted
 elements
 entering
the
 municipal
 WWTP
 were
 traced
 and
 remedied
 (Augustinsson,
2003).
In
 May
 1994
 a
 sludge
 agreement
 was
 signed
 between
 the
Swedish
 EPA,
 the
 Swedish
 Water
 &
 Wastewater
 Association
 and
 the
Federation
 of
 Swedish
 Farmers,
 concerning
 the
 connection
 policy
for
 WWTP,
 local
 Agenda
 21
 discussions
 and
 the
 quality
 of
 sludge
(Swedish
 EPA,
 1996).
 The
 ﬁrst
 audit
 of
 sludge
 according
 to
 new
legislation
 and
 the
 intention
 in
 the
 sludge
 agreement
 took
 place
 in
1993.
 The
 development
 of
 sludge
 certiﬁcation
 has
 continued
 and
in
 2010
 the
 Federation
 of
 Swedish
 Farmers
 recommended
 that
 its
members
 only
 use
 sludge
 from
 certiﬁed
 WWTPs
 on
 farmland
 (LRF,
2012).
 The
 Swedish
 initiative
 with
 sludge
 auditing
 and
 certiﬁca-
tion
 has
 been
 followed
 in
 other
 countries.
 For
 example
 in
 the
 UK
an
 agreement
 called
 The
 Sludge
 Matrix
 was
 reached
 in
 1998
 (ADAS,
2001).
 As
 a
 result,
 77%
 of
 the
 sewage
 sludge
 in
 UK
 was
 used
 as
 a
fertiliser
 in
 2008
 (Water
 UK,
 2010).
 Only
 26%
 of
 sludge
 was
 used
 as
a
 fertiliser
 in
 Sweden
 in
 the
 same
 year
 (Statistics
 Sweden,
 2010a),
which
 could
 be
 explained
 by
 the
 intensive
 debate
 going
 on
 and
 the
fact
 that
 the
 Swedish
 Food
 Federation
 advised
 its
 members
 against
trading
 in
 products
 fertilised
 with
 sludge
 (Cirkulation,
 2009).
The
 quality
 of
 Swedish
 sewage
 sludge,
 in
 terms
 of
 heavy
 metal
contamination,
 has
 improved
 substantially
 since
 1970
 (Bernes
and
 Lundgren,
 2009).
 As
 an
 example,
 the
 average
 sludge
 con-
tained
 43
 mg  Cd/kg
 P
 in
 1998
 (recalculated
 from
 Statistics
 Sweden,
2010a).
 Ten
 years
 later
 (2008),
 the
 content
 was
 29
 mg  Cd/kg
 P
(13
 mg  Cd/kg
 P2O5).
Even
 if
 Swedish
 sludge
 is
 of
 good
 quality
 as
 a
 fertiliser,
 with
a
 low
 content
 of
 heavy
 metals
 and
 other
 unwanted
 substances,
the
 discussion
 about
 using
 this
 sludge
 as
 a
 fertiliser
 seems
 to
 be
never-ending.
 In
 this
 debate,
 different
 techniques
 for
 separating
phosphorus
 from
 the
 wastewater
 streams
 have
 been
 suggested
(Cirkulation,
 2009).
 The
 topic
 of
 separating
 phosphorus
 is
 not
new.
 International
 conferences
 on
 phosphorus
 recovery
 and
 recy-
cling
 were
 held
 in
 1998,
 2001,
 2004
 in
 Europe
 and
 2009
 in
Canada
 (IWA
 WaterWiki,
 2009).
 Apart
 from
 the
 option
 to
 recy-
cle
 phosphorus
 and
 other
 nutrients
 as
 sludge
 on
 agricultural
 land
after
 sanitisation,
 there
 is
 a
 wide
 range
 of
 techniques
 available
for
 separating
 phosphorus
 in
 the
 wastewater
 streams
 or
 from
sewage
 sludge.
 At
 the
 conference
 in
 Canada
 in
 2009,
 many
 of
the
 projects
 presented
 concerned
 precipitation
 of
 magnesium
ammonium
 phosphate
 (MgNH4PO4·6H2O),
 also
 called
 struvite,
 and
phosphorus
 recovery
 from
 ash
 from
 incineration
 of
 sludge
 (Ashley
et
 al.,
 2009).
In
 the
 Nordic
 countries
 there
 have
 also
 been
 projects
 on
 phos-
phorus
 recovery.
 For
 example,
 the
 Krepro
 and
 BioCon
 processes
both
 use
 thermal
 treatment
 for
 removal
 of
 organic
 material
 and
dissolution
 of
 phosphorus
 compounds
 and
 metals
 by
 sulphuric
 acid
(Donatello
 et
 al.,
 2010;
 Levlin
 et
 al.,
 2002;
 Svensson,
 2000).
 Another
example
 is
 the
 AquaReci
 process,
 with
 supercritical
 water
 oxida-
tion
 of
 digested
 sludge
 followed
 by
 leaching
 of
 the
 residues
 by
acids
 or
 bases
 (Stark
 et
 al.,
 2006).
 No
 full-scale
 commercial
 facil-
ities
 employing
 these
 processes
 on
 wastewater
 are
 in
 operation
 to
date
 (Swedish
 EPA,
 2010b).
The
 main
 objective
 of
 this
 study
 was
 to
 assess
 the
 environ-
mental
 impact
 of
 different
 ways
 to
 supply
 Swedish
 agriculture
with
 phosphorus
 fertiliser
 of
 an
 acceptable
 quality
 as
 regards
 cad-
mium
 content.
 The
 study
 was
 limited
 to
 replacing
 the
 phosphorus
removed
 with
 the
 harvested
 crop
 from
 arable
 land
 in
 Sweden.
 In
other
 words,
 ﬁeld-scale
 balance
 for
 phosphorus
 in
 a
 long-term
 per-
spective
 was
 chosen
 for
 comparison
 of
 alternatives.
Sludge
 as
 a
 waste
 has
 been
 studied
 previously
 in
 life
 cycle
assessment
 (LCA)
 (Lederer
 and
 Rechberger,
 2010;
 Lundin
 et
 al.,
2000;
 Svanström
 et
 al.,
 2005;
 Tillman
 et
 al.,
 1998).
 Utilisation
 of
phosphorus
 in
 sludge
 for
 different
 purposes,
 e.g.
 land
 reclamation,
golf
 courses
 and
 agriculture,
 has
 also
 been
 investigated
 (Johansson
et
 al.,
 2008).
 Furthermore,
 recycling
 of
 phosphorus
 from
 small-
scale
 wastewater
 systems
 has
 been
 studied
 in
 a
 LCA
 perspective
by
 Tidåker
 et
 al.
 (2007).  However
 to
 our
 knowledge,
 no
 previous
study
 has
 examined
 the
 supply
 of
 phosphorus
 to
 agriculture
 on
 a
large
 scale.
Four
 different
 phosphorus
 fertilisers
 were
 compared
 here:
 min-
eral
 fertiliser,
 certiﬁed
 sewage
 sludge,
 struvite
 precipitated
 from
wastewater
 and
 phosphorus
 recovered
 from
 sludge
 incineration.
These
 were
 examined
 with
 respect
 to
 impacts
 on
 global
 warming,
energy
 demand
 and
 eutrophication.
 The
 cadmium
 ﬂows
 to
 farm-
land
 were
 also
 assessed
 for
 the
 four
 alternatives.
Calculations
 were
 also
 made
 to
 assess
 how
 far
 sewage
 sludge
 can
be
 transported
 according
 to
 avoided
 energy
 use
 and
 emissions
 of
CO2 equivalents
 (CO2-equiv.)
 when
 the
 nutrients
 in
 sludge
 replace
the
 nutrients
 in
 mineral
 fertiliser.
2.
 Methodology
 and
 description
 of
 phosphorus
 fertiliser
alternatives
2.1.
 Life
 cycle
 assessment
LCA
 was
 used
 to
 assess
 the
 environmental
 impact
 of
 differ-
ent
 ways
 to
 supply
 Swedish
 agriculture
 with
 phosphorus.
 LCA
 is
a
 well-established
 method
 for
 conducting
 a
 holistic
 environmental
evaluation
 and
 avoiding
 sub-optimisation.
 It
 is
 possible
 to
 handle
 a
large
 amount
 of
 data
 and
 compare
 the
 environmental
 impact
 of
 dif-
ferent
 options.
 LCA
 as
 a
 method
 is
 regulated
 in
 ISO
 standards
 (ISO,
2006a,
 2006b)
 and
 described
 in
 textbooks
 (Baumann
 and
 Tillman,
2004).
The
 options
 for
 phosphorus
 fertilisation
 of
 arable
 land
 studied
here
 were
 mining
 of
 virgin
 phosphorus
 and
 reuse
 of
 phosphorus
from
 wastewater
 though
 sewage
 sludge,
 struvite
 precipitation
 in
WWTP
 or
 recovery
 of
 phosphorus
 from
 incinerated
 sludge.
 It
 was
not
 possible
 to
 compare
 virgin
 sedimentary
 phosphorus
 with
 that
from
 apatite
 rock
 due
 to
 lack
 of
 information
 about
 the
 origin
 of
phosphorus
 in
 available
 data
 that
 could
 be
 used
 in
 the
 LCA.
 This
 is
explained
 in
 more
 detail
 in
 the
 discussion.
In the
 case
 of
 phosphorus
 mining,
 the
 whole
 chain
 from
 extrac-
tion
 of
 the
 mineral
 to
 spreading
 phosphorus
 fertiliser
 was
 included
in
 the
 analysis.
 In
 the
 case
 of
 reuse
 of
 phosphorus
 from
 wastewater,
only
 the
 processes
 between
 wastewater
 treatment
 and
 spreading
on
 the
 ﬁeld
 were
 included.
Data
 were
 collected
 from
 operations
 and
 plants
 working
 on
 a
commercial
 basis,
 ofﬁcial
 statistics,
 authorities
 and
 scientiﬁc
 pub-
lications.
The
 options
 for
 phosphorus
 fertilisation
 studied
 are
 shown
 in
Fig.
 1.

 Options
 for
 phosphorus
 fertilisation
 studied:
 mining
 of
 virgin
 phosphorus;
reuse
 of
 the

 in

 though

 and

 in

2.2.
 Functional
 unit
On
 average
 in
 2007,
 1
 ha
 of
 arable
 farmland,
 including
 perma-
nent
 grassland,
 in
 Sweden
 received
 13
 kg
 P
 in
 fertiliser,
 of
 which
5
 kg
 came
 from
 mineral
 fertiliser
 and
 8
 kg
 from
 manure.
 The
 har-
vested
 crop
 removed
 on
 average
 11
 kg
 P/ha
 (Statistics
 Sweden,
2011a,b).
The
 Swedish
 Board
 of
 Agriculture
 recommends
 balanced
 phos-
phorus
 fertilisation,
 i.e.
 only
 replacing
 harvested
 phosphorus,
on
 soils
 with
 satisfactory
 phosphorus
 status
 (Swedish
 Board
 of
Agriculture,
 2010).
 Swedish
 environmental
 monitoring
 data
 show
that
 34%
 of
 arable
 soils
 have
 good
 or
 surplus
 phosphorus
 con-
tent,
 37%
 satisfactory
 content
 and
 29%
 low
 or
 very
 low
 content
(Eriksson
 et
 al.,
 2010).
 This
 means
 that
 on
 average,
 replacement
of
 harvested
 phosphorus
 would
 be
 sufﬁcient
 in
 the
 long-term
perspective.
The
 functional
 unit
 (FU)
 chosen
 for
 this
 study
 was
 therefore
11
 kg
 pure
 P
 (25.2
 kg
 P2O5) to
 agricultural
 land.
2.3.
 Choice
 of
 impact
 categories
 and
 indicators
The
 resources
 studied
 were
 energy
 and
 phosphorus.
 Global
warming
 and
 eutrophication
 by
 phosphorus
 were
 studied
 as
ecological
 effects.
 Energy
 was
 considered
 as
 primary
 energy.
Global
 
warming
 
was
 
considered
 
as
 
CO2-equiv.
 
(GWP100
according
 to
 IPCC
 1995)
 using
 data
 taken
 from
 the
 litera-
ture.
Cadmium
 added
 to
 soil
 was
 calculated
 as
 an
 indicator
 of
 the
 risk
of
 cadmium
 contamination
 in
 food
 grown
 on
 arable
 land.
Release
 of
 phosphorus
 to
 waters
 was
 used
 as
 indicator
 of
eutrophication.
 It
 should
 be
 noted
 that
 release
 of
 phosphorus
 and
nitrogen
 (N)
 to
 waters
 from
 cultivation
 lay
 outside
 the
 scope
 of
 this
study.
2.4.
 System
 boundaries
2.4.1.
 Boundaries
 in
 relation
 to
 natural
 systems
This
 study
 examined
 phosphorus
 fertiliser,
 including
 its
 spread-
ing
 on
 farmland.
 Crop
 production
 in
 the
 ﬁeld
 was
 assumed
 to
 be
the
 same
 in
 all
 options
 studied.
 The
 difference
 in
 fertiliser
 effect
of
 phosphorus
 obtained
 from
 different
 sources
 was
 not
 consid-
ered,
 although
 there
 are
 differences
 in
 the
 short-term
 perspective.
For
 example,
 mineral
 phosphorus
 fertiliser
 is
 more
 directly
 plant-
available
 than
 organic
 fertilisers,
 which
 need
 some
 time
 for
mineralisation
 in
 the
 soil
 (Linderholm
 et
 al.,
 2003).
 For
 this
 rea-
son,
 mineral
 fertiliser
 is
 normally
 added
 in
 amounts
 sufﬁcient
 for
one
 year,
 while
 organic
 phosphorus
 fertilisers
 are
 often
 added
 for
several
 years,
 especially
 to
 crops
 such
 as
 cereals
 and
 grass.
 For
phosphorus-demanding
 crops,
 or
 soils
 lacking
 phosphorus,
 it
 is
 not
really
 an
 option
 to
 fully
 exclude
 mineral
 fertiliser.
 Questions
 about
phosphorus
 availability
 in
 the
 short
 term
 were
 omitted
 from
 the
present
 study,
 since
 the
 objective
 was
 to
 study
 options/alternatives
to
 replace
 the
 phosphorus
 removed
 with
 crops
 from
 arable
 land.
An
 assumption
 in
 the
 study
 was
 thus
 that
 in
 a
 longer-term
 per-
spective,
 all
 added
 phosphorus
 was
 of
 the
 same
 value
 for
 the
 crop
(Linderholm
 et
 al.,
 2003).
2.4.2.
 Geographical
 boundaries
The
 geographical
 boundary
 for
 phosphorus
 requirements
 was
taken
 as
 arable
 land
 in
 Sweden,
 concerning
 transport,
 techniques,
legislation
 and
 fertilisation
 strategy.
2.4.3.
 Time
 boundaries
The
 question
 examined
 was
 how
 to
 provide
 Swedish
 agriculture
with
 phosphorus
 every
 year
 in
 a
 long-term
 perspective.
 The
 statis-
tical
 data
 used
 were
 mainly
 from
 2009,
 since
 these
 were
 the
 most
recent
 available
 data.
2.4.4.
 Boundaries
 within
 technical
 systems
Cultivation
 and
 wastewater
 treatment
 lay
 outside
 the
 sys-
tem
 (Fig.
 1),
 as
 these
 activities
 are
 almost
 the
 same
 in
 all
 four
options
 studied.
 Use
 of
 WWTP
 for
 wastewater
 is
 compulsory
 in
Sweden
 since
 1969
 to
 protect
 surface
 waters
 and
 groundwater
(Swedish
 Code
 of
 Statutes,
 1969).
 Operation
 of
 WWTP
 is
 reported
to
 have
 a
 larger
 impact
 on
 the
 environment
 than
 investments
 in
WWTP
 (Tillman
 et
 al.,
 1998).
 Therefore
 investments
 in
 WWTP,
incinerators,
 equipment
 for
 struvite
 precipitation
 and
 phosphorus
extraction,
 etc.
 were
 excluded
 from
 the
 study.
 This
 is
 discussed
 fur-
ther
 in
 the
 sensitivity
 analysis
 in
 Section
 4.6.  Emissions
 of
 nitrous
oxide,
 methane
 and
 ammonia
 will
 possibly
 occur
 during
 handling,
transport,
 storage,
 spreading
 and
 landﬁll
 of
 sludge
 in
 all
 wastewa-
ter
 alternatives
 (Johansson
 et
 al.,
 2008).
 However,
 there
 is
 a
 lack
of
 quantitative
 data
 on
 emissions
 from
 sludge
 during
 handling,
storage,
 etc.
 The
 issue
 is
 therefore
 excluded
 from
 this
 study.
Packaging
 was
 assumed
 to
 be
 negligible.
2.5.
 System
 expansion
 and
 allocation
In
 the
 scenario
 involving
 incineration
 of
 sewage
 sludge,
 which
initially
 has
 a
 dry
 matter
 (DM)
 content
 of
 5%,
 it
 has
 to
 be
 heated
 from
15 ◦C
 to
 45 ◦C
 to
 obtain
 a
 DM
 content
 of
 34%.
 The
 normal
 treatment
at
 Swedish
 WWTP
 is
 to
 digest
 the
 sludge
 for
 about
 20
 days
 at
 35 ◦C.
This
 study
 assumed

 nitrogen

 fertiliser

 1.
 The

 (K)

 The

 (Mg)

On

 and

 to

 When

of

 Nitrogen,

over

 In

 and
phosphorus

 for

 and

 and

 on

 The

required
 for

 was

 into

 of

 studied
The
 
four

2.6.1.
 Mineral
 phosphorus
 fertiliser
 from
 mining
In
 the
 phosphate
 rock
 option,
 new
 phosphorus
 is
 added
 to
 the
food
 chain
 (Fig.
 2).
 This
 option
 ends
 when
 the
 fertiliser
 is
 spread
 on
the
 ﬁeld.
The
 most
 common
 mineral
 phosphorus
 fertiliser
 used
 in
 Sweden
is
 combined
 NPK
 fertiliser.
 Available
 data
 for
 NPK
 fertiliser
 are
 14
years

 and
 derive

 from

 abandoned
 production
sites
 in

 (Davis
 and
 Haglund,

 The

 has
 rad-
ically
 changed
 since
 the
 1990s,
 especially
 concerning

 of
greenhouse

 when

 N-fertiliser
 (Yara,

 Use
of
 this
 data
 for
 (combined)
 NPK
 fertiliser
 and

 between
nitrogen
 and
 phosphorous
 with
 updated
 data
 for
 nitrogen,
 would
result
 in

 impact
 from

 phosphorus
 part
 of
the
 fertiliser
 that
 is
 too
 high.
 Instead,
 this

 used
 data
 for
triple
 superphosphate
 (TSP),
 which

 only

 as
the
 nutrient.
 Data
 used
 for
 calculations
 on
 TSP
 are
 presented
 in
Tables
 1
 and
 2.
2.6.2.
 Recycled

In

technology

(Adam

 The

recovery

concerning

2.6.2.1.
 Sewage

 to

 Fertilisation

sludge

food

The

municipal

cipitation

 This

an

an

The

 for

 sludge
 are

 1
 and

 Struvite

MgNH4PO4·6H2O.

process

and

The

option

dissolved

 for

 (see
Fig.

 None
 of

vite

Technologies
 has
 a

and

 Data
 for
 the

 located
in

 Canada,
 were
 used
 in

The
 Edmonton
 WWTP
 has
 a
 connected
 population
 of
 approxi-
mately
 one
 million
 people.
 Struvite
 precipitation
 covers
 20%
 of
 the
total
 phosphorus
 load.
 The
 struvite
 formation
 system
 is
 integrated
into
 the
 sludge
 thickening
 and
 dewatering
 reject
 water
 systems.
The
 liquid
 phase,
 after
 dewatering
 of
 the
 sludge,
 goes
 to
 an
 up-ﬂow
ﬂuidised
 bed
 reactor
 with
 multiple
 reactive
 zones
 of
 increasing
diameter
 (Fig.
 4).
 Struvite
 crystallisation
 is
 controlled
 by
 a
 com-
bination
 of
 magnesium
 dose
 and
 pH
 control.
 Magnesium
 chloride
(MgCl2) and
 sodium
 hydroxide
 (NaOH)
 are
 added.
The
 struvite
 fertiliser
 product
 from
 Edmonton
 is
 called
 Crystal
Green
 and
 has
 a
 composition
 as
 shown
 in
 Table
 4.
 The
 ﬂowchart
 for
struvite
 as
 a
 phosphorus
 fertiliser
 is
 shown
 in
 Fig.
 5.
It
 was
 not
 possible
 to
 get
 reliable
 information
 about
 the
 ﬂow
of
 cadmium
 and
 phosphorus
 through
 Edmonton
 WWTP
 (Britton,
2010;
 Mumby,
 2010).
 A
 few
 analyses
 on
 cadmium
 in
 struvite
 were
found
 in
 the
 literature
 and
 used
 in
 the
 calculations.
The
 consultation
 around
 the
 Swedish
 EPA’s
 recent
 work
 on
wastewater
 has
 found
 that
 a
 minor
 part
 (20–25%)
 of
 incoming
phosphorus
 to
 a
 WWTP
 can
 be
 bound
 in
 struvite
 (SWWA,
 2010).
Calculations
 on
 struvite
 precipitation
 in
 the
 Edmonton
 WWTP
 ver-
ify
 this:
 200
 000
 persons
 contribute
 420
 kg
 P/day
 (Vinnerås,
 2002),
but
 only
 61
 kg
 P
 are
 bound
 in
 the
 500
 kg
 struvite
 produced
 every
day.
Besides
 struvite,
 sludge
 is
 produced.
 The
 sludge
 in
 Edmonton
is
 composted
 and
 then
 used
 on
 agricultural
 land
 or
 in
 mine
 recla-
mation
 (Mumby,
 2010).
 The
 sludge
 is
 less
 rich
 in
 phosphorus
 and
probably
 contains
 more
 cadmium
 than
 sludge
 from
 conventional
chemical
 WWTP
 in
 Sweden.
 For
 that
 reason,
 this
 study
 assumed
that
 the
 sludge
 would
 be
 used
 for
 land
 reclamation
 or
 plant
 soil
under
 Swedish
 conditions.
 Another
 option
 could
 be
 incineration.
The
 volume
 of
 sludge
 produced
 was
 assumed
 to
 be
 15%
 less
 than
sludge
 from
 chemical
 precipitation
 (Swedish
 EPA,
 2010b).
Since
 environmental
 laws
 are
 very
 strict
 about
 phosphorus
losses
 to
 waters,
 in
 Sweden
 struvite
 precipitation
 would
 need
 com-
plementary
 chemical
 precipitation.
 This
 study
 assumed
 20%
 less
precipitation
 chemicals
 than
 normally
 used
 in
 Swedish
 WWTP,
since
 about
 20%
 of
 the
 phosphorus
 is
 bound
 in
 struvite.
 Note
that
 precipitation
 chemicals
 also
 can
 give
 other
 beneﬁts
 to
 the
wastewater
 treatment
 process
 like
 improved
 biogas
 production
and
 energy
 savings
 (Hansen,
 2012).
 These
 are
 not
 included
 in
 the
calculation.
 The
 data
 used
 on
 struvite
 precipitation
 are
 presented
in
 Tables
 1
 and
 4.
2.6.2.3.
 Phosphorus
 recovery
 from
 ash
 from
 incinerated
 sludge.
 To
minimise
 unwanted
 substances
 in
 recycled
 phosphorus,
 one
 option
is
 to
 incinerate
 the
 sludge
 and
 recover
 the
 phosphorus
 from
 ash.
 A
project
 sponsored
 by
 the
 EU
 entitled
 Sustainable
 and
 Safe
 Re-use
 of
Municipal
 Sewage
 Sludge
 for
 Nutrient
 Recovery
 –
 SUSAN
 has
 resulted
in
 the
 Ash
 Dec
 process
 (Adam
 et
 al.,
 2008;
 Lederer
 and
 Rechberger,
2010)
 studied
 in
 this
 project.
The
 former
 company
 ASH
 DEC
 had
 a
 pilot
 plant
 in
 Austria.
The
 patented
 thermal
 phosphate
 recovery
 process
 Ash
 Dec
 was
acquired
 in
 February
 2011
 by
 Outotec
 Oyj,
 Finland
 (Outotec,
 2012).
The
 ﬂowchart
 for
 phosphorus
 recovered
 from
 ash
 is
 shown
 in
Fig.
 6.
 In
 the
 WWTP
 the
 sludge
 is
 preheated
 up
 to
 about
 45 ◦C
 before
dewatering
 with
 centrifuges.
 Through
 this
 treatment,
 it
 is
 possible
to
 get
 sludge
 with
 34%
 DM
 (Baumgartinger,
 2010).
The
 sewage
 sludge
 is
 then
 incinerated.
 There
 is
 a
 need
 to
 add
heavy
 oil
 or
 other
 substances
 with
 a
 high
 heating
 value
 when
 incin-
erating
 the
 sludge.
 It
 is
 not
 possible
 to
 extract
 phosphorus
 from
 ash
where
 sludge
 has
 been
 incinerated
 with
 other
 substrates
 such
 as
household
 waste,
 as
 the
 phosphorus
 content
 in
 the
 ash
 is
 then
 too
low.
 Ash
 from
 monoincineration
 of
 sludge
 is
 mixed
 with
 chlorine
donors
 (MgCl2 or
 CaCl2)
 and
 compacted
 in
 a
 pellet
 press.
 The
 pel-
lets
 are
 fed
 to
 a
 thermal
 reactor
 and
 exposed
 for
 about
 20
 min
 to
 a
temperature
 of
 1000 ◦C
 (Mattenberger,
 2010).
At
 this
 temperature
 a
 great
 deal
 of
 metals,
 for
 example
 cadmium,
lead
 (Pb),
 zinc
 (Zn)
 and
 copper
 (Cu),
 react
 with
 the
 additives
 (MgCl2
or
 CaCl2)
 and
 evaporate.
 However,
 chromium
 (Cr)
 and
 nickel
 (Ni)
are
 not
 so
 easily
 evaporated
 (Mattenberger
 et
 al.,
 2008).
The
 full-scale
 plants
 that
 were
 planned
 by

 former
 com-
pany
 ASH
 DEC
 would
 have
 had
 a
 capacity
 of
 15
 000–55
 000
 tonnes
ash
 per

 i.e.
 ash
 from
 incinerated
 sludge
 from
 about
 1.4
 to
 5
million
 persons
 (Mattenberger,

 This

 assumed
 that

the

 possible
 to

 capa-
ble
 of

 tonne

 of

 per

In

Sweden,

10

country.
 There

sludge
 in

 In
 a
 scenario
 of

 of
 sludge
 and

tion
 of
 phosphorus
 from

 ash,
 this
 study
 therefore
 assumed
that
 the
 incineration
 plants
 are
 situated
 in
 combination
 with
 the

2.7.
 Crediting
 for
 nitrogen
 content
To
 make
 the
 options
 comparable,
 products
 containing
 nitrogen
were
 credited
 for
 this.
 This
 was
 done
 by
 subtracting
 the
 CO2-
equiv.
 and
 energy
 that
 would
 have
 resulted
 from
 production
 of
 the
nitrogen
 fertiliser
 contained
 in
 the
 phosphorus
 fertiliser
 alterna-
tive.
 This
 study
 assumed
 nitrogen
 fertiliser
 produced
 with
 modern
techniques.
 Yara,
 which
 is
 the
 dominant
 company
 on
 the
 fertiliser
market
 in
 Sweden,
 gives
 a
 guarantee
 that
 the
 carbon
 footprint
 is
below
 4
 kg
 CO2-equiv./kg
 N
 for

 sold
 in
 Sweden.
 This
 has
been
 certiﬁed
 by
 DNV
 (Det
 Norske
 Veritas)
 (Yara,
 2012).
The
 nitrogen
 in
 organic
 fertilisers
 such
 as

 and

occurs
 in
 different
 forms
 and
 does
 not
 always
 have
 the
 same
value
 as
 mineral
 nitrogen
 fertiliser.
 Organically
 bound
 nitrogen
is
 released
 slowly
 and
 nitrogen
 that
 is
 released
 in
 autumn
 is
 not
available
 to
 crop
 plants.
 Struvite
 is
 also
 a
 fertiliser
 that
 releases
 its
nutrients
 slowly
 (Crystal
 Green,
 2012).
The
 value
 of
 nitrogen
 in
 organic
 fertilisers
 is
 therefore
 depen-
dent
 on:
1.
 The
 proportion
 of
 ammonia
 lost
 to
 air.
2.
 The
 proportion
 of
 nitrogen
 that
 can
 be
 used
 by
 plants
 in
 the
 ﬁrst
year.
3.
 The
 proportion
 of
 nitrogen
 released
 in
 the
 years
 after
 spreading
and
 used
 by
 plants.
Many
 studies
 have
 examined
 this
 subject,
 but
 mainly
 on
manure.
 Focus
 on
 Nutrients,
 a
 joint
 venture
 between
 the
 Swedish
Board
 of
 Agriculture,
 the
 County
 Administration
 Boards,
 the
 Fed-
eration
 of
 Swedish
 Farmers
 and
 a
 number
 of
 companies
 in
the
 agricultural
 sector,
 has
 published
 calculations
 on
 nitrogen
efﬁciency
 for
 organic
 fertilisers
 (Focus
 on
 Nutrients,
 2012).
 Their
data
 on
 the
 proportion
 of
 nitrogen
 in
 organic
 fertilisers
 that
 is
 of
beneﬁt
 to
 plants
 were
 used
 here
 together
 with
 expert
 judgment
(Nilsson,
 2011).
 Thus
 40%
 of
 total
 nitrogen
 in
 sewage
 sludge
 and
struvite
 was
 assumed
 to
 be
 used
 by
 the
 crop,
 which
 meant
 6.8
 and
1.8
 kg
 N/FU
 respectively.

 Results
3.1.
 Use
 of
 energy
 and
 emissions
 of

 gases
The
 results
 for
 energy
 use

 7)
 and

gases

 for

 to
Swedish

tiliser

gases

 Phosphorus

from

and

 emissions
 of

 In

 9
 and
 10
 a
more

 is

 of
 the
 results
 for

 phospho-
rus
 options.

 Use
 of
 virgin

The

 the

cess

 For

(25.2

 Fertilising

vite
 and
 P
 from

has

 of

 or

phorus

 of
the

 studied

4.
 Discussion
4.1.
 How
 to
 reuse
 the
 phosphorus
 in
 wastewater?
According
 to
 the
 results
 presented
 above,
 using
 sewage
 sludge
directly
 on
 farmland
 was
 the
 most
 efﬁcient
 option
 in
 terms
 of
energy
 and
 emissions
 of
 greenhouse
 gases.
 This
 technique
 is
 com-
mon  throughout
 Sweden
 and
 the
 same
 equipment
 as
 for
 manure
can
 be
 used.
The
 results
 of
 the
 LCA
 also
 showed
 that
 large-scale
 struvite
precipitation
 in
 WWTP
 is
 not
 as
 costly
 for
 the
 environment
 per
functional
 unit
 (11
 kg
 P)
 as
 phosphorus
 recovered
 from
 ash.
 How-
ever,
 only
 a
 minor
 part
 of
 the
 phosphorus
 in
 wastewater
 is
 captured
in
 the
 struvite
 and
 the
 remainder
 has
 to
 be
 precipitated
 in
 sludge
 to
fulﬁl
 the
 demands
 on
 emissions
 to
 waters.
 This
 gives
 two
 products
to
 handle,
 struvite
 and
 sludge.
 The
 sludge
 contains
 less
 phosphorus
than
 sludge
 from
 a
 conventional
 WWTP.
Phosphorus
 recovery
 from
 the
 ash
 of
 incinerated
 sludge
 accord-
ing
 to
 the
 Ash
 Dec
 process
 is
 costly,
 both
 in
 terms
 of
 energy
 and
emissions
 of
 greenhouse
 gases.
 Despite
 this,
 it
 can
 be
 a
 viable
 option
if
 the
 ﬂow
 of
 cadmium
 to
 arable
 land
 is
 viewed
 as
 more
 problematic
than
 energy
 demand
 and
 greenhouse
 gas
 emissions.
 Recovering
phosphorus
 from
 ash
 can
 be
 interesting
 in
 cases
 where
 hazardous
waste
 material
 is
 incinerated,
 e.g.
 fallen
 animals
 and
 some
 slaugh-
terhouse
 waste.
 A
 disadvantage
 with
 incineration
 is
 that
 nitrogen
is
 lost
 to
 the
 air
 and
 energy
 is
 required
 to
 re-capture
 this
 nitrogen
in
 mineral
 fertiliser
 or
 through
 nitrogen
 ﬁxation
 by
 legumes.
4.2.
 Eutrophication
 through
 phosphorus
 emissions
 to
 waters
outside
 the
 system
 boundaries
Emissions
 of
 phosphorus
 from
 WWTP
 were
 not
 included
 in
 this
LCA.
 However,
 from
 WWTP
 included
 in
 the
 statistics,
 313
 tonnes
 P
 is
lost
 to
 waters
 (Statistics
 Sweden,
 2010a).  In
 2008,
 these
 WWTP
 gave
more
 than
 0.5
 million
 FU
 in
 sludge
 (6000
 tonnes
 P).
 This
 means
 that
1
 FU
 from
 Swedish
 WWTP
 releases
 0.54
 kg
 phosphorus
 to
 water.
Struvite
 precipitation,
 as
 practised
 in
 Edmonton,
 would
 increase
phosphorus
 emissions
 to
 water.
 However
 Swedish
 legislation
 does
not
 allow
 this,
 so
 the
 assumption
 here
 was
 that
 chemical
 pre-
cipitation
 would
 be
 adjusted
 in
 the
 WWTP
 to
 avoid
 an
 increase
in
 eutrophication.
 The
 ﬁgure
 0.54
 kg
 P
 is
 then
 the
 same
 for
 all
recycled
 phosphorus
 options
 studied.
 Emissions
 from
 farmland
 fer-
tilised
 with
 more
 than
 1
 FU
 amount
 to
 0.3
 kg
 P/ha
 (Swedish
 Board
 of
Agriculture,
 2008).
 This
 means
 that
 1
 FU
 emits
 more
 phosphorus
 to
waters
 during
 production
 than
 during
 cultivation.
 Moreover,
 there
is
 probably
 a
 difference
 for
 blue-green
 algae
 between
 the
 emissions
of
 phosphorus
 from
 arable
 land
 and
 those
 from
 wastewater
 treat-
ment
 and
 private
 sewage
 systems
 (Krogstad
 and
 Løvstad,
 1991).
The
 phosphorus
 from
 wastewater
 is
 mainly
 dissolved
 phosphate,
while
 the
 phosphorus
 released
 from
 arable
 land
 is
 mainly
 bound
 to
soil
 particles.
We  did
 not
 ﬁnd
 any
 data
 to
 show
 that
 phosphorus
 losses
 from
agricultural
 land
 are
 affected
 by
 different
 fertilisers
 under
 normal
conditions.
The
 emissions
 of
 phosphorus
 from
 production
 of
 mineral
 fer-
tiliser
 are
 negligible
 compared
 with
 emissions
 from
 WWTP,
 but
these
 were
 the
 only
 emissions
 to
 water
 included
 in
 this
 LCA.
However,
 regardless
 of
 the
 use
 of
 phosphorus
 in
 wastewater,
 the
emissions
 of
 phosphorus
 to
 waters
 will
 still
 be
 the
 same.

This

in

 obtained

 However,

 in

(Linderholm
 et

The
 value
 of
 recycled
 phosphorus
 in
 sewage
 sludge
 and
 other
products
 has
 been
 investigated
 in
 a
 number
 of
 experiments
 and
varies
 from
 0
 to
 136%,
 depending
 on
 the
 design
 of
 the
 experiment.
These
 experiments
 are
 often
 conducted
 for
 a
 short
 time,
 less
 than
one
 year
 (de
 Haan,
 1980;
 Krogstad
 et
 al.,
 2005;
 Kuile
 et
 al.,
 1983;
Vigerust,
 1983).
Assuming
 that
 only
 50%
 of
 the
 phosphorus
 in
 recycled
 prod-
ucts
 is
 available
 for
 plants,
 the
 energy
 demand
 and
 emissions
 of
greenhouse
 gases
 for
 using
 the
 phosphorus
 would
 show
 the
 same
pattern
 as
 before,
 but
 on
 a
 higher
 level
 for
 struvite
 and
 phospho-
rus
 recovery
 from
 ash.
 The
 negative
 values
 for
 sludge
 in
 terms
 of
energy
 demand
 and
 emissions
 of
 CO2-equiv.
 would
 increase
 due
to
 the
 nitrogen
 content
 (if
 the
 phosphorus
 content
 is
 only
 valued
to
 50%,
 the
 amount
 of
 fertiliser
 is
 doubled
 and
 consequently
 the
nitrogen
 dose
 is
 doubled).
4.5.
 Sensitivity
 analysis
 with
 respect
 to
 investment,
 transport
distance,
 electricity
 and
 mineral
 nitrogen
 fertiliser
4.5.1.
 Investment
If investment
 costs
 had
 been
 considered
 in
 the
 analysis,
 the
Ash
 Dec
 and
 struvite
 scenarios
 would
 have
 been
 charged
 for
 extra
investments.
 This
 would
 have
 resulted
 in
 higher
 energy
 demand
and
 higher
 emissions
 of
 greenhouse
 gases.
 The
 normal
 WWTP
was
 the
 base
 in
 all
 three
 scenarios
 with
 recycled
 phosphorus.
 This
means
 that
 the
 sludge
 scenario
 would
 still
 be
 the
 most
 efﬁcient
option
 in
 terms
 of
 energy
 demand
 and
 emissions
 of
 greenhouse
gases.
4.5.2.
 Transport
 distance
The
 transport
 distance
 was
 estimated
 based
 on
 population,
country
 and
 farmland.
 In
 all
 options
 with
 recycled
 phosphorus
there
 is
 a
 need
 of
 transporting
 almost
 the
 same
 amount
 of
 sludge
 to
farmland,
 incineration
 or
 land
 reclamation
 and
 the
 estimated
 trans-
port
 distances
 have
 little
 impact
 when
 comparing
 the
 options.
 The
energy
 demand
 for
 transport
 in
 relation
 to
 total
 energy
 demand
in
 different
 options
 was
 most
 signiﬁcant
 for
 sludge.
 This
 study
assumed
 a
 one-way
 transport
 distance
 of
 40
 km
 for
 sewage
 sludge
to
 farmland.
As
 described
 in
 the
 introduction,
 concerns
 about
 using
 sludge
on
 arable
 land
 have
 limited
 the
 possibilities
 for
 use
 of
 sludge
 in
Sweden.
 For
 example,
 farmers
 who
 want
 to
 certify
 their
 produc-
tion
 according
 to
 ‘The
 Swedish
 Seal
 of
 Quality’
 are
 not
 allowed
to
 use
 sewage
 sludge
 (Swedish
 Seal
 of
 Quality,
 2012)
 and
 some
Swedish

 have
 banned
 farmers
 from
 using
 sewage
sludge
 owing
 to
 the
 precautionary
 principle
 (Ärnﬂykt,
 2012).
 The
Swedish
 Farmers’
 Federation
 only
 allows
 the
 use
 of
 sewage
 sludge
from
 WWTPs
 certiﬁed
 according
 to
 a
 special
 certiﬁcation
 system
that
 requires
 a
 long
 storage
 period,
 which
 leads
 to
 longer
 transport

4.5.3.
 Electricity
Sweden

 which

 compared

As

 in

 2.7
 and

 1,

a

 This

the

 The

 for

 in
Europe

lower.

 of

 data
 for

 used
 in
 this
 LCA
The

 Life
 Cycle
 Inventory
 Database
 (USDA,
 2011)
 states
 that
‘ﬁnding
 consistent
 and
 transparent
 data
 for
 life
 cycle
 assessments
(LCAs)
 is
 difﬁcult’.
 This
 study
 also
 found
 that
 available
 data
 on
 min-
eral
 P
 fertiliser
 that
 can
 be
 used
 in
 an
 LCA
 are
 limited
 and
 the
references
 are
 often
 linked.
The
 data
 used
 in
 this
 study
 were
 taken
 from
 Davis
 and
 Haglund
(1999),
 Jenssen
 and
 Kongshaug
 (2003),  Brentrup
 and
 Pallière
 (2008)
and
 the

 database
 (Nemecek
 et
 al.,
 2004).
 Although
Nemecek
 et
 al.
 (2004)
 refers
 to
 Davis
 and
 Haglund
 (1999),  the
 out-
come
 differs,
 as
 shown
 in
 Table
 6.
 Davis
 and
 Haglund
 refer
 to
 a
paper
 written
 by
 Kongshaug
 (1998)
 and
 presented
 at
 a
 seminar
 in
Prague.
 Data
 in
 Jenssen
 and
 Kongshaug
 (2003)
 are
 based
 on
 a
 paper
written
 and
 presented
 by
 Kongshaug
 in
 Marrakesh
 in
 1998.
 Bren-
trup
 and
 Pallière
 refer
 to
 Kongshaug.
 The
 data
 on
 TSP
 in
 all
 these
publications
 are
 average
 data
 for
 Europe
 and
 the
 origin
 of
 phos-
phate
 rock
 is
 unknown.
 Data
 for
 phosphorus
 fertilisers
 provided
 in
the

 cited,
 recalculated
 to
 phosphorus
 from
 P2O5, show
a
 wide
 variation
 (Table
 6),
 although
 they
 originate
 from
 the
 same
source.
 TSP
 contains
 48%
 P2O5.
Many
 attempts
 were
 made
 to
 get
 up-to-date
 and
 speciﬁc
 data
 on
phosphorus
 mineral
 fertiliser
 for
 this
 study.
 Ofﬁcial
 data
 for
 min-
eral
 fertiliser
 were
 scheduled
 for

 in
 the

 database
 for
LCA
 (European
 Commission,
 2012).
 However
 in
 July
 2010,
 the
 Joint
Research
 Centre
 reported
 that

 for

 fertilisers
 would
be

 soon
 and
 data
 for
 phosphorus
 fertilisers
 would
 be
published
 towards
 end
 of
 2010
 and
 recommended
 contact
 with
the
 European
 Fertiliser
 Manufacturers
 Association,
 which
 would
provide
 the
 data.
 The
 recommendation
 from
 the
 provider
 of
 the
datasets
 was
 to
 use
 Davis
 and
 Haglund
 (1999),  while

 for
the
 ofﬁcial

One

 for

 about
 ofﬁcial

 and
 the
variation
 in

 the

 of
 phosphorus.
Jenssen

ferences

phosphate

‘The

phosphate

 (0.3

Mining

 As
apatite

it,

 to

 GJ/t

Re-calculation

phosphate

cannot

Table

A

 of

H2SO4 process.

of

Conversion

acid.

generates

The

 of

5.
 Conclusions
Using
 sewage
 sludge
 directly
 on
 farmland
 is
 the
 most
 efﬁcient
option
 in
 terms
 of
 energy
 and
 emissions
 of
 greenhouse
 gases.
Phosphorus
 recovery
 from
 ash
 according
 to
 the
 Ash
 Dec
 process
is
 costly
 in
 terms
 of
 energy
 and
 emissions
 of
 greenhouse
 gases.
Large-scale
 recovery
 of
 phosphorus
 as
 struvite
 is
 not
 a
 suitable
technique
 for
 Sweden
 owing
 to
 technical
 and
 cost
 reasons.
An
 unexpected
 ﬁnding
 was
 that
 the
 cadmium
 imports
 with
 food
to
 Sweden
 are
 unknown,
 despite
 the
 fact
 that
 these
 may
 constitute
a
 substantial
 share
 of
 the
 overall
 cadmium
 ﬂows.
Nitrogen
 is
 a
 valuable
 nutrient
 and
 is
 energy-demanding
 to
replace.
 Nitrogen
 is
 lost
 both
 to
 water
 and
 air
 in
 WWTP,
 but
 the
sludge
 still
 contains
 some
 nitrogen
 which
 has
 a
 distinct
 impact
 in
LCA.
 With
 incineration
 of
 sludge,
 100%
 of
 the
 remaining
 nitrogen
in
 sludge
 is
 lost
 to
 air.

records.full_text:
Environmental sustainability of municipal wastewater treatment
through struvite precipitation: Inﬂuence of operational parameters
Avhafunani Mavhungu a, Spyros Foteinis b, *, Richard Mbaya a, Vhahangwele Masindi c, d,
Ioannis Kortidis e, Lizzy Mpenyana-Monyatsi f, Efthalia Chatzisymeon b
a Department of Chemical, Metallurgy and Materials Engineering, Staatsartillirie Rd, Pretoria West 0183, Tshwane University of Technology, Pretoria, South
Africa
b School of Engineering, Institute for Infrastructure and Environment, University of Edinburgh, Edinburgh, EH9 3JL, United Kingdom
c Council for Scientiﬁc and Industrial Research (CSIR), Built Environment (BE), Hydraulic Infrastructure Engineering (HIE), P.O Box 395, Pretoria, 0001, South
Africa
d Department of Environmental Sciences, School of Agriculture and Environmental Sciences, University of South Africa (UNISA), P. O. Box 392, Florida, 1710,
South Africa
e Department of Physics and Engineering, University of Zululand, Kwadlangezwa, 3886, South Africa
f Department of Environmental, Water and Earth Sciences, Faculty of Science, Tshwane University of Technology, Private Bag X680, Pretoria, 0001, South
Africa
a r t i c l e
i n f o
Article history:
Received 7 April 2020
Received in revised form
24 October 2020
Accepted 26 October 2020
Available online 28 October 2020
Handling editor: Cecilia Maria Villas B^oas de
Almeida
Keywords:
Wastewater valorisation
Waste beneﬁciation
Low- and middle-income countries (LMIC)
Magnesium ammonium phosphate (struvite
NH4MgPO46H2O)
SimaPro
Net impact or net environmental beneﬁt
(NEB) approach
a b s t r a c t
The environmental sustainability of wastewater treatment through phosphate (P) and ammonia (N)
chemical precipitation (struvite) was examined using the life cycle assessment methodology. Thermally
activated (calcined) cryptocrystalline magnesite was used towards struvite formation and four process
parameters (contact time, magnesite dosage, initial wastewater pH and temperature) were studied.
Raising wastewater’s temperature to promote ammonia stripping was found to be environmentally
unsound. Magnesite dosage and contact time were identiﬁed as environmental hotspots, but not pH. In
terms of environmental relevance, when using ReCiPe 2016 life cycle impact assessment method, the
human health damage category was mostly affected, followed by resource availability, while ecosystems
category was affected to a much lesser extent. Environmental impacts were grossly attributed to South
Africa’s fossil fuel-dependent energy mix, suggesting that renewable energy could largely improve the
system’s environmental performance. The optimal conditions, from the environmental perspective, were
found to be 0.2 g L1 feed dosage and 10 min mixing, at ambient temperature and pH (total environ-
mental footprint 60.9 mPt per treated L of wastewater). To improve N removal efﬁcacy, which is desirable
in real-world applications, higher feed dosages and mixing durations are required, albeit at the expense
of environmental sustainability (e.g. the 180 min and 16 g L1 environmental footprint sharply rises to
1.87 mPt L1). The net impact approach revealed that the avoided environmental impacts on the
midpoint impact categories freshwater and marine eutrophication grossly outweigh the corresponding
environmental impacts of the treatment process. Results suggest that when using a low contact time and
magnesite dosage, struvite precipitation could act as a fast, efﬁcient, and environmentally friendly pre-
treatment step to practically remove P and grossly reduce N from wastewater. On the other hand, with
higher feed dosages and mixing durations, struvite precipitation could act as a promising secondary
wastewater treatment method with an overall low environmental footprint. Overall, results complement
the existing body of knowledge on the techno-economic performance of such systems and provide
insight to decision- and policy-makers to sustainably scale up the process, at village- or industrial-level,
in rural South Africa, Lesotho, and further aﬁeld.
© 2020 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.
* Corresponding author. ,
E-mail addresses: sfoteini@exseed.ed.ac.uk, sfoteinis@gmail.com (S. Foteinis), VMasindi@csir.co.za, masindivhahangwele@gmail.com (V. Masindi), E.Chatzisymeon@ed.ac.
uk (E. Chatzisymeon).
Contents lists available at ScienceDirect
Journal of Cleaner Production
journal homepage: www.elsevier.com/locate/jclepro
https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jclepro.2020.124856
0959-6526/© 2020 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.
Journal of Cleaner Production 285 (2021) 124856

1. Introduction
The enrichment of natural water bodies with high nutrient load,
typically phosphate (P) and ammonia (N), comprise a growing
problem of environmental concern that grossly affects low- and
middle-income countries (LMICs). The problem is concomitantly
perpetuated by an ever-growing population that proportionally
increase nutrient enrichment, typically through untreated or
poorly treated wastewater discharges to different receiving aquatic
ecosystems. Among others, nutrients encourage the proliﬁc prop-
agation and growth of aquatic plants, which ultimately become a
severe problem when they die and start decomposing, since oxygen
depletion in water will ensue (Yagi and Fukushi, 2012). An aquatic
ecosystem deﬁcient in dissolved oxygen will suffocate and kill
aquatic organisms (Masindi et al., 2016b, c). In addition, excessive
or dead organisms block light penetration in water, hindering
photosynthesis, while the aesthetic value of the ecosystem is also
affected by plant infestations and dead organic matter (Peng et al.,
2018). However, nutrient discharges to surface water is on the rise,
primarily in LMICs (van Puijenbroek et al., 2019).
The above highlight the need to limit the amount of pollutants
discharged into receiving aquatic ecosystems. This can be achieved
through proper management of the evergrowing wastewater
quantities. A promising method towards this end is P and N pre-
cipitation from wastewater, preferably using inexpensive co-
agulants such as magnesium, with the added beneﬁt of struvite
(NH4MgPO46H2O) formation. Magnesite rock is a relative inex-
pensive source of magnesium that is readily available in many re-
gions around the world, including LMICs such as South Africa and
Nepal where capital and infrastructure for wastewater treatment is
weak or non-existent (Kr€ahenbühl et al., 2016; Masindi et al.,
2016b, c). Furthermore, struvite is a highly effective source of ni-
trogen, magnesium, and phosphorus and therefore can act as a
replacement of chemical fertilisers in the agriculture industry
(Lujan-Facundo et al., 2019) and particularly as a replacement of
phosphate rock including their reserves which are rapidly dwin-
dling (Ceasar, 2018). This highlights that wastewater sludge can be
used for materials recovery in the circular economy context
(Gherghel et al., 2019), and especially for phosphorus (struvite)
recycling (Jedelhauser and Binder, 2018).
The technical and economic perspectives of struvite recovery
systems have been extensively studied (Daneshgar et al., 2019;
Peng et al., 2018; Yu et al., 2017). However, this is not the case for
the environmental perspective, where the body of knowledge is
restricted to a small number of studies, primarily focusing on the
developed world (Pausta et al., 2018; Zhou et al., 2019). This high-
lights the need for further research to comprehensively consider
the environmental dimension of the process, with focus given on
struvite recovery systems outside of Europe (Lam et al., 2020; Sena
and Hicks, 2018), and particullarly in LMICs where the sustainable
treatment of wastewater is a pressing matter of environmental
concern (van Puijenbroek et al., 2019). The environmental dimen-
sion of the process can be identiﬁed using robust and well-
established tools, such as the life cycle assessment (LCA) method-
ology (Chatzisymeon et al., 2017; Ioannou-Ttofa et al., 2016). In this
regard, the net impacts or net environmental beneﬁt (NEB)
approach, which considers both the environmental impacts of the
wastewater treatment method and the environmental beneﬁts
from pollutants removal, i.e. the impacts that are avoided from
releasing treated wastewater instead of raw wastewater (Godin
et al., 2012; Igos et al., 2012; K€ohler et al., 2012), can also be
applied. However, even though this method seems very promising
for identifying the environmental beneﬁts arising from pollutants
removal from wastewater, it suffers from: i) the need to include as
many pollutants as possible (to properly characterize the avoided
impacts), and ii) large uncertainties, particularly in the (eco)toxicity
assessment (Igos, 2016).
South Africa and Lesotho, the latter a landlocked country
encircled by the ﬁrst, are LMICs with weak to non-existent infra-
structure, since in many rural areas wastewater usually returns
untreated to the environment. This highlights the urgent need for
introducing robust and versatile wastewater systems in rural and
peri-urban areas. Considering that struvite recovery systems are
relatively simple to install and easy to operate, these can be
installed in such areas. South Africa is rich in cryptocrystalline
magnesite, which, from the techno-economic perspective, is
promising for struvite precipitation (Masindi et al., 2016b, c;
Mavhungu et al., 2019). Nevertheless, to sustainably scale up the
process its environmental sustainability, which remains grossly
unknown, should also be identiﬁed. This is the main goal of this LCA
study. Speciﬁcally, bench-scale data from a batch experimental
procedure were used to comprehensively examine four process
parameters, i.e. contact time (mixing duration), magnesite dosage,
and initial wastewater pH and temperature. To the best of the au-
thors’ knowledge, this is the ﬁrst time that a comprehensive LCA
study dealing with process optimisation, when using South African
thermally activated cryptocrystalline magnesite, is carried out.
Furthermore, the results of this work provide insight into the
environmental sustainability of struvite recovery systems in LMICs,
which is largely under-reported in the literature (Sena and Hicks,
2018). As such, this manuscript makes available new LCA data for
an area where the existing body of knowledge is limited (e.g. in a
recent review paper by Lam et al. (2020) only 2 out of the 65
reviewed manuscripts were referring to Africa). It also provides
insight to decision- and policy-makers to promote and sustainably
scale up the process at village- or industrial-scale in South Africa,
Lesotho and further aﬁeld.
2. Materials and methods
First, the materials and methods for the struvite precipitation
experimental setup are discussed and then the methodology and
tools that were used for the environmental modelling are given.
2.1. Raw wastewater and magnesite rock collection
In the context of this work real municipal wastewater, rich in P
and N, was collected from a treatment facility in Pretoria, South
Africa and used for batch laboratory experiments. The collected
wastewater was pre-treated to remove solids and debris using
Macherey-Nagel ﬁlter papers (MN 615, Ø125mm). In scaled up
systems preliminary treatment, such as solid waste screening, will
be used. P and N concentrations, before and after treatment, were
measured by means of inductively coupled plasma mass spec-
trometry (ICP-MS) (Agilent 7500 Series, model 7500ce), while the
pH levels by a HANNA Multi-parameter probe (HI-9828 Multi-
Parameter Water Quality Portable Meter).
Regarding the source of magnesium oxide (MgO), this was ob-
tained from cryptocrystalline magnesite, a mineral abundant in
South
Africa
(Mavhungu
et
al.,
2019).
Speciﬁcally,
cryptocrystalline magnesite was hand-picked from an old (aban-
doned) mine in Folovhodwe, Limpopo Province, South Africa. In
scaled up systems large amounts of cryptocrystalline magnesite
will be required, and therefore the magnesite rock will be mined,
rather than hand-picked. For this reason, here magnesite was
assumed to be mined following the mining activities described
elsewhere (Cherubini et al., 2008). The morphology of the crypto-
crystalline magnesite comprises of irregular sheets homogenously
distributed across its surface (Magagane et al., 2019).
A. Mavhungu, S. Foteinis, R. Mbaya et al.
Journal of Cleaner Production 285 (2021) 124856
2

2.2. Thermal activation of the cryptocrystalline magnesite
MgO, which is required towards struvite formation, was pro-
duced by a fairly simple thermal activation process. Speciﬁcally, the
collected cryptocrystalline magnesite was ﬁrst milled into ﬁne
powder, by means of a vibratory ball miller (15 min at 500 rpm).
The milled cryptocrystalline magnesite was then thermally acti-
vated (calcined at 1000 C for 30 min) as to convert its magnesium
carbonate (MgCO3) content to MgO (Mavhungu et al., 2019). The
calcined magnesite was then remilled (15 min at 500 rpm) and
passed through a 32 mm perforated sieve to obtain the desired
particle sizes suitable for struvite precipitation. The thermally
activated magnesite (feed thereafter) is rich in MgO, while after
calcination new phases are formed in the feed, such as periclase,
brucite, and calcite (Masindi et al., 2016a; Mavhungu et al., 2019).
These will be dissolved in the wastewater, leading to an increase in
the pH of the supernatant (Masindi, 2017), which is favourable for
struvite precipitation (Mavhungu et al., 2019). After calcination the
morphology of raw magnesite changes to nanosheets with octag-
onal structures, i.e. calcination reduces the particle size of the non-
reactive magnesite and increases the surface area and reactive sites
(Magagane et al., 2019). Due to its large surface area and reactive
sites the feed has been also used for the remediation of acid mine
drainage (AMD) (Masindi et al., 2016a). The mineralogical charac-
teristics, along with the elemental and microstructural properties
of the feed can be found elsewhere (Masindi et al., 2016b, 2018b).
The feed was then used, at bench scale and in a batch experimental
procedure,
for
wastewater
treatment
and
towards
struvite
precipitation.
2.3. Environmental analysis
The main goal of this LCA study is to identify the environmental
performance and main environmental hotspots of a struvite pre-
cipitation system. The optimal conditions, in terms of pollutants
removal, have been identiﬁed elsewhere (Mavhungu et al., 2019),
but this is not the case for its environmental sustainability under
the South African setting. For this reason, the one-factor-at-a-time
(OFAT) method was used examining four process parameters
(Table 1). Even though, the OFAT method requires a relatively large
number of experiments to obtain exploitable results, and more
importantly cannot effectively estimate the interactions between
the examined parameters, as designed experiments do, it provides
a good estimate of the importance of each parameter and hence
was employed herein. OFAT results were used for environmental
modelling, through scenario analyses. It should be noted that
although for some of the values, such as the low pH, it is well
established that the process efﬁciency is very low (Mavhungu et al.,
2019), their effect on the environmental sustainability remains
grossly unknown and thus they were included in the analysis.
For environmental modelling, the LCA methodology was fol-
lowed, using the software programme SimaPro (version 9.1.0.11).
LCA can quantify environmental impacts attributed to a product,
process, or system, since it can trace and list material and energy
ﬂows throughout an examined life span. It has been widely used to
evaluate the environmental impacts of different wastewater
treatment systems (Sena and Hicks, 2018). Furthermore, the
effective removal of P and N from municipal wastewater, particu-
larly when considering the projected increase of nutrient dis-
charges
into
surface
water
(van
Puijenbroek
et
al.,
2019),
constitutes an environmental problem of emerging concern, both
in the developed and particularly in the developing world. There-
fore, the results of this work are of interest not only to researchers,
but also to decision- and policy-makers and the wastewater and
agricultural industry. The highlighted end users constitute the
intended audience of this LCA study.
The attributional, over the consequential LCA approach was
followed. The reason is that the ﬁrst estimates the environmental
impacts of a system attributed to the delivery of a speciﬁc amount
of a functional unit (FU), while the latter the environmental con-
sequences of a change in the production chain (Chatzisymeon et al.,
2017). Finally, the time-related coverage of this work refers to
present, i.e. 2020, and the geographical coverage includes South
Africa, Lesotho, and other LMICs that are rich in magnesite, as well
as the developed world where circular economy in wastewater
treatment is emerging as a promising management method.
2.3.1. Functional unit
The FU quantiﬁes the performance of the system under study by
normalising all input and output life cycle inventory (LCI) data
using the same reference. In order to provide context with the
literature, the FU of this LCA study was chosen as the treatment of
1 L of real wastewater. Volume is a commonly used FU for LCAs of
wastewater treatment plants (WWTPs), since their main purpose is
to treat inﬂuent wastewater (Sena and Hicks, 2018). Furthermore,
by using this FU the inﬂuence of each of the four examined pa-
rameters on the system’s environmental sustainability was iden-
tiﬁed, along with the main environmental hotspots.
However, depending on the examined parameter pollutants
removal efﬁciency largely varies, i.e. the ﬁnal characteristics of the
wastewater after treatment are not similar. For this reason, the total
environmental footprint of each OFAT run was also normalised per
mg L1 of P and N removed. Expressing results per P removed or
recovered is also commonly encountered in LCA studies dealing
with struvite recovery systems (Sena and Hicks, 2018). This nor-
malisation also enables the direct comparison of each OFAT run and
the identiﬁcation of the optimal conditions, in terms of environ-
mental relevance. It should be noted that laboratory experiments
were conducted in 500 mL volumetric ﬂasks and results were
extrapolated to the examined functional unit, i.e. 1 L of wastewater.
2.3.2. System boundary
The system boundary deﬁnes the smallest elements, i.e. unit
processes, for which input and output data are quantiﬁed in the LCI
analysis and thus are included in the LCA study. Here, all main in-
puts and outputs of the wastewater treatment process were
considered
(Schematic
1).
Speciﬁcally,
raw
cryptocrystalline
magnesite mining, as well as its transportation and processing (i.e.
energy, equipment used and associated emissions from magnesite
milling and calcination) are included in the analysis. This is also the
case for the reagents used to regulate the pH (NaOH and HCl), as
well as the energy required for stirring and wastewater tempera-
ture control. Ammonia airborne emissions, due to high tempera-
ture or pH which promote ammonia stripping, are included in the
analysis. Wastewater transportation to the laboratory, where the
batch experiments were carried out, is external to the system
boundary, since scaled up treatment systems are expected to be
within existing or included in newly build wastewater treatment
facilities.
The stirrer, as a material, is included in the analysis, while the
hotplate and the vibratory ball miller, as materials, are external to

is also included in the analysis. In general, the operational phase of
wastewater treatment processes is the main contributor to the
environmental impacts, compared to the construction phase

The feed and electricity inputs required for milling, mixing, and
temperature control are also included in the analysis. Finally, a
cradle to gate LCA was carried out and, as such, the ﬁnal use of the
treated efﬂuent and struvite are external to the system boundary
(Schematic 1). Speciﬁcally, the efﬂuent can be either released to the
environment, if it meets the required standards, or further treated
and reclaimed for various uses, such as for agriculture or even for
drinking purposes. Each route has its own environmental impacts
and beneﬁts, and therefore including a speciﬁc route will make the
LCA study case speciﬁc. This is also the case for the generated
sludge (struvite), where further treatment is required to produce
high quality fertiliser. For these reasons the treated efﬂuent and the
generated sludge (struvite) are external to the system boundary.
2.3.3. Life cycle inventory analysis
In LCI analysis, the resources, energy, and emissions entering
and leaving the system under study are listed. In this work, primary
LCI data were collected from batch laboratory experiments and
used towards the environmental modelling. After the total envi-
ronmental footprint of each examined value was quantiﬁed per FU
and the main environmental hotspots were identiﬁed, results were
then used for identifying the optimal conditions, in terms of envi-
ronmental relevance. This was achieved by normalising them with
the corresponding pollutant removal efﬁciency, i.e. expressing re-
sults per mg L1 of P and N removed.
For environmental modelling, LCI data from SimaPro’s pro-
prietary databases were used, with the preferred option being
ecoinvent v3.6 database (Table 2). In cases where relevant data
were not identiﬁed in SimaPro’s databases, proxy LCI data were
collected from the literature. Speciﬁcally, for the stirrer LCI data
were adapted from Grifﬁths et al. (2013), assuming a 10 year life-
span. Furthermore, the wastewater’s speciﬁc heat capacity was
considered to be 4.18 kJ per kilogram per degree Kelvin or Celsius,
i.e. it takes 4180 J to raise the temperature of 1 kg of wastewater by
1 C or K (McConnell and Tolley, 2015). The energy required to
account for heat losses was assumed to be 50% of the speciﬁc heat
capacity per hour. Following (de Bakker, 2014) it was assumed that
the speciﬁc energy required for the milling of 1 kg of magnesite is
60 Wh, when using a stirred media detritor. The rated power
output of the overhead stirrer was 8.4 W, assuming it operated at
4 W, i.e. not at full power, when treating 1 L of wastewater. The
electricity input considered throughout this work was assumed to
originate from South Africa’s fossil fuel-dependent energy mix, i.e.
~85% hard coal according to ecoinvent database.
For the vials, ecoinvent’s data for borosilicate glass tube were
used, assuming a 5 year lifespan. After the end of their useful life-
time a 70% recycling and a 30% disposal, as inert waste, was
considered. For magnesite mining, literature data were used
(Cherubini et al., 2008), while the transportation distance ascribed
to raw magnesite, i.e. from the place that is it mined to the place
that is thermally activated and used for struvite precipitation, was
500 km (i.e. from Folovhodwe to Pretoria, South Africa). Trans-
portation was assumed to be carried out by means of a diesel-
powered truck with emissions standard EURO 3. Following
(Kr€ahenbühl et al., 2016) the energy consumption for raw magne-
site calcination, i.e. at 1,000 C for 30 min, was approximated at
0.47 kWh per kg of cryptocrystalline magnesite. For the furnace
used for calcination LCI data were acquired from the literature
corresponding to a high temperature furnace (Grifﬁths et al., 2013),
assuming a 10 year lifespan. The efﬁciency of the furnace was
assumed to be 70%.
Furthermore, during magnesite calcination mass loss takes
place, attributed to the conversion of MgCO3 to MgO and leading to
CO2 volatilization. Here, 50% of the raw cryptocrystalline magnesite
was considered to be volatilized, which is in line with existing
literature (Kr€ahenbühl et al., 2016). The corresponding CO2

emissions are included in the analysis, which is also the case for the
emissions of ammonia which is attributed to high pH and tem-
perature values. Speciﬁcally, N evaporates to air at temperatures
higher than 30 C and at pH values higher than 10 (Sutiyono et al.,
2016). In order to account for these losses, it was assumed that 50%
of the additional N removal, compared to N removal for pH 10 and
for ambient temperature (i.e. 35 C) respectively, were considered
as ammonia airborne emissions. Land use and land use change
were not taken into account, since when the process will be scaled
up it would require a limited space to operate and is also not ex-
pected to cause land use change. The chemicals used for pH
adjustment, i.e. 0.1 mol L1 NaOH and 0.1 mol L1 HCl, were taken
directly from ecoinvent database, however insigniﬁcant amounts,
of the order of mL L1 per pH range, were used. The LCI data and
their corresponding sources are summarised in Table 2.
2.3.4. Life cycle impact assessment
In the life cycle impact assessment (LCIA) stage the collected LCI
data are associated with speciﬁc environmental impacts/damages
and an understanding of those impacts/damages is attempted. In
this work, ReCiPe 2016 (version 1.04), a robust and widely used
multi-issue LCIA method, was employed. The ReCiPe model ex-
presses results both at midpoint level, where environmental im-
pacts are examined earlier in the cause-effect chain, and at
endpoint level, where environmental impacts are translated into
issues of concern (e.g. effect on human health) and thus are
examined at the end of the cause-effect chain (Ioannou-Ttofa et al.,
2017). The endpoint or damage-oriented approach is associated
with higher levels of statistical uncertainty, compared to the
midpoint or problem-oriented approach, due to data gaps and as-
sumptions stacking up along the cause-effect chain. However,
endpoint results are easier to communicate to decision- and policy-
makers and the general public, while they also provide a common
reference for comparison between different processes/systems
(Chatzisymeon et al., 2017).
In order to reach endpoint, ReCiPe converts and aggregates
midpoint impact categories into three damage categories, namely,
damage to human health, damage to ecosystem diversity, and
damage to resource availability, and these can be further aggre-
gated into a single score. The single score approach is used here to
identify the total environmental footprint of each examined
parameter and the main environmental hotspots. In addition, the
net impacts approach was also preliminary examined, focusing on
the beneﬁts from reducing the eutrophication potential of receiving
water bodies, which is directly related to P and N removal. To this
end, ReCiPe was applied at midpoint level examining two impact
categories, i.e. freshwater eutrophication (P enrichment of fresh-
water) and marine eutrophication (N enrichment of seawater).
Finally, average technology was assumed and the Hierarchist
(H) perspective was employed, using the normalisation values of
the world and average weighting (i.e. world ReCiPe H/A).
3. Results and discussion
This is a comparative LCA study with the main goals being to
identify the effect of operating parameters and highlight optimal
conditions from the environmental perspective. For this reason, the
total environmental footprint (per FU) of each examined parameter
was ﬁrst estimated, as to identify the effect of operating parame-
ters, while the optimal treatment conditions were estimated by
normalising results per mg L1 of P and N removed.
3.1. Effect of operating parameters
First the effect of contact time was examined. Eleven mixing
durations, ranging from as low as 1 min to as high as 300 min
(Table 1), were considered, ceteris paribus pH (~7.5), temperature
(~35 C), and feed dosage (2 g L1). Results are shown in Fig. 1,
where it is observed that the higher the contact time the higher the
environmental footprint. Speciﬁcally, when treatment time in-
creases so does the electricity input required for wastewater mixing
(or for wastewater pumping in scaled up systems) leading to
increased environmental footprints. However, in the ﬁrst three
examined contact times, i.e. 1, 5, and 10 min, the mixing duration
has a relatively small effect on the system’s environmental sus-
tainability, i.e. the total environmental of 10 min is around 27%
