3.2. Economic analysis 
Table 3 shows the PHB production costs for each scenario. The total 
capital cost of the system was 406,868 € for all scenarios (Table 2). The 
most expensive part of the systems was the major equipment (157,554 €, 
39% of the total capital investment costs). The equipment with the 
higher costs were the photobioreactors (50,999 €, 13% of the total 
capital investment costs) and the evaporator (49,900 €, 12% of the total 
capital investment costs). After major equipment, the installation costs 
(74,050 €, 18% of the total capital investment costs) and the instru­
mentation costs (55,144 €, 14% of the total capital investment costs) 
were the categories that contributed the most to the capital costs. These

results were similar to the ones obtained by [6,43], who reported that 
the cost of the cultivation equipment has the highest contribution to the 
total capital costs. However, PHB extraction equipment costs had a 
much lower contribution in previous studies (< 10% of the total capital 
costs [6,43] compared to 25% of the total capital costs in this study). 
These differences may be attributed to the extraction method used in 
this study and the smaller size of the system considered. 
Regarding the operational and maintenance costs, in all the scenarios 
the labour and maintenance expenses were the highest operation costs 
(26% and 16% of the total operational and maintenance costs, respec­
tively). These results were in contradiction with previous studies on 
cyanobacteria PHB production costs, where labour accounted for less 
than 4% of the production costs [6,43]. Nevertheless, other studies on 
microalgae biomass production predicted a similar contribution. For 
instance, Aci´en, et al., [44] obtained a labour contribution of a 51.6%. 
Differences between studies may be attributed to the number of workers 
and the salary considered. For example, Aci´en, et al., [44] considered 3 
workers for a production surface of 0.04 ha, while Panuschka, et al., [43] 
considered only 1 worker for 0.45 ha. Moreover, the labour 
requirements and the system size are not linear [34]. Therefore, the 
bigger the system the lower the ratio between the number of workers 
and the system surface. 
Fig. 3A shows the effect of varying the PHB content from 15 to 
50%dcw on the PHB breakeven price. The increase in the PHB content 
strongly decreases the PHB breakeven prices from 449 € kgPHB−1 to 135 
€ kgPHB−1. On the other hand, very similar results are obtained when 
wastewater or BG-11 are used as nutrients source. 
These results showed the necessity to increase PHB productivity to 
make this process competitive. To the best of the authors' knowledge, 
the maximum PHB productivity obtained has been 59 mg L−1 d−1 [19]. 
Considering this productivity, a breakeven selling price of 38.5 € 
kgPHB−1 was obtained (Fig. 3B). This is still 9.6 folds higher than PHB 
current market price. According to our calculations, a PHB productivity 
of 810 mg L−1 d−1 would be necessary to obtain a PHB selling price of 4 € 
kgPHB−1 (PHB market price) (Fig. 3B). If this productivity is reached, 
the use of wastewater as a nutrient source would reduce the PHB price 
by 29% (2.9 € kgPHB−1) compared to the standard grow medium. 
Slightly lower PHB production costs were obtained previously using

scenarios using BG-11 or wastewater as a nutrient source. A biomass productivity of 25 mg L−1 d−1 was assumed in graph A.

Sustainable Materials and Technologies 35 (2023) e00579
10
other cultivation systems. For instance, Panuschka, et al., [43], found 
that the minimum selling price for PHB was 24 € kg−1 using a thin-layer- 
photobioreactor and a 60% PHB content. Price, et al., [6] predicted a 
minimum PHB selling price of 18.3 € kg−1 using an open raceway pond 
producing 10,000 tons per year. Moreover, they observed that the 
application of other strategies (e.g. pigment extraction, the use of 
wastewater as a nutrient source, use of flocculants, use of solar power, 
the digestion of residual biomass, the use of deep ripening ponds, the use 
of CO2 from anaerobic digestion) reduced the PHB producing price up to 
7.7 € kg−1. Differences between studies may be attributed to the type of 
reactor used (thin-layer, open raceway pond or vertical column), the 
design scale (4500 m2, 182 ha or 150 m2) and the PHB productivity 
considered (23.1 g m−2 d−1, 15 g m−2 d−1 or 8.3 g m−2 d−1) [6,43]. 
As previously stated, the results obtained in this study clearly showed 
the need to increase the PHB content and biomass productivity. Recent 
studies achieved up to 81%dcw of PHB using genetically modified mi­
croorganisms and photoheterotrophic growth conditions [22]. 
Regarding biomass productivity, the maximum theoretical biomass 
productivity (i.e. considering that all the cells in the reactor receive all 
the photons they can uptake) is 77 g m−2 d−1 (photosynthetic efficiency 
of 8–10%) [45]. Considering that the Volume:Surface ratio of the re­
actors used in this study was 666 L m−2, a maximum theoretical biomass 
productivity of 116 mgVSS L−1 d−1 would be possible, which will be 
lower than the previously calculated productivity (1000 mgVSS L−1 d−1) 
needed to reduce the PHB production costs to 4 € kg−1. However, if 
reactors had a lower Volume:Surface ratio, higher productivities could 
be obtained. For instance, Panuschka, et al., [43], used a thin-layer 
reactor with a Volume:Surface ratio of 0.01 m3 m−2. In that case, the 
maximum theoretical biomass productivity would be 7700 mgVSS L−1 
d−1. It can be therefore concluded that using appropriate reactors (low 
Volume:Surface ratio) and genetically improved strains would enhance 
the economic feasibility of PHB production from cyanobacteria. Another 
possible strategy to reduce PHB production costs and environmental 
impacts would be to produce PHB in a biorefinery concept and recover 
other bioproducts of industrial interest, such as pigments, lipids, or 
exopolysaccharides. For instance, Price, et al. (2022) [6] observed that 
the production of pigments together with PHB would reduce the PHB 
selling price from 18 $ kg−1 to 13.9 $ kg−1. However, experimental 
studies on the production of different bioproducts together with PHB 
using cyanobacteria are still very scarce [46]. 
3.2.1. Environmental impacts of improved and economically feasible 
scenarios 
To assess the effect of increased productivity on the environmental 
impact, the LCA was carried out considering two additional scenarios 
using high productivities and BG-11 or wastewater (Fig. S4), as 
described in Section 2.2.1. In these improved scenarios, the environ­
mental impacts were strongly reduced (57–97%) in all impact categories 
compared to the previously considered scenarios. As for the previous 
analysis, the use of wastewater as a nutrient source slightly reduced 
(1–19%) the environmental impacts in both scenarios. 
In both improved scenarios, the chemicals needed for cyanobacteria 
cultivation contributed the most to the overall environmental impact in 
all the impact categories except for Stratospheric Ozone Depletion. This 
was mainly attributed to the use of commercial CO2. Therefore, if flue 
CO2 would be used, the environmental impacts of this process could be 
further reduced. Similarly to previous scenarios, in the Stratospheric 
Ozone Depletion impact category, the environmental impact was mainly 
caused by the use of chemicals for PHB extraction in both scenarios. 
All in all, PHB production from cyanobacteria are competitive in 
most of the impact categories (see supplementary materials). However, 
process improvements are required, especially in PHB purification, 
photobioreactor design and PHB productivity. 
4. Conclusions 
The environmental analysis, carried out using the Life Cycle 
Assessment methodology, showed that increasing the PHB content in the 
biomass drastically reduce (between 67 and 75%) the environmental 
impacts of the process. On the other hand, using wastewater instead of 
standard growth medium slightly reduced the environmental impacts 
(up to 20%). Environmental impacts were mainly caused by construc­
tion materials and the use of chemicals (especially chloroform for PHB 
purification). 
The results of the economic evaluation showed that not only PHB 
content influenced the minimum PHB selling price, but also the biomass 
productivity. A PHB productivity of 810 mg L−1 d−1 would be necessary 
to obtain a PHB selling price of 4 € kgPHB−1 (PHB market price). If this 
productivity is obtained, the use of wastewater instead of standard 
growth medium will further reduce the PHB price to 2.9 € kgPHB−1. 
Using a well-designed reactor (low Volume:Surface ratio) and geneti­
cally improved strains would enhance the economic feasibility of PHB 
production from cyanobacteria.

records.full_text:
Can wastewater feed cities? Determining the feasibility and
environmental burdens of struvite recovery and reuse for
urban regions
Martí Rufí-Salís a,b, Nadin Brunnhofer a, Anna Petit-Boix c, Xavier Gabarrell a,b,
Albert Guisasola b,d, Gara Villalba a,b,⁎
a Sostenipra Research Group (SGR 01412), Institut de Ciència i Tecnologia Ambientals (ICTA-UAB) (MDM-2015-0552), Z Building, Universitat Autònoma de Barcelona (UAB), Campus UAB, 08193
Bellaterra, Barcelona, Spain
b Department of Chemical, Biological and Environmental Engineering, XRB, Universitat Autònoma de Barcelona (UAB), Campus UAB, 08193 Bellaterra, Barcelona, Spain
c Chair of Societal Transition and Circular Economy, University of Freiburg, Tennenbacher Str. 4, 79106 Freiburg i. Br., Germany
d GENOCOV, Departament d'Enginyeria Química, Biològica i Ambiental, Escola d'Enginyeria, Universitat Autònoma de Barcelona, 08193, Bellaterra, Barcelona, Spain
H I G H L I G H T S
• Scenarios of struvite recovery and reuse
are quantiﬁed for an urban region.
• Some WWTPs should be modiﬁed to re-
cover P, like the inclusion of EBPR.
• Agricultural P demand of Barcelona re-
gion is met with only one WWTP.
• Eutrophication potential of WWTPs is
mitigated through P recovery technolo-
gies.
• Impacts of electricity and chemical use
of WWTPs as the target to optimize
G R A P H I C A L
A B S T R A C T
a b s t r a c t
a r t i c l e
i n f o
Article history:
Received 30 March 2020
Received in revised form 25 May 2020
Accepted 26 May 2020
Available online 29 May 2020
Editor: Damia Barcelo
Phosphorus (P) resources are decreasing at an alarming rate due to global fertilizer use and insufﬁcient nu-
trient recovery strategies. Currently, more circular approaches are promoted, such as recovering P from
wastewater in the form of struvite. This is especially attractive for urban areas, where there is a growing
trend of local crop production and large volumes of wastewater are treated in centralized wastewater treat-
ment plants (WWTPs). This research aims to assess the technical and environmental feasibility of applying a
struvite recovery and reuse strategy to meet the P requirements to fertilize the agricultural ﬁelds of an
urban region. To do so, we analyze the potential P recovery and the environmental impacts of integrating
three recovery technologies (REM-NUT®, Ostara® and AirPrex®) in the two biggest WWTPs of the Àrea
Metropolitana de Barcelona. The results show that all technologies are able to recover between 5 and 30
times the amount of P required to fertilize the agricultural area of the region annually (36.5 t). As can be ex-
pected, including P recovery technologies result in additional impacts per m3 of wastewater due to in-
creased electricity consumption and chemicals required for the struvite precipitation. However, struvite
recovery results in less eutrophication potential, especially in the REM-NUT® case, with an average reduc-
tion of 5.4 times. On the other hand, Ostara®, that recovers P from the digestate, had the lowest impacts (9
kgCO2eq/kgP), even compared to the production of mineral fertilizer. When we apply our ﬁndings to the
whole region, we can see that chemical use for struvite precipitation and energy consumption during the
Keywords:
Life cycle assessment
Phosphorus recovery
Urban agriculture
Nutrient recirculation
Fertilizer substitution
Circular economy

1. Introduction
Phosphorus (P) is a macronutrient essential for plant growth, being
uptaken in H2PO4
−and HPO4
2−forms. Since only a small fraction of P
content in soil is readily available to plants, it needs to be added artiﬁ-
cially through mineral fertilizers in order to sustain global food produc-
tion (Elser and Bennett, 2011; Sarvajayakesavalu et al., 2018). Currently,
P is primarily obtained from phosphate rocks, with 80% of the available
stock used in the production of fertilizers (Shu et al., 2006). Considering
that phosphate deposits are renewed in a scale of geologic time
(Childers et al., 2011) and that unlike N, P doesn't have a mechanism
to move from marine to terrestrial stocks (Elser and Bennett, 2011),
mineral P resources are likely to be depleted in the 21st century
(Steen, 1998). Thus, given the scientiﬁc consensus on the depleting na-
ture of P (Rittmann et al., 2011) and the risks it poses to food systems,
sustaining P reserves has already reached the political agenda. Since
2014, the European Union recognizes P as a critical resource
(European Commission, 2014) and encourages P recovery from local
sources by enforcing a shift towards a more circular use of nutrients in
a planned amendment of the fertilizer regulation (European Commis-
sion, 2016).
Within the P biogeochemical cycle, humans excrete around 3 MMT
of P per year (Childers et al., 2011), accounting for 20% of the annual
worldwide phosphate fertilizer consumption (Elser and Bennett,
2011). According to Elser and Bennett (2011), only 10% of this ﬂow is
returned to agriculture. Considering the ongoing tendency of increasing
population in cities (SEDAC, 2016; United Nations, 2004), wastewater
treatment plants (WWTPs) have risen as potential facilities to recover
P in urban areas. In this sense, WWTPs have already been addressed
as a potential alternative to importing mineral fertilizer (e.g. de-
Bashan and Bashan, 2004; Kern et al., 2008; Shu et al., 2006). Local P re-
covery could thus improve the metabolism of urban areas through re-
source circulation. Given that urban and suburban areas are hotspots
of food consumption (FAO, 2018) and wastewater production
(Martínez et al., 2000), recovering P from WWTPs could reduce the en-
vironmental burdens of the water-food nexus. In other words, recov-
ered P could be applied to urban and periurban agricultural land and
feed an increasing population through enhanced wastewater treatment.
P can be recovered from wastewater in different forms, being mag-
nesium
ammonium
phosphate
(MAP
with
the
formula
NH4MgPO4·6H2O), commonly called struvite, one of the most promi-
nent. Struvite precipitates in a molar ratio of magnesium (Mg2+), am-
monium (NH4
+) and phosphate (PO4
3−) of 1:1:1 and under suitable pH
conditions (8.5–9.5) (Bouropoulos and Koutsoukos, 2000; Buchanan
et al., 1994; Le Corre et al., 2009). Currently, spontaneous struvite crys-
tallization is problematic in WWTPs. Especially after anaerobic diges-
tion, it creates a layer on the surface of pumps, centrifuges and pipes
that reduces operational efﬁciency and increases maintenance costs
(Doyle et al., 2003). In this sense, controlled P recovery seems a promis-
ing solution to prevent spontaneous struvite formation while increasing
resource circularity in cities.
Research has proven that struvite's fertilizer efﬁciency is comparable
to that of conventional fertilizers (Egle et al., 2016; Kataki et al., 2016;
Yetilmezsoy et al., 2017), whereas the heavy metal application to soil
can be reduced signiﬁcantly (Antonini et al., 2012; Latiﬁan et al., 2012;
Nziguheba and Smolders, 2008). Additionally, an improved efﬂuent
quality in WWTPs achieved through struvite recovery will decrease
the direct adverse effects of eutrophication in the receiving aquatic en-
vironment, thus preventing dead waters (Le Corre et al., 2009).
The environmental assessment of P recovery technologies is cru-
cial to align the improvement in environmental performance with
increased resource circularity. As pointed out by Sena and Hicks
(2018), further research is needed to have a comprehensive under-
standing of the environmental performance of struvite recovery pro-
cesses. Amann et al. (2018) shed some light on this topic, analyzing
the environmental performance of different P recovery technologies.
Moreover, Bradford-Hartke et al. (2015) highlights the need to in-
clude multiple impact indicators to select the most appropriate P re-
covery method.
Until now, we can ﬁnd different studies on the environmental as-
sessment of struvite recovery as a potential fertilizer. Bradford-Hartke
et al. (2015) and Amann et al. (2018) performed isolated life cycle as-
sessments (LCA) with and without case studies, respectively. A LCA re-
view of struvite precipitation was done by Sena and Hicks (2018), while
Rufí-Salís et al. (2020) assessed the potential environmental impacts of
precipitating struvite from agricultural wastewater streams compared
to other nutrient recovery strategies. On the other hand, only a few
studies have assessed struvite application on agricultural crops. Exam-
ples are Antonini et al. (2012) for ryegrass and maize or Liu et al.
(2011) for maize. A review of struvite application tests can be found
in Li et al. (2019). Moreover, there are even fewer studies that include
both struvite recovery and reuse, such as Uysal et al. (2014) or Gell
et al. (2011). However, these studies have focused only on the perfor-
mance of crops with struvite applications.
In this sense, the literature is still missing the environmental
evaluation of struvite recovery and reuse strategies on a larger
scale. Cordell et al. (2011) state that there are still research gaps re-
lated to the life cycle of P recovery and reuse systems, and how these
compare to the use of mineral fertilizers. Additionally, the design of
sustainable phosphorus recovery systems should consider parame-
ters such as climate change or eutrophication (Cordell et al., 2011).
Ashley et al. (2011) add that P recovery and reuse systems must
be region-speciﬁc to guarantee they are appropriate to local
conditions.
Hence, this study aims to add to this pool of knowledge by quantify-
ing the environmental burdens and beneﬁts of regional struvite recov-
ery and reuse to feed agricultural ﬁelds for urban food production. To
do so, we assess the P recovery potential from wastewater generated
in the metropolitan area of Barcelona to meet the P requirements of
the agricultural ﬁelds in the urban and peri-urban metropolitan area.
In this sense, we analyze three different recovery technologies that
could be integrated into the two main WWTPs, which treat 80% of the
wastewater produced by the 5 million residents of the AMB. We apply
LCA to estimate the impacts of refurbishing the existing WWTPs with
necessary equipment and the potential beneﬁts of substituting mineral
fertilizer consumption with struvite in agricultural areas of the entire
metropolitan region.
2. Methodology
The steps proposed in this study are detailed in the following sec-
tions. Section 2.1 describes the system, its main WWTPs and how to
calculate its P demand. Section 2.2 sets the basis to evaluate the P re-
covery and reuse potential by describing the technologies and their
major implications regarding the actual state of the WWTPs.
Section 3 describes three different approaches to environmentally
assess the system.

2.1. Characterization of the system
The metropolitan area of Barcelona (Àrea Metropolitana de Barce-
lona (AMB)) is the study area under analysis. The AMB is the region
around the city of Barcelona and comprises 36 municipalities (Fig. 1).
To characterize the system before proceeding with the feasibility and
environmental assessment, we need to understand two main vectors,
described in the following sections.
2.1.1. Generation and treatment of wastewater
AMB generates a yearly wastewater volume of 278.4 million of m3
(2018). This wastewater is treated by 7 WWTPs (Table A1 – Supple-
mentary Material). Two of these WWTPs (Llobregat and Besós –
Fig. 2) treat almost 80% of the total wastewater and were therefore cho-
sen for the analysis. Both plants were analyzed in terms of technical
data, material ﬂows and possibilities for P recovery.
A detailed understanding of the WWTP conﬁguration is required to
determine the feasibility of P recovery. The current status of the
WWTP is used to deﬁne the Business-as-Usual (BaU) or baseline sce-
nario. Some key questions to consider are: How complex is the treat-
ment? Is there an Enhanced Biological Phosphorus Removal (EBPR)
module? Are there nitriﬁcation-denitriﬁcation processes?
El Prat de Llobregat WWTP (hereinafter Llobregat WWTP) is lo-
cated in the municipality of El Prat de Llobregat, close to the
Llobregat river. This WWTP treated 94.4 million m3 in 2018,
representing 34% of the total wastewater treated in the AMB. After
passing through pretreatment and primary treatment phases,
water is treated in a biological reactor with organic matter and nutri-
ent removal (70% of total nitrogen (N) and P). Removal of N and P
was assumed to occur through nitriﬁcation/denitriﬁcation and
EBPR processes, respectively. Afterwards, the ﬂow is split into
treated water and sludge using the secondary settler. After this
step, water is discharged into the sea though a marine outfall. The
sludge obtained in the primary and secondary decanters is
dewatered to obtain at least 6% of solid matter. Afterwards, an anaer-
obic digester is used to produce biogas which is subsequently burned
to obtain electricity and heat to power the WWTP facility. Llobregat
WWTP also has a water regeneration system that can purify the
water with the purpose of reusing it through inﬁltration in an aqui-
fer. This step includes ultraviolet disinfection and ultraﬁltration
and reverses osmosis membranes, among others. A scheme of
Llobregat WWTP is shown in Fig. A1 of the Supplementary Material.
Besós WWTP is located in the municipality of Sant Adrià de Besós,
close to the Besós river. This WWTP treated 125.5 million m3 in 2018,
representing 45% of the total wastewater treated in the AMB. After pass-
ing through pretreatment and primary treatment phases, water is
treated in a biological reactor with organic matter removal and the
ﬂow is split into treated water and sludge using the secondary decanter.
After this step, water is discharged into the sea through a marine outfall.
The sludge obtained in the primary and secondary decanters is
dewatered to obtain at least 6% of solid matter. A scheme of Besós
WWTP is shown in Fig. A2 of the Supplementary Material.
2.1.2. P demand for crop production
The P demand of the region needs to be quantiﬁed to determine the
regional feasibility of struvite recovery and reuse. To do so, the best
available data is used, namely regional nutrient requirements of the re-
gion reported by the public administration. However, the reported land
coverage might not align with the scale of the assessment, which can be
solved through two different approaches. If data for smaller regional/
urban units are available, aggregation is possible. Alternatively, data
on fertilizer application per unit of area at any resolution can be com-
bined with data on productive agricultural area. We used the second
approach.
Geographic Information System (GIS) data to quantify urban and
peri-urban agricultural areas was retrieved from the 2019 Declaració
Única Agrària (DUN) provided by the Catalan Department for Agricul-
ture, Livestock, Fisheries and Food (DARPA-GENCAT, 2019). The DUN
is an annual declaration that requires every owner of an agricultural
holding to provide information on their agricultural activities. This in-
formation includes the size of the agricultural holding and the crops
that are being harvested. The area of every polygon was veriﬁed using
the “$area” function in the “ﬁeld calculator” from the QGIS open access
software. The data was ﬁltered using QGIS for the research area includ-
ing all 36 municipalities. Polygons within, intersecting or overlapping
with the municipalities' borders were included in the ﬁnal layer.
Data for the calculations of the fertilizer demand in urban and peri-
urban agriculture was taken from a study done by the Center for
Agro-food Economy and Development (Mercadé et al., 2010) in collab-
oration with the Catalan Department for Agriculture. To calculate the

2.2. Evaluation of P recovery and reuse potential
P recovery technologies have to be evaluated based on the features
of the existing facilities (Section 2.1). Different criteria can be applied
for selecting these technologies, such as full-scale operationality, mod-
ule of the plant where the P will be extracted or recovery efﬁciencies,
among others. In this step we quantify the extent to which the selected
technologies are able to cover the P demand of the region when imple-
mented at the WWTP scale. Considering the recovery efﬁciency of each
technology, we determine the range of P recovery and compare it with
the P demand of the region.
Three available phosphorus recovery technologies were selected to
determine the recovery potential of the AMB: REM-NUT® (Petruzzelli
et al., 2004), Ostara® (Ostara, 2019) and AirPrex® (CNP-Technology
Water GmbH and Biosolids, 2017). These were selected based on two
criteria. Firstly, they recover P at different stages of the wastewater
treatment process, thus having different recovery efﬁciencies, as well
as different chemicals and energy requirements. Secondly, their product
is only MAP struvite. Other technologies can generate other products.
Because our aim is to determine the MAP struvite recovery potential,
such technologies were left out of the analysis. Moreover, calcium phos-
phates were also left out because of the wide range of possible species,
e.g. hydroxyapatite (HAP), amorphous calcium phosphate (ACP) or
brushite (Angel, 1999; Egle et al., 2015; Maurer et al., 1999).
The REM-NUT® process is an ion-exchange-based process that can
recover P in the form of MAP struvite from the secondary treated efﬂu-
ent (Petruzzelli et al., 2004). By adding Mg2+ to comply with stoichio-
metric ratio, struvite is precipitated. As pointed out by Petruzzelli et al.
(2004), the main drawback of this process is an excess of NH4
+ with re-
spect to PO4
3−. Therefore, a prior nitriﬁcation-denitriﬁcation process re-
moving the excess of NH4
+ in the secondary treatment would balance
the species required for struvite precipitation. Other limitations of the
REM-NUT® process are high economic costs compared to other recov-
ery technologies, mainly due to the need for resins and chemicals
(Egle et al., 2016). Different recovery ratios from WWTP inﬂuent can
be found in the literature: 45–60% (Amann et al., 2018) or up to 70%
(Egle et al., 2015; Liberti et al., 1986). Since the goal is to concentrate
P in the secondary treated efﬂuent, the EBPR in Llobregat WWTP,
which accumulates P in the purged sludge, should be inoperative.
The Ostara® process requires a speciﬁc reactor and chemicals with
Mg2+ and NH4
+ to precipitate struvite from the digestate. Therefore,
an EBPR module is needed in the water line to accumulate P in the
purged sludge. Reported P recovery ratios range from 10% to 25%
(Amann et al., 2018; Egle et al., 2015).
The AirPrex® process recovers P from the digested sewage sludge
prior to sludge dewatering (CNP-Technology Water GmbH and Bio-
solids, 2017). Mg2+ is added via magnesium chloride (MgCl2) before
entering the AirLift reactor, where intense aeration is added by CO2
stripping to increase the pH, producing struvite crystals at the bottom
of the reactor. Different recovery ratios of WWTP Inﬂuent can be
found in the literature: 10–25% (Amann et al., 2018) or 10–15% (Egle
et al., 2015).
In our study, the existing WWTPs may not be prepared to allocate
these technologies. Table 1 shows the BaU scenario and the required
changes for their implementation in Llobregat and Besós WWTPs. To
quantify the P recovery range for each technology, the minimum and
the conservative maximum efﬁciency values were used.

2.3. Life cycle assessment
The environmental impacts are analyzed through LCA (ISO
14040:2016). The following steps aim to environmentally asses the sys-
tem from different perspectives, functional units and system boundaries
(Fig. 2). In this sense, Sections 2.3.1, 2.3.2 and 2.3.3 have their own goal
and scope and inventories.
Although high impacts reported by previous literature related to di-
rect emissions from WWTP operation of CH4 and N2O (Lorenzo-Toja
et al., 2016), they are not included in this study due to the lack of data
regarding the nitrogen balance of the assessed WWTP and the measure-
ment of direct emissions. Moreover, the construction and demolition
phase of new infrastructure was left out of the system, due to lack of
data and since previous studies showed minor relevance for this item
(Lundin et al., 2000; Machado et al., 2007), or that only becomes rele-
vant for low-tech or speciﬁc processes (Corominas et al., 2013).
2.3.1. Preliminary environmental evaluation
The goal of this step is to have a ﬁrst overview on the environmental
and technical feasibility of applying the technologies in the WWTPs. In
this sense, we need to ﬁrstly analyze if a WWTP should be upgraded if
a speciﬁc technology were to be applied in its BaU conﬁguration, ac-
cording to Table 1. For example, if the aim is to precipitate P from the
digestate, an EBPR module is likely needed. Therefore, if the WWTP
under assessment already has an EBPR, the environmental performance
of the P recovery and the BaU conﬁguration will be similar. In this case,
the impact increase will be minimum, only strictly related to electricity
and chemicals consumption of the struvite precipitation process.
On the other hand, if additional modules are needed, the impacts of
the WWTP will increase signiﬁcantly. This will be the case for impact
categories where the electricity consumption can have a great relevancy
in terms of impact contribution, such as global warming or cumulative
energy demand. On the other hand, we will likely see impacts decreas-
ing in impact categories beneﬁting from the extraction of both N and P
from the ﬁnal efﬂuent, such as freshwater and marine eutrophication.
In this sense, since the goal of this preliminary evaluation is to see
whether a technology is suitable within the operation of an existing
WWTP (business-as-usual conﬁguration), we use 1 m3 of wastewater
as the functional unit, which strictly refers to the function of cleaning
wastewater. The system boundaries of this step include the electricity
and chemicals used by the WWTP considering also the additional mod-
ules plus the chemical precipitation processes, the transport of all neces-
sary chemicals, the N and P direct emissions to water and the direct
emissions to air caused by biogas cogeneration (if applicable).
2.3.2. Evaluation of struvite recovery and application
After analyzing the technologies based on their performance within
the WWTP, these are compared based on their ultimate function: to re-
cover and apply P in the form of struvite. Thus, the functional unit in this
step is 1 kg of P. The aim of this step is to analyze the technologies based
on their performance in a speciﬁc WWTP, and to identify their
impacting hotspots. In parallel, in this step we consider struvite as a P
resource. Therefore, the production of 1 kg of P in the form of struvite
can be compared to the production of 1 kg of P in the form of mineral
fertilizer. To calculate the application impacts of the BaU, we assumed
that P was applied in the form of Diammonium Phosphate (DAP). DAP
was chosen as the baseline fertilizer as it is the most used P-fertilizer
worldwide (IPNI, 2007).
The system boundaries of this step include the electricity and
chemicals used by the process of chemical precipitation, the transport
of these chemicals, the transport of the struvite to the agricultural ﬁelds
and the direct N2O emissions to air and heavy metals to soil caused by
fertilization. In the BaU scenario, we included the production of DAP,
its transport to the agricultural ﬁelds as well as the direct N2O emissions
to air and heavy metals to soil caused by fertilization.
2.3.3. Scale up
After assessing the technologies with a functional unit of 1 kg P, we
expand the system and consider the entire region. With the aim of ana-
lyzing the environmental performance of the recovery and reuse of all
required P of the region, the functional unit combines the yearly waste-
water treatment of the AMB (considering the Besós and Llobregat
WWTPs) and meeting the P demand as fertilizer in the region. The
BaU scenario in this step consists on the BaU conﬁguration of the
WWTP described in Section 2.1 and the production and use of DAP. If
the technologies that recover more P than the actual regional demand,
this P is transformed into avoided impacts in terms of the P-mineral fer-
tilizer considered in the BaU scenario. Here, we assume that the excess
amount of struvite will be exported to bordering regions with more ag-
ricultural ﬁelds and less population.
The system boundaries of this step include the annual electricity and
chemicals used by the WWTP considering the additional modules and
the chemical precipitation processes, the transport of all necessary
chemicals, the N and P direct emissions to water, the direct emissions
to air caused by biogas cogeneration (if applicable), the transport of
the struvite to the agricultural ﬁelds and the direct N2O emissions to
air and heavy metals to soil. In the BaU scenario, we included the pro-
duction of DAP, its transport to the agricultural ﬁelds and the direct
N2O emissions to air and heavy metals to soil caused by fertilization.
2.3.4. Data sources for the life cycle inventory
Different sources of data were used for the LCA: the electricity con-
sumption of wastewater treatment was adapted from WRF and EPRI
(2013), interpolating the linear regression between wastewater treated
and electricity consumption of every module considered. The chemicals
consumption for the wastewater treatment was adapted from Foley
et al. (2010), and the electricity and chemicals consumption for the
struvite recovery was extracted from Amann et al. (2018). Biogas co-
generation emissions to air were adapted from reported public data
(MAPAMA, 2014), which provides data on direct emissions of CO, CH4,
N2O, NOx PM10, PM2.5 and TSP. Efﬂuent emissions to water in form of
N and P were also included for all technologies, calculated based on
data reported by the region administrative services (AMB, 2019). The
chemicals for the wastewater treatment and the chemical precipitation
were assumed to be transported by truck a distance of 50 km.
In the reuse phase, emissions to soil in the form of heavy metals
were retrieved form Egle et al. (2016) and Foley et al. (2010), while
emissions to air in the form of nitrous oxide (N2O) were calculated
based on standard emissions factors (IPCC, 2019). Transportation im-
pacts for the distribution of struvite to cropland are included consider-
ing the average distance from the WWTPs to AMB crop areas, being
18.3 km for Besós and 13.7 km for Llobregat. This data was obtained
using the “distance matrix” tool in QGIS, inputting the centroids of
every crop polygon. DAP was assumed to be imported from Morocco,
which holds the majority of remaining phosphate rock reserves
(Cordell and White, 2011; Van Kauwenbergh, 2010). After being
imported from Morocco, DAP was assumed to be distributed following
the same routes as struvite.
2.3.5. Life cycle impact assessment
The software used for the calculation was SimaPro 9.0 by Pré Consul-
tants. Background environmental data was retrieved from the
Ecoinvent 3.5 database (Wernet et al., 2016). When data was not avail-
able for a chemical, prior compounds in the production chain were used,
following stoichiometric relationships. An example is MgCl2, which was
accounted as a combination of MgO, H2O and HCl. The impact assess-
ment was performed using the ReCiPe (H) 2016 method (Huijbregts
et al., 2016). The selected midpoint categories for the assessment were
Global Warming, Freshwater Eutrophication, Marine Eutrophication
and Ecotoxicity (product of the sum of marine, terrestrial and freshwa-
ter ecotoxicity). Additionally, we also analyzed the Cumulative Energy
Demand (Hischier et al., 2010).

3. Results and discussion
3.1. Recovery and reuse potential
The recovery potential of every technology and WWTP is presented
in Table 2. The yearly P demand of the crops in the AMB was accounted
to be 36.5 t, based on an application approach. As shown in Table 2, all
recovery technologies meet the AMB's P requirements, even if only
one WWTP is operative. Our ﬁrst ﬁnding is that, if P recovery is partially
integrated into the current AMB scenario, it could minimize external
imports of mineral P for fertilization purposes. The REM-NUT® process
is the technology that recovers more struvite, meeting between 22.6
and 30.1 times the P requirements of the AMB just considering the
Besós WWTP. On the other hand, the least P is recovered with the
AirPrex® Methodology in Llobregat, which still exceeded the P require-
ments of the region by 20%.
Considering that all three assessed technologies in both WWTPs
meet the yearly phosphorus requirements of the region, we can
state that there is no need to modify the WWTP conﬁgurations of
other WWTPs in the region with a self-sufﬁciency perspective. More-
over, because the requirements are met in both WWTPs separately, it
would be feasible to just modify one of them to avoid the environ-
mental impacts derived from the upgrade of the WWTP conﬁgura-
tion. Since Llobregat already included the required elements for P-
recovery (e.g. EBPR or nitriﬁcation/denitriﬁcation modules), it
would be a suitable facility to implement P recovery through struvite
in the region.
Since all technologies recover the required amount of P to feed
the region, this parameter (i.e. amount of P recovered) may not be
the best for comparison purposes since recovering P without any
subsequent application could be seen as inefﬁcient. To refute this
perspective, synergies with areas adjacent to the AMB with less pop-
ulation but more agricultural land could be explored, optimizing
both P and food ﬂows. However, if we consider a larger scale, in-
creased transportation needs could have a detrimental effect on the
overall environmental impact and could be a target to optimize.
Moreover, the ﬁrst experimental on-site data on recovery rates
would be of great interest as recovery ranges for all technologies
assessed are still imprecise.
3.2. Impacts and implications of WWTP upgrades
Some technical modiﬁcations in the conﬁguration of both WWTPs
are needed in order to ensure P recovery according to each technology.
As shown in Table 3, these modiﬁcations affect the environmental per-
formance of the WWTPs in different ways. Since Llobregat WWTP al-
ready has a conﬁguration aiming at C, N and P removal, the required
changes for P recovery are less dramatic. The implementation of P re-
covery –and a small amount of N– in both WWTPs leads to less eutro-
phication regarding the direct emissions to water (Fig. A3 of the
Supplementary Material). This is particularly clear in two cases. First,
when using the REM-NUT® process in both WWTPs, as it is the technol-
ogy with the least impact on freshwater and marine eutrophication.
Second, in all technologies in Besós compared to BaU, due to the non-
existence of nutrient removal processes in this WWTP in the BaU con-
ﬁguration (i.e. nitriﬁcation-denitriﬁcation processes and EBPR). Consid-
ering the eutrophication impacts of the WWTP upgrades per m3 of
wastewater, the REM-NUT® is the best alternative above Ostara® and
AirPrex®, in terms of freshwater and marine eutrophication. However,
it is worthy of special attention that, when using the REM-NUT® pro-
cess, the EBPR module in Llobregat should not be operational in order
to keep the P in the water ﬂow, since the REM-NUT® process recovers
P from the secondary treated efﬂuent and thus stopping P elimination
during wastewater pretreatment is a pre-condition (Egle et al., 2015).
In this sense, the fact that the EBPR is not operational makes Llobregat
WWTP equivalent to the Besós baseline, where P is neither removed
nor recovered.
On the other hand, the electricity usage due to WWTP upgrades in all
technologies is the most impacting item in cumulative energy demand,
while chemical use exerts the majority of the impacts in global warming
and ecotoxicity. The differences between BaU in Besós and in Llobregat
regarding impact from chemical use are related to the use of lime for
nitriﬁcation-denitriﬁcation processes, which only occur in Llobregat.
For this reason, the difference between the impacts of the BaU and the
recovery scenario in Llobregat (which already has the required recovery
modules) is smaller than in Besós. Considering the impact categories of
global warming, ecotoxicity and cumulative energy demand, it is un-
clear whether Ostara® or AirPrex® are the best alternatives to imple-
ment, since they exert similar impacts in the mentioned categories.
In terms of WWTPs, Llobregat would be more appropriate to install P
recovery technologies in terms of global warming, ecotoxicity and cu-
mulative energy demand, since nitriﬁcation-denitriﬁcation and EBPR
modules are already equipped but are used from a removal perspective.
However, considering eutrophication categories, Besós would be more
appropriate to install P recovery technologies. Although it would re-
quire also the installation of additional modules, the impact savings
compared to the BaU scenarios are greater than in Llobregat.

the form of struvite or DAP is shown in Fig. 3. As we can see, the impacts

of BaU are the greatest among all scenarios, mainly due to impacts trig-
gered by the production of mineral fertilizer (DAP). Among recovery
technologies, the REM-NUT® impacts are the largest in global warming,
ecotoxicity and cumulative energy demand in both WWTPs considering
the maximum recovery rate. However, these impacts can be surpassed
by AirPrex® in all these impact categories if we consider the maximum
recovery rate for REM-NUT® and the minimum recovery ratio for
AirPrex®.
In terms of speciﬁc inventory impacts, Fig. 3 shows that chemical use
is a great contributor in the majority of impact categories, especially in
the REM-NUT®, due to the use of MgCl2 to comply with stoichiometric
precipitation ratios. Electricity use becomes relevant in global warming
and cumulative energy demand, although chemical use is still the
greatest contributor. Given the great impact exerted by the chemicals
in all technologies, experimental research should focus on choosing
the least impacting chemicals to comply with the stoichiometric rela-
tionships to precipitate struvite.
Application impacts, i.e. transport from the WWTP to the ﬁeld and
the direct emissions occurring there, are important in global warming,
mainly due to N2O emissions from fertilization, and in ecotoxicity,
mainly due to heavy metals emissions to soil, which are bigger in con-
ventional fertilizer (Foley et al., 2010) than in struvite (Egle et al.,
2016). Moreover, the impacts of the importation of the mineral fertil-
izers also play relevant roles in all impact categories. Because struvite
is produced locally, the impacts of its transport to agricultural ﬁelds
are lower than the ones exerted by the transport of DAP, which was as-
sumed to be imported from Morocco.
The second most impacting alternative in global warming,
ecotoxicity and cumulative energy demand is AirPrex®, again mainly
due to chemical use. Finally, Ostara® could be seen as the most suitable
technology for P recovery given that its impact is the lowest in all impact
categories shown in Fig. 3. Moreover, the impacts of Ostara® in global
warming are always lower than those exerted by mineral fertilizer,
even considering the lowest recovery rate reported for this technology.
3.4. Impacts and implications of regional struvite recovery and reuse
Fig. A4 in the Supplementary Material shows the impacts of an an-
nual regional strategy of wastewater treatment combined with struvite
recovery and reuse, compared to the BaU scenario. We can see that the
results when scaling up and expanding the system are similar in relative
terms as the ones found in Section 3.1. Electricity and chemical use in
the WWTP are the most impacting items in all technologies for global
warming, ecotoxicity and cumulative energy demand.
When comparing both WWTPs, we can observe two main behaviors
in these impact categories. First, Besós always exerts greater impacts
than Llobregat comparing the same technologies. This is due to the
wastewater volume that Besós treats (125 million of m3), compared to
Llobregat (94 million of m3). Second, all technologies have greater im-
pact differences compared to BaU in Besós than in Llobregat. This behav-
ior is due to the impact of the new elements required to have feasible
struvite recovery processes.
On the other hand, freshwater and marine eutrophication savings in
Besós are the most relevant improvements. Although these beneﬁts
may seem strictly related to ammonium extraction by struvite in the
crystallization process, it is also related to the installation of a
nitriﬁcation-denitriﬁcation process in Besós. This argument is rein-
forced by looking at the behavior of marine eutrophication in Llobregat,
which already had N removal processes in the BaU. In this WWTP, we
can see that the improvement related to the direct emissions of N is
strictly related to the ratio of NH4
+ co-compound recovery in struvite
precipitation.
Since the results are similar to Section 3.1, the conclusions regarding
the most appropriate technologies are identical. Ostara® and AirPrex®
are both feasible technologies in terms of global warming, ecotoxicity
and cumulative energy demand. In parallel, the REM-NUT® alternative
is the most suitable from a eutrophication perspective.
At this ﬁnal step, where we scale-up the system to a regional level
with a deﬁned time period, we can see where the overall impacts
come from. One of the main ﬁndings is that wastewater treatment
plays a major role in impact contribution, mainly due to the electricity
and chemical use. Graphical results excluding electricity and chemical
use to see the impacts of the application phase are shown in Fig. A5.
When these two items are left out of the system, we can better appreci-
ate the beneﬁts of the extra stock of struvite in terms of avoided fertil-
izer impacts, specially in ecotoxicity and cumulative energy demand.
3.5. Implications and further research
Different implications for other urban areas can be extrapolated
based on the results of the case study we have presented. The most suit-
able technology ultimately depends on the conﬁguration of the WWTP's
of the area under study. In general, it seems most appropriate to include
nutrient recovery in a WWTP that already has modules compatible with

M. Rufí-Salís et al. / Science of the Total Environment 737 (2020) 139783

the precipitation of struvite, such as EBPR or nitrogen removal. In such
case, integrating these new technologies will slightly increase the
WWTP's impacts on global warming or cumulative energy demand, as
shown in the Llobregat WWTP. However, implementing struvite recov-
ery only in Llobregat implies that Besós will keep its BaU conﬁguration,
thus discharging most P in the aquatic environment and contributing
both to the depletion of this nutrient and eutrophication impacts. In
this sense, we point out the relevance of studying the implications in
multiple impact categories before decision-making. Moreover, ﬁnding
alternatives for the chemicals involved in struvite precipitation should
also be the focus of further research given the high impact detected.
In terms of fertilizer application, our case study demonstrates that
nutrient recovery from wastewater in metropolitan areas can meet
the fertilizer needs of peri-urban agriculture, thereby promoting syner-
getic interactions between the urban and periurban areas for resource
optimization. Our study proves the potential for closing nutrient loops
in cities, thus enabling a circular approach in food and water systems.
In other words, upgrading WWTPs can help support a more sustainable,
local food production and consumption in cities. However, existing
challenges in terms of environmental tradeoffs call for further explora-
tion. Given the singular characteristics of every urban area, quantifying
the P recovery and reuse potential in additional case studies should be
the focus of further research aiming to improve the anthropogenic
ﬂows of the P cycle, which is a clear hotspot in urban wastewater
treatment.
In this sense, we encourage other metropolitan areas to adopt this
systemic focus to optimize resources, and to not only treat wastewa-
ter as a waste to be discarded, but as a source of nutrients for meeting
the fertilizer demand of local foodsheds. Moreover, expanding the
system under study gives more opportunity for circularity of re-
sources, a key aspect for urban areas. Our empirical ﬁndings support
existing discussions around the need for paradigm shifts in WWTPs
(Guest et al., 2009; Robles et al., 2020; Villarín and Merel, 2020).
Wastewater is a source of nutrients that must be recovered and
used locally to improve the resilience of future cities. Further re-
search should also focus on assessing the best way to apply struvite
recovery technologies in other complex urban areas like the AMB.
Recovering P in regions where it is being extensively wasted, i.e.
urban areas, is a great concern but also an opportunity to investigate
best practices.
4. Conclusions
Phosphorus depletion as a non-renewable resource needs to be in-
cluded in the political agenda when dealing with global food security.
With a P peak in a short-term horizon, the paradigm shift from removal
to recovery is a must. Moreover, recovery should include a reuse per-
spective to close the cycle. This study aimed to evaluate the technical
and environmental feasibility of phosphorus recovery implementation
and application in an urban region. Four main conclusions can be
drawn from this analysis.
First, a struvite recovery process applied to only one wastewater
treatment plant is suitable to feed with phosphorus all agricultural ﬁelds
in the AMB.
Second, struvite recovery presented great savings in eutrophication
since a great amount of phosphorus and part of the nitrogen was ex-
tracted from the efﬂuent, thus preventing these nutrients to end up in
the aquatic environment.
Third, the phosphorus recovery process that precipitates struvite
from the digester supernatant, developed by Ostara®, had the lowest
environmental impacts.
Forth and ﬁnally, attention should be paid on which are the most
suitable wastewater treatment plants to recover struvite and on which
are the technologies that best suit the selected facilities, since the deci-
sion may entail additional impacts.

Increasing resource circularity in wastewater treatment: Environmental
implications of technological upgrades
Martí Rufí-Salís a,b, Anna Petit-Boix a, Sina Leipold c,d, Gara Villalba a,b, Joan Rieradevall a,b, Eduard Moliné e,
Xavier Gabarrell a,b, Julián Carrera f, María Eugenia Suárez-Ojeda f,⁎
a Sostenipra, Institute of Environmental Science and Technology (ICTA), Unidad de excelencia “María de Maeztu” (MDM-2015-0552), Universitat Autònoma de Barcelona (UAB),
08193 Bellaterra, Barcelona, Spain
b Department of Chemical, Biological and Environmental Engineering, Universitat Autònoma de Barcelona, Escola d'Enginyeria, c/de les Sitges s/n, Ediﬁci Q, 08193 Bellaterra,
Barcelona, Spain
c Department of Environmental Politics, Helmholtz Centre for Environmental Research-UFZ, Permoserstr. 15, 04318 Leipzig, Germany
d Chair of Environmental Politics, University of Jena, Bachstr. 18k, 07743 Jena, Germany
e Depuración de Aguas del Mediterráneo (DAM), Guglielmo Marconi, 11 piso 2°, 10 despacho 19, Parque Tecnológico, 46980 Paterna, Valencia, Spain
f GENOCOV Research Group, Department of Chemical, Biological and Environmental Engineering, Universitat Autònoma de Barcelona, Escola d'Enginyeria, c/de les Sitges s/n,
Ediﬁci Q, 08193 Bellaterra, Barcelona, Spain
H I G H L I G H T S
• Rethinking wastewater treatment is key to
recover energy and resources in cities.
• Increased biogas production, nitrogen re-
moval and struvite recovery are studied.
• Radical upgrades through nutrient recov-
ery decrease the plant's environmental im-
pacts.
G R A P H I C A L
A B S T R A C T
A B S T R A C T
A R T I C L E
I N F O
Editor: Huu Hao Ngo
A paradigm shift is needed in wastewater treatment plants (WWTPs) to progress from traditional pollutant removal to
resource recovery. However, whether this transformation produces overall environmental beneﬁts will depend on the
efﬁcient and sustainable use of resources by emerging technologies. Given that many of these technologies are still
being tested at the pilot scale, there is a lack of environmental assessments quantifying their impacts and beneﬁts.
In particular, an integrated approach to energy and nutrient recovery can elucidate the potential conﬁgurations for
WWTPs. In this study, we conduct a life cycle assessment (LCA) of emergent wastewater treatment technologies
aimed at increasing resource circularity in WWTPs. We focus on increasing energy self-sufﬁciency through biogas up-
grades and a more radical circular approach aimed at nutrient recovery. Based on a case-study WWTP, we compare its
current conﬁguration with (1) implementing autotrophic nitrogen removal in the mainstream and deriving most of the
organic matter for biogas production, which increases the quality and quantity of biogas available for energy produc-
tion; (2) implementing struvite recovery through enhanced biological phosphorus removal (EBPR) as a radical ap-
proach to phosphorus management, offering an alternative to mineral fertilizer; and (3) a combination of both
approaches. The results show that incremental changes in biogas production are insufﬁcient for compensating for
Keywords:
Life cycle assessment
Emergent technologies
Circular economy
Resource recovery
Industrial ecology
Science of the Total Environment 838 (2022) 156422
⁎ Corresponding author.
E-mail address: mariaeugenia.suarez@uab.cat (M.E. Suárez-Ojeda).
http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.scitotenv.2022.156422
Received 9 March 2022; Received in revised form 27 May 2022; Accepted 30 May 2022
Available online 2 June 2022
0048-9697/© 2022 The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V. This is an open access article under the CC BY-NC license (http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/4.0/).
Contents lists available at ScienceDirect
Science of the Total Environment
journal homepage: www.elsevier.com/locate/scitotenv

the environmental investment in infrastructure, although autotrophic nitrogen removal is beneﬁcial for increasing the
quality of the efﬂuent. Combined phosphorus and energy recovery reduce the environmental impacts from the avoided
use of fertilizers and phosphorus and the nitrogen release into water bodies. An integrated approach to resource man-
agement in WWTPs is thus desirable and creates new opportunities toward the implementation of circular strategies
with low environmental impact in cities.
1. Introduction
The circular economy is becoming increasingly popular in cities to ap-
proach the sustainable management of natural resources. A circular urban
metabolism can help “keep products, components, and materials at their
highest utility and value at all times” (Bocken et al., 2017) by taking advan-
tage of the local resources available in cities. Urban circular strategies target
a large variety of resource ﬂows and urban sectors, from infrastructure to
consumers. (Petit-Boix and Leipold, 2018). The conception and implemen-
tation of strategies requires, however, a paradigm shift in different urban
sectors to increase the availability of local resources while reducing the en-
vironmental impacts of urban infrastructure. Wastewater treatment plants
(WWTPs) are an essential component of urban infrastructure; this paradigm
shift has been discussed but has yet to become a reality.
In recent decades, water research has called for a transition from “what
must be removed” from wastewater to comply with water quality standards
to “what can be recovered” from wastewater (Guest et al., 2009). The envi-
ronmental consequences of focusing on removal technologies stress this
need. For example, heterotrophic biological nitrogen removal (BNR)
would be the classical choice when implementing nitrogen removal tech-
nologies in WWTPs, but it would entail a large energy consumption due
to increased aeration needs to convert ammonia nitrogen to nitrite and to
convert its subsequent oxidation to nitrate (ENERWATER, 2015). Embrac-
ing the “circular water economy” has the potential to expand the functions
of urban wastewater systems, as becomes a valued service (e.g., clean water
from sanitation), energy source (e.g., biogas) and carrier of resources
(e.g., nitrogen and phosphorus) (Brears, 2020). Circular wastewater man-
agement aims to reuse, recycle, recover and reclaim the resources embed-
ded in water ﬂows, which is the result of rethinking the current system
(Smol et al., 2020). Examples include water reuse in agriculture
e.g., Almanaseer et al., 2020; Chen et al., 2021) or nutrient recovery in
the form of struvite (e.g., Mavhungu et al., 2020). Earlier in the circularity
debate, energy recovery and efﬁciency dominated the wastewater
industry's efforts, (Guest et al., 2009), whereas current political and scien-
tiﬁc debates encourage nutrient recovery. For instance, the European
Union's updated Circular Economy Action Plan mentions nutrient recovery
and wastewater among the planned measures (European Commission,
2020a). Both energy and nutrient recovery offer opportunities for resource
self-sufﬁciency; the question that remains is what kind of circularity ap-
proach holds the largest potential to minimize the environmental impacts
of wastewater treatment.
In the case of energy self-sufﬁciency, process optimization and onsite
energy generation through biogas have become widespread circular strate-
gies (Jenicek et al., 2012). To date, most self-sufﬁcient WWTPs take advan-
tage of biogas to heat their digesters and to generate electricity (Gu et al.,
2017), and the literature has long suggested that replacing conventional en-
ergy sources can reduce a WWTP's environmental impacts (e.g., Foley et al.,
2010; Pasqualino et al., 2009; Stokes and Horvath, 2010). However, the
system often depends on external waste inputs, as many WWTPs apply
codigestion of their sludge with urban organic waste to enhance biogas pro-
duction (Mata-Alvarez et al., 2014). Considering that current environmen-
tal targets (e.g., CO2 emissions) demand a more stringent implementation
of energy self-sufﬁciency measures, emergent treatment technologies
might be more beneﬁcial than classical alternatives. An example is the im-
plementation of autotrophic (i.e., without organic matter) BNR in the main-
stream WWTPs, i.e., implementation of partial nitriﬁcation and anammox
for removing nitrogen species from wastewater (Jetten et al., 1997; Kartal
et al., 2010; Siegrist et al., 2008), instead of the classical heterotrophic
BNR. This new conﬁguration of the mainstream line of an urban WWTP
(refer to Section 2.1 for more details) reduces the energy consumption for
aeration and uses most of the organic matter contained in the wastewater
for biogas production, which enhances the energy recovery potential and
reduces the overall operational costs of the facility.
This type of technology produces incremental changes in energy recov-
ery based on the existing infrastructure, meaning, in this case, that a larger
amount of biogas is available. Pilot-plant studies of new technologies, how-
ever, tend to focus on process efﬁciency, whereas the environmental im-
pacts associated with their implementation are often disregarded.
Upgrading WWTPs to enhance circularity requires an investment in natural
resources that needs offsetting. We need to ensure that the environmental
beneﬁts outbalance the impacts, which might not be accomplished with a
single technological upgrade. Here, nutrient recovery becomes an asset. No-
tably, the literature highlights the interest in examining the combined ben-
eﬁts of resource recovery approaches, such as onsite energy generation,
nutrient recovery and water reuse (Mo and Zhang, 2013; Robles et al.,
2020; Villarín and Merel, 2020; Wang et al., 2018). Additionally, Life
Cycle Assessments (LCAs) are becoming abundant to capture the environ-
mental effects of emergent technologies targeting nitrogen (N) and phos-
phorus (P) recovery in WWTPs (Roldán et al., 2020; Rufí-Salís et al.,
2020; Sena and Hicks, 2018; van Zelm et al., 2020). Nutrient recovery is
generally presented as an environmentally friendly strategy due to reduced
chemical use and the substitution of mineral fertilizer (Lam et al., 2020).
Nutrient recovery thus constitutes a more radical change in wastewater
treatment, as it shifts from the traditional removal paradigm and provides
valuable resources that can be utilized by other urban sectors, such as
local food production (Rufí-Salís et al., 2020). In parallel, enhancing energy
recovery generates a partial increase in a WWTP's existing energy self-
sufﬁciency. The question that remains is what kind of recovery strategy
(i.e., incremental, radical or a combination of both) can increase the avail-
ability of local resources with the lowest environmental cost. Addressing
this question can help redesign WWTPs and prioritize circular strategies
from an environmental standpoint. However, while academics call for
LCAs integrating both energy and nutrient recovery (e.g., Mo and Zhang,
2013), an increasing number of recovery solutions are being developed at
the pilot level and still lack an evaluation of their impacts and beneﬁts
(Bohra et al., 2022).
To ﬁll this gap, we assessed the environmental impacts and beneﬁts of
upgrading WWTPs for energy and nutrient recovery based on two emergent
technologies. Additionally, we analyze the extent to which increased en-
ergy and nutrient recovery compensate for the environmental investment
in infrastructure. Based on a case-study WWTP, we perform an LCA to ana-
lyze scenarios with technological upgrades, including i) implementing au-
totrophic BNR allowing an increase in biogas production, ii) recovering
nutrients in the form of struvite through enhanced biological phosphorus
removal (EBPR) and iii) implementing EBPR and autotrophic BNR allowing
the recovery of nutrients in the form of struvite and simultaneously increas-
ing biogas production.
2. Methods
2.1. Emergent resource recovery technologies
The exemplary technologies selected in this study are autotrophic BNR
and EBPR. Autotrophic BNR can be implemented in the water line (main-
stream) of an urban WWTP with several conﬁgurations, but the most prom-
ising setup includes two stages (Pérez et al., 2015). The ﬁrst biological stage
M. Rufí-Salís et al.
Science of the Total Environment 838 (2022) 156422
2

aerobically removes organic matter through a high-rate activated sludge
(HRAS) system operated at high organic loading rates (higher than 1 kg
of chemical oxygen demand (COD) m−3 d−1), low dissolved oxygen con-
centrations (lower than 1 g O2 m−3) and low sludge retention times
(lower than 3 days) (Carrera et al., 2022; Jimenez et al., 2015). Therefore,
the secondary sludge produced in this stage would have higher anaerobic
biodegradability than the secondary sludge produced in the conventional
activated sludge (CAS) system that is available in most urban WWTPs.
The efﬂuent of this HRAS stage is wastewater with a low COD/N ratio,
but the system does not remove ammonia nitrogen. HRAS can be imple-
mented as the sole stage for organic matter removal before the autotrophic
BNR process, or it can be implemented after a primary sedimentation and
just before the autotrophic BNR process, as demonstrated like in the study
system. In the selected conﬁguration, organic matter removal is carried
out in the primary sedimentation and HRAS processes. In the ﬁrst conﬁgu-
ration, which is only an HRAS process, the percentage of COD removal is
approximately 40–60% (Guven et al., 2019). In the second conﬁguration,
which comprises the primary sedimentation plus HRAS process, the per-
centage of COD removal can be increased up to 65–80% (Carrera et al.,
2022). The second stage is the autotrophic BNR process, which consists of
two biological steps: partial oxidation of ammonium to nitrite (partial
nitritation) and anaerobic ammonium oxidation (anammox) to di‑nitrogen
gas. Autotrophic BNR in the mainstream can be implemented through a sin-
gle reactor (Laureni et al., 2016; Lotti et al., 2014) or through two reactors
(Isanta et al., 2015; Pérez et al., 2015; Reino et al., 2018; Juan-Díaz et al.,
2022).
From a nutrient recovery perspective, EBPR has been extensively uti-
lized in recent years, as it is able to remove P without chemical precipitants
(Oehmen et al., 2007). Instead, EBPR uses bacteria that accumulate P and
transfer it to the sludge line. EBPR is implemented in current WWTPs
with CAS systems by adding an anaerobic reactor to produce volatile fatty
acids for P-accumulating bacteria (Izadi et al., 2020). If EBPR is imple-
mented with autotrophic BNR in the mainstream, the HRAS stage should
also be modiﬁed to include an anaerobic reactor. This technology has
been successfully demonstrated at the lab scale by operating the EBPR-
HRAS system at sludge retention times (SRTs) as low as 3 days (Roots
et al., 2020; Zhang et al., 2021). Regardless of how the EBPR is applied
(i.e., in a CAS or HRAS conﬁguration), P is recovered in the sludge line
after anaerobic sludge digestion. In the digester, P accumulates in the super-
natant or digestate (Pastor et al., 2008), which is the main target of P recov-
ery technologies. In this case, we selected Ostara® technology (Ostara,
2019) to recover struvite, which is a promising new P fertilizer with slow-
release characteristics (Latiﬁan et al., 2012; Rahman et al., 2014). Ostara®
was selected because of the environmental performance of this technology
when compared with other alternatives (Amann et al., 2018; Rufí-Salís
et al., 2020). Moreover, Ostara® is one of the few struvite recovery technol-
ogies that operates worldwide and produces a commercial struvite fertil-
izer.
2.2. Upgrade scenarios in a case-study WWTP
The environmental effects of these technological upgrades are exempli-
ﬁed through an existing urban WWTP selected for our case study. The
WWTP is located in the Metropolitan Area of Barcelona and serves
135,000 population equivalents (p.e.). This WWTP is representative of
large facilities (urban agglomerations >100.000 p.e.), which represent
52% of the EU's wastewater load (European Commission, 2020b). The anal-
ysis compares four scenarios that result in various modiﬁcations of the orig-
inal WWTP conﬁguration (Fig. 1). These modiﬁcations lead to incremental
changes in the quantity and quality of biogas production and/or radical
changes in nutrient management, i.e., from removal to recovery (Table 1).
The baseline scenario (BS) (Fig. 1) corresponds to the current conﬁgura-
tion of the WWTP. The facility removes organic matter through a CAS sys-
tem operated at an SRT of 14 days, while P is chemically precipitated by
adding iron chloride. This WWTP does not remove nitrogen species,
being representative of 34% of the WWTPs in the European Union that
do not comply with more stringent treatment, according to article 5 of
the Urban Wastewater Treatment Directive (European Commission,
1991). In fact, the nitriﬁcation process does not take place at SRT of 14
days because the plant was limited by the available volume of the reactors
of the biological treatment. Due to this limitation, the WWTP had an ex-
emption of the Public Administration regarding the nitrogen removal.
Moreover, the WWTP was operated at a very low dissolved oxygen concen-
tration to save energy consumption and to completely stop the nitriﬁcation
process at any season of the year. In terms of energy self-sufﬁciency, this
WWTP includes a biogas cogeneration unit for producing electricity and
heat. The biogas produced by anaerobic digestion of a mixture of primary
sludge and secondary sludge has 57% methane (CH4) content based on
the WWTP records. The selected technologies were assessed in three differ-
ent scenarios.
The SAVING-E scenario (SE) (Fig. 1) considers the implementation of
the autotrophic BNR in the mainstream through an HRAS system for re-
moving organic matter and two separated reactors (partial nitritation and
anammox) for removing nitrogen species. SAVING-E technology (www.
saving-e.eu) has been tested at the pilot scale for three years in the same
full-scale WWTP that is considered the baseline scenario. With this conﬁg-
uration, biogas production would be increased by 61%, and the CH4 con-
tent would reach 75%. In this case, nitrogen species are removed through
autotrophic BNR, whereas P is still precipitated through the addition of
iron chloride.
The struvite recovery scenario (SR) considers a theoretical CAS-EBPR to
transfer P to the sludge line (Izadi et al., 2020), where it can be recovered
from the digestate in the form of struvite through an Ostara® process.
From a technical point of view, struvite precipitation also contributes to N
recovery since struvite is composed by a mol of potassium, a mol of ammo-
nium and a mol of phosphate. However, if we make a mass balance, only
around 5–6% of the total inﬂow N of the WWTP can be recovered in form
of struvite. Considering the number and difﬁculty of the calculations
needed in this study, we decided not to consider that small N-recovery
amount. The total resource recovery scenario (TRR) combines autotrophic
BNR and HRAS-EBPR by adding an anaerobic reactor to the HRAS system
(Roots et al., 2020; Zhang et al., 2021), thus simultaneously enhancing
the production of biogas and P recovery.
2.3. Life cycle assessment methodology
This section includes the main considerations and data for three of the
phases of an LCA, namely, goal and scope deﬁnition, life cycle inventory
analysis, and life cycle impact assessment (ISO, 2006).
2.3.1. Goal and scope deﬁnition
To assess the environmental impacts of the four scenarios, a functional
unit needs to be deﬁned to enable a scenario comparison. In this case, the
impacts refer to 1 m3 of treated wastewater, which is one of the most com-
mon functional units in wastewater-related LCAs (Corominas et al., 2013).
We used the WWTP's average treated efﬂuent for the period 2014–2016,
which was 7,342,338 m3/year. A lifespan of 30 years was assumed for
the WWTP based on previous estimations (Morera et al., 2017; Risch
et al., 2015). The infrastructure associated with the upgrades has an ex-
pected lifespan of 20 years according to the producer.
The system boundaries are shown in Fig. 2. For the purpose of our anal-
ysis, the operation stage will elucidate the short-term variations in the envi-
ronmental impacts of WWTPs resulting from technological upgrades. The
construction of the whole WWTP was excluded from the analysis, as it is as-
sumed to remain constant before and after the upgrades (Guven et al.,
2018; Hadjimichael et al., 2016). However, we did consider the additional
impacts related to the construction of new infrastructure required for the
technological upgrades. The end of life of the new infrastructure was not
modeled as it is still uncertain whether the tanks could be reused or
recycled for similar purposes. Sludge ﬁnal disposal and solid waste manage-
ment were also excluded. In our case, sludge ﬁnal disposal undergoes ther-
mal drying, which is an energy-intensive process (Bennamoun et al., 2013)
M. Rufí-Salís et al.
Science of the Total Environment 838 (2022) 156422
3

that might overshadow the actual operational energy use of the WWTP.
Discussing the best sludge treatment processes is under debate (Hospido
et al., 2005) and is beyond the scope of this analysis.
2.3.2. Life cycle inventory
Table 2 shows the life cycle inventory for each scenario. Facility man-
agers provided detailed ﬂow charts that helped us to identify the main
ﬂows involved in the operation of the baseline scenario, which are electric-
ity, chemicals, and direct pollutant emissions to air and water. Each ﬂow
was quantiﬁed for the main treatment blocks depicted in Fig. 1,
i.e., wastewater pretreatment and treatment and sludge treatment. As
explained in Section 2.3.1, waste management was excluded. The con-
sumption of electricity and chemicals was also available in the WWTP re-
ports and databases provided by facility managers. To address the
changes in biogas production, we applied the average electricity and chem-
ical values for the period 2014–2016. In the case of electricity, the WWTP is
connected to the low voltage power grid, which we modeled using the
Spanish electricity mix (Red Eléctrica de España, 2014). The WWTP's mon-
itoring system already provided data on the amount of cogenerated and
purchased electricity, and no assumptions were needed. The chemicals
comprise iron chloride and polyelectrolyte, which are utilized in the pri-
mary settler and sludge management, respectively. Background inventory
Fig. 1. Original conﬁguration of the wastewater treatment plant (left column) and modiﬁcations to the treatment units in the selected scenarios (SE, SR and TRR). Autotrophic
BNR: autotrophic biological nitrogen removal; EBPR: enhanced biological phosphorus removal.

data were retrieved from Ecoinvent v3 (Wernet et al., 2016) (refer to
Table 1 in the Supplementary Material).
The new infrastructure required for the emergent technologies
(i.e., autotrophic BNR and EBPR) was included in the assessment (lifespan
of 20 years). We assumed that the current treatment capacity would be cov-
ered with these additional or substitutive technologies, per the technical as-
sessment of the pilot plant (Section 2.2). We did not account for space
limitations in the installation of the new reactors to show the full potential
of these upgrades. As a result, several airlift reactors (ALR) for the partial
nitritation process (Isanta et al., 2015) and Upﬂow Anammox Sludge Blan-
ket (UAnSB) for the anammox process (Reino et al., 2018) were designed
using steel for the SE scenario, and steel and concrete reactors were consid-
ered for the HRAS-EBPR unit. Regarding the operation, the SE scenario de-
mands less energy due to decreased oxygen consumption and a slight
increase in polyelectrolyte consumption. These data were obtained from
the SAVING-E pilot plant. In terms of P recovery, we accounted for the elec-
tricity and chemicals (magnesium chloride and sodium hydroxide) re-
quired in the Ostara© process as detailed by (Amann et al., 2018),
considering a P recovery efﬁciency of 25% with respect to the P content
in the inﬂuent. Iron chloride is thus not needed. We included the transport
of chemicals from the actual suppliers to the WWTP.
As resources, both in terms of energy and nutrients, are recovered from
WWTPs, the system becomes multifunctional. First, we have a function of
treating wastewater that aligns with the functional unit described in
Section 2.3.1. Second, we have a function of energy provision from biogas
cogeneration, either to increase the energy self-sufﬁciency of WWTPs or to
provide burden-free energy to the electricity market. Last, we have a func-
tion of phosphorus fertilizer provision to the regional agricultural market.
To account for the main function of treating wastewater and the beneﬁts
of nutrient recovery through struvite and energy production via biogas co-
generation, we conducted a system expansion and considered the substitu-
tion of mineral fertilizer, considering stochiometric ratios and the market
for electricity considering direct substitution, and accounted for this prod-
uct displacement as environmental credits. Diammonium phosphate
(DAP) was chosen as the baseline fertilizer, as it is the most prevalent P fer-
tilizer worldwide (IPNI, 2007). To model the effects of electricity cogenera-
tion through biogas, we accounted for the credits to energy production,
i.e., avoided consumption of energy from the grid. We made a theoretical
assumption in scenarios with enhanced biogas production (SE and TRR).
Ideally, increased biogas generation implies that all the gas is consumed
to boost energy production. For this reason, we assumed that the capacity
of the cogeneration turbines would be adapted, but the additional infra-
structure required in these cogeneration units was not modeled due to a
lack of data. The substitution process for each scenario is provided in
Eq. (1) for the BS and SE scenarios and in Eq. (2) for the SR and TRR
scenarios.

FTW ¼ FTWþEPþSP  FEM  FFM
