Impact assessment. Environmental impacts of the current
system are modeled using the SimaPro 8.5.2 software (PRé
Sustainability 2020) with Ecoinvent 3 (Ecoinvent 2020) and
United States Life Cycle Inventory (USLCI) (NREL 2020) da-
tabases. Tool for the Reduction and Assessment of Chem-
ical and Other Environmental Impacts (TRACI 2.1) (Bare
2012) and Building for Environmental and Economic Sus-
tainability (BEES) (Lippiatt 2002) impact assessment meth-
odologies
are
used
to
quantify
the
impacts
of
the
considered system. The midpoint environmental impact
categories, along with their units and abbreviations based
on TRACI 2.1 are ozone depletion (ODP in kg CFC11‐eq),
global warming (GWP in kg CO2‐eq), smog (SP in kg O3‐eq),
acidiﬁcation (AP in kg SO2‐eq), eutrophication (EP in kg
N‐eq.), carcinogenics (CP in comparative toxic unit for
human [CTUh]), noncarcinogenics (NCP in CTUh), respira-
tory effects (RP in kg PM2.5‐eq), ecotoxicity (ETP in com-
parative toxic unit for ecotoxicity), and fossil fuel depletion
(FFP in megajoules [MJ] surplus energy) potentials. Addi-
tionally, in order to assess the direct and indirect water
consumption of the system, BEES provides water intake
impact category in liters (WP in L). The TRACI 2.1 impact
assessment was selected because it considers the best ap-
plicable methodologies within each category for the Unites
States (Bare 2012), and BEES methodology is included be-
cause it provides the overall water footprint for the consid-
ered processes.
Sensitivity and uncertainty analyses. A sensitivity analysis
was performed in order to highlight the parameters to which
the LCA results of the struvite precipitation system are
sensitive. The value for each of the input parameters was
modiﬁed by 20%. Following this step, the potential envi-
ronmental impacts of the system were recalculated to de-
termine how the change in the inputs affected the overall
LCA results. Sensitivity factor (SFs; i.e., the relative change
of the output over relative change of the input) for each
parameter was calculated, and the parameters with an SF of
≤2% were considered as not sensitive to the system
(Ghamkhar et al. 2020). Additionally, uncertainty analysis
was conducted using Monte Carlo simulations in SimaPro
8.5.2 in order to estimate the highest and the lowest bounds
of environmental impacts for each scenario. For the present
evaluation, 95% conﬁdence interval was selected, and
analysis was run 1000 times. Results are provided in the
Supplemental Data Table S6 for the bench‐scale scenario,
Table S7 for the farm‐scale scenario using well water, and
Table S8 for the farm‐scale scenario using gray water.

manure) was performed, and characterization results are

presented in Figure 2 by involving the impact categories
from TRACI 2.1 and BEES. Impact assessment results of
each input and output parameter are tabulated in Supple-
mental Data Tables S9 and S10.
The analysis shows that the MgCl2 input has a negligible
impact in all categories. In the majority of the environmental
impact categories considered (GWP, SP, AP, NCP, RP, and
FFP), electricity is found to be the main contributor, ac-
counting for more than 51% impact share. Grid electricity
(US average grid) input is used for stirring purposes
throughout the bench‐scale struvite precipitation process.
Additionally, results indicated that oxalic acid is the major
contributor among other chemicals. It contributes more than
45% of ODP, 35% of CP, 34% of ETP, 29% of WP, 23% of
NCP, and 21% of EP. These ﬁndings are compatible with
those of Ishii and Boyer (2015), who found that the upstream
impacts of manufacturing chemicals as well as the grid
electricity are the main reasons for high environmental cost
of the system. As expected, in the WP category, deionized
water input is the most impactful contributor (>60%). Lastly,
for EP, the efﬂuent discharge (42%) is found as the most
impactful parameter. As mentioned in the Methods section
and depicted in Figure 1, the efﬂuent is considered as being
directly discharged to surface waters. Given that the efﬂuent
discharge contains the excess N and P that are not recov-
ered as struvite, its contribution to EP is reasonable.
There are several promising aspects of struvite precip-
itation. These include converting waste materials (e.g., dairy
manure in the present study) into valuable resources (i.e.,
slow‐release fertilizer), avoiding production of conventional
mineral fertilizers and nutrient management (Kataki and
Baruah 2018). In the present study, N and phosphate
fertilizer offsets due to the utilization of struvite are calcu-
lated and incorporated into the overall life cycle impact
assessment results. Avoided N fertilizer manufacturing
shows the most offset in the ODP impact category with a
–4% share. Offset in ETP follows ODP with a –3% impact
ratio. The rest of the categories show 0.3% to 2% decrease
in environmental impacts. In terms of avoided phosphate
fertilizer production, the largest offsets are observed in CP,
EP, ETP, NCP, and ODP categories with ratios of –14%,
–12%, –12%, –11%, and –8%, respectively. Offsets in WP
(–5%) and RP (–4%) follow the trend. Similar to the case of N
fertilizer, the rest of the categories show 1% to 2% decrease
in environmental impacts resulting from the decreased
manufacturing of conventional phosphate fertilizer. It should
be highlighted that these results are representative for 1 kg
of struvite precipitation on a bench‐scale study. Results for
struvite
precipitation from
1 kg
of AD‐L‐dairy
manure
treated are presented in Supplemental Data Figure S1.
Life cycle impact assessment for farm‐scale process
Considering farm‐scale inventory for struvite precipitation,
characterization results are presented in Figure 3 by in-
volving the impact categories from TRACI 2.1 and BEES.
Impact assessment results of each input and output param-
eter are tabulated in Supplemental Data Tables S11 and S12.
The farm‐scale LCA results show that MgCl2 and energy
(biogas) inputs have negligible impact contributions in all
impact categories. In the majority of the environmental im-
pact categories considered (ODP, GWP, SP, AP, CP, NCP,
RP, and ETP), oxalic acid is found to be the main contributor,
accounting for more than 52% impact share. Given that a
signiﬁcant amount of nitric acid is needed to produce oxalic

acid itself (Gürü et al. 2001), oxalic acid input has the
greatest contribution in the majority of impact categories.
Scaling the process for farm scale showed a negligible
change in the total WP. However, the impact contribution
resulting from the efﬂuent discharge on WP increased sig-
niﬁcantly (>58%). The efﬂuent is modeled by considering
that the excess nutrients contaminate the water that was
initially obtained from natural origins (e.g., well water).
Lastly, for EP, the efﬂuent discharge (49%) is found as the
most impactful parameter due to containing excess N and P
that cannot be recovered as struvite.
In terms of the negative impacts (i.e., environmental
beneﬁts) resulting from avoided N fertilizer manufacturing,
the largest offset is identiﬁed in the FFP impact category
with a –9% share. Offsets in GWP, SP, ODP, AP, and ETP
follow FFP with –8%, –6%, –5%, –5%, and –5% impact ratios,
respectively. The rest of the categories show less than 3%
decrease in environmental impacts. Considering associated
environmental beneﬁts resulting from avoided phosphate
fertilizer manufacturing, more than 17% offset is observed in
the NCP, CP, ETP, RP, and AP impact categories. Fossil fuel
depletion, EP, and SP follow the trend with equal amounts
of offsets as 14%. The rest of the impact categories show
less than 5% to 9% decrease in environmental impacts re-
sulting from the avoided manufacturing of conventional
phosphate fertilizer. Results for farm‐scale struvite precip-
itation from 1 kg of AD‐L‐dairy manure are presented in
Supplemental Data Figure S2.
As expected, farm‐scale environmental impact assess-
ment results showed lower impacts in all of the 11 potential
environmental impact categories. With the farm‐scale stru-
vite precipitation from AD‐L‐dairy manure, life cycle impacts
per unit mass of AD‐L‐dairy manure treated (or unit mass of
struvite precipitated) showed a signiﬁcant decrease (>55%)
in 8 of the 11 potential environmental impact categories
(GWP, SP, AP, CP, NCP, RP, ETP, and FFP). The main reason
for this decrease in the majority of environmental impacts is
the utilization of biogas as an energy source instead of grid
electricity. Research suggested that up to 49% decrease in
greenhouse gas emissions (and therefore potential envi-
ronmental impacts) can be achieved by substituting grid
electricity with biogas (Natividad P´erez‐Camacho et al.
2019), which is comparable to the results obtained in the
present study. Due to the excess nutrient discharge to sur-
face waters, EP did not show as much as reduction as other
impact categories (19%). Lastly, 21% decrease in ODP and
3% decrease in WP were observed as a result of farm‐scale
struvite precipitation model.
Environmental impacts using different functional units
Depending of the purpose of the project, results can be
presented using different functional units. For instance, if
the primary objective is to treat dairy manure, the mass of
treated manure can be used as a reference unit (Lijó
et al. 2018). Moreover, if the main objective is to precipitate
the struvite, the mass of struvite generation may be se-
lected. Analyzing LCA results using multiple units enables
incorporation of multifunctionality and increases the accu-
racy (Ghamkhar et al. 2020). Figure 4 shows four of the
potential environmental impacts of struvite precipitation
process for 2 different functional units under bench and farm
scales. Global warming potential is included in Figure 4, as it
has been historically used to report single category results
as climate change (Heijungs 2014). Ecotoxicity potential is

As demonstrated, LCA results based on the unit mass of
struvite precipitation resulted in more than 200 times higher
impacts in all impact categories. This is due to a relatively
larger amount of dairy manure need to precipitate 1 kg
of struvite. For reference, to obtain 1 kg of struvite, 200 to
260 kg of raw dairy manure is needed, considering manure
characteristics from Wisconsin farms (Aguirre‐Villegas et al.
2018, 2019; Sampat, Ruiz‐Mercado et al. 2018; Wang
et al. 2019), which corresponds to collecting manure from 4
to 5 cows per day. Therefore, potentially, larger farms (≥200
head) that have anaerobic digesters may produce a minimum
of 40 to 50 kg of struvite per day. Although a signiﬁcant
quantity of dairy manure is needed, it is an inescapable by-
product of farming, inexpensive, and abundantly available,
which make it an appropriate source for struvite recovery
(Kataki and Baruah 2018). From struvite production point of
view, given that it serves as an alternative fertilizer to de-
crease the amount of excess nutrients from reaching water
sources, it helps to reduce the risk of eutrophication. In both
ways, struvite recovery technology is promising in terms of its
contributions to managing dairy manure.

process. The red circle in the center indicates the cutoff
value (2%). Sensitivity factors that are located outside of the
red circle (i.e., cutoff value) are considered sensitive pa-
rameters, whereas SFs that are inside the circle are consid-
ered not sensitive. Because the impact assessment results
for both bench and farm scale processes are not sensitive to
magnesium chloride, it is excluded from Figure 5.
In terms of bench‐scale process, as depicted in Figure 5A,
the SFs for 6 inputs are sensitive for more than 5 of the
11 impact categories considered. These can be listed as
HCl, NaOH, energy produced from grid electricity, oxalic
acid, polyacrylamide, and deionized water inputs. With re-
spect to ODP, HCl, NaOH, oxalic acid, deionized water, and
polyacrylamide are found to be the sensitive parameters.
This means that any changes in other inputs (e.g., grid
electricity and MgCl2) will not have any signiﬁcant impact on
the overall ODP of the system. Considering CP, NCP, and
ETP categories, grid electricity is identiﬁed as an additional
sensitive parameter besides the aforementioned ones.
Considering EP, in addition to these parameters, efﬂuent is
identiﬁed as a sensitive parameter. For GWP, SP, and AP
categories, only two of the inputs, oxalic acid and grid
electricity, are identiﬁed as sensitive inputs. For RP, sensitive
inputs are found to be deionized water, oxalic acid, and
electricity grid. Finally, in FFP, polyacrylamide, oxalic acid,
and electricity grid inputs showed sensitivity.
Among all inputs, grid electricity has the highest SFs
(0.8 > SF > 0.15) for 9 of the 11 impact categories (i.e., all
except ODP and WP), meaning that any small change in
electricity input will signiﬁcantly change the overall envi-
ronmental impact of the struvite precipitation from the
AD‐L‐dairy manure process. As expected, the deionized
water input is the most sensitive parameter for WP (SF ≈
0.6), followed by oxalic acid (SF ≈0.31). The trend for
SFs is found to be very different for the ODP impact cat-
egory, where oxalic acid was the most sensitive parameter
(SF ≈0.53), followed by deionized water (SF ≈0.20), NaOH
(SF ≈0.14),
HCl
(SF ≈0.13),
and
polyacrylamide,
re-
spectively. All of the SFs for the bench‐scale struvite pre-
cipitation process are tabulated and can be found in
Supplemental Data Table S13.
Regarding the farm‐scale struvite precipitation process,
the trends for SFs changed drastically. Four inputs of the
system, HCl, NaOH, oxalic acid, and polyacrylamide, are
found to be sensitive parameters for all the 11 impact cat-
egories considered. As presented in Figure 5B, for the farm‐
scale process, oxalic acid showed the highest sensitivity in
all impact categories (SF > 0.65) except FFP (SF ≈0.53), EP
(SF ≈0.35), and WP (SF ≈0.32). This means any small
change in oxalic acid input would change the overall envi-
ronmental impact of the struvite precipitation process. For
EP and WP, the efﬂuent was identiﬁed as the most sensitive
parameter. This is due to the high consumption of ﬂush
water to dilute the AD‐L‐dairy manure and the excess N and
P contained in the discharged stream. All of the SFs for the

Discussion and implications
Struvite precipitation process contributes to both manure
management and nutrient management practices. Given
that N and P recovery can be achieved in the form of
struvite, it may replace the conventional fertilizers. This
decreases the utilization of Haber–Bosch process (Erisman
et al. 2008; Munasinghe‐Arachchige and Nirmalakhandan
2020) as well as reduces the need of phosphate mining
(Chowdhury et al. 2017; Zangarini et al. 2020) to manu-
facture synthetic fertilizers. Additionally, the nutrients (P and
N) that are present in dairy manure can cause eutrophication
(Lötjönen et al. 2020). Because P and N from AD‐L‐dairy
manure can be recovered in the form of struvite, there is a
lower risk of eutrophication due to agricultural runoff. For
reference, 7.29 kg N‐eq/kg of P and 0.986 kg N‐eq/kg of
N of impact (i.e., eutrophication potential) is omitted as a
result of nutrient recovery (USEPA 2014a).
Munasinghe‐Arachchige
and
Nirmalakhandan
(2020)
ranked 5 different technologies for nutrient recovery from
the liquid portion of anaerobically digested sludge, in-
cluding air stripping, ion exchange, struvite precipitation,
reverse osmosis, and gas permeable membrane separation,
using multicriteria decision analysis. Based on 10 perform-
ance criteria, they concluded that struvite precipitation
performs as the second best (after gas permeable mem-
brane technology) in terms of recovery performance, oper-
ating conditions, and the amount of chemical and energy
requirements (Munasinghe‐Arachchige and Nirmalakhandan
2020). Several other researchers recommended the im-
plementation of struvite precipitation technology for both
nutrient recovery and waste management practices due to
its relatively low cost and high performance (Vaneeckhaute
et al. 2017; Macura et al. 2019; Sigurnjak et al. 2020;
Zangarini et al. 2020).
Considering the AD‐L‐dairy manure characteristics (i.e.,
TP, TN, and other parameters as presented in Table 1), a
simple LCA for a direct discharge scenario is modeled in
SimaPro. It is found that, if the AD‐L‐dairy manure would be
discharged directly to the water streams (e.g., surface water,
river) without further treatment process, the eutrophication
potential would be 1.69 × 10–3 kg N‐eq per 1 kg AD‐L‐dairy
manure treated. However, the EP of farm‐scale struvite
precipitation from AD‐L‐dairy manure process is found as
3.80 × 10–4 kg N‐eq per 1 kg AD‐L‐dairy manure treated,
even though it also contains upstream impacts (e.g., energy,
acquisition of chemicals). In Wisconsin, there are 41 farms
with 109 685 cows, which have anaerobic digesters (USEPA
2020b). This is equivalent to 2.194 million tons of dairy
manure generation per year. Taking into account that all of
this would be converted to struvite, more than 2.87 × 106 kg
of N‐eq emissions could be saved annually. This amount of
N‐eq emissions is equivalent to releasing more than 23 900
tons of ammonia to air or dumping 395 tons of P to water.
For reference, the EP of releasing 1 kg of ammonia to air is
0.12 kg N‐eq, and discharging 1 kg of P to water is 7.3 kg
N‐eq (Bare 2012; USEPA 2014a). This highlights the im-
portance of further treatment approaches in nutrient man-
agement and environmental impact mitigation.
Another beneﬁt of struvite crystallization is the reduced
transportation costs for hauling the waste stream. Farmers
with limited available land spaces may want to transport the
manure generated from their farms to off‐site management
facilities, rather than managing them on‐site. However, the
waste stream generated from dairy farms is expected to be
very dilute, which results in high environmental and eco-
nomic costs associated with hauling. Struvite is a light crystal
(Kataki et al. 2016); therefore, its transportation would
be
easier
and
economical
(Sampat,
Martín‐Hernández
et al. 2018). Besides all these direct and indirect beneﬁts
of this technology, chemicals and energy consumed to re-
cover the struvite may have additional environmental im-
plications, which are presented throughout the present
study. Results are intended to inform future research in this
area, particularly evaluating potential costs and beneﬁts of
further processing the dairy manure in order to decrease
environmental
impacts
associated
with
managing
this
inescapable material.
CONCLUSIONS
Struvite crystallization, which is recovering valuable nu-
trients from waste materials (e.g., wastewater or manure), is
one of the promising approaches for nutrient management
practices. The present study investigated the environmental
implications as well as impact hotspots of bench‐scale and
farm‐scale struvite precipitation from AD‐L‐dairy manure
(i.e., digestate from anaerobically digested cow manure)
process using LCA methodology. The midpoint LCA results
highlight that incorporating the struvite recovery process
may help reduce EP up to 78% while managing the amount
of nutrients that reach water bodies as a result of agricultural
runoff. Oxalic acid, polyacrylamide, HCl, and NaOH were
found as sensitive parameters for the majority of impact
categories of both bench‐scale and farm‐scale processes. In
addition to those, deionized water and grid electricity were
identiﬁed as sensitive parameters for the bench‐scale stru-
vite recovery process. In other words, a minor reduction in
any of those inputs can result in a major reduction in overall
environmental impacts. Moreover, with the farm‐scale sce-
nario, due to the utilization of biogas instead of grid elec-
tricity, more than 55% impact reduction was observed in the
majority of environmental impacts associated with the stru-
vite precipitation process. Future work should combine both
economic (e.g., operational and capital expenditure) and
environmental cost and beneﬁt implications of these proc-
esses for strong and sustainable decision making. Finally,
incorporating all respective processes (e.g., anaerobic di-
gestion, screw press, struvite reactor) in the form of a con-
tinuous ﬂow should be considered for more thorough
evaluation.

records.full_text:
Using chlorella vulgaris for nutrient removal from hydroponic wastewater: experimental
investigation and economic assessment
Y. I. D. Yousifa, Essam Sh. Mohamedb and A. S. El-Gendyc,*
a Environmental Engineering Program, The American University in Cairo, New Cairo, Egypt
b Institute of Global Health and Human Ecology, The American University in Cairo, New Cairo, Egypt
c Department of Construction Engineering, The American University in Cairo, New Cairo, Egypt
*Corresponding author. E-mail: ahmed.elgendy@aucegypt.edu
ABSTRACT
The study evaluated the use of Chlorella vulgaris for bioremediating hydroponic wastewater and producing biomass under different cultiva-
tion modes and to explore the economic implications of microalgal biofuels. Total nitrogen (TN) removal efﬁciency was 98.5% in mixotrophic
conditions and 96% in heterotrophic conditions, and total phosphorus (TP) was completely removed (.99%) in both cultivation conditions. TN
removal was higher for that which was cultivated under the mixotrophic mode of cultivation. The maximum biomass production (1.26 g/L)
and biomass productivity (0.1108 g/L/day) were also reported for mixotrophic conditions. Lipid content was slightly higher for that which was
cultivated under heterotrophic conditions: 33 wt% on an ash-free dry weight (AFDW) basis. The highest lipid production was obtained under
mixotrophic growth (0.341 g/L). Higher net proﬁt was obtained for both mixotrophic and heterotrophic cultivations: 30.6 million $/year for a
plant capacity of 3.29  104 tone/year and 30.12 million $/year for a plant capacity of 3.17  104 tone/year respectively. Sensitivity analysis
showed that biodiesel and nutritious supplements from soluble protein have the greatest impact on the process economics regarding mix-
otrophic cultivation, while biodiesel and feeds from insoluble protein have the largest effect on the process economics regarding
heterotrophic and autotrophic cultivations.
Key words: biomass production, lipid production, nutrients recovery, techno-economic assessment
HIGHLIGHTS
• C. vulgaris was cultivated in hydroponic wastewater to remove nutrients and to produce lipid and biomass.
• C. vulgaris showed higher TN removal efﬁciency under mixotrophic growth (TN: 98.5%).
• Maximum lipid content was reported for heterotrophic growth (33 wt% AFDW).
• Mixotrophic cultivation showed maximum biomass production (1.26 g/L) and biomass productivity (0.1108 g/L/day).
• The best economic beneﬁts were obtained for mixotrophic (net proﬁt: 30.6 million $/year) and heterotrophic (net proﬁt: 30.12 million
$/year) cultivations.
This is an Open Access article distributed under the terms of the Creative Commons Attribution Licence (CC BY 4.0), which permits copying, adaptation and
redistribution, provided the original work is properly cited (http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/4.0/).
© 2022 The Authors

GRAPHICAL ABSTRACT
1. INTRODUCTION
In 2019, the average primary energy consumption growth was 1.3% down from 2.8% in 2018 (British Petroleum Company
2020). Natural gas and renewables were responsible for the highest increase in the primary energy consumption, contributing
to three-quarters of the development (British Petroleum Company 2020). The growth in the carbon emissions from energy use
was 0.5%, less than the higher increase in 2018 (British Petroleum Company 2020). Despite these positive trends, it is possible
that fossil fuels will continue to dominate the energy market for a certain period, especially with the exploration of uncon-
ventional resources. However, this trend will contribute directly to man-made climate change, as it is attributed primarily
to greenhouse gas emissions from the widespread usage of fossil fuels and land use change. Microalgae, however, represent
an alternative strategy for the removal of nutrients from wastewater, the mitigation of CO2, and the production of biofuels, for
they are capable of both bioremediating wastewater through the uptake of nutrients, and of producing a considerable amount
of biomass that can be used to produce biofuels (Razzak et al. 2017). Yet, the higher cost of microalgal biofuel production
represents a major challenge in the production of biofuels and other valuable byproducts (Park et al. 2011). It was estimated
that the cultivation accounts for the highest cost (77% of the total biodiesel production cost), followed by harvesting (12% of
the total biodiesel production cost) and extraction (8% of the total biodiesel production cost) (Ge et al. 2015). For enhancing
the commercialization of biomass and biofuel production, various strategies have been proposed. For instance, Abomohra
et al. (2021) assessed in-situ co-transesteriﬁcation of wet spent coffee ground/microalgae mixture to enhance the fatty acid
ethyl esters yield. Han et al. (2016) evaluated the impact of optimizing the aeration conditions on growth, fatty acid proﬁle,
and lipid production of Scenedesmus obliquus by culturing the strain in a pre-treated municipal wastewater. To enhance bio-
diesel production, Almutairi et al. (2021) designed a pioneering approach consisting of two-step biodiesel production from
lipidic food waste followed by microalgae cultivation. Abomohra & Almutairi (2020) examined the application of anaerobic
digestate for both microalgae cultivation and biodiesel production and the effect of dilute-acid pretreatment of the residual
biomass on biomethane production. Creating cultivation conditions for microalgal biomass and lipid production as well as
nutrient removal is critical and presents a major challenge in the production of biofuels and the bioremediation of wastewater
(Park et al. 2011).
Integrating microalgae production facility with waste streams, such as wastewater treatment, would play an important role
for reducing the environmental externalities and for boosting the commerciality of microalgal biofuels and the attainment of
Sustainable Development Goals. Wastewater contains a considerable amount of nutrients and energy-rich organics that
Water Science & Technology Vol 85 No 11, 3241
Downloaded from http://iwaponline.com/wst/article-pdf/85/11/3240/1062408/wst085113240.pdf
by guest

can be used to grow and cultivate microalgae (Abdel-Raouf et al. 2012). Various types of wastewater (e.g., agricultural and
aquaculture wastewaters) have been employed as a medium of cultivation for microalgal growth (Bhatnagar et al. 2011;
Hawrot-Paw et al. 2020). Strain selection, cultivation systems (open pond system or closed system), and cultivation conditions
(e.g., temperature, light intensity, pH, type of wastewater, cultural metabolism) are some of the important factors that can
positively or negatively affect microalgal biomass and lipid production (Chisti 2016). Furthermore, the enhancement of bio-
mass and lipid producing ability of microalgae for different cultivation modes (autotrophic, heterotrophic, mixotrophic
modes) present a major challenge for wastewater-based microalgal cultivation systems.
Enhancing microalgal growth rate cannot be achieved without selecting and optimizing an appropriate cultivation mode in
order to enhance biomass yields and lipid production and to boost the marketability of microalgal biofuels (Singh et al. 2011).
Autotrophic cultivation is the most widely used cultivation method for the growth of microalgae. However, autotrophic cul-
turing of microalgae offers lower nutrients removals and biomass production owing to photoinhibition, which indicates light
deﬁciency, as some microalgal cells do not receive enough light because of shading phenomena (Bux & Chisti 2016). This
directly affects and increases the biomass harvesting cost of autotrophic cultivation. Mixotrophic and heterotrophic, however,
may represent an alternative strategy for large-scale operation of microalgal biofuels as they offer a reasonable amount of bio-
mass and lipid and remove nutrients from wastewater. A multiplicity of biofuels and other valuable products can be obtained
by cultivating microalgae mixotrophically and heterotrophically (Bassi et al. 2014). The heterotrophic mode of cultivation
offers various products, from cellular storage compounds, such as lipids and starch, to a vast number of hydrocarbons and
polysaccharides that can boost the prospect for commercialization. The mixotrophic mode of cultivation is beneﬁcial
when it comes to producing pigments, lipids, proteins, and alkanes. Although many studies have studied the effect of different
cultivation modes on the growth of microalgae, removal of nutrients, biomass production, and lipid production for different
types of wastewater, there is a dearth of information in literature on the use of microalgae to remove nutrient, to produce
biomass, and to produce lipid from hydroponic wastewater under different cultivation modes.
On the economic assessment of utilizing microalgae to produce biofuels, a number of studies have been published on the
subject, with varying results that can be attributed to issues such as process boundaries, type of cultivation system, variations
in downstream conversion processing pathways, and core modeling assumptions (Clarens et al. 2010; Campbell et al. 2011).
Another reason for such varying results relates to the various range of oil yields from the microalgal biomass, a variation
which directly affects the cost of microalgal biodiesel and other valuable products (Quinn & Davis 2015). These inconsisten-
cies resulted in varying results in the cost of producing microalgal biomass and biodiesel as this technology is still in its early
stages. Though many studies have investigated the commercial viability of cultivating microalgae under autotrophic and het-
erotrophic conditions, evaluations of the economic implications of mixotrophic biomass are absent in the literature.
This study examined the effectiveness of the use of C. vulgaris for removing nutrients from hydroponic wastewater and pro-
ducing biomass and lipid under different cultivation conditions and evaluated the commercial viability of microalgal biofuels,
focusing on the effect of different cultivation modes. The novelty of this research was to evaluate alternatives for hydroponic
wastewater, wastewater that is rich with nutrients, to avoid discharging it to the nearby water channels by cultivating micro-
algae for the dual aim of wastewater treatment and biomass production. To examine the effect of different growth conditions
on nutrients removal, biomass, and lipid production using C. vulgaris, a laboratory experiment (small scale model) was
employed in highly controlled conditions. To assess the economic implications of different cultural metabolisms, data
obtained from the small-scale model and from the literature were used. These data were employed to stimulate the function
of the large-scale facility (industrial-scale model) and to integrate the laboratory-based production data from the small-scale
model.
2. MATERIALS AND METHODS
2.1. Preparation of culture medium and C. vulgaris inoculum
C. vulgaris was isolated at and obtained from the Department of Biology at The American University in Cairo, New Cairo,
Egypt (Badr 2020). Woods Hole Marine Biological Laboratory (MBL) medium recipe as recommended for freshwater micro-
algae was used to preculture and preserve the microalgae strain (C. vulgaris) before the experiments, and it consists of the
following stock solutions: 36.76 g CaCl2.2H2O, 36.97 g MgSO4.7H2O, 12.6 g NaHCO3, 8.71 g K2HPO4, 85.01 g NaNO3,
28.42 g Na2SiO3.9H2O, 4.36 g Na2EDTA, 3.15 g FeCl3.6H2O, 0.01 g CuSO4.5H2O, 0.022 g ZnSO4.7H2O, 0.01 g CoCl2.6H2O,
0.18 g MnCl2.4H2O, 0.006 g Na2MoO4.2H2O, 0.005 g/L dH2O Cyanocobalamin (Vitamin B12), 0.1 g/L dH2O Thiamine HCL
Water Science & Technology Vol 85 No 11, 3242
Downloaded from http://iwaponline.com/wst/article-pdf/85/11/3240/1062408/wst085113240.pdf
by guest

(Vitamin B1), 0.0005 g/L dH2O Biotin, 250 g/L dH2O tire stock (Nichols 1973). To prepare the MBL medium, 1 mL of each of
the above-mentioned solution was added to 1 L of distilled water. C. vulgaris was initially inoculated in a 500 mL ﬂask contain-
ing 250 mL of synthetic medium (MBL) at 10% concentration. The initial pH of the culture was adjusted between 7 to 8 by using
NaOH (base) or HCl (acid) while simultaneously using pH meter. Before microalgae inoculation, the culture media was auto-
claved for 15 min and 120C for sterilization. The experiment (inoculation stage) was performed under stationary conditions
with a temperature of 22 (þ=  3) C, a light intensity of 80 μmol m2 s1, and a light regime of 16:8 (light: dark).
2.2. Wastewater collection and characterization
Hydroponic wastewater was collected from the efﬂuent tank of an agriculture greenhouse located at the Center for Applied
Research on the Environment and Sustainability (CARES) at The American University in Cairo (AUC). The hydroponic
wastewater sample was stored immediately at 4 °C and ﬁltered to separate out the suspended particles. The wastewater
sample was then sterilized before inoculating C. vulgaris to kill or deactivate any microorganism (e.g., bacteria, fungi) that
could be present in the wastewater. Also, the wastewater sample was analyzed at the Agriculture Research Center to deter-
mine biochemical oxygen demand (BOD), chemical oxygen demand (COD), nutrients composition (total nitrogen and total
phosphorus) before and after using C. vulgaris to bioremediate hydroponic wastewater. COD and BOD were measured based
on the Standard Methods for the Examination of Water and Wastewater (Rice et al. 2017). Total nitrogen was analyzed
according to a method recommended by Jackson (1973) while total phosphorus was assessed based on a procedure described
by APHA (1989).
2.3. Experiment design
The experiment was conducted in sterile conditions in which the collected wastewater sample had undergone sterilization to
deactivate any form of bacteria or living microorganisms that could be present in the collected sample. The experiment con-
sisted of nine reactors in total with three treatments and three triplicates for each treatment, and it continued for 23 days. For
autotrophic culture, atmospheric CO2 was used as an inorganic carbon source for C. vulgaris growth. On heterotrophic cul-
ture, 5 g/L of glucose, as an organic source of carbon, was injected and cultured under complete darkness by wrapping the
reactors with aluminum foil. Regarding mixotrophic culture, both organic and inorganic carbon sources were used: 2.5 g/L of
glucose and atmospheric CO2 respectively. For each reactor, C. vulgaris was inoculated in a 500 mL Erlenmeyer ﬂask con-
tained 400 mL of hydroponic wastewater sample at 10% concentration. The experimental conditions were as following: an
initial pH 7 to 8, a temperature 25–30 °C, a light regime of 12:12 (light: dark) for mixotrophic conditions, and 24 h of light for
autotrophic conditions using white ﬂuorescent lamps.
2.4. Analytical methods
2.4.1. Nutrient analysis
To determine total nitrogen (TN) and total phosphorus (TP) removals, liquid samples of the culture media were collected at
the end of the experiment. To collect these samples, a predetermined volume of these samples was taken and then ﬁltered by a
0.45 Whatman GF/C membrane ﬁlter. Total nitrogen was then analyzed according to a method recommended by Jackson
(1973) while total phosphorus was assessed based on a procedure described by APHA (1989). The removal efﬁciency of
total nitrogen and total phosphorus is given by the Equation (1) (Salgueiro et al. 2016):
Removal efficency (%) ¼ (x0  x1)
x0
100
(1)
where x0 denotes the initial concentration of the nutrient, and x1 the ﬁnal concentration of the nutrient.
2.4.2. Growth rate and biomass determination
Microalgae growth was determined by measuring optical density (OD) at 680 nm using a spectrophotometer. Microalgal bio-
mass was determined gravimetrically at the initial, middle, and end of the experiment. A predetermined culture volume was
harvested using sterilized ﬁltrate and subsequently dewatered by centrifugation at 4,000 rpm for 15 min. The pellets were
washed twice with distilled water and ﬁltered by using a pre-dried Whatman GF/C ﬁlter paper (1.2 μm pore size). The micro-
algal cells on the ﬁlter paper were dried in a laboratory oven at 105 °C until constant weight, and then a desiccator was used
to cool down the cells to room temperature before weighing. The speciﬁc growth rates (μ) and biomass productivity (P) are

2.4.3. Lipid analysis
Total lipid content was performed according to a method reported by Bligh (1959) as follows: 8 mL of microalgae strain with
10 mL chloroform and 20 mL methanol were mixed and blended for 2 min using a blender. This was done to achieve a bal-
ance of 1:2:0.8 (parts of chloroform: parts of methanol: water). The mixture was again provided with another 10 mL
chloroform and blended for 30 seconds. To get a ratio of 2:2:1.8 (parts of chloroform: parts of methanol: water), 10 mL of
distilled water was added and mixed for 30 seconds using a blender. Whatman ﬁlter paper (No. 1) in line with Buchner
funnel with a slight section was used to ﬁlter the homogenate. The pressure was applied immediately at the bottom of a
beaker when the residue dried. This was done to ensure the maximum recovery of the solvent. Once the solvent was recov-
ered, it was necessary to transfer the ﬁltrate to a 50 mL graduated cylinder and to allow it to complete the separation into two
phases for some minutes, and then the chloroform layer’s volume was recorded. A pipette was used to remove the methanol
phase with a small volume of chloroform to ensure a complete removal of the methanol phase. Many pre-weighed dishes of
aluminum were prepared and then provided with 5 mL of the chloroform layer. To evaporate the chloroform, the aluminum
dishes were heated in the fume hood. When the heating process was completed, thin layers of lipids were left in the aluminum
dishes, and the dishes were immediately dried in a drying oven at 105 °C for 15 min to remove any remained chloroform’s
traces. A desiccator was used for a long time for cooling the aluminum dishes, and the dishes were weighted again to deter-
mine the lipid content. Then, Equation (4) was used to calculate the lipid productivity of C. vulgaris (Guldhe et al. 2017):
lipid productivity (g=L=d) ¼ biomass productivity (g=L=d) lipid content

2.5. Statistical analysis
Data reported as a mean and standard deviation of three replicate experiments. ANOVA test was used to assess the signiﬁ-
cance of the difference between treatments wherever applicable. All statistical tests were conducted using Microsoft Excel.
2.6. Techno-economic assessment (TEA)
2.6.1. TEA: cultivation
The techno-economic model assumed the cultivation of C. vulgaris in a plastic bag PBRs. It is based on a model characterized
by Zhu et al. (2018) (Table 1). According to Zhu et al. (2018), the areal productivity in conjunction with plastic bag PBR sys-
tems diverges based on a variety of conditions such as bag dimensions and temperature. For instance, biomass productivity
ranging from 5 to 35 g/m2/day has been reported by Ting et al. (2017) and Zittelli et al. (2013). Furthermore, it was not poss-
ible to construct a pilot-scale experiment to collect more data with regard to the techno-economic assessment, mainly due to
the associated costs. Therefore, only data obtained from the laboratory experiment were upscaled and used to conduct the
techno-economic assessment. Several lines of evidence suggest that reducing the costs of biomass production of large-
scale microalgae cultivation would not be achieved without ensuring a biomass productivity that of at least 25 g/m2/d on
an AFDW (Jones et al. 2014; Davis et al. 2016; Zhu et al. 2018). In two of the most current economic assessment studies
conducted by Jones et al. (2014) and Davis et al. (2016) to examine the economic potential for the production of algal biomass
for conversion to biofuels and to assess the downstream production costs of microalgal biofuel, respectively, future target
cases with regard to biomass productivity were assumed. Jones et al. (2014) assumed a target of 25 g/m2/day of biomass pro-
ductivity on an AFDW on the strength of the annual average rates across individual seasons whereas Davis et al. (2016)
assumed a target of 30 g/m2/day of biomass productivity for producing an annual average of 1,340 tons/day of algae on
an AFDW. A biomass productivity that of 25 g/m2/day on an AFDW was also assumed according to the model characterized

by guest

by Zhu et al. (2018). Based on the foregoing, biomass productivities (for autotrophic, heterophonic, and mixotrophic modes)
obtained from the laboratory experiment (small-scale model) were upscaled by a factor of 5 to get approximate values of bio-
mass productivities that would be suitable for large microalgae cultivation facility (a target of 25 g/m2/day). The harvesting
density of biomass was assumed to be 2 g/L. In accordance with industrial input and commercial feasibility, the cultivation
area was assumed to be 1,000 acres. The PBRs energy requirements were assumed to be mainly for water circulation
(287 W/m3), aeration (340 kWh/short tonne AFDW), and algae dewatering (Zhu et al. 2018).
2.6.2. Techno-economic assumptions
The techno-economic model for this research work was conducted in light of the costs presented by Zhu et al. (2018), Wijffels
et al. (2010), and Xu et al. (2006). These costs included direct and indirect capital investment cost regarding the microalgal
facility, ﬁxed operating costs, and variable operating costs, biomass production cost, and the market values of the products.
According to Zhu et al. (2018), the biomass production cost per annual AFDW short ton algae is $1,137, which is equivalent
to $1,253.328 per annual AFDW metric ton (1.253 $/kg) (Table 2). This value (1.253 $/kg) was used to calculate the total
biomass production cost per each cultivation condition ($/year): biomass production cost ($/kg) multiplied by algae
annual productivity (kg/year). Following that, microalgae biomass (obtained from the experiment) was assumed to be reﬁned
into various products for bulk chemical markets (Figure 1). Sajadian et al. (2018) assumed different biomass compositions
based on the growth mode of microalgae (mixotrophic, heterotrophic, and autotrophic conditions). For mixotrophic
growth mode, a composition of 44.25% lipids, 31.2% protein, 19.44% carbohydrates, and other products of 5.11% were
assumed while a composition of 48.68% lipids, 33.55% protein, 13.16% carbohydrates, and other products of 4.61% were
assumed for heterotrophic mode. For autotrophic mode, a composition of 34.01% lipids, 41.7% protein, 17.41% carbo-
hydrates, and 6.88% of other products were assumed. Wijffels et al. (2010) and Xu et al. (2006) also assumed different
compositions of microalgae biomass for the autotrophic and heterotrophic mode of cultivation in line with their market
prices. The two analyses assumed that 25% of the lipids fraction can be used for producing bulk chemicals with a market
value of 2.5 $/kg while the remaining can be utilized for producing a renewable source of energy (biodiesel) with a
market value of 0.72 $/kg (Table 2). For protein fraction, 20% of a water-soluble fraction with a market value of 6.3 $/kg
and 80% of a water-insoluble fraction with a feed value of 0.95 $/kg were assumed (Table 2). A 100% of carbohydrates frac-
tion was assumed to have a market value of 1.26 $/kg (Table 2). These costs were assumed for this study to calculate the total

3. RESULTS AND DISCUSSION
3.1. Wastewater characteristics
The wastewater quality parameters of hydroponic wastewater sample collected from the efﬂuent (disposal) tank located at the
Center for Applied Research on the Environment and Sustainability (CARES) at The American University in Cairo were
assessed in terms of total nitrogen (TN), total phosphorus (TP), COD, and BOD as shown in Table 3.
3.2. Microalgae growth
The inoculum of C. vulgaris was grown in the wastewater sample to get the amount of starter inoculum that can be used for
bioremediation of wastewater sample and the production of biomass and lipid. The proﬁle of the absorbance at 680 nm wave-
length during the acclimation of C. vulgaris in the hydroponic wastewater sample is shown in Figure 2. As can be seen from
Figure 2, the OD680 values for the growth of C. vulgaris after 23 days of cultivation in mixotrophic, heterotrophic, and auto-
trophic conditions were 1.3, 1.2, and 0.23 respectively. The growth of microalgae was higher in mixotrophic and
heterotrophic conditions than in autotrophic conditions. C. vulgaris growth rates in mixotrophic, heterotrophic, and auto-
trophic modes were 0.154 day1, 0.144 day1, and 0.073 day1, respectively.
As regards the autotrophic growth of microalgae, the most fundamental factor is light intensity and atmospheric CO2, as
microalgae use both as a major source for energy and carbon. Microalgae use organic carbon as the sole source for carbon
and energy for heterotrophic conditions (Cheirsilp & Torpee 2012). Mixotrophic microalgae use light as an energy source,
and both organic and inorganic carbon as a carbon source. A possible explanation for the slower growth rate in autotrophic
culture might be due to the photoinhibition arising from high microalgae cells and the shading phenomena. This effect indi-
cates that microalgal cells did not receive enough light due to the high density of microalgal cells that were present in the
upper zone of the culture (light did not distribute equally) or due to the low ambient atmospheric CO2 concentration (Bux
& Chisti 2016). However, C. vulgaris growth in both mixotrophic and heterotrophic cultures was higher, indicating that

organic carbon and light were the major reasons for the higher growth of the strain. These results are in agreement with other
studies found in the literature (Liang et al. 2009; Bhatnagar et al. 2011).
3.3. Algal nutrients removal
The total nitrogen removal amounts were 27 mg/L, 28.8 mg/L, 29.55 mg/L in autotrophic, heterotrophic, and mixotrophic
conditions, respectively, and the removal amount in mixotrophic conditions was higher than in autotrophic and hetero-
trophic cultures (Table 4). Phosphorus was almost completely in all cultural conditions. TN removal efﬁciency was 98.5%
in mixotrophic conditions, 96% in heterotrophic conditions, and 90% in autotrophic conditions, and TP removal efﬁciency
was completely removed (.99%) in mixotrophic and heterotrophic conditions (Table 4).
Microalgae have a higher capacity to remove nutrients from wastewater as they use them for various physiological pro-
cesses to generate biomass rich in lipids, carbohydrates, and proteins. Nitrogen is a critical nutrient that is used by
microalgae for protein and genetic material synthesis.
Also, phosphorous is another important nutrient that is necessitated for short term storage and transfer of energy (Kim et al.
2016). Inorganic nitrogen compounds (ammonium, nitrate, and nitrite) are utilized by microalgae through a process of assim-
ilation in which nitrite and nitrate undertake reduction to form ammonium, which is subsequently incorporated into amino
acid using glutamine synthetase (Cai et al. 2013b). The removal efﬁciency of total nitrogen under mixotrophic and hetero-
trophic growth was higher than under autotrophic growth while total phosphorus removal efﬁciency was almost the same
in mixotrophic and heterotrophic conditions unlike that for autotrophic conditions.
During heterotrophic mode of microalgae growth, the organic carbon source presence in the medium plays an essential
role in the removal efﬁciency of both nitrogen and phosphorus. Microalgae use light and both organic and inorganic
carbon sources during mixotrophic growth to enhance microalgal growth, thereby increasing the removal efﬁciency of nutri-
ents. The addition of glucose as a carbon source for both heterotrophic and mixotrophic might be a major reason for the
higher nitrogen and phosphorous removal efﬁciency that were obtained from the experiment compared to that for auto-
trophic growth. According to Martínez et al. (2000), the transfer rate of energy absorbed by microalgae with regard to
ATP for autotrophic, heterotrophic, and mixotrophic are 10%, 18%, and 12% respectively. For autotrophic growth, ATP is
generated from mitochondria and then around 77% of ATP is used for ﬁxation of CO2 via the Calvin cycle while the remain-
ing is converted to organic compounds. According to that explanation, the small growth rate and removal efﬁciency of
nitrogen and phosphorous of autotrophic mode can be attributed to the low energy portion for cell synthesis in contrast
to heterotrophic and mixotrophic modes, where the higher growth rate and nutrient removal rate might be attributed to
the higher energy portion for cell synthesis.
However, the higher removal efﬁciency of nitrogen and phosphorous obtained under autotrophic conditions (around 90%)
indicates that the utilization of C. vulgaris for bioremediation of hydroponic wastewater is also a plausible strategy for remov-
ing nitrogen and phosphorous from wastewater. Almost complete removal of nitrogen and phosphorus has been achieved
under mixotrophic and heterotrophic culturing of C. vulgaris which might be possibly due to the higher transfer rate of
energy absorbed by microalgae with regard to ATP under these modes. These ﬁndings are in accordance with other results
obtained from the literature. In a study conducted by Wang & Lan (2011) to examine the removal efﬁciency of nutrients by
using Neochloris oleoabundans cultured in a synthetic secondary municipal wastewater efﬂuent, they found that total nitro-
gen removal can reach 99% based on nitrogen to phosphorous ratio and that complete removal of phosphorous can be
achieved. Almost 100% removal efﬁciencies for total nitrogen and total phosphorous were obtained by Yang et al. (2016).
This higher removal efﬁciencies were corresponding to the initial total nitrogen and total phosphorous of 12 mg/L and
1.8 mg/L, respectively.

Analysis of variance (ANOVA one-way) was performed to assess the signiﬁcance of difference between treatments. Sum of
squares, degree of freedom, means of squares, p-value, and f-value were obtained by using the test. If the obtained p-value is
higher than 0.05, the difference between the assessed values is not signiﬁcant while the difference is signiﬁcant when the
value is lower than 0.05. In this regard, total nitrogen (TN) and total phosphorus (TP) removal efﬁciencies determined for
autotrophic, heterotrophic, and mixotrophic conditions were found to be signiﬁcantly different (P-values ,0.05).
3.4. Biomass concentration and biomass productivity of C. vulgaris
Biomass concentration (g/L) and biomass productivity (g/L/day) under mixotrophic, heterotrophic, and autotrophic cultur-
ing of microalgae in hydroponic wastewater sample is shown in Table 5. Biomass concentration and biomass productivity
under mixotrophic growth were 1.26 g/L and 0.1108 g/L/day which were higher than that of heterotrophic growth
(1.03 g/L and 0.107 g/L/day) and autotrophic growth (0.23 g/L and 0.016 g/L/day).
The lower biomass concentration and biomass productivity obtained during autotrophic growth compared with during het-
erotrophic and mixotrophic growth might be due to photoinhibition, which indicates light deﬁciency as some microalgal cell
do not receive enough light because of shading phenomena (Bux & Chisti 2016). Also light energy – the amount of light
received by microalgae – that is directly correlated with photon ﬂux density that hits the surface of the culture might be a
possible reason for the lower biomass concentration and productivity as it directly affects microalgal growth and therefore
biomass productivity (Richmond 2004). Furthermore, insufﬁcient inorganic carbon supplementation might be also a cause
for the lower biomass concentration and biomass productivity due to the low ambient atmospheric CO2 concentration.
This observation supports evidence from previous studies. For example, Abedini Najafabadi et al. (2015) reported a biomass
concentration and biomass productivity of 1.1 g/L and 0.076 g/L/day when no carbon was supplied (with fresh air) and that
cell density and biomass productivity were increased when carbon was supplemented with 3% CO2 resulting in 1.46 g/L of
biomass concentration and 0.111 g/L/day of biomass productivity.
Under mixotrophic culturing, microalgae use light as an energy source while they use both organic and inorganic carbon as
carbon source. The highest biomass concentration and productivity were obtained during the mixotrophic growth of C. vulgaris
with 2.5 g/L glucose followed by heterotrophic growth. Some studies have demonstrated that C. vulgaris possesses a hexose
transport system that can be achieved by glucose (Komor & Tanner 1971; Haass & Tanner 1974). The current research
found that C. vulgaris can grow well on hydroponic wastewater supplemented with a predetermined glucose concentration
under mixotrophic and heterotrophic conditions. This carbon source addition has resulted in higher biomass concentration
and biomass productivity in mixotrophic and heterotrophic growth than in autotrophic growth. Besides, C. vulgaris can also
be affected by the light, as it was reported in some studies that some strains of C. vulgaris grow only in darkness (Haass &
Tanner 1974) while others grow only under the presence of light (Karlander & Krauss 1966). Based on this study’s observations,
it can be concluded that C. vulgaris grows better under mixotrophic growth and therefore results in higher biomass concen-
tration and biomass productivity than under heterotrophic and autotrophic cultures. The result reported here agrees with the
conclusions drawn by previous work. For instance, Liang et al. (2009) reported that biomass concentration under mixotrophic
growth has been enhanced from 0.25 g/L under autotrophic conditions to 0.77 g/L when glycerol was added as a carbon source.
Growth rates, biomass productions, and biomass productivities determined for different cultivation modes (autotrophic,
heterotrophic, and mixotrophic modes) were found to be statistically different (P-values ,0.05).

3.5. Lipid accumulation of C. vulgaris under autotrophic, heterotrophic, and mixotrophic conditions
C. vulgaris produced its highest lipid content values under heterotrophic, followed by under mixotrophic and then auto-
trophic conditions (Table 5). The ﬁnal lipid content was 0.374 g/L (29.8 wt% on an ash-free dry weight ‘AFDW’), 0.341 g/L
(33 wt% AFDW), and 0.0342 g/L (15 wt% AFDW) under mixotrophic, heterotrophic, and autotrophic conditions, respectively.
It appears that the highest lipid production was obtained under mixotrophic growth followed by under heterotrophic growth
and then autotrophic growth, while the highest lipid productivity, which is a product of biomass productivity and lipid content,
was obtained under heterotrophic conditions (Table 5).
Lipid is one of the important products from microalgal biomass production, and it is produced by microalgae for the
purpose of energy-saving regarding their cytoplasm. Lipid also is a key factor in the production of biofuels through var-
ious processes, such as esteriﬁcation. The highest lipid concentration was obtained under mixotrophic conditions even
though the lipid content was higher under heterotrophic mode. The higher biomass production obtained under mixo-
trophic conditions might be a reason for the highest lipid concentration, where lipid concentration is a product of dry
weight and lipid content. The distinctive process regarding mixotrophic microalgae which use both organic and inorganic
carbon as a carbon source played a vital role in enhancing the biomass production of microalgae and subsequently the
lipid producing ability of C. vulgaris. The two sources of carbon, glucose and atmospheric CO2 were used in this study to
grow C. vulgaris mixotrophically, indicating that carbon was available for the growth of C. vulgaris, and therefore boosted
the proportion of the storage lipids. Heterotrophic is also a plausible strategy for lipid production, and it is considered to
be useful in lipid accumulation, which is obvious based on the highest lipid content obtained under this mode (Perez-
Garcia et al. 2011). It also seems possible that the presence of organic carbon (glucose) as the sole source of carbon
and energy for heterotrophic growth enhanced the lipid content of C. vulgaris; however, the lowest lipid concentration
obtained under this mode in comparison to under mixotrophic mode might be due to some factors, such as the glucose
concentration and nitrogen concentration (Mohammad Mirzaie et al. 2016). While autotrophic microalgae use light and
inorganic carbon as both energy and carbon sources, the obtained lipid concentration and lipid content were very small
compared to that for mixotrophic and heterotrophic growth. Low ambient atmospheric CO2 and unequal light distri-
bution among microalgal cells might be a cause that affected the lipid producing ability of C. vulgaris under
autotrophic cultivation.
In accordance with the present results, previous studies have demonstrated that the highest lipid production and lipid con-
tent can be obtained under mixotrophic and heterotrophic modes of cultivation. In a study conducted by Mohammad Mirzaie
et al. (2016), a maximum lipid concentration of 0.86 g/L was obtained under mixotrophic conditions, a concentration corre-
sponding to the maximum biomass concentration of 2.62 g/L. Liu et al. (2011) reported that a high amounts of lipid can be
accumulated by Chlorella protothecoides when the strain was cultivated under heterotrophic mode. Liang et al. (2009)
reported a higher lipid content of C. vulgaris cultivated under photoautotrophic conditions than under mixotrophic and het-
erotrophic cultivations, an observation that is not in accordance with the results presented in this study. This rather
contradictory result may be due to enhanced light intensity and inorganic carbon source supplementation, for the light inten-
sity and inorganic carbon source are the fundamental factors in enhancing biomass production as well as the lipid producing
ability of microalgae under autotrophic cultivation. It is possible, therefore, that the lipid producing ability of microalgae is
dependent on many factors, such as strain selection, organic and inorganic carbon source, and light intensity. However, it
seems that the addition of glucose as an organic source of carbon played an essential role in enhancing both the biomass
and the lipid producing ability under both mixotrophic and heterotrophic growth.
Lipid productions and lipid productivities determined for autotrophic, heterotrophic, and mixotrophic conditions were
found to be statistically different (P-values ,0.05).
3.6. Techno-economic assessment (TEA)
3.6.1. TEA: biomass productivities and scale-up
Biomass productivities for mixotrophic, heterotrophic, and autotrophic modes of cultivation obtained from the experiment
were upscaled by a factor of 5 as explained earlier in Section 2.6.1 and used to calculate the algal annual productivity
(tons/year AFDW) (Table 6). From data presented in Table 5, it is apparent that the highest algal annual productivity was
obtained for mixotrophic culture of 32,900 tons/year, followed by an algal annual productivity of 31,700 tons/year for hetero-
trophic growth while the lowest algal annual productivity was obtained for autotrophic growth.

3.6.2. TEA: biomass production cost, market values of the products, and Net proﬁt
Biomass compositions, percentages of the products from biomass compositions, and their market prices were used to calcu-
late the total biomass production cost, the total value of the products, and the net proﬁt. Results regarding biomass production
and market values of the products for mixotrophic, heterotrophic, and autotrophic modes are shown in Table 7, Table 8, and
Table 9, respectively. As regards mixotrophic mode, annual total production cost and market values of the products are 41.2
million $/year and 71.8 million $/year, respectively, resulting in a net proﬁt of 30.6 million $/year as presented in Table 7.
What stands out in Figure 3 are that biofuels and nutritious supplements from soluble protein accounted for the highest bio-
mass production cost and market values of the products, respectively. Nitrogen removal resulted in a cost-saving of 7.71
million $/year while 18.4 million $/year of a cost-saving accounted for phosphorous removal. The total yearly production
cost and market values of the products regarding heterotrophic culture were 39.8 million $/year and 69.9 million $/year
while the net proﬁt was 30.12 million $/year (Table 8). From Figure 4, the greatest contribution for biomass production
cost was accounted for by biofuels from lipids followed by feeds from insoluble protein and then by a small share from nutri-
tious supplements from soluble protein. Nutritious supplements from soluble protein accounted for the highest market values
of the products, with a small contribution from bulk chemicals from carbohydrates. A saving of 7.44 million $/year was
reported for nitrogen removal while 17.7 million $/year was reported for phosphorous removal (Table 8). The results regard-
ing autotrophic mode, as shown in Table 9, indicate that a 5.95 million $/year was the total annual production cost, while a
10.68 million $/year was the yearly total market value of the products. This resulted in a net proﬁt of 4.73 million $/year
annually. Nitrogen and phosphorous removal resulted in a cost-saving of 1.11 million $/year and 2.65 million $/year, respect-
ively. From Figure 5, the production cost is dominated by the feed from insoluble protein and biofuels, while the market
values of the products are dominated by the nutritious supplement from soluble protein as well as feed from insoluble protein.

3.6.3. TEA: discussion
In general, higher net proﬁts were attributed to both mixotrophic and heterotrophic culturing of microalgae, while a small net
proﬁt was attributed to autotrophic mode. This indicates that mixotrophic and heterotrophic cultivations offer many oppor-
tunities for boosting the commercial feasibility of producing biofuels. Considering the higher composition of lipid in
heterotrophic culture (48.68 wt% AFDW) and mixotrophic cultivation (44.25 wt% AFDW), the production of biodiesel is
highly attractive. The highest biomass obtained from mixotrophic mode might be a reason for the higher proﬁt obtained
from this economic assessment. This is because mixotrophic microalgae grow both heterotrophically and autotrophically,
which play an important role in enhancing the growth rate and biomass production of mixotrophic microalgae. Hetero-
trophic cultivation also showed a higher possibility of increasing the proﬁt if a large cultivation facility is built since it

offers a reasonable amount of lipids (48.68 wt% AFDW) which is a key factor for biodiesel production. Heterotrophic micro-
algae are completely dependent on carbon source as they use it as both carbon and energy source, eliminating the cost of light
supplementation that is needed for both autotrophic and mixotrophic microalgae. Although autotrophic microalgae also offer
many advantages with regards to the environment, the economic assessment showed that huge improvements in terms of
enhancing biomass production are needed to boost the prospects of commercialization. The lower biomass production
obtained from autotrophic microalgae could be a probable reason for the lower proﬁt obtained from this mode of cultivation.
This lower biomass could be increased with the enhancement of photosynthetic efﬁciency and avoidance of photoinhibition.
Despite the highest cost of biomass production with regards to large-scale microalgae facility, more opportunities exist if the
current technology is developed and if more products are explored to reduce the cost of biomass production and reﬁnery. The
economic analysis also showed that additional economic and environmental beneﬁts can be attained if wastewater is used as
a cultivation medium. This conclusion agrees with other studies found in the literature. For instance, Xin et al. (2016) con-
ducted a techno-economic analysis on the production of bio-oil, syngas, bio-char, and a phosphorus-rich co-product from
microalgae cultivated on centrate from municipal wastewater, and they found that a cost of $2.23/gallon regarding microalgal
bio-oil can be competitive to the cost of crude oil. The study also reported that the use of co-products as fertilizers can play an
important role towards enhancing the economic viability and environmental sustainability of microalgal biofuels. Using
wastewater as a cultivation medium for microalgal growth is signiﬁcantly important as it can play a simultaneous role in
removing the nutrients load in wastewater and producing biomass for sustainable biofuel production as well as in reducing
the burden on freshwater resources. As the removal of nitrogen and phosphorous from wastewater through biological
removal technologies is an expensive process, the utilization of microalgae for the purpose of removing such nutrients will
tremendously eliminate such expensive processes. This can be achieved through the integration of the microalgae production
system with wastewater treatment, leading to higher saving in cost for both phosphorous and nitrogen removals. The success-
ful integration of microalgae cultivation facility with wastewater treatment plants will play a big role in reducing
environmental externalities and simultaneously boosting the prospects of attaining SDG 6, 14, 13.
Techno-economic assessment (TEA) also showed that there is a need for reﬁning microalgal biomass into various products
to reduce the cost of biomass production while transforming the economics of bioreﬁnery. For instance, with the optimization
of biomass production cost and the enhancement of the lipids content regarding heterotrophic and mixotrophic microalgae, it
is possible to transform the entire lipids into biodiesel and to subsequently reduce the cost of biomass production. Such
enhancement would play a critical role in boosting the commercial feasibility of microalgal biodiesel and even make it com-
petitive with petroleum biodiesel. Other studies also reported that there is a need to establish a valuable co-product stream so
as to boost progress toward economic parity of microalgal biofuels (Xin et al. 2016; Somers et al. 2021).
However, based on the results obtained from this economic assessment, it can be concluded that microalgal biofuels are
not competitive with traditional fossil fuels, even with the large-scale facility due to huge investment onset. For instance,
the cost of microalgal biomass based on this analysis is 1.253 $/kg. If we compare it with the average market price of

petroleum biodiesel, which is 0.8344 $/kg, it is clear that the value of producing biodiesel from petroleum is lower than that of
biofuels. This economic assessment agrees with other studies found in the literature (Wijffels et al. 2010; Jones et al. 2014;
Davis et al. 2016) indicating that microalgal heterotrophic and autotrophic biofuels could be successful with the development
of the current technologies and the exploration of other valuable products. Considering the huge cost of biomass production
that can be attributed to issues, such as harvesting cost and bioreactors set up, it is of great importance to comprehensively
develop new technologies that would reduce the cost of production and enhance the proﬁtability of large microalgae facility.
The effective design of photobioreactor and a comprehensive assessment of different mathematical models in microalgae
biomass production are important. This design and assessment should indicate the content of useful compounds of microalgal
biomass as they directly affect the cost of bioreactor. Enhancing the content of useful compounds is a vital strategy for redu-
cing the cost of biomass production and for successfully boosting the prospects of commercialization. For the effective design
of photobioreactor, irradiation conditions, mixing, photosynthetic efﬁciency of the system, cultural medium selection, and
carbon dioxide supplementation and cost are some of the critical issues regarding photo-autotrophic and mixotrophic bio-
fuels, as they signiﬁcantly affect the biomass production cost. Strain selection, subsequent development of an appropriate
cultivation and harvesting system, and proper downstream conversion processes are also important factors for a successful
biofuel system (Davis et al. 2016).
Based on the results obtained from the experiment and TEA, mixotrophic mode of cultivation showed a higher biomass
production, growth rate, and the highest net proﬁt compared with other modes of cultivation. Considering the simultaneous
assimilation of both organic and inorganic carbon as a source of carbon and light as an energy source, it is important to inves-
tigate closely the commercial success of mixotrophic biofuels in a large-scale bioreﬁnery. The successful implementation of
mixotrophic biofuels will ensure the full utilization of comparative advantage. This is even more attractive, particularly when
the microalgae production facility is integrated with waste biomasses (wastewater and waste CO2) that would contribute
toward saving the environment. The successful integration of microalgal biofuels production facility with waste biomasses
will play a big role in mitigating CO2 while boosting the prospect of attaining the sustainable development goals. This will
also reduce the cost of large industrial operations of bioreﬁneries as it can play an important role in transforming the econ-
omics of industrial production.
4. CONCLUSION
This study assessed the use of microalgae for bioremediating hydroponic wastewater and for producing biomass and lipid and
explored the economic implications of microalgal biofuels, focusing on the effect of different cultivation modes. The best
results in terms of total nitrogen (TN) and total phosphorous (TP) removals over the test periods were observed for mixo-
trophic growth supplied with 2.5 g/L glucose and atmospheric CO2 showing reasonable removals of TN (98.5%), TP
(99.99%). The reason for the stimulated increase in C. vulgaris and nutrients removals efﬁciencies in mixotrophic conditions
than in heterotrophic and autotrophic conditions is due to the simultaneous assimilation of both organic (glucose) and inor-
ganic (atmospheric CO2) carbon sources as well as light by C. vulgaris. This extra organic source of carbon enhances the
growth of microalgae strain and the removal capacity of total nitrogen and total phosphorous. The maximum biomass con-
centration and productivity were observed for mixotrophic conditions showing a reasonable amount of biomass
concentration (1.26 g/L) and biomass productivity (0.1108 g/L/day). Moreover, the highest lipid content values were
obtained under heterotrophic of that of 33 wt% AFDW. On the other hand, the highest lipid production was obtained
under mixotrophic (0.374 g/L) growth, followed by heterotrophic (0.341 g/L) growth. The reason for the highest lipid content
and lipid production is due to the addition of glucose as an organic source of carbon, which played an essential role in enhan-
cing both the biomass and lipid producing ability under both mixotrophic and heterotrophic growth.
The study also assessed the economic implications of microalgal biofuels, focusing on the effect of different cultivation
modes based on the results obtained from the experiment. Cultivation modes assessed included mixotroph, heterotroph,
and autotroph. Results from the economic assessment determined net proﬁt for each type of cultivation mode. The best
results regarding the net proﬁt were obtained for both mixotrophic and heterotrophic cultivations of 30.6 million $/year
and 30.12 million $/year, respectively, while the net proﬁt for autotrophic cultivation was 4.73 million $/year. Sensitivity
analysis shows that biodiesel and nutritious supplements from soluble protein have the greatest impact on the process econ-
omics with respect to mixotrophic cultivation, while biodiesel and feeds from insoluble protein have the largest effect on the
process economics in both heterotrophic and autotrophic cultivations. The higher biomass and lipid concentration observed

for mixotrophic and heterotrophic cultivation enhanced the economic feasibility of microalgal biofuels and subsequently
boosted the prospects of commercialization. TEA showed that additional beneﬁts for the environment can be attained if
wastewater is used as a cultivation medium to bioremediate the waste and produce biomass. TEA also showed that it is impor-
tant to reﬁne microalgal biomass into various products for reducing the process economics of the entire system and for
transforming the economics of industrial operations.
The results obtained from this study show that the use of wastewater as a cultivation medium for microalgae growth, con-
current removal of nutrients and production of biomass under mixotrophic and heterotrophic conditions may be a strategy for
biological wastewater treatment technologies and biofuels production.

Bioresource Technology 384 (2023) 129326

Environmental and economic evaluation of implementing membrane 
technologies and struvite crystallisation to recover nutrients from 
anaerobic digestion supernatant 
Sergi Vinardell a,b,*, Jose Luis Cortina a,b,c, C´esar Valderrama a,b 
a Chemical Engineering Department, Escola d’Enginyeria de Barcelona Est (EEBE), Universitat Polit`ecnica de Catalunya (UPC)-BarcelonaTECH, C/Eduard Maristany 
10-14, Campus Diagonal-Bes`os, 08930 Barcelona, Spain 
b Barcelona Research Center for Multiscale Science and Engineering, Campus Diagonal-Bes`os, 08930 Barcelona, Spain 
c CETaqua, Carretera d’Esplugues, 75, 08940 Cornell`a de Llobregat, Spain   
H I G H L I G H T S  
G R A P H I C A L  A B S T R A C T  
• The implementation of membrane tech­
nologies and struvite recovery was 
evaluated. 
• Implementing membrane technologies 
improved the sustainability of N and P 
recovery. 
• Struvite crystalliser and membrane 
contactor were the most impactful 
processes. 
• PN/anammox featured a high impact in 
global warning and ozone depletion 
categories. 
• The consumption of chemicals and the 
recovered ammonium sulphate played a 
key role.  
A R T I C L E  I N F O   
Keywords: 
Life cycle assessment (LCA) 
Techno-economic assessment (TEA) 
Biorefinery 
Circular economy 
Sustainability analysis 
A B S T R A C T   
The present study investigates the environmental and economic feasibility of implementing membrane tech­
nologies and struvite crystallisation (SC) for nutrient recovery from the anaerobic digestion supernatant. To this 
end, one scenario combining partial-nitritation/Anammox and SC was compared with three scenarios combining 
membrane technologies and SC. The combination of ultrafiltration, SC and liquid–liquid membrane contactor 
(LLMC) was the less environmentally impactful scenario. SC and LLMC were the most important environmental 
and economic contributors in those scenarios using membrane technologies. The economic evaluation illustrated 
that combining ultrafiltration, SC and LLMC (with or without reverse osmosis pre-concentration) featured the 
lowest net cost. The sensitivity analysis highlighted that the consumption of chemicals for nutrient recovery and 
the ammonium sulphate recovered had a large impact on environmental and economic balances. Overall, these 
results demonstrate that implementing membrane technologies and SC for nutrient recovery can improve the 
economic and environmental implications of future municipal wastewater treatment plants.  
* Corresponding author at: Chemical Engineering Department, Escola d’Enginyeria de Barcelona Est (EEBE), Universitat Polit`ecnica de Catalunya (UPC)-Barce­
lonaTECH, C/Eduard Maristany 10-14, Campus Diagonal-Bes`os, 08930 Barcelona, Spain. 
E-mail address: sergi.vinardell@upc.edu (S. Vinardell).  
Contents lists available at ScienceDirect

journal homepage: www.elsevier.com/locate/biortech 
https://doi.org/10.1016/j.biortech.2023.129326

Bioresource Technology 384 (2023) 129326
