1. Introduction 
Resource recovery from wastewater is pushing the transformation of 
urban wastewater treatment plants (WWTPs) towards biorefineries 
(Garrido-Baserba et al., 2020; Perez-Esteban et al., 2022). Nitrogen and 
phosphorus are two valuable components present in urban wastewater, 
whose recovery has emerged as one of the most relevant research topics 
in the wastewater field (Robles et al., 2020). The recovery of both ele­
ments is crucial (i) to reduce the dependency to energy-intensive Haber- 
Bosch process needed to produce nitrogen fertilizers (Spiller et al., 2022) 
and (ii) to reduce the extraction and consumption of non-renewable 
phosphate rock needed to produce phosphorus fertilizers (Bouzas 
et al., 2019). The increasing interest in this topic has promoted the 
development of different technologies to recover nutrients in WWTPs. 
The anaerobic digestion supernatant (liquid fraction from the dew­
atering stage) is the most suitable flux for nutrient recovery in WWTPs 
because it contains high concentrations of nitrogen and phosphorus. 
However, despite the potential to recover nutrients from this stream, 
limited full-scale technologies for nutrient recovery have been imple­
mented in WWTPs. The full-scale application of nutrient recovery 
technologies includes struvite crystallisation (SC) (Desmidt et al., 2015; 
Saerens et al., 2021) or ammonia stripping/absorption (De Vrieze et al., 
2016; Menkveld and Broeders, 2017). In this regard, membrane systems 
can be an interesting approach to facilitate the recovery of nutrients 
from the supernatant and promote the full-scale implementation of these 
technologies. Membrane systems allow (i) concentrating the nutrient- 
rich stream to enhance and reduce the costs of the subsequent recov­
ery stages and/or (ii) selectively separating the nutrients for valorisation 
as fertilizers (Kavitha et al., 2022; Vu et al., 2022; Yan et al., 2018). 
Furthermore, the application of dense membranes can also allow 
recovering fresh water suitable for potential reuse applications. How­
ever, the implementation of membrane technologies for nutrient re­
covery is scarce and further studies are important to evaluate the 
environmental and economic implications of their application at full- 
scale. 
Different membrane technologies have been tested at lab- or pilot- 
scale (i) to concentrate the supernatant before the nutrient recovery 
stage by using reverse osmosis (RO) or forward osmosis (FO) membranes 
(Holloway et al., 2007; Jafarinejad, 2021) and (ii) to selectively separate 
nitrogen from the supernatant for subsequent nutrient valorisation with 
liquid–liquid membrane contactor (LLMC) (Darestani et al., 2017; 
Noriega-Hevia et al., 2021). Vu et al. (2022, 2021) evaluated a FO sys­
tem to concentrate the supernatant with the objective to enhance 
nutrient recovery. Holloway et al. (2007) compared the performance of 
an FO, RO and FO-RO system for supernatant concentration with water 
production. Al-Juboori et al. (2022) and Noriega-Hevia et al. (2020) 
studied the impact of operating conditions on the recovery of nitrogen 
from the anaerobic digestion supernatant using LLMC. These studies 
show that membrane technologies can be an attractive alternative for 
nutrient recovery in WWTPs. 
Economic and environmental factors are crucial to understand the 
potential implications of implementing technologies to promote 
resource recovery in WWTPs (Farag`o et al., 2021). Techno-economic 
assessment (TEA) and life-cycle assessment (LCA) are widely used 
decision-making tools aimed at evaluating the economic and environ­
mental performance of a wastewater system during its life-cycle (Cortes- 
Pe˜na et al., 2020; Lam et al., 2020). In the wastewater field, TEA and 
LCA have been conducted to analyse the feasibility to transform WWTPs 
into water resource recovery factories (WRRFs) (Corominas et al., 2020; 
Li et al., 2023). Accordingly, these tools can be important to understand 
how the implementation of nutrient recovery technologies would affect 
the economic and environmental prospects of future WRRFs. 
Different studies have conducted TEA and/or LCA to evaluate the 
economic and environmental implications of recovering/removing nu­
trients from the anaerobic digestion supernatant. Rodriguez-Garcia et al. 
(2014) compared the environmental performance of three options (i.e. 
partial nitritation (PN)/Anammox, nitritation-denitritation and SC) to 
remove/recover nutrients from the supernatant. Ochs et al. (2023) 
conducted a TEA to evaluate the economic impacts of implementing 
nitrogen removal (i.e. PN/Anammox, nitrification–denitrification) or 
nitrogen recovery (thermal ammonia stripping with scrubbing) tech­
nologies in the sludge line of the WWTP. However, to the best of the 
authors’ knowledge, the implementation of membrane treatment trains 
for the recovery of nutrients from the anaerobic digestion supernatant 
has not yet been evaluated from environmental and economic points of 
view. 
This study aims to evaluate the environmental and economic im­
plications of recovering/removing nitrogen and phosphorus from the 
anaerobic digestion supernatant combining membrane technologies 
with SC. To this end, the LCA and TEA were conducted for three different 
scenarios integrating membrane technologies and SC, which were 
compared with a baseline scenario integrating PN/Anammox and SC. 
This is the first study in which the economic and environmental impli­
cations of implementing different membrane technologies for nutrient 
recovery to promote circular economy in WWTPs are evaluated. The 
final objective of this novel study is to unravel under which conditions 
the implementation of membrane technologies for nutrient recovery 
form the anaerobic digestion supernatant is more favourable consid­
ering both economic and environmental factors. 
2. Materials and methods 
2.1. Scenarios description and design criteria 
Fig. 1 shows the four scenarios included in the study. The different 
scenarios were selected based on available literature regarding mem­
brane technologies used for nutrient recovery from the anaerobic 
digestion supernatant. The mass and energy balances were conducted 
considering a high-sized WWTP treating 100,000 m3/d of wastewater 
(500,000 population equivalent) (Vinardell et al., 2021). The effluent 
flow rate from the sludge anaerobic digester was assumed to represent 
1% of the WWTP influent. Detailed information on the different sce­
narios is described as follows: 
Scenario 1: Scenario 1 represents the baseline case of the present 
study. In Scenario 1, PN/Anammox and SC technologies were imple­
mented to remove nitrogen and recover phosphorus in the form of 
struvite (NH4MgPO4⋅6H2O) from the supernatant of the WWTP, 
respectively (Fig. 1A). These technologies have been selected as baseline 
because they have already been successfully implemented in full-scale 
WWTPs (Lackner et al., 2014; Saerens et al., 2021). MgO (s) and 
NaOH were added to the crystalliser to provide Mg2+ for struvite for­
mation and to adjust the pH to 9, respectively (Peng et al., 2018; 
Yetilmezsoy et al., 2017). PN/Anammox was placed prior to the SC stage 
because PN/Anammox consumes alkalinity and allows reducing the 
requirements of external alkaline solution for pH adjustment in the 
crystalliser (Campos et al., 2019; Vinardell et al., 2021). 
Scenario 2: Scenario 2 was the first scenario implementing mem­
brane technologies for nutrient recovery. The supernatant was pre- 
treated in an ultrafiltration (UF) unit to remove suspended solids and 
reduce clogging in subsequent membrane units (Barampouti et al., 
2020). The effluent from the UF unit was fed to the SC unit for phos­
phorus recovery and the effluent from the crystalliser was fed to a LLMC 
unit for nitrogen recovery (Fig. 1B). LLMC was selected because this is 
one of the most promising technologies to recover nitrogen from the 
supernatant stream (Al-Juboori et al., 2022). In this technology, the 
ammonia contained in the supernatant diffuses as NH3 (g) through the 
hydrophobic membrane and it is absorbed by a H2SO4 solution to form 
(NH4)2SO4, which can be valorised as a fertiliser (Noriega-Hevia et al., 
2021; Serra-Toro et al., 2022). H2SO4 has been selected because H2SO4 
features a lower cost compared with other strong acids such as HNO3 or 
H3PO4. The pH of the supernatant was adjusted to 10 prior to the LLMC 
to ensure that the total inorganic nitrogen is present as NH3 (g). The SC

3
Fig. 1. Schematic representation of the four scenarios under study. PN/AMX: partial nitritation/Anammox; SC: struvite crystallisation; WWTP: wastewater treatment 
plant; UF: ultrafiltration; LLMC: liquid–liquid membrane contactor; FO: forward osmosis; RO: reverse osmosis. 
S. Vinardell et al.

Bioresource Technology 384 (2023) 129326
4
unit was placed before the LLMC to reduce the spontaneous precipita­
tion of phosphorus in the LLMC stage. It is worth mentioning that the 
high pH needed to displace the equilibrium can lead to spontaneous 
precipitation, which can favour membrane clogging and fouling in the 
LLMC unit (Noriega-Hevia et al., 2020). 
Scenario 3: Scenario 3 was an extension of Scenario 2 and included 
RO technology prior to the phosphorus and nitrogen recovery stages 
(Fig. 1C). RO was used to pre-concentrate the supernatant stream with a 
direct impact on the capital and operating costs of the subsequent 
nutrient recovery stages (Kavitha et al., 2022). RO technology was 
selected because this process has been considered a suitable technology 
to concentrate the supernatant and to obtain fresh water suitable for its 
reuse (Bolzonella et al., 2018; Holloway et al., 2007; Rizzioli et al., 
2023). The RO concentrate was fed to the SC unit and the effluent from 
the crystalliser was fed to the LLMC unit for phosphorus and nitrogen 
recovery, respectively. 
Scenario 4: Scenario 4 included FO technology (instead of RO) to 
pre-concentrate the supernatant before the phosphorus and nitrogen 
recovery stages (Fig. 1D). FO technology has gained attention to 
concentrate the supernatant since FO membranes feature low fouling 
propensity when compared with pressure-driven RO membranes (Sule­
man et al., 2022; Vu et al., 2022, 2021). The draw solution was a syn­
thetic NaCl solution with a concentration of 35 g/L (Vinardell et al., 
2022). RO was used to regenerate the diluted draw solution and produce 
fresh water (Holloway et al., 2007). NaCl was continuously added to the 
FO-RO system to keep the osmotic pressure constant in the loop despite 
losses of salt in both FO and RO membrane systems (Vinardell et al., 
2022). Finally, the FO concentrate was fed to the SC unit and the effluent 
from the crystalliser was fed to the LLMC unit for phosphorus and ni­
trogen recovery, respectively. 
The size of the different equipment and the consumption of energy, 
chemicals and water for the four scenarios were calculated considering 
average design and operating parameters reported in the literature. 
Chemical cleaning protocols were applied to the different membrane 
systems to control fouling based on available literature data. Detailed 
information on the operating and design parameters used to calculate 
the mass fluxes, energy fluxes and equipment dimensions for the 
different unit processes can be found in the online version (see supple­
mentary material). 
2.2. Anaerobic digestion supernatant composition 
The composition of the supernatant fed to the nutrient recovery 
process for the different scenarios can be found in the online version (see 
supplementary material). This composition was estimated using steady- 
state model equations for an anaerobic digester configured as a 
continuous stirred tank reactor. The calculations were performed 
considering an organic loading rate of 1 kg VS/m3/d for sewage sludge 
anaerobic digestion (Vinardell et al., 2021). The thickened sewage 
sludge was composed of a mixture of secondary sludge and primary 
sludge (50:50% on VS basis) and its composition was obtained from 
Astals et al. (2013) (see supplementary material). Detailed information 
about the equations and kinetic parameters used for the calculations can 
be found elsewhere (Vinardell et al., 2021). 
2.3. Life cycle assessment methodology 
LCA was conducted to evaluate the environmental impacts of 
implementing membrane technologies and SC to recover nutrients from 
the anaerobic digestion supernatant. The LCA followed the standard 
reported in the ISO 14040 and 14044 (ISO, 2006a, 2006b). In this sec­
tion, the different phases included in the LCA are described. 
2.3.1. Goal and scope definition 
The main goal of this LCA is to analyse the environmental perfor­
mance of implementing nutrient recovery schemes in the sludge line of 
the WWTP. To this end, one scenario using PN/Anammox and SC was 
compared with three scenarios combining different membrane tech­
nologies and SC. In this study, the functional unit was 1 m3 of the 
incoming anaerobic digestion supernatant. This functional unit was 
selected because (i) supernatant treatment represents a common service 
for the four scenarios and (ii) it is a typical functional unit for process- 
related studies in which different nutrient recovery technologies are 
compared to decide the best option for their full-scale implementation 
(H¨ogstrand et al., 2023; Lam et al., 2020). 
Fig. 2 shows the system boundaries and flows included in the 
different scenarios. The system boundaries of all the scenarios include 
the life cycle associated with the operation of the different nutrient re­
covery/removal technologies implemented to treat the anaerobic 
digestion supernatant. In this regard, all the background processes to 
produce the chemicals, energy and water needed for operating the 
different systems were included. The impacts associated with the con­
struction of the nutrient recovery/removal plant were not included in 
the evaluation because the construction phase is expected to have a 
relatively low impact on the total environmental impacts compared with 
the operation of the plant (Amann et al., 2018; Rodriguez-Garcia et al., 
2014). The disposal and management of the waste streams produced in 
the different scenarios were not included within the boundaries of the 
study since the selected alternative for sludge management is highly site- 
specific and their inclusion in the analysis could overshadow the real 
impacts of the different alternatives (Rufí-Salís et al., 2022). Similar 
approaches have been used in other studies evaluating the environ­
mental implications of nutrient recovery in a WWTP (H¨ogstrand et al., 
2023; Rufí-Salís et al., 2022). 
Besides struvite, ammonium sulphate is recovered in Scenario 2, 3 
and 4, while fresh water is recovered in Scenario 3 and 4. To account for 
the environmental benefits of recovering these three resources and to 
address the multifunctionality issue, a system expansion was conducted 
considering that these resources substituted their equivalent product 
from the market (Rufí-Salís et al., 2022). System expansion was selected 
to deal with multifunctionality because (i) it is the most used approach 
in studies evaluating nutrient recovery from wastewater (Lam et al., 
2020) and (ii) it has been widely used to compare conventional WWTP 
scenarios with alternative scenarios promoting resource recovery from 
wastewater (Farag`o et al., 2021; H¨ogstrand et al., 2023; Rodriguez- 
Garcia et al., 2014). Finally, it is worth mentioning that the imple­
mentation of these nutrient recovery technologies would reduce the load 
of nutrients in the water line of the WWTP. However, the nutrient 
backload to the water line would be similar for the four scenarios since 
all of them remove the nutrients contained in the anaerobic digestion 
supernatant. For this reason, the water line of the WWTP has not been 
included within the scope of this study because its environmental im­
pacts are expected to be similar for all the scenarios regardless of the 
nutrient treatment option considered. Similar approaches have been 
considered in other TEA and LCA articles evaluating the recovery/ 
removal of nutrients in the sludge line of the WWTP (Ochs et al. (2023); 
Rodriguez-Garcia et al., 2014). 
2.3.2. Life cycle inventory 
Table 1 shows the life cycle inventory regarding the inputs and 
outputs considered in the LCA for each scenario. The inventory data in 
Table 1 includes the different nutrient recovery/removal technologies 
under study (see Figs. 1 and 2). The inventory list was obtained from the 
mass and energy balances for each scenario. These mass and energy 
balances were conducted considering average design and operating 
parameters reported in the literature (see Section 2.1). The materials for 
construction and the waste streams have not been included as already 
mentioned in Section 2.3.1. The inventory data of the different back­
ground processes were obtained from Ecoinvent database (v3.7) (see 
supplementary material). 
S. Vinardell et al.

5
Fig. 2. System boundaries for the four scenarios under study. The solid box represents the system boundaries of each scenario.  
S. Vinardell et al.

Bioresource Technology 384 (2023) 129326
6
2.3.3. Life cycle impact assessment 
The ReCIPe 2016 Midpoint Hierarchist (H) method was used for the 
characterization and classification phases of the life cycle impact 
assessment (LCIA). The LCIA was conducted using the OpenLCA soft­
ware (v1.10.2). In this study, six representative impact categories for 
WWTP including nutrient recovery/removal technologies were included 
in the LCIA (Corominas et al., 2020; Lam et al., 2020). Specifically, the 
six mid-point categories included in the analysis were: global warming 
(kg CO2eq), freshwater eutrophication (kg Peq), stratospheric ozone 
depletion (kg CFC11eq), terrestrial acidification (kg SO2eq), freshwater 
eco-toxicity (kg 1,4-DCB) and human non-carcinogenic toxicity (kg 1,4- 
DCB). 
2.4. Techno-economic assessment 
The TEA was carried out to evaluate the economic implications of 
implementing membrane technologies and SC to recover nutrients from 
the supernatant. The TEA included the capital expenditures (CAPEX), 
operating expenditures (OPEX) and revenues obtained from the 
different scenarios under evaluation. Firstly, the CAPEX included the 
costs of purchasing (i) the different membrane systems (i.e. LLMC, UF, 
RO and FO), (ii) the struvite crystalliser and (iii) the tanks for chemical 
storage. Second, the OPEX included the costs of purchasing (i) the 
chemicals needed for struvite (i.e. MgO and NaOH) and ammonium 
sulphate (i.e. H2SO4 and NaOH) recovery, (ii) the chemicals needed for 
chemical membrane cleaning (i.e. citric acid, NaOH, HCl and NaOCl), 
(iii) the water needed for the preparation of the chemical solutions, (iv) 
the electricity consumption of the different stages, (v) the replacement 
of the membrane systems at the end of their lifetime and (vi) the NaCl 
needed in the FO-RO system to keep the osmotic pressure constant in the 
loop. Third, the revenues included the commercialisation of (i) the 
struvite and ammonium sulphate recovered and (ii) the fresh water 
produced in the RO stage. Including the revenues within the TEA scope 
allows accounting for the potential economic incomes of WWTPs aimed 
at promoting resource recovery and circular economy (Farag`o et al., 
2021). The unit costs obtained from the literature were updated to the 
present considering the Chemical Plant Cost Index and the year from the 
original source. The reference year for the TEA was 2023. Finally, the 
present gross cost, revenue and net cost were calculated according to the 
following equations:

Detailed information of the cost parameters and equations used for the 
TEA can be found in the online version (see supplementary material). 
2.5. Sensitivity analysis 
The sensitivity analysis was conducted to evaluate the variation of 
the economic and environmental results for a ± 30% variation of the 
most relevant input parameters (Vinardell et al., 2022). Regarding the 
sensitivity analysis of the TEA, it was evaluated how the variation in the 
cost of chemicals, electricity, water, equipment, revenues and influent 
nutrient concentration impacted the net cost of the different scenarios. 
Regarding the sensitivity analysis of the LCA, it was evaluated how the 
variation in the chemical consumption, electricity consumption, water 
consumption, ammonium sulphate production, struvite production, 
direct emissions and influent nutrient concentration impacted the net 
global warming impact category. 
3. Results and discussion 
3.1. Life cycle assessment 
The LCA of the four scenarios was conducted for six different impact 
categories. Fig. 3 shows the environmental results of recovering nutri­
ents from the anaerobic digestion supernatant using different membrane 
technologies. Scenario 2 featured the lowest net impact in three of the 
impact categories under evaluation (i.e. freshwater ecotoxicity, global 
warming and human non-carcinogenic toxicity). These results reveal 
that implementing a nutrient recovery train combining SC and LLMC 
was the most attractive scenario in most of the categories from an

net impact in terrestrial acidification and freshwater eutrophication 
categories. The higher net impact of Scenario 2, 3 and 4 when compared 
to Scenario 1 in these categories can be attributed to the higher con­
sumption of chemicals (i.e. NaOH, H2SO4) required (i) to adjust the pH 
in both SC and LLMC units and (ii) to produce ammonium sulphate in 
the LLMC. Importantly, the amount of NaOH needed in the crystalliser 
was substantially lower in Scenario 1 than the other scenarios because 
the PN/Anammox process reduces the alkalinity in the supernatant with 
a direct impact on the amount of alkaline solution needed for pH 
adjustment in this process (Campos et al., 2019; Vinardell et al., 2021). 
Finally, it is worth mentioning that the implementation of nutrient 
removal technologies in the sludge line would reduce the concentration 
of these compounds in the WWTP effluent, which could affect the out­
puts of these categories when including the whole WWTP in the analysis 
(Sena and Hicks, 2018). Further studies are important to determine how 
the implementation of the proposed nutrient recovery technologies 
could affect the effluent standards with a direct impact on the envi­
ronmental and economic prospects of the WWTP. 
Scenario 2, 3 and 4 featured the highest gross environmental impact 
(without subtracting the avoided impacts) in five of the impact cate­
gories evaluated (Fig. 3). Although the implementation of membrane 
technologies allows maximising resource recovery in WWTP, it also 
leads to higher consumption of energy and chemicals. Unlike the other 
scenarios, Scenario 1 does not implement membrane technologies and 
uses PN/Anammox for nitrogen removal. PN/Anammox is a biological 
process that consumes low amounts of energy and chemicals when 
compared with other physicochemical processes aimed at removing/ 
recovering nitrogen (Cruz et al., 2019; Wang et al., 2022). For this 
reason, Scenario 2, 3 and 4 featured a higher gross environmental 
impact than Scenario 1. However, these scenarios recover ammonium 
sulphate, which substantially reduces the net environmental impact of 
these alternatives (Fig. 3). The recovery of ammonium sulphate through 
LLMC is crucial to reduce the dependency of the Haber-Bosch process to 
produce the same nitrogen-based fertilizer with direct impact on the 
environmental performance of the technology (H¨ogstrand et al., 2023). 
Besides environmental implications, full-scale PN/Anammox systems 
need to deal with stability issues, such as nitrogen accumulation, solids 
retention, foaming or scaling, among others (Lackner et al., 2014). 
In Scenario 2, 3 and 4, SC followed by LLMC were the most relevant 
environmental contributors in freshwater ecotoxicity, freshwater 
eutrophication, global warming, stratospheric ozone depletion and 
human non-carcinogenic toxicity categories (Fig. 3A-E). These results 
further corroborate that the high consumption of chemicals in the SC (i. 
e. NaOH and MgO (s)) and LLMC (i.e. NaOH, H2SO4 and citric acid) units 
had a major impact on the environmental prospect of these scenarios. 
These results are in line with other studies, which also reported the key 
role of chemicals in the environmental impacts of nutrient recovery 
technologies (H¨ogstrand et al., 2023). Interestingly, the contribution of 
SC was lower for Scenario 1 than for the other scenarios implementing 
nutrient recovery technologies. As mentioned above, Scenario 1 reduced 
the consumption of NaOH for pH adjustment with a positive effect on the 
environmental impacts associated with the SC technology. This illus­
trates that NaOH consumption features a high impact on the different 
environmental categories under study due to the high energy con­
sumption (8.2 MJ/kg NaOH) needed for its production (H¨ogstrand et al., 
Fig. 3. Net environmental impact and contribution of the different processes in freshwater ecotoxicity, freshwater eutrophication, global warning, human non- 
carciogenic toxicity, stratospheric ozone depletion and terrestrial acidification categories of the LCA. The impact categories results are referred to the functional 
unit (1 m3 of treated supernatant). 
S. Vinardell et al.

Bioresource Technology 384 (2023) 129326
8
2023). LLMC featured a higher environmental contribution than SC in 
one of the categories evaluated (i.e. terrestrial acidification). This can be 
attributed to the high emissions of sulphur oxides during sulphuric acid 
production, which substantially increased the environmental impact of 
LLMC in the terrestrial acidification category. Sulphur oxide is an 
important promoter of acid rain after its oxidation to SO3 (g) and sub­
sequent dissolution in water to form H2SO4, which has a direct impact 
on the terrestrial acidification category (Corominas et al., 2020). Finally, 
it is worth highlighting that the UF, RO and FO-RO membrane systems 
only had a minor contribution to the different impact categories and 
their contribution was mainly caused by the energy required to operate 
these systems. 
In Scenario 1, SC was the most important environmental contributor 
in freshwater ecotoxicity, freshwater eutrophication, human non- 
carcinogenic toxicity and terrestrial acidification categories due to the 
consumption of chemicals needed for struvite production. However, PN/ 
Anammox featured a higher environmental impact in global warming 
and stratospheric ozone depletion categories, since this process leads to 
uncontrolled emissions of N2O (g) (Kampschreur et al., 2008). N2O (g) is 
a powerful ozone-depleting and greenhouse gas, whose emission has a 
large impact on the global warming and ozone depleting categories 
(Desloover et al., 2012; Ravishankara et al., 2009). Finally, it is worth 
mentioning that the emission of N2O (g) during PN/Anammox process is 
the main reason why Scenario 1 was the worst-case scenario for 
stratospheric ozone depletion and global warming categories. 
3.2. Techno-economic evaluation 
Fig. 4 illustrates the economic balance of the four scenarios under 
study. Scenario 3 and 2 were the most competitive scenarios because 
they featured a lower net cost than Scenario 1 and 4 (Fig. 4A). These 
results show that implementing membrane technologies and SC for 
Fig. 4. Economic results for the different scenarios: (A) Present net cost, gross cost and revenue, (B) gross cost contribution of the different processes and (C) revenue 
contribution of the final products. 
S. Vinardell et al.

Bioresource Technology 384 (2023) 129326
9
nutrient recovery can be a competitive option from both economic and 
environmental points of view when compared with using PN/Anammox 
and SC. The results also highlight that implementing RO pre- 
concentration can reduce the net cost of the nutrient recovery train 
since this process (i) reduces the capital and operating costs of the 
subsequent nutrient recovery stages and (ii) increases the revenues from 
the fresh water recovered. 
The economic competitiveness of Scenario 2 and 3 can be mainly 
attributed to the high revenues achieved from the commercialisation of 
the recovered ammonium sulphate (Fig. 4A). However, the revenues 
achieved from the commercialisation of struvite, ammonium sulphate 
and water did not offset the CAPEX and OPEX derived from the imple­
mentation of nutrient recovery/removal technologies. This is an 
important implication, which highlights that implementing nutrient 
recovery/removal technologies goes beyond the economic performance 
of the system. The removal of the nutrients from the supernatant has a 
direct impact on the technical performance of the WWTP since the nu­
trients removed in the sludge line do not need to be treated in the water 
line (out of the scope of the present study). Reducing the load of nutri­
ents in the water line is important (i) to reduce the use of chemicals and 
energy in this line with a direct impact on the economic and environ­
mental performance of the whole WWTP and (ii) to fulfil the discharge 
requirements especially in those WWTP without spare capacity to treat 
the excess of nutrients (Rodriguez-Garcia et al., 2014; Vinardell et al., 
2021). The results also illustrate that Scenario 4 was the worst-case 
scenario among the scenarios implementing membrane technologies 
(Fig. 4A). This output reflects that the costs related to FO-RO application 
did not compensate potential technical and economic incomes of this 
configuration 
(i.e. 
water 
production 
and 
supernatant 
pre- 
concentration). Additionally, FO membranes feature relatively low 
rejection of ammonia nitrogen (~70–85%) when compared with phos­
phate rejection (higher than 95%) (Suleman et al., 2022). The low ni­
trogen rejection in FO membranes reduces the ammonium sulphate 
revenue achieved in the subsequent LLMC process, thus affecting the 
economic balance of Scenario 4. However, it is worth mentioning that 
the implementation of a FO-RO pre-concentration system has the po­
tential (i) to reduce the membrane fouling when compared with stand- 
alone RO because FO is a non-pressure driven process and (ii) to in­
crease the quality of the recovered water because FO and RO membranes 
act as a dual barrier for pollutants (Blandin et al., 2015; Vu et al., 2022). 
The gross cost of Scenario 2, 3 and 4 was higher than Scenario 1 since 
the implementation of membrane technologies increases the CAPEX and 
OPEX of these alternatives (Fig. 4B). SC was the costliest process for all 
the scenarios (40–60%) because this system consumes high amounts of 
NaOH for pH adjustment and MgO to provide enough Mg2+ for struvite 
production. LLMC was the second most important gross cost contributor 
(30–40%), which can also be attributed to the high consumption of (i) 
NaOH to displace the equilibrium towards NH3 formation and (ii) H2SO4 
to chemically absorb the NH3 and produce (NH4)2SO4 fertilizer. These 
results further corroborate that the consumption of chemicals has an 
important impact on both environmental and economic prospects of 
nutrient recovery alternatives. 
Ammonium sulphate represented more than 50% of the economic 
revenues for all the scenarios in which this product is recovered 
(Fig. 4C). This highlights the key role of ammonium sulphate in the 
economic balance of Scenario 2, 3 and 4. Struvite revenue also repre­
sented an important fraction of the revenues (~30%) for these scenarios, 
although its contribution was always lower than ammonium sulphate. 
This is because the concentration of ammonium in the supernatant is 
substantially higher than phosphate, which leads to a higher revenue in 
the ammonium recovery stage (Fig. 4C). However, the recovery of 
phosphorus from wastewater is expected to be a pivotal process in future 
WRRFs since (i) phosphate rock is a non-renewable and valuable 
resource, (ii) the phosphorus demand for food production through 
agriculture is expected to increase in the following years and (iii) it 
improves the technical performance of the plant because it reduces the 
spontaneous phosphorus precipitation in the piping system (Bouzas 
et al., 2019; Peng et al., 2018). Finally, it is worth mentioning that the 
water produced through RO and FO-RO systems only had a minor 
impact on the economic balance since this revenue did not account for 
more than 10% of the total revenues achieved in Scenario 3 and 4. 
3.3. Sensitivity analysis 
Fig. 5 shows the sensitivity analysis for a ± 30% variation of the most 
relevant economic and environmental input parameters. The sensitivity 
analysis is a useful tool to evaluate the influence of the input parameters 
on the results as well as to identify the most impactful economic and 
operating factors. In Scenario 2, 3 and 4, the variation of the revenues 
had the most relevant impact on the net cost (Fig. 5A). These results 
corroborate that the recovery of valuable fertilizers is a key factor 
governing the economics of the scenarios combining membrane tech­
nologies and SC. The variation of the chemical cost featured the second 
most relevant effect on the net cost of Scenario 2, 3 and 4, which is 
attributed to the high consumption of chemicals needed to recover 
ammonium sulphate and struvite (see Section 3.2). Conversely, the net 
cost of these scenarios did not experience substantial variation when the 
equipment and electricity costs were changed. The relatively low impact 
of equipment and electricity costs on the net cost of Scenario 2, 3 and 4 
illustrated that (i) CAPEX has a lower impact than OPEX on the overall 
costs of the alternatives combining membrane technologies and SC and 
(ii) the impact of the electricity consumption is lower than the chemical 
consumption for the nutrient recovery technologies under study. 
Scenario 1 featured a different net cost variation trend than the other 
scenarios (Fig. 5A). In Scenario 1, the variation of the equipment cost 
had the highest impact on the net cost, which illustrates that CAPEX is a 
key economic factor for this scenario (Fig. 5A). This situation can be 
attributed to the substantially lower consumption of chemicals in Sce­
nario 1 since (i) PN/Anammox is a biological process that does not 
require chemicals for its operation and (ii) the amount of NaOH needed 
in the crystalliser for pH adjustment is substantially lower because of the 
consumption of alkalinity in the previous PN/Anammox process. 
Influent nutrient concentration featured an important impact on the net 
cost of Scenario 1 since the energy and chemical consumption is directly 
dependent on the supernatant nutrient concentration. The impact of 
nutrient concentration on the net cost was higher in Scenario 1 than in 
Scenario 2, 3 and 4. In these scenarios, ammonium sulphate is recovered 
and its production increases as the nutrient concentration increases. In 
this regard, the revenue achieved from ammonium sulphate allows 
reducing the impact of influent nutrient concentration on the net cost of 
Scenario 2, 3 and 4. Overall, these results highlight that further studies 
are necessary to evaluate the influence of the anaerobic digestion su­
pernatant characteristics on the technical performance of the nutrient 
recovery train with a direct impact on the effluent quality, net cost and 
environmental performance of the different alternatives (out of the 
scope of the present study). Finally, the water consumption needed for 
the preparation of the chemical solutions only led to a minor variation 
on the net cost (<5%) in all the scenarios under study. 
Chemical consumption was the most sensitive environmental factor 
on the global warming potential category of the LCA for Scenario 2, 3 
and 4 (Fig. 5B). These results further confirm that the consumption of 
chemicals needed for nutrient recovery has a large impact on the envi­
ronmental prospect of these scenarios. The recovered ammonium sul­
phate, which substituted their equivalent product from the market, 
featured the second most relevant impact on the global warming cate­
gory. The recovery of nitrogen as ammonium sulphate allows reducing 
the dependence of conventional Haber-Bosch process, which is highly 
dependent on fuel fossils (~2% of the global natural gas consumption) 
and leads to high greenhouse gas emissions (~1.2% of the global 
emissions) (Spiller et al., 2022). This is in accordance with the results 
obtained in Section 3.1, which highlighted the importance of recovering 
ammonium sulphate to reduce the net environmental impacts associated 
S. Vinardell et al.

Bioresource Technology 384 (2023) 129326
10
with LLMC technology. Finally, it is worth mentioning that direct 
emission was the most sensitive parameter in Scenario 1 (Fig. 5B). These 
results point out that N2O (g) emissions in the PN/Anammox process is a 
determinant factor for the global warning impact category of Scenario 1. 
4. Conclusions 
The results revealed that combining ultrafiltration, SC and LLMC 
featured the lowest net environmental impact. SC followed by LLMC 
were the most environmentally impactful processes in those scenarios 
implementing membrane systems. Conversely, the use of PN/Anammox 
for nitrogen removal increased the impacts in the global warning and 
ozone depletion categories due to the uncontrolled emission of N2O. The 
economic analysis highlighted that combining ultrafiltration, SC and 
LLMC (with or without RO pre-concentration) was the most economi­
cally attractive option. Finally, the sensitivity analysis illustrated that 
the chemical consumption and ammonium sulphate had a large impact 
on the sustainability of these alternatives.

Life cycle assessment of electrodialysis for sidestream nitrogen recovery in
municipal wastewater treatment
Donald Vineyard a, Andrea Hicks a, K.G. Karthikeyan b, Christy Davidson c, Phillip Barak c,*
a University of Wisconsin-Madison, Department of Civil and Environmental Engineering, 1415 Engineering Drive, Madison, WI, 53706, USA
b University of Wisconsin-Madison, Department of Biological Systems Engineering, 228 Agricultural Engineering Building, 460 Henry Mall, Madison, WI, 53706, USA
c University of Wisconsin-Madison, Department of Soil Science, 1525 Observatory Drive, Madison, WI, 53706, USA
A R T I C L E I N F O
Keywords:

A B S T R A C T
Nitrogen is removed during municipal wastewater treatment to reduce eutrophication of waterways and preserve
drinking water quality. Nitriﬁcation-denitriﬁcation and sidestream partial nitriﬁcation-anammox are state-of-the-
art municipal N removal technologies, but they require energy for aeration and can release nitrous oxide as a
fugitive greenhouse gas. An emerging sidestream electrodialysis technology is intended to both remove and
recover N as ammonium-based fertilizer. This midpoint life cycle assessment compiles literature values to
compare the state-of-the-art technologies of municipal wastewater nitrogen removal to the new electrodialysis
nitrogen removal and recovery technology, accounting for the offset of Haber-Bosch-derived ammonia. Electro-
dialysis is projected to be environmentally favorable compared to the state-of-the-art, predicting electricity sav-
ings similar to anammox and, with the offset of industrial ammonia manufacture, net negative emissions in ﬁve of
ten midpoint environmental impact categories, including global warming potential.
1. Introduction
The United States consumes approximately 12 million tons of nitro-
gen (N) fertilizer each year (USDA, 2018), which leads to a signiﬁcant
load of nutrient pollution to surface waters through surface runoff and
wastewater discharge. Environmental policy is shifting in order to
discourage nutrient pollution of waterways (USEPA, 2009) as the nation
becomes more conscious of the deleterious effects of eutrophication and
mitigating technologies mature. Point sources such as municipal waste-
water utilities are easy and effective targets for regulation and control.
Partial removal of N as part of municipal wastewater treatment is
currently a common practice to reduce nutrient load to surface water
(USEPA, 2007). Existing targeted N removal methods primarily facilitate
the decomposition by microbial consortia of complex nitrogenous com-
pounds into ammonium (NH4
þ) and then oxidation to atmospheric ni-
trogen gas (N2) (Clark et al., 2010); nitriﬁcation-denitriﬁcation is the
most common example while sidestream partial nitriﬁcation-anammox is
an emerging technology (van Dongen et al., 2001, Fux et al., 2002,
Kampschreur et al., 2009; Clark et al., 2010, Eini 2012). The
nitriﬁcation-denitriﬁcation and anammox processes require energy in
the form of electricity for aeration and secondary processes like mixing.
Nitriﬁcation-denitriﬁcation also requires a carbon (C) source for energy
and biomass; this C is typically derived from extant organic molecules
and thus serves to decrease biological oxygen demand (USEPA, 1973).
Some reactors add an external organic substrate, such as methanol, to
improve N removal (USEPA, 1973; Purtschert et al., 1996; Theis and
Hicks, 2012).
Stoichiometrically, anammox requires only 50% of NH4
þ to be
oxidized to nitrite (NO2
) but 57% is considered ideal (Magri et al., 2012).

requires
roughly
one-third
of
the
oxygen
as
nitriﬁcation-denitriﬁcation and no C amendment for the same NH4
þ
removal, but is incapable of processing nitrate (NO3
) (Eini, 2012).
Anammox reactors are well-suited for side stream processing (USEPA,
2007) of anaerobic digester efﬂuent liquids and can achieve high N
removal rates (Lu et al., 2011; Nifong et al., 2013). Both technologies run
the risk of releasing powerful greenhouse gases in the form of methane
(CH4) and nitrous oxide (N2O), depending on reactor conditions
(Kampschreur et al., 2009; Law et al., 2011).
Electrodialysis with monovalent-selective membranes is a newly
patented technology (Barak, 2018) to recover NH4
þ directly from waste-
water and concentrate it for reuse as fertilizer. Electrodialysis is a mature
desalination technology that uses an electric ﬁeld to transport ions
through ion-permeable membranes (Pruyn et al., 1969). The electrodi-
alysis stack typically consists of alternating cation- and anion-exchange

membranes (CEM, AEM) separated with spacers, with inert electrodes on
each end by which an electrical ﬁeld is applied to the stack. As aqueous
solution ﬂows between these specialized membranes, ions of the inﬂuent
stream are separated into an enriched concentrate stream and a partially
desalinated diluate stream. Electrodialysis can be an energy-efﬁcient
method of concentrating ions but solutions with high electrical resis-
tance or large desalting goals can affect stack performance.
Electrodialysis has been previously explored as a method of removing
NH4
þ ions from municipal wastewater (Eisenmann and Smith, 1967; Ali
et al., 2004; Ippersiel et al., 2012; Gurreri, 2020; Vineyard et al., 2020),
but the process was hampered by accumulation of calcium (Ca2þ) and
magnesium (Mg2þ) scale on membranes that blocked ion ﬂow. To
minimize energy costs, this sidestream electrodialysis technology is
intended to be applied to high-NH4
þ, low-C feeds such as ﬁltered liquids
from biosolids after anaerobic digestion. In contrast to other N removal
technologies, electrodialysis recovers an aqueous NH4
þ product that has
potential use as an N fertilizer.
Typical N fertilizers are derived from the Haber-Bosch process, a re-
action that converts atmospheric N2 gas and hydrogen gas into ammonia
(NH3) using a metal catalyst and high temperatures. This reaction re-
quires use of fossil fuel in the form of methane, a large industrial facility,
and a broad scale transportation network. By recapturing NH4
þ at treat-
ment plants for use in local agriculture, an electrodialysis stack can
theoretically offset the costs and environmental impacts of producing an
equivalent amount of industrial NH3. Electrodialysis will also recover
other monovalent ions such as potassium (Kþ), sodium (Naþ), and
chloride (Cl) in amounts that will vary depending on the composition of
the feed and selectivity of the ion exchange membranes. Of note, these
membranes will also exclude polyvalent heavy metal ions, previously
identiﬁed as a key risk in municipal nutrient recovery technologies (Fang
et al., 2016; Lam et al., 2019).
Life cycle assessment (LCA) has been used previously to evaluate the
environmental impacts associated with wastewater treatment (Guest
et al., 2009; Byrne et al., 2017). The wastewater treatment sector is
exploring the beneﬁts of resource recovery as part of the global shift
towards sustainability and circular economies (Guest et al., 2009). While
some studies focus on water (Fang et al., 2016) or energy recovery (Hao
et al., 2019), the myriad of emerging nutrient recovery technologies are
an ongoing area of research (Lebuf et al., 2012; de Faria et al., 2015;
Malila et al., 2019; Sena and Hicks, 2018; Lam et al., 2019).
Nitrogen-speciﬁc recovery technologies (Lin et al., 2016) receive less
focus in the literature as most technologies recover a combined N and P
product (Lam et al., 2019). While other N recovery technologies exist in
various stages of implementation, we are aware of none that are wide-
spread or industry standard. A literature review found no previous LCA
studies of electrodialysis for nitrogen recovery.
2. Methods
2.1. Goal and scope deﬁnition
The goal of this study was to evaluate an emerging wastewater N
recovery technology and compare the life cycle impacts to existing N
removal methods. We evaluate four different municipal N management
strategies: (1) Do nothing; (2) Nitriﬁcation-denitriﬁcation; (3) Anam-
mox; and (4) Electrodialysis for NH4
þ recovery. We built life cycle in-
ventories for the purpose of proﬁling their environmental impacts using
LCA.
This study is an expansion of Vineyard et al. (2020), which focused on
comparative economic and energy performance of electrodialysis for
these same technologies. Though cost is typically the major deciding
factor for municipalities mandated to implement nutrient removal, such
mandates are ultimately written with the goal of improving environ-
mental quality, which can be evaluated using LCA with standard prac-
tices outlined in ISO 14040 (ISO, 2006) and 14044 (ISO, 2006). This
study is intended for wastewater engineers and policymakers to consider
environmental impacts of new and existing technologies to achieve
increased N removal goals; while technologies have immediate practical
considerations on-site, their environmental beneﬁts ripple out.
We used the OpenLCA 1.8 software package (GreenDelta, Berlin,
Germany) and the ecoinvent 3.5 database (Wernet et al., 2016) to build
the life cycle inventory and apply impact assessment methods. OpenLCA
was chosen for ﬂexibility and ease of use. Where possible, U.S.-speciﬁc
processes were chosen. To interpret the inventory, we used the
midpoint categories of the USEPA's Tool for the Reduction and Assess-
ment of Chemicals and other environmental Impacts (TRACI) version 2.1
(Bare, 2011) packaged with OpenLCA. TRACI was chosen for its high
regard and ubiquity in LCA literature and for its comprehensive range of
midpoint environmental impacts for comparison, but it should be noted
that the characterizations were also U.S.-speciﬁc.
When wastewater speciﬁcs were necessary, data were taken from the
Nine Springs Wastewater Treatment Plant in Madison, Wisconsin, a mid-
size treatment plant that serves a population of 360,000 over a 180 sq.
mile (466 km2) service area, with a design capacity of 50 million gallons
per day (50 MGD; 189,000 m3/day) (AECOM, 2015). This plant contains
a mesophilic anaerobic digester and dewaters its anaerobic digestor
sludge to produce biosolids using a gravity belt thickener (GBT). The
liquid stream separated out by GBT for recycling to the headworks is high
in nutrients and well-suited for side-stream treatment processes such as
anammox or electrodialysis, but can also be returned to headwaters or
fed directly to nutrient removal reactors. Our technologies are assumed
to be fed the high nutrient liquid stream from this stream. The total GBT
ﬁltrate ﬂow is 0.25 MGD, or 946 m3/day, and averages 1437 mg/L
NH4
þ-N, 214 mg/L Kþ, and 256 mg/L Naþ for 103 meq/L, 5.47 meq/L,
and 11.1 meq/L, respectively. Electricity is assumed to be of low voltage
from the U.S. characterized by data from the Midwest Reliability Orga-
nization (MRO), one of the eight regional electric reliability councils
under the North American Electric Reliability Corporation (NERC).
In the literature search, publications were mined for reactor perfor-
mance data to be incorporated into a master list. If a single publication
reported data from multiple reactors, those reactors were given equal
weight in the list. When publications did not directly report the values
needed, values were instead calculated from available data within the
same publication; missing data were ﬁlled with the aggregate average of
publications that reported the needed upstream data. In condensing this
distribution to singular unit processes for an OpenLCA input, the geo-
metric mean of values was used for the process inventory. Full values are
contained within the SI for further exploration. The intention of this
method was to create a process representative of the real world by taking
surveyed data from multiple publications. This assumes that studied and
surveyed plants are representative of plants as a whole and that the data
collection methods have comparable results.
We performed an uncertainty analysis for discussion characterizing
the potential impacts of the variability in literature values and addressing
possible alternative assumptions. High and low values from literature are
used to put boundaries on emissions and energy and material con-
sumptions for
traditional technologies. Electrodialysis
alternatives
address varied operating conditions and necessary material inputs as
suggested by Vineyard et al. (2020) from electrochemical modeling and
literature review. We use these values to create new inventories and
reassess them for the same environmental impacts. Differences between
scenarios are highlighted with comment to potential causes, effects, and
probabilities.
2.2. Functional unit
Our work assumed that new technology would be implemented with
the intention of meeting a nutrient removal goal and as such uses a mass
basis for N removed; this allows decision-makers to directly compare the
estimated impacts incurred by meeting their goals and then size their
reactors appropriately. The functional unit for comparison in this study
was a single kilogram (kg) of NH4
þ-N removed, a metric with precedent in

nutrient recovery LCA (Byrne et al., 2017; Lin et al., 2016). Comparisons
were performed based on total supply chain environmental impacts, with
highlights on the local energy consumption and fugitive emissions due to
their high impacts.
2.3. System Boundaries
All three technologies embody high energy consumption, material
consumption, and/or fugitive emissions, have relatively simple con-
struction and disposal, and are expected to have useful lifetimes in excess
of 20 years. Construction and disposal impacts are expected to be
negligible compared to 20 years of operation impacts (see Supplemental
Information). As such, this was a gate-to-gate study beginning with the
entry of wastewater N into the reactor and ending with the N leaving that
reactor to the environment (Fig. 1). The intervention point for the side
stream technologies is the high-nutrient liquid efﬂuent from an anaerobic
digester (Fig. 2). We assumed that removal of N from a small side stream
is equivalent to removal from a large stream, effectively disregarding
scalability and total nutrient removal potential in favor of marginal
impact of nutrient removal. This assessment allocated all impacts of the
process to nutrient removal, disregarding the effects of any secondary
beneﬁts
and
externalities within other
plant processes,
such
as
decreasing biochemical oxygen demand (USEPA, 1973) or reduced
digester inhibition by ammonium (Hansen et al., 2004; Calli et al., 2005).
While there are no existing nitrogen recovery technologies in place to
compare, comparing removal to recovery processes is made possible by
the inclusion of the fertilizer offset (ISO, 2006). The production of
fertilizer-quality N is accounted as an offset of its nearest industrial
analog, ammonia.
2.4. Scenario description
2.4.1. Do nothing
The default scenario is a simple release of treated wastewater N to
surface water through efﬂuent discharge, the bar against which other
scenarios are compared. In this scenario, the N leaves the sidestream
untreated and re-enters the main treatment train where it will eventually
be discharged from the plant. This N will then be utilized by plants and
microbiota in natural waters. No energy consumption is required in this
scenario. In addition to eutrophic effects of excess nutrients on drinking
water supplies, ﬁsheries, and recreation usability, a targeted IPCC report
estimated that 0.01 kg N2O-N is emitted to the atmosphere for every kg of
N released to rivers (IPCC, 2000); as TRACI does not include a global
warming characterization factor for NH4
þ to surface water, we supple-
mented the IPCC value. One kg of NH4
þ-N, for our purposes equated to
1.29 kg of NH4
þ ions and 0.0157 kg of N2O gas.
2.4.2. Nitriﬁcation-denitriﬁcation
The second scenario, nitriﬁcation-denitriﬁcation, is a traditional N
removal technology that uses large cisterns and pumps to move and
aerate process water through a series of aerated (oxic) and anoxic zones.
We used a value of 3.20 kWh per kg of N removed for electricity to run
pumps and aerators (SI, Table 1A). For methanol, we used the stoichio-
metric ratio for heterotrophic digestion of 2.44 kg methanol per 1 kg of
NO3
-N, which is metabolized to 2.40 kg of CO2 (Rittmann and McCarty,
2001; Theis and Hicks, 2012). These values are similar to the 3.5 kWh
and 3.1 kg of methanol per kg N calculated by the Chesapeake Bay Pro-
gram for marginal reduction of the efﬂuent from 8 to 5 ppm N (2002).
In

nitriﬁcation-
denitriﬁcation model considered values from a number of studies and
metastudies (SI, Table 2A). Many publications reported fugitive emis-
sions per N inﬂuent in various units, requiring conversion. Two publi-
cations (Wicht and Beier, 1995; Foley et al., 2010) contained a combined
70 data points, but were treated as a single data point due to inaccessi-
bility of associated data. The uncertainty distribution removed two out-
liers from both the top and bottom of the emissions proﬁle. Despite being
an anoxic, metabolically active, and C-containing environment, we did
not expect methane (CH4) emissions from the denitriﬁcation process

2.4.3. Anammox
Energy consumption data were obtained from Maurer et al. (2003),
WSHD (2012), and Lackner et al. (2014), through Hauck et al. (2016),
where the geometric mean was 1.44 kWh/kg N (SI, Table 3A). The N2O
emission data for anammox reactors were taken from literature values
(SI, Table 4A) and averaged 0.0209 kg N2O/kg N; anammox was not
expected to yield fugitive CH4 emissions. It should be noted that the
relationship between reactor conditions and fugitive emissions is not yet
well understood and might be improved by alternative reactor schemas
and aeration regimes (Domingo-Felez et al., 2014).
2.4.4. Electrodialysis
An electrodialysis stack is a relatively simple machine composed of a
pump, electrodes, and hydrocarbon-based membranes. The electrodial-
ysis membranes are predicted to have a lifetime of 10 years (Vineyard
et al., 2020; AECOM, 2015). Other components are not expected to
require unusual maintenance or replacement. The impacts of this stan-
dard upkeep are expected to be negligible compared to the high elec-
tricity consumption and fertilizer offset. The manufacture and disposal of
the unit is expected to be similarly negligible. In an electrodialysis stack,
the total energy consumption is the product of the voltage and the
electric current. The relationship between these stack conditions and
resultant voltage is a complicated one. Vineyard et al. (2020), a com-
panion article to this study, predicted that an economically optimal stack
of in our assumed conditions would consume 2.36 kWh/kg N. This value
represented an optimal proﬁt method of current selection and was pre-
dicted to remove roughly 41.6% of NH4
þ from the stream in a single stage
of treatment.
As electrodialysis is also a N recovery technology that produces
fertilizer-quality NH4
þ, this process contained an offset of 1 kg of N in
NH4
þ for each kg of N removed, manifested here as an input of negative
production. Ammonia was chosen as the offset fertilizer because it is the
direct chemical analogue to the NH4
þ salts produced. No weight, positive
or negative, was given to K, Na, bicarbonate or chloride byproducts. The
ecoinvent library's market option for NH3 was chosen for the inventory
ﬂow. No N2O or CH4 emissions from the electrodialysis process were
expected.
3. Inventories and impacts
The life cycle inventories of the four scenarios (1) Do nothing; (2)
Nitriﬁcation-denitriﬁcation; (3) Anammox; and (4) Electrodialysis for
NH4
þ recovery are shown in Table 1. Looking at the inputs, electrodialysis
used 74% of the electricity of an equivalent capacity nitriﬁcation-
denitriﬁcation
reactor;
an
anammox
reactor
used
40%.
Only
nitriﬁcation-denitriﬁcation required a chemical methanol input and its
conjugate respiration to CO2 as an output. Only electrodialysis generated
a fertilizer offset, shown here as a negative ammonia liquid input.
Considering the outputs, electrodialysis generated no fugitive N2O
emissions; emissions were three times higher for an anammox reactor
than a denitriﬁcation reactor and twice as high for the do nothing sce-
nario, which also was the only scenario with an ammonium ion output.
Electrodialysis is the most environmentally favorable technology in
six of ten environmental impact categories considered (Table 2), with
anammox having a narrow advantage in the other four. The offset of
industrial NH4
þ production in the electrodialysis model led to a net
negative impact, i.e., an environmental beneﬁt, in ﬁve of the impact
categories, while the combination of high electricity usage with meth-
anol consumption in the nitriﬁcation-denitriﬁcation model led to the
largest impact in every TRACI category tested except for eutrophication.
All N removal strategies generated lower eutrophication than the Do-
Nothing scenario by two orders of magnitude. While allowing the N to
discharge freely into the environment did generate global warming po-
tential through the generation of N2O, both nitriﬁcation-denitriﬁcation
and anammox generated higher total global warming impacts from the
combination of fugitive emissions and energy consumption due to aera-
tion. Fossil fuel depletion, acidiﬁcation, and smog production were
strongly impacted by methanol production and the fertilizer offset. The
toxicity emission categories of carcinogens, non-carcinogens, ecotoxicity,
and respiratory effects were electricity-dominated effects.
The four scenarios had signiﬁcant differences in their greenhouse gas
(GHG) proﬁles (Fig. 3). NH4
þ discharge to surface water bodies in the do-
nothing scenario was predicted to result in signiﬁcant N2O generation.
The nitriﬁcation-denitriﬁcation process had double the electricity-
related emissions of anammox, as well as additional methanol produc-
tion and respiration emissions, but it emitted a third of the N2O of
anammox and less than half the N2O of a surface water discharge.
Fugitive N2O generation dominated the global warming proﬁle of the
anammox process. Despite requiring no C substrate and less oxygenation
than nitriﬁcation-denitriﬁcation, anammox showed only an 11% savings
against nitriﬁcation-denitriﬁcation. By contrast, electrodialysis was not
projected to generate N2O and the offset of NH4
þ production eliminated
methane-related emissions from the supply chain. Greenhouse gas
emissions from its moderate energy consumption were outweighed by
offsets in NH4
þ production.
4. Discussion
4.1. Uncertainty analysis
Each of the four scenarios had uncertainty in at least one input or
output due to the range of literature reported values. In order to address
this uncertainty, we selected literature values as alternative LCA inputs
and outputs (Table 3) to assess the effects on predicted environmental
impacts (Tale 4). These alternative models were intended to help
conceptualize the ranges of possible emissions proﬁles from real world
implementation. The SI contains tables with a more detailed accounting
of the data sources and uncertainty (Tables 1A–4A) and modeling of
additional scenarios (Tables 5A–12A).

A recent metastudy (Hu et al., 2013) strongly suggested that the IPCC
(2000) estimate of fugitive N2O from surface water discharged NH4
þ is an
overestimate and that a more representative emission factor might be as
low as 0.0016 kg. If the Hu et al., 2013 data were correct, then the
Do-Nothing scenario might have a much smaller global warming impact
of 0.75 kg CO2-Eq/kg N, an 84% reduction. This would reduce the
Do-Nothing scenario to the lowest global warming impact among the
options, with the exception of the electrodialysis stack that recycles the N
to fertilizer to offset NH4
þ production. The oxidation reaction of NH4
þ to
NO3
 in a natural waterway stoichiometrically results in a net two
hydrogen ions produced per NH4
þ ion oxidized. Literature estimated that
half of this NO3
 will undergo plant uptake with an equivalent amount of
alkalinity production upon assimilation (Havlin et al., 2014), leading to a
potential net of 1 mol of Hþ per mole of NH4
þ; as the acidiﬁcation impact
category includes effects on natural waterways, this pH change might be
appropriately included in the impacts. This alternative accounting would
make doing nothing a greater acidiﬁer than any other option by multiple
orders of magnitude.
Nitriﬁcation-denitriﬁcation had a range of alternative energy con-
sumptions and fugitive emissions reported in literature. Both high and
low reported values were tested alongside a nitriﬁcation-denitriﬁcation
basin that did not require methanol as a C substrate. In a standard
nitriﬁcation-denitriﬁcation reactor, methanol was found to account for
over half of the global warming impacts and two-thirds of acidiﬁcation,
fossil depletion, and non-carcinogenic human health impacts due to both
the industrial production itself and the eventual oxidation to CO2;
reducing the usage of methanol equivalently reduced these emissions.
Methanol sourced from a non-fossil C substrate such as food waste or
sludge cycled from another plant stream might also lower the global
warming impacts as the eventual decomposition would not result in a net
GHG increase; an alternative C source might even be allocated an offset of
the disposal costs for such a substrate. Fugitive N2O emissions ranged
from insigniﬁcant (<0.5 kg CO2-Eq/kg N) to completely dominant over
the GHG proﬁle (>28 kg CO2-Eq/kg N). Variability in reported electricity
usage did not dramatically affect results; the difference between the
lowest (2.5 kWh/kg N) and highest (4.17 kWh/kg N) reported energy
consumptions was about 1 kg CO2-Eq/kg N.

denitriﬁcation (34.6 kg CO2-Eq/kg N). The highest electricity consump-
tion in an anammox reactor (4.17 kWh/kg N) was equivalent to the
highest electricity consumption in a nitriﬁcation-denitriﬁcation reactor.
This suggested that reactor performance was of particular importance to
anammox reactors as poor function leading to fugitive emissions can
eliminate expected technological gains from energy savings. It was also
possible that the mesophilic bacteria require additional energy for heat
pumping in some reactors where prior digestion does not create warm
enough conditions, though data of this level of detail were not provided
in-source.
Electrodialysis with the economically optimal current as predicted by
Tanaka (2015) (2.36 kWh/kg N) (Vineyard et al., 2020) was the scenario
considered, but four alternative energy consumption values were tested:
energy consumption at the limiting current density model (3.67 kWh/kg
N) (Vineyard et al., 2020); energy consumption at maximum hourly
proﬁtability (3.09 kWh/kg N); energy consumption at optimal cost as
predicted by an alternative model (2.04 kWh/kg N) (McGovern et al.,
2014); and energy consumption for a chloride targeting analogue
considered in a vendor proposal (2.42 kWh/kg N) (AECOM, 2015),
covered in the SI. The vendor proposal included acid usage to remove
mineral scale.
Each of these scenarios showed changes in impacts proportionate to
the change in electricity usage; acid addition played a qualitatively
insigniﬁcant role in the vendor scenario. Three alternative energy mixes
were also tested in the SI: the Midwest Reliability Organization, the
Western Electric Coordinating Council, and the Northeast Power Coor-
dinating Council. The Midwestern mix was found to be the greatest GHG
emitter (0.577 kg CO2-Eq/kg N after methanol offset), likely owing to
the disproportionate use of coal in the Midwest region.
The offset of industrial ammonia production is a large contributor to
the emissions proﬁle of electrodialysis. Photochemical oxidation, acidi-
ﬁcation, and global warming impacts are the most sensitive to the offset.
If captured ammonia is not used to offset industrial fertilizer, modeled in
the No-Recovery scenario, global warming impacts would be positive and
more than double the reported magnitude; acidiﬁcation and oxidation
would be four and six times their reported magnitudes, respectively. In
the ozone depletion, eutrophication, and non-carcinogenic human health
categories, the impacts of energy consumption are negligible compared
to the offset, while in the respiratory effects and ecotoxicity categories
the offsets are of a lower magnitude compared to the impacts of energy
consumption. Without the ammonia recovery offset, N removal by elec-
trodialysis would have a larger impact than anammox in every category
except global warming. As a ﬁnal test, ammonia fertilizer was replaced
with the common fertilizer urea (SI), which resulted in higher offsets in
each category and net negative emissions of greater magnitude in the
cumulative results.
4.2. Additional considerations
Electrodialysis run at optimal cost efﬁciency was projected to
consume more electricity per kg of N than an anammox reactor, but the
increased energy consumption was offset in the supply chain by the
avoided industrial ammonia production; though a utility will not expe-
rience these energy savings itself, it may be able to pursue a grant or
subsidy for beneﬁts to the local market.
An electrodialysis stack can theoretically concentrate NH4
þ with
equimolar chloride and bicarbonate up to the saturation point of about
4 M, 6% N by mass, before precipitation and scaling begin at 20 C
(Barak, 2018; unpublished data). The product would be a dilute liquid
fertilizer composed of NH4
þ with chlorides and bicarbonates. Though this
product would avoid typical waste-derived fertilizer concerns about the
over application of phosphorus, there will be concerns regarding excess
chloride application. Compared to anhydrous ammonia (82%), urea
(46%), or even aqua ammonia (20%), a 6% solution would incur greater

transportation, storage, and application costs as a fertilizer. These costs
might be offset by a much shorter transportation distance between pro-
duction and application. Comparing a 6% solution to a 46% urea load
suggests 7.8 times the transportation costs both environmentally and
economically. With a rough, ecoinvent-based estimate of 0.22 kg CO2-Eq
per 1000 kg-kilometers transported, the kg of electrodialyzed N incurs
3.19 extra kg of CO2-Eq per 1000 km compared to urea. It would take an
average facility-to-farm transport distance of over 750 km by truck to
invalidate the industrial ammonia offset in the comparison. Volatilizing
and condensing recovered N as ammonia or ammonium bicarbonate
would increase electricity costs but reduce transportation costs. Though
construction-phase impacts were expected to be negligible, other studies
of different scopes and methods (Lin et al., 2016; Hao et al., 2019) have
suggested the high concrete, steel, and fuel consumption in construction
may be signiﬁcant. Accounting for these impacts would likely widen the
gap between electrodialysis and traditional technologies.
Short and long term effects of membrane scale and fouling remain an
unresolved issue here. Eisenmann and Smith (1967) also found that
membranes rapidly scaled with Ca and Mg under operating conditions.
Electrodialysis also faces potential membrane fouling from trace organic
material. Wastewater, even post-treatment, contains high levels of
complex biologically-derived C molecules. These molecules tend to
behave as if they were strongly negatively charged and so interact with
an electrodialysis current, forming a layer of sorbed organic molecules on
the membranes that will impede ion ﬂow (Oztekin and Altin, 2016).
Scale, whether organic or inorganic, may dramatically increase energy
costs by increasing electrical resistance. Prevention and remediation of
scale within the electrodialysis chamber might require pretreatment of
the solution either to remove solutes before precipitation or prevent their
detrimental effects on the electrodialysis process. Pretreatment could
have impacts to both the reactor's environmental life cycle inventory and
to the function of the whole treatment plant. Monovalent-selective
membranes are expected to vigorously exclude the large, highly
charged organic molecules in a fashion that should prevent fouling and
reject the Ca and Mg ions that cause mineral scaling. Future work should
investigate the performance of reactors in broader operating conditions,
particularly with respect to scale formation and membrane fouling.
Given the complex interrelations of wastewater treatment processes,
there may be unforeseen externalities to implementation of these tech-
nologies not captured in this study. Some wastewater treatment systems
may not have suitable inﬂuent for processing, may have side stream
processes incompatible with new nutrient removal, or may have existing
products degraded or reactors impeded by nutrient removal. It is also
possible that N removal in-plant can reduce the occurrence of ammonia-
toxicity in municipal sludge digesters (Desloover et al., 2012; Desloover
et al., 2015); improved digester performance can decrease end waste
mass and cost, while yielding higher biogas recovery for on-site energy
generation (Karim et al., 2005; Gungor and Karthikeyan, 2008; Holly
et al., 2017). Current allocation methods disregard any costs or beneﬁts
of pathogen reduction or C removal in the reaction. Future analysis
would beneﬁt greatly from a characterization of the interaction between
N removal processes and overall treatment plant performance, particu-
larly in the emission of fugitive GHGs and aeration energy requirements
of main stages. An expanded scope would be within the recommenda-
tions of Corominas et al. (2013).
5. Conclusions
Based on these theoretical LCA results, N removal and recovery by
unimpeded electrodialysis appear to be far and away the preferable
environmental choice of N removal technologies, projecting net negative
impacts in key categories including GHG emissions and fossil fuel
depletion. Electrodialysis is predicted to consume 64% more electricity
than an equivalent capacity anammox reactor but 26% less than a
nitriﬁcation-denitriﬁcation reactor. Models of aeration-based N removal
technologies, particularly anammox, were found to be extremely
sensitive to fugitive N2O emissions, suggesting that proper reactor
function should be a top priority for operators seeking to reduce global
warming impacts.

Abstract: A “cradle-to-grave” life cycle assessment is performed to identify the environmental issues
of polyhydroxyalkanoates (PHAs) produced through a hybrid thermochemical-biological process
using anaerobically digested sewage sludge (ADSS) as feedstock. The assessment includes a measure
of the energy performance of the process. The system boundary includes: (i) Sludge pyrolysis
followed by volatile fatty acids (VFAs) production; (ii) PHAs-enriched biomass production using
a mixed microbial culture (MMC); (iii) PHAs extraction with dimethyl carbonate; and iv) PHAs
end-of-life. Three scenarios diﬀering in the use of the syngas produced by both pyrolysis and biochar
gasiﬁcation, and two more scenarios diﬀering only in the external energy sources were evaluated.
Results show a trade-oﬀbetween environmental impacts at global scale, such as climate change
and resources depletion, and those having an eﬀect at the local/regional scale, such as acidiﬁcation,
eutrophication, and toxicity. Process conﬁgurations based only on the sludge-to-PHAs route require
an external energy supply, which determines the highest impacts with respect to climate change,
resources depletion, and water depletion. On the contrary, process conﬁgurations also integrating the
sludge-to-energy route for self-sustainment imply more onsite sludge processing and combustion;
this results in the highest values of eutrophication, ecotoxicity, and human toxicity. There is not a
categorical winner among the investigated conﬁgurations; however, the use of a selected mix of
external renewable sources while using sludge to produce PHAs only seems the best compromise.
The results are comparable to those of both other PHAs production processes found in the literature
and various fossil-based and bio-based polymers, in terms of both non-biogenic GHG emissions and
energy demand. Further process advancements and technology improvement in high impact stages
are required to make this PHAs production process a competitive candidate for the production of
biopolymers on a wide scale.
Keywords: LCA; energy metrics; PHAs; bio-based polymers; biodegradable plastics; pyrolysis;
volatile fatty acids

1. Introduction
According to the European Commission [1], the transition to a more circular economy is
an essential contribution to the eﬀorts to develop a sustainable, low-carbon, resource-eﬃcient,
and competitive economy. In a circular economy, closed material cycles should be encouraged
where possible [1].
Bio-based materials, i.e., those derived from renewable resources, such as
wood, crops, or ﬁbers, have various applications in a large variety of industries (e.g., construction,
furniture, packaging, coatings, textiles, cardboard, chemicals, etc.) and energy uses (e.g., biofuels).
Their characteristic of being made of organic carbon, which can be recycled and reused many times,
in many ways, goes towards the principles of the waste hierarchy and, more generally, results in
better overall environmental performances [1]. The bioeconomy, consequently, oﬀers alternatives to
fossil-based energy and products, and can make an important contribution to the circular economy.
Moreover, bio-based materials can provide beneﬁts connected to their renewability, biodegradability,
or compostability. Nevertheless, the use of biological resources requires attention and a careful
assessment through their life-cycle environmental impacts. Indeed, their use can also create competition
for them and generate pressure on land use [2]. In this context, bioplastics are a very promising research
area, usually indicated as a sustainable alternative to conventional fossil-based products. They have
the advantage, over traditional plastics, of diminishing the use of non-renewable resources and also
decreasing the environmental impact related to fossil resources’ consumption [3,4].
Polyhydroxyalkanoates (PHAs) are polymers belonging to a group of polyesters that are generated
by some bacteria as carbon and energy intracellular reserve granules. Currently, more than 90 bacterial
species that produce PHAs and about 150 diverse monomers of PHAs have been recognized [5].
Their accumulation is usually observed when one nutrient (e.g., nitrogen, phosphorus, and oxygen)
is present in the fermentation broth in a limiting concentration, while, at the same time, there is an
available excess source of carbon. Most bacterial strains, such as Cupriavidus necator, accumulate
PHAs as secondary products under nutrient-limiting conditions. However, some bacterial strains,
such as recombinant Escherichia coli and Alcaligenes latus, synthesize PHAs as a primary metabolite
during microbial growth [6]. Generally, PHAs are produced by pure microbial cultures grown on
renewable feedstocks (i.e., sugar or oils) under sterile conditions, but recently, several authors have
investigated the exploitation of residues and waste as growing substrates [7–9]. Commonly, PHAs are
considered eco-friendly because they are produced from renewable natural resources instead of
petrochemicals, and because they biodegrade without producing harmful or toxic by-products and
leaving no worrisome waste [10]. Anyway, in order to conclude if biopolymers are environmentally
advantageous over petrol-based plastics, it is necessary to analyze their entire life cycle. Moreover, to
make PHAs really competitive, ﬁrst of all they need to equal their petrochemical counterparts both
in terms of quality and economic performances [11]. Sustainable PHAs production is multifaceted
and several criticisms need to be tackled in the process in order to make the manufacture of PHAs
on an industrial scale convenient both environmentally and economically. For this purpose, cheaper
microbial cultures and reutilization of waste streams as growing substrates appear among the main
points for large-scale industrial production [12–14].
Among the many residues and wastes tested as growth substrates [9], agro-industrial and municipal
eﬄuents and sewage sludge have received considerable attention in recent years. Combinations
of food waste and sewage sludge [15,16], industry and municipal primary sludge [17], municipal
secondary sludge [18], and sewage sludge after hydrothermal carbonization and acidogenic microbial
fermentation [14,19] are examples of the substrates investigated in order to obtain volatile fatty acids
(VFAs) suitable as a carbon source for PHAs producing mixed microbial cultures (MMCs). At the
same time, agro-industrial wastewater and sludge have also attracted great interest. A plethora of
agro-industrial eﬄuents have been tested as substrate for PHAs production: Sugarcane molasses,
paper mill eﬄuent, dairy eﬄuent [20], distillery eﬄuents [21], rice winery wastewater [22], and yeast
industry wastewater [23]. Authors often agree in concluding that the reutilization of wastewater
and sludge as a carbon substrate not only abates the cost of PHAs production [20,21] but also
