records.gt_title: Plant-wide modeling of a metropolitan wastewater treatment plant to reduce energy consumption and carbon footprint
records.full_text:
https://doi.org/10.1007/s11356-022-23054-0
RESEARCH ARTICLE
Plant‑wide modeling of a metropolitan wastewater treatment plant 
to reduce energy consumption and carbon footprint
Bora Okan1 · Tuba Hande Erguder1 · Ayşegül Aksoy1 
Received: 16 March 2022 / Accepted: 13 September 2022 
© The Author(s), under exclusive licence to Springer-Verlag GmbH Germany, part of Springer Nature 2022
Abstract
A real metropolitan wastewater treatment plant (RWWTP) serving a population equivalent of 1.55 million was modeled to 
reduce energy consumption and carbon footprint (CFP). An approach was proposed to handle the dilution factor and partial 
aeration due to discontinuous air diffuser locations in the Bardenpho-5 configuration. Various operational, structural, and 
configurational modifications were evaluated. Results indicated that management scenarios might provide conflicting out-
comes for different targets. Reduced energy consumption may not result in lower CFP at the same time. Moreover, operational 
changes that would impact total nitrogen (TN) concentrations and ­N2O release may significantly impact CFP. A policy of 
using a modified Bardenpho-5 process with reduced internal recycle (IR) ratio, waste activated sludge (WAS), and return 
activated sludge (RAS) flow rates provided the lowest CPF. Modified Bardenpho-5 process and replacing belt thickeners 
with gravity thickeners supplied the highest savings in energy consumption. Overall, up to 14% and 20% reductions were 
possible in the energy consumption and CFP of the plant, respectively. The RWWTP may save up to 10% in energy expenses 
annually by operational modifications.
Keywords  Plant-wide modeling · Carbon footprint · Energy · GHC emissions · Wastewater treatment · BioWin
Introduction
Electricity usage in wastewater treatment constitutes 1% 
of the overall global electricity consumption in the World 
(IEA 2016). The largest share of water-related electricity 
consumption (42%) is for wastewater treatment in developed 
countries (IEA 2016). The electricity demand by wastewa-
ter treatment plants (WWTPs) in developing countries is 
expected to increase as wastewater quality becomes a prior-
ity due to stringent effluent standards (Lopes et al. 2020). As 
a result, efforts and financial resources allocated to reduce 
the energy demand and usage for wastewater treatment can 
be a non-zero-sum game as energy savings can compensate 
for the initial costs over time. As a result, researchers have 
studied a variety of alternatives and management options 
that may decrease the energy demand and operational costs 
(Maktabifard et al. 2018). These include scheduling of influ-
ent load to benefit from lower energy tariffs at night (Simon-
Varhelyi et al. 2020), optimization of operational parameters 
(i.e., adjustment of DO concentrations in bioreactors, pump 
speeds, etc.) (Wei 2013), process upgrades (Soliman and 
Eldyasti 2016; Callegari et al. 2018), etc.
As well as energy usage and operating costs, grow-
ing energy consumption due to the increasing number of 
WWTPs and energy-demanding treatment processes are 
crucial for greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions (Ashrafi et al. 
2014). WWTPs contribute to both direct and indirect GHG 
emissions due to treatment processes and electricity demand, 
respectively. GHGs emitted from domestic WWTPs are 
mainly ­CO2, ­CH4, and ­N2O (Delre et al. 2019). These emis-
sions could account for a significant portion of the total CFP 
of a WWTP (Mannina et al. 2019; Vasilaki et al. 2020). 
Walker et al. (2021) estimated the GHG emission due to 
wastewater treatment in the range of 0.013 to 0.91 kgCO2e/
Responsible Editor: Angeles Blanco
 *	 Ayşegül Aksoy 
	
aaksoy@metu.edu.tr
	
Bora Okan 
	
bora.okan@outlook.com
	
Tuba Hande Erguder 
	
etubahan@metu.edu.tr
1	
Department of Environmental Engineering, Middle East 
Technical University, 06800 Ankara, Turkey
/ Published online: 30 September 2022
Environmental Science and Pollution Research (2023) 30:16068–16080
1 3

m3 of wastewater treated. Maktabifard et al. (2020) reported 
a range of 0.6 to 1.7 kgCO2e/m3 for 6 WWTPs.
There is no legislation or limitation regarding energy 
consumption in WWTPs. Energy usage varies significantly 
among different plants. It is tough to regulate municipal 
WWTPs as they are in community service. They do not 
have profit goals which may limit the incentive for improv-
ing the energy efficiency of operations (Crisan and Korodi 
2018; Panepinto et al. 2016). According to Silva and Rosa 
(2015), the unit energy consumption for wastewater treat-
ment ranges from 0.1 to 2.5 kWh/m3. Walker et al. (2021) 
report the range of global average electricity consumptions 
for wastewater treatment as 0.24 to 1.35 kWh/m3. The aver-
age energy consumptions in Slovakia are 0.485 kWh/m3 and 
0.915 kWh/m3 for large and small rural WWTPs, respec-
tively (Bodik and Kubaska 2013). Specific power consump-
tions are between 0.44 and 2.07 kWh/m3 for oxidation ditch 
plants, and 0.30 kWh/m3 and 1.89 kWh/m3 for conventional 
activated sludge plants without sludge incineration (Mizuta 
and Shimada 2010). Overall, energy-related operating costs 
depend primarily on treatment technology and WWTP 
capacity (Pabi et al. 2013). Management experiences alone 
may not be sufficient to achieve energy-efficient operations 
(Panepinto et al. 2016).
Energy usage optimization for wastewater treatment is 
budget-friendly and may also help reduce CFP. Therefore, 
energy efficiency-related studies for WWTPs have drawn 
attention (Wu et al. 2010). Existing WWTPs may benefit 
from process optimization for energy-efficient treatment 
(Yin and Liu 2018). For this purpose, treatment process 
simulation models can evaluate WWTPs subject to various 
operational parameters and process modifications. They can 
be employed for developing better strategies for cost-effective 
treatment (Fernandez-Arevalo et al. 2017; Elawwad 2018), 
benchmarking of existing plants (Abusam et al. 2001), and 
feasibility evaluations of new treatment options (Khiewwijit 
et al. 2015). Modeling provides an advantage in testing 
potential improvements in a WWTP, as trial-and-error-
based modifications in an already-installed facility may not 
be possible. Nevertheless, plant-wide modeling studies are 
relatively few in the literature, limited in context (Rosen et al. 
2006), or model-based influent loads are used for control 
strategies (Sheik et al. 2021).
In this study, different from the existing studies, the treat-
ment scheme and operation of a real metropolitan WWTP 
(RWWTP) were modeled to evaluate the impacts of various 
operational, structural, and treatment process (configura-
tional) modifications on treatment efficiency, energy usage, 
and CFP. Therefore, the importance of different forms of 
modification alternatives was compared. The RWWTP was 
modeled using BioWin version 5.3 (EnviroSim 2019). More-
over, an approach was proposed to model a Bardenpho-5 
process in an oxidation ditch (OD) bioreactor configuration 
using Biowin through a specific distribution of discontinu-
ous air diffusers. Combinations of management alternatives 
were tested to maximize reductions in CFP and energy 
consumption.
Methodology
Studied RWWTP
The RWWTP considered in the study serves an approximate 
population equivalent of 1.55 million. It has a maximum 
design capacity of 320,000 ­m3/day (2030 projection). The 
average design flow rate is 240,000 ­m3/day. The name and 
location of the RWWTP are not provided due to confidenti-
ality. Nevertheless, Fig. S.1 in Supplementary Notes depicts 
the top view of the facility. Treatment scheme is Barden-
pho-5 realized through OD bioreactors in series. Treatment 
aims to decrease total suspended solids (TSS), organic car-
bon, nitrogen, and phosphorus contents of wastewater to 
levels dictated by relevant effluent discharge criteria.
Raw wastewater enters the RWWTP through an intake 
chamber. Screening is applied to remove coarse particles 
from wastewater. Then, three screw pumps elevate wastewa-
ter to grit chambers. After grit removal, a second pumping 
station delivers the wastewater to a selector tank. Then, the 
Bardenpho-5 process is used to treat wastewater in an OD 
bioreactor configuration. Sludge produced as the by-product 
of treatment is first introduced into a sludge buffer tank in 
which aeration is applied to control phosphorus release. 
Afterwards, sludge is sent to belt thickeners. Then, through 
centrifugal decanters, sludge is dewatered. Finally, sludge is 
combusted following lime stabilization.
In the RWWTP, there are three parallel wastewater lines, 
each including four OD bioreactors. Each line contains four 
secondary clarifiers (Fig. S.1). The Bardenpho-5 process is 
in the following order: an anaerobic tank, a pre-anoxic tank 
(OD2), an aerobic tank (OD1 followed by OD3), and a sec-
ondary anoxic tank with re-aeration (OD4). Figure 1 shows 
the OD bioreactors, flow directions (shown by arrows), and 
wastewater inlet and outlet points (bold arrows). Table 1 pro-
vides the average influent and effluent characteristics. The 
average flow rate is 249,325 ­(m3/day) (Personal communi-
cation with plant operators 2019), and the total bioreactor 
volume is 326,840 ­m3, including a selector tank.
Modeling approach
Figure 2 depicts the BioWin layout of the RWWTP units 
and processes for a single line for easy readability. In simu-
lation of the OD bioreactors, each OD had two zones to 
account for varying oxygen levels upstream and down-
stream. Using these zones provided the advantage of taking 
16069
Environmental Science and Pollution Research  (2023) 30:16068–16080
1 3

the discontinuity in diffuser locations, which created partial 
aeration, into consideration. Partially aerated zone volumes 
within an OD were determined through calibration. There-
fore, to simulate the zones in an OD and address dilution 
factor and partial aeration, each OD bioreactor was portrayed 
as two tanks connected through a virtual OD internal recycle 
(VODIR) flow rate. VODIR provided cyclic motion in ODs. 
Wastewater velocity was considered as 0.3 m/s in setting up 
the VODIR flow rate and the recycle ratio between virtual 
ODs in an OD (Personal communication with plant opera-
tors, 2019). The cyclic motion area was 225 ­m2. Wastewater 
level in an OD was at least 5 m. The total channel width of 
an OD bioreactor was 45 m. As calculated, the internal flow 
rate was 67.5 ­m3/s at the minimum wastewater level of 5 
m. The average flow rates of waste activated sludge (WAS) 
and return activated sludge (RAS) were 9457 ­m3/day and 
255,063 ­m3/day, respectively. IR (internal recycle) ratio for 
the plant was 2.6.
In order to set up and calibrate the model, initial runs 
were conducted to achieve a better agreement between 
observed and predicted effluent concentrations which were 
then set the bases for energy consumption and CFP calcu-
lations. Three sets of decisions were made before further 
calibration through parameter adjustments. In the first set, 
the decision to be made was on whether the actual RWWTP 
design values or the default values in BioWin should be 
used in simulations for given parameters. There were 7 such 
parameters whose design values were reported, and therefore 
known apriori (Table S.1). In the second set, the choice was 
on the dissolved oxygen (DO) simulation options available 
in BioWin. DO simulations could be performed either to 
achieve a preset DO value (1.5 mg/L in this study) in aerated 
ODs, or the resultant DO concentration was calculated for a 
given air flow rate. In the final set, the aerated portion of the 
total upstream volume in an anoxic OD was determined. In 
the RWWTP, a part in the upstream of the anoxic OD was 
aerated due to diffuser locations. Assumptions considered 
were 0%, 30%, 50%, or 70% of the anoxic OD volume was 
aerated. In initial runs, all other parameter values (see Sup-
plementary Notes) were kept as the default ones in BioWin. 
Different permutations of all initial run sets were assessed. 
Then for the combination providing the best agreement 
between observed and predicted effluent concentrations, 
further calibration was pursued by tuning the influential 
parameters determined through sensitivity analyses as dis-
cussed below. Following calibration, management scenarios 
were proposed and run to predict potential improvements in 
energy consumption and CFP.
Model calibration
Sensitivity analyses were employed for 55 model parameters/
constants to identify the ones that would be used in the further 
calibration of the model. Based on the categorization for input 
parameters in BioWin, 20 of them were fractional and stoi-
chiometric parameters/constants related to wastewater influent 
characteristics (Table S.2). Twenty-nine of them were treat-
ment kinetics-related parameters (Table S.3). In this group, 
reaction rate constants related to treatment units/processes, and 
biomass growth factors are present. Finally, remaining 6 were 
solid separation-related parameters in clarifiers (Table S.4). 
Fig. 1   Oxidation ditch (OD) bioreactor configuration in the RWWTP 
to achieve Bardenpho-5 process and locations of air diffusers (shown 
by crossed-out boxes). Bold arrows show the inlet and outlet

The initial values (default values in BioWin) of 55 parameters 
were modified by ±1%, and the percent changes in predicted 
effluent concentrations were determined. By this way, the sen-
sitivity of effluent concentration predictions for each model 
parameter was identified.
Due to confidentiality, limited observation data was avail-
able that had sufficient information to enable BioWin simula-
tions (spanning 12 months in 2017). As a result, the model 
was calibrated in a two-stage approach to overcome data 
limitations. First, calibration was employed on yearly aver-
age influent and effluent chemical oxygen demand (COD), 
biochemical oxygen demand (BOD), total suspended solids 
(TSS), total nitrogen (TN), and total phosphorus (TP) data by 
modifying the parameters the model is sensitive to (based on 
sensitivity analyses). Then, calibrated parameter values were 
validated against monthly data. The aim was to minimize the 
total error score (TES) (Eq. (1)). Model prediction errors were 
expressed based on predicted and observed treatment efficien-
cies (% removal in a given constituent) so that prediction errors 
in effluent quality constituents of different magnitudes could 
be compared and summed up to calculate TES. Deviations 
(or errors) in the observed and predicted treatment levels for 
a given constituent at a given month were evaluated (Eq. (2)). 
The aim was to minimize the error between observed and pre-
dicted treatment levels for monthly COD, BOD, TSS, TN, and 
TP concentrations.

Energy consumption simulations
The RWWTP had an average daily energy consumption of 
5242 kWh. The highest energy-consuming units were blow-
ers (2870 kWh) and pumps (1397 kWh). Thickening and 
dewatering units (323 kWh) and mixers (220 kWh) followed 
those (Personal communication with plant operators 2019). 
Electricity consumptions by these units comprised 92% of 
the overall energy consumption in the RWWTP. The model 
calibration was based on these units.
In BioWin, the energy consumption of blowers that pro-
vide air to an aeration tank changes with flow rates, DO 
(4)
Tpi,j = Cii,j −Cpi,j
Fig. 2   System layout (single line) of the studied RWWTP in BioWin
16071

1 3

concentrations in ODs, and COD reduction. Utilized ener-
gies by pumps and dewatering units are a function of the 
wastewater flow rate going through. On the other hand, the 
energy usage for mixers depends on the volume of ODs. 
Blowers consume energy based on achieved DO levels and 
air input to aerated ODs. The energy consumption by pumps 
was estimated for a total of five pumping stations existing 
in the facility: pumping station following screens, station 
after grit chamber, IR, RAS, and WAS pumping stations. 
The unit energy consumption of mixers was calibrated based 
on the unit bioreactor area. Lastly, unit energy consump-
tion was calibrated for the unit sludge amount for thicken-
ing and dewatering units. The targeted prediction accuracy 
of the energy consumption model was set as 10% error at 
most relative to observed energy consumption (Eq. (5)). 
This error margin also accounted for the electricity used 
in official buildings included in the observed data of the 
RWWTP but not simulated by the model. The energy model 
was calibrated and validated for 8 months of data based on 
data availability.

Carbon footprint calculations
Both direct and indirect GHG emissions were considered 
in determining the total carbon footprint (CFP) of the 
RWWTP. The direct carbon footprint was due to ­CO2, ­CH4, 
and ­N2O emissions from each bioreactor. BioWin calculated 
unit emissions. These were then multiplied by bioreactor 
volumes. Finally, ­CH4, and ­N2O were converted into ­CO2 
equivalences with the assumptions of 25 and 298 times 
higher greenhouse potential, respectively, compared to ­CO2 
(US EPA 2018). The indirect CFP was calculated based on 
the weighted carbon equivalent emission of electricity pro-
duction in Turkey. In Turkey, electricity production is sup-
plied through coal-powered thermal power plants (37.3%), 
natural gas-powered thermal power plants (29.8%), hydro-
power (19.8%), wind power (6.6%), solar power (2.6%), 
geothermal energy (2.5%), and others (MENR 2019). The 
carbon equivalent emission of a conventional coal-burning 
power plant is approximately 1000 ­gCO2eq/kWh (POST 
2006). Another fossil fuel-based, natural gas-powered elec-
tricity generation has 500 ­gCO2eq/kWh carbon equivalent

emission. Hydropower, wind energy, and solar energy have 
carbon equivalent emissions of 10–30 ­gCO2eq/kWh, 5 
­gCO2eq/kWh, and 35 ­gCO2eq/kWh, respectively (POST 
2006). Based on these quantities, the weighted-average car-
bon equivalent emission of electricity production in Turkey 
is approximately 540 ­gCO2eq/kWh. Indirect CFP was cal-
culated based on this value and the electricity usage in the 
RWWTP.
Management scenarios to reduce energy 
consumption and CPF
In order to lower the energy consumption and CFP of the 
RWWTP, potential improvements were proposed as scenar-
ios, and their impacts were evaluated using the calibrated 
model. Scenarios were grouped under operational, struc-
tural, and configurational changes in the system (Table 2).
Operational changes
In the management scenarios involving operational changes 
(Table 2), IR ratio, WAS, RAS, and re-aeration flow rates 
were varied to an extent they would not violate effluent dis-
charge quality standards (Table 1). In Scenario 1.1, the IR 
ratio was reduced from the initial value of 261% to 200%. 
Scenario 1.2 involved reduction in WAS flow rate. The flow 
rate was decreased from 9457 to 6000 ­m3/day. RAS flow rate 
was declined to 80% from the initial 102% in Scenario 1.3. 
At the field, the DO concentration in OD4 was not exceeding 
1.02 mg/l for the airflow rate applied in current practice. The 
main aim of supplying oxygen in OD4 in the plant was to 
supply ­N2 stripping. In this regard, Scenario 1.4 was tested 
as a potential approach to decrease energy demand through 
reducing airflow rate to sustain 0.5 mg/l DO concentration 
in the re-aeration zone in OD4.
Structural changes
Structural change scenarios involved the replacement of 
an existing component with another, such as replacing belt 
thickening units with gravity thickeners (Scenario 2.1), 
and replacing the decanter with a belt press (Scenario 2.2) 
(Table 2). Management alternatives under structural changes 
mainly aimed at achieving a difference in energy consump-
tion and CFP for sludge handling rather than impacting efflu-
ent concentrations.
Configurational changes
The scenarios under this group (Table 2) did not involve 
any new unit installations but utilized the flexibility of the 
existing system to achieve configurational changes through 
omitting an existing unit or modifying flow directions. These 
16072
Environmental Science and Pollution Research  (2023) 30:16068–16080
1 3

scenarios included changing the Bardenpho-5 configuration 
in the plant to A2O configuration (Scenario 3.1), using a 
modified Bardenpho-5 configuration (Scenario 3.2), and 
removal of the sludge buffer tank from the process flow 
scheme (Scenario 3.3) (Table 2).
Scenario 3.1 utilized the flexibility of the existing sys-
tem such that all four ODs (Fig. 1) in a given line connect 
through channels and gates. A specific operation order of 
gate valves could change the configuration from Barden-
pho-5 to A2O (Personal communication with plant operators 
2019). In A2O configuration, the last OD (OD4) downstream 
of flow is located between aerated ODs (OD1 & OD3), and 
the tank is getting the same aeration as OD1 and OD3. This 
lineup creates constant aeration in three ODs followed by 
one un-aerated OD (OD2). Therefore, OD2 is the anoxic 
zone, and the OD1-OD4-OD3 series combination represents 
the aerobic zones of A2O. Figure 1 depicts the current flow 
directions in the configuration of OD bioreactors to achieve 
Bardenpho-5 process as well as wastewater inlet and effluent 
outlet points. The BioWin layout of the OD line up for A2O 
for a single line is depicted in Fig. S.2.
The OD4 re-aeration zone is aerated in the existing lineup 
to achieve ­N2 stripping only at a lower aeration rate. This 
aeration is not sufficient to achieve the preset DO concen-
tration of 1.5 mg/l. With the A2O configuration, the airflow 
rate in OD4 could be increased to keep a continuous aeration 
zone between OD1, OD4, and OD3. This application may 
also constitute a potential approach to reduce the energy 
demand for treatment. The area occupied by the diffusers 
in OD4 is smaller than those in OD1 and OD3 (Fig. 1). 
Therefore, they do not provide the oxygen levels observed 
in OD1 and OD3. In this study, the operation of OD4 in 
anoxic conditions, rather than providing continuous aera-
tion, was tested. In the current situation (in RWWTP), OD4 
is already not supplying high oxygen levels. This scenario 
makes the configuration as anaerobic, anoxic (OD2), aero-
bic (OD1), anoxic (OD4), and aerobic (OD3) in the given 
order, which may look like a Bardenpho-5 process. However, 
in the proposed configuration, IR was diverted to the last 
aerobic tank (from OD3 to OD2) creating deviation from the 
Bardenpho-5 process. Therefore, this scenario was referred 
to as Modified Bardenpho-5 (Scenario 3.2).
Results and discussion
Model calibration
Treatment
The current system contained a total of twelve ODs in three 
lines (4 in each line). There were two fundamental chal-
lenges in simulating an OD as a bioreactor. One of them was 
the dilution occurring due to the cyclic motion of wastewater 
in an OD. The other one was estimating the extent of aerobic 
zones in aerobic bioreactors due to the non-homogeneous 
placement of air diffusers. In a conventional aerobic bioreac-
tor, the distribution of diffusers is generally homogeneous. 
However, the diffuser placement in aerobic tanks was non-
homogeneous and discontinuous in the RWWTP (Fig. 1). 
As a result, the spatial distribution of DO concentrations 
in aeration tanks was heterogeneous, i.e., leading to vary-
ing DO levels. For this matter, aerated volumes of given 
OD bioreactors were determined through initial simulations 
(Section 2.2) that aimed reaching observed quantities. The 
bioreactor configuration used in the division of the system 
into zones of different DO levels in BioWin is illustrated 
in Fig. S.3. Among the initial runs for 8 different options 
(given in Section 2.2) used to configure the model, con-
stituent concentration prediction errors were lower when 
50% of the aerobic tanks were unaerated, and the preset DO 
concentration (1.5 mg/L) option was used in BioWin simula-
tions. Moreover, BioWin default values rather than reported 
design values (Table S.1) for 7 parameters provided better 
fits to observed effluent concentrations. For the combination 
of these assumptions, TES was 8.46. TESs for other combi-
nations ranged between 9.58 and 23.03. For the best com-
bination (aeration in 50% of bioreactor volume, preset DO,

default BioWin values for model parameters), the VODIR 
flow rate was 67.5 ­m3/s between the aerated and unaerated 
zones in relevant ODs. Errors in treatment efficiencies were 
4.0%, 0.8%, 1.5%, 0,6%, and 1.7% for COD, BOD, TSS, 
TN, and TP concentrations, respectively, with respect to 
observed treatment efficiencies.
The model configuration was finalized such that 50% of 
the aerobic tanks were unaerated, the set DO concentration 
option was used to simulate reactors, and BioWin default 
values were utilized for relevant parameters. Then, sensitiv-
ity analyses were conducted for 55 selected model param-
eters and constants (Tables S.2, S.3, and S.4). Among them, 
only 6 parameters contributed to more than ±3% variation in 
at least one effluent quality constituents (BOD, COD, TSS, 
TP, TN) (Table S.5). These were readily biodegradable 
COD fraction, the ratio of unbiodegradable soluble COD to 
total COD (Fus), phosphorus in endogenous residue, anoxic 
hydrolysis factor, anoxic/anaerobic decay rate, and solid 
removal percent in secondary clarifiers (SR). The model 
was further calibrated for selected parameters for further 
improvement in effluent concentration predictions. In initial 
runs, the need for focusing on COD and BOD concentration 
predictions appeared to decrease overall prediction errors. 
Moreover, during the model configuration phase (initial 
runs for calibration), parameter default values in BioWin 
provided as good predictions as the actual RWWTP design 
values providing that the system was not significantly sen-
sitive to those parameters. As it was preferred to base the 
calibration on a minimum number of parameters, the model 
was further calibrated for Fus and SR parameters by chang-
ing their values within the ranges provided by Rossle and 
Pretorios (2001) and Sanin et al. (2011). The SR parameter 
impacted all effluent constituents significantly (Table S.5).
A Fus value of 0.03 and an SR value of 99.85% improved 
COD treatment efficiency prediction. Predicted effluent con-
centrations in comparison to observed values are depicted 
in Fig. 3. Errors in treatment efficiencies following Fus and 
SR adjustment were 1.3%, 0.9%, 0.8%, 1.1%, and 5.8% for 
COD, BOD, TSS, TN, and TP concentrations, respectively. 
Errors were higher for effluent TN and TP concentrations. 
However, as COD was one of the primary parameters con-
sidered, the Fus and SR values providing lower COD treat-
ment efficiency prediction errors were adopted. As should be 
emphasized, the predicted effluent phosphorus concentration 
(0.5 mg/L) was still below discharge standards (Table 1), 
although it exhibited the highest prediction error compared 
to other constituents.
In the validation period, the temperature of raw wastewa-
ter varied between 15 °C (January) and 26.8 °C (August). 
Steady-state mixed liquor suspended solid (MLSS) concen-
tration ranged from 2129 to 3786 mg/l in different months. 
Temporal variations in observed and predicted concentra-
tions are provided in Fig. 4. Figure S.4 presents observed 
versus predicted values. TSS and COD concentrations were 
generally over-estimated. BOD concentrations were, on the 
other hand, underestimated. Nevertheless, the target of hav-
ing at most 5% error in predicted treatment efficiencies for 
given effluent constituents was met in the majority. Only 
in June, the error observed for effluent TN concentration 
was 5.3%. In a total of 6 months, the error in the predicted 
TP treatment efficiencies was greater than 5%. Up to 10.6% 
error was observed (Table 3). At this error, the predicted 
effluent TP concentration was 0.51 mg/L, while the observed 
value was 1.33 mg/L. The average error in predicted treat-
ment efficiency was 5% for TP and less than 1.6% for others 
(Table 3).
Energy and CFP
The energy consumption of each pumping station was esti-
mated and calibrated for given head and pump diameter val-
ues and compared to observed annual equipment operation 
energy consumptions (Personal communication with plant 
operators 2019). The model slightly underestimated the 
power consumption of air blowers compared to observation 
values. Blower efficiencies could not be defined in BioWin. 
In order to overcome this constraint, the off-gas ­O2 ratio was 
changed (from 18.8 to 17.5%).
In the calibrated model, the system consumed a daily 
average of 5204 kWh electricity over 8 months. This value 
corresponded to 0.5 kWh/m3 of wastewater treated which 
was in the range of 0.1 to 2.5 kWh/m3 reported by Silva 
and Rosa (2015). Observed average consumption in the 
RWWTP was 5242 kWh for the same period. As a result, 
overall energy consumption in the plant was predicted with 
high accuracy. It was only less than 1% lower compared to 
the observed value. Observed and predicted monthly energy 
consumptions are depicted in Fig. 5. The figure shows that 
predicted energy consumption exhibits a similar trend with 
the observations starting from February. The targeted model

accuracy (10%) was achieved for energy consumption simu-
lations. The average error in energy consumption was 8.05%.
It was impossible to show comparisons for predicted 
and observed GHG emissions as there was no measured or 
reported data. However, as effluent constituent treatment 
efficiencies and energy consumptions were estimated suc-
cessfully, emissions calculated by the model were accepted 
as representative and used for CFP determination. The sys-
tem had 7221 ­kgCO2eq/h of GHG emissions. The overall 
emission source distribution is provided in Fig. 6. ­N2O strip-
ping from reactors contributed significantly to CFP, reaching 
59%. This contribution was mostly due to the ­CO2 equiva-
lence of ­N2O besides the emission rate. Indirect CFP had 
a significant share in the total by almost 40%. Therefore, 
strategies that target reduction in electricity consumption 
in the plant and ­N2O stripping may significantly reduce the 
CFP of the RWWTP.
Management scenarios
Impacts on effluent characteristics
In all scenarios, effluent BOD, COD, TSS, TN, and TP con-
centrations were below the effluent discharge standards. 
Changes in effluent constituent concentrations with respect 
to the base-case concentrations (calibrated model constitut-
ing Scenario 0.0) (in mg/L) are summarized in Table S.6. 
In the base-case, effluent BOD, COD, TSS, TN, and TP 
concentrations were 35.7 mg/L, 3.5 mg/L, 9.6 mg/L, 2.7 
mg/L, and 0.4 mg/L, respectively. In most cases, changes 
in effluent concentrations were less than ±1 mg/L. When 
percentage changes in concentrations were examined, vari-
ations were less than ±5%. The highest impact was in Sce-
nario 1.2 (WAS flow rate reduction), mostly due to higher 
solids retention time (SRT). The SRT increased from 16.8 
to 26.2 days. Reduction in WAS flow rate increased effluent 
COD and TSS concentrations by about 5 mg/L and 4 mg/L, 
respectively. For this scenario, other constituent concentra-
tions increased by at most 0.3 mg/L. COD, BOD, TSS, TN, 
and TP concentrations increased by 12.7%, 6.6%, 43.3%, 
3.7%, and 69.8%, respectively, in comparison to base-case 
effluent concentrations. Nevertheless, all concentrations 
were still below discharge standards. Other scenarios that 
exhibited a relatively higher effect on effluent concentra-
tions were Scenario 1.3 (RAS reduction), Scenario 3.1 (A2O 
configuration), and Scenario 3.2 (Modified Bardenpho-5). 
Structural change scenarios (Scenarios 2.1 and 2.2) had no 
impact on effluent concentrations as these scenarios were 
related to sludge handling.
Modifying the Bardenpho-5 process (Scenario 3.2) had a 
negative impact such that, except TN, effluent concentrations 
increased by up to 18%. Effluent TN concentration increased 
by 1.8 mg/L (65.8%), but it was still below effluent discharge 
standards. Modification of Bardenpho-5 increased BOD and 
TP concentrations by 18.0% and 16.3%, respectively. How-
ever, in absolute concentration values, these corresponded 
to less than 0.63 mg/L and 0.07 mg/L, respectively. RAS 
flow rate reduction (Scenario 1.3) resulted in a decline in all 
effluent parameters, reaching up to a concentration variation 
of 1.9 mg/L (5.2%) for COD. This effect might be related to 
better operating conditions due to amended SRT (15 days) 
and MLSS concentration (3000 mg/L) in this scenario.

This scenario was the only one with reduced effluent TP 
concentrations. Although the decline was slight in mg/L, 
it corresponded to 11.6% decrease. Scenario 1.3 was the 
only scenario that lowered SRT. Lower SRT aided in higher 
TP removal as Tchobanoglous et al. (2014) also stated. The 
highest percentage reduction in this scenario was for TSS 
(15.2%).
As represented by Scenario 3.1, changing the system 
to A2O configuration positively impacted COD and BOD 
by decreasing the effluent concentrations by up to 4.8%. 
However, TSS, TN, and TP concentrations increased by 
2.2%, 24.6%, and 14.0%, respectively. For the same aera-
tion volume, nutrient removal efficacies were higher for the 
Bardenpho-5 configuration compared to A2O, which was 
reported by others as well (Tchobanoglous et al. 2014).
Although not given as a scenario, adding a primary sedi-
mentation tank before an anaerobic tank was simulated as 
well. Such applications are in practice (ASAT 2005). Pri-
mary sedimentation resulted in a dramatic decline in the 
MLSS concentrations in ODs by up to 250 mg/L. This 
decline resulted in an increased effluent TN concentration 
reaching 35 mg/l, which is 3.5 times higher than the effluent 
discharge standard.
Impacts on energy consumption
Energy consumptions and CFPs under different management 
scenarios are summarized in Fig. 7 in terms of deviations 
from the base-case (Scenario 0.0) values. The base-case rep-
resents the actual plant operation in which electricity usage 
was 5204 kWh. Total GHG emission was 63,261 ton ­CO2eq/
year. In Fig. 7, values above zero indicate a worsening situ-
ation in energy usage or CFP. Values below 0, on the other 
hand, indicate improvement such that energy usage or CFP 
decreases due to a change in management or operation.
All scenarios, except Scenario 2.2 (use of a belt filter for 
decanter) and Scenario 3.1 (A2O configuration), resulted

energy consumptions

bution of the RWWTP

Environmental Science and Pollution Research  (2023) 30:16068–16080
1 3

in reduced energy consumptions. The lowest reduction in 
energy usage was 0.1% for Scenario 3.3. Among the sce-
narios considered, replacing the belt filter thickener with a 
gravity thickener (Scenario 2.1) appeared as the best option 
to reduce energy consumption by a relatively substantial 
fraction (11%). Gravity thickeners have lower energy con-
sumption than mechanical belt thickeners (Tchobanoglous 
et al. 2014). A2O configuration (Scenario 3.1) increased 
energy consumption by 5%. For the other scenarios, man-
agement approaches in which pumps operated at lower flow 
rates (Scenarios 1.1, 1.2, 1.3, and 1.4) resulted in lower elec-
tricity usage. For these scenarios, energy savings were in 
the range of 0.8 to 4.8%. Therefore, in general, designing a 
WWTP with lower pumping demand is crucial for decreas-
ing the energy demand for treatment and supply an energy-
efficient operation.
In the RWWTP, there is a risk of phosphorus release from 
sludge. Simulation results indicated no phosphorus accu-
mulation in the system when a gravity thickener is used. 
In the RWWTP, operators are trying to prevent phosphorus 
accumulation and release by using an aerated sludge buffer 
tank. Results obtained for Scenario 3.3 (removal of sludge 
buffer tank) indicated no significant effect on the energy 
consumption (Fig. 7). Therefore, keeping the sludge buffer 
tank has no disadvantage relative to energy usage.
Impacts on CFP
Scenarios exhibiting lower CFPs in comparison to the base-
case were Scenarios 3.2 (modified Bardenpho-5), 1.2 (WAS 
flow rate reduction), 3.1 (A2O configuration), and 2.1 (use 
of gravity thickeners), in descending order. For these scenar-
ios, 3 to 19% lower CFPs were achieved. Scenarios 2.2 (belt 
filter usage for dewatering), 1.3 (RAS flow reduction), 1.4 
(re-aeration reduction in OD4), and 1.1 (IR reduction) led 
to higher CFPs (0.3 to 5%) in the ascending order. Scenario 
3.3. (sludge buffer tank removal) had no significant change 
in CFP. When CFPs obtained for different scenarios were 
examined together with the changes in energy consump-
tion (Fig. 7), it was clear that trends and relative improve-
ments may not be in the same direction. An energy reduc-
tion brought by an operational change in a WWTP operation 
might be offset by an increase in CFP. Although Scenarios 
1.2, 2.1, 2.2, and 3.2 had either an increase or a decrease 
both in energy usage and CFP, this was not the case for 
Scenarios 1.1, 1.3, 1.4, 3.2, and 3.3.
It must be emphasized that this study considers both 
direct and indirect CFPs in the total. Therefore, although 
a reduction could be expected in the indirect CFP due to 
lower energy consumption for a given scenario, releasing 
higher amounts of GHGs from the WWTP due to changes 
in operations and conditions may be possible, or vice versa. 
For example, although the A2O configuration (Scenario 3.1) 
ended up with 5% higher energy consumption, its CFP was 
15% lower than the base-case. This is observed as the over-
all footprint due to ­N2O stripping decreased from 4200 kg 
­CO2eq/h to 3000 kg ­CO2eq/h. ­N2O gas emission was lower 
in the A2O configuration as effluent TN concentration 
increased by 24.6%. Massara et al. (2017) reported advanced 
nitrogen removal resulting in higher ­N2O emissions as well.

A lower WAS flow rate reduced the CFP of the system. An 
increase in the MLSS concentration from 3500 to 5000 mg/l 
reduced the ­N2O stripping level from 4200 to 3200 ­gCO2eq/
hr.
Combined impact of selected management scenarios
Above, impacts of each scenario on treatment, energy usage, 
and CFP were assessed individually. Based on the above 
discussions, scenarios were selected and combined to check 
for further potential synergistic improvements to decrease 
the energy demand and CFP of the RWWTP. Scenarios 
that exhibited reductions in both energy usage and CFP had 
priority in selection. A combined management alternative 
was proposed such that the RWWTP employed the modi-
fied Bardenpho-5 process and reduced the IR ratio, WAS, 
and RAS flow rates to 200%, 6,000 ­m3/day, and 200,000 ­m3/
day, respectively. This proposed combination of manage-
ment alternatives resulted in increased effluent COD, BOD, 
TSS, TN, and TP concentrations by 10%, 3%, 25%, 2%, and 
44%, respectively. However, the concentrations were still 
below discharge standards. Energy consumption and CFP 
were 4730 kWh and 6404 kgCO2e/h, respectively. These 
corresponded to approximately 9% and 20% reductions in 
energy usage and CFP, respectively.
Another scenario combination was reducing WAS flow 
rate, replacing belt thickeners with gravity thickeners, and 
modifying Bardenpho-5. This combination provided 14% 
energy reduction. However, the decline in CFP was rela-
tively minor (6%) compared to the previous combination of 
management alternatives.
In 2017, the RWWTP spent nearly €2 million on energy 
expenses. This quantity was estimated as €3.14 million for 
2020 with the increase in the unit cost of electricity. Some 
of the scenarios given in Table 2 provided reduced energy 
consumptions in the WWTP with no significant invest-
ment, such as optimizing the IR ratio, RAS, and WAS 
flow rates, and modifying the Bardenpho-5 configuration. 
These modifications did not indicate any violations of efflu-
ent discharge standards. For the figures obtained in this 
study, the facility may save up to €0.44 million annually 
just by operational and configurational modifications in the 
WWTP. Monetary savings may be improved with reduced 
CFP as well through the carbon market. Up to 20% reduc-
tion in CFP is possible, equivalent to12.97 tons ­CO2eq/year. 
According to the greenhouse gas converter of EPA (US 
EPA 2017), this reduction is equivalent to 1,654,093 num-
bers of smartphones charged or 5522.9 liters of gasoline 
consumed in a year. Even higher energy savings might be 
possible with some investment by replacing belt thickeners 
with gravity thickeners.
Conclusions
In this study, plant-wide modeling of a real metropolitan WWTP 
was conducted to evaluate the impacts of various operational, 
structural, and configurational modifications on the treatment 
performance with a focus on energy consumption, CFP, and 
treatment efficiencies. It was shown that processes in a large-
scale WWTP can be simulated, and the model can be used to test 
the response of a WWTP to different management alternatives.
Overall, different scenarios provided the best outcomes 
for varying aims. Management scenarios provided conflict-
ing outcomes for different targets. For example, a manage-
ment alternative could supply energy and CFP savings. How-
ever, effluent concentrations may rise in return. Moreover, 
lower energy consumption may not result in a reduction in 
CFP at the same time. Replacing belt thickeners with gravity 
thickeners provides significant energy savings as well as a 
decline in CFP. The WAS flow rate reduction and Barden-
pho-5 modification alternatives resulted in the best outcomes 
with a significant decrease in CFP. Energy savings were pos-
sible for these scenarios as well. For effluent quality, on the 
other hand, RAS flow rate reduction enabled a decrease in 
all effluent constituent concentrations. In general, options or 
operational changes that would impact effluent TN concen-
trations and ­N2O release significantly impacted CFP due to 
the high ­CO2 equivalence of ­N2O. In the WWTP, 59% of the 
CFP was due to ­N2O emissions. 39% of the CFP was from 
indirect emissions due to electrical consumption.
Evaluation of the management alternatives showed that 
it is possible to improve the energy efficacy of the RWWTP 
and reduce CFP without compromising effluent quality with 
respect to discharge standards. Savings in energy usage 
and CFP can be up to 14% and 20%, respectively. Through 
modeling, more sustainable solutions can be tested for other 
existing WWTPs as well.

DOI: 10.1111/jiec.12993
R E S E A R C H A N D A N A LY S I S
Life cycle assessment of side stream removal and recovery
of nitrogen from wastewater treatment plants
Rosalie van Zelm1
Raquel de Paiva Seroa da Motta1
Wan Yee Lam1
Wilbert Menkveld2
Eddie Broeders2
1Department of Environmental Sciences,
Institute for Water and Wetland Research,
Radboud University, Nijmegen, The Netherlands
2Nijhuis Water Technology BV, Doetinchem, The
Netherlands
Correspondence
RosalievanZelm,DepartmentofEnvironmen-
talSciences,InstituteforWaterandWetland
Research,RadboudUniversity,P.O.Box 9010,
6500GLNijmegen,TheNetherlands.
Email:r.vanzelm@science.ru.nl
EditorManagingReview:StefanieHellweg
Abstract
In the light of a circular economy, the Nijhuis Ammonia Recovery system (AECO-NAR) was devel-
oped to not only remove nitrogen from wastewater streams, but also produce ammonium sulfate
(AS), used as fertilizer, in a single plant. The goal of this paper was to quantify the environmental
impacts of side stream ammonia recovery with the AECO-NAR system and compares them with
the impacts of side stream nitrogen removal combined SHARON (partly nitrification)-anammox
plant. For this, an environmental life cycle assessment was performed with a functional unit (FU)
of the treatment of 1 kg of total dissolved nitrogen inflow. Since AS obtained by the AECO-NAR
is a by-product of the ammonia removal process, allocation was based on system expansion.
Foreground inventory data were obtained from a full-scale plant. ReCiPe2016 was used to deter-
mine human health and biodiversity impacts. Results show that due to the production of AS in an
integrated water treatment and production system, the AECO-NAR avoids impacts of current AS
production, leading to negative impact scores. Impacts per FU decrease with increasing inflow
concentrations of ammonia. Main improvement options are the use of renewable energy and the
replacement of the cleaning chemical citric acid with a sustainable alternative. Total impacts of the
AECO-NAR system diminish when comparing the system to the biological SHARON-Anammox
system, due to production of AS fertilizer product. Due to the fertilizer production step being
integrated in the side stream treatment, the complete system is beneficial over ammonia recovery
and wastewater treatment as separate systems.
K E Y W O R D S
allocation, ammonia, ammonium sulfate, circular economy, fertilizer, nutrient recovery
1
INTRODUCTION
(Inter)national policies currently aim to advance a circular economy (CE), including efforts to take the bio-based economy a step further by focus-
ing on recycling and reuse of products and materials to minimize waste and resource use (e.g., EC, 2015, 2019; Rijksoverheid, 2019). As part of
the European Union action plan toward a CE, nutrient recycling and water-efficiency measures are addressed (EC, 2015). Globally, an increase
in wastewater emissions of nitrogen (N) is predicted, at least a doubling from 2000 to 2050 (Van Drecht, Bouwman, Harrison, & Knoop, 2009).
Nutrient over-enrichment, that is, eutrophication, may lead to aquatic ecosystem degradation due to algal blooms, the growth of macrophytes, and
aquatic hypoxia and anoxia (as summarized by Van Drecht et al., 2009).
Several countries have set nitrogen discharge limits applicable to wastewater treatment plants (WWTPs), such as the ones by the European
Water Framework Directive (2000/60/EC) that calls for the implementation of the Urban Waste Water Directive (92/271/EEC) and sets discharge
This is an open access article under the terms of the Creative Commons Attribution License, which permits use, distribution and reproduction in any medium, provided
the original work is properly cited.
c
⃝2020 The Authors. Journal of Industrial Ecology published by Wiley Periodicals, Inc. on behalf of Yale University
Journal of Industrial Ecology 2020;1–10.
wileyonlinelibrary.com/journal/jiec
1

2
VAN ZELM ET AL.
limits of 10–15 mg N/L for European WWTPs. Next to eutrophication, energy use related impacts are of concern since wastewater treatment
plants (WWTPs) are large energy consumers (Di Fraia, Massarotti, & Vanoli, 2018; EPA, 2013).
Rodriguez-Garcia et al. (2014) showed the potential for nitrogen removal from the supernatant resulting from the anaerobic digestion of
sludge, also called side stream, which substantially decreased eutrophication impacts, while also slightly reducing other environmental impacts.
Among investigated side stream technologies are more common technologies such as nitritation-anammox (Rodriguez-Garcia et al., 2014;
Schaubroeck et al., 2015), SHARON-Anammox (Hauck, Maalcke-Luesken, Jetten, & Huijbregts, 2016; Lin, Guo, Shah, & Stuckey, 2016), nitrifica-
tion/denitrification (Lin et al., 2016; Rodriguez-Garcia et al., 2014), and struvite crystallization (Rodriguez-Garcia et al., 2014). But there are also
promising new technologies such as ion-exchange (Lin et al., 2016) and cyanobacterial removal (Quiroz-Arita et al., 2019).
Various authors (e.g., Foley, de Haas, Hartley, & Lant, 2010; Hauck et al., 2016; Lederer & Rechberger, 2010; Lin et al., 2016; Rodriguez-Garcia
et al., 2011; Vidal, Poch, Marti, & Rodriguez-Roda, 2002) highlighted the trade-off between more nitrogen removal on the one hand and higher
energy demand, and related greenhouse gas emissions, on the other hand when comparing different types of wastewater treatment. Higher energy
and chemical demands generally lead to higher costs. Hall, Priestley, and Muster (2018) investigated the environmental and economic impacts of
wastewater treatment systems and showed a possible win-win situation for costs and impacts. Resource recovery is an important factor to achieve
such win-win situations. Ye et al. (2018) reviewed ammonium recovery systems, indicating benefits of using recovered ammonium to supplement
fertilizer production and save the expenses required in the conventional manufacturing of fertilizer, the so-called Haber–Bosch (H–B) process.
However, no system was included that contains the actual production of fertilizer.
In the light of a circular economy, the Nijhuis Ammonia Recovery system (AECO-NAR1) was developed to not only remove nitrogen from the
side stream anaerobic digestate supernatant, but also create a valuable product. The system combines ammonium removal and nitrogen-fertilizer
production in a single plant. AECO-NAR is a novel chemical recovery technology developed in response to the current biological techniques and
their existing obstacles when implemented in a full-scale, such as the slow growth rate and sensitivity of the Anammox bacteria to dissolved oxygen
(Eini, 2012). The chemical removal of nutrients from wastewater has its own challenges, such as CO2 dosage and expected climate change impacts
related to the elevated use and consumption of chemicals and energy. However, due to the recovery of ammonium sulfate (AS), it is hypothesized
that the AECO-NAR system will overall cause less environmental impacts. This stems from the avoidance of electricity and chemicals used for the
production of AS by the mainstream Haber–Bosch process.
The goal of this research was to quantify the environmental impacts of side stream ammonia recovery with the AECO-NAR system and to
compare these impacts with the impacts of side stream nitrogen removal combined SHARON (Stable High rate Ammonia Removal Over Nitrite)-
anammox plant. For this, an environmental life cycle assessment was performed on the AECO-NAR’s plant located in the east of the Netherlands
at Groot Zevert Vergisting in Beltrum. This is, to our knowledge, the first time the environmental impacts of this system are investigated.
2
METHODOLOGY
2.1
System description
Figure 1 shows the system boundaries of the AECO-NAR system, which is categorized into two main phases:
Phase A: Where high nutrient loaded digestate supernatant inflows are fed into a CO2 stripping tank and an ammonia stripping column.
Phase B: After the stripping process, ammonium that is left goes to a neutralization column, where pH is controlled at +/−6. Subsequently, the
flow is sent to the scrubber column where the ammonia removed reacts with H2SO4 and fertilizer (NH4)2SO4 can be obtained with ammonium
concentrations up to 38%.
Calcium (CaO) is used as an ion exchanger that prepares the solution for the ammonia removal step allowing up to 80% removal. When lower
removal efficiencies (60–70%) are sufficient, the system can operate without the addition of calcium. A Combined Heat and Power gas engine (CHP)
provides most of the electricity and heat for the system. Those engines are commonly implemented at digesters and are part of most WWTPs’
designs. The advantage of the CHP is that the AECO-NAR system can be fully fed with residual heat from the process itself. Moreover, the AECO-
NAR’s electricity demand is constant and very little dependent on the NH4-N concentration in digestate. To prevent emission to the environment
a gas-lock (filled with a small amount of H2SO4) is installed at the exhaust of the AECO-NAR. Therefore, what is emitted to the air is mostly moist
and a small percentage of CO2.
At the moment, there is a full-scale AECO-NAR plant installed in the United Kingdom and a plant running in the Netherlands, where improve-
ments and tests are constantly made.
1 The term AECO is used by Nijhuis Technologies to indicate technologies they developed that recover products from wastewater and sludge.

VAN ZELM ET AL.
3
FIGURE 1
AECO-NAR’s system boundaries: Phase A represents the ammonia removal, while phase B represents the AS production. Phase B
is methodologically equivalent to the H-B process (Source: Nijhuis Water Technology)
2.2
Life cycle assessment
Life Cycle Assessment (LCA) is a tool that quantifies the environmental impacts associated with a product or service (Consoli et al., 1993). It reviews
the complete life cycle of the product or service in several steps. We performed an LCA based on the guidelines described in the ISO 14040–44
standards (ISO, 2006). The functional unit (FU) was defined as the treatment of one kg of total dissolved nitrogen inflow (kg Ntotal,in). We chose
this FU because the specific function of the AECO-NAR system is to remove and reuse nitrogen. Since the AS obtained by the AECO-NAR is a by-
product of the ammonia removal process and its local commercialization and consumption are expected, allocation was based on system expansion.
That is, first, an LCA of the entire side stream AECO-NAR process, including wastewater treatment and AS production process, was performed.
Subsequently, to arrive at the impacts of the wastewater treatment only, the environmental impacts of the mainstream AS production processes
(Haber–Bosch) were subtracted from the AECO-NAR’s impact. This allows a fair comparison of the outcomes with other side stream treatments.
As the full AECO-NAR system is an integrated system, it is not possible to separate the impacts of the digestate treatment from the impacts of the
AS production.
Since we chose an attributional LCA, the ammonium sulfate replaced is the market mix. According to the European nitrogen assessment, 88%
of the ammonium sulfate is a by-product of caprolactam and 12% comes from other processes, such as Haber–Bosch but also nickel and copper
production processes (Leip & Prud’homme, 2011).
Construction materials used for infrastructure involved in facilities were not included in the system boundaries due to data immaturity and an
expected small difference with SHARON-Annamox facilities.
An inventory of the energy and material inputs and outputs of the AECO-NAR was done based on the data collected from the AECO-NAR’s
plant located in the east of the Netherlands at Groot Zevert Vergisting in Beltrum. A digestate flow capacity of 270 m3/day and 2.5 kg of NH4-N
concentration per m3 of digestate and 80% of ammonia removal efficiency was taken. All the parameters of the AECO-NAR were modeled accord-
ing to the FU (kg Ntotal,in) and can be found in Table 1. Background data on chemicals and energy were taken from ecoinvent v3.3, “allocation at
point of substitution—unit processes” database (Wernet et al., 2016).
The ammonium sulfate, current market share, was modeled as 88% by-product of caprolactam, 4.6% from nickel mine operation (ecoinvent
data), 0.2% smelting and refining of nickel ore (ecoinvent data), and the remaining 7.2% from Haber–Bosch process. For this, the ammonium sulfate
production processes in SimaPro, including energy and materials only, were adapted to include the production of sulfuric acid and ammonia (see
Supporting Information for all data used). For the allocation of impacts of ammonium sulfate as a by-product from caprolactam, plastics Europe
was followed (Boustead, 2005), where the burdens associated with the production of the ammonia and sulfuric acid used in the production of
ammonium sulfate were assigned to the ammonium sulfate by-product.
A transport distance of 150 km was included as part of the H–B process, which represents the average distance that the AS has to travel, via
truck, from the Rotterdam Port to a consumer located in the center of the country. The Rotterdam port was used as a baseline location in the
Netherlands due to its trading relevance (Shi & Xing, 2015). Because of the smaller quantities of the AS produced with the AECO-NAR system,
transport is estimated to be a maximum of 50 km.
The ReCiPe2016 LCIA method (H) v1.1 on endpoint level was used to quantify the environmental impacts of the AECO-NAR system (Huijbregts
et al., 2016). All calculations were performed with the software SimaPro v8.5 (Pré consultants, 2017).

4
VAN ZELM ET AL.

2.3
Comparison with biological removal
The two-step SHARON-Anammox sidestream removal system configured at the Rotterdam Dokhaven WWTP was chosen as the system to be
compared with the AECO-NAR. For this, data from Hauck et al. (2016) was used, where an LCA of the WWTP is performed using the same FU as in
our study. Foreground inventory data can be found in Table S1 in the Supporting Information.
2.4
Scenario analyses
To assess the robustness of the results three sensitivity analyses were performed:
1. Availability residual heat: On average, the amount of heat from the CHP is 500 kW, while 200 kW is needed to heat the system. However, to
test the extra impacts when residual heat would not be available, we calculated the damage impacts in case extra heat would need to be produced
from natural gas (European market mix).
2. Varying influent concentrations: Influent concentrations may vary over the year as well as per location, depending on nitrogen deposi-
tion and nitrogen addition to the soil, for example, in agricultural areas. Because these influent characteristics could have implications for the
choice of ammonia side stream removal technologies, the effect of NH4-N inflow concentration on the environmental impact of the AECO-NAR
and the SHARON-Annamox system was investigated. For this, all the chemical and electricity consumption of the AECO-NAR were adapted to a
0.5 kg variance, starting from 1 to 3.5 kg of NH4-N concentration per m3 of digestate inflow. The inventory data are in Table S2 in the Supporting
Information.
3. Replacement of fertilizer: As a default, the ammonium sulfate produced with the AECO-NAR system was assumed to replace the market
mix. However, since ammonium sulfate will be produced as a by-product of various processes, the fertilizer sulfate produced via AECO-NAR will
most likely replace the Haber–Bosch produced product only. Therefore, we tested the impacts by replacing this only, as well as a maximum impact
scenario where no impacts were allocated to the fertilizer produced as a by-product in the caprolactam production process.

VAN ZELM ET AL.
5
FIGURE 2
Human health impacts (a,b) and ecosystem impacts (c,d) of the full AECO-NAR system, divided by contributing process (a,c) and
contributing impact category (b,d). These impacts exclude the positive impacts of replacing current ammonium sulfate production. Underlying
data used to create this figure can be found in Tables S6 and S7 in the Supporting Information
3
RESULTS
To get an idea of the contribution to environmental impacts of each part of the system, Figure 2 shows the impacts of the full AECO-NAR
system, including both the wastewater treatment and the ammonium sulfate production per kg Ntotal,in. It can be seen that for human health
as well as ecosystem impacts, sulfuric acid production contributes to a large extent. This is because of particulate matter formation and acid-
ification, respectively. Next to that, the direct phosphorus emissions in the wastewater causing eutrophication contribute most to ecosystem
damage.
To be able to compare the system with other wastewater treatment technologies, the impacts were determined including avoided impacts of
the ammonium sulfate production. Figure 3 shows the comparison of the AECO-NAR system and the SHARON-Anammox system.
Due to the production of ammonium sulfate in an integrated water treatment and production system, the AECO-NAR avoids impacts of current
ammonium sulfate production, resulting in negative impacts. These results are caused by the avoidance of liquid ammonia production, which
mainly causes impacts on global warming, ozone formation, acidification, water consumption, and fine particulate matter formation. For human
health, net negative impacts were estimated for the AECO-NAR system, while anammox has positive impacts, mainly caused by global warming.
For ecosystem damage, net positive impacts were estimated due to the phosphorous emissions, which are similar for the anammox system. Overall,
the AECO-NAR system causes less damage to ecosystems. When residual heat was assumed not to be available from the CHP, human health and
ecosystem impacts increased by 1.5% only.
Figure 4 shows the decrease in environmental impacts for increasing concentrations of ammonia. This is mainly caused by a decrease in energy
consumption for 80% removal of low ammonium concentrations versus high ammonium concentrations. All impacts decrease for increasing con-
centrations, per FU of 1 kg of total dissolved nitrogen inflow, but global warming the most with 50%.
Figure 5 shows the results of the scenario analysis performed to check the results with changing assumption of replacement of the produced
ammonium sulfate of current used fertilizer. It can be seen that in the likely scenario that the ammonium sulfate produced with AECO-NAR only

6
VAN ZELM ET AL.
FIGURE 3

Underlying data used to create this figure can be found in Table S8 in the Supporting Information
replaces the virgin ammonium sulfate produced with Haber–Bosch, the avoided impacts are 1.7 times larger for human health impacts and slightly
larger for ecosystem impacts. In case no impacts from caprolactam production are allocated to the by-product ammonium sulfate, the impacts of
AECO-NAR are 3.5 (human health) and 2.2 (ecosystems) times larger than the impacts of SHARON-Anammox treatment.
4
DISCUSSION
4.1
Uncertainties and limitations
In several cases, estimations were necessary for this study that could lead to uncertainties. First, due to the AECO-NAR’s system infrastructure
data immaturity, not yet accountable by the time this study was performed, we had to limit the impact analysis by excluding the hydraulic and civil
materials involved at the plant construction. Although in several cases the influence of major infrastructure to environmental impacts was shown
to be negligible (e.g., Hauck et al., 2016; Morelli et al., 2018; Rahman, Eckelman, Onnis-Hayden, & Gu, 2018), some studies showed a contribution
of 20–40% to overall environmental impact (Ortiz, Raluy, Serra, & Uche, 2007; Remy & Jekel, 2008). Next to that, improved levels of wastewater
treatment and nutrient removal, such as the AECO-NAR system, are usually accompanied by improvements in the plant design and physical infras-
tructure. Consequently, the production and consumption of materials needed for the improvement can cause an increased environmental burden
(Foley et al., 2010). However, the same counts for the SHARON-Anammox system, being at early-stage development, making the difference in the
systems related to infrastructure small.
Second, emissions and energy inputs were determined as accurately as possible, based on various measurements done for the AECO-NAR sys-
tem. Logically, these data could change over time, not the least because the design of the system is constantly being fine-tuned and the emissions

might also vary. In a near future, once the AECO-NAR design achieves a final format, we recommend new emissions measurements for a longer
period of time in order to include seasonal and concentration variations and provide a less uncertain average.
Third, boththe AECO-NARsystem and the SHARON-Anammoxare technologiesthatcanbe implemented asside streamsona WWTP. Although
Rodriguez-Garcia et al. (2014) showed that, compared to the impacts of a full WWTP, the environmental impacts of side stream supernatant result-
ing from the anaerobic digestion of sludge are small, we chose to focus on this part only for two reasons: (a) the main goal of this work was to
specifically compare a biological side stream removal technology with a new chemical side stream recovery technology; and (b) the impacts of the
mainstream WWTP will stay the same for both technologies. Our results are therefore not comparable to other studies investigating full WWTPs,
but are comparable to LCA investigating specific side stream technologies.
4.2
Interpretation of contribution analysis
The main contributors to the AECO-NAR impacts are the sulfuric acid needed to produce the fertilizer and the direct phosphorus emissions. Back-
ground data used on sulfuric acid production were from ecoinvent and judged to be of low uncertainty according to the pedigree matrix approach
(Althaus et al., 2007). Production of this chemical contributes mainly to acidifying, fine dust, and global warming emissions, and to a lesser extent
to water consumption, contributing to 84% of the impacts on human health and 27% of the impacts on ecosystems. However, if the wastewater
treatment and ammonia production was not integrated in one system, the impacts of ammonia sulfate production would be much larger, since the
ammonia stream would not be reused, and the ammonium sulfate would need to be transported from further away to its application site. The com-
parison with the combined SHARON-Anammox plant shows that, due to the fertilizer production step being integrated in the WWTP, the complete
system is beneficial over ammonia recovery and wastewater treatment as separate systems.
Direct phosphorus emissions in the wastewater flow contribute for 63% to the total ecosystem damage, showing the importance of removing
pollutants from the wastewater. Here, we assumed the same amount of total phosphorus emissions as when using the SHARON-Anammox system,
since both systems focus on ammonia recovery only. These results show that technologies combining nitrogen and phosphorus removal, such as
struvite crystallization (see e.g., Rodriguez-Garcia et al., 2014) could be beneficial, and it would be interesting to see the possibilities of recovering
both nutrients from the water flow.

8
VAN ZELM ET AL.
Electricity use and citric acid, used as cleaning agent, both contribute a large share to the total human health and ecosystem impacts. Regarding
electricity use, the AECO-NAR system becomes more efficient with increasing concentrations of nitrogen in the influent. This means that the
system is especially useful in areas with nitrogen-rich wastewater, such as agricultural areas and digestate from digesters. In the future, electricity
sources will also become more and more renewable which might reduce the impact of electricity use on several categories. Citric acid, even though
used in small amounts once in a while to clean the system, clearly contributes to the overall results (see Figure 2). The large contribution of citric
acid in the production step was also found by Golsteijn et al. (2015), which stresses the need to look into potentially more sustainable alternatives
(e.g., lemon juice from sustainable sources).
4.3
Allocation and functional unit choices
As argued by Vadenbo, Hellweg, and Astrup (2017), when it comes to allocation procedures, it is important to systematically determine and com-
municate substitution processes. Here, we assumed that the ammonium sulfate produced with the AECO-NAR system replaces the market mix,
as is commonly done in attributional assessments. Since most of the ammonium sulfate originates from caprolactam production, a choice needed
to be made on how to allocate the impacts of this production process to ammonium sulfate. As argued by Boustead (2005), a relatively high mass
of ammonium sulfate is produced compared to caprolactam. A simple mass partition would therefore assign most of the burdens to ammonium
sulfate. Since the primary aim of the process is caprolactam production, and there are considerably simpler routes available for the production of
ammonium sulfate, it would not be fair to allow most of the burdens to be assigned to ammonium sulfate. Therefore the choice was made to only
assign the production of the ammonia and sulfuric acid used in the production of ammonium sulfate to the fertilizer. Our results showed that these
impacts already are considerable, making the AECO-NAR an efficient process. Allocating parts of the energy consumption, for example, also to the
fertilizer, would only increase the advances shown for the AECO-NAR process and were therefore not tested here. When allocating no impacts to
the by-product (as the process will always be in place to create caprolactam and thus ammonium sulfate, independent of the need for it), however,
we showed that the ecosystem impacts of the AECO-NAR system were more than two times larger than the impacts of the SHARON-Anammox
system and the impacts on human health were even four times larger. However, because of the current market potential and thereby profit of the
fertilizer, this is currently not a recommended way of allocating the impacts.
Next to the logical choice to replace the ammonium sulfate market mix, it could also be argued that in practice only the newly produced fertilizer
would be replaced, as ammonium sulfate will be continued to be produced as a by-product, regardless of the potential market. Therefore, we tested
this assumption and results showed the increase in environmental benefits with the AECO-NAR system compared to the SHARON-Anammox
system.
For wastewater treatment in general an FU of 1 m3 water treated is chosen. However, as the specific function of the AECO-NAR system is to
remove and reuse nitrogen, an FU of the treatment of one kg of total dissolved nitrogen inflow (kg Ntotal,in) was chosen. Another option would have
been to look at the final product and to choose an amount of fertilizer produced, as for example, done by Pradel and Aissani (2019) for phosphorous
removal. However, in that case, only part of the wastewater treatment can be allocated to the fertilizer production, as part needs to be allocated
to the flow of clean wastewater out of the system. This will lead to another allocation problem. Moreover, due to the integrated nature of the
AECO-NAR, that is, with the function to clean water but the side benefit to reuse ammonia, it is more appropriate to focus on the wastewater
treatment. Pradel and Aissani (2019) found that fertilizer produced with their technology was less environmentally friendly over the production
via the conventional way. This, while it should be seen as an extra benefit to produce fertilizer from wastewater streams and not as the main aim to
produce fertilizers in this way.
5
CONCLUSIONS
Ye et al. (2018) already showed that ammonium recovery from wastewater reduces the costs, energy, and environmental footprint associated with
this removal process, and additionally ensures that the final product can be used to supplement fertilizer production and save the expense required
in the industrial Haber–Bosch process. This paper shows the actual benefits of an integrated removal, recovery, and fertilizer production system
in terms of environmental costs. Main improvement options are the use of renewable energy and the replacement of the cleaning chemical citric
acid with a sustainable alternative. Looking more broadly to technological developments, the focus should be put on recovery of multiple substance
flows from the wastewater. The total impacts of the AECO-NAR system diminish when comparing the system to the biological SHARON-Anammox
system, due to ammonium sulfate production. Due to the fertilizer production step being integrated in the WWTP, the complete system is beneficial
over ammonia recovery and wastewater treatment as separate systems.
